topics.title: Surface Treatment for Plastics and Steel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT864 - PLASTICS & DECORATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Surface Treatment for Plastics and Steel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Surface Treatment in Plastics & Decoration

In the field of Plastics & Decoration, **Surface Treatment** refers to the systematic application of physical, chemical, or electrochemical processes to alter the topmost molecular layers (typically **0.1 nm to 100 μm** depth) of a substrate — either a **polymer (plastic)** or a **metallic (steel)** surface — to enhance its functional and aesthetic performance. These processes are the critical "first bridge" between a raw, inert, or contaminated substrate and a high-performance decorated end-product (e.g., a chromed ABS faucet handle, a powder-coated steel chair, or a printed PET bottle).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1):**
> *Surface treatment is defined as the preparation and modification of the outermost boundary layer of a substrate to improve adhesion, corrosion resistance, wettability, printability, gloss, and decorative compatibility. For plastics, it focuses on energy enhancement of low-surface-energy polymers. For steel, it focuses on removal of mill scale, oxide layers, and contaminants while depositing a conversion coating for paint adhesion.*

### 1.2 The "Why" — Functional Drivers of Surface Treatment

A B.Tech engineer working in a polymer processing or product design firm must understand that untreated surfaces fail for predictable, measurable reasons. The drivers for surface treatment are categorized into five engineering needs:

| # | Functional Driver | Engineering Reason | Typical Industrial Consequence if Skipped |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Adhesion Promotion** | Polymers have low surface energy (e.g., PP ≈ **31 mN/m**, PE ≈ **30 mN/m**); paint/adhesive needs ≥ **38 mN/m** | Paint peels, labels fall off, delamination of metallization |
| 2 | **Corrosion Resistance** | Steel naturally oxidizes; bare iron forms Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺ rust | Rusting within weeks, structural failure |
| 3 | **Wettability / Printability** | Inks, dyes, and primers must spread uniformly (contact angle < 30°) | Beading of ink, dot gain, illegible printing |
| 4 | **Cleanliness / Interfacial Bonding** | Oils, dust, release agents, and oxide layers form weak boundary layers | Pinholes in coatings, poor bond strength |
| 5 | **Decorative Aesthetics** | Gloss, DOI (Distinctness of Image), and color uniformity | Patchy finishes, visual rejection of parts |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wallpaper Wall" Intuition

Imagine you want to stick expensive wallpaper onto a room wall.

- **Plastic (e.g., a polypropylene chair)** is like a **smooth, greasy, plastic-coated wall**. Wallpaper will not stick because the surface energy is too low — it is "non-wettable."
- **Steel** is like a **rough, dusty brick wall** with peeling old paint and oily fingerprints. Wallpaper will *physically* touch it, but the moment you pull, the dust and rust come off with the paper — the *weak boundary layer* fails.

**Surface treatment is the "wall preparation step":**
- For plastic, you **raise the surface energy** (sand lightly, apply primer, or treat with corona/plasma) so the wallpaper glue (paint/adhesive) can wet and grip.
- For steel, you **remove the dust/rust/grease** (degreaser + sandblaster) and then **apply a primer coat** (phosphate layer) so the wallpaper glue bonds chemically and mechanically to a fresh, clean, micro-rough surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Takeaway:** Every decorative process (painting, electroplating, vacuum metallization, hot-stamping, printing) is only as good as the **surface preparation** that preceded it. Surface treatment is the silent enabler of *all* downstream decoration steps covered later in this course (Modules 2–5).

### 1.4 Substrate Comparison — Plastics vs. Steel

A core differentiator in this topic is the **fundamentally opposite nature** of the two substrates:

| Parameter | Plastic (Polymer) | Steel (Ferrous Alloy) |
|---|---|---|
| **Surface Energy** | Very low (**20–35 mN/m**) | Very high (**~ 70–250 mN/m** for clean metal) |
| **Primary Problem** | Inert, non-polar, poor wettability | Reactive, oxidized, contaminated, rough |
| **Treatment Goal** | *Increase* surface energy, introduce polar groups | *Decrease* reactivity, remove oxides, deposit barrier |
| **Typical Depth** | **0.01–1 μm** (top monolayers) | **1–100 μm** (conversion coatings) |
| **Chemical Nature** | Organic (C–C, C–H backbone) | Inorganic (Fe, FeO, Fe₃O₄) |
| **Primary Treatments** | Corona, plasma, flame, primers, etching | Degreasing, pickling, phosphating, chromating, blasting |

This dichotomy drives the rest of the notes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Theory — Surface Energy, Wettability, and Adhesion

### 2.1.1 Young's Equation (the foundational wettability model)

The behaviour of a liquid droplet (e.g., paint, ink, adhesive) on any solid surface is governed by **Young's Equation (1805)**, the cornerstone of surface science. A student in Plastics & Decoration must internalize this equation because it is repeatedly tested in KTU exams and applied across Modules 1–5.

For a liquid droplet at equilibrium on a flat, smooth, homogeneous, rigid solid surface, three interfacial tensions act on the contact line:

- $\gamma_{SG}$ — solid–gas interfacial tension (surface free energy of the solid)
- $\gamma_{SL}$ — solid–liquid interfacial tension
- $\gamma_{LG}$ — liquid–gas interfacial tension (surface tension of the liquid)

Balancing these horizontally gives **Young's Equation**:

$$\gamma_{SG} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG} \cos\theta$$

Where:
- $\theta$ = **contact angle** (measured through the liquid phase, in degrees)
- $\theta < 90°$ → liquid **wets** the surface (good for painting/printing)
- $\theta > 90°$ → liquid **does not wet** (beading, dewetting, poor adhesion)
- $\theta = 0°$ → **complete wetting** (ideal for adhesion/printing)

### 2.1.2 The Work of Adhesion (Dupré Equation)

Once a liquid wets a surface, the *strength* of the bond is quantified by the **Work of Adhesion ($W_a$)** — the reversible work required to separate the liquid from the solid at the interface. Dupré (1869) combined Young's equation with thermodynamic arguments:

$$W_a = \gamma_{SG} + \gamma_{LG} - \gamma_{SL}$$

Substituting Young's equation yields the **Young–Dupré Equation**:

$$W_a = \gamma_{LG} (1 + \cos\theta)$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** For a *given* paint ($\gamma_{LG}$ fixed), the **lower the contact angle $\theta$**, the **higher the work of adhesion $W_a$**, and the **stronger the decorative bond**.

### 2.1.3 Surface Energy Components (Fowkes / Owens–Wendt Theory)

Real surfaces are rarely purely dispersive. Total surface energy has components:

$$\gamma_{total} = \gamma^d + \gamma^p$$

Where:
- $\gamma^d$ = dispersive (London) component
- $\gamma^p$ = polar (Keesom + Debye + H-bonding) component

The **Owens–Wendt–Rabel–Kaelble (OWRK) Equation** relates contact angle to surface energy:

$$\gamma_{LG}(1 + \cos\theta) = 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_S^d \cdot \gamma_L^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_S^p \cdot \gamma_L^p}\right)$$

This is critical for plastics: PP and PE have $\gamma_S^p \approx 0$, so they are "invisible" to polar paints. Corona/plasma treatment **introduces polar –OH, –C=O, –COOH groups**, raising $\gamma_S^p$ from ~0 to **5–15 mN/m**, dramatically increasing $W_a$.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbols & Units | Where Applied in Plastics & Decoration |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\gamma_{SG} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG}\cos\theta$ | $\gamma$ in **N/m** or **mN/m**; $\theta$ in **°** | Wettability of paint/ink on plastic or steel |
| 2 | $W_a = \gamma_{LG}(1+\cos\theta)$ | $W_a$ in **mN/m** | Adhesion strength prediction |
| 3 | $\gamma_{total} = \gamma^d + \gamma^p$ | mN/m | Surface energy breakdown for polymer activation |
| 4 | $\text{Critical Surface Tension } \gamma_c$ | mN/m | Zisman plot (extrapolated $\cos\theta = 1$ intercept) |
| 5 | $R_a = \frac{1}{L}\int_0^L \vert z(x)\vert \,dx$ | **μm** | Average surface roughness (steel after blasting) |
| 6 | Phosphating Coating Weight: $W = \frac{m_2 - m_1}{A}$ | **g/m²** | Phosphating quality control on steel |
| 7 | Plating throwing power: $TP = \frac{T - M}{T + M - 2}\times 100\%$ | % | Electroplating uniformity on plastics (pre-etch) |
| 8 | Dyne Test (Solution): $\gamma_{test} \geq \gamma_c$ | mN/m | Quick QC for surface energy of treated film |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Practical Constants** (commit to memory for KTU):
> - Distilled water: $\gamma_{LG} = 72.8$ mN/m at 20°C
> - Glycerol: $\gamma_{LG} = 63.4$ mN/m
> - Formamide: $\gamma_{LG} = 58.2$ mN/m
> - Diiodomethane: $\gamma_{LG} = 50.8$ mN/m (purely dispersive)
> - Untreated PP: $\gamma_c \approx 31$ mN/m
> - Untreated PE: $\gamma_c \approx 30$ mN/m
> - Untreated PET: $\gamma_c \approx 43$ mN/m
> - Untreated PVC: $\gamma_c \approx 39$ mN/m
> - Corona-treated PP (typical): $\gamma_c \approx 40$–**46 mN/m**

### 2.3 Engineering Utility and Real-World Relevance

The above equations are not academic curiosities — they are the **decision-making engine** in any modern coatings lab:

1. **Pre-Treatment Validation in Industry:** A quality control (QC) inspector in a Mumbai-based automotive plastics plant receives a batch of injection-moulded PP bumpers. Before painting, she performs a **Dyne test** (ASTM D2578) using a series of calibrated dye solutions (30, 36, 38, 40, 44 dyne/cm). If the surface "wets out" at 38 dyne/cm but beads at 40 dyne/cm, the surface energy is between 38 and 40 mN/m — *insufficient* for the water-based polyurethane paint (requires ≥ **42 mN/m**). The batch is re-corona-treated.

2. **Selection of Pre-treatment for Steel:** A white goods manufacturer in Pune must decide between iron phosphating (cheaper, indoor use) and zinc phosphating (costlier, superior corrosion resistance for outdoor appliances). This is decided using the **Preece test (ASTM B117)** — salt spray hours.

3. **Failure Analysis:** A consumer goods company reports "paint peeling off ABS showerheads." The failure analysis lab measures contact angle with three probe liquids, applies the OWRK equation, and finds $\gamma_S^p = 0.2$ mN/m — clear evidence of **insufficient chromic–sulfuric acid etching** before electroless nickel plating (a Module 3 process).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Most students lose marks in Module 1 by stating that surface treatment "improves adhesion" without ever *quantifying* it. Always state the **dyne level, contact angle, or work of adhesion** you are targeting. Vague answers get only 1/3 marks in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Surface Treatment Methods at a Glance

The following master matrix is the single most important **revision table** for this topic. Every KTU question on Module 1 maps to one or more rows of this table.

| Substrate | Treatment Method | Process Type | Depth / Coating | Primary Function | Typical Process Parameters |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Plastic** | Corona Treatment | Electrical discharge | 0.01–0.1 μm | Raise surface energy | 10–50 kV, 1–50 kHz, web speed 10–300 m/min |
| **Plastic** | Plasma Treatment (low-pressure / atmospheric) | Ionized gas | 0.01–1 μm | Functionalize surface, add –OH, –COOH | 13.56 MHz RF, 10–500 W, vacuum 0.1–1 mbar |
| **Plastic** | Flame Treatment | Oxidizing flame | 0.1–1 μm | Oxidize to –OH, –C=O | Flame ratio 1:6 (gas:air), dwell 0.01–0.1 s |
| **Plastic** | Chemical Etching (CrO₃/H₂SO₄) | Wet chemical | 0.1–10 μm | Micro-roughen, introduce pits for plating | 60–70°C, 5–15 min, ABS only |
| **Plastic** | Solvent Wipe / Degrease | Physical | Surface film only | Remove release agent/oil | IPA, acetone, MEK wipe |
| **Plastic** | Primer / Adhesion Promoter | Coating | 1–10 μm | Bridge polymer and paint | Chlorinated polyolefin (CPO) on PP |
| **Steel** | Solvent/Alkaline Degreasing | Wet chemical | Film only | Remove oils | 60–80°C, 5–15 min, pH 10–13 |
| **Steel** | Pickling (H₂SO₄ / HCl) | Wet chemical | 1–10 μm | Remove mill scale, rust | 10–20% H₂SO₄, 50–70°C, 5–30 min |
| **Steel** | Mechanical Cleaning (Blasting) | Abrasive | Variable | Roughen, remove scale | Grit 60–120 mesh, 4–6 bar pressure |
| **Steel** | Zinc Phosphating | Conversion coating | 5–20 μm | Paint base, corrosion resistance | 30–50°C, 5–15 min, pH 2.5–3.5 |
| **Steel** | Iron Phosphating | Conversion coating | 0.5–2 μm | Indoor paint base | 40–60°C, 3–10 min |
| **Steel** | Chromating (Cr⁶⁺) | Conversion coating | 0.1–1 μm | Corrosion resistance, paint base | RT, 30–60 s, RoHS concern |
| **Steel** | Trivalent Chrome Passivation | Conversion coating | 0.1–1 μm | Eco-friendly Cr³⁺ alternative | RT, 30–60 s |

## 3.2 Derivation 1 — Coating Weight for Phosphating (Industry QC Test)

The **phosphating coating weight test (ASTM B767, B499)** is the standard QC metric for steel pre-treatment. It is also a frequently asked numerical problem in KTU exams. The student must be able to derive the formula and execute the calculation.

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define Coating Weight ($W$):**
The coating weight is the *mass of phosphate coating per unit area* of the steel substrate.

$$W = \frac{m_{\text{coating}}}{A_{substrate}}$$

**Step 2 — Decompose Mass Using the Stripping Method:**
The coated steel panel is weighed *before stripping* ($m_2$) and *after stripping* ($m_1$). The stripping solution (e.g., 5% ammonium dichromate at 70°C for zinc phosphate, or 50% HCl inhibited for iron phosphate) dissolves the coating **without attacking the steel substrate**.

Therefore:

$$m_{\text{coating}} = m_2 - m_1$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and Express in Standard Units:**

$$W = \frac{m_2 - m_1}{A}$$

Where:
- $m_2 - m_1$ is in **grams (g)**
- $A$ is the panel area in **m²**
- Resulting $W$ is in **g/m²** (the SI unit preferred by ASTM)

If panel dimensions are in cm², multiply by $10^4$ to convert to m².

**Step 4 — Numerical Example (Worked):**
A steel panel of dimensions **10 cm × 15 cm** is phosphated. The panel is weighed:
- After phosphating and drying: $m_2 = 145.823$ g
- After stripping the coating: $m_1 = 145.498$ g

Calculate the coating weight.

First, compute the panel area:
$$A = 10\,\text{cm} \times 15\,\text{cm} = 150\,\text{cm}^2 = 150 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^2 = 0.015\,\text{m}^2$$

Next, compute the mass difference:
$$m_2 - m_1 = 145.823 - 145.498 = 0.325\,\text{g}$$

Then, the coating weight:
$$W = \frac{0.325}{0.015} = 21.67\,\text{g/m}^2$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
For **zinc phosphating**, the typical industrial specification is **15–30 g/m²**. Our value of **21.67 g/m²** falls within the acceptable range — the bath chemistry is correct, immersion time and temperature are good. If $W$ were **< 5 g/m²**, the coating would be too thin to provide adequate corrosion resistance. If $W$ were **> 40 g/m²**, the coating would be powdery and chalk off.

## 3.3 Derivation 2 — Throwing Power in Electroplating of Plastics (Linked to Pre-etch)

When a plastic part (e.g., ABS faucet handle) is electroplated (Module 3), the quality of the **chromic–sulfuric acid etch** in Module 1 directly determines the uniformity of the metal layer. The industry metric is **Throwing Power (TP)**.

### 3.3.1 Definition

Throwing power is the ability of an electroplating bath to deposit metal **uniformly** over a complex 3D part (recesses, edges, cavities). It is calculated from a **Haring–Blum cell** experiment using two cathodes at distances $L_{near}$ and $L_{far}$ (typically $L_{near} = L_{far}/5 = $ 1:5 ratio).

**Primary Current Distribution Ratio ($M$):**
$$M = \frac{W_{near}}{W_{far}}$$

Where $W_{near}$ and $W_{far}$ are the metal masses (or thicknesses) deposited on the near and far cathodes.

**Throwing Power (Field's Formula):**
$$TP = \frac{K - M}{K + M - 2} \times 100\%$$

Where $K$ is the cell geometry ratio ($K = L_{far}/L_{near} = 5$ for a standard Haring–Blum cell).

### 3.3.2 Numerical Example (Worked)

A standard Haring–Blum cell test on a freshly prepared ABS plating line yields:
- $W_{near} = 0.285$ g (mass deposited on near cathode)
- $W_{far} = 0.180$ g (mass deposited on far cathode)
- $K = 5$

Calculate the throwing power.

**Step 1 — Compute $M$:**
$$M = \frac{W_{near}}{W_{far}} = \frac{0.285}{0.180} = 1.583$$

**Step 2 — Apply Field's Formula:**
$$TP = \frac{K - M}{K + M - 2} \times 100\%$$

Substituting:
$$TP = \frac{5 - 1.583}{5 + 1.583 - 2} \times 100\%$$

$$TP = \frac{3.417}{4.583} \times 100\%$$

$$TP = 74.56\%$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Interpretation:**
A throwing power of **~75%** is *excellent* for a Haring–Blum cell with $K=5$. A perfect bath gives 100%; an "ideal" non-polarized bath gives 0%. Values above **60%** indicate that the **etch in Module 1 has produced uniform micro-pits** (typical of $60\text{°C, 10 min, CrO}_3 400\text{ g/L, H}_2\text{SO}_4 400\text{ g/L}$) suitable for uniform Pd/Sn activation and subsequent Ni plating. If the etch were *insufficient*, $M$ would rise (because the far cathode would be barely activated) and $TP$ would fall below 30%, leading to "thin spots" or "skip plating" in production.

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Surface Energy from Contact Angle (Owens–Wendt Two-Liquid Method)

This is the *most common* numerical problem in KTU Module 1.

### 3.4.1 Setup

Two probe liquids of known $\gamma_L^d$ and $\gamma_L^p$ are used. The contact angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ are measured. We solve the OWRK equation for the two unknowns $\gamma_S^d$ and $\gamma_S^p$.

**OWRK Equation:**
$$\gamma_{LG}(1 + \cos\theta) = 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^d \gamma_L^d} + 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^p \gamma_L^p}$$

### 3.4.2 Numerical Example (Worked)

A corona-treated PP film is tested with two liquids at 20°C:

**Liquid 1: Distilled water**
- $\gamma_{L1} = 72.8$ mN/m
- $\gamma_{L1}^d = 21.8$ mN/m
- $\gamma_{L1}^p = 51.0$ mN/m
- Measured $\theta_1 = 65°$

**Liquid 2: Diiodomethane**
- $\gamma_{L2} = 50.8$ mN/m
- $\gamma_{L2}^d = 50.8$ mN/m (purely dispersive)
- $\gamma_{L2}^p = 0$ mN/m
- Measured $\theta_2 = 32°$

**Step 1 — Compute $(1 + \cos\theta)$:**
For water:
$$\cos 65° = 0.4226$$
$$1 + \cos\theta_1 = 1.4226$$
$$Y_1 = \gamma_{L1}(1 + \cos\theta_1) = 72.8 \times 1.4226 = 103.57\,\text{mN/m}$$

For diiodomethane:
$$\cos 32° = 0.8480$$
$$1 + \cos\theta_2 = 1.8480$$
$$Y_2 = \gamma_{L2}(1 + \cos\theta_2) = 50.8 \times 1.8480 = 93.88\,\text{mN/m}$$

**Step 2 — Write the OWRK Equation for Each Liquid:**

For water:
$$103.57 = 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^d \times 21.8} + 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^p \times 51.0}$$

For diiodomethane (with $\gamma_{L2}^p = 0$):
$$93.88 = 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^d \times 50.8} + 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve the Diiodomethane Equation for $\gamma_S^d$:**
$$93.88 = 2\sqrt{50.8 \cdot \gamma_S^d}$$

$$\sqrt{50.8 \cdot \gamma_S^d} = 46.94$$

$$50.8 \cdot \gamma_S^d = 2203.4$$

$$\gamma_S^d = 43.37\,\text{mN/m}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the Water Equation:**
$$103.57 = 2\sqrt{43.37 \times 21.8} + 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^p \times 51.0}$$

Compute the first term:
$$2\sqrt{43.37 \times 21.8} = 2\sqrt{945.5} = 2 \times 30.75 = 61.50$$

Therefore:
$$103.57 - 61.50 = 2\sqrt{51.0 \cdot \gamma_S^p}$$

$$42.07 = 2\sqrt{51.0 \cdot \gamma_S^p}$$

$$\sqrt{51.0 \cdot \gamma_S^p} = 21.04$$

$$51.0 \cdot \gamma_S^p = 442.4$$

$$\gamma_S^p = 8.67\,\text{mN/m}$$

**Step 5 — Total Surface Energy:**
$$\gamma_S = \gamma_S^d + \gamma_S^p = 43.37 + 8.67 = 52.04\,\text{mN/m}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
The corona treatment has raised the PP film from its virgin $\gamma_c \approx 31$ mN/m to **52 mN/m**, comfortably above the **42 mN/m** requirement of a typical water-based polyurethane ink. The treatment is *successful*. The polar component ($\gamma_S^p = 8.67$ mN/m) is non-zero, confirming that oxygen-containing functional groups (–OH, C=O) have been grafted onto the surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall #1 (very common error):** Students often forget that the OWRK equation has **two unknowns** ($\gamma_S^d$ and $\gamma_S^p$) and requires **two liquids** to solve. Using only one liquid is mathematically impossible — make sure your answer always references both probe liquids.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Surface Treatment Workflow in a Production Line

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **end-to-end surface treatment workflow** as it appears in a real industrial plant producing decorated plastic + steel products (e.g., a chromed ABS+steel faucet handle).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Plastic Part<br>e.g. Injection-moulded ABS] --> B[Solvent Degreasing<br>IPA wipe, 25C, 30 s]
    B --> C[Pre-Etch Bath<br>NaOH 50 g/L, 50C, 5 min]
    C --> D[Chromic-Sulfuric Etch<br>CrO3 400 g/L, H2SO4 400 g/L<br>60C, 10 min]
    D --> E[Neutralization<br>Na2SO3 20 g/L, 30C, 2 min]
    E --> F[Pre-Activation<br>HCl 100 mL/L, 30 s]
    F --> G[Activation<br>PdCl2-SnCl2 colloid, 30C, 3 min]
    G --> H[Electroless Nickel<br>Ni plating 8-12 min]
    H --> I[Strike Copper<br>acid Cu, 5 min]
    I --> J[Bright Acid Copper<br>20-30 min]
    J --> K[Bright Nickel<br>15-20 min]
    K --> L[Chromium<br>acid CrO3, 2-5 min]
    L --> M[Finished Decorated Part]

    N[Raw Steel Part<br>e.g. Mild Steel Cabinet] --> O[Alkaline Degreasing<br>pH 12, 70C, 10 min]
    O --> P[Water Rinse<br>overflow]
    P --> Q[Pickling<br>15% H2SO4, 60C, 15 min]
    Q --> R[Water Rinse<br>double cascade]
    R --> S[Surface Conditioning<br>colloidal Ti phosphate, RT, 1 min]
    S --> T[Zinc Phosphating<br>Zn 1.5 g/L, PO4 15 g/L<br>45C, 10 min]
    T --> U[Water Rinse]
    U --> V[Passivation<br>Cr-free rinse, RT, 30 s]
    V --> W[Drying Oven<br>120C, 10 min]
    W --> X[Electrostatic Powder Coat<br>60-80 kV, 15 s]
    X --> Y[Curing Oven<br>180C, 15 min]
    Y --> Z[Finished Painted Steel Part]

    M --> QC{Visual +<br>Adhesion +<br>Thickness QC}
    Z --> QC
    QC -->|Pass| Ship[Ship to Customer]
    QC -->|Fail| Reject[Rework or Reject]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Mechanism of Corona Treatment on Polymer Surface

The following diagram explains the *internal physics* of how corona discharge raises the surface energy of a polyolefin film. This is a frequent 7-mark or 14-mark KTU question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph HighVoltage[High-Voltage Electrode Assembly]
        HV[High-Frequency HV Generator<br>10-50 kV, 10-50 kHz]
    end

    HV --> Discharge[Corona Discharge Zone<br>air ionized to plasma]
    Discharge --> Energy[Electron Bombardment<br>Energy 1-10 eV]
    Energy --> Chain[Chain Scission of C-C<br>and C-H Bonds]
    Chain --> Radicals[Free Radical Formation<br>R-CH2-CH2-R*]
    Radicals --> O2[Reaction with O2<br>and O3 from discharge]
    O2 --> Func[Functional Groups Formed<br>-OH, -COOH, -C=O, peroxide]
    Func --> Surface[Treated Film Surface<br>gamma_S raised 31 to 42 mN/m]
    Surface --> Roll[Treated Film Rewound<br>downstream decoration]

    subgraph Properties[Resulting Surface Properties]
        P1[Increased wettability]
        P2[Improved ink adhesion]
        P3[Better print quality]
    end

    Surface --> Properties
```

## 4.3 Process Flow Comparison — Decision Logic for Choosing a Treatment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Substrate Arrives] --> Q1{Is substrate<br>plastic or steel?}
    Q1 -->|Plastic| Q2{Goal of<br>decoration?}
    Q1 -->|Steel| Q3{Operating<br>environment?}

    Q2 -->|Print/Coat on flat film| P1[Use Corona Treatment]
    Q2 -->|3D part, high durability| P2[Use Plasma or Flame]
    Q2 -->|Electroplating needed| P3[Use Chromic-Sulfuric Etch]
    Q2 -->|Paint, simple| P4[Use Primer e.g. CPO on PP]

    Q3 -->|Indoor, low corrosion| S1[Use Iron Phosphating]
    Q3 -->|Outdoor, high corrosion| S2[Use Zinc Phosphating]
    Q3 -->|Severe environment| S3[Use Zinc Phosphating + Chromate]
    Q3 -->|Just cleaning, no paint| S4[Use Pickling + Passivation]
```

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Diagram Questions:** Notice how each diagram uses *purely uppercase or alphanumeric* node IDs (e.g., `HighVoltage`, `P1`, `S4`) and *clean, unformatted labels*. This is the professional style expected in the answer booklet. Mermaid diagrams like the above can be drawn on a plain A4 sheet and converted to a clean hand-drawn block diagram for the exam.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Q1]**

> **"Define surface energy. Why is it critical in plastics decoration? Give the surface energy values of untreated PP and corona-treated PP."**

**Model Answer (Word-Count-Optimized for 3 Marks):**

Surface energy ($\gamma_S$) is the excess free energy per unit area at the surface of a material, measured in **mN/m**. It quantifies the ability of a solid to attract a liquid (paint/ink) and form a bonded interface. For plastics decoration, surface energy is critical because **adhesion, wettability, and printability** all depend on it. A paint or ink with $\gamma_L = 42$ mN/m will only wet a surface whose $\gamma_S \geq 42$ mN/m.

- Untreated **PP**: $\gamma_S \approx 31$ mN/m → ink beads, **poor adhesion**.
- Corona-treated **PP**: $\gamma_S \approx 42$–$46$ mN/m → ink spreads, **good adhesion**.

Thus, raising $\gamma_S$ via corona, plasma, or flame treatment is the **first and most critical step** in plastics decoration.

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark each for definition, significance, and the two numerical values.)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Expected Pattern]**

> **"List any three differences between surface treatment of plastics and surface treatment of steel."**

**Model Answer (Tabular Form, 3 Marks):**

| # | Plastics | Steel |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Low surface energy (**20–35 mN/m**); treatment **increases** it | High surface energy; treatment **cleans and protects** it |
| 2 | Methods: corona, plasma, flame, chemical etch | Methods: degreasing, pickling, phosphating, chromating |
| 3 | Treatment depth: **0.01–1 μm** (top monolayers) | Treatment depth: **1–100 μm** (conversion coatings) |
| 4 | Goal: introduce polar groups (–OH, C=O) | Goal: remove mill scale, deposit phosphate/chromate film |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Pitfall 1 (–2 marks):** Writing vague statements like "surface treatment improves adhesion" without **quantifying** the improvement (dyne level, contact angle, or work of adhesion). Always give numbers.
> - **Pitfall 2 (–2 marks):** Confusing "surface tension" of a *liquid* with "surface energy" of a *solid*. They are related but not identical.
> - **Pitfall 3 (–3 marks):** In numerical problems, forgetting to convert **cm² → m²** by multiplying by $10^{-4}$ (or area in m² directly). This is the single most common calculation error.
> - **Pitfall 4 (–2 marks):** Not drawing a **labelled block diagram** in 14-mark questions where the question says "explain with a neat diagram." A diagram is worth **2–3 marks minimum**.
> - **Pitfall 5 (–2 marks):** Mentioning "phosphating" without specifying **iron vs. zinc** and their operating conditions/differences.

---

### Question Choice A — Q1 (A) (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1 Pattern]**

> **(a)** Explain the **corona treatment process** for plastic films with a neat block diagram. Discuss the underlying mechanism by which the surface energy of polymers is increased. State the typical process parameters. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A **PP film** is to be printed with a water-based ink. The ink manufacturer specifies a **minimum surface energy of 42 mN/m** for proper adhesion. The contact angles measured on the corona-treated PP film are **65°** with distilled water ($\gamma_L = 72.8$ mN/m, $\gamma_L^d = 21.8$, $\gamma_L^p = 51.0$) and **32°** with diiodomethane ($\gamma_L = 50.8$ mN/m, $\gamma_L^d = 50.8$, $\gamma_L^p = 0$). Calculate the surface energy of the treated film using the **Owens–Wendt method** and state whether the film is **acceptable** for printing. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Q1 (A) (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Definition of Corona Treatment (1 mark):**
Corona treatment is a **non-equilibrium, atmospheric-pressure electrical discharge process** in which a high-frequency, high-voltage electrical field ionizes the air gap between an electrode and a grounded metal roller, over which the polymer film passes. The ionized air ("corona") bombards the polymer surface, raising its surface energy by 10–15 mN/m.

**2. Equipment / Block Diagram (2 marks):**
The setup consists of:
- An **electrode** (typically a stainless steel or aluminium bar) carrying the HV.
- A **grounded dielectric roller** (covered with silicone rubber or ceramic) supporting the film.
- A **high-frequency generator** (10–50 kV, 10–50 kHz).
- An **ozone extraction hood** (corona generates toxic O₃).

*(Draw the labelled block diagram from Section 4.2 of the notes.)*

**3. Process Parameters (2 marks):**
- Voltage: **10–50 kV**
- Frequency: **10–50 kHz**
- Web speed: **10–300 m/min**
- Electrode gap: **1–3 mm**
- Treatment level (W·min/m²): **controlled by speed and power**

**4. Mechanism — Why It Works (2 marks):**
The corona discharge produces:
- **Electrons of energy 1–10 eV** (greater than C–C and C–H bond energies of ~3.5 eV and ~4.3 eV respectively).
- These electrons **scission** the polymer backbone, generating **free radicals** (R•).
- The radicals rapidly **react with nascent oxygen, ozone, and water vapour** in the discharge.
- New **polar functional groups** (–OH, –C=O, –COOH, –OOH) are grafted onto the surface.
- The **dispersive component** $\gamma_S^d$ remains nearly unchanged, but the **polar component** $\gamma_S^p$ rises from ~0 to **5–15 mN/m**.
- Net effect: total $\gamma_S$ rises from ~31 mN/m to ~42–46 mN/m.

**5. Effect on Ink Adhesion:**
The increased polar component enables **hydrogen bonding and dipole–dipole interactions** with water-based (polar) inks, dramatically increasing the **Work of Adhesion $W_a$** as per the Young–Dupré equation.

---

### Model Solution for Q1 (A) (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Compute $Y = \gamma_L(1 + \cos\theta)$ for each liquid.** (1 mark)

For water:
$$\cos 65° = 0.4226$$
$$Y_1 = 72.8 \times (1 + 0.4226) = 72.8 \times 1.4226 = 103.57\,\text{mN/m}$$

For diiodomethane:
$$\cos 32° = 0.8480$$
$$Y_2 = 50.8 \times (1 + 0.8480) = 50.8 \times 1.8480 = 93.88\,\text{mN/m}$$

**Step 2 — Write OWRK equations.** (1 mark)

$$103.57 = 2\sqrt{21.8\,\gamma_S^d} + 2\sqrt{51.0\,\gamma_S^p} \quad \cdots (1)$$

$$93.88 = 2\sqrt{50.8\,\gamma_S^d} + 0 \quad \cdots (2)$$

**Step 3 — Solve equation (2) for $\gamma_S^d$.** (1 mark)

$$\sqrt{50.8\,\gamma_S^d} = 46.94$$
$$50.8\,\gamma_S^d = 2203.4$$
$$\boxed{\gamma_S^d = 43.37\,\text{mN/m}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into equation (1) and solve for $\gamma_S^p$.** (2 marks)

First term:
$$2\sqrt{21.8 \times 43.37} = 2\sqrt{945.5} = 2 \times 30.75 = 61.50$$

Therefore:
$$103.57 - 61.50 = 2\sqrt{51.0\,\gamma_S^p}$$
$$42.07 = 2\sqrt{51.0\,\gamma_S^p}$$
$$\sqrt{51.0\,\gamma_S^p} = 21.04$$
$$51.0\,\gamma_S^p = 442.4$$
$$\boxed{\gamma_S^p = 8.67\,\text{mN/m}}$$

**Step 5 — Total Surface Energy and Decision.** (2 marks)

$$\gamma_S = \gamma_S^d + \gamma_S^p = 43.37 + 8.67 = 52.04\,\text{mN/m}$$

**Decision:** Since **52.04 mN/m > 42 mN/m** (the ink manufacturer's minimum), the corona-treated PP film is **ACCEPTABLE** for printing. The polar component of 8.67 mN/m confirms successful surface activation.

*(2 marks: 1 for total, 1 for decision and engineering interpretation.)*

---

### Question Choice B — Q1 (B) (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Alternative Pattern]**

> **(a)** Explain the **phosphating process for steel** with a neat block diagram. Differentiate between **zinc phosphating and iron phosphating** in terms of chemistry, coating weight, and applications. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A steel panel of dimensions **15 cm × 20 cm** is zinc-phosphated. After drying, the panel weighs **185.425 g**. The phosphate coating is then stripped, and the bare panel weighs **184.880 g**. Calculate the **coating weight** in g/m² and state whether it falls within the **industrial specification for zinc phosphating (15–30 g/m²)**. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Q1 (B) (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Definition and Chemistry of Phosphating (2 marks):**
Phosphating is a **chemical conversion coating** process in which a dilute acidic phosphate solution reacts with the iron surface to form an **insoluble, adherent, crystalline layer of metal phosphates** integral to the substrate.

**General Reaction (for zinc phosphating):**
$$3\,\text{Zn}^{2+} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4^- + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Zn}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2\cdot 4\text{H}_2\text{O}_{\text{(hopeite)}} + 4\,\text{H}^+$$

Simultaneously, the iron substrate participates:
$$\text{Fe} + 2\,\text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\uparrow$$
The liberated H₂ bubbles help dislodge loose scale and indicate active reaction.

**2. Process Steps (with Diagram) (2 marks):** *(Use the diagram from Section 4.1, lower branch.)*

1. **Alkaline Degreasing** (pH 12, 70°C, 10 min) — removes oils.
2. **Water Rinse** — removes residual alkali.
3. **Pickling** (15% H₂SO₄, 60°C, 15 min) — removes mill scale and rust.
4. **Water Rinse** — double cascade.
5. **Surface Conditioning** (colloidal titanium phosphate, RT, 1 min) — seeds fine crystal formation.
6. **Zinc Phosphating** (Zn²⁺ 1.5 g/L, PO₄³⁻ 15 g/L, NO₂⁻ accelerator, 45°C, 10 min).
7. **Water Rinse** — removes unreacted acid.
8. **Passivation / DI Rinse** (Cr-free or chromate, RT, 30 s).
9. **Drying** (120°C, 10 min) before painting.

**3. Zinc vs. Iron Phosphating — Comparison (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Zinc Phosphating | Iron Phosphating |
|---|---|---|
| **Chemistry** | Zn₃(PO₄)₂·4H₂O (hopeite) | Fe₃(PO₄)₂·8H₂O (vivianite) + Fe₂O₃ |
| **Coating weight** | **15–30 g/m²** (heavy) | **0.5–2 g/m²** (light) |
| **Colour** | Grey to dark grey | Blue/iridescent to grey |
| **Crystal structure** | Crystalline, plate/needle | Amorphous to fine crystalline |
| **Corrosion resistance** | **Excellent** (≥ 1000 h salt spray) | Moderate (≤ 250 h salt spray) |
| **Paint adhesion** | Very high | Good (sufficient for indoor) |
| **Application** | Automotive bodies, outdoor furniture, white goods | Indoor appliances, electrical cabinets, low-corrosion environments |
| **Cost** | Higher (Zn²⁺, accelerator, heating) | Lower (simpler bath, room-temp variants) |

---

### Model Solution for Q1 (B) (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Compute Panel Area.** (1 mark)

$$A = 15\,\text{cm} \times 20\,\text{cm} = 300\,\text{cm}^2 = 300 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^2 = 0.03\,\text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute Mass Difference (= Coating Mass).** (1 mark)

$$m_2 - m_1 = 185.425 - 184.880 = 0.545\,\text{g}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Coating Weight Formula.** (2 marks)

$$W = \frac{m_2 - m_1}{A} = \frac{0.545}{0.03}$$

$$\boxed{W = 18.17\,\text{g/m}^2}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation and Acceptance Decision.** (2 marks)

The industrial specification for **zinc phosphating** is **15–30 g/m²**. Our value of **18.17 g/m²** lies **within this range**.

**Verdict: ACCEPT.** The phosphating bath is operating correctly:
- Temperature and concentration are appropriate.
- Immersion time is sufficient.
- The coating will provide the required paint adhesion and corrosion resistance for downstream powder coating or liquid painting.

**Step 5 — What-if Scenarios (KTU Favourite).** (1 mark)

- If $W$ were **< 5 g/m²**: Bath is under-activated, time too short, or accelerator depleted — coating will be powdery and not protect the steel.
- If $W$ were **> 40 g/m²**: Bath is over-concentrated, time too long, or temperature too high — coating will be chalky, powdery, and will not bond well to paint (it will flake off with the paint film).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

This is a **rapid-revision checklist** for the night before the exam. Read it three times.

### 5.3.1 Conceptual Checklist
- [ ] Surface treatment is the **enabler** of all downstream decoration (paint, print, plate, metallize).
- [ ] Plastics: **low $\gamma_S$ (problem)** → **high $\gamma_S$ (goal)**.
- [ ] Steel: **dirty, oxidized (problem)** → **clean, coated (goal)**.
- [ ] Young's Equation: $\gamma_{SG} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG}\cos\theta$.
- [ ] Work of Adhesion: $W_a = \gamma_{LG}(1+\cos\theta)$ — **lower $\theta$ = higher $W_a$**.
- [ ] Corona, plasma, flame, chemical etch, primer — **five weapons** for plastics.
- [ ] Degrease, pickle, phosphate, chromate, blast — **five weapons** for steel.
- [ ] Zinc phosphating for outdoor; iron phosphating for indoor.
- [ ] Coating weight formula: $W = (m_2 - m_1)/A$.
- [ ] OWRK equation requires **two probe liquids** to solve for $\gamma_S^d$ and $\gamma_S^p$.

### 5.3.2 Numerical Must-Knows
- [ ] Convert cm² → m² by multiplying by $10^{-4}$.
- [ ] Standard probe liquid surface energies: water = 72.8, glycerol = 63.4, formamide = 58.2, diiodomethane = 50.8 mN/m.
- [ ] Industrial dyne level for water-based paint adhesion: **≥ 40–42 dyne/cm**.
- [ ] Zinc phosphate coating weight: **15–30 g/m²**.
- [ ] Iron phosphate coating weight: **0.5–2 g/m²**.
- [ ] Standard chromic-sulfuric etch for ABS: **CrO₃ 400 g/L, H₂SO₄ 400 g/L, 60–70°C, 10 min**.

### 5.3.3 Common Vocabulary Mastery
- [ ] **Surface energy** ($\gamma_S$, mN/m) — solid property.
- [ ] **Surface tension** ($\gamma_L$, mN/m) — liquid property.
- [ ] **Contact angle** ($\theta$, degrees) — wettability metric.
- [ ] **Work of adhesion** ($W_a$, mN/m) — bond strength metric.
- [ ] **Dyne level** — colloquial industry term for $\gamma_S$ of films.
- [ ] **Conversion coating** — a coating *chemically grown from* the substrate (phosphate, chromate, oxide).
- [ ] **Conversion coating vs. deposited coating** — phosphate grows *from* the steel; paint is *deposited on* the steel.
- [ ] **Hopeite** = zinc phosphate mineral; **Vivianite** = iron phosphate mineral.

### 5.3.4 KTU Exam-Day Pearls
- [ ] Always draw a **labelled block diagram** in 14-mark questions (worth 2–3 marks minimum).
- [ ] Always **state numerical values** (dyne level, contact angle, coating weight) — never write vague qualitative claims.
- [ ] Always **cite the formula** (Young's, OWRK, Field's) before plugging in numbers.
- [ ] For plastics questions, mention **surface energy** and **critical surface tension**.
- [ ] For steel questions, mention **cleanliness** and **conversion coating weight**.
- [ ] End every long answer with an **engineering interpretation** sentence — "Therefore, the batch is acceptable / unacceptable because …" — this guarantees the application-level marks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Revision Tip:** The single most important takeaway from Module 1 is the **dyadic nature of the field** — *plastics need activation, steel needs protection.* If a student internalizes only this sentence and the OWRK equation, they can answer 60% of KTU Module 1 questions correctly. The remaining 40% comes from numerical fluency (coating weight, throwing power, surface energy calculation).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermodynamics of Wetting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT864 - PLASTICS & DECORATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermodynamics of Wetting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Thermodynamics of Wetting** is the branch of surface science that quantitatively describes the energy interactions and equilibrium conditions at a three-phase contact line where a liquid, a solid substrate, and a surrounding fluid (typically air) meet. It establishes the fundamental thermodynamic criteria that govern whether a liquid will spread over, partially wet, or be completely repelled by a solid surface.

In the context of plastics and decoration (adhesion, coating, printing, painting, and lamination), the thermodynamics of wetting is the *primary gatekeeper* that determines whether a molten polymer, an ink droplet, an adhesive, or a decorative coating will properly intimate with a plastic substrate. It is governed by the minimization of the total free energy of the system, as dictated by the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The thermodynamics of wetting forms the *theoretical foundation* for all subsequent decoration technologies, including surface pretreatment, corona/ plasma treatment, primer selection, and adhesion promotion. A weak grasp of this topic will directly impair understanding of printing, coating, and laminating behavior in Modules 2, 3, and 4.

## 1.2 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|------|--------|------------------|
| Surface Free Energy | $\gamma$ | Excess Gibbs free energy per unit area at a surface (J/m² or mN/m) |
| Interfacial Tension | $\gamma_{SL}, \gamma_{LV}, \gamma_{SV}$ | Energy per unit area of the solid–liquid, liquid–vapor, and solid–vapor interfaces |
| Contact Angle | $\theta$ | Angle formed between the liquid–vapor interface and the solid surface at the three-phase line |
| Work of Adhesion | $W_A$ | Reversible work required to separate a unit area of liquid from a solid |
| Work of Cohesion | $W_C$ | Reversible work required to separate a unit area of liquid into two bulk liquid surfaces |
| Spreading Coefficient | $S$ | Net free energy change when a liquid spreads over a solid |
| Critical Surface Tension | $\gamma_C$ | Empirical value characterizing the wettability of a solid surface |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine pouring a small drop of **water** onto three different surfaces:

1. **A clean glass slide** → the water spreads out completely into a thin film ($\theta \approx 0°$). The water "loves" the glass.
2. **A waxed car body** → the water beads up into a near-perfect sphere ($\theta \approx 110°$). The water "hates" the wax.
3. **A polyethylene plastic film** → the water sits as a hemispherical drop ($\theta \approx 94°$). Moderate repulsion.

The *thermodynamics of wetting* is precisely the mathematical machinery that predicts *which one* of these three behaviors will occur, *and how strongly*, based purely on the energies of the interfaces involved. Each drop finds the shape that **minimizes the total surface free energy** of the system — nature always seeks the lowest energy state.

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Minimization Principle (The "Why" of Wetting):**
> A liquid droplet on a solid adopts its equilibrium contact angle $\theta$ such that the total Gibbs free energy of the system $G = \sum \gamma_i A_i$ is at its minimum. The three interfaces ($SL$, $LV$, $SV$) rearrange their contact area to satisfy this condition.

## 1.4 Significance in Plastics & Decoration Engineering

In decorative plastics manufacturing — be it **gravure printing on BOPP film**, **flexographic ink transfer on LDPE**, **hot-melt adhesive bonding**, or **vacuum metallization on PET** — the wetting thermodynamics dictates:

- **Ink transfer efficiency** and dot fidelity.
- **Adhesive bond-line integrity** and peel strength.
- **Coating uniformity** and defect formation (e.g., dewetting, pinholes, reticulation).
- **Primer/substrate compatibility** selection.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** Whenever a question asks "Why does ink not adhere to a polyolefin film?" — the answer *always* traces back to the thermodynamics of wetting: the polyolefin has a low surface free energy ($\gamma_S \approx 31$ mN/m for PE) and is unable to satisfy Young's equation with the high-surface-tension ink.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Phase Contact Line — The Wetting Stage

At the heart of wetting thermodynamics lies the **three-phase contact line** (also called the *triple line* or *wetting line*). This is a one-dimensional boundary at the perimeter of a liquid drop where three phases coexist:

- The **solid substrate** (S) — the plastic film or molded part.
- The **liquid drop** (L) — molten polymer, ink, adhesive, or paint.
- The **vapor phase** (V) — usually ambient air, sometimes a controlled atmosphere.

Three interfacial tensions act at this line, each trying to "pull" the contact line toward itself, much like three tug-of-war ropes attached to a single point:

1. $\gamma_{SV}$ — pulls the contact line *along* the solid surface (toward un-wetted solid).
2. $\gamma_{SL}$ — pulls the contact line *toward* the bulk liquid (opposes spreading).
3. $\gamma_{LV}$ — pulls the contact line *tangent* to the liquid surface (acts along the liquid meniscus).

The liquid drop settles at an **equilibrium contact angle** $\theta$ (measured through the liquid) where the vector sum of the horizontal components of these three forces equals zero.

## 2.2 Young's Equation — The Master Equation of Wetting

**Thomas Young (1805)** proposed the foundational force balance at the contact line. For an ideal, smooth, homogeneous, rigid, and non-reactive solid, equilibrium requires:

$$\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this equation is foundational in KTU examinations:**
> Every subsequent equation in wetting thermodynamics (work of adhesion, spreading coefficient, Young–Dupré) is either a direct rearrangement of Young's equation or a thermodynamic consequence of it. Memorize this equation with its three symbols *by reflex*.

**Physical Interpretation of Each Term:**

- $\gamma_{SV}$: Energy gained by the solid when the vapor adsorbs onto its surface. The "driving force" for spreading.
- $\gamma_{SL}$: Energy cost of creating the solid–liquid interface (replaces the solid–vapor interface). Always opposes wetting.
- $\gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$: Horizontal component of the liquid–vapor tension that balances the system.

**Spreading Conditions Derived from Young's Equation:**

- **Complete wetting**: $\theta = 0°$ $\Rightarrow$ $\gamma_{SV} \geq \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}$
- **Partial wetting**: $0° < \theta < 180°$
- **Non-wetting**: $\theta \geq 90°$ (poor adhesion expected)
- **Complete non-wetting**: $\theta = 180°$ (theoretically possible, never observed practically)

## 2.3 Work of Adhesion ($W_A$) — Dupré's Equation

The **work of adhesion** is the reversible thermodynamic work required to separate a unit area of the solid–liquid interface into two separate unit areas of solid–vapor and liquid–vapor interfaces:

$$W_A = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL}$$

This is **Dupré's equation (1869)** and represents the thermodynamic *strength* of the solid–liquid bond.

## 2.4 The Young–Dupré Equation

Combining Young's equation with Dupré's equation by eliminating $\gamma_{SL}$, we obtain the most practically used wetting equation:

$$W_A = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Equation:** The Young–Dupré equation is the *direct link* between a measurable quantity (the contact angle $\theta$ and the liquid's surface tension $\gamma_{LV}$) and a thermodynamic quantity (the work of adhesion $W_A$). Examiners frequently ask numerical problems based on this.

## 2.5 Work of Cohesion ($W_C$)

The **work of cohesion** is the reversible work required to separate a unit area of bulk liquid into two new liquid–vapor surfaces:

$$W_C = 2\gamma_{LV}$$

This represents the *internal cohesive strength* of the liquid itself. It is used as a reference against $W_A$ to predict spreading behavior.

## 2.6 Spreading Coefficient ($S$) — Harkins' Criterion

The **spreading coefficient** quantifies the net free energy change when a liquid spreads over a solid substrate (replacing a solid–vapor interface with a solid–liquid plus a liquid–vapor interface):

$$S = W_A - W_C = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LV}$$

Equivalently, using Dupré's rearrangement:

$$S = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL} - 2\gamma_{LV} = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LV}$$

**Spreading Criteria:**

- $S \geq 0$ $\Rightarrow$ **Complete spreading** (wetting film forms spontaneously).
- $S < 0$ $\Rightarrow$ **Partial wetting** (drop forms with $\theta > 0°$).
- $S = \gamma_{LV}(\cos\theta - 1) \Rightarrow$ always $\leq 0$, consistent with $\theta \geq 0°$.

## 2.7 Critical Surface Tension of Wetting ($\gamma_C$)

**Zisman (1964)** empirically observed that $\cos\theta$ for a homologous series of liquids on a given solid is approximately a linear function of $\gamma_{LV}$. Extrapolating to $\cos\theta = 1$ ($\theta = 0°$) defines the **critical surface tension** $\gamma_C$:

$$\cos\theta = 1 - \frac{2}{m}(\gamma_{LV} - \gamma_C)$$

where $m$ is a constant. The critical surface tension is *not* the surface free energy of the solid, but a closely related empirical parameter. For a liquid with $\gamma_{LV} \leq \gamma_C$, complete spreading occurs on the solid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical $\gamma_C$ values for plastics:**
> - PTFE (Teflon): $\gamma_C \approx 18.5$ mN/m (very low — hard to wet)
> - Polyethylene: $\gamma_C \approx 31$ mN/m
> - Polypropylene: $\gamma_C \approx 32$ mN/m
> - PET: $\gamma_C \approx 43$ mN/m
> - PVC: $\gamma_C \approx 39$ mN/m
> - PMMA: $\gamma_C \approx 39$ mN/m
> - Corona-treated PE: $\gamma_C$ rises to $44$–$50$ mN/m

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Form | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|----------|------|------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Young's Equation | $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$ | All $\gamma$ in mN/m; $\theta$ in degrees | Force balance at contact line |
| 2 | Dupré's Equation | $W_A = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL}$ | $W_A$ in mJ/m² | Thermodynamic work of adhesion |
| 3 | Young–Dupré Equation | $W_A = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$ | $W_A$ in mJ/m² | Direct experimental determination of $W_A$ from $\theta$ |
| 4 | Work of Cohesion | $W_C = 2\gamma_{LV}$ | $W_C$ in mJ/m² | Internal strength of liquid |
| 5 | Spreading Coefficient | $S = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LV}$ | $S$ in mJ/m² | Predicts complete vs. partial spreading |
| 6 | Spreading Criterion (Alt.) | $S = W_A - W_C$ | $S$ in mJ/m² | Quick check: $S>0$ means spreading |
| 7 | Zisman Relation | $\cos\theta = 1 - k(\gamma_{LV} - \gamma_C)$ | $\gamma_C$ in mN/m; $k$ constant | Empirical wettability ranking of solids |
| 8 | Young–Laplace Capillary Rise | $\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{r}$ | $\Delta P$ in Pa; $r$ in m | Capillary penetration in porous substrates |

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

The thermodynamic framework above is the *decision tree* used by plastics decoration engineers in the following practical scenarios:

1. **Ink Formulation**: Selecting a vehicle/binder system with $\gamma_{LV}$ low enough that $\gamma_{LV} < \gamma_C$ of the substrate (after any surface treatment).
2. **Adhesive Selection**: Choosing a hot-melt or solvent-based adhesive with $W_A$ greater than the cohesive strength of the weaker adherend.
3. **Surface Pretreatment Verification**: Confirming that corona/plasma treatment has raised $\gamma_C$ of the polyolefin to a level exceeding the coating's $\gamma_{LV}$.
4. **Process Troubleshooting**: Diagnosing "fish-eyes," "cratering," or "dewetting" defects by evaluating spreading coefficient signs at the operating temperature.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Young's Equation from First-Principles Energy Minimization

The most rigorous derivation of Young's equation begins with the free energy functional of the three-phase system and applies the variational principle.

### Step 1: Define the System Free Energy

Consider a small sessile liquid drop resting on a flat, smooth, homogeneous, non-deformable solid surface in vapor. The total Gibbs free energy of the system is the sum of the energies of all interfaces present:

$$G_{total} = \gamma_{SL} A_{SL} + \gamma_{LV} A_{LV} + \gamma_{SV} A_{SV}$$

where $A_{SL}$, $A_{LV}$, and $A_{SV}$ are the contact areas of the solid–liquid, liquid–vapor, and solid–vapor interfaces respectively.

### Step 2: Apply Geometric Constraint

For a fixed drop volume $V$ on a horizontal surface, the sum of the solid–liquid and solid–vapor contact areas equals the total wetted footprint area on the solid:

$$A_{SL} + A_{SV} = A_{Solid, total} = \text{constant} = A_{T}$$

Therefore, $dA_{SV} = -dA_{SL}$.

### Step 3: Take the Differential of the Free Energy

Differentiating the total free energy and using the constraint:

$$dG = \gamma_{SL} \, dA_{SL} + \gamma_{LV} \, dA_{LV} + \gamma_{SV} \, dA_{SV}$$

$$dG = \gamma_{SL} \, dA_{SL} + \gamma_{LV} \, dA_{LV} - \gamma_{SV} \, dA_{SL}$$

$$dG = (\gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{SV}) \, dA_{SL} + \gamma_{LV} \, dA_{LV}$$

### Step 4: Relate the Differential Areas to Contact-Angle Displacement

For a small virtual displacement of the contact line by an infinitesimal distance $\delta x$ along the solid surface (per unit length of the contact line), the geometrical changes are:

- $dA_{SL} = +\delta x$ (wetted area increases by $\delta x$ per unit length of contact line)
- $dA_{LV} = +\delta x \cos\theta$ (the liquid–vapor interface area increases by the projection of $\delta x$ onto the tangent of the meniscus)

### Step 5: Apply the Equilibrium Condition

At equilibrium, the free energy is at its minimum, so the first-order variation must vanish ($dG = 0$):

$$0 = (\gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{SV})(\delta x) + \gamma_{LV}(\delta x \cos\theta)$$

Dividing throughout by $\delta x$ (non-zero, since it represents a real displacement):

$$0 = \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$$

### Step 6: Rearrange to Obtain Young's Equation

Rearranging the equilibrium condition:

$$\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}$$

This is the rigorous thermodynamic form of Young's equation. The sign convention places $\theta$ *inside* the liquid phase.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Young–Dupré Equation

The Young–Dupré equation is obtained by combining Young's equation with Dupré's definition of the work of adhesion.

### Step 1: State Dupré's Equation

By definition, the work of adhesion between a solid and a liquid in a vapor environment is:

$$W_A = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL}$$

### Step 2: Substitute Young's Equation for $\gamma_{SL}$

From Young's equation, the solid–liquid interfacial tension is:

$$\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$$

Substituting this into Dupré's equation:

$$W_A = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - (\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta)$$

### Step 3: Simplify

The $\gamma_{SV}$ terms cancel:

$$W_A = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$$

$$W_A = \gamma_{LV} + \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$$

### Step 4: Factor the Result

$$\boxed{W_A = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)}$$

This is the **Young–Dupré equation (also called the Young–Dupré–Bangham equation)**. It is the *single most important* equation in wetting thermodynamics for experimentalists, because both $\gamma_{LV}$ and $\theta$ are directly measurable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Insight:** For a liquid that completely wets a solid ($\theta = 0°$), $W_A = 2\gamma_{LV} = W_C$. This is the *maximum possible* work of adhesion — the liquid–solid bond is as strong as the liquid's own cohesive bond. For $\theta = 90°$, $W_A = \gamma_{LV}$ (half of the cohesive work). For $\theta = 180°$ (theoretical), $W_A = 0$ (no adhesion).

## 3.3 Derivation of the Spreading Coefficient

### Step 1: Define the Initial and Final States

Consider unit area of a solid–vapor interface being replaced by a solid–liquid interface plus a new liquid–vapor interface (i.e., a thin liquid film is deposited on the solid).

- **Initial state**: solid–vapor interface of unit area, energy $= \gamma_{SV}$
- **Final state**: solid–liquid interface + liquid–vapor interface, energy $= \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}$

### Step 2: Compute the Free Energy Change

The free energy change of the system per unit area is:

$$\Delta G = (\gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}) - \gamma_{SV}$$

### Step 3: Define the Spreading Coefficient

By definition, the spreading coefficient $S$ is the *negative* of the free energy change (positive when spreading is thermodynamically favorable):

$$S = -\Delta G = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LV}$$

$$\boxed{S = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LV}}$$

### Step 4: Express $S$ in Terms of the Contact Angle

Substituting Young's equation $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$:

$$S = (\gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta) - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LV}$$

$$S = \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta - \gamma_{LV}$$

$$\boxed{S = \gamma_{LV}(\cos\theta - 1)}$$

Since $\cos\theta \leq 1$ for any real $\theta$, we always have $S \leq 0$ for a partial wetting situation. For *complete spreading*, $\cos\theta = 1$ and $S = 0$; the liquid continues to spread as a film.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Exam Pattern)

**Problem:** A droplet of a flexographic ink vehicle has a surface tension $\gamma_{LV} = 28$ mN/m. When placed on an untreated BOPP film, the equilibrium contact angle is measured as $\theta = 78°$. Calculate:

(a) The work of adhesion between the ink and the BOPP film.
(b) The work of cohesion of the ink vehicle.
(c) The spreading coefficient of the ink on BOPP.
(d) Comment on whether the ink will spontaneously spread on the film.

### Solution

**Given:**
$\gamma_{LV} = 28$ mN/m, $\theta = 78°$, $\cos(78°) = 0.2079$

**(a) Work of Adhesion:**

Using the Young–Dupré equation:

$$W_A = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta) = 28 \times (1 + 0.2079)$$

$$W_A = 28 \times 1.2079 = 33.82 \text{ mJ/m}^2$$

**(b) Work of Cohesion:**

$$W_C = 2\gamma_{LV} = 2 \times 28 = 56 \text{ mJ/m}^2$$

**(c) Spreading Coefficient:**

$$S = \gamma_{LV}(\cos\theta - 1) = 28 \times (0.2079 - 1)$$

$$S = 28 \times (-0.7921) = -22.18 \text{ mJ/m}^2$$

**(d) Comment:**

Since $S < 0$, the ink will *not* spontaneously spread on the BOPP film. The drop will retain a finite contact angle of $78°$, and the ink will bead up rather than form a uniform film. This result is consistent with the fact that BOPP has a relatively low surface free energy ($\gamma_C \approx 32$ mN/m, comparable to the ink's $\gamma_{LV}$ of 28 mN/m but with significant $\gamma_{SL}$ penalty).

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Remedy:** To enable good ink wetting, the BOPP film is typically **corona-treated** to raise $\gamma_C$ to $44$–$50$ mN/m, which reduces the contact angle to below $20°$ and promotes uniform ink lay-down.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Spreading Behavior | Contact Angle $\theta$ | $\cos\theta$ | $S$ | $W_A$ vs. $W_C$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Complete wetting (film) | $0°$ | $1$ | $0$ | $W_A = W_C$ |
| Strong wetting | $< 30°$ | $> 0.87$ | slightly negative | $W_A$ close to $W_C$ |
| Moderate wetting | $30°$–$60°$ | $0.5$–$0.87$ | moderately negative | $W_A < W_C$ |
| Weak wetting | $60°$–$90°$ | $0$–$0.5$ | strongly negative | $W_A \ll W_C$ |
| Poor wetting | $> 90°$ | $< 0$ | very negative | $W_A$ very small |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Wetting Thermodynamics System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional architecture of the wetting thermodynamics analysis pipeline, illustrating how the measured input parameters flow through the equations to yield the engineering output decisions used in plastics decoration.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Measured Input Parameters] --> B[Liquid Surface Tension gamma LV]
    A --> C[Equilibrium Contact Angle theta]
    A --> D[Solid Substrate Type and Pretreatment]
    B --> E[Young-Dupre Equation Module]
    C --> E
    E --> F[Work of Adhesion W_A]
    B --> G[Work of Cohesion Module]
    G --> H[Work of Cohesion W_C = 2 gamma LV]
    F --> I[Spreading Coefficient Calculator]
    H --> I
    C --> I
    I --> J{Sign of S Decision Block}
    J -->|S greater than 0| K[Complete Wetting Output]
    J -->|S equals 0| L[Critical Spreading Output]
    J -->|S less than 0| M[Partial Wetting Output]
    D --> N[Zisman Critical Surface Tension Lookup Table]
    N --> O[Comparison gamma LV vs gamma C]
    O --> P{Wettability Verdict}
    P -->|gamma LV less than gamma C| Q[Liquid Wets the Solid]
    P -->|gamma LV greater than gamma C| R[Liquid Does Not Wet the Solid]
    K --> S[Final Engineering Recommendation]
    L --> S
    M --> S
    Q --> S
    R --> S
    S --> T[Coating / Ink / Adhesive Process Decision]
```

The diagram below visualizes the three interfacial tensions acting at the three-phase contact line and their vector relationship, which is the physical basis of Young's equation.


This block diagram illustrates the transformation of interfacial energy inputs into the three principal thermodynamic output quantities.
