1. Mass of masterbatch required
2. Mass of pigment in the final product
3. Reflectance $R$ expected if K/S of the final product is measured as **0.85**

### Step 1 — Mass of masterbatch

From the LDR formula:

$$m_{\text{mb}} = \text{LDR} \times m_{\text{total}} = 0.03 \times 1000 = \mathbf{30 \text{ kg}}$$

### Step 2 — Mass of pigment in the final product

$$m_{\text{pig}} = m_{\text{mb}} \times C_{\text{mb}} = 30 \times 0.40 = \mathbf{12 \text{ kg}}$$

### Step 3 — Verify reflectance from K-M equation

$$\frac{K}{S} = 0.85 = \frac{(1 - R)^2}{2R}$$

Multiply both sides by $2R$:

$$1.70 \, R = (1 - R)^2 = 1 - 2R + R^2$$

Rearrange to standard quadratic form:

$$R^2 - 2R - 1.70 R + 1 = 0$$

$$R^2 - 3.70 R + 1 = 0$$

Apply the quadratic formula $R = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$:

$$R = \frac{3.70 \pm \sqrt{13.69 - 4}}{2} = \frac{3.70 \pm \sqrt{9.69}}{2} = \frac{3.70 \pm 3.113}{2}$$

Two roots: $R = 3.407$ (rejected, >1) or $R = 0.294$.

$$\boxed{R \approx 0.294 \text{ (i.e., 29.4\% reflectance)}}$$

> **Interpretation:** The colour sample reflects 29.4% of incident light, indicating a fairly dark / saturated shade.

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example — CIELAB Colour Difference

**Problem:** A target colour has CIELAB coordinates **L₁\* = 50, a₁\* = 25, b₁\* = 40**. A produced sample measures **L₂\* = 52, a₂\* = 23, b₂\* = 38**. Calculate ΔE and judge acceptability.

### Step 1 — Compute component differences

$$\Delta L^* = L_2^* - L_1^* = 52 - 50 = +2$$

$$\Delta a^* = a_2^* - a_1^* = 23 - 25 = -2$$

$$\Delta b^* = b_2^* - b_1^* = 38 - 40 = -2$$

### Step 2 — Apply the ΔE formula

$$\Delta E^* = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2}$$

$$\Delta E^* = \sqrt{(2)^2 + (-2)^2 + (-2)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 4 + 4} = \sqrt{12}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta E^* \approx 3.46}$$

### Step 3 — Acceptability judgement

| ΔE Range | Visual Perception | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| 0 – 1 | Not perceptible by human eye | Excellent match |
| 1 – 2 | Perceptible only on close inspection | Acceptable (tight tolerance) |
| 2 – 3.5 | Perceptible at arm's length | Borderline |
| 3.5 – 5 | Clearly different | Reject |
| > 5 | Strongly different | Reject |

> **Verdict:** ΔE = 3.46 falls in the **borderline** zone. The sample is **just outside** the typical commercial tolerance of ΔE ≤ 2.0. The moulder must adjust the pigment loading (e.g., increase red component) before production release.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix — Bulk vs Surface Colouration

| Selection Criterion | Bulk (Masterbatch) | Surface (Paint / Print) |
|---|---|---|
| Colour penetration through part | Yes (throughout) | No (surface only) |
| Scratch resistance | High (colour is in the matrix) | Low (overlay can be scratched) |
| Cost for large volume | Lower | Higher (labour, ink, equipment) |
| Multi-colour on one part | Difficult | Easy (printing) |
| Suitable for transparent parts | Yes (with dyes) | Yes |
| Tooling/equipment complexity | Low (dosing unit) | High (printing/painting line) |
| Environmental concern | Low | High (VOC from paints) |
| Best for | Mono-colour, high volume, durable | Multi-colour graphics, short runs |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Masterbatch Production & Let-Down Process (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Pigment Powder] --> B[High-Shear Twin-Screw Compounding Extruder]
    C[Carrier Resin Pellets] --> B
    D[Dispersing Agent / Wax] --> B
    B --> E[Filter Screen Mesh 200]
    E --> F[Strand or Underwater Pelletiser]
    F --> G[Masterbatch Pellets 20 to 60 percent Pigment]
    G --> H[Storage Silo]
    H --> I[Gravimetric Dosing Unit at Injection Moulder]
    J[Natural Polymer Resin] --> I
    I --> K[Plasticising Screw and Melt Homogenisation]
    K --> L[Injection Mould or Extrusion Die]
    L --> M[Coloured Plastic Part]
```

> **Reading Guide:** Pigment and carrier are compounded into masterbatch pellets, which are then *let-down* (diluted) into the natural polymer at the processing machine. The final part has uniform colour throughout its volume.

---

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Colour-Decoration Route

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1{Is multi-colour graphic required?}
    P1 -- Yes --> P2[Consider Surface Decoration IMD Pad Print Screen Print]
    P1 -- No --> P3{Is the part transparent or translucent?}
    P3 -- Yes --> P4[Use Dyes not Pigments for clarity]
    P3 -- No --> P5{Production volume per year?}
    P5 -- Greater than 50000 --> P6[Use Masterbatch or Pre Coloured Compound]
    P5 -- Less than 50000 --> P7[Use Dry Blend or Liquid Colour]
    P6 --> P8{Is outdoor UV exposure expected?}
    P8 -- Yes --> P9[Add Carbon Black 2 to 3 percent or HALS]
    P8 -- No --> P10[Standard let down ratio]
```

---

## 4.3 In-Mould Decoration (IMD) Process Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pre Printed Decoration Film on Reel] --> B[Film Feed Unit]
    B --> C[Position Film in Open Mould Cavity]
    C --> D[Mould Closes]
    D --> E[Polymer Melt Injected Behind Film]
    E --> F[Film Bonds to Polymer Surface]
    F --> G[Mould Opens]
    G --> H[Finished Decorated Part Ejected]
    H --> I[Film Reel Advances to Next Position]
```

> **KTU Examiner's Note:** IMD is the modern replacement for spray painting in automotive interior parts. The graphic is *buried* under a thin polymer layer, making it **scratch-proof and chemical-resistant**.

---

## 4.4 CIELAB Colour Space — Schematic Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Tristimulus["CIE XYZ Tristimulus Values from Spectrophotometer"]
        X["X Red Cone Response"]
        Y["Y Green Cone Response Luminance"]
        Z["Z Blue Cone Response"]
    end
    X --> Lab["CIELAB Conversion"]
    Y --> Lab
    Z --> Lab
    Lab --> L["L* Lightness Axis 0 to 100"]
    Lab --> A["a* Red Green Axis Negative Green to Positive Red"]
    Lab --> B["b* Yellow Blue Axis Negative Blue to Positive Yellow"]
```

> **Note:** A 3-dimensional colour solid is mathematically constructed from the L*, a*, b* axes. Any colour visible to the standard observer (2° field, D65 illuminant) can be located as a single point within this solid.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between pigments and dyes used as colourants in plastics. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Pigment | Dye |
|---|---|---|
| Solubility in polymer | **Insoluble** — exists as dispersed particles | **Soluble** — molecularly dissolves |
| Transparency | Opaque / semi-opaque | Transparent, brilliant |
| Heat & light stability | Generally **higher** | Lower; can fade or bleed |
| Mode of action | Two-phase dispersion (scatters + absorbs light) | Single-phase solution (absorbs light only) |

**Examples (½ mark each):**
- **Pigment** → Titanium dioxide (white) or Phthalocyanine blue
- **Dye** → Solvent Red 111 or Disperse Blue 183

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a masterbatch? Mention two advantages of colouring plastics with masterbatch.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **masterbatch** is a *concentrated thermoplastic compound* in which a high loading of pigment (typically **20% to 60%**) is pre-dispersed in a **carrier resin** that is compatible with the base polymer. It is supplied in pellet form and let down (diluted) at low ratios (typically **1% to 4%**) into the natural polymer at the processing machine.

**Two advantages (1½ marks each):**
1. **Dust-free handling** — unlike dry powder colourants, masterbatch pellets do not generate airborne pigment dust, improving factory hygiene and worker safety.
2. **Excellent pigment dispersion** — the high-shear compounding step in masterbatch production breaks down agglomerates, giving uniform colour in the final part with no streaks.
3. *(Optional 3rd point — bonus)* — **Accurate, repeatable dosing** via gravimetric feeders, and **easy colour changeover** between production runs.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students **confuse pigments and dyes** — losing 1–2 marks per occurrence.
> - In numerical questions on K-M theory, students **drop the negative sign** in the quadratic solution. Always report only the root lying between **0 and 1** (since reflectance is a fraction).
> - For ΔE calculations, **do not forget to square each ΔL\*, Δa\*, Δb\*** before adding. Common error: adding the linear values and taking a square root.
> - In surface-decoration questions, students often **omit the durability comparison** (e.g., scratch, chemical, UV resistance) between methods — this costs 2 marks.
> - Masterbatch questions require **mentioning let-down ratio (LDR)** — many students skip this and lose 1 mark.

---

### PART B — QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** Explain the **Kubelka–Munk theory** of colour absorption and scattering. Derive the expression for K/S in terms of reflectance R. State any **two limitations** of the K-M model. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the various **methods of colour incorporation in plastics**. Compare **masterbatch colouring** with **dry-blend colouring** in terms of dispersion quality, dust generation, dosing accuracy, and cost. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Answer to Q A (a) — Kubelka–Munk Theory (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
Kubelka–Munk (K-M) theory is a two-flux radiative transfer model that describes the reflectance of a coloured, opaque layer in terms of two coefficients — the **absorption coefficient (K)** and the **scattering coefficient (S)** — which together characterise how pigment particles interact with incident light.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

*Setup:* Consider a thick, opaque, coloured layer illuminated by diffuse monochromatic light. Two opposing light fluxes are tracked: a downward flux $i(x)$ and an upward (back-scattered) flux $j(x)$ at depth $x$.

*Step 1 — Differential balance for downward flux:*
$$di = -(K + S) \, i \, dx + S \, j \, dx$$

*Step 2 — Differential balance for upward flux:*
$$dj = (K + S) \, j \, dx - S \, i \, dx$$

*Step 3 — Steady-state for opaque layer:* Since the layer is opaque, the net change vanishes:
$$(K + S) i = S j$$

*Step 4 — Express reflectance:* Reflectance at the surface is $R = j/i = 1 + K/S$.

*Step 5 — Algebraic rearrangement to solve for K/S in terms of R:*
$$\frac{K}{S} = \frac{(1 - R)^2}{2R}$$

**Two limitations (1 mark):**
1. Assumes **diffuse, monochromatic** light — fails for glossy surfaces or angle-dependent effect pigments (metallics, pearlescents).
2. Assumes the pigment particles are **randomly distributed** and do not interact — breaks down at very high pigment loadings (multiple scattering not captured).

---

#### Model Answer to Q A (b) — Methods of Colour Incorporation (7 marks)

**Methods (4 marks):**

1. **Dry colour blending** — Pigment powder tumble-mixed with natural resin. Suitable for small lots, R&D.
2. **Masterbatch colouring** — Concentrated pigment in carrier resin pellets; let-down into natural resin.
3. **Liquid colour** — Pumped dispersion of pigment in liquid carrier.
4. **Pre-coloured (compounded) resin** — Fully compounded at resin supplier.
5. **Surface colouration** — Painting, printing, in-mould decoration, metallisation.

**Comparison table (3 marks):**

| Property | Masterbatch | Dry Blend |
|---|---|---|
| Dispersion quality | Excellent (pre-dispersed) | Poor to moderate |
| Dust generation | Negligible (pellet form) | High (powder) |
| Dosing accuracy | High (gravimetric) | Low (powder metering erratic) |
| Capital cost | Moderate (dosing unit) | Low |
| Per-kg colour cost | Lower at high volumes | Lower at very small batches |

**Conclusion:** Masterbatch is the **industry-preferred** method for high-volume production of uniform-coloured plastic parts.

---

### PART B — QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** Explain the **CIELAB colour space (L\*a\*b\*)** with a neat sketch. How is the **total colour difference (ΔE\*)** calculated? What is the industrial significance of ΔE values? (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the following **surface colour-decoration techniques** for plastics: (i) Pad printing, (ii) Hot stamping, (iii) In-mould decoration. Compare their suitability for **multi-colour graphics on 3D curved surfaces**. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Answer to Q B (a) — CIELAB Colour Space (7 marks)

**CIELAB definition (3 marks):**
CIELAB is a **device-independent, 3-dimensional colour model** defined by the CIE in 1976. Its three axes are:
- **L\*** — Lightness, ranging from 0 (black) to 100 (white).
- **a\*** — Red–Green axis (+a\* = red, −a\* = green).
- **b\*** — Yellow–Blue axis (+b\* = yellow, −b\* = blue).

The model is derived from CIE XYZ tristimulus values normalised against a reference white (typically D65 illuminant). The cube-root nonlinearity mimics human visual perception.

**Conversion equations (2 marks):**
$$L^* = 116 \left( Y/Y_n \right)^{1/3} - 16$$
$$a^* = 500 \left[ (X/X_n)^{1/3} - (Y/Y_n)^{1/3} \right]$$
$$b^* = 200 \left[ (Y/Y_n)^{1/3} - (Z/Z_n)^{1/3} \right]$$

**ΔE calculation (1 mark):**
$$\Delta E^* = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2}$$

**Industrial significance of ΔE (1 mark):**
ΔE quantifies the **perceived colour difference** between a sample and a target. It is used as a **single-number pass/fail criterion** in plastic-part quality control. Industry thresholds:
- **ΔE ≤ 1** → invisible difference (premium match)
- **ΔE ≤ 2** → acceptable for most commercial parts
- **ΔE > 3** → reject batch

---

#### Model Answer to Q B (b) — Surface Decoration Techniques (7 marks)

**(i) Pad Printing (Tampon Printing) (2 marks):**
An **etched (intaglio) metal plate** holds ink in engraved recesses. A **silicone rubber pad** picks up the inked image from the plate and presses it onto the **3D curved or recessed** plastic part. Common for **pen barrels, syringes, golf balls, promotional items**. Allows 1–4 colours per pass.

**(ii) Hot Stamping (Foil Stamping) (2 marks):**
A **metallic or pigmented foil** (PET carrier + release layer + colour/metallic layer + adhesive) is pressed against the part by a heated die. Heat softens the adhesive, transferring the colour layer. Used for **cosmetic caps, nameplates, electrical ratings**. Cannot easily do full multi-colour.

**(iii) In-Mould Decoration (IMD / IML) (2 marks):**
A **pre-printed film** is placed inside the mould cavity before injection. Molten polymer is injected behind the film, **fusing it to the part surface**. Result: **scratch-proof, chemical-resistant, photographic-quality graphics** buried under a clear polymer skin. Common in **automotive dashboards, mobile-phone covers, washing-machine panels**.

**Comparison for multi-colour 3D curved surfaces (1 mark):**

| Method | Multi-colour capability | 3D-curve ability | Durability |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pad printing | Up to 4 colours per cycle | Excellent (conforms to curves) | Moderate |
| Hot stamping | Single colour/foil per press | Moderate (flat-ish areas) | High |
| IMD | Unlimited photographic colour | Excellent (mould-shaped) | Very high (graphic buried in polymer) |

> **Best choice for multi-colour 3D curved surface:** **IMD** (in-mould decoration) for high-volume durable products; **Pad printing** for low-volume promotional or industrial marking.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Decorating Plastics with Colour**

- **Definition:** Colour decoration = controlled chromatic appearance of a plastic part via **bulk** (in-mass) or **surface** (overlay) methods.
- **Pigments ≠ Dyes** — Pigments are insoluble particles (opacity, stability); Dyes are soluble in polymer (transparency, brilliancy).
- **Masterbatch** is the industrial-standard bulk method: **20–60% pigment in carrier resin**, let down at **1–4%** into the base polymer.
- **Dry blend** is dusty, inconsistent, and used only for small R&D batches.
- **Liquid colour** gives excellent dispersion but needs pumping equipment.
- **Pre-coloured compound** gives best consistency at highest cost.
- **CIELAB (L\*a\*b\*)** is the standard industrial colour space; **ΔE** quantifies colour match.
- **Kubelka–Munk equation** $\dfrac{K}{S} = \dfrac{(1 - R)^2}{2R}$ links reflectance to pigment concentration — used in Computer Colour Matching.
- **Surface decoration** methods: spray painting, screen printing, pad printing, hot stamping, IMD/IML, sublimation, vacuum metallisation.
- **In-mould decoration (IMD)** gives the highest durability by bonding the printed film into the polymer matrix.
- **Carbon black 2–3%** is the most common UV stabiliser for outdoor plastics.
- **Common defects:** colour streaks, plate-out, bleeding, UV fading, weld-line colour shift.
- **ΔE tolerance:** ≤1 = premium; ≤2 = commercial; >3 = reject.
- **Always** include the **let-down ratio (LDR)** in any masterbatch numerical problem.
- **K-M reflectance R** must always lie in the range **0 to 1**; reject the second root of the quadratic if it exceeds 1.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Colour Selection Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT864 - PLASTICS & DECORATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Colour Selection Criteria ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the discipline of **Plastics & Decoration**, **Colour Selection Criteria** refers to the systematic, multi-parameter engineering process of choosing an appropriate colourant — pigment, dye, masterbatch, or compound — for incorporation into a polymeric matrix such that the resulting finished product satisfies a defined set of functional, aesthetic, economic, regulatory, and processing constraints. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Examination Standard)** > Colour selection in plastics is **not a purely artistic decision**. It is a **constrained optimisation problem** where a colourant must simultaneously satisfy the following five primary requirement vectors: > 1. **End-use performance** (light fastness, weathering, chemical resistance) > 2. **Process compatibility** (thermal stability, shear stability, dispersion) > 3. **Aesthetic fidelity** (target shade match, gloss, opacity) > 4. **Regulatory compliance** (FDA, RoHS, REACH, EN-71 toy safety) > 5. **Commercial viability** (cost, availability, supply chain reliability) The measurable target for this selection process is the minimisation of the **colour difference (ΔE)**, governed by the **CIE Lab (1976) colour space** equation: $$\Delta E^{*}_{ab} = \sqrt{(\Delta L^{*})^{2} + (\Delta a^{*})^{2} + (\Delta b^{*})^{2}}$$ where $L^{*}$ denotes **lightness**, $a^{*}$ denotes the **red-green axis**, and $b^{*}$ denotes the **yellow-blue axis** of the CIE perceptual colour model. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "House Paint Selection" Model > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy for First-Time Learners** > Imagine you are choosing the exterior paint for a house in coastal Kerala. You would NOT simply pick "blue" because you like it. You would consider: > - **Climate**: Will the pigment resist salt-spray and tropical UV? *(= Light Fastness)* > - **Substrate**: Will it bond to the plastered wall? *(= Polymer–Pigment Compatibility)* > - **Application method**: Brush, roller, or spray? *(= Processing Conditions)* > - **Budget**: Premium vs. economy brands. *(= Cost Constraint)* > - **Local society rules**: Some apartments ban vivid reds. *(= Regulatory / Cultural Compliance)* > - **Fading concern**: How long should the colour last? *(= Durability / Warranty)* > > The **same logic tree** governs colour selection in a moulded plastic bucket, a PET bottle, a dashboard panel, or a children's toy. The polymer is your "wall", the colourant is your "paint", and the moulding process is your "brush". ### 1.3 Standard Reference Systems Used in Industry | System | Domain | Year | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | **Pantone Matching System (PMS)** | Graphic arts, branding | 1963 | Corporate brand identity, packaging | | **RAL** | Industrial coatings | 1927 | Engineering plastics, powder coatings | | **Munsell** | Soil/pigment science | 1905 | Raw pigment characterisation | | **CIE Lab (L\*a\*b\*)** | Instrumental measurement | 1976 | Quantitative ΔE-based approval | | **NCS** (Natural Colour System) | Scandinavian standard | 1979 | Architectural, consumer goods | > [!NOTE] > **Why KTU 2024 Emphasises CIE Lab:** The **ΔE metric** is the only system that allows *numerical, reproducible* colour approval in the **End-Semester Examination (ESE)** and in ISO 9001-compliant plastic manufacturing plants. --- ### 1.4 The Three Fundamental Categories of Plastic Colourants > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favourite — Memorise This Triad** 1. **Pigments** — Insoluble, dispersed as fine particles. *Example:* Titanium dioxide (white), Carbon black, Phthalocyanine Blue. 2. **Dyes** — Soluble, dissolve in polymer melt. *Example:* Anthraquinone reds. (Rare in thermoplastics; common in PS, PMMA.) 3. **Masterbatches** — Concentrated pigment dispersed in a carrier resin at 20–60 % loading, let-down into base polymer at 1–4 %. **Specialty Effect Colourants** (not in triad, but KTU-relevant): - **Metallic flakes** (aluminium, bronze) — for automotive trim - **Pearlescent / Interference pigments** (mica-coated TiO₂) — for cosmetics packaging - **Fluorescent pigments** — for safety helmets, toys - **Thermochromic / Photochromic** — smart applications ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Seven Pillars of Colour Selection > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "7-Pillar Decision Matrix" — KTU 2024 Examinable Framework** A colour cannot be selected on a single criterion. The engineer must evaluate **seven orthogonal pillars** simultaneously. Each pillar is independent; failure of any one disqualifies the candidate colourant. #### Pillar 1 — End-Use Functional Requirements - Indoor vs. outdoor exposure - UV exposure dose (kLy/year) - Contact with chemicals (oils, acids, detergents, fuels) - Mechanical abrasion (scrub resistance) - Food contact (FDA 21 CFR, EU 10/2011) - Toy contact (EN 71-3, ASTM F963) #### Pillar 2 — Polymer Matrix Compatibility - **Polar polymers** (PVC, PA, PET, PC) → accept polar pigments easily - **Non-polar polymers** (PE, PP) → require surface-treated pigments - Polarity mismatch → agglomeration, specks, plate-out #### Pillar 3 — Thermal / Processing Stability - Pigment decomposition temperature must exceed processing temperature by at least **20–30 °C**. - Example: Quinacridone red decomposes above 280 °C → **unsuitable** for PA6 (melt 260 °C with shear heating). | Polymer | Typical Processing Temp | Minimum Pigment Stability | |---|---|---| | PE / PP | 180–240 °C | **≥ 260 °C** | | PS / ABS | 200–240 °C | **≥ 260 °C** | | PA 6 / 6,6 | 240–290 °C | **≥ 310 °C** | | PC | 280–320 °C | **≥ 340 °C** | | PEEK | 370–400 °C | **≥ 430 °C** | #### Pillar 4 — Optical Performance - **Opacity** controlled by **Kubelka-Munk K/S ratio** - **Tint strength** — ability to tint white base - **Gloss retention** — does the colourant dull the surface? - **Transparency** — dyes yield clear tints; pigments yield opaque shades #### Pillar 5 — Regulatory & Safety Compliance - Heavy metals ban: Cd, Pb, Hg, Cr(VI) — **RoHS, REACH SVHC** - Azo dyes releasing carcinogenic amines — **banned in textiles and toys** - Phthalates in PVC colour plasticisers - Food-grade certification (FDA, EFSA, BfR) #### Pillar 6 — Colour Communication & Approval - Specified by **Pantone, RAL, Munsell, or customer physical sample** - Approved using **spectrophotometer + ΔE ≤ 1.0** (tight tolerance) to **ΔE ≤ 2.0** (commercial) - Visual approval under standardised D65 / A / TL84 light booths #### Pillar 7 — Cost & Supply Chain - Inorganic pigments: ₹ 200 – 800 / kg - Organic pigments: ₹ 1,500 – 25,000 / kg - Effect pigments (pearl, metallic): ₹ 5,000 – 1,00,000 / kg - Masterbatch premium over dry pigment: 30 – 60 % --- ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Examination Reference) > [!NOTE] > **All equations below are mathematically verifiable and KTU-board-standard.** | # | Equation / Parameter | Symbols | Units | KTU Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\Delta E^{*}_{ab} = \sqrt{(\Delta L^{*})^{2} + (\Delta a^{*})^{2} + (\Delta b^{*})^{2}}$ | ΔE = colour difference | unitless | Approve/reject colour match | | 2 | $\dfrac{K}{S} = \dfrac{(1 - R)^{2}}{2R}$ | K = absorption, S = scattering, R = reflectance | — | Predict opacity of pigmented film | | 3 | $T_{\text{pigment}} > T_{\text{process}} + 30\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ | T = temperature | °C | Pigment thermal screening | | 4 | $L^{*}_{w} = 116\left(\dfrac{Y}{Y_{n}}\right)^{1/3} - 16$ | Y = tristimulus, $Y_n$ = white reference | — | CIE Lightness calculation | | 5 | $C^{*} = \sqrt{(a^{*})^{2} + (b^{*})^{2}}$ | C* = chroma | — | Saturation/colour purity | | 6 | $h^{\circ} = \arctan\left(\dfrac{b^{*}}{a^{*}}\right)$ | h° = hue angle | degrees | Identify dominant hue | | 7 | $\eta_{\text{let-down}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{mb}}}{m_{\text{mb}} + m_{\text{base}}} \times 100$ | mb = masterbatch, base = base resin | % | Let-down ratio calculation | | 8 | $\Delta E \leq 1.0$ (tight), $\Delta E \leq 2.0$ (commercial), $\Delta E > 3.0$ (rejection) | Threshold | — | Colour approval limits | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Mark-Saver Tip:** If the question asks *"How do you numerically approve a colour match?"* — always quote **ΔE < 1.0 for tight tolerance** and write the **CIE Lab equation**. Examiners award full marks only when the **numerical threshold AND the equation** are both stated. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The 7-pillar selection framework is used directly in: - **Automotive OEM paint and trim shops** (ΔE < 0.5 between interior parts) - **Medical device manufacturing** (FDA-compliant colourants only) - **Consumer electronics housing** (Pantone-matched brand identity across global plants) - **Toys and childcare articles** (EN 71-3 migration limits) - **Recycled plastics** (colour sorting by NIR + visible spectroscopy) - **Smart textiles and wearables** (photochromic / thermochromic selection) ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The CIE Lab ΔE Equation > [!NOTE] > The CIE 1976 (L\*a\*b\*) colour difference is the **de-facto numerical basis for colour approval** in plastics decoration. Below is the explicit derivation from first principles. **Step 1 — Tristimulus values from spectrophotometer.** The instrument measures reflected light at 10 nm intervals from 400–700 nm, and integrates with standard observer functions $\bar{x}(\lambda), \bar{y}(\lambda), \bar{z}(\lambda)$ under illuminant D65 (CIE standard daylight, 6504 K colour temperature). $$X = k \int_{400}^{700} R(\lambda)\, \bar{x}(\lambda)\, S(\lambda)\, d\lambda$$ $$Y = k \int_{400}^{700} R(\lambda)\, \bar{y}(\lambda)\, S(\lambda)\, d\lambda$$ $$Z = k \int_{400}^{700} R(\lambda)\, \bar{z}(\lambda)\, S(\lambda)\, d\lambda$$ Here $R(\lambda)$ is the spectral reflectance of the plastic sample, $S(\lambda)$ is the illuminant power distribution, and $k$ is the normalising constant. **Step 2 — Convert tristimulus to CIE XYZ normalised against reference white $(X_n, Y_n, Z_n)$.** $$x = \dfrac{X}{X_{n}}, \quad y = \dfrac{Y}{Y_{n}}, \quad z = \dfrac{Z}{Z_{n}}$$ **Step 3 — Apply CIE Lab non-linear cube-root transformation.** $$L^{*} = 116 \cdot f\!\left(\dfrac{Y}{Y_{n}}\right) - 16$$ $$a^{*} = 500 \left[\,f\!\left(\dfrac{X}{X_{n}}\right) - f\!\left(\dfrac{Y}{Y_{n}}\right)\right]$$ $$b^{*} = 200 \left[\,f\!\left(\dfrac{Y}{Y_{n}}\right) - f\!\left(\dfrac{Z}{Z_{n}}\right)\right]$$ where the non-linear function $f(t)$ is: $$f(t) = \begin{cases} t^{1/3} & \text{if } t > \left(\dfrac{6}{29}\right)^{3} \\[4pt] \dfrac{1}{3}\left(\dfrac{29}{6}\right)^{2} t + \dfrac{4}{29} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ **Step 4 — Compute colour difference between standard and batch.** $$\Delta L^{*} = L^{*}_{\text{batch}} - L^{*}_{\text{standard}}$$ $$\Delta a^{*} = a^{*}_{\text{batch}} - a^{*}_{\text{standard}}$$ $$\Delta b^{*} = b^{*}_{\text{batch}} - b^{*}_{\text{standard}}$$ **Step 5 — Combine into Euclidean distance in L\*a\*b\* space (perceived as approximately uniform).** $$\Delta E^{*}_{ab} = \sqrt{(\Delta L^{*})^{2} + (\Delta a^{*})^{2} + (\Delta b^{*})^{2}}$$ **Step 6 — Decision rule (KTU industrial standard).** | $\Delta E^{*}_{ab}$ | Interpretation | Action | |---|---|---| | < 0.5 | Imperceptible difference | **Accept — premium tier** | | 0.5 – 1.0 | Perceptible to trained eye | **Accept — tight tolerance** | | 1.0 – 2.0 | Perceptible at a glance | **Accept — commercial** | | 2.0 – 3.5 | Clearly visible | **Conditional / shade sort** | | > 3.5 | Strong mismatch | **Reject** | --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Kubelka-Munk K/S Ratio for Opacity Opacity of a pigmented plastic film depends on the ratio of absorption ($K$) to scattering ($S$) coefficients. For an **infinitely thick opaque layer**, the reflectance $R_\infty$ relates to $K/S$ as: **Step 1 — Reflectance at maximum hiding (infinite film thickness).** Let $R_\infty$ be the reflectance of a sample so thick that further thickness does not change $R$. This is measured by stacking multiple sample layers until the reading stabilises. **Step 2 — Apply the two-flux Kubelka-Munk remission function.** $$\frac{K}{S} = \frac{(1 - R_\infty)^{2}}{2 R_\infty}$$ This is derived from the two-flux differential equations for a scattering-absorbing medium under diffuse illumination. Solving the boundary conditions at the back surface (zero reflectance for a black substrate) and at infinite thickness (no further change) yields the above relation. **Step 3 — Interpret K/S values.** | K/S Range | Optical Behaviour | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---| | 0 – 0.05 | Highly scattering, low absorption | **White / opaque base** | | 0.05 – 0.5 | Moderate absorption | **Pastel tints** | | 0.5 – 5.0 | Strong absorption | **Saturated colours** | | > 5.0 | Near-black | **Carbon black, very dark** | **Step 4 — Predict required pigment loading.** For a target opacity at thickness $h$, the required $K \cdot h$ product is: $$K \cdot h = S \cdot h \cdot \left(\dfrac{K}{S}\right) \geq 2.0 \quad \text{(for full hiding)}$$ This is used to calculate the **minimum pigment loading** to achieve opacity in injection-moulded parts, blow-moulded bottles, and extruded films. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Weighted Decision Scoring for Colour Selection > [!NOTE] > This is the **KTU 2024 ESE-style analytical problem**. The examiner gives you a real scenario and you must score and rank candidate colourants. **Problem Statement:** A KTU 2024 examination question (Dec 2023 pattern, adapted) states: > *"A plastic furniture manufacturer wishes to select a colour masterbatch for an outdoor chair moulded in PP. Three options are available. Using a weighted decision matrix, recommend the best option."* **Step 1 — Define selection criteria and assign weights (must sum to 1.00).** | Criterion | Weight $w_i$ | Justification | |---|---|---| | Light Fastness | 0.25 | Outdoor exposure, 5-year warranty | | Weather Resistance | 0.20 | Tropical Kerala monsoon, UV dose 100 kLy/yr | | Cost per kg | 0.15 | Volume product, cost-sensitive | | Shade Match to Target RAL 6005 (green) | 0.20 | Customer-specified | | Regulatory (REACH / RoHS) | 0.10 | Export to EU market | | Heat Stability (PP at 240 °C) | 0.10 | Processing margin | Verification: $0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.20 + 0.10 + 0.10 = 1.00$ ✓ **Step 2 — Score each option on a 1–10 scale (10 = best, 1 = worst).** | Criterion | Masterbatch A | Masterbatch B | Masterbatch C | |---|---|---|---| | Light Fastness | 7 | 9 | 6 | | Weather Resistance | 6 | 8 | 7 | | Cost | 9 | 6 | 8 | | Shade Match | 8 | 9 | 6 | | Regulatory | 8 | 9 | 7 | | Heat Stability | 7 | 8 | 6 | **Step 3 — Compute weighted score $S_k$ for each option k.** $$S_{k} = \sum_{i=1}^{6} w_{i} \cdot s_{i,k}$$ For **Option A**: $$S_{A} = (0.25)(7) + (0.20)(6) + (0.15)(9) + (0.20)(8) + (0.10)(8) + (0.10)(7)$$ $$S_{A} = 1.750 + 1.200 + 1.350 + 1.600 + 0.800 + 0.700$$ $$S_{A} = 7.40$$ For **Option B**: $$S_{B} = (0.25)(9) + (0.20)(8) + (0.15)(6) + (0.20)(9) + (0.10)(9) + (0.10)(8)$$ $$S_{B} = 2.250 + 1.600 + 0.900 + 1.800 + 0.900 + 0.800$$ $$S_{B} = 8.25$$ For **Option C**: $$S_{C} = (0.25)(6) + (0.20)(7) + (0.15)(8) + (0.20)(6) + (0.10)(7) + (0.10)(6)$$ $$S_{C} = 1.500 + 1.400 + 1.200 + 1.200 + 0.700 + 0.600$$ $$S_{C} = 6.60$$ **Step 4 — Rank and recommend.** $$S_{B} \;(8.25) \;>\; S_{A} \;(7.40) \;>\; S_{C} \;(6.60)$$ > **Recommendation:** Select **Masterbatch B** despite its higher cost, because the weighted performance (driven by light fastness, weather resistance, and shade match) yields the highest overall score. The 35 % cost premium over A is justified by the 5-year outdoor warranty and export-grade regulatory compliance. **Step 5 — Sensitivity check.** If the weight on Cost were increased to 0.30 (and Shade Match reduced to 0.05), the ranking would change. This illustrates that the **weights, not the raw scores, drive the decision** — a critical KTU-evaluative insight. ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Colour Selection Process > [!NOTE] > The diagram below maps the **decision flow** that a colour technologist follows in a real plastics manufacturing plant. Each block is a discrete evaluation stage; the colourant only proceeds if it **passes every gate**. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Customer Requirement
Pantone RAL or Sample] --> B[End Use Analysis
Indoor Outdoor Food Contact] B --> C[Polymer Matrix Selection
PE PP PA PC PET] C --> D[Colourant Family Filter
Pigment Dye Masterbatch] D --> E[Pigment Property Check
Heat Stability Light Fastness] E --> F{Process Temp
Compatible} F -- No --> G[Reject and Re-Loop] F -- Yes --> H[Regulatory Screening
RoHS REACH FDA EN71] H --> I{Compliant} I -- No --> G I -- Yes --> J[Lab Trial Compounding
Twin Screw Extruder] J --> K[Spectrophotometric Evaluation
CIE Lab Delta E] K --> L{Delta E within Tolerance} L -- No --> M[Shade Adjustment
or Reject] L -- Yes --> N[Weathering and Ageing Test
Xenon Arc UV Chamber] N --> O{Passes Durability} O -- No --> M O -- Yes --> P[Cost and Supply Audit
Commercial Approval] P --> Q[Final Selected Colourant
Released to Production] G --> D M --> D ``` ### 4.2 Pigment Decision Tree — Organic vs Inorganic vs Effect ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Target Colour Shade] --> B{Opacity Required} B -- Transparent Tint --> C[Dye Selection
Soluble in Polymer] B -- Opaque Shade --> D{Outdoor Exposure} D -- Yes High UV --> E[Inorganic Pigment
High Light Fastness] D -- No Indoor --> F[Organic Pigment
Bright Chromatic] F --> G{Special Effect} G -- Metallic --> H[Aluminium Bronze Flake] G -- Pearlescent --> I[Mica Coated TiO2] G -- Fluorescent --> J[Daylight Fluorescent Pigment] G -- None --> K[Standard Organic Pigment
Phthalocyanine Azo] E --> L[Carbon Black Iron Oxide
Cobalt Chromate] ``` ### 4.3 The 7-Pillar Selection Schematic (Block View) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Pillar 1
End Use Performance] --> MERGE{Weighted Score} P2[Pillar 2
Polymer Compatibility] --> MERGE P3[Pillar 3
Thermal Stability] --> MERGE P4[Pillar 4
Optical Performance] --> MERGE P5[Pillar 5
Regulatory Compliance] --> MERGE P6[Pillar 6
Colour Communication] --> MERGE P7[Pillar 7
Cost and Supply] --> MERGE MERGE --> OUT[Recommended Colourant] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Read this diagram as a 7-input fan-in:** all seven pillars are evaluated **in parallel** for every candidate colourant. The weighted score is the final arbiter. **Never short-circuit the process** by approving on the basis of a single criterion (e.g., shade match alone) — this is a common KTU 2024 practical error. ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]** *Define "colour selection criteria" in the context of plastics decoration. List any **four** factors that govern the choice of a colourant for a polymeric product.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Definition (1 Mark):** Colour selection criteria is the systematic engineering process of choosing a pigment, dye, or masterbatch for incorporation into a polymer matrix such that the final product meets defined functional, aesthetic, regulatory, processing, and economic requirements. > > **Four Governing Factors (2 Marks — 0.5 each):** > 1. **End-use performance** — light fastness, weathering, chemical resistance > 2. **Polymer–pigment compatibility** — polarity match, dispersion stability > 3. **Thermal stability** — pigment decomposition temperature must exceed processing temperature > 4. **Regulatory compliance** — RoHS, REACH, FDA, EN-71 depending on application > > *(Acceptable alternatives: cost, shade match accuracy, gloss retention, opacity.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]** *State the **CIE Lab (1976) colour difference equation** and specify the **industrial acceptance threshold** for a "tight tolerance" colour match in plastics.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Equation (2 Marks):** > $$\Delta E^{*}_{ab} = \sqrt{(\Delta L^{*})^{2} + (\Delta a^{*})^{2} + (\Delta b^{*})^{2}}$$ > > where $\Delta L^{*}, \Delta a^{*}, \Delta b^{*}$ are the differences in lightness, red-green, and yellow-blue components respectively between the standard and the production batch. > > **Threshold (1 Mark):** For a *tight-tolerance* colour match, the industrial acceptance limit is **$\Delta E^{*}_{ab} \leq 1.0$**. A value between 1.0 and 2.0 is acceptable for *commercial* grade; a value exceeding 3.5 results in outright rejection. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before You Write** > - **Never** answer only the "definition" part. A 7-mark sub-question demands *explanation + example + diagram reference + 2 supporting points*. > - **Avoid one-word answers** like "heat stability." The examiner wants the *mechanism* — "Carbon black absorbs IR and raises melt temperature, while organic reds can decompose above 280 °C, releasing chromophores." > - **Always cite the standard** (e.g., ISO 105-B02 for light fastness, EN 71-3 for toys). Standards alone can earn 1–2 marks even when the rest of the answer is weak. > - **Diagrams score extra marks.** Even a hand-drawn block flow chart of the 7-pillar decision matrix wins the examiner's favour. > - **Numerical ΔE problems are mandatory in 14-mark questions.** If the question gives a number, your answer MUST contain a calculation. --- #### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks) — Choose Any ONE** **Q.A [KTU ESE — Module 1 Choice Pattern, Adapted 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Discuss the **seven pillars of colour selection** for plastic products. For each pillar, give one example of a polymer–colourant application where it is the *critical* decision factor. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compare **organic pigments, inorganic pigments, and dyes** as colourants for plastics. Tabulate your answer covering **five** properties: chemical nature, heat stability, light fastness, colour strength, and typical applications. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer — Q.A:** **(a) The Seven Pillars (7 Marks — 1 mark per pillar):** > 1. **End-Use Performance:** Critical for automotive exterior trim. A red moulded bumper must withstand 5 years of UV, acid rain, and car-wash chemicals. Pigment: Quinacridone red (high light fastness, 8 on Blue Wool Scale). > 2. **Polymer Matrix Compatibility:** Critical for PP (non-polar). Surface-treated carbon black (oxidised, pH 7–9) is mandatory to prevent agglomeration and specks. > 3. **Thermal Stability:** Critical for PA6,6 processed at 290 °C. Only inorganic pigments (Fe₂O₃ red, Co-Al blue) and high-grade phthalocyanines survive. Many organic reds decompose. > 4. **Optical Performance:** Critical for HDPE bottle blow-moulding. TiO₂ at 3 % loading provides the white opacity required for dairy bottle brand identity. > 5. **Regulatory Compliance:** Critical for children's toys (EN 71-3). Heavy-metal-free organic pigments only; cadmium and lead chromates are banned. > 6. **Colour Communication & Approval:** Critical for global brand consistency. A pantone-matched RAL 3020 red across Mumbai and Mexico plants requires spectrophotometric approval within ΔE ≤ 1.0. > 7. **Cost & Supply:** Critical for mass-market PP woven sacks. Cheap inorganic pigments (iron oxide, carbon black) at ₹ 200 / kg are preferred over organic pigments at ₹ 5,000 / kg. **(b) Comparison Table (7 Marks — 0.5 per cell, 5 properties × 2 marks = 5 + 2 marks for application discussion):** | Property | Organic Pigment | Inorganic Pigment | Dye | |---|---|---|---| | Chemical Nature | Complex aromatic rings (C, H, N, O); insoluble in polymer | Metal oxides / sulphides (TiO₂, Fe₂O₃, CdS); insoluble | Soluble in polymer melt; no particulate form | | Heat Stability | Low to moderate (200–280 °C typical) | **High (up to 600 °C)** | Low (often < 200 °C) | | Light Fastness | Moderate (3–7 Blue Wool) | **Excellent (7–8 Blue Wool)** | Poor to moderate (1–5) | | Colour Strength | **Very high** (1–2 % loading) | Moderate (2–5 % loading) | High (0.1–0.5 % loading) | | Transparency | Translucent / transparent tints | **Opaque** | Fully transparent | | Migration / Bleed | Some bleed in plasticised PVC | **Zero bleed** | **High bleed risk** | | Cost (₹ / kg) | 1,500 – 25,000 | 200 – 800 | 800 – 5,000 | | Typical Applications | Automotive, packaging, toys | Pipes, chairs, fibres, masterbatches | PS, PMMA, PET optical clarity | > **Application Note (1 Mark):** Dyes are restricted to **optical-grade transparent polymers** (PS, PMMA, PC) where brilliant transparent tints are required and where bleeding into adjacent materials is not a concern (e.g., spectacle lenses, decorative lighting). In all other cases, **pigments dominate** the plastics colourant market. --- #### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **Q.B [KTU ESE — Module 1 Choice Pattern, Adapted 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Describe the **CIE Lab (L\*a\*b\*) colour space** with a neat diagram. Explain the **ΔE colour difference** equation, derive the components $\Delta L^{*}, \Delta a^{*}, \Delta b^{*}$, and state the industrial acceptance thresholds. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A moulded ABS part has the CIE Lab coordinates $L^{*}=45.2,\; a^{*}=62.5,\; b^{*}=28.7$. The customer-approved standard has $L^{*}=46.0,\; a^{*}=61.0,\; b^{*}=27.0$. Calculate the colour difference $\Delta E^{*}_{ab}$ and state whether the batch is **accepted, shade-sorted, or rejected** under commercial tolerance. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer — Q.B:** **(a) CIE Lab Description (7 Marks):** > The CIE Lab (1976) colour space is a **three-dimensional, perceptually uniform** model in which Euclidean distance corresponds approximately to perceived colour difference. (1 Mark) > > **Three axes (2 Marks):** > - $L^{*}$ — **Lightness** axis, vertical; $L^{*}=0$ is black, $L^{*}=100$ is white. > - $a^{*}$ — **Red–Green** axis, horizontal; positive $a^{*}$ = red, negative $a^{*}$ = green. > - $b^{*}$ — **Yellow–Blue** axis, horizontal; positive $b^{*}$ = yellow, negative $b^{*}$ = blue. > > **Hue and Chroma (1 Mark):** > - Chroma $C^{*} = \sqrt{(a^{*})^{2} + (b^{*})^{2}}$ — saturation / colour purity. > - Hue angle $h^{\circ} = \arctan(b^{*}/a^{*})$ — identifies the dominant colour family. > > **ΔE Equation (2 Marks):** > $$\Delta E^{*}_{ab} = \sqrt{(\Delta L^{*})^{2} + (\Delta a^{*})^{2} + (\Delta b^{*})^{2}}$$ > where $\Delta L^{*} = L^{*}_{\text{batch}} - L^{*}_{\text{standard}}$, and similarly for $\Delta a^{*}, \Delta b^{*}$. > > **Thresholds (1 Mark):** $\Delta E \leq 1.0$ (tight), $\leq 2.0$ (commercial), 2.0–3.5 (sort), $> 3.5$ (reject). **(b) Numerical Calculation (7 Marks — full marks for step-by-step):** **Step 1 — Compute component differences (2 Marks):** $$\Delta L^{*} = L^{*}_{\text{batch}} - L^{*}_{\text{standard}} = 45.2 - 46.0 = -0.8$$ $$\Delta a^{*} = a^{*}_{\text{batch}} - a^{*}_{\text{standard}} = 62.5 - 61.0 = +1.5$$ $$\Delta b^{*} = b^{*}_{\text{batch}} - b^{*}_{\text{standard}} = 28.7 - 27.0 = +1.7$$ **Step 2 — Square each component (2 Marks):** $$(\Delta L^{*})^{2} = (-0.8)^{2} = 0.64$$ $$(\Delta a^{*})^{2} = (1.5)^{2} = 2.25$$ $$(\Delta b^{*})^{2} = (1.7)^{2} = 2.89$$ **Step 3 — Sum the squares (1 Mark):** $$\sum = 0.64 + 2.25 + 2.89 = 5.78$$ **Step 4 — Take the square root (1 Mark):** $$\Delta E^{*}_{ab} = \sqrt{5.78} = 2.404$$ $$\boxed{\Delta E^{*}_{ab} \approx 2.40}$$ **Step 5 — Decision (1 Mark):** Since $2.0 < \Delta E = 2.40 < 3.5$, the batch falls in the **"shade-sort / conditional acceptance"** band. > **Conclusion:** The batch is **NOT auto-rejected** but is **shade-sorted** — i.e., segregated from batches of visibly different shade and used for non-critical or paired assemblies where the small colour deviation is acceptable. It cannot be shipped as a "first-quality commercial" batch. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read This 10 Minutes Before the Exam** **Key Definitions** - ☐ Colour selection criteria = **constrained multi-parameter optimisation** for plastic colourants. - ☐ Colourant triad: **Pigments (insoluble), Dyes (soluble), Masterbatches (concentrate).** - ☐ Specialty colourants: **metallic, pearlescent, fluorescent, thermochromic, photochromic.** **The 7 Pillars (in order of priority)** - ☐ **End-use performance** → light fastness, weathering, chemicals - ☐ **Polymer compatibility** → polarity match, dispersion - ☐ **Thermal stability** → $T_{\text{pigment}} > T_{\text{process}} + 30\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ - ☐ **Optical performance** → $K/S$ ratio, opacity, gloss - ☐ **Regulatory compliance** → RoHS, REACH, FDA, EN-71 - ☐ **Colour communication** → Pantone, RAL, Munsell, CIE Lab - ☐ **Cost & supply** → inorganic (cheap) vs organic (premium) **Must-Memorise Equations (without error)** - ☐ $\Delta E^{*}_{ab} = \sqrt{(\Delta L^{*})^{2} + (\Delta a^{*})^{2} + (\Delta b^{*})^{2}}$ - ☐ $K/S = (1 - R)^{2}/(2R)$ — Kubelka-Munk opacity - ☐ $C^{*} = \sqrt{(a^{*})^{2} + (b^{*})^{2}}$ — chroma - ☐ $h^{\circ} = \arctan(b^{*}/a^{*})$ — hue angle **Threshold Values (Top 3 Most-Asked)** - ☐ $\Delta E \leq 1.0$ → **tight** (premium / automotive) - ☐ $\Delta E \leq 2.0$ → **commercial** (general consumer goods) - ☐ $\Delta E > 3.5$ → **rejection** (always) **Organic vs Inorganic Comparison — One-Liners for Quick Recall** - ☐ Organic: **bright, transparent, low heat, costly, may bleed** - ☐ Inorganic: **dull, opaque, high heat, cheap, no bleed, weather-proof** - ☐ Dyes: **soluble, transparent, optical clarity only** **Industry-Standard References (Quote One for Free Marks)** - ☐ ISO 105-B02 — Light fastness (Xenon arc) - ☐ ASTM D2244 — Instrumental colour difference - ☐ ASTM D1729 — Visual colour assessment - ☐ EN 71-3 — Toy safety migration limits - ☐ FDA 21 CFR — Food contact colourants - ☐ RoHS 2 / REACH SVHC — EU heavy-metal & chemical restrictions **Common KTU Mistakes to Avoid** - ☐ Confusing **pigment** with **dye** in exam answers (pigment = insoluble; dye = soluble). - ☐ Forgetting the **+30 °C safety margin** when comparing pigment decomposition vs. processing temperature. - ☐ Writing ΔE without **state the threshold** — examiner will deduct 1 mark. - ☐ Omitting **regulatory** pillar — the 2024 scheme explicitly tests this for toys and food contact. - ☐ Using "ΔE < 1" but not specifying **CIE Lab 1976** — always cite the standard. > **End of Module 1 Notes — Colour Selection Criteria.**

topics.title: Colorant Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT864 - PLASTICS & DECORATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Colorant Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Colorant Classification** refers to the systematic categorization of color-imparting additives used in plastics processing based on their chemical nature, solubility characteristics, optical behavior, and functional performance within the polymer matrix. According to KTU 2024 scheme syllabus for *Plastics & Decoration*, colorants are broadly classified into two primary families: **Dyes** (soluble colorants that dissolve in the polymer melt) and **Pigments** (insoluble colorants that disperse as discrete particles in the polymer matrix).

Pigments are further sub-classified into **Organic Pigments**, **Inorganic Pigments**, and **Special Effect Pigments (Functional Pigments)** based on their chemical composition and optical mechanism.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The classification of colorants is fundamental to Module 1, as the type of colorant directly governs processing parameters, end-use applications, and aesthetic performance of decorated plastic products. Examiner questions frequently test the differentiation between dyes and pigments.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of coloring a cup of **hot water versus a sandy beach**:

- **Dye** = Sugar dissolved in hot tea. The sugar disappears into the liquid completely. You cannot see individual sugar crystals; the entire liquid becomes uniformly sweet and colored. This is exactly how a **dye** behaves in molten plastic — it dissolves completely into the polymer matrix, producing transparent, vivid colors but offering poor migration resistance.

- **Pigment** = Colored sand mixed into the beach. The sand particles remain distinct, scattered throughout. You can see them under a microscope. This is how a **pigment** behaves — it sits as tiny insoluble particles inside the plastic, giving opacity, heat stability, and migration resistance, but requires proper dispersion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Memory Hook:** **D**ye = **D**issolves, **P**igment = **P**article (insoluble particle). This single rule resolves 80% of KTU conceptual questions.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics

The following physical/chemical properties are used to evaluate any plastic colorant:

- **Heat Stability** (°C): Ability to withstand processing temperatures without degradation.
- **Light Fastness** (1–8 scale, ISO 105-B02): Resistance to fading upon UV exposure.
- **Migration Resistance**: Tendency to bleed, bloom, or plate-out.
- **Chemical Resistance**: Stability against acids, alkalis, solvents.
- **Toxicology**: Compliance with **FDA**, **REACH**, and **RoHS** standards.
- **Opacity / Transparency**: Determined by refractive index and particle size.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Master Classification Tree

The complete classification hierarchy as per KTU Module 1 is structured as follows:

### 2.1.1 DYES (Soluble Colorants)
- Dissolve at the molecular level in the polymer melt.
- Produce **transparent, brilliant colors** with high tinting strength.
- **Poor migration resistance** — tend to bleed in contact with solvents or under heat.
- Examples: **Solvent Red 111**, **Disperse Blue 14**, **Anthraquinone dyes**.

### 2.1.2 PIGMENTS (Insoluble Colorants)

**A. Organic Pigments** (carbon-based molecules, particle size 0.01–10 µm)
- **Azo Pigments** (–N=N– chromophore): Cheap, bright, but limited heat stability. Example: **Pigment Red 3 (Toluidine Red)**.
- **Phthalocyanine Pigments**: Excellent thermal, chemical, and light stability. Example: **Phthalocyanine Blue (Pigment Blue 15)**.
- **Quinacridone Pigments**: Outstanding light and weather fastness. Example: **Pigment Violet 19**.
- **Anthraquinone Pigments**: High-performance, used in engineering plastics.

**B. Inorganic Pigments** (metal compounds, particle size 0.1–50 µm)
- **Metal Oxides**: Titanium Dioxide (**TiO₂**, white), Iron Oxides (red/yellow/black), Chromium Oxide (green).
- **Sulfides & Selenides**: Cadmium Sulfide (**CdS**, yellow), Cadmium Selenide (red).
- **Chromates**: Lead Chromate (yellow), though restricted due to toxicity.
- **Carbon Black**: Elemental carbon, excellent UV stabilizer and black colorant.
- **Ultramarines**: Complex alumino-silicate, blue/violet.

**C. Special Effect Pigments (Functional Pigments)**
- **Pearlescent / Interference Pigments**: Mica coated with TiO₂ or Fe₂O₃, produce lustrous effect.
- **Metallic Pigments**: Aluminum flakes, bronze powder — give metallic sheen.
- **Fluorescent Pigments**: Absorb UV and emit visible light, appear to "glow".
- **Phosphorescent Pigments**: Store light energy and release slowly (glow-in-dark).
- **Thermochromic / Photochromic Pigments**: Change color with temperature or light.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind the Classification Logic

| Step | Reasoning |
|------|-----------|
| **Why separate Dyes from Pigments?** | Solubility directly determines whether the colorant forms a molecular solution (transparent) or a dispersion (opaque). This affects optical, mechanical, and migration behavior. |
| **Why sub-divide Organic vs Inorganic?** | Organic pigments offer brighter, more saturated colors but lower heat/light stability. Inorganic pigments offer superior durability but duller shades. |
| **Why introduce Special Effect Pigments?** | Modern plastic decoration requires not just color but also *optical effects* (pearl, metallic, glow) — hence a distinct functional category. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Property / Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Kubelka-Munk Opacity | $K/S = \frac{(1-R_{\infty})^{2}}{2 R_{\infty}}$ | dimensionless | $K$ = absorption coeff., $S$ = scattering coeff., $R_{\infty}$ = reflectance over black |
| Tinting Strength | $TS = \frac{m_{\text{std}}}{m_{\text{sample}}} \times 100$ | % | Mass of standard giving same color depth |
| Particle Size Effect (Rayleigh) | $I \propto \frac{d^{6}}{\lambda^{4}}$ | — | Scattering intensity for small particles |
| Critical Pigment Volume Concentration | $CPVC = \frac{V_{\text{pigment}}}{V_{\text{pigment}} + V_{\text{binder}}}$ | % | Beyond CPVC, binder is insufficient to wet pigment |
| Heat Stability Index | $\Delta E^{*} = \sqrt{(\Delta L^{*})^{2} + (\Delta a^{*})^{2} + (\Delta b^{*})^{2}}$ | CIELAB units | Total color difference after heat aging |

> [!NOTE]
> **CIE L\*a\*b\*** color space is the universally accepted metric in KTU examinations for expressing color difference. Always report $\Delta E^{*}$ to quantify fading or shift.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The classification framework directly drives industry decisions:
- **Food packaging** → FDA-approved inorganic pigments (TiO₂, iron oxides).
- **Automotive exterior** → High-performance quinacridones and pearlescents for weather resistance.
- **Toys & children's products** → Heavy-metal-free organic pigments, REACH compliant.
- **Outdoor signage** → Phthalocyanines + carbon black for UV endurance.
- **Cosmetics packaging** → Pearlescent and metallic pigments for premium aesthetics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Kubelka-Munk Opacity Relationship

The **Kubelka-Munk (K-M) two-flux model** is the most cited theoretical framework for understanding how pigmented polymer films interact with light. It is a high-yield topic for KTU Module 1 derivations.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

Consider a pigmented plastic film of thickness $h$ illuminated by diffuse monochromatic light. Light flux splits into:
- **Downward flux** $I$ (incident)
- **Upward flux** $J$ (back-scattered)

Two fundamental coefficients describe the medium:
- $K$ = absorption coefficient (energy lost per unit path)
- $S$ = scattering coefficient (energy redirected per unit path)

### 3.1.2 Derivation of the K-M Differential Equations

The net change in downward flux $I$ over an infinitesimal thickness $dx$ is governed by absorption and back-scattering:

$$
\frac{dI}{dx} = -(K+S) \cdot I + S \cdot J
$$

The net change in upward flux $J$ over the same thickness is governed by forward-scattering and absorption:

$$
-\frac{dJ}{dx} = -(K+S) \cdot J + S \cdot I
$$

### 3.1.3 Boundary Conditions

For an infinitely thick opaque film (practical engineering limit for plastics), the reflectance becomes:

$$
R_{\infty} = 1 + \frac{K}{S} - \sqrt{\left(\frac{K}{S}\right)^{2} + 2\frac{K}{S}}
$$

### 3.1.4 Inversion to K-M Function

Inverting the above to express the **absorption-to-scatter ratio** in terms of measured reflectance:

$$
\frac{K}{S} = \frac{(1 - R_{\infty})^{2}}{2 R_{\infty}}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- A **high $K/S$ value** indicates strong absorption relative to scattering → **opaque, deep colors**.
- A **low $K/S$ value** indicates dominant scattering → **white or pastel shades** (e.g., TiO₂-filled).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Selection Criteria for Common Plastics

| Polymer | Processing Temp | Recommended Colorant Class | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PE / PP** | 180–240 °C | Inorganic pigments, Phthalocyanines | Adequate heat stability; cost-driven |
| **PVC (rigid)** | 180–210 °C | Inorganic + select organic | Acidic/basic environment requires stable pigments |
| **PS / ABS** | 200–260 °C | Organic pigments, dyes acceptable | Lower chemical aggression; brighter colors possible |
| **PET** | 260–290 °C | High-performance organics, inorganics | High processing temp demands heat-stable classes |
| **PMMA (acrylic)** | 220–250 °C | Dyes + organic pigments | Optical clarity preserved; used in transparent lenses |
| **PA (Nylon)** | 260–300 °C | Inorganic, select quinacridones | High temp + moisture sensitivity |
| **PC (Polycarbonate)** | 280–320 °C | High-performance inorganics | Very high heat requirement |
| **Engineering Plastics (POM, PPS)** | 280–340 °C | Specialty inorganics + carbons | Extreme thermal load |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** A frequent Part A question asks — *"Why are dyes unsuitable for outdoor applications?"* The answer revolves around **migration**, **low UV stability**, and **lack of opacity** — all consequences of their molecular dissolution mechanism.

## 3.3 Comparative Performance Matrix

| Parameter | Dyes | Organic Pigments | Inorganic Pigments | Special Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Solubility in polymer | Soluble | Insoluble | Insoluble | Insoluble |
| Color brilliance | Excellent | Very Good | Moderate | Variable |
| Heat Stability | Poor–Moderate | Good–Excellent | Excellent | Good–Excellent |
| Light Fastness | Poor | Good | Excellent | Good |
| Migration Resistance | Poor | Good | Excellent | Good |
| Opacity | Transparent | Semi-opaque | Opaque | Variable |
| Cost | Low–Medium | Medium | Low–Medium | High |
| Toxicity Concerns | Moderate | Low–Moderate | Heavy metal issues | Variable |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["COLORANTS FOR PLASTICS"] --> B["DYES (Soluble)"]
    A --> C["PIGMENTS (Insoluble)"]

    B --> B1["Solvent Dyes"]
    B --> B2["Disperse Dyes"]
    B --> B3["Anthraquinone Dyes"]

    C --> D["Organic Pigments"]
    C --> E["Inorganic Pigments"]
    C --> F["Special Effect Pigments"]

    D --> D1["Azo Pigments"]
    D --> D2["Phthalocyanine Blue and Green"]
    D --> D3["Quinacridone Pigments"]
    D --> D4["Anthraquinone Pigments"]

    E --> E1["Metal Oxides - TiO2, Iron Oxide"]
    E --> E2["Sulfides - CdS, CdSe"]
    E --> E3["Carbon Black"]
    E --> E4["Ultramarines"]

    F --> F1["Pearlescent Mica Based"]
    F --> F2["Metallic Flakes"]
    F --> F3["Fluorescent Pigments"]
    F --> F4["Phosphorescent Pigments"]
    F --> F5["Thermochromic Pigments"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Light–Pigment Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L["Incident Light Source"] --> M1["Polymer Matrix Surface"]
    M1 --> P1["Pigment Particle A"]
    P1 --> R1["Reflected Light"]
    P1 --> A1["Absorbed Light"]
    P1 --> S1["Scattered Light"]

    M1 --> P2["Pigment Particle B"]
    P2 --> R2["Reflected Light"]
    P2 --> A2["Absorbed Light"]
    P2 --> S2["Scattered Light"]

    A1 --> OUT["Perceived Color Signal"]
    A2 --> OUT
    S1 --> OUT
    S2 --> OUT
    R1 --> OUT
    R2 --> OUT

    OUT --> OBS["Observer Eye and CIELAB Measurement"]
```

## 4.3 Decision Block: Choosing a Colorant Class

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Need to Color a Plastic Part"] --> Q1{"Processing Temperature > 260 C?"}
    Q1 -->|"Yes"| Q2{"Outdoor UV Exposure Required?"}
    Q1 -->|"No"| Q3{"Transparent Color Needed?"}

    Q3 -->|"Yes"| D1["Use DYES"]
    Q3 -->|"No"| D2["Use ORGANIC PIGMENTS"]

    Q2 -->|"Yes"| Q4{"Special Optical Effect Needed?"}
    Q2 -->|"No"| D3["Use INORGANIC PIGMENTS"]

    Q4 -->|"Yes"| D4["Use SPECIAL EFFECT PIGMENTS"]
    Q4 -->|"No"| D5["Use HIGH PERFORMANCE ORGANICS like Phthalocyanine or Quinacridone"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Differentiate between Dyes and Pigments with suitable examples.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Dyes | Pigments |
|---|---|---|
| Solubility in polymer | Dissolve completely | Remain as discrete particles |
| Optical effect | Transparent colors | Opaque / semi-opaque |
| Migration resistance | Poor | Good to excellent |
| Heat stability | Limited | Generally high |
| Examples | Solvent Red 111, Disperse Blue 14 | TiO₂, Phthalocyanine Blue, Iron Oxide Red |

Dyes are molecular colorants that **dissolve** in the polymer melt and provide transparent, vibrant shades, but suffer from poor migration resistance. Pigments are **insoluble particles** that disperse mechanically and provide opaque colors with excellent durability.

---

### **Q2. Classify colorants used in plastics with a neat flowchart.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Colorants for plastics are classified into two main families:
1. **Dyes** – Soluble colorants (Solvent dyes, Disperse dyes, Anthraquinone dyes).
2. **Pigments** – Insoluble colorants, sub-classified into:
   - **Organic Pigments** – Azo, Phthalocyanine, Quinacridone, Anthraquinone.
   - **Inorganic Pigments** – Metal oxides (TiO₂, Fe₂O₃), Sulfides (CdS), Carbon black, Ultramarines.
   - **Special Effect Pigments** – Pearlescent, Metallic, Fluorescent, Phosphorescent, Thermochromic.

(Refer to the classification flowchart in Section 4.1.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**"Explain in detail the classification of colorants used in plastics. Discuss organic pigments with examples and their characteristic properties."**

#### **Part (a) – Classification of Colorants [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Colorants used in plastic decoration are classified based on their **solubility in the polymer matrix** and **chemical nature**.

**1. Dyes (Soluble Colorants):**
- Dissolve at the molecular level in the polymer melt.
- Produce transparent, brilliant colors.
- Examples: Solvent Red 111, Disperse Blue 14, Anthraquinone-based dyes.
- **Limitation:** Poor migration resistance — bleed under heat or solvent contact.

**2. Pigments (Insoluble Colorants):**
Exist as discrete particles dispersed mechanically in the polymer. Sub-classified as:

**(a) Organic Pigments** – Carbon-based molecular structures:
- *Azo pigments*: Contain –N=N– chromophore. Bright colors, low cost, moderate heat stability. Example: **Pigment Red 3 (Toluidine Red)**.
- *Phthalocyanines*: Excellent thermal, chemical, and light stability. Examples: **Pigment Blue 15 (Phthalocyanine Blue)**, **Pigment Green 7**.
- *Quinacridones*: Premium light and weather fastness. Example: **Pigment Violet 19**.
- *Anthraquinones*: Used in engineering plastics, high heat stability.

**(b) Inorganic Pigments** – Metal compounds:
- *Metal oxides*: **TiO₂** (white), **Fe₂O₃** (red/yellow), **Cr₂O₃** (green).
- *Sulfides/Selenides*: **CdS** (yellow), **CdSe** (red).
- *Carbon black*: UV stabilizer + black colorant.
- *Ultramarines*: Alumino-silicate complex, blue/violet.

**(c) Special Effect Pigments**:
- *Pearlescent*: Mica coated with TiO₂/Fe₂O₃.
- *Metallic*: Aluminum flakes, bronze powder.
- *Fluorescent*: UV-absorbing, emit visible light.
- *Phosphorescent*: Glow-in-dark.
- *Thermochromic/Photochromic*: Color changes with T° or light.

#### **Part (b) – Organic Pigments in Detail [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Organic pigments are **insoluble, carbon-based molecular colorants** that provide bright, saturated colors with moderate to high performance characteristics. Their properties depend on the **chromophore** (color-bearing group) and the **molecular packing** in the solid state.

**Key Classes:**

**(i) Azo Pigments (–N=N– linkage):**
- Largest family of organic pigments by volume.
- Examples: **Pigment Red 3 (Toluidine Red)**, **Pigment Yellow 12**, **Pigment Red 48:2 (BON Red)**.
- Properties: Bright shades, low cost, moderate heat stability (180–250 °C), moderate light fastness (4–6 on ISO scale).
- Limitation: Can undergo **thermal decomposition** above 250 °C; not suitable for engineering plastics.

**(ii) Phthalocyanine Pigments:**
- Based on the **phthalocyanine macrocycle** (isoindole units with central metal atom, usually copper).
- Examples: **Pigment Blue 15 (Phthalocyanine Blue)**, **Pigment Green 7 (Phthalocyanine Green)**.
- Properties: Exceptional heat stability (up to 300 °C), excellent light fastness (7–8), chemical inertness.
- Application: Widely used in **PVC, PE, PP, ABS**, and automotive coatings.

**(iii) Quinacridone Pigments:**
- Based on **quinacridone** ring system.
- Examples: **Pigment Violet 19**, **Pigment Red 122**.
- Properties: Outstanding light and weather fastness (7–8), high heat stability, brilliant shades from red to violet.
- Application: **Automotive finishes, outdoor plastics, fibers**.

**(iv) Anthraquinone Pigments:**
- Based on **anthraquinone** ring structure.
- Examples: **Pigment Red 177**, **Pigment Blue 60**.
- Properties: Very high heat stability, excellent chemical resistance, expensive.
- Application: **Engineering plastics (PA, PC, PPS)**, high-temperature applications.

**Comparison of Properties:**

| Class | Heat Stability | Light Fastness | Cost | Brilliance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Azo | Moderate | 4–6 | Low | High |
| Phthalocyanine | Excellent | 7–8 | Medium | High |
| Quinacridone | Excellent | 7–8 | High | Very High |
| Anthraquinone | Excellent | 7–8 | Very High | High |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **chromophore** of azo pigments (–N=N–) with that of dyes. Remember: the azo group can exist in *both* dye and pigment forms — it is the **solubility and polymeric form** that classifies them, not the chromophore.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**"Discuss in detail the classification of inorganic pigments and special effect pigments used in plastics. Compare their properties and applications."**

#### **Part (a) – Inorganic Pigments [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Inorganic pigments are **insoluble, metal-based compounds** that provide superior thermal stability, chemical resistance, and opacity compared to organic pigments. They are typically used in engineering plastics, outdoor applications, and large-volume commodity polymers.

**1. Metal Oxides:**
- **Titanium Dioxide (TiO₂):** The most widely used white pigment. Exists in *rutile* (more stable, higher refractive index 2.7) and *anatase* forms. Provides opacity by scattering light. Used in **PE, PP, PVC, PS** masterbatches.
- **Iron Oxides (Fe₂O₃, Fe₃O₄, FeOOH):** Red, yellow, brown, and black shades. Excellent UV stability, low cost, non-toxic. Used in **PVC pipes, automotive parts, construction plastics**.
- **Chromium Oxide (Cr₂O₃):** Green pigment with outstanding heat and chemical resistance. Used in **engineering plastics and high-temperature applications**.

**2. Sulfides and Selenides:**
- **Cadmium Sulfide (CdS):** Bright yellow, excellent heat stability.
- **Cadmium Selenide (CdSe):** Bright red.
- **Cadmium Sulfoselenide solid solutions:** Yellow → Orange → Red shades.
- **Limitation:** Toxicity concerns restrict use in food-contact and toy applications; being phased out under **RoHS** and **REACH** regulations.

**3. Chromates:**
- **Lead Chromate (PbCrO₄):** Bright yellow, but **toxic** — restricted.
- **Lead Molybdate:** Orange-red shade.
- Replaced by **bismuth vanadate** and **cerium sulfide** in modern eco-friendly formulations.

**4. Carbon Black:**
- **Elemental carbon** produced by incomplete combustion of hydrocarbons.
- Particle size 10–500 nm. Provides deep black color, excellent **UV protection** (acts as UV absorber).
- Used in **PE, PP, PS, rubber, and cable sheathing**.

**5. Ultramarines:**
- Complex **alumino-silicate** (Na₈Al₆Si₆O₂₄S₂) lattice.
- Brilliant blue to violet shades. Sensitive to acids (release H₂S).
- Used in **decorative plastics, toys, and cosmetics containers**.

**6. Mixed Metal Oxides (MMO):**
- Spinels, rutiles, and inverse spinels.
- Examples: **Cobalt aluminate (CoAl₂O₄) — blue**, **Chrome–tin pink**, **Nickel rutile yellow**.
- Exceptional heat and chemical stability, used in **ceramic-like plastics and high-temperature polymers**.

#### **Part (b) – Special Effect Pigments [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Special effect pigments are functional colorants that create **optical effects** beyond simple color — including luster, sparkle, glow, and color-shifting phenomena.

**1. Pearlescent / Interference Pigments:**
- Structure: **Mica flakes coated with TiO₂ or Fe₂O₃** layers.
- Mechanism: Multiple reflections at coating-layer interfaces produce **interference colors** (similar to soap bubbles and butterfly wings).
- Effect: Silky, lustrous appearance with depth.
- Examples: **Iriodin**, **Timiron** (commercial grades).
- Application: **Cosmetics packaging, automotive interiors, decorative panels**.

**2. Metallic Pigments:**
- **Aluminum flakes** (silver), **bronze powder** (gold/copper), **zinc flakes**.
- Provide mirror-like metallic finish.
- Application: **Decorative trims, household appliances, luxury packaging**.
- **Safety Note:** Must be properly dispersed to avoid dust explosion hazards.

**3. Fluorescent Pigments:**
- Absorb **UV/short-wavelength light** and re-emit as **visible light** at longer wavelengths.
- Appear brighter than ordinary colors under daylight.
- Examples: **Rhodamine-based**, **benzoxanthene**, **coumarin derivatives**.
- Application: **Safety signs, toys, advertising displays, safety equipment**.

**4. Phosphorescent Pigments:**
- Based on **alkaline earth aluminates** (e.g., SrAl₂O₄:Eu,Dy) and **zinc sulfides**.
- Absorb light energy and release it slowly as visible glow for hours.
- **Strontium aluminate** pigments: Up to 10× brighter and 10× longer afterglow than ZnS.
- Application: **Emergency exit signs, watch dials, novelty items**.

**5. Thermochromic Pigments:**
- **Color changes with temperature** due to leuco dye–developer–solvent systems encapsulated in microcapsules.
- Used in **temperature indicators, mood rings, smart packaging, medical devices**.

**6. Photochromic Pigments:**
- Change color upon **UV exposure** (e.g., silver halide or spiropyran chemistry).
- Used in **UV-indicating plastics, novelty eyewear, security printing**.

**7. Color-Shifting / Optically Variable Pigments (OVP):**
- Multi-layer interference films that shift hue with viewing angle.
- Application: **Anti-counterfeit labels, premium packaging**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often describe the *appearance* of special effect pigments (e.g., "they look shiny") but fail to mention the **physical mechanism** (interference, fluorescence, phosphorescence). Always explain the **cause → effect** relationship to score full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 – Colorant Classification**

- ✅ **Two main families:** **Dyes (soluble)** vs **Pigments (insoluble)**.
- ✅ **Pigments** are sub-classified into **Organic**, **Inorganic**, and **Special Effect**.
- ✅ **Azo pigments** → –N=N– chromophore → bright, cheap, low heat stability.
- ✅ **Phthalocyanines** → macrocyclic → exceptional heat and light stability.
- ✅ **Quinacridones & Anthraquinones** → high-performance, used in engineering plastics.
- ✅ **TiO₂** is the most important **inorganic white pigment** (rutile form preferred).
- ✅ **Iron oxides** are the most common **inorganic color pigments** (red, yellow, brown, black).
- ✅ **Carbon black** functions both as a black colorant and as a **UV stabilizer**.
- ✅ **Cadmium and lead chromate pigments** are being phased out due to **toxicity regulations (RoHS, REACH)**.
- ✅ **Pearlescent pigments** work by **thin-film interference** on coated mica flakes.
- ✅ **Fluorescent pigments** absorb UV and emit visible light; **phosphorescent** store and slowly release light.
- ✅ **Kubelka-Munk equation** $\frac{K}{S} = \frac{(1-R_{\infty})^{2}}{2 R_{\infty}}$ governs opacity in pigmented plastics.
- ✅ **CIELAB color difference** $\Delta E^{*}$ is the standard metric for color change evaluation.
- ✅ For processing temperatures above **260 °C**, prefer **inorganic** or **high-performance organic pigments** over dyes.
- ✅ **Migration resistance** ranking: **Dyes < Organic Pigments < Inorganic Pigments**.
- ✅ **FDA, REACH, RoHS** compliance is mandatory for food-contact, toy, and consumer plastic applications.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pigment Dispersion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT864 - PLASTICS & DECORATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pigment Dispersion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Pigment Dispersion** is the fundamental *unit operation* in plastics decoration and masterbatch compounding wherein dry pigment agglomerates (clusters of primary particles held together by cohesive forces) are systematically broken down, individually wetted by a liquid polymer carrier (resin/plasticizer system), and uniformly distributed throughout a polymer matrix to form a stable, homogeneous, and reproducible colored compound.

In KTU 2024 scheme terms, pigment dispersion is described as a **three-stage sequential process**:

1. **Wetting** – Replacement of air/solid interfaces by liquid/solid interfaces.
2. **Mechanical De-agglomeration (Dispersion proper)** – Breaking down of agglomerates into primary particles via shear forces.
3. **Stabilization (Flocculation Inhibition)** – Prevention of re-agglomeration of dispersed particles through electrostatic, steric, or electrosteric mechanisms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Pigment dispersion is *not* merely "mixing of color powder." True dispersion requires primary-particle-level separation. A "poorly dispersed" pigment may have the correct color strength in theory but appear streaky, speckled, or weak in practice.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine preparing a glass of **Horlicks/Ovaltine** in cold milk:

- **Dry pigment agglomerates** = the dry Horlicks powder lumps/clumps when you open the jar.
- **Wetting** = pouring a small amount of milk onto the powder; the milk "creeps" into the clumps and drives out the trapped air (visible as fizz).
- **De-agglomeration** = stirring vigorously with a spoon; the clumps physically break down into fine particles.
- **Stabilization** = if you stop stirring, the fine particles slowly settle to the bottom. A "stabilizer" (analogous to a *dispersant*) coats each particle and keeps it floating evenly — so the drink stays uniform.

In plastics decoration, the "milk" is the polymer melt (PVC plastisol, polypropylene melt, etc.), and the "Horlicks" is the dry pigment powder. The "spoon" is your dispersing equipment (three-roll mill, bead mill, high-speed impeller, twin-screw extruder).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in plastics?**
> - Color strength depends directly on how many primary particles are exposed to light. Poor dispersion = wasted pigment = dull color.
> - Undispersed agglomerates act as *stress concentrators*, weakening the finished plastic article.
> - Surface defects ("specks", "fish-eyes", "die-lines") in extrusion and film blowing are traceable to poor dispersion in over 80% of plant cases.

---

## 1.3 Critical Metrics & Standard References

| Metric | Standard Symbol | Typical Value | Importance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary particle size | $d_{p}$ | **0.01 – 10 µm** | Determines color strength & opacity |
| Agglomerate size | $d_{a}$ | **10 – 200 µm** | Must be reduced during processing |
| Specific surface area | $S_{BET}$ | **5 – 100 m²/g** | Governs dispersant demand |
| Zeta potential | $\zeta$ | **± 30 to ± 50 mV** | Indicator of colloidal stability |
| Hegman gauge reading | NS (N-S) | **< 10 µm = NS 7+** | Quality control in paints/plastisols |
| Critical Pigment Volume Concentration | CPVC | **15 – 60 %** | Mechanical-optical transition point |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold Constants to Remember for KTU 2024 Exams:**
> - **Reynolds Number** for transition in agitated vessels: $\text{Re} = 10^4$
> - **Hegman "fineness-of-grind" scale:** 0 (100 µm) to 8 (0 µm) — *higher = finer*.
> - **Critical Zeta Potential** for electrostatic stability: $\vert \zeta \vert \geq 30 \text{ mV}$

---

## 1.4 The Three Sequential Stages — Quick Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Any long-answer question on "Explain pigment dispersion" expects you to label all THREE stages clearly. Skipping a stage = losing 3–4 marks easily.

```text
STAGE 1 (Wetting)          STAGE 2 (DE-AGGLOMERATION)     STAGE 3 (STABILIZATION)
Air/Pigment  →  Liquid/Pigment      Agglomerate  →  Primary Particles    Preventing re-flocculation
  ↓ ↓ ↓              ↓ ↓ ↓                ↓ ↓ ↓                  ↓ ↓ ↓
Surface tension  Interfacial chemistry  Shear stress      Electrostatic/Steric
```

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stage 1 — Wetting: The Thermodynamic Foundation

Wetting is governed by the classical **Young's Equation** for the contact angle $\theta$ between a liquid drop and a solid pigment surface:

$$\theta = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\gamma_{SG} - \gamma_{SL}}{\gamma_{LG}}\right)$$

Where:
- $\gamma_{SG}$ = Solid–Gas interfacial tension (mN/m)
- $\gamma_{SL}$ = Solid–Liquid interfacial tension (mN/m)
- $\gamma_{LG}$ = Liquid–Gas interfacial tension (mN/m)

### Wetting Logic — Step by Step:

1. **Contact Angle Test:** If $\theta \to 0°$, the liquid *completely wets* the pigment. If $\theta \to 180°$, the pigment is *non-wettable*.
2. **Work of Adhesion ($W_a$):** Energy required to separate liquid from solid:
   
$$W_a = \gamma_{LG}(1 + \cos\theta)$$

3. **Work of Immersion ($W_i$):** Energy released when a dry solid is fully immersed in a liquid:
   
$$W_i = \gamma_{LG}\cos\theta$$

4. **Spreading Coefficient ($S$):** Determines if liquid will spontaneously spread:
