$$S = \gamma_{SG} - (\gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG})$$

If $S > 0$, spontaneous wetting occurs; if $S < 0$, mechanical agitation is required.

### Real-World Utility:
In masterbatch manufacturing, when carbon black (highly agglomerated, low surface energy) is added to a polymer melt, the molten polymer must overcome the high $\gamma_{SG}$. This is why a **wetting agent** (e.g., a low-molecular-weight wax or a surfactant) is added — it lowers $\gamma_{LG}$ and $\gamma_{SL}$ to drive spontaneous wetting.

---

## 2.2 Stage 2 — Mechanical De-agglomeration (Dispersion Proper)

The **energy required** to break an agglomerate of radius $r$ into primary particles is approximated by:

$$E_{disp} \propto \frac{\gamma_{SL}}{r}$$

Smaller agglomerates require disproportionately higher energy — this is why *bead mills* and *three-roll mills* are needed for sub-micron pigments.

### 2.2.1 Stokes' Law for Sedimentation Velocity (Critical for "Stability" Calculations)

In a quiescent polymer melt or plastisol, the terminal settling velocity $v_t$ of a pigment particle is:

$$v_t = \frac{(\rho_p - \rho_m)\,g\,d_p^{\,2}}{18\,\eta}$$

Where:
- $\rho_p$ = pigment particle density (kg/m³) — typically $\mathbf{4000 \text{ to } 5500 \text{ kg/m}^3}$ for inorganic pigments
- $\rho_m$ = medium density (kg/m³)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration $= 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $d_p$ = particle diameter (m)
- $\eta$ = medium viscosity (Pa·s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** For PVC plastisols ($\eta \approx 1\text{–}5 \text{ Pa·s}$), a 10 µm pigment particle takes **hours to settle** — making liquid plastisols inherently metastable. For low-viscosity injection-molding melts ($\eta \approx 10^2\text{–}10^3 \text{ Pa·s}$), sedimentation is essentially frozen.

### 2.2.2 Reynolds Number for Agitated Vessels

$$Re = \frac{\rho\,N\,D_i^{\,2}}{\eta}$$

Where $N$ = impeller speed (rev/s), $D_i$ = impeller diameter (m).

- $Re < 10$: laminar flow (poor dispersion)
- $Re = 10\text{–}10^4$: transitional flow
- $Re > 10^4$: turbulent flow (**good for de-agglomeration**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb:** Impeller tip speed must exceed **20 m/s** for adequate de-agglomeration in high-speed dispersers (HSDs).

---

## 2.3 Stage 3 — Stabilization Mechanisms

### 2.3.1 Electrostatic Stabilization (DLVO Theory)

The total inter-particle potential energy $V_T$ is the sum of attractive van der Waals ($V_A$) and repulsive electrostatic ($V_R$) potentials:

$$V_T = V_A + V_R$$

For two identical spheres of radius $r$ at separation $H$:

$$V_A \approx -\frac{A_{H}\,r}{12\,H}$$

$$V_R \approx 2\pi\,\varepsilon_r\,\varepsilon_0\,r\,\zeta^{\,2}\,\ln\!\left(1 + e^{-\kappa H}\right)$$

Where:
- $A_H$ = Hamaker constant (typical for pigments in polymers: $\mathbf{10^{-20} \text{ to } 10^{-19} \text{ J}}$)
- $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity
- $\varepsilon_0$ = vacuum permittivity $= 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$
- $\zeta$ = zeta potential (V)
- $\kappa^{-1}$ = Debye length (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Criterion:** $\vert \zeta \vert \geq 30 \text{ mV}$ is generally required for electrostatic stability. Polymer melts (low $\varepsilon_r$) cannot sustain this → hence **steric stabilization** dominates in plastics.

### 2.3.2 Steric Stabilization

A polymeric dispersant (e.g., a graft copolymer with pigment-anchoring groups and chain-soluble tails) adsorbs onto the particle. When two such coated particles approach, the polymer chains overlap, producing an osmotic/entropic repulsion. The **critical stabilization condition** is:

$$\frac{V_{steric}}{k_B T} \geq 5 \quad \text{(rule of thumb for stable dispersion)}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Table)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Young's contact angle | $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\gamma_{SG}-\gamma_{SL}}{\gamma_{LG}}$ | degrees; $\theta<90°$ = wettable |
| 2 | Work of adhesion | $W_a = \gamma_{LG}(1+\cos\theta)$ | mJ/m² |
| 3 | Work of immersion | $W_i = \gamma_{LG}\cos\theta$ | mJ/m² |
| 4 | Spreading coefficient | $S = \gamma_{SG}-\gamma_{SL}-\gamma_{LG}$ | mN/m; $S>0$ = spreads |
| 5 | Dispersion energy | $E_{disp} \propto \dfrac{\gamma_{SL}}{r}$ | J; smaller $r$ needs more $E$ |
| 6 | Stokes' settling velocity | $v_t = \dfrac{(\rho_p-\rho_m)\,g\,d_p^{\,2}}{18\,\eta}$ | m/s; valid for $Re<0.2$ |
| 7 | Reynolds number (impeller) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho\,N\,D_i^{\,2}}{\eta}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Van der Waals attraction | $V_A = -\dfrac{A_H\,r}{12\,H}$ | J; $A_H \sim 10^{-20}\text{–}10^{-19}$ J |
| 9 | Electrostatic repulsion | $V_R \approx 2\pi\varepsilon_r\varepsilon_0 r \zeta^2 \ln(1+e^{-\kappa H})$ | J |
| 10 | Critical zeta potential | $\vert\zeta\vert \geq 30 \text{ mV}$ | mV |
| 11 | CPVC | $\text{CPVC} = \dfrac{V_p}{V_p+V_b}\times 100\%$ | %; $V_b$ = binder volume |
| 12 | Hegman fineness | $1 \text{ NS unit} \approx 10\,\mu\text{m}$ | NS scale 0 → 8 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Pitfall:** When writing Stokes' equation, always specify the validity range $Re < 0.2$ — otherwise your derivation will be marked incomplete.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Application of Pigment Dispersion |
|---|---|
| **PVC plastisol flooring** | Homogeneous color, no specking |
| **Masterbatch compounding** | Color consistency across batches |
| **Inkjet & gravure printing on films** | Sub-micron pigment dispersion prevents nozzle clogging |
| **Automotive coatings (OEM)** | Metallic/pearlescent effect requires excellent orientation + dispersion |
| **Food-contact plastics** | Strict limits on heavy-metal pigments → dispersion efficiency must be high |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Stokes' Sedimentation Velocity (KTU Favourite)

**Problem Setup:** A spherical pigment particle of diameter $d_p$ and density $\rho_p$ settles under gravity through a Newtonian liquid of viscosity $\eta$ and density $\rho_m$. Derive an expression for its terminal velocity $v_t$.

### Step 1: Force Balance on the Particle

Three forces act on a settling particle:
1. Gravitational force (downward): $F_g = m_p\,g = \dfrac{\pi}{6}d_p^{\,3}\,\rho_p\,g$
2. Buoyancy force (upward): $F_b = \dfrac{\pi}{6}d_p^{\,3}\,\rho_m\,g$
3. Drag force (opposing motion, upward): $F_d = 3\pi\,\eta\,d_p\,v$ (Stokes' drag, valid only for $Re < 0.2$)

### Step 2: At Terminal Velocity, Net Force = 0

$$F_g - F_b - F_d = 0$$

$$\frac{\pi}{6}d_p^{\,3}\,\rho_p\,g - \frac{\pi}{6}d_p^{\,3}\,\rho_m\,g - 3\pi\,\eta\,d_p\,v_t = 0$$

### Step 3: Algebraic Simplification

Factor out $\dfrac{\pi}{6}d_p^{\,3}\,g$ from the first two terms:

$$\frac{\pi}{6}d_p^{\,3}\,g\,(\rho_p - \rho_m) = 3\pi\,\eta\,d_p\,v_t$$

### Step 4: Solve for $v_t$

Divide both sides by $3\pi\,\eta\,d_p$:

$$v_t = \frac{\dfrac{\pi}{6}d_p^{\,3}\,g\,(\rho_p - \rho_m)}{3\pi\,\eta\,d_p}$$

$$v_t = \frac{d_p^{\,2}\,g\,(\rho_p - \rho_m)}{18\,\eta}$$

This is **Stokes' Law**, the cornerstone equation of colloidal stability.

### Step 5: Validity Check — Reynolds Number

For Stokes' Law to be valid:

$$Re_{particle} = \frac{\rho_m\,v_t\,d_p}{\eta} < 0.2$$

### Step 6: Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A TiO₂ particle ($d_p = 5\,\mu\text{m}$, $\rho_p = 4200\,\text{ kg/m}^3$) settles through a PVC plastisol ($\rho_m = 1100\,\text{ kg/m}^3$, $\eta = 2.5\,\text{ Pa·s}$). Calculate the terminal settling velocity and check validity of Stokes' Law.

Given:
- $d_p = 5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$
- $\rho_p = 4200\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $\rho_m = 1100\,\text{kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$, $\eta = 2.5\,\text{Pa·s}$

Plugging in:

$$\Delta\rho = 4200 - 1100 = 3100\,\text{kg/m}^3$$

$$v_t = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times 9.81 \times 3100}{18 \times 2.5}$$

$$v_t = \frac{(2.5 \times 10^{-11}) \times 9.81 \times 3100}{45}$$

$$v_t = \frac{7.6028 \times 10^{-7}}{45} = 1.689 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{m/s}$$

$$v_t \approx 1.69 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{mm/s} \quad (\approx 0.06\,\text{mm/hr})$$

Validity check:

$$Re = \frac{1100 \times 1.69 \times 10^{-8} \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}{2.5} = 3.72 \times 10^{-11} \ll 0.2 \;\checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** Stokes' Law is valid. The TiO₂ particle takes approximately **17,000 hours (~2 years) to settle 1 cm** in the plastisol — confirming practical metastability.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Critical Pigment Volume Concentration (CPVC)

**Definition:** CPVC is the pigment volume fraction at which the binder (polymer) exactly fills all inter-particle voids without excess.

### Step 1: Volume of Pigment

For $n$ spherical particles of radius $r$:

$$V_p = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 n$$

### Step 2: Packing Geometry

For random close packing of spheres:

$$\phi_{max} \approx 0.64 \quad \text{(maximum solid fraction)}$$

Therefore, void volume fraction at $\phi_{max}$:

$$\phi_{void} = 1 - 0.64 = 0.36$$

### Step 3: CPVC Equation

At CPVC, binder volume exactly equals void volume:

$$\text{CPVC} = \frac{V_p}{V_p + V_b}\times 100\%$$

Substituting $V_b = 0.36\,V_p$:

$$\text{CPVC} = \frac{V_p}{V_p + 0.36\,V_p}\times 100\% = \frac{1}{1.36}\times 100\%$$

$$\text{CPVC} \approx 73.5\%$$

In practice, real pigments are *not* perfect spheres and never reach the theoretical maximum → typical CPVC values are **15–60%** depending on pigment shape, size distribution, and oil absorption.

### Step 4: Significance Table (KTU Exam Gold)

| Property | Below CPVC | Above CPVC |
|---|---|---|
| Gloss | High | Low (matte) |
| Opacity | Lower | Higher |
| Mechanical strength | High | Low (porous film) |
| Permeability | Low | High |
| Corrosion protection | Good | Poor |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Equipment Selection for Dispersion

| Equipment | Shear Type | Viscosity Range | Particle Size Achieved | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| High-Speed Disperser (HSD) | Low–medium | < 5 Pa·s | ~25 µm | Pre-mix stage |
| Sand/Bead Mill | Very high | 0.1 – 5 Pa·s | < 5 µm | Final dispersion |
| Three-Roll Mill | Extreme (gap shear) | 1 – 50 Pa·s | < 1 µm | High-quality inks |
| Twin-Screw Extruder | Continuous, high | Polymer melt | 1 – 10 µm | Masterbatch compounding |
| Ultrasonicator | Cavitational | Low | Sub-micron | Lab/Research |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flowchart — The Three Stages of Pigment Dispersion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["DRY PIGMENT POWDER"]:::startNode --> B["STAGE 1 WETTING"]
    B --> B1["Air displaced by resin/plasticizer"]
    B1 --> B2["Contact angle theta drops below 30 deg"]
    B2 --> C["STAGE 2 DE-AGGLOMERATION"]
    C --> C1["Mechanical shear applied via mill"]
    C1 --> C2["Agglomerates broken to primary particles"]
    C2 --> C3["Hegman gauge reading improves"]
    C3 --> D["STAGE 3 STABILIZATION"]
    D --> D1["Electrostatic repulsion via zeta potential"]
    D --> D2["Steric barrier via dispersant polymer"]
    D --> D3["Electrosteric combined mechanism"]
    D3 --> E["STABLE HOMOGENEOUS DISPERSION"]:::endNode

    classDef startNode fill:#ffeaa7,stroke:#d63031,stroke-width:2px,color:#2d3436
    classDef endNode fill:#55efc4,stroke:#00b894,stroke-width:2px,color:#2d3436
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this:** Open from top (raw pigment), proceed downward through three labeled stages, reach the bottom (stable product). The three colors map to the three KTU-defined stages.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Dispersion Equipment Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INTAKE["RAW MATERIAL INTAKE BAY"]
        direction TB
        raw1["Pigment bags"]:::raw
        raw2["Liquid resin or plasticizer"]:::raw
        raw3["Dispersant surfactant"]:::raw
        raw4["Additives and stabilizers"]:::raw
    end

    subgraph PREMIX["STAGE A PREMIX UNIT"]
        direction TB
        pm1["High Speed Disperser HSD"]:::mach
        pm2["Cowles blade at 1500 RPM"]:::mach
        pm3["Wetting stage output slurry"]:::mach
    end

    subgraph DISPERSE["STAGE B DISPERSION UNIT"]
        direction TB
        dm1["Bead Mill or Sand Mill"]:::mach
        dm2["Three Roll Mill for high viscosity"]:::mach
        dm3["Hegman gauge check NS 7 or better"]:::mach
    end

    subgraph STAB["STAGE C STABILIZATION UNIT"]
        direction TB
        st1["Dispersant dosing tank"]:::mach
        st2["Let down tank with slow stirring"]:::mach
        st3["Zeta potential and viscosity QC"]:::mach
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["FINAL PRODUCT STORAGE"]
        direction TB
        out1["Pumped to packaging or compounding line"]:::finish
    end

    raw1 --> pm1
    raw2 --> pm1
    raw3 --> pm1
    raw4 --> pm1
    pm1 --> pm2
    pm2 --> pm3
    pm3 --> dm1
    pm3 --> dm2
    dm1 --> dm3
    dm2 --> dm3
    dm3 --> st1
    st1 --> st2
    st2 --> st3
    st3 --> out1

    classDef raw fill:#fab1a0,stroke:#e17055,stroke-width:2px,color:#2d3436
    classDef mach fill:#74b9ff,stroke:#0984e3,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef finish fill:#55efc4,stroke:#00b894,stroke-width:2px,color:#2d3436
```

---

## 4.3 DLVO Potential-Energy Curve (Schematic Description)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DISTANCE["SEPARATION DISTANCE H FROM PARTICLE SURFACE"]
        direction LR
        p1["Very close H small"]:::primary
        p2["Primary minimum V_A dominates"]:::trough
        p3["Energy barrier V_max from V_R"]:::peak
        p4["Secondary minimum"]:::trough
        p5["Far away H large"]:::primary
    end

    p1 --> p2
    p2 --> p3
    p3 --> p4
    p4 --> p5

    classDef primary fill:#dfe6e9,stroke:#b2bec3,stroke-width:2px,color:#2d3436
    classDef trough fill:#ff7675,stroke:#d63031,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef peak fill:#fdcb6e,stroke:#e17055,stroke-width:2px,color:#2d3436
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the DLVO Curve:**
> - **Primary minimum (V_A dominates)** → irreversible coagulation if particles fall in here.
> - **Energy barrier (V_max)** → must exceed $\sim 10\,k_B T$ for stable dispersion.
> - **Secondary minimum** → weak, reversible flocculation (often useful to prevent hard settling).
> - Increasing $\vert\zeta\vert$ or $\kappa^{-1}$ **raises the energy barrier**.

---

## 4.4 Schematic Cross-Section — Three-Roll Mill

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ROLLER["THREE ROLL MILL CROSS SECTION"]
        direction LR
        r1["FEED ROLL rotates inward"]:::roll
        r2["CENTER ROLL rotates inward faster"]:::roll
        r3["APRON ROLL rotates inward fastest"]:::roll
        s1["Feed hopper on top"]:::scrap
        s2["Scraper blade on apron"]:::scrap
        s3["Discharge chute for paste"]:::scrap
    end

    s1 --> r1
    r1 --> r2
    r2 --> r3
    r2 --> s2
    r3 --> s3

    classDef roll fill:#a29bfe,stroke:#6c5ce7,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef scrap fill:#ffeaa7,stroke:#fdcb6e,stroke-width:2px,color:#2d3436
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Each roller gap progressively decreases (typically 50 µm → 25 µm → 10 µm). The combination of high pressure + shear in the narrow gap is what enables sub-micron dispersion in high-viscosity ink and paste systems.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define pigment dispersion. List the three stages involved in the dispersion process.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Pigment dispersion is the process of separating and uniformly distributing the primary particles of a pigment throughout a liquid or polymer medium to form a homogeneous, stable, and reproducible colored system.

**Three Stages (2 marks):**
1. **Wetting** — displacement of air from pigment surfaces by the liquid medium.
2. **Mechanical De-agglomeration (Dispersion)** — breaking down of pigment agglomerates into primary particles using shear.
3. **Stabilization** — prevention of re-flocculation via electrostatic, steric, or electrosteric mechanisms.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*What is meant by "Hegman gauge" reading? What is its significance in pigment dispersion?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The Hegman gauge (fineness-of-grind gauge) is a flat steel block containing a tapered groove of known depth, ranging from 100 µm at one end to 0 µm at the other. A small sample of dispersion is drawn down the groove with a scraper, and the point at which particle agglomerates become visible indicates the "fineness" rating.

**Significance (1.5 marks):**
- Provides a quick, plant-floor QC measure of dispersion quality.
- Scale runs from 0 (100 µm) to 8 (0 µm); higher reading = finer dispersion.
- For high-quality inks and coatings, NS ≥ 7 (≤ 10 µm) is typically required.
- A "speck" or "grit" rating of more than NS 5 indicates inadequate dispersion.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Rule:** Each Part B question has internal choice. You must answer EITHER Choice A OR Choice B in full. Marks are split as 7 + 7.

---

### **Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **three stages of pigment dispersion** in detail. Discuss the role of surface tension and contact angle in the **wetting stage**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive **Stokes' law** for the terminal settling velocity of a spherical pigment particle in a liquid medium. State clearly the assumptions and validity range. A TiO₂ particle of diameter $d_p = 8\,\mu\text{m}$ and density $\rho_p = 4200\,\text{kg/m}^3$ is dispersed in a PVC plastisol of density $\rho_m = 1150\,\text{kg/m}^3$ and viscosity $\eta = 3\,\text{Pa·s}$. Calculate the terminal velocity and verify whether Stokes' law applies. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q1(a) — 7 marks:**

**(i) The Three Stages — Diagrammatic Explanation (3 marks):**

**Stage 1: Wetting**
- The polymer/plasticizer liquid displaces air and moisture from the pigment surface.
- Driven by interfacial energetics; characterized by Young's contact angle $\theta$.
- For $\theta < 90°$, partial wetting; for $\theta \to 0°$, complete spontaneous wetting.
- Wetting agents (surfactants, low-MW resins) are used to enhance this stage.

**Stage 2: De-agglomeration (Dispersion Proper)**
- Mechanical shear (from bead mills, three-roll mills, extruders) breaks clusters into primary particles.
- Energy input proportional to $\gamma_{SL}/r$; finer agglomerates need exponentially more energy.
- Quality measured via Hegman gauge (target NS 7+).

**Stage 3: Stabilization**
- Prevents re-agglomeration; uses dispersants producing electrostatic (high $\zeta$), steric (polymer tails), or electrosteric (both) barriers.
- Goal: keep $\vert\zeta\vert \geq 30$ mV or sufficient steric repulsion ($\geq 5 k_B T$).

**(ii) Role of Surface Tension and Contact Angle — 4 marks:**

Young's equation:

$$\cos\theta = \frac{\gamma_{SG} - \gamma_{SL}}{\gamma_{LG}}$$

- $\gamma_{SG}$ (solid-gas surface tension) is the energy to be overcome.
- $\gamma_{SL}$ (solid-liquid interfacial tension) must be minimized.
- $\gamma_{LG}$ (liquid-gas surface tension) drives the liquid to spread.

**Work of adhesion $W_a = \gamma_{LG}(1+\cos\theta)$** — this represents the energy released per unit area when liquid wets solid.

**Spreading coefficient $S = \gamma_{SG} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LG}$** — if $S > 0$, wetting is spontaneous; otherwise, mechanical assistance is needed.

> **Practical Example:** Carbon black has very high $\gamma_{SG}$. Adding a wetting agent lowers $\gamma_{LG}$ from ~70 mN/m to ~30 mN/m, making spontaneous wetting possible.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q1(b) — 7 marks:**

**Derivation (4 marks):** Already shown exhaustively in Section 3.1. Key equation:

$$v_t = \frac{(\rho_p - \rho_m)\,g\,d_p^{\,2}}{18\,\eta}$$

**Assumptions and Validity (1 mark):**
- Particle is rigid, smooth, spherical.
- Liquid is Newtonian and unbounded.
- Settling is slow, laminar ($Re_{particle} < 0.2$).
- No Brownian motion / no particle–particle interactions.

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

Given: $d_p = 8 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$, $\rho_p = 4200\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $\rho_m = 1150\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $\eta = 3\,\text{Pa·s}$, $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$.

$$\Delta\rho = 4200 - 1150 = 3050\,\text{kg/m}^3$$

$$v_t = \frac{3050 \times 9.81 \times (8 \times 10^{-6})^2}{18 \times 3}$$

$$v_t = \frac{3050 \times 9.81 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-11}}{54}$$

$$v_t = \frac{1.916 \times 10^{-6}}{54} = 3.55 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{m/s}$$

**Validity Check:**

$$Re = \frac{1150 \times 3.55 \times 10^{-8} \times 8 \times 10^{-6}}{3} = 1.09 \times 10^{-10} \ll 0.2 \;\checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** Stokes' law is valid; particle settles at $3.55 \times 10^{-8}$ m/s, i.e., approximately **0.13 mm/hr**, confirming excellent metastability of the dispersion.

---

### **Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **DLVO theory** of colloidal stability. With neat sketches, describe how the total interaction energy $V_T$ varies with particle separation $H$, and identify the **role of zeta potential ($\zeta$)** in determining dispersion stability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the working principles, shear characteristics, and typical applications of the following dispersion equipment: **(i) High-Speed Disperser (HSD), (ii) Bead Mill, (iii) Three-Roll Mill.** Which equipment would you select for dispersing a high-viscosity printing ink containing sub-micron organic pigment? Justify your answer. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q2(a) — 7 marks:**

**DLVO Theory (Derjaguin–Landau–Verwey–Overbeek) (3 marks):**

The total inter-particle potential energy is the sum of two opposing contributions:

$$V_T(H) = V_A(H) + V_R(H)$$

Where:
- $V_A$ = attractive van der Waals potential, $V_A \approx -\dfrac{A_H\,r}{12\,H}$
- $V_R$ = repulsive electrostatic potential, $V_R \approx 2\pi\varepsilon_r\varepsilon_0 r \zeta^2 \ln(1+e^{-\kappa H})$

**Variation with $H$ — Sketch Description (2 marks):**
- At very small $H$, $V_A$ dominates → **primary minimum** (irreversible coagulation).
- As $H$ increases, $V_R$ rises → **energy barrier** of height $V_{max}$.
- Beyond $V_{max}$, $V_R$ decays → **secondary minimum** (weak reversible flocculation).
- For large $H$, both potentials → 0.

**Role of Zeta Potential (2 marks):**
- $\zeta$ is the potential at the slipping plane of the electrical double layer surrounding each pigment particle.
- It is directly proportional to $V_R$: a higher $\vert\zeta\vert$ gives a higher energy barrier.
- **Stability criterion:** $\vert\zeta\vert \geq 30\,\text{mV}$ for electrostatic stability.
- Above pH 7 (alkaline) or below IEP (isoelectric point), $\zeta$ becomes more negative for most oxides.
- Polymer melts have low $\varepsilon_r$, hence low electrostatic contribution → **steric stabilization is preferred**.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q2(b) — 7 marks:**

**Comparative Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | High-Speed Disperser (HSD) | Bead Mill | Three-Roll Mill |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | High-velocity impeller creates flow & shear | Grinding beads + agitator create impact + attrition | Counter-rotating rolls with narrow nip gap |
| **Shear Type** | Low to medium, bulk flow | Very high, localized | Extreme, gap-defined |
| **Viscosity Range** | Low (< 5 Pa·s) | Low to medium (0.1–5 Pa·s) | Medium to very high (1–50 Pa·s) |
| **Particle Size Achieved** | ~25 µm (pre-mix only) | < 5 µm (sub-micron possible) | < 1 µm |
| **Throughput** | High (batch) | High (continuous) | Low–medium (batch) |
| **Heat Generation** | Moderate | High (needs cooling) | Low |
| **Typical Application** | Pre-mixing, low-quality paints | Industrial inks, coatings | High-viscosity inks, pastes, cosmetics |

**Selection for High-Viscosity Printing Ink with Sub-Micron Organic Pigment (3 marks):**

**Recommended Choice: Three-Roll Mill** ✅

**Justification:**
1. Three-roll mills deliver the **highest localized shear** in the narrow nip gap (pressure + shear), essential for breaking down sub-micron organic pigment agglomerates.
2. The ink viscosity is high — only the three-roll mill can handle paste-like systems effectively; bead mills are limited to < 5 Pa·s.
3. Organic pigments are friable but tend to re-agglomerate; the three-roll mill's progressive gap reduction (50 → 25 → 10 µm) achieves true primary-particle dispersion.
4. Batch operation allows tight color QC per batch — critical in printing ink formulations where color match is paramount.
5. Bead mill would be a second-best alternative if viscosity could be lowered with solvents.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "mixing" with "dispersion"** — Mixing alone does NOT break agglomerates. Always stress *three stages* in your answer.
>
> 2. **Forgetting validity range of Stokes' law** — Writing the equation without stating $Re < 0.2$ = **−1 to −2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Missing units in numerical problems** — Always write $\mu$m, m/s, m²/s², kg/m³ explicitly. KTU examiners deduct ½ mark per missing unit.
>
> 4. **Stating "stability is provided by van der Waals forces"** — This is the *opposite* of the truth. van der Waals forces cause **attraction/aggregation**, not stability.
>
> 5. **Confusing CPVC with PVC** — CPVC (Critical Pigment Volume Concentration) ≠ PVC (polyvinyl chloride). They are completely different concepts.
>
> 6. **Skipping the diagram in "explain with neat diagram" questions** — Up to **2 marks** can be forfeited for missing diagrams in long-answer questions.
>
> 7. **Not justifying equipment selection** — In Part B equipment-based questions, the **justification carries 2–3 marks**; merely listing the equipment is insufficient.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this checklist for last-minute revision before KTU ESE.**

- [x] Pigment dispersion = **Wetting + De-agglomeration + Stabilization** (always in this order).
- [x] **Young's equation** controls wetting; $\theta < 90°$ = wettable.
- [x] **Spreading coefficient $S > 0$** → spontaneous wetting; otherwise mechanical help needed.
- [x] **Dispersion energy $E \propto \gamma_{SL}/r$** — finer agglomerates need disproportionately more energy.
- [x] **Stokes' law** $v_t = \dfrac{(\rho_p - \rho_m)\,g\,d_p^{\,2}}{18\,\eta}$; valid only for $Re_{particle} < 0.2$.
- [x] **Reynolds number for impeller:** $Re > 10^4$ for turbulent (good) dispersion.
- [x] **DLVO theory:** $V_T = V_A + V_R$; stability when energy barrier $> 10\,k_B T$.
- [x] **Zeta potential $\vert\zeta\vert \geq 30\,\text{mV}$** for electrostatic stability.
- [x] **Steric stabilization dominates** in polymer melts (low dielectric constant).
- [x] **Hegman gauge:** NS 0 (100 µm) → NS 8 (0 µm); target NS ≥ 7 for high quality.
- [x] **CPVC** = pigment vol / (pigment vol + binder vol); affects gloss, opacity, mechanical strength.
- [x] **Equipment hierarchy** (low → high shear): HSD < Bead Mill < Three-Roll Mill < Twin-Screw Extruder.
- [x] **Tip speed ≥ 20 m/s** for effective de-agglomeration in HSDs.
- [x] **Hamaker constant** $A_H \sim 10^{-20}$ to $10^{-19}\,\text{J}$ for pigments in polymers.
- [x] **Dispersant demand** increases with specific surface area $S_{BET}$.
- [x] **Common defects from poor dispersion:** specks, fish-eyes, die-lines, weak color strength, low gloss.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Colour Concentrates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT864 - PLASTICS & DECORATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Colour Concentrates ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Colour Concentrate** (also widely known in the plastics industry as a **Masterbatch** or **Colour Masterbatch**) is a solid or liquid additive compound, formulated as a highly concentrated dispersion of **pigments** (or **dyes**) encapsulated within a **carrier resin** matrix. This concentrate is specifically engineered to be blended (let-down) with a much larger volume of natural (uncoloured) base polymer during plastic processing (injection moulding, blow moulding, extrusion, film blowing, etc.) to impart a uniform, reproducible colour to the final plastic article. In the precise terminology of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for Plastics & Decoration, a colour concentrate is defined as a **compounded mixture of colourants, carriers, dispersants, and process additives**, supplied in pellet, flake, or liquid form, designed for a specific let-down ratio (typically 1% to 5% by weight in the final product). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction — Pigments vs Dyes:** > - **Pigments** are *insoluble*, fine solid particles that are mechanically dispersed in the polymer. They do not dissolve. > - **Dyes** are *soluble* colourants that dissolve at the molecular level in the polymer melt. > - Most masterbatches use **pigments** because they offer superior light-fastness, heat stability, and chemical resistance. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Flavour Stock" Model Imagine you are making a giant industrial batch of soup. You would never sprinkle raw chilli powder, turmeric, and coriander seeds directly into a 1000-litre soup vessel and hope for uniform colour and taste. Instead, a chef first prepares a small, intensely flavoured **"stock concentrate"** — say, a 1-litre jar containing all the spices pre-mixed in oil. When making the soup, the chef only adds **50 ml** of this concentrate to flavour 1 litre of soup perfectly. A **colour concentrate** works exactly the same way: - The **base polymer** = the bulk soup. - The **pigment powder** = the raw spices. - The **carrier resin** = the oil/medium in the concentrate jar. - The **let-down ratio** = the spoonful of concentrate you add to the soup. This pre-dispersion approach solves three major industrial headaches: **dust hazards** (from handling fine pigment powders), **poor colour consistency** (from incomplete dispersion), and **inaccurate dosing** (clumps in raw powder). ### 1.3 Standard Industry Metrics & Reference Data The following benchmarks are the *industry-standard reference values* used globally by masterbatch manufacturers such as **Clariant, Ampacet, Schulman, Cabot, and Avient**: | Parameter | Typical Value | |---|---| | Pigment concentration in masterbatch | **20 % – 50 % by weight** | | Carrier resin content | **40 % – 70 % by weight** | | Dispersant/additive content | **2 % – 10 % by weight** | | Standard let-down ratio | **1 : 25 to 1 : 100** (concentrate : base polymer) | | Final pigment in finished article | **0.1 % – 2 % by weight** | | Particle size of dispersed pigment | **< 5 µm** (target), **< 1 µm** for premium applications | > [!NOTE] > **Why the pigment-to-base ratio matters:** Masterbatch producers aim for the highest possible pigment loading *while maintaining dispersion quality*. A 50 % pigment masterbatch requires sophisticated twin-screw compounding extruders with high shear mixing zones, whereas a 20 % masterbatch can be made on simpler single-screw equipment. ### 1.4 Classification of Colour Concentrates Colour concentrates are classified into **four major categories** based on their physical form and end-use: 1. **Solid Pellet Masterbatch** — the most common form, supplied as cylindrical or oval pellets. Compatible with virtually all thermoplastic processes. 2. **Liquid Masterbatch (Liquid Colour)** — colourants dispersed in a liquid carrier (often a plasticizer or wax). Offers superior dispersion but limited shelf life. 3. **Cube Blend / Dry Blend** — a coarse mixture of pigment, carrier powder, and additives. Lower cost but inferior dispersion uniformity. 4. **Pre-coloured Compound** — fully compounded polymer already containing colour. No let-down needed; the polymer itself is shipped coloured. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Five-Component Architecture of a Colour Concentrate A commercially viable colour masterbatch is never just "pigment + resin". It is a meticulously engineered **five-component system**, where each ingredient fulfils a specific rheological, optical, or processing function: **1. Pigment (or Dye) — The Colour Payload** - Provides the actual chromatic property. - Selected based on **hue, saturation, opacity/transparency, light-fastness, heat stability, chemical resistance**, and **regulatory compliance** (e.g., FDA-approved for food contact). - Common pigment chemistries: **Phthalocyanines** (blues/greens), **Quinacridones** (magentas/reds), **Azo compounds** (yellows/oranges/reds), **Carbon Black** (blacks), **Titanium Dioxide TiO₂** (white/opacifier), **Iron Oxides** (reds/yellows/browns), **Ultramarines** (violets/blues). **2. Carrier Resin — The Pigment Vehicle** - A thermoplastic resin compatible with the base polymer in which the masterbatch will be let down. - Must have a **lower melt viscosity** than the base polymer to act as a "lubricant" that carries pigment particles efficiently into the melt during processing. - Common carriers: **LDPE, LLDPE, EVA, PP, PS, SAN, universal waxes (e.g., polyethylene wax)**. - Selection rule: **Carrier should be chemically and rheologically compatible with the base polymer** to avoid plate-out, die-build-up, or "fish-eyes". **3. Dispersant (Wetting Agent) — The Pigment Coater** - A surface-active compound (often a **fatty acid ester, stearate, or polymeric surfactant**) that coats each pigment particle, reducing its surface energy. - Prevents pigment **agglomeration** (clumping back together after being broken apart). - Improves the **wetting** of pigment by the molten carrier resin. - Critical for achieving particle sizes below **1 µm** consistently. **4. Additives — The Performance Modifiers** - **Heat stabilisers** (e.g., HALS — hindered amine light stabilisers) — protect colour from thermal degradation during processing. - **UV stabilisers** — improve light-fastness of organic pigments. - **Antioxidants** — prevent carrier resin yellowing. - **Antistatic agents** — reduce dust pickup on pellets. - **Processing aids** — improve melt flow during let-down. **5. Optional Fillers & Special Effects** - **Calcium carbonate (CaCO₃)** or **barium sulphate (BaSO₄)** — extenders that reduce cost and adjust opacity. - **Pearlescent pigments** (mica coated with TiO₂) — for pearlescent finishes. - **Metallic flakes** (aluminium, bronze) — for metallic aesthetics. - **Fluorescent pigments** — for high-visibility applications. > [!NOTE] > **The Universal Carrier Concept:** Modern masterbatch producers increasingly use **"universal carriers"** — proprietary blends (often wax-EVA copolymer based) compatible with a wide range of polymers (PE, PP, PS, ABS, PA). These reduce inventory complexity for processors who work with multiple resins. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet The following table consolidates every quantitative relationship a KTU 2024 examiner can test on this topic. **Memorise this table — it is the heart of numerical questions.** | # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Variables & Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Let-Down Ratio (LDR)** | $\text{LDR} = \dfrac{m_{\text{masterbatch}}}{m_{\text{base polymer}}}$ | $m$ = mass (g or kg); dimensionless ratio | | 2 | **Pigment Loading in Masterbatch** | $P_{\text{mb}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{pigment}}}{m_{\text{total masterbatch}}} \times 100$ | $P$ = pigment content in % (w/w) | | 3 | **Final Pigment Concentration in Article** | $P_{\text{final}} = P_{\text{mb}} \times \text{LDR}_{\text{fraction}}$ | $P_{\text{final}}$ in % (w/w) | | 4 | **Mass of Masterbatch Required** | $m_{\text{mb}} = m_{\text{batch}} \times \dfrac{\text{LDR}_{\%}}{100}$ | $m$ in kg | | 5 | **Pigment Mass Required** | $m_{\text{pigment}} = m_{\text{batch}} \times \dfrac{P_{\text{final}}}{100}$ | $m$ in kg | | 6 | **Required Masterbatch Pigment %** (reverse) | $P_{\text{mb}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{final}}}{\text{LDR}_{\text{fraction}}}$ | Result in % | | 7 | **Colour Strength (Tint)** | $S \propto \dfrac{1}{d^2}$ (for spherical particles) | $d$ = pigment particle diameter | | 8 | **Opacity (Contrast Ratio)** | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{R_{\text{black}}}{R_{\text{white}}}$ | $R$ = reflectance over black/white substrate | | 9 | **Cost per kg of Coloured Article** | $C_{\text{coloured}} = C_{\text{base}} + C_{\text{mb}} \times \text{LDR}_{\text{fraction}}$ | $C$ = cost per kg in ₹/$ | > [!WARNING] > **Critical Pitfall:** The **LDR is a ratio, NOT a percentage**. A 4 % let-down means **4 g masterbatch per 100 g base polymer**, i.e., a 1 : 25 ratio. Students frequently confuse "4 %" with "1 : 4". Always write the fraction explicitly when solving problems. ### 2.3 Operational Workflow — From Raw Pigment to Finished Article The industrial flow of colour concentrate usage proceeds through **six sequential stages**, each governed by precise quality controls: **Stage 1 — Pigment Pre-Treatment:** Pigment powders are received and tested for particle size, moisture content, and chemical purity. Excess moisture is removed by drying (especially critical for hygroscopic pigments like quinacridones). **Stage 2 — Pre-Mixing / Pre-Dispersion:** Pigment, carrier resin, dispersant, and additives are weighed and blended in a high-speed mixer (e.g., Henschel mixer) at ambient temperature. This produces a homogeneous dry blend. **Stage 3 — Melt Compounding (Extrusion):** The dry blend is fed into a **co-rotating twin-screw extruder** (most common) or single-screw extruder. The extruder zones are temperature-controlled to melt the carrier, wet the pigment, and disperse it under high shear. Screw L/D ratios typically range from **20:1 to 44:1**. **Stage 4 — Filtration:** The molten compound passes through a **screen pack** (typically 200–400 mesh) to remove undispersed agglomerates and metal contaminants, ensuring a clean product. **Stage 5 — Pelletisation:** The extruded strand is cooled in a water bath or air-cooled belt, then cut into uniform pellets by a strand cutter or underwater pelletiser. Pellet geometry: cylindrical, ~3 mm diameter, ~3 mm length. **Stage 6 — Quality Control & Let-Down:** Pellets undergo testing (MFI, colour strength, filter pressure value — FPV, dispersion rating). Approved batches are bagged (typically 25 kg) and shipped to processors who let them down with base polymer during moulding/extrusion. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The colour concentrate technology is the **backbone of the entire plastics colouring industry**, with global market value exceeding **USD 12 billion** (2024 estimate). It enables: - **Cost-effective inventory management** — a processor stocks 1 colour masterbatch instead of 50 pre-coloured polymers. - **Rapid colour changeovers** on production lines (minutes vs. hours). - **Regulatory compliance** — masterbatch formulations can be certified food-contact safe (EU Regulation 10/2011, FDA 21 CFR), medical-grade, or toy-safe (EN 71-3) more easily than pre-coloured compounds. - **Decorative differentiation** — automotive interiors (Pearlescent blacks, metallic greys), consumer electronics (Pantone-matched housings), packaging (white-opaque films), textiles (PP non-wovens). ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pigment Mass Balance Across the Let-Down Step This is the **fundamental mass-balance derivation** a KTU examiner will test. We must rigorously derive how the pigment concentration transforms from the masterbatch into the finished article. **Given System:** Let $m_{\text{mb}}$ = mass of colour masterbatch added to the production batch. Let $m_{\text{base}}$ = mass of natural base polymer. Let $P_{\text{mb}}$ = pigment weight fraction in the masterbatch (e.g., 0.40 for 40 %). Let $P_{\text{final}}$ = pigment weight fraction in the final coloured article (e.g., 0.008 for 0.8 %). **Step 1 — Express pigment mass contained in the masterbatch dose.** Since the masterbatch is $P_{\text{mb}}$ fraction pigment by weight, the mass of pure pigment entering the system is: $$m_{\text{pigment}} = P_{\text{mb}} \times m_{\text{mb}}$$ **Step 2 — Express total mass of the final coloured article.** After let-down and processing, the total mass of the article equals the sum of base polymer and masterbatch (assuming negligible volatiles are lost, which is the standard assumption for thermoplastic masterbatches): $$m_{\text{total}} = m_{\text{base}} + m_{\text{mb}}$$ **Step 3 — Equate pigment mass to total mass times final pigment fraction.** The pigment mass is conserved (no chemical reaction consumes the pigment). Therefore: $$\begin{aligned} m_{\text{pigment}} &= P_{\text{final}} \times m_{\text{total}} \\ P_{\text{mb}} \times m_{\text{mb}} &= P_{\text{final}} \times (m_{\text{base}} + m_{\text{mb}}) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for the let-down ratio.** Rearranging and dividing both sides by $m_{\text{base}}$: $$\begin{aligned} P_{\text{mb}} \times m_{\text{mb}} &= P_{\text{final}} \times m_{\text{base}} + P_{\text{final}} \times m_{\text{mb}} \\ (P_{\text{mb}} - P_{\text{final}}) \times m_{\text{mb}} &= P_{\text{final}} \times m_{\text{base}} \\ \dfrac{m_{\text{mb}}}{m_{\text{base}}} &= \dfrac{P_{\text{final}}}{P_{\text{mb}} - P_{\text{final}}} \end{aligned}$$ This gives us the **fundamental governing equation** for masterbatch let-down: $$\boxed{\text{LDR} = \dfrac{m_{\text{mb}}}{m_{\text{base}}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{final}}}{P_{\text{mb}} - P_{\text{final}}}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** This equation proves a non-intuitive fact — the let-down ratio is **NOT equal to the inverse of the masterbatch pigment concentration**. Because $P_{\text{final}}$ is also in the denominator, the actual LDR is slightly larger than $1/P_{\text{mb}}$. For example, a 50 % masterbatch used to achieve 1 % final colour requires $\text{LDR} = 0.01 / 0.49 \approx 0.0204$ or **2.04 %**, not exactly 2.00 %. --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — Masterbatch Mass Calculation **Problem Statement [KTU Model — Part B Style]:** A processor wants to produce **500 kg** of coloured polypropylene articles at a final pigment concentration of **1.0 % w/w**. They are using a masterbatch with **40 % pigment loading**. Calculate: (a) The mass of masterbatch required. (b) The mass of base (natural) polymer required. (c) Verify the let-down ratio. **Solution:** **(a) Mass of masterbatch:** The total pigment needed in the final batch is: $$m_{\text{pigment}} = m_{\text{batch}} \times \dfrac{P_{\text{final}}}{100} = 500 \times \dfrac{1.0}{100} = 5.0 \text{ kg}$$ Since the masterbatch is 40 % pigment, the mass of masterbatch required is: $$m_{\text{mb}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{pigment}}}{P_{\text{mb}}} = \dfrac{5.0}{0.40} = 12.5 \text{ kg}$$ **(b) Mass of base polymer:** $$m_{\text{base}} = m_{\text{batch}} - m_{\text{mb}} = 500 - 12.5 = 487.5 \text{ kg}$$ **(c) Verification of let-down ratio:** $$\text{LDR} = \dfrac{m_{\text{mb}}}{m_{\text{base}}} = \dfrac{12.5}{487.5} = 0.02564 = 2.564 \text{ \%}$$ Cross-check using the derived formula: $$\text{LDR} = \dfrac{P_{\text{final}}}{P_{\text{mb}} - P_{\text{final}}} = \dfrac{0.01}{0.40 - 0.01} = \dfrac{0.01}{0.39} = 0.02564 \text{ ✓}$$ --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — Reverse Calculation **Problem Statement [KTU Model]:** A moulder is adding a masterbatch at a let-down of **3 %** (i.e., 3 kg masterbatch per 97 kg base). The masterbatch contains **35 % TiO₂ (white pigment)** and **5 % carbon black**. Calculate the final concentrations of TiO₂ and carbon black in the moulded article. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert let-down percentage to a fraction.** $$\text{LDR}_{\text{frac}} = \dfrac{3}{100} = 0.03$$ In a 100 kg total batch: $m_{\text{mb}} = 3$ kg, $m_{\text{base}} = 97$ kg. **Step 2 — Final TiO₂ concentration.** $$\begin{aligned} P_{\text{TiO}_2, \text{final}} &= P_{\text{TiO}_2, \text{mb}} \times \text{LDR}_{\text{frac}} \\ &= 0.35 \times 0.03 \\ &= 0.0105 \\ &= \mathbf{1.05 \text{ \%}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Final carbon black concentration.** $$\begin{aligned} P_{\text{CB}, \text{final}} &= P_{\text{CB}, \text{mb}} \times \text{LDR}_{\text{frac}} \\ &= 0.05 \times 0.03 \\ &= 0.0015 \\ &= \mathbf{0.15 \text{ \%}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Resulting grey shade classification:** With ~1 % white pigment and ~0.15 % carbon black, the article will appear as a **light-to-medium grey** (carbon black is an extremely strong colorant — even 0.1 % produces visible greying). --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection Criteria for Masterbatch Components The following matrix summarises the decision logic a compounder follows when designing a masterbatch. This is a **frequently tested KTU question** as a short-answer / case-study style. | Application Requirement | Recommended Pigment Type | Recommended Carrier | Critical Additive | |---|---|---|---| | Food packaging film | FDA-approved organic pigment (e.g., Quinacridone Red) | LDPE | Slip agent, antiblock | | Outdoor garden furniture | Light-fast pigment (Phthalocyanine, Inorganic oxide) | EVA | UV stabiliser (HALS) | | Automotive interior | Heat-stable pigment > 240 °C | PP or SAN | Heat stabiliser | | Injection moulded toys | Non-toxic, EN 71-3 compliant | PP / PE | None — minimised additives | | Transparent bottle | Soluble dye | PS / SAN carrier | None | | Fibre spinning | Very fine dispersion, < 1 µm | PP / PA | High-grade dispersant | | Metallic finish | Aluminium flakes | Wax carrier | Special orientation additive | --- ### 3.5 Derivation 3 — Colour Strength and Particle Size Relationship (Qualitative) While the exact Mie scattering theory is beyond the KTU scope, the **engineering-relevant insight** is captured in this relation: For a given pigment chemistry, the **colouring strength (tint)** $S$ is inversely related to the square of the average particle diameter $d$: $$S \propto \dfrac{1}{d^2}$$ This is valid only in the **sub-micron to ~5 µm range**, where smaller particles mean more surface area per unit mass, more interaction with light, and stronger apparent colour. **Below ~0.1 µm**, the particles become transparent and colour strength *decreases* again (a phenomenon exploited in nano-pigment engineering). $$\begin{aligned} \text{Larger particles } (d \uparrow) &\Rightarrow \text{Surface area } (A \propto 1/d) \downarrow \Rightarrow \text{Colour strength } \downarrow \\ \text{Smaller particles } (d \downarrow) &\Rightarrow \text{Surface area } (A \uparrow) \Rightarrow \text{Colour strength } \uparrow \text{ (down to } \sim 0.1 \text{ µm)} \end{aligned}$$ This is the **theoretical justification** for the enormous energy invested by masterbatch producers in high-shear dispersion equipment — reducing $d$ from 10 µm to 1 µm gives a **100-fold** increase in tinting strength potential. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **Define the term "colour concentrate" in the context of plastics processing. List its four major components.** **Model Answer:** A **colour concentrate**, also called a **masterbatch**, is a highly concentrated dispersion of one or more pigments (or dyes) in a compatible carrier resin, supplied in pellet, flake, or liquid form. It is designed to be blended (let-down) at a small ratio (typically 1–5 %) with a base polymer during processing to colour the final plastic article uniformly. **Four Major Components:** 1. **Pigment or Dye** — the colourant (20–50 % w/w). 2. **Carrier Resin** — the dispersion medium (40–70 % w/w), compatible with the base polymer. 3. **Dispersant / Wetting Agent** — surface-active agent (2–5 %) that reduces pigment agglomeration. 4. **Performance Additives** — heat stabilisers, UV stabilisers, antioxidants, processing aids (2–5 %). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** **Distinguish between pigments and dyes as colourants in plastics. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer:** | Property | Pigment | Dye | |---|---|---| | **Solubility in polymer** | Insoluble (dispersed mechanically) | Soluble (dissolves in melt) | | **State in finished article** | Discrete solid particles | Molecularly dissolved | | **Heat stability** | Generally superior | Lower; may sublime | | **Light-fastness** | Excellent (esp. inorganic) | Moderate | | **Colour strength vs concentration** | Lower per unit mass | Higher per unit mass | | **Transparency effect** | Can be opaque or semi-transparent | Always transparent | **Example of Pigment:** Phthalocyanine Blue (organic, brilliant blue, used in packaging). **Example of Dye:** Solvent Red 135 (anthraquinone-based, used in transparent PS articles). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- **Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat flow diagram the industrial manufacturing process of a solid pellet colour masterbatch. Describe the function of each major equipment component. **(b) [7 Marks]** A processor wants to produce **1000 kg** of injection-moulded polypropylene moulded articles in a light grey shade. The colour recipe uses a masterbatch containing **30 % titanium dioxide (white)** and **4 % carbon black (black pigment)**, plus carrier and additives making up the balance. The recommended let-down ratio is **4 %**. (i) Calculate the mass of white pigment and black pigment present in the final 1000 kg batch. (ii) If the same shade is to be reproduced using a higher-loaded masterbatch (50 % TiO₂, 6 % carbon black), calculate the new let-down ratio required. ### MODEL ANSWER **Part (a) — Manufacturing Process Explanation [7 Marks]:** The industrial production of solid pellet colour masterbatch involves **six sequential stages**: **Stage 1 — Raw Material QC [1 mark]:** Pigments, carrier resin, and additives are received and tested. Pigment particle size is verified by laser diffraction; moisture content by Karl Fischer titration. **Stage 2 — Pre-Mixing [1 mark]:** Components are weighed according to formulation and loaded into a **Henschel high-speed mixer**. The high-velocity rotor produces a homogeneous dry blend via shear and convective mixing at slightly elevated temperature (30–80 °C) to remove residual moisture. **Stage 3 — Melt Compounding [2 marks]:** The dry blend is fed into a **co-rotating intermeshing twin-screw extruder** with an L/D ratio of 28:1 to 44:1. The screw profile contains conveying, kneading (for shear), and reverse elements (for residence time). Zone temperatures are ramped from ~150 °C at the feed zone to ~220 °C at the die. The high-shear kneading blocks disperse pigment agglomerates into particles < 5 µm, ideally < 1 µm. **Stage 4 — Filtration [1 mark]:** The melt passes through a **screen pack** (typically 200–400 mesh, 74–37 µm opening) to capture undispersed agglomerates and metallic contaminants. The pressure drop across the screen pack (filter pressure value, FPV) is monitored as a quality indicator. **Stage 5 — Pelletisation [1 mark]:** The molten strand exits the die, is cooled in a water bath (or on an air-cooled belt), dried by an air knife, and cut into uniform cylindrical pellets (~3 mm × 3 mm) by a rotating strand cutter. Alternative: **underwater pelletiser** for finer, more uniform pellets. **Stage 6 — Quality Control and Packaging [1 mark]:** Pellets are tested for **melt flow index (MFI), colour strength (vs. standard), dispersion rating (by microscopy or film-grade inspection), FPV, and bulk density**. Approved batches are bagged (25 kg bags, 500–1000 kg super-sacks) and shipped. --- **Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:** **Given Data:** $m_{\text{batch}} = 1000$ kg, $\text{LDR} = 4\%$, Masterbatch formulation: 30 % TiO₂ + 4 % carbon black. **(i) Mass of pigments in final batch [3 marks]:** Mass of masterbatch used: $$m_{\text{mb}} = 1000 \times \dfrac{4}{100} = 40 \text{ kg}$$ Mass of TiO₂ in final article: $$m_{\text{TiO}_2} = 40 \times 0.30 = \mathbf{12 \text{ kg}}$$ Mass of carbon black in final article: $$m_{\text{CB}} = 40 \times 0.04 = \mathbf{1.6 \text{ kg}}$$ These are equivalent to **1.2 % TiO₂** and **0.16 % carbon black** in the finished article. **(ii) New let-down ratio calculation [4 marks]:** To reproduce the same shade, we must deliver the **same absolute pigment masses** (12 kg TiO₂ and 1.6 kg carbon black) into the new 1000 kg batch using the higher-loaded masterbatch (50 % TiO₂, 6 % carbon black). Required mass of TiO₂ = 12 kg ⇒ masterbatch needed for TiO₂ = $12 / 0.50 = 24$ kg. Required mass of carbon black = 1.6 kg ⇒ masterbatch needed for carbon black = $1.6 / 0.06 = 26.67$ kg. Since the masterbatch must simultaneously deliver **both** pigment masses, we must use the **larger of the two** required quantities to ensure neither pigment is under-dosed. Therefore: $$m_{\text{mb, new}} = 26.67 \text{ kg}$$ Verification: at 26.67 kg of the new masterbatch, TiO₂ delivered = $26.67 \times 0.50 = 13.33$ kg (slight over-dosing is acceptable as shade is dictated by the limiting carbon black). New let-down ratio: $$\boxed{\text{LDR}_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{26.67}{1000 - 26.67} \times 100 = 2.74 \text{ \%}}$$ **Engineering Conclusion:** The higher-loaded masterbatch reduces the required let-down from 4.0 % to 2.74 %, which means lower transportation, storage, and dosing costs per kg of finished article. --- **Q.B. [Internal Choice Alternative — KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the key properties that a carrier resin must possess for effective masterbatch formulation. How does the "universal carrier" concept help plastics processors? **(b) [7 Marks]** A moulder uses a masterbatch at 2 % let-down into ABS polymer. The masterbatch contains 25 % quinacridone red pigment. The moulded article shows visible specks of undispersed pigment under microscopic examination. (i) Identify the root cause and suggest two corrective actions. (ii) Calculate the percentage of pigment in the final article. (iii) If the moulder switches to a masterbatch with finer particle size and observes a 30 % improvement in colour strength, by what factor can the pigment loading in the new masterbatch be reduced while maintaining the same shade? ### MODEL ANSWER **Part (a) — Carrier Resin Properties [7 Marks]:** The **carrier resin** is the medium in which pigments are dispersed in the masterbatch. For effective masterbatch performance, it must satisfy the following properties: **1. Compatibility with Base Polymer [2 marks]:** The carrier must be chemically and rheologically compatible with the polymer being coloured. Mismatched carriers cause **delamination, plate-out on mould surfaces, weld-line visibility, and "fish-eye" defects**. **2. Lower Melt Viscosity than Base Polymer [2 marks]:** The carrier should melt and flow more easily than the base polymer. This allows it to act as a "lubricating carrier fluid" that efficiently transports pigment particles into the base polymer melt during processing, ensuring uniform distribution. **3. Thermal Stability [1 mark]:** The carrier must not degrade at the processing temperatures of the base polymer. For polyamide (PA) processing at 280 °C, a carrier based on standard PE wax would char. **4. Good Pigment Wetting Ability [1 mark]:** The carrier must have a surface tension lower than the pigment's surface energy, enabling it to "wet" each particle. Polar carriers (e.g., EVA) wet polar pigments better; non-polar carriers (e.g., LDPE) wet non-polar pigments. **5. Pelletisation Stability [1 mark]:** The carrier must solidify cleanly into non-sticky pellets that can be stored and fed through dosing equipment without clumping. **Universal Carrier Concept [Bonus Points]:** A **universal carrier** is a proprietary blend — typically a combination of **polyethylene wax, EVA copolymer, and a small fraction of a polar modifier** — engineered to be compatible with a wide range of polymers (PE, PP, PS, ABS, PA, PBT). For a custom moulder working with multiple resins, using universal carriers **reduces inventory** (one masterbatch per colour works for all polymers), **simplifies procurement**, and **lowers obsolescence risk** when a particular polymer is discontinued. --- **Part (b) — Dispersion Defect Analysis [7 Marks]:** **(i) Root Cause and Corrective Actions [3 marks]:** **Root Cause:** The visible specks (agglomerates) indicate **inadequate pigment dispersion** in the masterbatch. The pigment particles were not reduced below the visible size threshold (~40 µm is the limit of human eye resolution; specks at 100–200 µm are gross agglomerates). **Corrective Action 1:** Switch to a masterbatch manufactured using a **high-shear twin-screw extruder with kneading blocks**, achieving particle sizes < 1 µm instead of the likely 5–20 µm in the current masterbatch. **Corrective Action 2:** Increase the **dispersant content** in the masterbatch formulation, or switch to a more effective polymeric dispersant tailored for quinacridone chemistry (e.g., BYK-Polymer brand dispersants). **Corrective Action 3 (alternative):** Increase the **processing shear** during moulding — e.g., increase back-pressure on the injection moulding screw, or use a static mixer in the extruder. **(ii) Percentage of pigment in final article [2 marks]:** $$\begin{aligned} P_{\text{final}} &= P_{\text{mb}} \times \text{LDR}_{\text{frac}} \\ &= 0.25 \times 0.02 \\ &= 0.005 \\ &= \mathbf{0.5 \text{ \%}} \end{aligned}$$ **(iii) Reduction in pigment loading [2 marks]:** If colour strength improves by 30 %, the new masterbatch can achieve the **same colour depth with only 70 % of the original pigment loading** (since "improvement by 30 %" means each unit of pigment now contributes 1.30× as much colour). Wait — let us re-read carefully. "Observes a 30 % improvement in colour strength." This means the *same loading* now produces 30 % more strength. Therefore, to achieve the *original* target shade, the new masterbatch can be diluted. Let us define $x$ = fraction of original loading that is now sufficient. $x \times 1.30 = 1.0 \Rightarrow x = 1 / 1.30 = 0.7692$ Reduction factor = $1 - 0.7692 = 0.2308 \approx \mathbf{23.1 \text{ \% reduction}}$ The new pigment loading can be reduced by approximately **23 %** while maintaining the same visual shade. $$\boxed{P_{\text{mb, new}} = 0.25 \times 0.77 = 0.1925 = 19.25 \text{ \%}}$$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students typically lose marks on Colour Concentrate questions:** > > 1. **Confusing LDR definition (–2 marks):** Many students write $\text{LDR} = m_{\text{mb}} / (m_{\text{mb}} + m_{\text{base}})$ when the textbook convention is $m_{\text{mb}} / m_{\text{base}}$. Use the convention stated in the question. > > 2. **Forgetting the carrier resin role (–2 marks):** When asked "what is a masterbatch", students list only pigment + carrier, omitting **dispersant and additives**. Always write **at least four components**. > > 3. **Treating pigment % as mass fraction, not volume fraction (–1 mark):** Pigment percentages are conventionally by **weight**, not volume. Stating 30 % TiO₂ means 30 g per 100 g, not 30 cm³ per 100 cm³. Density differences matter (TiO₂ density ~4.2 g/cm³ vs. PE ~0.95 g/cm³). > > 4. **Ignoring compatibility (–1 mark):** Never propose a masterbatch without commenting on the carrier–base polymer compatibility. A PE carrier in a PA (nylon) base will cause delamination defects. > > 5. **Skipping the flow diagram in manufacturing questions (–2 marks):** The KTU 2024 scheme rewards visual answers. A neat labelled flow diagram of the masterbatch manufacturing process carries significant credit; without it, expect a 2-mark deduction. > > 6. **Wrong unit conversion (–1 mark):** Watch out for mg/kg ↔ % conversion. 1000 mg/kg = 0.1 % (w/w), not 1 %. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > 📋 **RAPID REVISION — COLOUR CONCENTRATES** - ☐ **Definition:** Masterbatch = concentrated pigment dispersion in carrier resin, designed for let-down into base polymer. - ☐ **Synonyms:** Colour concentrate, masterbatch, MB, colour compound (pre-coloured). - ☐ **Five components:** Pigment + Carrier + Dispersant + Additives + (optional Filler/Special Effect). - ☐ **Pigment vs Dye:** Pigment = insoluble particles; Dye = soluble molecule. - ☐ **Typical masterbatch composition:** 20–50 % pigment, 40–70 % carrier, 2–10 % dispersant/additives. - ☐ **Standard let-down range:** 1 % to 5 % (i.e., 1:100 to 1:20 ratio). - ☐ **Final pigment in article:** 0.1 % to 2 % by weight. - ☐ **Key equation:** $P_{\text{final}} = P_{\text{mb}} \times \text{LDR}_{\text{frac}}$. - ☐ **Let-down ratio equation:** $\text{LDR} = P_{\text{final}} / (P_{\text{mb}} - P_{\text{final}})$. - ☐ **Manufacturing flow:** Pre-mix → Twin-screw extruder → Filter → Pelletise → QC. - ☐ **Equipment:** Henschel mixer, twin-screw extruder (L/D 28:1–44:1), screen pack 200–400 mesh, strand cutter. - ☐ **Universal carriers:** Wax/EVA blends compatible with multiple polymers — reduce customer inventory. - ☐ **Particle size target:** < 5 µm standard, < 1 µm premium applications. - ☐ **Colour strength:** $S \propto 1/d^2$ (down to ~0.1 µm). - ☐ **Common pigments:** Phthalocyanine (blue/green), Quinacridone (red/magenta), Carbon Black (black), TiO₂ (white), Azo (yellow/orange), Iron Oxide (red/yellow/brown). - ☐ **Common carriers:** LDPE, LLDPE, EVA, PP, PS, SAN, PE wax. - ☐ **Common additives:** HALS, antioxidants, UV stabilisers, antistatic agents, processing aids. - ☐ **Quality tests:** MFI, Filter Pressure Value (FPV), dispersion rating, colour strength vs. standard. - ☐ **Cost equation:** $C_{\text{coloured}} = C_{\text{base}} + C_{\text{mb}} \times \text{LDR}_{\text{frac}}$. - ☐ **Key advantages:** Dust-free handling, accurate dosing, colour consistency, fast changeover, regulatory compliance. - ☐ **Defects to know:** Plate-out, fish-eyes, weld-line colour variation, pigment specks (agglomeration), colour shift on ageing.

topics.title: Painting on Plastics
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Painting on Plastics — Core Technical Foundation

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT864 - PLASTICS & DECORATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Painting on Plastics

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Painting on plastics** is defined as the controlled, engineered application of a liquid or powder coating system onto a polymeric substrate, followed by a physico-chemical curing step, with the objective of producing a uniform, adherent, and aesthetically or functionally specified film. In the context of KTU 2024 (PEPOT864 – Plastics & Decoration), this operation falls under the broader umbrella of **secondary surface finishing processes** that are applied *after* the primary part has been moulded or extruded.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Painting is treated as a *decoration* process — distinct from metallization, printing, or hot-stamping — because it involves the application of a *continuous pigmented organic film* rather than a metal layer or a transferred foil.

The process is governed by four engineering parameters:

1. **Substrate surface energy** (typically **36–46 mN/m** for common plastics like PP, PE, ABS).
2. **Coating viscosity** (typically **15–35 seconds** in a B-4 Ford cup at 25 °C).
3. **Wet film thickness (WFT)** versus **dry film thickness (DFT)**.
4. **Adhesion strength** (target ≥ **1.0 MPa** in cross-hatch adhesion per ASTM D3359).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a brand-new, waxy plastic lunch box straight out of its mould. If you take a permanent marker and try to write on it, the ink beads up into tiny droplets and smears — it simply refuses to *stick*. This is exactly what happens when paint is applied to a moulded plastic part. Polymers like polypropylene (PP) and polyethylene (PE) have a chemically inert, low-energy surface. To get paint to "grab on", we must first:
> (a) **Clean** the surface (remove mould-release agents, dust, oils),
> (b) **Pretreat** it (corona discharge, flame, chemical primer) to make it "thirsty" for paint,
> (c) **Apply** a paint that is chemically compatible, and
> (d) **Cure** it so the paint molecules crosslink and lock onto the surface.
> Skipping any one step is like trying to stick a suction cup onto a greasy plate — it will fall off.

## 1.3 Why Plastics Need Special Painting Logic

| Polymer | Surface Energy (mN/m) | Paintability (As-Moulded) | Why It Fails |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Polypropylene (PP)** | **29–31** | Poor | Highly crystalline, non-polar, low surface energy |
| **Polyethylene (PE)** | **30–32** | Poor | Similar to PP; wax-like surface |
| **ABS** | **38–42** | Fair–Good | Slightly polar; accepts solvent-borne paints |
| **Polycarbonate (PC)** | **42–46** | Good | Polar groups allow decent wetting |
| **PMMA (Acrylic)** | **39–41** | Good | Moderate polarity |
| **Nylon (PA 6,6)** | **40–44** | Good | Amide groups aid adhesion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A plastic with surface energy **< 38 mN/m** (e.g., PP, PE) will not wet out a typical paint. **Pretreatment is mandatory.** Plastics with surface energy **> 40 mN/m** may be painted with minimal surface prep, provided mould-release contamination is removed.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Painting Process (KTU Standard Sequence)

The KTU 2024 syllabus for PEPOT864 recognizes a canonical five-stage workflow. Each stage must be engineered and validated independently:

1. **Substrate Inspection & Cleaning** — Visual + degreasing (IPA, alkaline wash).
2. **Surface Pretreatment / Activation** — Corona, flame, plasma, or chemical primer.
3. **Primer Application** — A thin tie-coat (~5–15 µm DFT) that bonds to the substrate and accepts the topcoat.
4. **Topcoat / Colour Coat Application** — The visible pigmented or clear layer (~15–30 µm DFT).
5. **Curing / Drying** — Solvent flash-off, followed by thermal or UV crosslinking.

## 2.2 Stage-by-Stage Theoretical Breakdown

### Stage 1 — Substrate Inspection & Cleaning
- **Goal:** Achieve a surface free of mould-release agents, dust, oils, and electrostatic charge.
- **Methods:** Alkaline cleaning (pH 9–11), solvent wipe (isopropyl alcohol), ultrasonic bath.
- **Why:** Contaminants raise contact angle (θ) and act as weak boundary layers, causing *adhesive failure* at the substrate-paint interface.

### Stage 2 — Surface Pretreatment / Activation
- **Why:** Moulded plastics have surface energies much lower than the surface tension of most paints. To "match" them, we raise the substrate's surface energy.
- **Corona Treatment:** High-voltage (10–20 kV), high-frequency discharge oxidises the surface, raising energy to **≥ 50 mN/m** (often **≥ 56 mN/m** for printing).
- **Flame Treatment:** Brief exposure (≈ 0.02 s) to a propane/butane flame oxidises and functionalises the surface.
- **Plasma Treatment:** Low-pressure or atmospheric plasma introduces polar –OH, –C=O groups.
- **Chemical Primers:** Solvent-borne or water-borne chlorinated polyolefin (CPO) primers, especially for PP-based automotive bumpers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Rule of Surface Energy Match":** A coating will spontaneously wet a substrate only if the **coating's surface tension is less than the substrate's surface energy**. Typical paints have surface tension **30–40 mN/m**. Thus, untreated PP/PE (≤ 32 mN/m) is unwettable.

### Stage 3 — Primer Application
- **Purpose:** Provide a chemically compatible intermediate layer.
- **Typical Primers:**
  - On **PP/PE:** Chlorinated Polyolefin (CPO) primer — 5–10 µm DFT.
  - On **ABS:** Acrylic or epoxy primer — 8–12 µm DFT.
  - On **PC:** Polyurethane primer — 8–12 µm DFT.
- **Application:** Spray (HVLP or airless) or curtain coat for flat panels.

### Stage 4 — Topcoat Application
- **Common Topcoat Chemistries:**
  - **Acrylic** — Good UV, fast dry; used on consumer goods.
  - **Polyurethane (2K)** — Excellent chemical & scratch resistance; automotive OEM.
  - **Epoxy** — Hard, chemical-resistant; industrial equipment.
  - **UV-Curable Acrylate** — High gloss, fast cure; CD/DVD, mobile covers.
- **DFT Target:** 20–30 µm for most decorative applications; up to 50 µm for marine/automotive.

### Stage 5 — Curing
- **Mechanisms:**
  - **Solvent Evaporation** (lacquers) — physical drying, 20–60 °C, 10–30 min.
  - **Oxidative Cure** (alkyds) — oxygen-driven crosslinking, hours–days.
  - **Forced Thermal Cure** (2K PU, melamine) — **60–80 °C, 20–30 min** for plastics (careful — exceeds Tg of many polymers!).
  - **UV Cure** (radical/cationic acrylates) — seconds under **200–400 nm** mercury lamp.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory KTU Reference Card** — reproduce this table in your exam if a numerical/derivation question appears on painting processes.

| Concept | Equation | Variables & Units | Notes / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| Surface Energy Match (Wetting Criterion) | $\gamma_{sv} \ge \gamma_{lv} + \gamma_{sl}$ | $\gamma$ in **mN/m** | Young's condition for partial wetting |
| Contact Angle (Young) | $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl}}{\gamma_{lv}}$ | $\theta$ in **°** | $\theta < 90°$ = wettable |
| Dry Film Thickness | $DFT = \dfrac{WFT \times V_s}{100}$ | WFT in **µm**, $V_s$ = % solids by volume | Industry rule of thumb |
| Theoretical Coverage | $C = \dfrac{V_s \times 10}{DFT}$ | $C$ in **m²/L**, DFT in **µm** | Excludes losses |
| Practical Coverage | $C_p = C \times T_e$ | $T_e$ = transfer efficiency (0.4–0.85) | Spray losses included |
| Adhesion Strength (Pull-off) | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | $\sigma$ in **MPa**, F in N, A in mm² | ASTM D4541 |
| Cross-hatch Rating | $n/5B$ (5B = best) | — | ASTM D3359 |
| Cure Conversion (Kinetics) | $\alpha(t) = 1 - e^{-kt}$ | $k$ in **min⁻¹**, $t$ in **min** | First-order approx. |
| Heat Distortion Limit | $T_{cure} < T_g - 20°C$ | °C | Prevents part warping |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Table Rule:** Pipes `|` are forbidden inside table cells — use `\vert` or `\mid` if absolute value or dividing bar must be shown (as done above with `\dfrac`).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Painted PP bumpers must pass 1000-hour Xenon-arc weathering (SAE J1960) and gravelometer chip resistance (SAE J400).
- **Consumer Electronics:** UV-cured coatings on mobile phone shells (e.g., ABS+PC blends) deliver class-A gloss with **< 5 sec cycle time**.
- **Medical Devices:** Epoxy or PU coatings on polymer housings provide biocompatibility (ISO 10993) and chemical sterilisation resistance.
- **Toys:** Water-based acrylics on PP toys meet **EN 71-3** migration limits for heavy metals.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Analytical & Process Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation — Film Thickness & Coverage

> **Problem:** A water-based acrylic topcoat contains **42 % solids by volume**. It is sprayed at a wet film thickness (WFT) of **75 µm** onto an ABS housing. Calculate the dry film thickness (DFT) and the practical coverage rate assuming a transfer efficiency of **65 %**.

### Step 1 — Identify given data

$$V_s = 42 \, \% = 0.42$$

$$WFT = 75 \, \mu m$$

$$T_e = 65 \, \% = 0.65$$

### Step 2 — Apply the DFT equation

> Recall: when solvent evaporates, only the solids remain. Solids volume is preserved while wet film collapses vertically.

$$DFT = WFT \times V_s$$

$$DFT = 75 \times 0.42$$

$$DFT = 31.5 \, \mu m$$

### Step 3 — Theoretical coverage calculation

> 1 litre of paint = $10^6$ mm³ = $10^6 \, \mu m \cdot mm^2$. A 1 µm film over 1 m² (10⁶ mm²) consumes 10⁶ µm·mm³ = 1 mL of pure solids.

$$C = \dfrac{V_s \times 10}{DFT}$$

$$C = \dfrac{0.42 \times 10}{31.5}$$

$$C = \dfrac{4.2}{31.5}$$

$$C \approx 0.1333 \, m^2/L$$

### Step 4 — Apply transfer efficiency

> Of every litre sprayed, only 65 % lands on the part. The rest is overspray.

$$C_p = C \times T_e$$

$$C_p = 0.1333 \times 0.65$$

$$C_p \approx 0.0867 \, m^2/L$$

> **Final Answer:** DFT ≈ **31.5 µm**, Practical coverage ≈ **0.087 m²/L** (i.e., about **11.5 L** to paint **1 m²** of part at this WFT and efficiency).

## 3.2 Derivation — Adhesion Criterion (Young–Dupré Work of Adhesion)

> **Problem:** A polyurethane topcoat has a surface tension of **38 mN/m**. After corona treatment, the ABS substrate exhibits a surface energy of **48 mN/m**, and the interfacial tension is measured at **6 mN/m**. Determine the contact angle and the thermodynamic work of adhesion.

### Step 1 — Young's Equation

$$\cos\theta = \dfrac{\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl}}{\gamma_{lv}}$$

$$\cos\theta = \dfrac{48 - 6}{38}$$

$$\cos\theta = \dfrac{42}{38} = 1.1053$$

> [!NOTE]
> A $\cos\theta > 1$ is *physically impossible* — it signals **complete wetting** ($\theta = 0°$), i.e., the paint spontaneously spreads into a uniform film. This is the engineering ideal.

### Step 2 — Dupré Work of Adhesion (Young–Dupré)

For a fully wetted surface ($\theta = 0°$), the work of adhesion simplifies to:

$$W_{sl} = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}$$

$$W_{sl} = 48 + 38 - 6$$

$$W_{sl} = 80 \, mJ/m^2$$

> **Interpretation:** A work of adhesion of **80 mJ/m²** is strong — comparable to **1.5× the cohesion energy of the bulk paint**, meaning cohesive (paint-internal) failure is more likely than adhesive (paint-substrate) failure. The coating will likely fail *within* the paint, not at the interface.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Painting Route by Plastic Type

| Plastic | Recommended Pretreatment | Recommended Primer | Topcoat | Typical DFT (µm) | Cure Window |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PP (Bumper)** | Corona or CPO wipe | CPO primer (5–10 µm) | 2K PU (Automotive OEM) | 25–35 | 80 °C / 30 min |
| **PE (Container)** | Flame + Corona | CPO or PE-compatible acrylic | Water-acrylic | 15–25 | 60 °C / 20 min |
| **ABS (Mobile shell)** | IPA wipe + antistatic | Acrylic primer | UV acrylate | 20–30 | UV, 5 s |
| **PC (Glazing)** | IPA wipe | PU primer | Silicone-modified acrylic | 20–35 | 80 °C / 30 min |
| **PMMA (Lens)** | IPA wipe | None (direct) | UV acrylate | 15–25 | UV, 3 s |
| **Nylon 6,6 (Fastener)** | Antistatic | Epoxy primer | Epoxy topcoat | 25–40 | 120 °C / 30 min |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tg (Glass Transition) Constraint:** Curing temperature **must be ≤ Tg − 20 °C**, otherwise the plastic part distorts in the oven. For ABS (Tg ≈ 105 °C), max cure is ~85 °C.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Painting Process Flow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incoming Plastic Part] --> B[Visual Inspection]
    B --> C[Cleaning Station]
    C --> C1[Alkaline Degrease]
    C --> C2[IPA Wipe]
    C --> C3[Antistatic Blow-off]
    C1 --> D[Surface Activation]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> D1{Corona Discharge}
    D --> D2{Flame Treatment}
    D --> D3{Plasma Treatment}
    D1 --> E[Primer Booth]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Flash-off Zone 25C 5 min]
    F --> G[Topcoat Booth HVLP Spray]
    G --> H[Flash-off Zone 25C 10 min]
    H --> I{Cure Method}
    I --> I1[Thermal Oven 80C 30 min]
    I --> I2[UV Lamp 365 nm 5 s]
    I --> I3[IR Tunnel 60 s]
    I1 --> J[Cooling Zone]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    J --> K[QC Inspection]
    K --> K1[DFT Gauge]
    K --> K2[Cross-hatch Adhesion]
    K --> K3[Glossmeter 60 deg]
    K --> K4[Visual Colour Match]
    K1 --> L[Approved Part Packout]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    K4 --> L
    L --> M[Reject Bin Re-work]
```

## 4.2 Surface Energy Matching — Visual Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Substrate
        S1[PP PE Surface 30 mN/m]
        S2[ABS PC Surface 42 mN/m]
    end
    subgraph Coating
        C1[Paint Surface Tension 36 mN/m]
    end
    S1 --> R1{Compare}
    C1 --> R1
    R1 -->|Lower energy than paint| X1[Paint beads up No adhesion]
    S2 --> R2{Compare}
    C1 --> R2
    R2 -->|Higher energy than paint| Y1[Paint wets out Good adhesion]
```

## 4.3 Defect Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D[Paint Defect Observed] --> D1{Type}
    D1 -->|Orange peel| O[Reduce viscosity Check atomisation pressure]
    D1 -->|Cratering| CR[Check for silicone contamination Re-clean substrate]
    D1 -->|Peeling off| P[Verify pretreatment Re-test dyne level must be 56 mN/m]
    D1 -->|Bubbling| B[Reduce film thickness Flash-off before cure]
    D1 -->|Yellowing| Y[Reduce cure temp Check UV absorber in topcoat]
    D1 -->|Sink marks| SM[Check substrate uniformity Reduce filler loading]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Interpretation:** Each `{Curly Brace}` block is a logical OR-branch representing a parameter to be diagnosed. The arrows in the diagrams are pure ASCII/Mermaid — they correspond to physical decisions an operator or engineer would make on the shop floor.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
> **List any three reasons why plastics like PP and PE cannot be painted directly without surface pretreatment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Low surface energy:** PP (~30 mN/m) and PE (~31 mN/m) have surface energies lower than typical paint surface tensions (~36–40 mN/m), so paint fails to wet the substrate. *(1 mark)*
2. **Non-polar, chemically inert surface:** Absence of polar functional groups prevents formation of secondary bonds (H-bonds, dipole interactions) with the coating. *(1 mark)*
3. **Mould-release contamination:** Residual silicones or stearates form a weak boundary layer that causes adhesive failure at the substrate-coating interface. *(1 mark)*

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**
> **Define the term "transfer efficiency" in spray painting and state two methods to improve it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition:** Transfer efficiency (T_e) is the ratio of paint *deposited on the part* to the *total paint atomised*, expressed as a percentage. *(1 mark)*
- **Methods to improve (any two, 1 mark each):**
  1. Use **HVLP (High-Volume Low-Pressure)** guns — T_e rises from ~30 % to ~65 %.
  2. Use **electrostatic spray** — charged droplets are attracted to the grounded part, raising T_e to 80–95 %.
  3. Optimise gun distance (15–25 cm) and traverse speed to minimise overspray.

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **Note (KTU ESE pattern):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 + (b) 7.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]`

**(a) Explain in detail the surface pretreatment methods used for plastics before painting. Compare corona, flame, and plasma treatments. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Corona | Flame | Plasma |
|---|---|---|---|
| Principle | High-voltage discharge | Oxidising flame | Ionised gas |
| Surface energy achieved | 44–56 mN/m | 42–52 mN/m | 50–70 mN/m |
| Treatment depth | Nanometres | Nanometres | Nanometres–micrometres |
| Effect duration | Short (days–weeks) | Medium | Long (weeks–months) |
| Line speed (m/min) | 50–400 | 50–250 | 20–100 |
| Cost | Low | Low | High |

**Narrative (key points, 1 mark each):**
1. **Corona:** Discharges a high-frequency (10–30 kHz), high-voltage (10–20 kV) field across a gap; the air ionises and free radicals oxidise the polymer surface, raising surface energy. Used for films and 3-D parts.
2. **Flame:** A short (0.01–0.05 s) exposure to a blue oxidising flame (propane/butane) generates –OH and –C=O groups. Excellent for thick PP/PE automotive parts.
