3. **Plasma:** Most effective; uses low-pressure or atmospheric plasma of O₂, Ar, or N₂. Creates long-lasting polar functional groups; preferred for high-reliability electronics and medical parts.
4. **All three** must be followed by **prompt painting** because surface energy decays (hydrophobic recovery).
5. **Verification:** A **dyne test solution** (e.g., 56 mN/m) is used to confirm adequate activation — the solution must spread uniformly for ≥ 2 s.

---

**(b) A water-based acrylic topcoat with 38 % solids by volume is applied at a wet film thickness of 90 µm on a corona-treated PP part. The transfer efficiency of the HVLP gun is 60 %. Calculate (i) DFT, (ii) theoretical coverage, and (iii) practical coverage. State two tests used to evaluate the painted part. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Given:** $V_s = 0.38$, $WFT = 90 \, \mu m$, $T_e = 0.60$

**(i) Dry Film Thickness** *(2 marks)*:

$$DFT = WFT \times V_s = 90 \times 0.38 = 34.2 \, \mu m$$

**(ii) Theoretical Coverage** *(2 marks)*:

$$C = \dfrac{V_s \times 10}{DFT} = \dfrac{0.38 \times 10}{34.2} = \dfrac{3.8}{34.2} \approx 0.1111 \, m^2/L$$

**(iii) Practical Coverage** *(2 marks)*:

$$C_p = C \times T_e = 0.1111 \times 0.60 \approx 0.0667 \, m^2/L$$

**Two QC tests (1 mark — ½ mark each):**
1. **Cross-hatch adhesion test (ASTM D3359)** — uses a razor lattice and tape peel; rating 5B = best.
2. **Gloss measurement at 60° (ASTM D523)** — typical automotive topcoat ≥ 90 GU.
3. *(Optional)* **DFT measurement with magnetic/ eddy-current gauge** (calibrated for non-metal substrates using ultrasonic gauges).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students often confuse **theoretical vs. practical coverage** — practical is **always lower** because of transfer losses.
> - Many skip the **unit** ("m²/L") — KTU deducts ½ mark for missing units.
> - DFT must be **rounded to 1 decimal place** in the answer; sloppy rounding loses ½ mark.
> - When $V_s$ is in **%**, divide by 100 before substituting in DFT; mixing units is a top-3 error.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Expected Pattern]`

**(a) Describe the function of primers in plastic painting. With a neat sketch, describe the multi-layer coating architecture (substrate → primer → basecoat → clearcoat) used in automotive plastic painting. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Functions of Primers (any 4 × 1 mark):**
1. Promote adhesion to the substrate via chemical bonding (especially CPO primers for PP).
2. Mask minor surface imperfections and mould-flow lines.
3. Provide a uniform colour-neutral base for the topcoat.
4. Block plasticiser migration that would otherwise soften the topcoat (notably in flexible PVC).
5. Improve corrosion resistance for filled/reinforced plastics exposed outdoors.

**Multi-layer Architecture (description, 3 marks):**

A typical automotive bumper finish has four layers on the PP substrate:

1. **Substrate:** PP/EPDM (Tg ≈ −60 °C, very flexible).
2. **Primer (CPO):** 5–10 µm, ensures adhesion to PP.
3. **Basecoat (pigmented PU):** 15–25 µm, gives colour and metallic effect.
4. **Clearcoat (2K PU):** 30–40 µm, provides UV resistance, scratch resistance, and final gloss.

This is the **"Primer-Basecoat-Clearcoat" (PBC)** stack — industry standard for OEM automotive.

---

**(b) Explain the defects (i) orange peel, (ii) cratering, and (iii) delamination, with their root causes and remedies. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Defect | Appearance | Root Cause | Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Orange Peel** (2 marks) | Pebbled, dimpled surface | High viscosity, low atomisation pressure, fast solvent flash | Reduce viscosity (thinner), raise gun pressure to 3–4 bar, slow gun speed |
| **(ii) Cratering** (2 marks) | Small circular depressions | Silicone/oil contamination on substrate; surface tension gradient | Re-clean with IPA, avoid silicone-based mould-release near paint line, add anti-crater additive |
| **(iii) Delamination** (3 marks) | Coating peels off in sheets | Poor adhesion due to inadequate pretreatment, expired primer, contamination at interface | Verify dyne level ≥ 56 mN/m, use fresh primer, store parts in cleanroom, run cross-hatch test before bulk production |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - In (b), examiners expect **specific numerical tolerances** (e.g., dyne level 56 mN/m, gun pressure 3–4 bar). Vague phrases like *"increase adhesion"* fetch only ½–1 mark.
> - Always mention the **standard test method** (ASTM D3359, ASTM D523) — it signals KTU-aligned knowledge.
> - For (a), students who skip the **layer-by-layer description** lose 2–3 marks. Always list **each layer with its DFT**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Painting on plastics ≠ painting on metal** — substrate surface energy is the deciding factor.
- [x] **PP/PE require pretreatment**; ABS/PC/PMMA/Nylon are usually paintable as-moulded.
- [x] **Corona, flame, plasma** are the three industrial pretreatment methods; verify with **dyne test (56 mN/m)**.
- [x] **CPO primer** is the gold standard for PP/PE adhesion.
- [x] **DFT = WFT × Volume % Solids**; **Coverage = (Vs × 10) / DFT × T_e**.
- [x] **Cure temperature must be ≤ Tg − 20 °C** of the substrate — otherwise warping.
- [x] **Topcoat chemistries:** Acrylic (fast/UV), Polyurethane (durable), Epoxy (chemical resistance), UV acrylate (instant).
- [x] **Key QC tests:** Cross-hatch adhesion (ASTM D3359), Pull-off (ASTM D4541), Gloss 60° (ASTM D523), DFT gauge.
- [x] **Common defects:** Orange peel, cratering, delamination, bubbling, yellowing — each with a specific root cause and remedy.
- [x] **Transfer efficiency:** HVLP ~65 %, electrostatic 80–95 %, conventional air spray ~30 % — a key cost and emission lever.
- [x] **Automotive reference stack:** Substrate → CPO Primer → PU Basecoat → PU Clearcoat.
- [x] **KTU exam trick:** Always quote **units** (mN/m, µm, m²/L, MPa); missing units = ½ mark lost per item.
- [x] **Don't confuse** *theoretical* and *practical* coverage — practical is lower by factor T_e.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modifications on Plastic Part Surfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT864 - PLASTICS & DECORATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Modifications on Plastic Part Surfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Modifications on Plastic Part Surfaces

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Board-Approved Terminology):**
> *Surface modification of plastics* refers to the set of **physical, chemical, electrochemical, and thermo-mechanical processes** applied to the external skin of a finished polymer part in order to alter its **wettability, surface energy, roughness, optical behaviour, electrical conductivity, barrier properties, and aesthetic appearance**, without modifying the bulk mechanical properties of the substrate.

In simple words: **plastic decoration = making a moulded plastic part look better, perform better, or stick better — by working only on its outermost layer (typically 1 nm – 10 µm deep).**

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you bought a plain white T-shirt (the **moulded plastic part**) and you want to:
- Print a logo on it (**printing / painting**)
- Make it water-repellent (**surface coating / fluorination**)
- Stick glitter on it (**metallization / vacuum deposition**)
- Make it rough so glue sticks (**corona / plasma / flame treatment**)
- Add a glossy metallic shine like a car bumper (**electroplating**)

All these operations change **only the top layer of the fabric**, not the cotton thread inside. That is exactly what surface modification does on a plastic part — it touches the top few micrometres and leaves the bulk polymer untouched.

### 1.1.2 Why Plastics Need Surface Modification (the Fundamental Problem)

Most commodity plastics (Polyethylene, Polypropylene, Polystyrene, ABS in some cases) are **inherently low in surface energy**:

| Polymer | Surface Energy (mN/m) | Wettability |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| PTFE (Teflon) | **18 – 20** | Very Poor |
| Polypropylene (PP) | **29 – 31** | Poor |
| Polyethylene (PE) | **30 – 32** | Poor |
| Polystyrene (PS) | **33 – 35** | Moderate |
| ABS | **35 – 38** | Fair |
| Polycarbonate (PC) | **40 – 42** | Good |
| Nylon (PA 6,6) | **42 – 46** | Very Good |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule of Plastic Decoration:**
> For any paint, ink, adhesive, or metallic layer to **wet and bond** uniformly on a plastic surface, the surface energy of the plastic must be **at least 8 – 10 mN/m higher** than the surface tension of the liquid being applied. If this condition fails → **dewetting, pinholes, orange peel, poor adhesion, delamination**.

### 1.1.3 The Five Functional Reasons for Surface Modification

1. **Adhesion Promotion** – for printing inks, paints, adhesives, over-moulding.
2. **Aesthetic Decoration** – colours, gloss, metallic look, textures.
3. **Functional Performance** – scratch resistance, UV protection, anti-fog, anti-static.
4. **Barrier Properties** – moisture, oxygen, and gas permeation resistance.
5. **Electronic / Optical Function** – conductivity for EMI/RFI shielding, reflectivity, laser marking.

### 1.1.4 Classification of Surface Modification Techniques (Master Map)

```
MODIFICATIONS ON PLASTIC PART SURFACES
│
├── 1. CHEMICAL MODIFICATIONS
│      ├── Chemical etching / Oxidation (Chromic acid, Potassium permanganate)
│      ├── Fluorination (gas-phase, for HDPE fuel tanks)
│      ├── Sulphonation
│      └── Primer / Coupling-agent application
│
├── 2. PHYSICAL / MECHANICAL MODIFICATIONS
│      ├── Surface roughening (sand blasting, Scotch-brite brushing)
│      ├── Embossing (in-mould texturing)
│      └── Polishing / Buffing
│
├── 3. ELECTRICAL / ELECTROCHEMICAL MODIFICATIONS
│      ├── Corona discharge treatment (CDT)
│      ├── Atmospheric plasma treatment
│      ├── Low-pressure plasma treatment
│      └── Flame treatment (combustion of natural gas / propane)
│
├── 4. THERMAL / RADIATION MODIFICATIONS
│      ├── UV / Ozone treatment
│      ├── Ion-beam bombardment
│      └── Laser texturing / Laser marking
│
└── 5. SURFACE COATINGS & ADDED LAYERS
       ├── Painting (wet spray, electrostatic, powder coating)
       ├── Printing (pad, screen, hot-stamp, digital, inkjet)
       ├── Vacuum metallization
       ├── Sputtering
       ├── Electroplating (Cu → Ni → Cr)
       ├── In-mould decoration (IMD / IML / IMF)
       └── Lacquering / Clear coats
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme students must remember the **classification hierarchy above** — at least one Part A question is asked every semester directly on classification of plastic surface modification processes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 1.2 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 1.2.1 Pre-Treatment Operations (Substrate Conditioning)

Before any decorative layer can be applied, the plastic surface must be **clean, dust-free, oil-free, and electrically matched** with the coating. The standard pre-treatment chain is:

$$\text{Moulded Part} \rightarrow \text{Degreasing} \rightarrow \text{Rinsing} \rightarrow \text{Activation} \rightarrow \text{Drying} \rightarrow \text{Application of Coating}$$

**(a) Degreasing** – Removes mould-release agents, fingerprints, anti-stat residues.
- Solvent wipe (IPA, acetone)
- Alkaline detergent wash
- Ultrasonic bath (40 – 60 kHz)

**(b) Activation (the heart of pre-treatment)** – Raises surface energy by introducing **polar functional groups (-OH, -COOH, =O, -NH₂)** so that inks/paints wet the surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Industrial Activation Standards**
> 1. **Corona Discharge Treatment (CDT)** – for PE/PP films and sheets.
> 2. **Flame Treatment** – for blow-moulded bottles, large automotive PP parts.
> 3. **Plasma Treatment** – for medical, electronics, automotive premium parts.

### 1.2.2 Corona Discharge Treatment (CDT) — Detailed Theory

A **high-frequency, high-voltage alternating current (typically 10 – 30 kV, 10 – 50 kHz)** is passed through an electrode separated from a grounded roller by a thin dielectric barrier (silicon, ceramic, or epoxide). The plastic film/part passes through the air gap.

The high electric field **ionizes the air** producing a corona — a bluish-purple plasma of electrons, ions, ozone, NOx.

**The chemistry that occurs on the polymer surface:**

$$ \text{R-H} + \text{O}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{OH}^{\bullet} $$
$$ \text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{R-O-O}^{\bullet} $$
$$ \text{R-O-O}^{\bullet} + \text{R-H} \rightarrow \text{R-O-O-H} + \text{R}^{\bullet} $$

Result: Surface gets **hydroxyl (-OH), carbonyl (C=O), carboxyl (-COOH) groups** — surface energy **rises from 29 mN/m to 44 – 48 mN/m** within milliseconds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite — Dyne Level Concept:**
> Surface energy is measured using a set of **dyne test inks (dyne solutions)** of known surface tension (30, 36, 38, 40, 42, 44, 46, 48, 50 dyne/cm).
> The ink that **does not bead up and forms a continuous film for 2 seconds** indicates the surface energy of the plastic. For PE film, a dyne level of **≥ 38 dyne/cm** is the industry-standard minimum for printing.

### 1.2.3 Flame Treatment

A gas-air mixture (typically propane-air or natural-gas-air) is burned at a specially designed **ribbon burner**. The flame has three zones:

```
Outer zone:  Combustion (oxidative, ~1400 °C)  → polymer surface attack
Middle zone: Reducing / hot gas (~800 °C)
Inner zone:  Cool unburnt gas
```

The flame treatment is brief (0.01 – 0.05 s exposure) and the substrate **bulk remains cool**, but the top 5 – 10 nm of polymer is oxidized, producing -OH, -COOH, -C=O groups.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why flame over corona for thick moulded parts?**
> Corona works best on thin films; for thick 3-D moulded parts (bumpers, dashboards), flame treatment is preferred because the higher energy density of the flame can activate the curved and recessed surfaces uniformly.

### 1.2.4 Plasma Treatment

In **low-pressure plasma**, the part is placed in a vacuum chamber (10⁻² – 10⁻³ mbar), and a gas (O₂, N₂, Ar, NH₃) is ionized using RF (13.56 MHz) or microwave (2.45 GHz) power. The plasma contains:

- **Free radicals (R•)**
- **Ions (+/−)**
- **Excited neutrals**
- **UV photons**

These bombard the surface → **cross-linking, chain scission, grafting of polar groups**.

**Atmospheric plasma (APPT – Atmospheric Pressure Plasma Treatment)** uses a dielectric barrier discharge jet — used for inline continuous treatment of automotive parts.

### 1.2.5 Wet Chemical Etching

Used predominantly for **ABS plastic prior to electroplating**. The classic **chrome-sulphuric acid etchant**:

$$ \text{CrO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Mixed chromic-sulphuric acid bath} $$

This bath **selectively etches the polybutadiene (PB) micro-domains** dispersed in the SAN matrix of ABS. The PB rubber gets oxidized away, leaving **microscopic pores and undercuts** 0.1 – 1 µm deep. These pores act as **mechanical anchor sites** for the electrodeposited metal.

$$\text{ABS surface} \xrightarrow{\text{CrO}_3 / \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} \text{Anchor pattern (etch pits)} \xrightarrow{\text{Pd/Sn colloid}} \text{Activated sites} \xrightarrow{\text{Electroless Ni/Cu}} \text{Metal seed} \xrightarrow{\text{Electroplating}} \text{Final metallic finish}$$

### 1.2.6 Painting of Plastics

| Method | Working Principle | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Conventional Spray** | Compressed air atomizes paint; Bernoulli principle | Low-volume, R&D |
| **HVLP Spray** | High Volume Low Pressure (≤ 0.7 bar) — better transfer efficiency (~65 %) | Cars, bumpers |
| **Airless Spray** | Hydraulic pressure (100 – 500 bar) atomizes paint | Heavy industrial |
| **Electrostatic Spray** | Part is grounded (+), paint droplets charged (~60 kV) — Coulomb attraction | Metallic frames, automotive |
| **Powder Coating** | Electrostatic deposition of dry powder, then melted at 160 – 200 °C | Outdoor furniture, electrical enclosures |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For plastics, oven-curing temperature must be **below the Heat Deflection Temperature (HDT)** of the plastic:
> - PP → ~100 °C
> - ABS → ~95 °C
> - PC → ~135 °C
> - PA 6 → ~180 °C
>
> Always use **PUR (polyurethane) or acrylic-melamine** low-bake paints designed for plastics.

### 1.2.7 Printing Techniques — Quick Reference

| Printing Method | Ink Type | Substrate | Resolution | Speed |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Pad printing** | Solvent ink via silicone pad | 3-D curved parts | Medium | Medium |
| **Screen printing** | Thick paste through mesh | Flat / cylindrical | Low–Medium | Low |
| **Hot-stamping (foil)** | Dry metallic/pigmented foil + heat + pressure | Labels, logos | High (sharp) | Medium |
| **In-mould labelling (IML)** | Printed PP label placed in mould | Injection moulded parts | High | High |
| **Digital / Inkjet** | UV-curable inks | Complex graphics | Very high | Variable |
| **Tampography** | Engraved cliché transfers ink | Pens, bottles | High | Medium |

### 1.2.8 Vacuum Metallization

The plastic part is placed in a **vacuum chamber (10⁻⁴ – 10⁻⁵ mbar)**. A small amount of aluminium is evaporated from a **tungsten filament (boat)** or by **electron-beam gun**.

$$ \text{Al}_{(\text{solid})} \xrightarrow{\Delta H_{\text{vap}} = 290 \text{ kJ/mol}} \text{Al}_{(\text{vapour})} $$
$$ \text{Al}_{(\text{vapour})} + \text{Cooled plastic surface} \rightarrow \text{Al}_{(\text{thin film, 50 – 100 nm})} $$

The vapour travels in straight lines → a **uniform metallic film** deposits. A top-coat of clear lacquer protects the soft aluminium layer.

### 1.2.9 Electroplating on Plastics (the KTU 2024 Hot Topic)

The full industrial sequence is **ABS → etch → neutralise → activate → accelerate → electroless → electrolytic**.

$$\text{Step 1: Etch} \rightarrow \text{Step 2: Neutralise} \rightarrow \text{Step 3: Predip} \rightarrow \text{Step 4: Activate (Pd/Sn colloid)} \rightarrow$$
$$\text{Step 5: Accelerator (H}_2\text{SO}_4\text{)} \rightarrow \text{Step 6: Electroless Ni or Cu} \rightarrow \text{Step 7: Electrolytic Cu} \rightarrow$$
$$\text{Step 8: Electrolytic Ni} \rightarrow \text{Step 9: Electrolytic Cr}$$

**Adhesion strength of plated ABS** ranges from **1.0 – 2.5 kN/m** (peel test) which is excellent.

### 1.2.10 KTU Formula Sheet — Plastic Surface Modification

| # | Equation / Parameter | Description | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\gamma_{sv} \ge \gamma_{lv} + \gamma_{sl}$ (modified Young's equation) | For complete wetting | mN/m |
| 2 | $S = \gamma_{sv} - (\gamma_{lv} + \gamma_{sl})$ | **Spreading coefficient**, S ≥ 0 means spontaneous spreading | mN/m |
| 3 | $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl}}{\gamma_{lv}}$ | Young's contact-angle equation | dimensionless |
| 4 | $W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}$ | **Work of adhesion** (Dupré) | mJ/m² |
| 5 | $\text{Dyne level of plastic} \ge \text{Dyne level of ink} + 8$ | Practical adhesion rule | dyne/cm |
| 6 | $\tau = \dfrac{F_{\text{peel}}}{w}$ | Peel adhesion strength | N/m or kN/m |
| 7 | $R_a$ (arith. mean roughness) = $\dfrac{1}{L}\int_0^L \vert y(x) \vert \, dx$ | Surface roughness (use $\vert$ in inline math only) | µm |
| 8 | $V_{\text{cathode}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{metal}}}{E \, I \, t}$ | Faraday's law for plating thickness | — |
| 9 | $m = \dfrac{M \, I \, t}{n \, F}$ | **Mass of metal deposited** (Faraday) | grams |
| 10 | $E_{\text{surface}} = \dfrac{\sigma_{polar}}{\sigma_{total}} \times 100$ | Wu's polar contribution analysis | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall — Never write** $\vert x \vert$ **(vertical pipes)** **inside a markdown table cell — the parser breaks.** Always write it as a standalone `$$ ... $$` block.

### 1.2.11 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Surface Modification Used | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Automotive bumpers (PP/EPDM) | Flame + Primer + PUR top-coat | Paint adhesion + UV durability |
| Automotive grills (ABS) | Etch → Electroless Cu → Electroplated Cr | Metallic finish |
| Mobile phone housings (PC/ABS) | Vacuum Al + UV clear coat | Premium look |
| HDPE fuel tanks | Fluorination (F₂ / N₂ gas) | Barrier against hydrocarbon permeation |
| Medical disposables (PP) | Plasma + γ-sterilization | Wettability for printing |
| Packaging films (BOPP, LDPE) | Corona treatment (online) | Inkm receptivity |
| EMI shielding enclosures (ABS/PC) | Electroless Cu + Electrolytic Cu | RFI shielding ≥ 60 dB |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 1.3 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 1.3.1 Derivation: Why Surface Energy Must Exceed Liquid Surface Tension

**Young's Equation (1805)** — foundational for the entire field of wetting & adhesion.

Consider a sessile liquid droplet resting on a smooth, rigid, homogeneous solid. At the three-phase contact line, three interfacial tensions act:

- $\gamma_{sv}$ — solid–vapour interfacial tension (pulls solid outward)
- $\gamma_{sl}$ — solid–liquid interfacial tension (pulls solid inward)
- $\gamma_{lv}$ — liquid–vapour interfacial tension (pulls tangentially along the droplet surface)

**Mechanical equilibrium at the contact line** (horizontal force balance):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_{sv} &= \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta \\
\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl} &= \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta \\
\cos\theta &= \frac{\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl}}{\gamma_{lv}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\theta$ is the **equilibrium contact angle**.

**For complete wetting**, $\theta = 0^\circ \Rightarrow \cos\theta = 1$:

$$
\gamma_{sv} \ge \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}
$$

**Spreading coefficient (S)** is defined as:

$$
S = \gamma_{sv} - (\gamma_{lv} + \gamma_{sl})
$$

- If $S > 0$ → liquid spreads spontaneously (perfect wetting).
- If $S < 0$ → droplet forms a finite contact angle (partial wetting).

**Dupré's work of adhesion** — the work required to separate 1 m² of solid–liquid interface:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_a &= \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl} \\
\text{Substituting Young's equation: } \cos\theta &= \frac{\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl}}{\gamma_{lv}} \\
W_a &= \gamma_{lv}(\cos\theta + 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical example (KTU-type numerical):**

Given: $\gamma_{sv} = 44$ mN/m, $\gamma_{lv} = 38$ mN/m, $\gamma_{sl} = 12$ mN/m. Find $\theta$, $S$, and $W_a$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos\theta &= \frac{44 - 12}{38} = \frac{32}{38} = 0.842 \\
\theta &= \cos^{-1}(0.842) \approx 32.6^\circ \\
S &= 44 - (38 + 12) = 44 - 50 = -6 \text{ mN/m (partial wetting)} \\
W_a &= 38 \times (0.842 + 1) = 38 \times 1.842 \approx 69.99 \text{ mJ/m}^2 \approx 70 \text{ mJ/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### 1.3.2 Derivation: Electroplating Thickness using Faraday's Law

Faraday's two laws of electrolysis combine into:

$$
m = \frac{M \, I \, t}{n \, F}
$$

where:
- $m$ = mass of metal deposited (g)
- $M$ = molar mass of the metal (g/mol)
- $I$ = current (A)
- $t$ = time (s)
- $n$ = valency / number of electrons transferred per ion
- $F$ = **Faraday constant = 96,485 C/mol**

**Thickness of deposit** (uniform over area A):

$$
\text{Thickness } t_{film} = \frac{m}{\rho \, A}
$$

where $\rho$ = density of deposited metal (g/cm³) and $A$ = coated area (cm²).

**KTU Numerical Example:**

A plastic part of surface area **500 cm²** is to be electroplated with **copper (Cu, n = 2, M = 63.5 g/mol, ρ = 8.96 g/cm³)** to a thickness of **30 µm**. Calculate the **current required** if plating time is fixed at **25 minutes**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Volume of Cu needed} &= A \times t_{film} = 500 \text{ cm}^2 \times 30 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm} \\
&= 1.5 \text{ cm}^3 \\
m &= V \times \rho = 1.5 \times 8.96 = 13.44 \text{ g} \\
I &= \frac{m \, n \, F}{M \, t} = \frac{13.44 \times 2 \times 96485}{63.5 \times (25 \times 60)} \\
I &= \frac{2\,593\,852.8}{95\,250} \approx 27.23 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Current density check:**
$$
\text{CD} = \frac{I}{A} = \frac{27.23}{500} = 0.0545 \text{ A/cm}^2 = 5.45 \text{ A/dm}^2
$$

This is within the standard range (2 – 6 A/dm²) for acid copper plating. ✔

### 1.3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Surface Modification

| Plastic | Recommended Activation | Recommended Decoration | Comments |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **PP** | Corona / Flame / Plasma | Painting, IML, Pad printing | Most difficult to decorate |
| **PE** | Corona (online) | Printing (films), Extrusion coating | Corona treated films: 38 – 42 dyne/cm |
| **ABS** | Chromic etch → Electroless Cu | Electroplating (Cr / Ni / Cu) | Industry standard for plating |
| **PC** | Mild corona / plasma | Pad print, screen, vacuum Al | Excellent clarity, UV sensitive |
| **PMMA** | None / mild solvent | Back-printed signage, IML | Already 38 – 40 dyne/cm |
| **PA (Nylon)** | None | Painting, printing | High surface energy, absorbs water |
| **PVC (rigid)** | Corona | Pad printing, screen | Plasticizer migration is an issue |
| **POM (Acetal)** | Special primer / etching | Printing only after primer | Low surface energy + poor adhesion |

### 1.3.4 Comparative Performance Matrix — Decorating Processes

| Property | Painting | Hot-stamping | Vacuum Metallization | Electroplating | IMD / IML |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Durability** | Good | Very Good | Moderate | Excellent | Very Good |
| **Cost (large volume)** | Medium | Low | Medium | High | Low |
| **Cost (small volume)** | High | High | High | Very High | Very High |
| **3-D capability** | Yes | Limited | Yes | Yes | Mould-shape only |
| **Metallic look** | No (unless metallic paint) | Yes | Yes (mirror) | Yes (chrome) | Yes (via foil) |
| **Cycle time** | Slow | Medium | Medium | Very Slow | Fast (single moulding) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 1.4 Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 1.4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Plastic Decoration Process Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Polymer Granules] --> B[Injection Moulding Machine]
    B --> C[Freshly Moulded Plastic Part]
    C --> D[Degreasing / Cleaning Station]
    D --> E[Surface Activation Unit]
    E --> F[Drying / Cooling Tunnel]
    F --> G{Application Zone}
    G --> G1[Spray Painting Booth]
    G --> G2[Pad Printing Station]
    G --> G3[Hot Stamping Press]
    G --> G4[Vacuum Metallization Chamber]
    G --> G5[Electroplating Bath Line]
    G1 --> H[Curing Oven]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> I[Inline Inspection]
    G4 --> J[Top Coat Application]
    J --> H
    H --> I
    G5 --> K[Multi-layer Plating Tank]
    K --> I
    I --> L[Quality Control]
    L --> M[Finished Decorated Part]
    M --> N[Packaging and Dispatch]
```

### 1.4.2 Block Diagram — Corona Discharge Treatment System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[High Frequency Generator 10 to 50 kHz] --> B[High Voltage Transformer]
    B --> C[Electrode Assembly]
    C --> D[Silicone Rubber Dielectric Roller]
    E[Plastic Film / Sheet Web] --> D
    D --> F[Corona Discharge Gap 1 to 2 mm]
    F --> G[Grounded Steel Roller]
    G --> H[Treated Plastic Web]
    H --> I[Dyne Test Station]
    I --> J[Rewind Roll]
```

### 1.4.3 Block Diagram — Electroplating Process on Plastic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[ABS Moulded Part] --> B[Etching Tank CrO3 and H2SO4 at 60 to 70 deg C]
    B --> C[Rinse Tank 1]
    C --> D[Neutralisation Tank Na2S2O3 or HCl]
    D --> E[Rinse Tank 2]
    E --> F[Pre-dip Tank]
    F --> G[Activation Tank Pd and Sn Colloid]
    G --> H[Rinse Tank 3]
    H --> I[Acceleration Tank H2SO4]
    I --> J[Rinse Tank 4]
    J --> K[Electroless Nickel or Copper Bath]
    K --> L[Rinse Tank 5]
    L --> M[Electrolytic Copper Bath]
    M --> N[Electrolytic Nickel Bath]
    N --> O[Electrolytic Chromium Bath]
    O --> P[Final Rinse and Drying]
    P --> Q[Plated Plastic Component]
```

### 1.4.4 Block Diagram — Decision Logic for Plastic Surface Treatment Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plastic Part to be Decorated] --> B{Is part film or 3D moulded?}
    B -- Film / Sheet --> C{Continuous web production?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Use Corona Treatment]
    C -- No --> E[Use Plasma Treatment]
    B -- 3D Moulded --> F{Polymer type?}
    F -- PP or PE --> G{Part size?}
    G -- Small --> H[Use Corona Jet or Plasma Pen]
    G -- Large --> I[Use Flame Treatment]
    F -- ABS --> J[Need metallic finish?]
    J -- Yes --> K[Use Etch + Electroless + Electroplating]
    J -- No --> L[Use Painting after Corona]
    F -- PC or PMMA --> M[Use Pad or Screen Printing]
    M --> N[Optional Top Coat]
    D --> O[Apply Ink / Adhesive]
    E --> O
    H --> O
    I --> O
    K --> O
    L --> O
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above flowcharts are **block-level functional architecture** diagrams. They map process interactions exactly as required by KTU 2024 board valuation rubrics (process flow marks → marks for accuracy of sequence).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 1.5 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define surface modification of plastics. Why is it necessary for low-surface-energy polymers like PP and PE?*

**Model Answer:**
Surface modification of plastics refers to the set of physical, chemical, electrochemical, and thermal treatments applied to the top layer (1 nm – 10 µm) of a finished plastic part to improve wettability, adhesion, printability, and barrier properties without affecting bulk characteristics.

PP and PE have very low surface energy (29 – 32 mN/m). Liquids such as printing inks, paints, and adhesives have surface tensions between 30 – 50 mN/m. As the **Young–Dupré condition** ($S = \gamma_{sv} - (\gamma_{lv} + \gamma_{sl}) > 0$) is not satisfied, droplets bead up and adhesion fails. Hence, surface modification is mandatory to introduce polar groups (-OH, -COOH, =O) and raise the dyne level of the polymer to ≥ 38 dyne/cm.

**(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**
*Briefly explain the principle of corona discharge treatment. State any two industrial applications.*

**Model Answer:**
Corona Discharge Treatment (CDT) is the process of exposing a polymer surface to a low-temperature ionized plasma generated by applying a high-frequency (10 – 50 kHz) high-voltage (10 – 30 kV) alternating current across a narrow air gap between an electrode and a grounded dielectric-covered roller.

The ionized air species (electrons, ozone, NOx) bombard the surface, abstract hydrogen atoms, and graft polar oxygen-containing groups (-OH, -COOH, =O), thereby raising surface energy from ~30 mN/m to ~46 mN/m within milliseconds.

**Two applications:**
1. Inline treatment of BOPP/LDPE films for printing and lamination.
2. Surface activation of PP sheets before printing and adhesive bonding.

**(3 marks)**

---

### **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Module 1, KTU 2024 Pattern**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **electroplating process on ABS plastic parts**. Describe in detail the role of **chromic acid etching** and the **electroless copper/nickel strike** in achieving adhesion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **physics and chemistry of corona discharge treatment** of plastic surfaces. Explain the **dyne test** method used to assess the level of surface activation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A (a):**

The electroplating of plastics is achieved through a sequence of wet chemical and electrochemical steps. The full process line is:

| Step | Bath | Composition / Condition | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Etching | CrO₃ (400 g/L) + H₂SO₄ (400 g/L), 65 °C, 5 – 10 min | Selective oxidation of polybutadiene rubber micro-domains of ABS |
| 2 | Rinsing | Tap water + DI water | Remove residual acid |
| 3 | Neutralising | HCl or Na₂S₂O₃, 5 % | Remove any residual chromate ions |
| 4 | Pre-dip | Same electrolyte as activator bath | Prevent contamination of activator |
| 5 | Activation | Pd/Sn colloidal solution (catalyst) | Deposit catalytic Pd nuclei |
| 6 | Acceleration | H₂SO₄ (10 %), 1 min | Strip Sn, expose Pd |
| 7 | Electroless Cu or Ni | CuSO₄ + HCHO + EDTA (or NiSO₄ + NaH₂PO₂) | Deposit thin 0.5 – 1 µm conductive seed layer without external current |
| 8 | Electrolytic Cu | CuSO₄ + H₂SO₄, 2 – 4 A/dm², 25 – 30 min | Build thickness to 20 – 30 µm |
| 9 | Electrolytic Ni | NiSO₄ + NiCl₂ + H₃BO₃, pH 4, 50 °C | Provide corrosion resistance |
| 10 | Electrolytic Cr | CrO₃ + H₂SO₄, 20 – 40 A/dm² | Provide decorative + wear layer |

**Role of chromic acid etching (the most critical step for adhesion):**

- ABS is a two-phase polymer: a continuous **styrene-acrylonitrile (SAN)** matrix and dispersed **polybutadiene (PB)** rubber particles.
- CrO₃ / H₂SO₄ **selectively etches the unsaturated C=C double bonds of polybutadiene** (oxidative cleavage), leaving behind **microscopic pores and undercuts** typically 0.1 – 1 µm deep.
- These pores serve as **mechanical anchor sites** for the subsequent metal layer — this is called **mechanical interlocking or "anchor effect"**.
- Without this micro-roughening, peel strength of the plated layer would fall below 0.2 kN/m (vs. ≥ 1.5 kN/m with proper etching).

**Role of electroless Cu/Ni strike:**

- After etching, the polymer is electrically **non-conductive**, so direct electrolytic deposition is impossible.
- The electroless bath uses **autocatalytic chemical reduction** of Cu²⁺ or Ni²⁺ ions (by formaldehyde or sodium hypophosphite) onto the **Pd-activated sites**.
- The result is a **uniform, electrically conductive seed layer of 0.5 – 1 µm** that allows the part to act as a cathode in subsequent electrolytic plating.

**(7 marks — distributed: 2 marks block diagram, 2 marks etch chemistry, 1.5 marks electroless strike, 1.5 marks sequence clarity)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A (b):**

**Physics of Corona Discharge:**

When a sinusoidal high-voltage (10 – 30 kV peak), high-frequency (10 – 50 kHz) AC potential is applied across an electrode and a dielectric-covered grounded roller, the **electric field in the 1 – 2 mm air gap exceeds the dielectric breakdown strength of air (~3 kV/mm)**. This causes **avalanche ionization** of the air, producing:

- **Free electrons** (very mobile, accelerated by the field)
- **Positive ions** (N₂⁺, O₂⁺)
- **Excited neutral species** (O, N, metastable)
- **UV photons**
- **Ozone (O₃)** and **nitrogen oxides (NO, NO₂)** — strong oxidisers

**Chemistry on the polymer surface:**

1. Energetic electrons bombard the polymer → abstract hydrogen atoms → form surface radicals.
2. Surface radicals react with O atoms, O₂, O₃ → form hydroperoxides (-OOH) → decompose to **carbonyl (C=O), hydroxyl (-OH), carboxyl (-COOH)** groups.
3. Nitrogen can also graft **amine (-NH₂)** groups.

These polar groups raise the **surface energy** of the polymer (PE from 30 to 46 mN/m) and improve wetting.

**Key process parameters:**
- Electrode voltage, frequency, gap distance.
- Web speed (1 – 600 m/min).
- Treatment level (W·min/m²) — typical industrial value: **38 – 45 W·min/m²** for BOPP.
- Number of electrodes (single, double, multiple).

**Dyne Test Method:**

A set of **dyne test inks** of calibrated surface tension is available (e.g., 30, 32, 34, 36, 38, 40, 42, 44, 46, 48, 50 dyne/cm). The procedure:

1. Using a cotton swab, draw a stripe of the ink on the activated surface.
2. Observe for **2 seconds**:
   - If the ink **remains a continuous unbroken film** → surface energy ≥ ink value.
   - If the ink **beads up, retracts, or shows dewetting** → surface energy < ink value.
3. Repeat with progressively higher dyne inks until the highest value that does not bead is identified.
4. **Acceptance standard for PP/PE**: ≥ 38 dyne/cm.

Other (more precise) methods: **Wilhelmy plate**, **Owens-Wendt-Rabel-Käble (OWRK) analysis** using contact angle goniometer.

**(7 marks — distributed: 2 marks physics, 2 marks chemistry, 1.5 marks parameters, 1.5 marks dyne test)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Module 1 Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternate]**

**(a)** Compare **vacuum metallization** and **electroplating** as techniques for providing a metallic finish to plastic parts. Discuss the operating principle, equipment, advantages, limitations, and typical applications of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the principle and process of **flame treatment** of plastic surfaces. Compare it with **corona discharge treatment** in terms of substrate suitability, equipment, surface modification effect, and application areas. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B (a):**

| Aspect | Vacuum Metallization | Electroplating |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Operating principle** | Physical vapour deposition of evaporated metal (Al) under high vacuum | Electrochemical reduction of metal ions onto an electrically conductive plastic surface |
| **Equipment** | Vacuum chamber (10⁻⁴ mbar), Al evaporation source (tungsten boat or e-beam gun), top-coat spray booth | Multi-tank plating line, rectifier, heaters, filters, ventilation |
| **Metal used** | Aluminium (95 % of cases), also Cu, Ag, Au | Cu (build), Ni (barrier), Cr (decorative) |
| **Layer thickness** | 50 – 150 nm | 20 – 40 µm (Cu) + 10 µm (Ni) + 0.3 µm (Cr) |
| **Surface energy / Adhesion** | Moderate; needs base coat | Excellent; ≥ 1.5 kN/m peel strength |
| **Durability** | Moderate (soft Al, scratches easily) — needs top coat | Excellent (hard Cr surface) |
| **Appearance** | Bright mirror finish | Bright chrome-look |
| **Cost** | Moderate | High (multi-step, chemicals) |
| **Cycle time** | Short (one batch ~ 30 min) | Long (several hours per batch) |
| **Environmental** | Clean; no toxic effluents | Toxic effluents; needs ETP |
| **Typical applications** | Headlamp reflectors, decorative trims, toys, packaging | Bathroom fittings, automotive grills, nameplates |

**Vacuum metallization working detail:**

1. Part is **base-coated** (acrylic primer) and dried to seal surface defects.
2. Loaded into vacuum chamber; pump-down to 10⁻⁴ mbar.
3. Al pellets / wire fed onto **tungsten filament** heated to 1200 – 1500 °C.
4. Al evaporates → travels in straight lines → deposits uniformly on the cool plastic.
5. After venting, a **clear top coat (UV or 2-pack PU)** is applied for scratch and abrasion protection.

**Electroplating working detail:**

1. Etch → activate → accelerate → electroless strike (covered above).
2. Electrolytic copper build-up to 20 – 40 µm.
3. Semi-bright nickel → bright nickel → microporous nickel (multi-layer for corrosion).
4. Decorative chromium plating (0.25 – 0.5 µm).
5. Final rinses → hot DI rinse → dry-off.

**(7 marks — distributed: 2 marks principle, 2 marks comparison table, 1.5 marks working detail, 1.5 marks applications)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B (b):**

**Flame Treatment — Principle:**

A controlled gas-air flame is impinged on the polymer surface for a brief moment (0.01 – 0.05 s). The flame temperature in the **oxidative zone** (~1400 °C) thermally cracks and oxidizes the top 5 – 10 nm of the polymer surface, introducing polar functional groups (-OH, C=O, -COOH).

**Equipment:**

- Ribbon burner (slotted, stainless steel).
- Gas train (propane / butane / natural gas + air → mixer → burner).
- Gas-flow controllers.
- Conveyor or rotary indexing table.

**Process sequence:**

1. **Moulded part** is loaded onto a conveyor.
2. The ribbon burner is positioned 5 – 50 mm above the part.
3. Air-gas ratio is set so that the **oxidative zone** contacts the surface — typically **stoichiometric-to-slightly-rich** combustion.
4. Exposure time: **0.02 – 0.1 s**.
5. The treated part is allowed to cool and is then immediately coated/painted (within minutes to retain activation).

**Comparison — Flame vs Corona Treatment:**

| Aspect | Flame Treatment | Corona Discharge |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Substrate** | 3-D thick moulded parts | Films, sheets, thin-walled parts |
| **Equipment cost** | Low | Low–Moderate |
| **Activation depth** | 5 – 10 nm | 0.5 – 5 nm |
| **Treatment level achievable** | Up to 56 dyne/cm | Up to 50 dyne/cm |
| **Speed (continuous)** | 100 – 600 parts/min | Up to 600 m/min film |
| **Effect on bulk polymer** | Risk of distortion if mis-set | Negligible |
| **Operator skill** | Higher (gas-air ratio critical) | Lower |
| **3-D capability** | Excellent (rotary indexing) | Limited to flat surfaces |
| **Typical applications** | Automotive bumpers, dashboards, blow-moulded bottles, containers | BOPP/LDPE/PP films, laminates, sheets |

**(7 marks — distributed: 2 marks flame principle, 2 marks process & equipment, 3 marks comparison)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Plastic Surface Modification Questions:**
> 1. **Never write "corona discharge modifies the bulk polymer"** — the modification depth is **nanometres**, not micrometres.
> 2. **Confusing chromic acid etching with sulphuric acid alone** — only the **CrO₃ + H₂SO₄ combination** produces the correct anchor pattern on ABS. Pure H₂SO₄ will not work.
> 3. **Writing "plasma = flame treatment"** — plasma is non-thermal (electronic temperature >> gas temperature), flame is a thermal process.
> 4. **Forgetting the top coat** in vacuum metallization — Al layer is only ~ 80 nm and **scratches immediately**; top coat is mandatory.
> 5. **Wrong unit of dyne** — dyne = 10⁻⁵ N, and **dyne/cm = mN/m** (numerically equal). KTU students often write dyne/m² which is wrong.
> 6. **Mixing up electrolytic and electroless plating** — electroless uses **no external current**, electrolytic requires a rectifier.
> 7. **Forget Faraday's constant (96,485 C/mol)** in any electroplating numerical.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The five classes** of plastic surface modification: **Chemical, Physical, Electrical/Electrochemical, Thermal/Radiation, Coatings/Added Layers.**
- **Low surface energy polymers (PP, PE, PTFE)** need activation before any decoration.
- **Corona Discharge Treatment (CDT)** — high-voltage AC + dielectric barrier → polar groups → surface energy ↑ from 30 → 46 mN/m.
- **Dyne level standard**: For PE/PP film, target **≥ 38 dyne/cm** for printing.
- **Flame Treatment** — best for thick 3-D PP parts (bumpers, dashboards).
- **Plasma Treatment** — cleanest, lowest temperature, for medical / electronics.
- **Chromic acid etching** of ABS selectively removes polybutadiene → **anchor pattern → peel strength ≥ 1.5 kN/m**.
- **Electroplating sequence on plastic**: Etch → Neutralise → Activate (Pd/Sn) → Accelerate → Electroless Cu/Ni → Electrolytic Cu → Ni → Cr.
- **Vacuum metallization** deposits **50 – 150 nm of Al** under 10⁻⁴ mbar vacuum → requires a top coat.
- **Young's equation**: $\cos\theta = \frac{\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl}}{\gamma_{lv}}$ governs all wetting behaviour.
- **Spreading condition**: $S = \gamma_{sv} - (\gamma_{lv} + \gamma_{sl}) > 0$.
- **Dupré's work of adhesion**: $W_a = \gamma_{lv}(\cos\theta + 1)$.
- **Faraday's plating law**: $m = \dfrac{M \, I \, t}{n \, F}$, where $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol.
- **In-mould decoration (IMD / IML / IMF)** integrates decoration into the moulding cycle — highest durability.
- **Painting cycle**: Degrease → Activate → Primer → Base coat → Top coat → Cure (always below the plastic's HDT).
- **Hot stamping** uses dry foil + heat + pressure → sharp metallic / pigmented logos on plastics.
- **Pad printing** uses a silicone pad to transfer ink from a cliché to curved 3-D parts.
- **Plasma vs Corona**: plasma is more uniform and effective but costlier; corona is the workhorse for films.
- **Fluorination of HDPE** improves hydrocarbon barrier → used for automotive fuel tanks.
- **Top-coat on vacuum-metallized plastic** is non-negotiable for scratch resistance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plastic Surface Conductivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT864 - PLASTICS & DECORATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Plastic Surface Conductivity # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Plastic Surface Conductivity** is the engineered ability of an otherwise electrically insulating polymer substrate to conduct electrical current along its surface. Since base plastics have volume resistivities in the order of $10^{14}$ to $10^{18}\ \Omega\cdot\text{cm}$, surface conductivity must be *induced* through secondary treatments, fillers, or coatings. Surface conductivity is quantified by **Surface Resistivity ($\rho_s$)**, measured in **ohms per square ($\Omega/\square$ or $\Omega/\text{sq}$)**. It is the resistance between two opposing edges of a square of any size on the material's surface. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Surface conductivity in plastics refers to the deliberate modification of the outermost layer (skin) of a polymer article to allow dissipation of static charges or conduction of electricity, without necessarily altering the bulk (core) dielectric properties of the polymer. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **dry wooden floor in winter**. Walking across it with socks, electrons transfer from the carpet to your body, and because the wood is an insulator, your body retains the charge — giving you a shock when you touch a doorknob. Now imagine coating that same wooden floor with a thin **layer of aluminum paint**. The floor is still made of wood (insulator underneath), but the **surface** can now carry charges away harmlessly. The floor has gained "surface conductivity." > [!NOTE] > **Plastic surface conductivity is the same concept:** the polymer body remains an insulator, but a specially engineered *surface layer* allows electrons to glide across, like skaters on ice, instead of staying trapped and causing ESD (Electrostatic Discharge) damage. ## 1.3 Why Surface Conductivity Matters in Plastics Engineering Plastics are inherently insulators. However, in modern engineering applications they must often *behave* like conductors on the surface. This is needed for: - **ESD Protection** — preventing damage to microchips during handling. - **EMI/RFI Shielding** — blocking electromagnetic interference in enclosures. - **Electrostatic Painting** — enabling paint to adhere uniformly to auto parts. - **Printed Circuit Board (PCB) Substrates** — copper-clad laminates. - **Capacitive Touch Surfaces** — touchscreens, control panels. - **Heating Elements** — defrosting windows, medical blankets. ## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics | Metric | Symbol | Unit | Typical Target | |---|---|---|---| | Surface Resistivity | $\rho_s$ | $\Omega/\square$ | $10^{3}$–$10^{9}$ for antistatic | | Static Decay Time | $t_d$ | seconds | $< 2$ s (MIL-STD-3010) | | Shielding Effectiveness | SE | dB | $> 40$ dB for commercial EMI | | Charge Decay Rate | $dq/dt$ | $\mu\text{C/s}$ | Lower = better dissipation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Constant / Reference Value:** The threshold of antistatic behavior is **$\rho_s < 10^{12}\ \Omega/\square$**. Below $10^{9}\ \Omega/\square$ is considered static-dissipative, and below $10^{3}\ \Omega/\square$ is considered conductive. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Plastics by Surface Behavior KTU 2024 classifies plastics by their electrical surface response into three tiers: 1. **Insulative Plastics** — $\rho_s > 10^{12}\ \Omega/\square$ (e.g., PTFE, PE, PP — virgin). 2. **Static-Dissipative Plastics** — $10^{6} \leq \rho_s \leq 10^{12}\ \Omega/\square$ (slow charge bleed-off). 3. **Conductive Plastics** — $\rho_s < 10^{5}\ \Omega/\square$ (rapid charge transfer; EMI shielding capable). ## 2.2 Methods of Achieving Surface Conductivity — Structured Logic Steps ### A. Surface Coating Methods - **Conductive Paints / Inks:** Silver, copper, nickel, or carbon pigment dispersed in a polymeric binder. Applied by spray, screen-print, or dip-coating. - **Vacuum Metallization:** Aluminum or copper vapor-deposited under high vacuum ($10^{-4}$–$10^{-6}\ \text{torr}$). Sub-types: Thermal evaporation, Sputtering. - **Electroless Plating:** Catalytic deposition of Ni, Cu, or Ag from aqueous solution without external current. The most important industrial method for plastics. ### B. Bulk + Surface Hybrid Methods - **Conductive Filler Compounding:** Carbon black, carbon fibers, metal fibers, or graphite blended into resin, then molded. Conductivity arises from a percolation network that intersects the surface. - **Inherently Conductive Polymers (ICPs):** Polyaniline, polypyrrole, PEDOT:PSS blended or coated onto the surface. ### C. Surface Activation + Metallization - **Plasma Treatment** (corona, atmospheric plasma) → enhances surface energy → improves adhesion of subsequent metallization. - **Chemical Etching** (chromic-sulfuric acid) → creates micro-roughness and polar groups → enables strong bonding of electroless copper/nickel. > [!NOTE] > **"Why" and "How" logic:** Plastics are insulators because their carbon backbones have all valence electrons locked in covalent $\sigma$-bonds. Surface conductivity methods work by either (a) introducing free electrons via a metallic layer, OR (b) creating conjugated $\pi$-bond systems (in ICPs) that allow electron delocalization, OR (c) providing a percolating carbon/metal pathway that touches the surface. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Surface Resistance | $R_s = \dfrac{V}{I}$ | $\Omega$ | Ohm's law applied between two surface electrodes | | 2 | Surface Resistivity | $\rho_s = R_s \cdot \dfrac{w}{l}$ | $\Omega/\square$ | For a rectangle: $w$ = electrode width, $l$ = gap length. **For a square, $w = l$, so $\rho_s = R_s$ numerically.** | | 3 | Surface Conductivity | $\sigma_s = \dfrac{1}{\rho_s}$ | $\text{S}/\square$ | Siemens per square | | 4 | Shielding Effectiveness (Absorption) | $SE_A = 8.686 \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$ | dB | $t$ = thickness, $f$ = frequency, $\mu$ = permeability, $\sigma$ = conductivity | | 5 | Shielding Effectiveness (Reflection) | $SE_R = 39.5 + 10 \log\left(\dfrac{\sigma}{2\pi f \mu}\right)$ | dB | Dominant at low frequencies | | 6 | Skin Depth | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}}$ | m | Depth at which current density falls to $1/e$ | | 7 | Percolation Threshold | $\sigma = \sigma_0 (p - p_c)^t$ | S/m | $p$ = filler fraction, $p_c$ = critical fraction, $t$ = critical exponent | > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall Alert:** Do NOT confuse $\Omega/\square$ (ohms per square) with $\Omega\cdot\text{cm}^2$. The "per square" means a unitless geometric factor — the resistance of *any* square of material is the same regardless of size. Always use $\vert x \vert$ notation in exam answers if absolute values appear in formula manipulation. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility - **Automotive industry:** Electrostatic painting of PP bumper fascia — requires $\rho_s \approx 10^{8}$–$10^{10}\ \Omega/\square$. - **Electronics packaging:** IC trays must have $\rho_s \approx 10^{6}$–$10^{9}\ \Omega/\square$ to safely dissipate ESD. - **Aerospace:** Carbon-fiber-reinforced composites achieve EMI shielding of **60–80 dB** in aircraft housings. - **Medical:** Conductive plastic housings for MRI rooms prevent RF interference. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Surface Resistivity from a Rectangular Geometry We begin with two parallel electrodes of length $L$, separated by a gap $g$, placed on a flat plastic surface. **Step 1 — Start with Ohm's Law.** The measured resistance between the electrodes is: $$ R_s = \dfrac{V}{I} $$ **Step 2 — Express Resistance Geometrically.** For a thin surface layer of negligible thickness carrying current in a rectangular pattern of width $w$ and length $g$: $$ R_s = \rho_s \cdot \dfrac{g}{w} $$ **Step 3 — Solve for Surface Resistivity.** Rearranging: $$ \rho_s = R_s \cdot \dfrac{w}{g} $$ **Step 4 — Demonstrate the "Per Square" Property.** If we choose $w = g$ (a perfect square of side $g$), then: $$ \rho_s = R_s \cdot \dfrac{g}{g} = R_s $$ This confirms that the numerical value of surface resistivity equals the measured resistance **only when the sample is a square**. The unit is therefore written as $\Omega/\square$, not $\Omega$. ## 3.2 Derivation: Total Shielding Effectiveness (SE) The total SE is the sum of three mechanisms: $$ SE_{total} = SE_R + SE_A + SE_M $$ Where $SE_R$ is reflection loss, $SE_A$ is absorption loss, $SE_M$ is multiple-reflection correction (often negligible if $SE_A > 10$ dB). **Step 1 — Reflection Loss for a Conductive Surface.** $$ SE_R = 39.5 + 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\sigma}{2 \pi f \mu}\right) $$ **Step 2 — Absorption Loss.** $$ SE_A = 8.686 \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma} $$ **Step 3 — Combine.** $$ SE_{total} = 39.5 + 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\sigma}{2 \pi f \mu}\right) + 8.686 \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma} $$ > This is the **Schelkunoff equation** for EMI shielding, routinely tested in KTU board problems. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A plastic enclosure is coated with a 50 µm electroless nickel layer. The conductivity of nickel is $\sigma = 1.45 \times 10^{7}\ \text{S/m}$, $\mu_r \approx 100$. Calculate the shielding effectiveness at $f = 1\ \text{GHz}$. **Step 1 — Identify Parameters.** $$ \sigma = 1.45 \times 10^{7}\ \text{S/m} $$ $$ \mu = \mu_0 \mu_r = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 100 = 1.257 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{H/m} $$ $$ t = 50\ \mu\text{m} = 50 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m} $$ $$ f = 10^{9}\ \text{Hz} $$ **Step 2 — Calculate $SE_R$.** $$ SE_R = 39.5 + 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{1.45 \times 10^{7}}{2 \pi \times 10^{9} \times 1.257 \times 10^{-4}}\right) $$ Inside the logarithm: $$ \dfrac{1.45 \times 10^{7}}{7.90 \times 10^{5}} \approx 18.35 $$ $$ \log_{10}(18.35) = 1.264 $$ $$ SE_R \approx 39.5 + 12.64 = 52.14\ \text{dB} $$ **Step 3 — Calculate $SE_A$.** $$ SE_A = 8.686 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times \sqrt{\pi \times 10^{9} \times 1.257 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.45 \times 10^{7}} $$ Inside the square root: $$ \pi \times 10^{9} \times 1.257 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.45 \times 10^{7} = 5.726 \times 10^{12} $$ $$ \sqrt{5.726 \times 10^{12}} = 2.393 \times 10^{6} $$ $$ SE_A = 8.686 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 2.393 \times 10^{6} = 1039.1\ \text{dB} $$ **Step 4 — Total SE.** $$ SE_{total} \approx 52.14 + 1039.1 \approx 1091\ \text{dB} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** In practice, values above ~120 dB are capped by measurement limits. Real-world plastics achieve **40–100 dB** depending on coating method. The model shows absorption dominates at high frequencies for thin coatings. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Surface Conductivity Methods | Method | Typical $\rho_s$ ($\Omega/\square$) | Thickness | Adhesion | Cost | Best Application | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Carbon-filled coating | $10^{3}$–$10^{6}$ | 10–25 µm | Fair | Low | Antistatic trays | | Silver paint | $10^{-2}$–$10^{0}$ | 10–30 µm | Good | High | PCB repairs, EMI gaskets | | Electroless Cu/Ni | $10^{-3}$–$10^{-1}$ | 0.5–5 µm | Excellent | Medium | EMI enclosures, decoratives | | Vacuum Al metallization | $10^{0}$–$10^{2}$ | 0.05–0.5 µm | Poor without primer | Medium | Decorative, reflective | | Sputtered Cu | $10^{-2}$–$10^{0}$ | 0.1–2 µm | Excellent | High | Heaters, flexible circuits | | ICP (PEDOT:PSS) | $10^{2}$–$10^{5}$ | 0.1–1 µm | Good | Very High | Flexible electronics | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Surface Conductivity Methods ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Plastic Substrate
Insulator rho_s greater than 10 to power 12] --> B{Surface Conductivity Method} B --> C[Coating Route] B --> D[Activation plus Metallization] B --> E[Bulk plus Surface Hybrid] C --> C1[Conductive Paints
Silver Copper Carbon] C --> C2[Vacuum Metallization
Evaporation Sputtering] D --> D1[Plasma or Corona Etching] D --> D2[Electroless Plating
Ni Cu Ag] E --> E1[Conductive Filler Compounding
Carbon Black Metal Fibers] E --> E2[Inherently Conductive Polymers
PEDOT PSS Polyaniline] C1 --> F[Functional Surface
rho_s between 10 to power 0 and 10 to power 9] C2 --> F D1 --> D2 D2 --> F E1 --> F E2 --> F F --> G[Application Outcomes] G --> G1[ESD Dissipation] G --> G2[EMI Shielding] G --> G3[Electrostatic Painting] G --> G4[Heater Elements] G --> G5[Capacitive Touch] ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow: Electroless Plating (Most Important Industrial Method) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Step 1 Cleaning] --> P2[Step 2 Etching Chromic Sulfuric] P2 --> P3[Step 3 Neutralizing] P3 --> P4[Step 4 Catalysis Pd Sn Colloid] P4 --> P5[Step 5 Acceleration] P5 --> P6[Step 6 Electroless Nickel Bath] P6 --> P7[Step 7 Electroless Copper Bath] P7 --> P8[Step 8 Rinse and Dry] P8 --> P9[Conductive Plastic Surface] ``` ## 4.3 Schematic of a Four-Point Probe Surface Resistivity Test ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph S[Plastic Surface Sample] direction LR E1[Outer Electrode 1] --> G1[Gap L] G1 --> E2[Inner Electrode 2] E2 --> G2[Gap L] G2 --> E3[Inner Electrode 3] E3 --> G3[Gap L] G3 --> E4[Outer Electrode 4] end SRC[Current Source I] --> E1 E4 --> SRC VM[Voltmeter V] --- E2 VM --- E3 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** A current $I$ is forced through the outer two electrodes. Voltage $V$ is sensed across the inner two. This four-point (Kelvin) method eliminates contact resistance, giving accurate $\rho_s$ values. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define surface resistivity of a plastic. Why is it expressed in ohms per square rather than ohms?** **Model Answer:** Surface resistivity $\rho_s$ is the resistance to current flow along the surface of a material, measured between two parallel electrodes. It is expressed in $\Omega/\square$ (ohms per square) because, for a square sample, the resistance is independent of the absolute size of the square — only the material property matters. This makes it a true intensive property of the surface, analogous to density. **(3 marks)** ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Differentiate between static-dissipative and conductive plastics with examples.** **Model Answer:** - **Static-dissipative plastics:** $\rho_s$ between $10^{6}$ and $10^{12}\ \Omega/\square$. Slow charge bleed-off. Used for IC trays, packaging. Example: Carbon-black-loaded PP. - **Conductive plastics:** $\rho_s < 10^{5}\ \Omega/\square$. Rapid charge transfer. Used for EMI enclosures. Example: Electroless-nickel-plated ABS. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > - **NOT writing units** on $\rho_s$ results in 0.5–1 mark loss. > - **Confusing volume resistivity** ($\Omega\cdot\text{cm}$) **with surface resistivity** ($\Omega/\square$) is a fatal error. Read the question carefully. > - **Skipping the "why" of "per square"** in Part (a) questions = lose 2 marks. > - In EMI shielding sums, forgetting to convert $\mu\text{m}$ to $\text{m}$ = full step zero. --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** **(a) Explain in detail the various methods used to make plastics surface conductive. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The various methods are: 1. **Conductive Coatings:** Silver, copper, or carbon-based paints applied by spray/dip. $\rho_s$ reaches $10^{0}$–$10^{6}\ \Omega/\square$. *(1 mark)* 2. **Vacuum Metallization:** Aluminum evaporated under vacuum ($10^{-4}$–$10^{-6}$ torr). Thin, decorative, poor adhesion. *(1 mark)* 3. **Sputtering:** Plasma-driven deposition of Cu, Ag, or Al. Better adhesion than evaporation. *(1 mark)* 4. **Electroless Plating:** Chemical (no current) deposition of Ni or Cu on catalyzed plastic. Best industrial method. Steps: clean → etch → neutralize → catalyze (Pd/Sn) → accelerate → plate. *(2 marks)* 5. **Conductive Fillers:** Carbon black, graphite, metal fibers compounded with resin. Conductivity via percolation at critical loading. *(1 mark)* 6. **Inherently Conductive Polymers (ICPs):** Polyaniline, polypyrrole, PEDOT:PSS — conjugated backbone enables intrinsic electron delocalization. *(1 mark)* **(b) Discuss the importance of surface conductivity for ESD and EMI applications. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **ESD (Electrostatic Discharge) Protection:** - Plastics with $\rho_s < 10^{9}\ \Omega/\square$ dissipate triboelectric charges slowly and safely. - Critical in semiconductor and electronics packaging — prevents microchip damage during handling. - Quantified by **static decay time** (target < 2 s, MIL-STD-3010). *(3 marks)* **EMI (Electromagnetic Interference) Shielding:** - Conductive surface acts as a Faraday cage, reflecting and absorbing incident EM waves. - Quantified by **Shielding Effectiveness (SE)** in dB. Targets: 30 dB (consumer), 60 dB (military/aerospace), 80 dB (medical). - Achieved by electroless Cu/Ni plating (most common), conductive paints, or carbon-loaded composites. *(3 marks)* - The **Schelkunoff equation** governs SE: $SE = 50 + 10 \log(\rho/(\mu_r f)) + 1.7 t \sqrt{f \mu_r / \rho}$. *(1 mark)* --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]** **(a) With a neat diagram, describe the four-point probe method for measuring surface resistivity. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Principle:** Four equally spaced collinear electrodes are pressed onto the plastic surface. Current $I$ is passed through the outer two electrodes, and voltage $V$ is measured across the inner two. **Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.3 above — the four-point probe schematic.) **Working Formula:** For a thin surface with sample dimension $>>$ probe spacing $s$: $$ \rho_s = \dfrac{\pi}{\ln 2} \cdot \dfrac{V}{I} \approx 4.532 \cdot \dfrac{V}{I} $$ **Why four points?** Eliminates contact and lead resistance — current flows only through outer pair, voltage sensed without current draw at inner pair. *(2 marks for diagram + 2 marks for principle + 2 marks for formula + 1 mark for advantages)* **Advantages:** - Eliminates contact resistance error. - Non-destructive. - Independent of sample geometry if sample is large. **(b) A plastic substrate is metallized with copper ($\sigma = 5.96 \times 10^7$ S/m) to a thickness of 2 µm. Calculate the absorption shielding effectiveness at 100 MHz. Take $\mu_r = 1$. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Given Data:** $\sigma = 5.96 \times 10^7\ \text{S/m}$ $t = 2 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$ $f = 100 \times 10^6\ \text{Hz}$ $\mu_r = 1$, so $\mu = \mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m} = 1.257 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{H/m}$ **Step 2 — Apply Absorption Loss Formula:** $$ SE_A = 8.686 \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma} $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate Inside the Root:** $$ \pi f \mu \sigma = \pi \times 10^{8} \times 1.257 \times 10^{-6} \times 5.96 \times 10^7 $$ $$ = \pi \times 7.49 \times 10^{9} = 2.354 \times 10^{10} $$ **Step 4 — Take Square Root:** $$ \sqrt{2.354 \times 10^{10}} = 1.534 \times 10^{5} $$ **Step 5 — Final Calculation:** $$ SE_A = 8.686 \times 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.534 \times 10^{5} $$ $$ SE_A = 26.65\ \text{dB} $$ **Conclusion:** Even a 2 µm copper layer provides ~27 dB absorption loss, suitable for basic EMI shielding of plastic enclosures. *(7 marks: 1 for data + 2 for setup + 2 for calculation + 2 for result & interpretation)* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Surface resistivity unit is ALWAYS $\Omega/\square$** — never $\Omega\cdot\text{cm}$ (that's volume resistivity). - **Antistatic threshold:** $\rho_s < 10^{12}\ \Omega/\square$. - **Conductive threshold:** $\rho_s < 10^{5}\ \Omega/\square$. - **Electroless plating is the industrial gold standard** for permanent surface conductivity on plastics. - **EMI shielding equation** (Schelkunoff): $SE = SE_R + SE_A + SE_M$. - **Skin depth** decreases with frequency — at GHz, current flows only in the topmost few microns. - **Four-point probe** is the accurate method to measure $\rho_s$ without contact-resistance error. - **Percolation threshold** ($p_c$) is the minimum filler loading for bulk conductivity; for surface conductivity, a smaller loading suffices if the network intersects the surface. - **Inherently Conductive Polymers (ICPs)** like PEDOT:PSS are emerging for flexible electronics. - **Always convert $\mu$m to m** in shielding calculations — examiners check this step strictly. - **Static decay time < 2 s** is the MIL-STD reference for ESD-safe plastics. - For decorative applications, vacuum Al + lacquer topcoat is the most economical route. - For functional EMI enclosures, electroless Cu + electroless Ni (Cu-Ni dual layer) is preferred.

topics.title: Aerosol Paints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT864 - PLASTICS & DECORATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Aerosol Paints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Aerosol Paint** is a pressurised, hermetically sealed, self-contained packaging system in which a liquid paint formulation is dispensed through a manually actuated valve as a fine, atomised cloud of droplets by the energy supplied by a compressed or liquefied propellant. The product conforms to the global standard **ISO 2249 / ASTM D3064** for "Aerosol Products" and is governed by FEA (Fédération Européenne des Aérosols) performance guidelines.

In the context of **PEPOT864 — Plastics & Decoration**, aerosol paints are studied as a **high-value surface-coating delivery system** used for decorative finishing, protective coating, and customised aesthetic applications on plastics, metals, wood, ceramics, and composite substrates.

The product is a *three-phase thermodynamic system* consisting of:
1. **Liquid phase** — paint concentrate (resin binder + pigment + solvent + additives)
2. **Vapour phase** — propellant gas in equilibrium
3. **Dispensed phase** — atomised droplets ejected into the atmosphere

## 1.2 The Five Functional Sub-Systems of an Aerosol Can

| # | Sub-system | Engineering Function |
|---|------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Container (tin-plated steel / aluminium) | Pressure vessel, hermetic seal |
| 2 | Paint concentrate | Provides film-forming matrix, colour, gloss |
| 3 | Propellant | Generates internal pressure for atomisation |
| 4 | Valve + dip tube | Metering and directional control |
| 5 | Actuator (button) + nozzle | Atomisation geometry, spray pattern |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Shaking Soda Bottle" Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine a sealed soda bottle that has been vigorously shaken. The dissolved CO₂ is under pressure, and the moment you open the cap, the liquid rushes out as a fine fizz. An aerosol paint can is essentially the *same thermodynamic principle* — only that:
> - The "drink" is replaced by paint
> - The "CO₂" is replaced by a precisely engineered propellant (LPG, DME, or HFC-152a)
> - The "cap" is replaced by a metering valve that controls the *flow rate* and *droplet size*

The internal pressure of a standard aerosol can is typically **0.4 – 0.8 MPa (4 – 8 bar)** at 25 °C, and the propellant mass fraction is **25 % – 50 %** of the total can content.

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Board-Exam Ready)

> [!NOTE]
> Standardised industry parameters every KTU student must memorise:
> - **Internal pressure:** 0.4 – 0.8 MPa (at 25 °C)
> - **Can volume:** 150 mL, 250 mL, 400 mL, 500 mL
> - **Spray rate:** 0.8 – 1.5 g/s (consumer grade)
> - **Mean droplet diameter (Dv 0.5):** 30 – 80 µm
> - **VOC content (modern EU limit):** < 680 g/L
> - **Coverage:** 1.5 – 2.5 m² per 400 mL can (single coat)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Aerosol Functionality

The system works through three coupled engineering principles:

### Pillar 1 — Pressurisation (Thermodynamics)
The propellant occupies the headspace above the paint and exerts a *constant vapour pressure* characteristic of its boiling point. As paint is dispensed, liquid propellant vaporises to maintain equilibrium — this is why the can remains "pressurised until the very last drop".

### Pillar 2 — Metering (Fluid Mechanics)
The dip tube carries liquid concentrate from the bottom of the can up to the valve chamber. The valve restricts flow, and the actuator nozzle accelerates the liquid to a velocity sufficient to shear it into droplets via **Rayleigh instability**.

### Pillar 3 — Atomisation (Surface Physics)
The high-velocity jet undergoes **primary atomisation** (ligament breakup) and **secondary atomisation** (droplet collision/coalescence in the spray cone), giving the characteristic hollow-cone or flat-fan spray pattern.

## 2.2 Classification of Propellants (Critical for Exam)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Liquefied Gas Propellants (LGPs)** — *most common in decorative aerosols*
> - **LPG** (Liquefied Petroleum Gas: n-butane / iso-butane / propane blend) — BP = −0.5 °C, Vapour P ≈ 0.21 MPa
> - **DME** (Dimethyl Ether) — BP = −24.8 °C, Vapour P ≈ 0.51 MPa — high solvency
> - **HFC-152a** (1,1-difluoroethane) — eco-friendly alternative, low GWP
>
> **Compressed Gas Propellants (CGPs)** — *used in non-flammable / industrial aerosols*
> - **N₂, CO₂, Air** — pressure drops as product is dispensed (unlike LGPs)

| Parameter | LPG (Butane) | DME | CO₂ |
|-----------|--------------|-----|-----|
| Boiling point (°C) | −0.5 | −24.8 | −78.5 (sublimes) |
| Vapour pressure @ 25 °C (MPa) | 0.21 | 0.51 | 6.4 |
| Flammability | High | High | None |
| Solvent power | Low | High | None |
| Pressure consistency | Constant | Constant | Drops with use |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` and `\mid` (never bare `\vert`) inside Markdown tables to avoid breaking the table parser.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $P V = n R T$ | Ideal gas law (for propellant in headspace) | P [Pa], V [m³], n [mol] |
| 2 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | Clausius–Clapeyron (propellant vapour pressure vs temperature) | P [Pa], T [K], $\Delta H_{vap}$ [J/mol] |
| 3 | $Q = C_d \, A \, \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | Volumetric flow rate through valve orifice | Q [m³/s], A [m²] |
| 4 | $\dot{m} = \rho \, Q$ | Mass flow rate of paint concentrate | $\dot{m}$ [kg/s] |
| 5 | $A_{cov} = \dfrac{m_{paint}}{\rho_{film} \cdot t_{dry}}$ | Surface area covered by a single can | A [m²], m [kg], t [m] |
| 6 | $P_{can} = P_{prop}^{sat}(T) + \rho_{liq} \, g \, h$ | Hydrostatic head correction (deep cans) | P [Pa], h [m] |
| 7 | $D_{v0.5} = k \, \dfrac{\mu^{0.5} \sigma^{0.25}}{\Delta P^{0.5} \rho^{0.25}}$ | Sauter mean diameter (atomisation) | D [m] |
| 8 | $\eta_{can} = \dfrac{m_{dispensed}}{m_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Can emptying efficiency | $\eta$ [%] |

where:
- $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (typically 0.6 – 0.85)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure differential across valve
- $\rho$ = density of liquid concentrate
- $\sigma$ = surface tension [N/m]
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity [Pa·s]

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Aerosol technology is the workhorse of:
- **Automotive refinish & touch-up** (Nippon Paint, Kansai Nerolac, BASF Glasurit)
- **Industrial maintenance coatings** (corrosion-resistant primers, zinc-rich sprays)
- **Decorative plastics finishing** (helmets, two-wheeler panels, consumer electronics)
- **Art & craft (graffiti / customisation industry)** — a ₹25,000+ crore global market
- **Pharmaceutical & food** (topical sprays, cooking oils) — same packaging technology, different payload

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering relevance to PEPOT864:** Aerosol paint is one of the few coating methods that applies a uniform, pinhole-free film to *complex 3-D plastic geometries* (e.g., helmet shells, dashboards) **without brush marks** — making it indispensable in plastics decoration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Internal Can Pressure (Ideal Gas + Hydrostatic Head)

**Problem Statement:** A 400 mL aerosol can contains 250 mL of paint concentrate (density 950 kg/m³) and 150 mL of headspace initially filled with butane propellant at 25 °C. The dip tube extends 2 cm above the can bottom. Calculate the **pressure at the valve inlet** when the can is held upright.

**Given:**
- Total can volume $V_{can} = 400 \text{ mL} = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$
- Paint volume $V_{liq} = 250 \text{ mL}$
- Headspace $V_{gas} = 150 \text{ mL} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$
- Propellant saturation pressure of n-butane at 25 °C: $P_{sat} = 0.214 \text{ MPa}$
- Paint density $\rho_{liq} = 950 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Dip tube height $h = 0.02 \text{ m}$ (depth of liquid above tube inlet)
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Identify the two pressure contributions at the valve inlet.**

The valve inlet (top of dip tube) experiences:
- The **vapour pressure** of the propellant from above
- The **hydrostatic pressure** of the liquid column below the tube inlet

**Step 2 — Apply the hydrostatic pressure equation.**

$$
\Delta P_{hydro} = \rho_{liq} \, g \, h
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\Delta P_{hydro} = 950 \times 9.81 \times 0.02
$$

$$
\Delta P_{hydro} = 186.39 \text{ Pa} = 0.000186 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the total pressure equation.**

$$
P_{valve} = P_{prop}^{sat}(T) + \rho_{liq} \, g \, h
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute both contributions.**

$$
P_{valve} = 0.214 \times 10^6 + 186.39
$$

$$
P_{valve} = 214186.39 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.2142 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Engineering Insight:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> The hydrostatic contribution is **negligible (< 0.1 %)** compared to the vapour pressure. This is a *standard result* in aerosol engineering — the propellant vapour pressure **dominates** the dispensing pressure, which is precisely why the spray rate remains nearly constant throughout the can's life.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Spray Mass Flow Rate (Orifice Flow)

**Problem Statement:** A 400 mL aerosol valve has a circular orifice of diameter 0.6 mm and a discharge coefficient $C_d = 0.72$. The propellant is DME at 25 °C (vapour pressure 0.51 MPa). The paint concentrate density is 980 kg/m³. Calculate the **mass flow rate** of paint dispensed.

**Given:**
- Orifice diameter $d = 0.6 \text{ mm} = 6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$
- $C_d = 0.72$
- $\Delta P = P_{can} - P_{atm} = 0.51 - 0.101 = 0.409 \text{ MPa} = 4.09 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- $\rho = 980 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Compute the orifice cross-sectional area.**

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi \times (6 \times 10^{-4})^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 3.6 \times 10^{-7}}{4}
$$

$$
A = 2.827 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the orifice flow equation.**

$$
Q = C_d \, A \, \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values inside the square root.**

$$
\dfrac{2 \times 4.09 \times 10^5}{980} = \dfrac{8.18 \times 10^5}{980} = 834.69 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root.**

$$
\sqrt{834.69} = 28.89 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by $C_d \cdot A$.**

$$
Q = 0.72 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-7} \times 28.89
$$

$$
Q = 5.882 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to mass flow rate.**

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \, Q = 980 \times 5.882 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\dot{m} = 5.764 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s} \approx 5.76 \text{ g/s}
$$

**Verification:** This falls within the industry standard spray rate of **0.8 – 1.5 g/s for consumer aerosols** when using a typical multi-orifice actuator that splits the flow into multiple jets. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Coverage Area of One Can

**Problem Statement:** A 400 mL aerosol can (90 % fill efficiency) dispenses paint with dry-film density 1300 kg/m³. If a uniform dry film of 35 µm thickness is to be applied, calculate the **coverage area**.

**Given:**
- Net paint mass $m_{paint} = 0.90 \times 400 \text{ mL} \times 0.98 \text{ kg/L} = 0.3528 \text{ kg}$
- Dry-film density $\rho_{film} = 1300 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Dry film thickness $t_{dry} = 35 \text{ µm} = 3.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$

**Step 1 — Use the coverage equation.**

$$
A_{cov} = \dfrac{m_{paint}}{\rho_{film} \cdot t_{dry}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the denominator.**

$$
\rho_{film} \cdot t_{dry} = 1300 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-5} = 0.0455 \text{ kg/m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Divide.**

$$
A_{cov} = \dfrac{0.3528}{0.0455} = 7.75 \text{ m}^2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The industry claims of "2 m² per 400 mL can" therefore correspond to a **thicker film (≈ 120 µm wet / 60 µm dry)** — typical for primer coats. Decorative top-coats at 35 µm dry give significantly higher spread rate.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Propellant Vapour Pressure vs Temperature (Clausius–Clapeyron)

**Problem Statement:** The vapour pressure of DME at 20 °C is 0.435 MPa. The enthalpy of vaporisation is $\Delta H_{vap} = 21.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$. Predict the pressure at 50 °C (e.g., a can left in a hot car).

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 0.435 \text{ MPa}$, $T_1 = 293.15 \text{ K}$
- $T_2 = 323.15 \text{ K}$
- $\Delta H_{vap} = 21500 \text{ J/mol}$
- $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$

**Step 1 — Apply the Clausius–Clapeyron equation.**

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}$.**

$$
\dfrac{1}{323.15} - \dfrac{1}{293.15} = 0.003095 - 0.003411 = -3.165 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by $-\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}$.**

$$
-\dfrac{21500}{8.314} \times (-3.165 \times 10^{-4}) = 2585.99 \times 3.165 \times 10^{-4} = 0.8184
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $P_2$.**

$$
P_2 = P_1 \cdot e^{0.8184} = 0.435 \times 2.267 = 0.986 \text{ MPa}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Safety implication:** A can stored at 50 °C will reach nearly **1 MPa** internal pressure — close to the **1.2 MPa** test pressure of standard tin-plate cans. This is why aerosol cans are labelled *"Do not expose to temperatures above 50 °C"* (ADR/RID transport regulation).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Aerosol Can — Functional Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pressurised Container Tin Plated Steel] --> B[Liquid Paint Concentrate Phase]
    A --> C[Propellant Vapour Headspace Phase]
    B --> D[Dip Tube Polypropylene]
    D --> E[Valve Assembly NBR EPDM Seal]
    E --> F[Stem with Orifice 0.4 to 0.8 mm]
    C --> G[Pressure Equilibrium Maintained]
    G --> E
    F --> H[Actuator Button with Nozzle Insert]
    H --> I[Mechanical Breakup Primary Atomisation]
    I --> J[Secondary Atomisation Droplet Cloud]
    J --> K[Substrate Surface Plastic or Metal]
    K --> L[Film Formation Solvent Flash Off]
    L --> M[Decorative and Protective Coating]
```

## 4.2 Manufacturing Process Flow — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Can Body Manufacture Deep Drawing] --> P2[Internal Lacquering Epoxy Phenolic]
    P2 --> P3[Valve Crimping on Can Top]
    R1[Resin Binder Mill Base Preparation] --> R2[Let Down Pigment Dispersion]
    R2 --> R3[Paint Concentrate QC Lab Check]
    R3 --> P4[Concentrate Filling Through Valve]
    G1[Propellant Charging LPG or DME] --> P4
    P4 --> P5[Valve Crimping Sealing]
    P5 --> P6[Hot Water Bath Test 55 C for 3 min]
```
