```mermaid
    P6 --> P7[Actuator and Cap Assembly]
    P7 --> P8[Weighing Coding and Labelling]
    P8 --> P9[Carton Packing and Palletisation]
```

## 4.3 Spray Pattern — Block Schematic of Atomisation Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Pressurised Liquid Paint Enters Actuator] --> OR[Passes Through Pre Orifice 0.3 mm]
    OR --> SW[Swirl Chamber Tangential Slots]
    SW --> EP[Exit Nozzle 0.5 mm Diameter]
    EP --> VB[Liquid Sheet Formation]
    VB --> RI[Rayleigh Instability Wave Growth]
    RI --> BR[Ligament Breakup]
    BR --> DR[Droplet Cloud Dv50 = 30 to 80 micrometre]
    DR --> DP[Deposition on Substrate]
    DP --> FI[Film Formation and Leveling]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for the diagrams:** Each labelled block represents a *physical component or process step*. Arrows denote the direction of mass or energy flow. The block-level representation is used in lieu of photorealistic cross-section drawings because Mermaid cannot render physical CAD geometry — but the *engineering information content* is equivalent to a textbook schematic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Pattern, Dec 2024 Scheme]**

### Q1. Define aerosol paint. List its five functional sub-systems. (3 Marks)
**[Model Answer]:**
An **aerosol paint** is a pressurised, sealed, self-contained packaging system in which a liquid paint concentrate is dispensed as an atomised cloud of fine droplets through a manually actuated valve, the energy for atomisation being supplied by a liquefied or compressed propellant.

The five functional sub-systems are:
1. **Container** (pressure vessel — tin-plated steel or aluminium)
2. **Paint concentrate** (resin + pigment + solvent + additives)
3. **Propellant** (LPG, DME, HFC-152a, CO₂, N₂)
4. **Valve + dip tube** (metering)
5. **Actuator + nozzle** (atomisation geometry)

---

### Q2. Differentiate between liquefied gas propellants (LPGs) and compressed gas propellants (CGPs). (3 Marks)
**[Model Answer]:**

| Property | LPG (e.g., Butane, DME) | CGP (e.g., N₂, CO₂) |
|----------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Physical state in can | Liquid + vapour | Gas only |
| Pressure behaviour | Constant throughout use | Drops as can empties |
| Spray rate consistency | Excellent | Decreases with time |
| Solvent power (DME) | High (co-solvents for resin) | None (inert) |
| Typical use | Decorative paints, cosmetics | Industrial coatings, food |
| Flammability | Flammable (except HFC) | Non-flammable |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 / Model Paper Pattern, PEPOT864]**

### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Option 1)

#### (a) Describe with a neat block diagram the construction and working of an aerosol paint can. List the major components and explain the function of each. (7 Marks)

**[Model Answer — Step-by-step]:**

**1. Container (2 marks):**
- Tin-plated steel (most common) or aluminium, 0.2 – 0.3 mm wall thickness.
- Internally lacquered with epoxy-phenolic to prevent corrosion by paint solvents.
- Bottom dome shape provides resistance to internal pressure.
- Top has a 1-inch (25.4 mm) opening for valve crimping.
- Test pressure: **≥ 1.2 MPa**; Burst pressure: **≥ 1.5 MPa**.

**2. Paint concentrate (1.5 marks):**
- **Resin (15–30 %)** — film former (acrylic, alkyd, epoxy, vinyl)
- **Pigments (5–15 %)** — colour + opacity (TiO₂ for white, carbon black, organic reds/yellows)
- **Solvents (40–60 %)** — acetone, toluene, xylene, butyl acetate (VOC-regulated)
- **Additives (1–3 %)** — dispersants, anti-settling, anti-foam, UV stabilisers, flow agents

**3. Propellant (1.5 marks):**
- Liquefied petroleum gas (LPG: n-butane/iso-butane/propane) or DME
- 25–50 % of total can content
- Provides constant vapour pressure throughout dispensing

**4. Valve + Dip tube (1 mark):**
- Dip tube — polypropylene, ID 3–5 mm, length matching can height
- Valve — nylon/PE body, EPDM or nitrile rubber gasket
- Spring-loaded stem with calibrated metering orifice (0.3–0.8 mm)

**5. Actuator / Nozzle (1 mark):**
- Polypropylene button with mechanical-breakup insert
- Generates hollow-cone or flat-fan spray pattern
- Controls flow rate, spray angle (typically 30°–60°)

**Working principle:** Propellant vapour pressure pushes liquid concentrate up the dip tube → through the valve → into the actuator → through the nozzle → atomised as fine droplets → deposited on substrate → solvent flashes off → continuous film forms.

---

#### (b) Discuss the manufacturing process of aerosol paints with a flow diagram. What are the critical quality control tests? (7 Marks)

**[Model Answer]:**

**Manufacturing Steps (4 marks):**

1. **Can body production** — tinplate sheet → deep drawing → flanging → internal lacquering → curing.
2. **Valve crimping** — valve mounted in 1-inch cup, crimped onto can top.
3. **Paint concentrate preparation** — mill base (pigment + dispersant) → let down with resin solution → filtration (25 µm).
4. **Filling** — concentrate metered into can through the valve (cold or rotary fill).
5. **Propellant charging** — liquefied gas injected under pressure through valve stem (under-cup gasser) or through bottom (through-the-cup gasser).
6. **Valve crimping & sealing** — final sealing of the valve.
7. **Hot water bath test** — can immersed in **55 °C water for 3 minutes** to check for leaks or deformation (regulatory requirement).
8. **Actuator assembly, capping, weighing, coding, labelling, packing.**

**Critical QC Tests (3 marks):**

| Test | Standard | Purpose |
|------|----------|---------|
| Hot water bath | FEA / BIS | Detects leaks & weak seams |
| Internal pressure test | ASTM D3074 | Ensures ≤ 80 % of test pressure |
| Spray rate | FEA 603 | 0.8 – 1.5 g/s consumer grade |
| Spray pattern | Visual + gravimetric | Uniformity of cone/fan |
| Particle size (Dv50) | Laser diffraction (Malvern) | 30 – 80 µm |
| Coverage / yield | Gravimetric | m² per can claim |
| Valve delivery / total discharge | FEA 611 | ≥ 97 % of declared content |
| Flammability / flash point | ASTM D1310 | Hazard classification |
| VOC content | EPA Method 24 | Regulatory compliance |

---

### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Option 2)

#### (a) Explain with a neat block diagram the working of the atomisation process in an aerosol spray. Derive an expression for the volumetric flow rate through the valve orifice. (7 Marks)

**[Model Answer — Step-by-step]:**

**1. Atomisation process (4 marks):**

When the actuator is pressed:
- The valve stem retracts, opening the metering orifice.
- Pressurised paint (ΔP ≈ 0.4 MPa) flows into the actuator's swirl chamber.
- The liquid enters through **tangential slots**, acquiring a swirling motion.
- It exits through the nozzle as a **hollow conical sheet** (cone angle 30°–60°).
- The thin liquid sheet is unstable and breaks up via **Rayleigh instability** → ligaments → droplets.
- **Primary atomisation** occurs at the nozzle exit; **secondary atomisation** occurs downstream by aerodynamic shear.
- Droplets (Dv50 = 30–80 µm) are deposited on the substrate.

**2. Derivation of volumetric flow rate (3 marks):**

The valve is treated as a sharp-edged orifice. Apply **Bernoulli's equation** between the can interior (1) and the valve exit (2), and introduce the discharge coefficient $C_d$ to account for viscous losses:

$$
P_1 + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 = P_2 + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2
$$

Since $v_1 \approx 0$ (large can cross-section) and $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$:

$$
v_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho}}
$$

The theoretical (ideal) volumetric flow rate is $Q_{ideal} = A \, v_2$. In practice, the actual flow is lower due to contraction and viscous effects, hence:

$$
\boxed{Q = C_d \, A \, \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho}}}
$$

where $A = \pi d^2 / 4$ is the orifice area, $C_d = 0.6 - 0.85$ (experimentally determined), and $\Delta P = P_{can} - P_{atm}$.

---

#### (b) An aerosol can of 400 mL capacity is filled to 90 % with paint concentrate of density 980 kg/m³. The valve orifice diameter is 0.5 mm, discharge coefficient 0.7, and propellant vapour pressure 0.5 MPa. Calculate (i) the mass flow rate and (ii) the surface area that can be covered for a dry film thickness of 40 µm with dry-film density 1200 kg/m³. (7 Marks)

**[Model Answer — Step-by-step]:**

**Given:**
- $V_{can} = 400 \text{ mL}$, fill = 90 % → $V_{paint} = 360 \text{ mL} = 3.6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$
- $\rho_{paint} = 980 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{film} = 1200 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $d = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$, $C_d = 0.7$
- $P_{prop} = 0.5 \text{ MPa}$, $P_{atm} = 0.101 \text{ MPa}$
- $t_{dry} = 40 \text{ µm} = 4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$

---

**Part (i) — Mass flow rate (3.5 marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute orifice area.

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi \, d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (5 \times 10^{-4})^2}{4} = 1.963 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2:** Compute pressure differential.

$$
\Delta P = P_{prop} - P_{atm} = 0.5 - 0.101 = 0.399 \text{ MPa} = 3.99 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the orifice flow equation.

$$
Q = C_d \, A \, \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho_{paint}}}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the square root term.

$$
\dfrac{2 \times 3.99 \times 10^5}{980} = 814.29 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \sqrt{814.29} = 28.54 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute volumetric flow rate.

$$
Q = 0.7 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-7} \times 28.54 = 3.923 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 6:** Convert to mass flow rate.

$$
\dot{m} = \rho_{paint} \times Q = 980 \times 3.923 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{m} = 3.844 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s} \approx 3.84 \text{ g/s}}
$$

---

**Part (ii) — Coverage area (3.5 marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute the net mass of paint in the can.

$$
m_{paint} = \rho_{paint} \times V_{paint} = 980 \times 3.6 \times 10^{-4} = 0.3528 \text{ kg} = 352.8 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the coverage equation.

$$
A_{cov} = \dfrac{m_{paint}}{\rho_{film} \cdot t_{dry}}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute denominator.

$$
\rho_{film} \cdot t_{dry} = 1200 \times 4 \times 10^{-5} = 0.048 \text{ kg/m}^2
$$

**Step 4:** Divide.

$$
A_{cov} = \dfrac{0.3528}{0.048}
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{cov} = 7.35 \text{ m}^2}
$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in PEPOT864 — Aerosol Paints questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting atmospheric pressure subtraction** — students write $\Delta P = 0.5$ MPa directly in Bernoulli's equation instead of $P_{can} - P_{atm}$. Examiner deducts **1 mark** for this common slip.
> 2. **Mixing up units** — orifice diameter is in mm, area in m². Always square and convert: $(0.5 \text{ mm})^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2$. Marks lost for unit errors: **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing paint density vs film density** — coverage uses **dry film density** ($\rho_{film}$), not liquid paint density. Failing to do so gives answers 25–35 % off. Examiner deducts **1 mark**.
> 4. **Omitting the discharge coefficient** in orifice flow — writing $Q = A\sqrt{2\Delta P/\rho}$ instead of $Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot ...$ . Penalty: **1 mark**.
> 5. **Writing diagrams without labels** — KTU examiners expect each block in the schematic to carry a name and (where applicable) a numerical value. Schematic-only answers without explanation get **partial credit only**.
> 6. **Not mentioning regulatory standards** (FEA, IS 14847, ASTM D3064, ADR/RID) in descriptive answers — at least **½ mark** lost.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Before you enter the exam hall, ensure you can confidently write/explain the following:**

- [ ] **Definition** of aerosol paint as a *pressurised, atomised, self-contained coating system*.
- [ ] **Five functional sub-systems**: container, concentrate, propellant, valve, actuator.
- [ ] **Three propellant categories** — LPG (butane, propane), DME, CGP (CO₂, N₂) with their pros/cons.
- [ ] **Why liquefied propellants give constant spray rate** — vapour pressure remains constant because the propellant boils to replace dispensed liquid (phase equilibrium).
- [ ] **Orifice flow formula** $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2\Delta P/\rho}$ and **mass flow rate** $\dot{m} = \rho Q$.
- [ ] **Coverage formula** $A_{cov} = m_{paint} / (\rho_{film} \cdot t_{dry})$ — uses **film density**, not liquid density.
- [ ] **Clausius–Clapeyron** — vapour pressure rises exponentially with temperature → safety limit 50 °C.
- [ ] **Mean droplet diameter (Dv50) = 30–80 µm** for decorative aerosols; finer mists (10–20 µm) are *health hazards* (paint aerosols are NOT to be inhaled).
- [ ] **Manufacturing steps in order**: can forming → valve crimp → concentrate fill → propellant charge → final crimp → hot water bath (55 °C, 3 min) → actuator assembly → packing.
- [ ] **Critical QC tests**: hot water bath, internal pressure, spray rate, spray pattern, particle size, valve delivery, VOC.
- [ ] **Atmospheric pressure = 0.101 MPa** — always subtract in flow calculations.
- [ ] **Hydrostatic head contribution to pressure is negligible** (< 0.1 %) — propellant vapour pressure dominates.
- [ ] **Environmental / safety**: VOC limits (EU 680 g/L), GWP of HFCs, flammability classification (FEA categories: non-flammable, low-flammable, highly-flammable, extremely-flammable).
- [ ] **Standards to quote** in answers: FEA 603, FEA 611, ASTM D3064, ASTM D3074, IS 14847, ISO 2249, ADR/RID (transport), EPA Method 24 (VOC).
- [ ] **Applications to mention** in the Plastics & Decoration context: helmet finishing, two-wheeler panels, mobile-phone shells, dashboard trims, custom-painted helmets, DIY plastics refinishing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of Multiple Paint Films
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT864 - PLASTICS & DECORATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Application of Multiple Paint Films

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiple Paint Films** refer to a structured coating system composed of **two or more distinct paint layers** applied sequentially over a substrate, where each layer performs a **specific, dedicated function** within the overall protective and decorative scheme. The total film thickness is the cumulative sum of the dry film thickness (DFT) contributed by every individual layer.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, multiple paint film systems are classified by their **functional layering architecture**:

- **Primer Coat (Sealer/Base Coat):** Provides adhesion, corrosion resistance, and substrate sealing.
- **Undercoat / Intermediate Coat (Build Coat/Surfacer):** Builds film thickness, provides opacity, improves surface smoothness, and acts as a bridge between primer and topcoat.
- **Topcoat / Finish Coat:** Provides the final aesthetic appeal, gloss, color, weather resistance, and chemical resistance.

The overall **Dry Film Thickness (DFT)** is governed by:

$$DFT_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} DFT_i = DFT_{primer} + DFT_{undercoat} + DFT_{topcoat}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Layered Cake of Coatings"**
> Imagine a **wedding cake with three tiers**: the bottom tier (primer) holds everything together and touches the table, the middle tier (undercoat) adds bulk and smooths out imperfections, and the top tier (topcoat) is the decorative icing that everyone sees. Just as you would never serve a cake with only the icing, a paint system is incomplete with only a topcoat. Each layer in a multi-film paint system has a job — protection, build, or beauty — and skipping a layer compromises the entire system.

### 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics

The following standardized parameters govern the application of multiple paint films:

- **Wet Film Thickness (WFT):** The thickness of the paint film immediately after application, measured in **micrometers (µm)** or **mils**.
- **Dry Film Thickness (DFT):** The final cured thickness, typically **25 µm – 100 µm** per coat for industrial systems.
- **Spread Rate:** Area covered per unit volume, expressed in **m²/L**.
- **Volume Solids (%):** The non-volatile fraction of the coating that remains as the dry film.
- **Coverage Calculation:** $Spread\ Rate\ (m^2/L) = \dfrac{Volume\ Solids\ (\%) \times 10}{DFT\ (\mu m)}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The application of multiple paint films is a foundational concept in surface engineering, connecting directly to **Course Outcome CO1** — *Understand the principles of surface preparation and coating application in industrial decoration processes.*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Why Multiple Films?

A single paint film is rarely sufficient because **no single coating chemistry** can simultaneously deliver:
1. Strong adhesion to diverse substrates
2. High film build to mask surface defects
3. UV resistance and color retention
4. Flexibility to accommodate substrate expansion
5. Chemical and corrosion resistance

By **distributing these properties across multiple layers**, each formulated with optimized chemistry, the system achieves **synergistic performance** unattainable by any single coat.

## 2.2 Functional Breakdown of Each Film Layer

### **Layer 1 — Primer Coat**
- **Primary Role:** Adhesion promotion and corrosion inhibition.
- **Key Properties:** Low viscosity, excellent wetting, chemically active with substrate.
- **Common Chemistries:** Epoxy primers, zinc-rich primers, alkyd primers, etch primers (wash primers).
- **Typical DFT:** **20 – 50 µm**.
- **Application Methods:** Spray, brush, dip, or electrostatic.

### **Layer 2 — Undercoat / Intermediate Coat**
- **Primary Role:** Film build, surface leveling, opacity enhancement, and barrier reinforcement.
- **Key Properties:** High build, sandable, good intercoat adhesion.
- **Common Chemistries:** Polyurethane surfacers, epoxy intermediates, alkyd undercoats.
- **Typical DFT:** **30 – 80 µm**.
- **Critical Function:** Acts as a **sacrificial layer** that can be sanded smooth without damaging the primer or substrate.

### **Layer 3 — Topcoat / Finish Coat**
- **Primary Role:** Aesthetic appeal, UV resistance, chemical resistance, and weatherability.
- **Key Properties:** High gloss/ matte control, color stability, scratch resistance.
- **Common Chemistries:** Polyurethane (PU), acrylic, fluoropolymer, polyester topcoats.
- **Typical DFT:** **25 – 50 µm**.
- **Special Note:** Often applied as a **two-coat system** (base coat + clear coat) in automotive applications.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula / Relation** | **Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Dry Film Thickness | $DFT_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} DFT_i$ | µm | Multi-layer system thickness |
| Wet-to-Dry Film Conversion | $WFT = \dfrac{DFT}{Volume\ Solids\ (\%)} \times 100$ | µm | Calculating required wet application |
| Theoretical Spread Rate | $S = \dfrac{Volume\ Solids\ (\%) \times 10}{DFT\ (\mu m)}$ | m²/L | Paint consumption estimation |
| Volatile Organic Compounds (VOC) | $VOC = \dfrac{(1 - VS)}{Density} \times 10^6$ | g/L | Environmental compliance |
| Film Shrinkage Ratio | $FSR = \dfrac{DFT}{WFT} = Volume\ Solids\ (\%)$ | Dimensionless | Curing characterization |
| Number of Coats Required | $N = \lceil \dfrac{Target\ DFT}{DFT_{per\ coat}} \rceil$ | Integer | Planning application sequence |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reminder:** In all multi-film calculations, ensure **unit consistency**. The spread rate formula assumes **DFT in µm** and yields results in **m²/L**. Mixing units (e.g., mils with liters) is a common KTU exam error.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Multi-layer paint systems (E-coat + primer + base coat + clear coat) provide >10 years of corrosion warranty.
- **Aerospace:** Chromate-free primer + epoxy primer + polyurethane topcoat for aircraft fuselage.
- **Marine:** Zinc-rich primer + epoxy intermediate + antifouling topcoat for ship hulls.
- **Architecture:** Primer + textured undercoat + weatherproof topcoat for buildings.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Conductive primer + color coat + UV-cured clear coat for devices.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Film Thickness in a Three-Layer System

Consider a standard industrial coating system with three layers: Primer (P), Undercoat (U), and Topcoat (T).

**Step 1 — Define individual layer contributions:**

$$DFT_P = 40\ \mu m \quad (\text{primer})$$

$$DFT_U = 60\ \mu m \quad (\text{undercoat})$$

$$DFT_T = 35\ \mu m \quad (\text{topcoat})$$

**Step 2 — Sum the contributions:**

$$DFT_{total} = DFT_P + DFT_U + DFT_T$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$DFT_{total} = 40 + 60 + 35 = 135\ \mu m$$

**Step 4 — Verify against industry standards:**

Industrial coating standards (e.g., ISO 12944) specify DFT ranges of **120 – 200 µm** for moderate corrosivity environments. Our result of **135 µm** falls within this acceptable range. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Wet Film Thickness Calculation

**Problem:** A topcoat has 50% volume solids. If the required DFT for the topcoat is **40 µm**, what WFT must be applied?

**Step 1 — State the WFT formula:**

$$WFT = \dfrac{DFT}{Volume\ Solids\ (\%)} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$WFT = \dfrac{40}{50} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Calculate:**

$$WFT = 0.8 \times 100 = 80\ \mu m$$

**Interpretation:** The applicator must lay down **80 µm of wet paint** to achieve **40 µm of dry film** after solvent evaporation and curing.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Spread Rate & Paint Consumption

**Problem:** A primer has **65% volume solids** and is applied to achieve a DFT of **50 µm**. Calculate the theoretical spread rate and the paint required for **500 m²** of substrate.

**Step 1 — Apply the spread rate formula:**

$$S = \dfrac{VS \times 10}{DFT} = \dfrac{65 \times 10}{50}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate:**

$$S = \dfrac{650}{50} = 13\ m^2/L$$

**Step 3 — Calculate paint volume needed:**

$$V = \dfrac{Area}{Spread\ Rate} = \dfrac{500}{13}$$

**Step 4 — Result:**

$$V \approx 38.46\ L$$

**Step 5 — Apply a loss factor (typically 20-30% for spray application):**

$$V_{actual} = 38.46 \times 1.25 = 48.08\ L$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Number of Coats for Target DFT

**Problem:** A single spray pass of a high-build epoxy produces **75 µm DFT**. The specification requires a total primer + undercoat DFT of **200 µm**. How many coats are needed?

**Step 1 — Apply the coat count formula:**

$$N = \left\lceil \dfrac{DFT_{target}}{DFT_{per\ coat}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{200}{75} \right\rceil$$

**Step 2 — Calculate:**

$$N = \lceil 2.67 \rceil = 3\ \text{coats}$$

**Step 3 — Verify total DFT achieved:**

$$DFT_{achieved} = 3 \times 75 = 225\ \mu m$$

> [!NOTE]
> The achieved DFT exceeds the target by 25 µm (12.5% over). This is within acceptable engineering tolerance (typically ±20%), and the additional build provides a safety margin.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Intercoat Compatibility

| **Primer Type** | **Compatible Undercoat** | **Compatible Topcoat** | **Incompatibility Warning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Epoxy Primer | Epoxy, PU Surfacer | PU, Acrylic, Epoxy | Avoid Alkyd topcoat directly |
| Alkyd Primer | Alkyd Undercoat | Alkyd, PU | Avoid strong solvent topcoats |
| Zinc-Rich Primer | Epoxy Intermediate | Epoxy, PU, Acrylic | Avoid acidic-cure topcoats |
| Wash Primer (Etch) | Epoxy, PU | All types | Requires topcoat within 24 hrs |
| PU Primer | PU Surfacer | PU, Acrylic | Excellent all-PU systems |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Multi-Layer Paint System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph substrateLayer["SUBSTRATE LAYER"]
        sub["Metal or Plastic Substrate"]
    end

    subgraph primerSystem["PRIMER SYSTEM - LAYER 1"]
        prep["Surface Preparation: Cleaning, Degreasing, Abrasion"]
        primerApp["Primer Application: 20 to 50 um DFT"]
        primerCure["Curing: Air Dry or Oven Bake"]
    end

    subgraph undercoatSystem["UNDERCOAT SYSTEM - LAYER 2"]
        sand1["Sanding and Defect Removal"]
        undercoatApp["Undercoat Application: 30 to 80 um DFT"]
        undercoatCure["Curing and Leveling"]
    end

    subgraph topcoatSystem["TOPCOAT SYSTEM - LAYER 3"]
        sand2["Final Sanding and Tack Ragging"]
        basecoatApp["Base Coat Application: 20 to 40 um DFT"]
        clearcoatApp["Clear Coat Application: 25 to 50 um DFT"]
        finalCure["Final Curing: Oven or Ambient"]
    end

    subgraph qcSystem["QUALITY CONTROL"]
        dftCheck["DFT Measurement"]
        adhesionTest["Adhesion Test: Cross Hatch"]
        glossCheck["Gloss and Color Verification"]
    end

    sub --> prep
    prep --> primerApp
    primerApp --> primerCure
    primerCure --> sand1
    sand1 --> undercoatApp
    undercoatApp --> undercoatCure
    undercoatCure --> sand2
    sand2 --> basecoatApp
    basecoatApp --> clearcoatApp
    clearcoatApp --> finalCure
    finalCure --> dftCheck
    dftCheck --> adhesionTest
    adhesionTest --> glossCheck
```

## 4.2 Layer Interaction Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Adhesion Force: Primer to Substrate"] --> B["Intercoat Bond: Primer to Undercoat"]
    B --> C["Intercoat Bond: Undercoat to Topcoat"]
    C --> D["External Environment: UV, Chemicals, Abrasion"]
    D --> E{"Film Integrity Maintained?"}
    E -->|Yes| F["System Performs as Designed"]
    E -->|No| G["Failure Modes: Delamination, Blistering, Cracking"]

    H["Corrosion Protection: Barrier and Inhibitor"] --> A
    I["Film Build: Smoothness and Opacity"] --> B
    J["Aesthetic: Color, Gloss, Texture"] --> C
```

## 4.3 Application Method Selection Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start(["Substrate and Production Requirements"]) --> q1{"Production Volume?"}
    q1 -->|Low| q2{"Film Quality Required?"}
    q1 -->|High| q3{"Automation Available?"}

    q2 -->|Standard| brush["Brush or Roller Application"]
    q2 -->|High| spray1["Conventional Air Spray"]

    q3 -->|Yes| electro["Electrostatic Spray"]
    q3 -->|No| spray2["Airless Spray or HVLP"]

    brush --> end1(["Completed Multi-Film System"])
    spray1 --> end1
    electro --> end1
    spray2 --> end1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**Q: Define multiple paint films. Why is a single coat generally insufficient for industrial coating applications?**

**Model Answer:**
Multiple paint films refer to a coating system where two or more distinct paint layers are applied sequentially, each serving a dedicated function (adhesion, build, or aesthetics). A single coat is insufficient because no single chemistry can simultaneously provide strong substrate adhesion, high film build for defect coverage, and superior environmental resistance. Distributing properties across layers achieves **synergistic performance** — the primer handles adhesion and corrosion, the undercoat builds film and smooths the surface, and the topcoat delivers aesthetics and weatherability.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**Q: What is the difference between Wet Film Thickness (WFT) and Dry Film Thickness (DFT)? Establish the mathematical relationship.**

**Model Answer:**

- **WFT** is the thickness of the paint layer **immediately after application** (before solvent evaporation).
- **DFT** is the thickness of the cured, solvent-free film remaining on the substrate.

The mathematical relationship is:

$$WFT = \frac{DFT}{Volume\ Solids\ (\%)} \times 100$$

Or equivalently, $DFT = WFT \times \frac{Volume\ Solids\ (\%)}{100}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **three-layer coating system** (Primer, Undercoat, Topcoat) in detail. For each layer, state the **primary function, typical DFT range, common resin chemistry, and application method**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A steel tank is to be coated with a three-layer system: Epoxy Primer (50% VS, target DFT 40 µm), Polyurethane Undercoat (55% VS, target DFT 60 µm), and PU Topcoat (50% VS, target DFT 35 µm). Calculate:
- (i) The total DFT of the system
- (ii) The WFT required for each layer
- (iii) The theoretical spread rate for the primer if applied to 300 m² of surface **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Three-Layer Coating System:**

| **Layer** | **Function** | **DFT Range** | **Chemistry** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Primer | Adhesion + Corrosion resistance | 20–50 µm | Epoxy, Zinc-rich, Alkyd | Spray, Dip, Brush |
| Undercoat | Film build + Surface leveling | 30–80 µm | PU Surfacer, Epoxy | Spray |
| Topcoat | Aesthetics + Weatherability | 25–50 µm | PU, Acrylic, Fluoropolymer | Spray (HVLP/Conventional) |

The primer ensures the coating system **sticks** to the substrate and prevents rust. The undercoat provides **bulk** to cover surface irregularities and can be sanded smooth. The topcoat provides the **visible finish**, resisting UV, chemicals, and abrasion.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Total DFT:**

$$DFT_{total} = 40 + 60 + 35 = 135\ \mu m$$

**(ii) Wet Film Thickness for Each Layer:**

For the **Primer** (VS = 50%, DFT = 40 µm):

$$WFT_{primer} = \frac{40}{50} \times 100 = 80\ \mu m$$

For the **Undercoat** (VS = 55%, DFT = 60 µm):

$$WFT_{undercoat} = \frac{60}{55} \times 100 = 109.09\ \mu m$$

For the **Topcoat** (VS = 50%, DFT = 35 µm):

$$WFT_{topcoat} = \frac{35}{50} \times 100 = 70\ \mu m$$

**(iii) Spread Rate for Primer:**

$$S_{primer} = \frac{50 \times 10}{40} = 12.5\ m^2/L$$

**Paint Volume Required for 300 m²:**

$$V = \frac{300}{12.5} = 24\ L\ \text{(theoretical)}$$

With 25% application loss:

$$V_{actual} = 24 \times 1.25 = 30\ L$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Discuss the **importance of intercoat adhesion** in a multi-layer paint system. What are the common causes of **intercoat delamination**, and how can it be prevented? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An automotive painting line applies four layers: E-Coat (20 µm DFT), Primer Surfacer (35 µm DFT), Base Coat (20 µm DFT), and Clear Coat (40 µm DFT). If the base coat has **45% volume solids** and the clear coat has **50% volume solids**, calculate:
- (i) The total DFT of the system
- (ii) The WFT for the base coat and clear coat
- (iii) The total number of paint layers and their individual contribution percentage to total DFT **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Intercoat Adhesion:**

Intercoat adhesion is the **bonding force between successive paint layers**. It is critical because a multi-layer system is only as strong as its **weakest interface**.

**Common Causes of Delamination:**
1. **Insufficient surface preparation** between coats (dust, oil, or contamination).
2. **Over-curing** of the underlying layer, making it chemically inert.
3. **Incompatible chemistries** (e.g., applying a solvent-borne coat over a fully cured water-borne coat).
4. **Exceeding the recoat window** — applying the next coat too late or too soon.
5. **Moisture or contamination** on the cured surface.

**Prevention Measures:**
- Sand or scuff the previous layer lightly before recoating.
- Adhere to the manufacturer's **recoat window** (typically 4–24 hours).
- Ensure **chemical compatibility** between layers.
- Maintain a **clean, dust-free environment**.
- Use **tack rags** and degreasing solvents before each coat.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Total DFT:**

$$DFT_{total} = 20 + 35 + 20 + 40 = 115\ \mu m$$

**(ii) Wet Film Thickness:**

For **Base Coat** (VS = 45%, DFT = 20 µm):

$$WFT_{base} = \frac{20}{45} \times 100 = 44.44\ \mu m$$

For **Clear Coat** (VS = 50%, DFT = 40 µm):

$$WFT_{clear} = \frac{40}{50} \times 100 = 80\ \mu m$$

**(iii) DFT Contribution Percentage:**

| **Layer** | **DFT (µm)** | **% Contribution** |
|---|---|---|
| E-Coat | 20 | $\frac{20}{115} \times 100 = 17.39\%$ |
| Primer Surfacer | 35 | $\frac{35}{115} \times 100 = 30.43\%$ |
| Base Coat | 20 | $\frac{20}{115} \times 100 = 17.39\%$ |
| Clear Coat | 40 | $\frac{40}{115} \times 100 = 34.78\%$ |
| **Total** | **115** | **100%** |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Students frequently mix µm and mm in WFT/DFT problems, leading to answers off by a factor of 1000. Always verify units before calculation.
> 2. **Rounding Prematurely:** In multi-step problems, round only the **final answer** to 2 decimal places. Intermediate rounding causes cumulative errors.
> 3. **Confusing Volume Solids with Weight Solids:** The WFT formula uses **volume solids (%)**, not weight solids. This is a common KTU trap.
> 4. **Forgetting Application Loss:** Always state whether your spread rate is **theoretical** or **practical** (with loss factor). Examiners deduct marks if this is not clarified.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ A **multi-layer paint system** consists of Primer + Undercoat + Topcoat, each with a dedicated function.
- ☐ **DFT_total** = Sum of individual layer DFTs.
- ☐ **WFT** = DFT ÷ (Volume Solids %) × 100.
- ☐ **Spread Rate** = (VS × 10) ÷ DFT, expressed in m²/L.
- ☐ **Primer** = Adhesion + Corrosion resistance (DFT: 20–50 µm).
- ☐ **Undercoat** = Film build + Surface leveling (DFT: 30–80 µm).
- ☐ **Topcoat** = Aesthetics + Weatherability (DFT: 25–50 µm).
- ☐ **Intercoat adhesion** is the weakest link — respect recoat windows.
- ☐ **Volume Solids (VS)** is a dimensionless percentage, not a fraction, in the WFT formula.
- ☐ Always include a **20–30% application loss factor** for spray-applied systems.
- ☐ Industrial standards (ISO 12944) specify total DFT ranges of **120–200 µm** for moderate environments.
- ☐ **Automotive systems** typically use 4 layers: E-Coat + Primer + Base + Clear.
- ☐ **Chemical compatibility** between layers is non-negotiable — check manufacturer datasheets.
- ☐ Always perform **DFT measurement, adhesion testing, and gloss/color verification** as part of QC.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plastic Film-Substrate Interactions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT864 - PLASTICS & DECORATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Plastic Film-Substrate Interactions # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plastic Film–Substrate Interaction** is defined as the sum total of physical, chemical, and mechanical phenomena that occur at the interface between a continuous polymeric film (the adherend/coating) and an underlying solid support (the substrate) during and after the lamination, coating, or decorative finishing process. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context (PEPOT864 – Plastics & Decoration), this interaction governs the **bond durability, optical clarity, barrier performance, and decorative finish quality** of products such as decorative laminates (Sunmica-type), flexible packaging films, PVC floorings, and vacuum-metallized overlays. The interaction is quantitatively described by the **Work of Adhesion ($W_A$)**, which is the reversible thermodynamic work required to separate two contacting phases at their common interface, measured in **mJ/m²** or **dynes/cm**. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy – The Post-it Note on Different Walls:** > Imagine sticking a Post-it note on (a) a freshly painted smooth wall, (b) a rough brick wall, and (c) a Teflon-coated pan. The same adhesive behaves differently because the **interface** between adhesive and wall changes. A plastic film behaves the same way — its "stickiness" is not just a property of the film, but an **emergent property of the film + substrate pair**. Surface energy, roughness, porosity, and chemistry of the substrate decide whether the film grips firmly, peels off cleanly, or anchors through mechanical interlocking. ## 1.3 The Five Primary Interaction Zones | Zone | Description | Dominant Force | |------|-------------|----------------| | **Surface Region of Film** | Outer 1–10 nm of polymer | Vander Waals, dipole | | **Interface (Buried)** | True boundary, ~0.3–1 nm | Chemical bonds, acid-base | | **Surface Region of Substrate** | Top layer of paper/board/metal | Porosity, mechanical key | | **Interphase** | Diffused/mixed region | Interdiffusion, chain entanglement | | **Bulk Substrate** | Below surface | Mechanical support | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **interface** itself is rarely a sharp mathematical plane — it is a **3-D gradient zone** where film molecules gradually transition into substrate molecules (especially for porous substrates like paper or board). This is a key KTU concept. ## 1.4 Why KTU Treats This as a Foundation Module-1 Topic Without understanding film–substrate interactions, a student cannot rationally select: - A **primer** (e.g., PU, acrylic, EVA-based) - A **surface treatment** (corona, plasma, flame, chemical etching) - A **laminating adhesive** (solvent-based, water-based, hot-melt, solventless) - A **decorative overlay** (PVC, PET, PP, BOPP, metallized films) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Classical Mechanisms of Film–Substrate Adhesion > [!NOTE] > Every practical plastic film–substrate bond is governed by **one or more** of the following mechanisms. KTU examiners frequently test the student's ability to *identify the dominant mechanism* for a given substrate. ### Mechanism 1 — Mechanical Interlocking (Anchoring) - Liquid polymer melt or adhesive **flows into pores, capillaries, and surface roughness** of the substrate. - Upon solidification, it forms a **3-D mechanical key** — like a tree's roots gripping soil. - **Dominant for:** paper, board, textiles, wood, etched metals, anodized aluminium. - Maximized by: substrate roughness $R_a$ in the range **0.4 – 2.0 µm**. ### Mechanism 2 — Adsorption (Wetting) Theory - Adhesion arises from **intermolecular forces** (van der Waals, dipole-dipole, hydrogen bonds, acid-base). - Requires **intimate molecular contact** — the polymer must "wet out" the substrate surface. - Governed by the **thermodynamic work of adhesion** (see formula sheet). ### Mechanism 3 — Chemical Bonding - Primary covalent or ionic bonds form across the interface (e.g., isocyanate–OH, silane coupling, epoxy–COOH). - Provides the **strongest, most durable bonds** (5–20× higher than van der Waals). - Requires **reactive functional groups** on both film and substrate. ### Mechanism 4 — Electrostatic (Contact Charge) Theory - When two materials of differing **Fermi levels** contact, electrons transfer, forming an **electrical double layer**. - Separating the surfaces requires work against this capacitance. - Significant for **metal–polymer** interfaces. ### Mechanism 5 — Interdiffusion (Chain Entanglement) - Polymer chain ends from the film **diffuse into** the substrate (or vice versa). - Requires molecular mobility → favored at elevated temperatures near $T_g$ or above. - **Dominant for:** film-on-film lamination (PE/PP/PA combinations), solvent welding. ## 2.2 The Wetting Criterion — Foundation for Good Adhesion > [!IMPORTANT] > For a polymer film (L) to adhere well to a substrate (S), the **liquid polymer must spontaneously spread** on the substrate surface during the lamination step. The classical thermodynamic criterion is: A liquid spontaneously wets a solid when its **spreading coefficient** $S$ is positive. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **KTU High-Yield**. Memorize the symbol meaning, the boundary condition, and the unit. These appear in Part-A, Part-B, and viva questions. | # | Formula | Name | Variables & Units | Key Condition | |---|---------|------|-------------------|---------------| | 1 | $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ | **Young's Equation** | $\gamma$ = surface energy (mJ/m²); $\theta$ = contact angle (°) | Valid at equilibrium drop on smooth, rigid, non-reactive solid | | 2 | $W_A = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - \gamma_{SL}$ | **Dupré's Work of Adhesion** | $W_A$ in mJ/m² | Thermodynamic reversible separation work | | 3 | $W_A = \gamma_{LV}(1+\cos\theta)$ | **Young–Dupré Equation** | Combines (1) and (2) | Used directly from measured contact angle | | 4 | $S = \gamma_{SV} - (\gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV})$ | **Spreading Coefficient** | $S$ in mJ/m² | $S > 0$ → spontaneous spreading; $S < 0$ → partial wetting | | 5 | $\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^d \cdot \gamma_L^d}$ | **Fowkes Geometric Mean (dispersion only)** | Superscript $d$ = dispersive component | Applies to non-polar systems | | 6 | $\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_S^d\gamma_L^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_S^p\gamma_L^p}\right)$ | **Owens–Wendt–Rabel–Käble (OWRK)** | $p$ = polar component | Applies to polar + dispersive systems | | 7 | $\gamma_c \approx \gamma_L$ at $\cos\theta = 0$ | **Zisman Critical Surface Tension** | $\gamma_c$ in mN/m | Empirical; $\gamma_L \le \gamma_c$ → complete wetting | | 8 | $\tau = F / A$ | **Lap Shear Bond Strength** | $\tau$ in MPa or N/mm² | Used in lap-shear testing | | 9 | $G_c = \frac{F}{b \cdot d}\,(2E h^3 / 16)^{1/2}$ (peel) | **Peel Energy per unit width** | $G_c$ in J/m; $b$ = width; $d$ = displacement | For 90° or 180° peel test | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard surface energy values** (KTU typical reference): PTFE ≈ **18 mN/m**, PP ≈ **29–31 mN/m**, PE ≈ **31 mN/m**, PVC ≈ **39–42 mN/m**, PET ≈ **41–44 mN/m**, Glass ≈ **300+ mN/m**, Aluminium ≈ **500+ mN/m**, Paper (uncoated) ≈ **30–45 mN/m** depending on porosity. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The five mechanisms are **not academic curiosities** — they are decision-making tools: - A **plastic film manufacturer** choosing between PE and BOPP for metallization looks at **wetting + adsorption** behavior with the aluminium vapor. - A **decorative laminate plant** choosing between solvent PU adhesive and water-based acrylic adhesive evaluates **mechanical interlocking** in melamine-impregnated paper. - A **flexible packaging converter** doing retort pouches must achieve **chemical bonding** (epoxy or isocyanate cured PU) to survive 121 °C sterilization. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Young's Equation from Force Balance > Consider a small liquid drop resting in equilibrium on a flat, smooth, non-deformable solid substrate in a vapor atmosphere. Three interfaces meet at the **contact line** (a microscopic circular boundary). Each interface exerts a tension $\gamma$ per unit length trying to **minimize** its area. **Step 1 — Identify the three interfacial tensions acting along the contact line:** - $\gamma_{SV}$ — tension between **solid** and **vapor** (pulls contact line outward along the solid). - $\gamma_{SL}$ — tension between **solid** and **liquid** (pulls contact line inward along the solid). - $\gamma_{LV}$ — tension between **liquid** and **vapor** (pulls contact line tangent to the liquid surface). **Step 2 — Write horizontal force balance along the solid surface direction.** The horizontal pull of $\gamma_{LV}$ has a component $\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ (where $\theta$ is the contact angle measured through the liquid). $$ \gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta $$ **Step 3 — Valid boundary conditions.** - $\theta = 0°$ → complete wetting, $\cos\theta = 1$ → $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}$. - $\theta = 180°$ → perfect non-wetting (theoretically), $\cos\theta = -1$. - $0° < \theta < 90°$ → partial wetting but adhesive-friendly. - $\theta > 90°$ → poor wetting, generally poor adhesion (e.g., water on PTFE, $\theta \approx 108°$). > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for Plastics & Decoration:** Every corona-treatment unit on a BOPP film line is calibrated to **lower $\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$** of the printing ink or lamination adhesive by raising $\gamma_{SV}$ of the film. The numerical goal: bring $\theta$ below **38°** for solvent inks and below **42°** for water-based adhesives. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Dupré's Work of Adhesion **Step 1 — Define reversible work to separate.** Bringing two phases from infinite separation to contact releases energy (a negative free energy change). The reversible work per unit area to **separate** them is therefore: $$ W_A = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - \gamma_{SL} $$ **Step 2 — Interpretation.** - $\gamma_S$ = energy to create a new solid surface (in vacuum). - $\gamma_L$ = energy to create a new liquid surface. - $\gamma_{SL}$ = energy *recovered* by removing the interface. > The greater the attractive interaction across the interface (which lowers $\gamma_{SL}$), the **larger** $W_A$ becomes. **Step 3 — Eliminate $\gamma_{SL}$ using Young's equation** ($\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$). Assuming $\gamma_{SV} \approx \gamma_S$ (low vapor adsorption): $$ W_A = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - (\gamma_S - \gamma_L\cos\theta) = \gamma_L(1+\cos\theta) $$ This is the celebrated **Young–Dupré equation**, allowing $W_A$ to be measured by a simple contact-angle goniometer. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Owens–Wendt Surface Energy Decomposition **Step 1 — Start from Good's approach.** Good proposed that interfacial tension between two phases can be approximated by geometric mean of like-like interactions plus a polar correction: $$ \gamma_{SL} = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^d \gamma_L^d} - 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^p \gamma_L^p} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute into Young–Dupré.** $$ W_A = 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_S^d \gamma_L^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_S^p \gamma_L^p}\right) $$ **Step 3 — Linear form used experimentally.** By measuring $\theta$ for two probe liquids of known $\gamma_L^d$ and $\gamma_L^p$ (typically **diiodomethane** — purely dispersive — and **water** — both polar and dispersive), one solves two linear equations: $$ \frac{(1+\cos\theta)\,\gamma_L}{2\sqrt{\gamma_L^d}} = \sqrt{\gamma_S^p}\cdot\sqrt{\frac{\gamma_L^p}{\gamma_L^d}} + \sqrt{\gamma_S^d} $$ > This is a **straight-line plot** of $Y$ vs $X$ with slope $\sqrt{\gamma_S^p}$ and intercept $\sqrt{\gamma_S^d}$ — directly yielding the substrate's polar and dispersive components. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examination Insight:** Students are often asked to compute $\gamma_S^d$ and $\gamma_S^p$ from two-probe-liquid contact-angle data. The expected accuracy is **±1.5 mN/m**. Drawing the linear regression plot is mandatory for full marks. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A corona-treated BOPP film has $\gamma_S = 42$ mN/m, $\gamma_S^d = 30$ mN/m, $\gamma_S^p = 12$ mN/m. A solvent-based PU lamination adhesive has $\gamma_L = 38$ mN/m, $\gamma_L^d = 24$ mN/m, $\gamma_L^p = 14$ mN/m. Calculate: (a) interfacial tension $\gamma_{SL}$, (b) work of adhesion $W_A$, (c) spreading coefficient $S$. **Solution:** **(a) Interfacial tension (OWRK):** $$ \gamma_{SL} = 42 + 38 - 2\left(\sqrt{30 \cdot 24} + \sqrt{12 \cdot 14}\right) $$ Computing the inner roots: $$ \sqrt{30 \cdot 24} = \sqrt{720} = 26.833 \;\text{mN/m} $$ $$ \sqrt{12 \cdot 14} = \sqrt{168} = 12.962 \;\text{mN/m} $$ Substituting: $$ \gamma_{SL} = 80 - 2(26.833 + 12.962) = 80 - 2(39.795) = 80 - 79.59 $$ $$ \boxed{\gamma_{SL} \approx 0.41 \;\text{mN/m}} $$ **(b) Work of adhesion:** $$ W_A = 2\left(26.833 + 12.962\right) = 2 \times 39.795 $$ $$ \boxed{W_A \approx 79.59 \;\text{mJ/m}^2} $$ **(c) Spreading coefficient:** $$ S = \gamma_{SV} - (\gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}) \approx 42 - (0.41 + 38) $$ $$ \boxed{S \approx 3.59 \;\text{mN/m} > 0} $$ > **Engineering verdict:** Since $S > 0$, the adhesive will **spontaneously wet** the corona-treated BOPP film. Combined with $W_A \approx 80$ mJ/m² (a high value), this predicts **excellent dry-bond lamination strength** — exactly the design intent of corona treatment. ## 3.5 Failure Mode Classification Matrix (KTU-Exam Important) | Failure Mode | Where the crack propagates | Visual clue | Cause | |--------------|----------------------------|-------------|-------| | **Adhesive Failure** | Between adhesive & film/substrate | Clean, glossy side | Poor wetting, low $\gamma_S$, contamination | | **Cohesive Failure (Adhesive)** | Inside adhesive layer | Adhesive on both sides | Weak adhesive, under-cured | | **Cohesive Failure (Substrate)** | Inside substrate (paper tear) | Fibrous tear | Substrate weaker than interface | | **Cohesive Failure (Film)** | Inside plastic film | Ductile stretch | Film too thin, over-cured | | **Mixed Failure** | Combination | Patchy | Borderline bond; **acceptable in industry** | > [!WARNING] > KTU examiners distinguish **adhesive failure** (bad — interfacial problem) from **cohesive failure** (acceptable — substrate weaker than interface, ideal bond). In lab reports, students must report the **percentage of each mode** by area. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Five Adhesion Mechanisms at a Film–Substrate Interface ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Plastic Film L = Polymeric Adherend]:::film B[Substrate S = Paper / Board / Metal / Plastic]:::sub C[Interface I = Buried Boundary Region]:::inter D[Bulk Substrate Region]:::bulk A --> C C --> B B --> D M1[Mechanism 1 - Mechanical Interlocking
Polymer fills pores and roughness]:::mech M2[Mechanism 2 - Adsorption Wetting
VDW dipole and H bonds]:::adsorb M3[Mechanism 3 - Chemical Bonding
Covalent and ionic cross links]:::chem M4[Mechanism 4 - Electrostatic
Electron transfer double layer]:::elec M5[Mechanism 5 - Interdiffusion
Chain entanglement across I]:::diff M1 --> C M2 --> C M3 --> C M4 --> C M5 --> C classDef film fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0a1a2f,stroke-width:2px classDef sub fill:#7a5230,color:#ffffff,stroke:#3a2810,stroke-width:2px classDef inter fill:#b8860b,color:#ffffff,stroke:#5a3d00,stroke-width:3px classDef bulk fill:#2e5e2e,color:#ffffff,stroke:#103a10,stroke-width:2px classDef mech fill:#e6f2ff,color:#0a2e5c,stroke:#1f3a5f,stroke-width:1px classDef adsorb fill:#fff0e6,color:#5c2e0a,stroke:#a05a1f,stroke-width:1px classDef chem fill:#f2e6ff,color:#3a0a5c,stroke:#5c1f8f,stroke-width:1px classDef elec fill:#e6ffe6,color:#0a4a1f,stroke:#1f7a3a,stroke-width:1px classDef diff fill:#ffe6f2,color:#5c0a3a,stroke:#8f1f5c,stroke-width:1px ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Logic for Selecting the Dominant Adhesion Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Plastic Film to be bonded to Substrate]):::start Q1{Is substrate porous or rough?}:::decision Q2{Is substrate metal or inorganic?}:::decision Q3{Is substrate polymeric above Tg?}:::decision Q4{Are reactive groups present on both?}:::decision Q5{Fermi level differs strongly?}:::decision M1([Mechanism 1 Mechanical Interlocking dominant]):::result1 M2([Mechanism 2 Adsorption Wetting dominant]):::result2 M3([Mechanism 3 Chemical Bonding dominant]):::result3 M4([Mechanism 4 Electrostatic dominant]):::result4 M5([Mechanism 5 Interdiffusion dominant]):::result5 M2a([Wetting plus Adsorption]):::result2a Start --> Q1 Q1 -- YES --> M1 Q1 -- NO --> Q4 Q4 -- YES --> M3 Q4 -- NO --> Q5 Q5 -- YES --> M4 Q5 -- NO --> Q3 Q3 -- YES --> M5 Q3 -- NO --> Q2 Q2 -- YES --> M2 Q2 -- NO --> M2a classDef start fill:#222831,color:#ffd369,stroke:#ffd369,stroke-width:2px classDef decision fill:#393e46,color:#ffffff,stroke:#ffd369,stroke-width:2px classDef result1 fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0a1a2f classDef result2 fill:#a0522d,color:#ffffff,stroke:#3a1f10 classDef result2a fill:#cd853f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#5a3a1a classDef result3 fill:#5c1f8f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#2a0a4a classDef result4 fill:#1f7a3a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0a3a1a classDef result5 fill:#8f1f5c,color:#ffffff,stroke:#4a0a30 ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Corona-Treatment Line for BOPP Films ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Unwinding Station]:::unit A2[Web Tension Control]:::unit A3[Corona Treater Electrode Unit]:::core A4[Dyne Test Station]:::quality A5[Rewinding Station]:::unit A6[Exhaust Ozone Scrubber]:::aux A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 A3 -. Ozone byproduct .-> A6 N1[Input BOPP film
gamma S 29 mN per m]:::note N2[High voltage 10 to 30 kV at 20 to 40 kHz]:::note N3[Output BOPP film
gamma S 40 plus mN per m]:::note N1 -.-> A1 N2 -.-> A3 N3 -.-> A5 classDef unit fill:#264653,color:#e9c46a,stroke:#e9c46a,stroke-width:1px classDef core fill:#e76f51,color:#ffffff,stroke:#a83232,stroke-width:3px classDef quality fill:#2a9d8f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1f6f5c,stroke-width:2px classDef aux fill:#6c757d,color:#ffffff,stroke:#343a40,stroke-width:1px classDef note fill:#f4a261,color:#264653,stroke:#a05a1f,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray: 4 2 ``` ## 4.4 Schematic — T-Peel Test Geometry for Film–Substrate Bond Strength ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Upper Jaw Crosshead]:::mech P2[Substrate A Right half]:::film P3[Plastic Film Centre strip]:::core P4[Substrate B Left half]:::film P5[Lower Jaw Fixed]:::mech P6[Load Cell Reads Force F in N]:::core P7[Peel Front Crack Tip propagates at 50 to 300 mm per min]:::note P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> P4 P4 --> P5 P2 -.-> P6 P3 -.-> P7 classDef mech fill:#1d3557,color:#a8dadc,stroke:#a8dadc,stroke-width:2px classDef film fill:#457b9d,color:#f1faee,stroke:#1d3557,stroke-width:1px classDef core fill:#e63946,color:#ffffff,stroke:#a83232,stroke-width:3px classDef note fill:#f1faee,color:#1d3557,stroke:#a8dadc,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray: 4 2 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the term "Work of Adhesion" with reference to plastic film–substrate interactions. State Dupré's equation and explain the significance of each term.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Work of Adhesion ($W_A$)** is the reversible thermodynamic work required per unit area to separate two contacting phases at their common interface under vacuum, restoring each to its own free surface. Dupré's equation is: $$ W_A = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - \gamma_{SL} $$ - $\gamma_S$ = surface free energy of the **solid substrate** in vacuum (mJ/m²). - $\gamma_L$ = surface free energy of the **liquid plastic film / molten adhesive** (mJ/m²). - $\gamma_{SL}$ = interfacial free energy between solid and liquid (mJ/m²). **Significance:** A higher $W_A$ predicts stronger adhesive bond formation. In decorative lamination, achieving $W_A > 70$ mJ/m² is the typical industrial design target. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **List the five classical mechanisms of adhesion. For a PVC film laminated onto rough kraft paper, identify the dominant mechanism and justify.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The five mechanisms are: **(1) Mechanical Interlocking, (2) Adsorption (Wetting), (3) Chemical Bonding, (4) Electrostatic, (5) Interdiffusion**. For **PVC film on rough kraft paper**, the dominant mechanism is **(1) Mechanical Interlocking**, because kraft paper is highly porous with surface roughness $R_a \approx 2$–$5$ µm and open fibre capillaries. The molten/plasticized PVC penetrates these pores and, on cooling, forms a 3-D mechanical anchor. Wetting contributes secondarily, but chemical bonding and interdiffusion are negligible due to absence of reactive groups and the rigid PVC below its $T_g$. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **(a)** *Derive Young's equation from a force balance at the three-phase contact line. State clearly the assumptions made. (7 marks)* **(b)** *For a polyethylene (PE) film ($\gamma_L = 31$ mN/m) on an untreated aluminium foil ($\gamma_S^d = 30$ mN/m, $\gamma_S^p = 10$ mN/m), the contact angle of water is 88°. Calculate (i) the work of adhesion $W_A$, (ii) the critical surface tension of wetting $\gamma_c$, and (iii) comment on whether corona treatment of PE would improve lamination onto this foil. (7 marks)* #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) **Step 1 — System description.** A sessile drop of liquid polymer rests on a smooth, rigid, isotropic, chemically inert solid substrate, surrounded by saturated vapour. Three interfaces meet at the contact line: solid–vapour (SV), solid–liquid (SL), and liquid–vapour (LV). **Step 2 — Force balance.** At equilibrium, horizontal forces per unit length along the contact line sum to zero. Resolving $\gamma_{LV}$ along the solid surface yields $\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$: $$ \gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta $$ **Step 3 — Key assumptions** (must list all for full marks): 1. Solid surface is perfectly **smooth, rigid, and homogeneous**. 2. Solid is **non-deformable** under interfacial tension. 3. Line tension at the three-phase contact line is **negligible** (valid for drops > 1 mm). 4. System is at **thermodynamic equilibrium** — no viscous or kinetic hysteresis. 5. **No chemical reaction** at the interface. **Step 4 — Limiting cases** (for extra credit): - $\theta = 0°$ → $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}$ → complete wetting. - $\theta = 90°$ → $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL}$. - $\theta > 90°$ → $\gamma_{SL} > \gamma_{SV}$ → thermodynamically unstable contact. #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Given:** $\gamma_L = 31$ mN/m for PE; $\gamma_S^d = 30$ mN/m; $\gamma_S^p = 10$ mN/m; water contact angle on treated PE = 88°. **Step (i) Work of adhesion (PE–Al system):** Using OWRK geometric-mean form: $$ W_A = 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_S^d \gamma_L^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_S^p \gamma_L^p}\right) $$ Assuming PE is mostly dispersive: $\gamma_L^d \approx 28$ mN/m, $\gamma_L^p \approx 3$ mN/m. $$ \sqrt{30 \times 28} = \sqrt{840} = 28.98 \;\text{mN/m} $$ $$ \sqrt{10 \times 3} = \sqrt{30} = 5.477 \;\text{mN/m} $$ $$ W_A = 2(28.98 + 5.477) = 2 \times 34.457 $$ $$ \boxed{W_A \approx 68.91 \;\text{mJ/m}^2} $$ **Step (ii) Critical surface tension $\gamma_c$:** By Zisman's empirical method, plotting $\cos\theta$ vs $\gamma_L$ for a homologous series and extrapolating to $\cos\theta = 1$ gives $\gamma_c$. For a single-point estimate from Young–Dupré assuming complete wetting: $$ \gamma_c \approx \gamma_L \quad \text{at} \quad \theta = 0 $$ For PE: empirical value from literature is **$\gamma_c \approx 31$ mN/m** (Zisman plot). **Step (iii) Comment on corona treatment:** Without corona treatment, untreated PE has $\gamma_S \approx 31$ mN/m which **just matches** its $\gamma_L$. With corona treatment, $\gamma_S$ rises to **≥ 42 mN/m** (treated PE dyne level ≥ 42 dyne/cm). The spreading coefficient becomes positive, $W_A$ increases, and adhesion improves dramatically. **Therefore, corona treatment is highly recommended** before lamination of PE onto aluminium foil. --- ### Question B (Alternative Choice, 14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **(a)** *Explain the Fowkes and Owens–Wendt–Rabel–Käble (OWRK) approaches to interfacial tension. Why is OWRK preferred for polar plastics like PVC and PET? (7 marks)* **(b)** *A decorative laminate plant is bonding a melamine-impregnated paper overlay onto a particle board substrate using a UF (urea-formaldehyde) adhesive. Discuss (i) the dominant adhesion mechanism, (ii) two possible failure modes, and (iii) two surface-preparation techniques to improve bond strength. (7 marks)* #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) **Fowkes Approach (1958):** Fowkes recognized that total surface energy can be split into independent contributions. For a non-polar system (e.g., PE on paraffin), only the **dispersive (London) component** contributes: $$ \gamma_{SL} = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^d \cdot \gamma_L^d} $$ This uses the **geometric mean assumption** that the work of adhesion equals twice the geometric mean of the like-like dispersive interactions. It is highly accurate for non-polar pairs but **fails** for polar plastics. **Owens–Wendt–Rabel–Käble Approach (1969):** Extends Fowkes by adding a **polar component** $\gamma^p$ that accounts for dipole–dipole, induction, and hydrogen-bonding interactions: $$ \gamma_{SL} = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_S^d\gamma_L^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_S^p\gamma_L^p}\right) $$ The corresponding **work of adhesion** becomes: $$ W_A = 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_S^d\gamma_L^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_S^p\gamma_L^p}\right) $$ **Why OWRK is preferred for PVC ($\gamma^p \approx 15$ mN/m) and PET ($\gamma^p \approx 12$ mN/m):** 1. PVC and PET possess **C–Cl, C=O, and ester dipoles** that contribute significant polar interactions. 2. Fowkes would **under-predict** $W_A$ by ignoring acid-base and dipole contributions. 3. OWRK allows **separate quantification** of polar and dispersive contributions, useful for **primer selection** (e.g., a polar primer raises $\gamma_S^p$ to match a polar film). 4. Linear regression form allows experimental determination using just **two probe liquids** (diiodomethane + water), making it lab-friendly. #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **(i) Dominant adhesion mechanism:** **Mechanical interlocking** is the dominant mechanism. Particle board is porous (density 600–700 kg/m³, surface porosity 25–35%), and melamine-impregnated paper has cellulose fibres with abundant micro-capillaries. UF adhesive (52–65% solids) penetrates 50–200 µm into the substrate, forming a 3-D mechanical anchor. Secondary contribution: adsorption (van der Waals + hydrogen bonding between UF –NH–CH₂–OH and cellulose –OH groups). **(ii) Two possible failure modes:** - **Adhesive failure** at the UF–particle-board interface (caused by poor wetting, dust contamination, or excessive resin penetration leaving a starved bond line). - **Cohesive failure within the particle board** (substrate fails before interface — visible as fibrous tear). This indicates the bond is *stronger than the substrate itself*, which is the **ideal failure mode** for a structural laminate. **(iii) Two surface-preparation techniques:** - **Sanding with 120–180 grit paper**: raises $R_a$ to 3–6 µm, opens capillary pores, removes loose dust and release-agent contamination, increases effective bonding area by 40–60%. - **Application of a primer coat** (e.g., diluted PVA or PU dispersion at 5–10% solids): seals excessive porosity uniformly, raises surface energy to ≥ 55 mN/m, prevents over-penetration of UF resin, and provides a chemically compatible bridge layer. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do not** confuse "**contact angle**" with "**work of adhesion**" — they are linked via Young–Dupré but are different physical quantities. Marks deducted if units are wrong (° vs mJ/m²). > 2. **Do not** quote Fowkes for polar systems like PVC, PET, or UF resin. Always specify OWRK for these. > 3. In numerical problems, **forget to balance units** (mN/m vs dyne/cm — remember 1 mN/m = 1 dyne/cm; many students confuse this). > 4. Always report **which probe liquids** were used when applying OWRK — diiodomethane + water is the KTU-standard pair. > 5. In T-peel questions, distinguish **peel force (N)** from **peel strength (N/mm)** — both are valid but must be dimensionally consistent. > 6. **Cohesive failure is GOOD** (proves the bond exceeds substrate strength); **adhesive failure is BAD** (interfacial weakness). Reversing these deductions costs 1 mark. > 7. **Zisman's $\gamma_c$ is empirical** — students often wrongly equate it with $\gamma_S$. It is only an approximation valid for homologous series. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Plastic Film–Substrate Interactions** - ☐ **Definition:** Work of Adhesion $W_A$ = energy to reversibly separate two phases at interface (mJ/m²). - ☐ **Young's Equation:** $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ — valid for smooth, rigid, non-reactive solids. - ☐ **Dupré's Equation:** $W_A = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - \gamma_{SL}$ — derived from thermodynamic separation work. - ☐ **Young–Dupré (combined):** $W_A = \gamma_L(1 + \cos\theta)$ — used to compute $W_A$ from a single contact-angle measurement. - ☐ **Five adhesion mechanisms:** Mechanical Interlocking, Adsorption (Wetting), Chemical Bonding, Electrostatic, Interdiffusion. - ☐ **Fowkes (dispersive only):** $\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^d \gamma_L^d}$ — for non-polar systems. - ☐ **OWRK (dispersive + polar):** $\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_S^d\gamma_L^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_S^p\gamma_L^p}\right)$ — for polar plastics like PVC, PET, PMMA. - ☐ **Zisman $\gamma_c$:** empirical critical surface tension — $\gamma_L \le \gamma_c$ gives complete wetting. - ☐ **Spreading coefficient $S > 0$** → spontaneous wetting → good adhesion. - ☐ **Corona treatment** raises $\gamma_S$ of polyolefins from ~31 mN/m to ≥ 42 mN/m — standard KTU numerical. - ☐ **Standard probe liquid pair for OWRK:** **diiodomethane** (pure dispersive, $\gamma_L^d = 50.8$ mN/m) + **water** ($\gamma_L^d = 21.8$, $\gamma_L^p = 51.0$ mN/m). - ☐ **Failure mode priority (best → worst):** Cohesive in substrate > Cohesive in adhesive > Mixed > Cohesive in film > Adhesive (interfacial). - ☐ **T-peel / 90° / 180° peel tests** report strength in **N/mm** (or N/25 mm per ASTM standard). - ☐ **Surface energy reference values:** PTFE 18, PP 29–31, PE 31, PVC 39–42, PET 41–44, PMMA 39, Glass 300+, Al 500+ mN/m. - ☐ **Rule of thumb:** "Like wets like" — match polarities of film and adhesive for high $W_A$. - ☐ **KTU hot keywords:** "wetting", "wetting criterion", "contact angle", "surface energy", "corona dyne level", "primer", "adhesive failure", "cohesive failure".

topics.title: Measurement of Quality of Painted Films
Plating on Plastics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT864 - PLASTICS & DECORATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Plating on Plastics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Plating on Plastics (POP)* is a specialized surface-engineering process in which a thin, adherent, and continuous metallic layer is deposited onto a non-conductive polymer substrate through a sequence of chemical and electrochemical steps. The primary aim is to bestow metallic properties (reflectivity, conductivity, solderability, corrosion resistance, and aesthetic appeal) upon plastics while retaining their inherent advantages of lightweight, design flexibility, and low cost.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you have a **plain white plastic ball** (cheap, light, but dull and non-conductive). Now imagine wrapping it carefully with a **thin layer of shiny gold foil** that sticks perfectly and never peels off. The ball still floats, still feels light, still insulates from heat, but now it looks luxurious, conducts electricity on its surface, and resists rust. That, in essence, is **Plating on Plastics**.

In engineering terms:
- The **plastic** provides the **shape and structural body** (like a chocolate mould).
- The **metal coating** provides the **functional surface properties** (like the foil wrap).
- Together, they form a **composite product** superior to either material alone.

### 1.2 Why Plate Plastics? (Engineering Drivers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept — Drivers for POP:**
> 1. **Aesthetic Decoration** — Mirror-finish chrome look without using solid metal (cost reduction by ~60–70%).
> 2. **Electrical Conductivity** — For EMI/RFI shielding of electronic enclosures.
> 3. **Corrosion & Wear Resistance** — Surface protection without mass increase.
> 4. **Solderability & Assembly** — Enables PCB-style mounting on plastic housings.
> 5. **Weight Reduction** — Plastics are ~**5× lighter** than steel and ~**1.5× lighter** than aluminum.

### 1.3 Common Plastics Used for Plating

| Plastic | Plating Suitability | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **ABS** (Acrylonitrile-Butadiene-Styrene) | **Excellent** (Industry Standard) | Automotive trims, faucet handles |
| Polypropylene (PP) | Moderate (requires special pre-treatment) | Battery cases |
| Polycarbonate (PC) | Good | Light reflectors |
| Polyamide (Nylon) | Moderate | Functional gears |
| **Polysulfone / PEEK** | Specialty grades | Aerospace, medical |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ABS dominates the POP industry:** ABS contains butadiene rubber domains that are preferentially attacked by the etchant (chromic/sulfuric acid). This creates **micro-cavities** (1–2 µm deep) that act as **mechanical anchor sites**, giving the metallic layer exceptional adhesion — often measured at **15–25 N/25 mm** peel strength.

### 1.4 Typical Thickness Standards (KTU Standard Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Standard Layer Stack on Plated ABS:**
> - **Electroless Copper/Nickel layer:** **0.3 – 0.8 µm**
> - **Electroplated Copper:** **15 – 35 µm**
> - **Electroplated Nickel:** **5 – 15 µm**
> - **Electroplated Chromium (decorative):** **0.25 – 0.8 µm**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Sequential Process Stages of POP

The complete POP process is a **multi-stage chemical-engineering chain** where each step prepares the surface for the next. Skipping or poorly executing any stage causes **delamination**, the most common POP failure mode.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Must-Know — The 7 Canonical Stages:**
> 1. **Pre-treatment (Cleaning)** — Removal of mould-release agents, oils, fingerprints.
> 2. **Etching** — Chemical roughening using chromic/sulfuric acid (the *anchor-creating* step).
> 3. **Neutralization** — Removal of residual hexavalent chromium (Cr⁶⁺).
> 4. **Pre-activation / Conditioning** — Tin/Colloid bath.
> 5. **Activation** — Exposure to precious-metal nuclei (Palladium-tin colloid).
> 6. **Electroless Plating (Autocatalytic)** — Deposit of initial conductive layer of Cu or Ni.
> 7. **Electroplate** — Build-up of final metal thickness using external current.

### 2.2 Detailed Function of Each Stage

**Stage 1 — Cleaning:**
- Alkaline cleaners (NaOH, Na₂CO₃, surfactants) at **55–65 °C**.
- Function: Lower the **contact angle** of the substrate so etchant can wet uniformly.

**Stage 2 — Etching:**
- Solution: CrO₃ + H₂SO₄ + H₂O (typical ratio 400 g/L CrO₃ + 400 mL/L H₂SO₄).
- Temperature: **60–70 °C**, Time: **5–15 minutes**.
- Chemical action: Oxidizes the butadiene double bonds of ABS → forms micro-cavities.

**Stage 3 — Neutralization:**
- HCl or sodium bisulfite bath reduces Cr⁶⁺ to Cr³⁺.
- Critical for environmental compliance (RoHS/WEEE).

**Stage 4 — Pre-activation:**
- Acidic solution of SnCl₂ in HCl → tin ions adsorbed on cavity walls.

**Stage 5 — Activation:**
- PdCl₂ solution → Pd nuclei deposit on tin sites. These nuclei act as **catalytic seeds** for electroless plating.

**Stage 6 — Electroless Plating (Autocatalytic):**
- For **Copper:** CuSO₄·5H₂O + HCHO (formaldehyde) + NaOH + EDTA + NaK-tartrate.
- For **Nickel:** NiSO₄ + NaH₂PO₂ (hypophosphite) + chelators.
- Thickness built: ~**0.5 µm** in ~**10–20 minutes**.

**Stage 7 — Electroplating:**
- Now the part is conductive → standard electroplating applies.
- Sequential layers of Cu (thick, levelable) → Ni (barrier) → Cr (decorative).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for POP (KEE 2024 Module-2 Weightage: ~12 Marks)**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{z \cdot F}$ | **Faraday's First Law** — mass deposited (m) | g |
| 2 | $T = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{z \cdot F \cdot A \cdot \rho}$ | **Coating thickness (T)** derived from Faraday | m |
| 3 | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$ | **Current efficiency (%)** | % |
| 4 | $R = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | **Current density (R)** | A/m² |
| 5 | $\Delta t = \dfrac{T_{\text{target}} \cdot \rho \cdot z \cdot F}{R \cdot M}$ | **Time required** to reach target thickness | s |
| 6 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{w}$ | **Peel strength (σ)** — adhesion measure | N/m |
| 7 | $\text{Volume} = \dfrac{m}{\rho}$ | Mass-to-volume conversion | m³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Numerical Constants (Bold = memorise):**
> - **F (Faraday constant)** = **96,485 C/mol** ≈ **96,500 C/mol**
> - **Density of Cu** = **8.96 g/cm³**
> - **Density of Ni** = **8.90 g/cm³**
> - **Density of Cr** = **7.19 g/cm³**
> - **Molar mass of Cu** = **63.55 g/mol**, z = 2
> - **Molar mass of Ni** = **58.69 g/mol**, z = 2
> - **Molar mass of Cr** = **52.00 g/mol**, z = 3 (from Cr³⁺) or 6 (from CrO₃)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (e.g., Tata, Maruti grills):** POP replaces die-cast zinc, reducing vehicle weight by ~40%.
- **Sanitary fittings (Jaquar, Hindware):** Chrome-plated ABS faucet handles cost **1/4th** of brass equivalents.
- **EMI Shielding:** 1.5 µm of electroless Cu provides **60–80 dB** attenuation — used in mobile phone housings.
- **3D-MID (Molded Interconnect Devices):** POP enables circuits on 3D plastic substrates for automotive sensors.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Faraday's Law of Electrolysis → Plating Thickness Equation

**Starting Principle (Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis):**
The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed through the electrolyte.

$$m \;\propto\; Q$$

where the quantity of charge $Q$ is:

$$Q \;=\; I \cdot t$$

Introducing the proportionality constant (involving molar mass, valency, and Faraday constant):

$$m \;=\; \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{z \cdot F}$$

**Step-by-Step Algebraic Transition from Mass to Thickness:**

Starting from the definition of density:

$$\rho \;=\; \dfrac{m}{V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V \;=\; \dfrac{m}{\rho}$$

For a uniformly coated surface of area $A$ and thickness $T$, the plated volume is:

$$V \;=\; A \cdot T$$

Equating the two expressions for volume:

$$A \cdot T \;=\; \dfrac{m}{\rho}$$

Substituting Faraday's mass expression into the numerator:

$$A \cdot T \;=\; \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{z \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

Solving explicitly for thickness $T$:

$$T \;=\; \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{z \cdot F \cdot A \cdot \rho}$$

Recognising that current density $R = I/A$, we can simplify to:

$$\boxed{\,T \;=\; \dfrac{R \cdot t \cdot M}{z \cdot F \cdot \rho}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Thickness is directly proportional to **current density** and **plating time**, and inversely proportional to **valency (z)** and **density (ρ)**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Type]** An ABS substrate of 200 cm² is to be electroplated with copper using a current of 8 A for 25 minutes. Assume 100% current efficiency. Density of Cu = **8.96 g/cm³**. Calculate the deposited thickness.

**Given Data:**
- $I = 8 \;A$
- $t = 25 \;\text{min} = 1500 \;\text{s}$
- $A = 200 \;\text{cm}^2 = 200 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^2 = 0.02 \;\text{m}^2$
- $M_{Cu} = 63.55 \;\text{g/mol}$
- $z = 2$
- $F = 96{,}485 \;\text{C/mol}$
- $\rho = 8.96 \;\text{g/cm}^3 = 8960 \;\text{kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Compute Mass Deposited:**

$$m \;=\; \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{z \cdot F}$$

Substituting:

$$m \;=\; \dfrac{8 \times 1500 \times 63.55}{2 \times 96485}$$

$$m \;=\; \dfrac{762{,}600}{192{,}970}$$

$$m \;=\; 3.952 \;\text{g}$$

**Step 2 — Convert Mass to Thickness:**

$$T \;=\; \dfrac{m}{\rho \cdot A}$$

Using density in g/cm³ and area in cm²:

$$T \;=\; \dfrac{3.952}{8.96 \times 200}$$

$$T \;=\; \dfrac{3.952}{1792}$$

$$T \;=\; 0.002205 \;\text{cm}$$

$$\boxed{\,T \;\approx\; 22.05 \;\mu\text{m}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Result Validation:** This falls **within the 15–35 µm range** specified in the KTU 2024 syllabus for decorative copper underlayer. ✔

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Time Required for Target Thickness

Starting from:

$$T \;=\; \dfrac{R \cdot t \cdot M}{z \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

Solving for time $t$:

$$t \;=\; \dfrac{T \cdot z \cdot F \cdot \rho}{R \cdot M}$$

This is the standard **plating-time calculator** used by industry process engineers to programme rectifiers.

**Example:** To deposit **25 µm** of nickel at a current density of **300 A/m²** on a part:

$$t \;=\; \dfrac{25 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 96485 \times 8900}{300 \times 58.69}$$

$$t \;=\; \dfrac{42{,}929.825}{17{,}607}$$

$$\boxed{\,t \;\approx\; 2438 \;\text{s} \;\approx\; 40.6 \;\text{minutes}\,}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Failure Modes vs Causes (KTU Diagnostic Table)

| Failure Mode | Probable Stage Cause | Diagnostic Test |
|---|---|---|
| Peeling/Blistering | Poor etching or activation | SEM of cross-section |
| Patchy plating | Inadequate cleaning | Contact-angle measurement |
| Burning at edges | Excessive current density | Hull-cell test |
| Dull/dark deposit | Low current, contamination | pH + titration |
| Stress cracking | Excess ABS etching | DSC for residual stress |
| Pink hue on Cr | Insufficient Ni underlayer | XRF thickness gauge |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Complete POP Process Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Plastic Part ABS] --> B[Step1 Alkaline Clean 60C]
    B --> C[Step2 Water Rinse]
    C --> D[Step3 Chromic Etching CrO3 H2SO4]
    D --> E[Step4 Neutralization NaHSO3]
    E --> F[Step5 Rinse DI Water]
    F --> G[Step6 Pre Activation SnCl2]
    G --> H[Step7 Activation PdCl2 Nucleation]
    H --> I[Step8 Electroless Copper 0.5um]
    I --> J[Part Now Conductive]
    J --> K[Step9 Electroplated Copper 20um]
    K --> L[Step10 Electroplated Nickel 10um]
    L --> M[Step11 Electroplated Chromium 0.5um]
    M --> N[Step12 Final Rinse and Drying]
    N --> O[End: Finished Plated Plastic Component]

    B -.Quality Check.-> P{QC Visual}
    D -.Quality Check.-> P
    I -.Quality Check.-> P
    M -.Quality Check.-> P
    P --> Q[Pass to Shipment]
    P --> R[Rework or Reject]
```

### 4.2 Layer Stack Architecture on Plastic Substrate

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[ABS Plastic Substrate] --> L1[Etched Micro-Cavity Layer]
    L1 --> L2[Palladium Nuclei Catalyst Layer]
    L2 --> L3[Electroless Copper Seed Layer 0.5 um]
    L3 --> L4[Electroformed Copper Leveling Layer 20 um]
    L4 --> L5[Semi-Bright Nickel Barrier Layer 8 um]
    L5 --> L6[Bright Nickel Leveling Layer 5 um]
    L6 --> L7[Microporous Chromium Top Layer 0.5 um]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram of Functional Engineering Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Process[Chemical Engineering Process]
        P1[Pre-Treatment Unit]
        P2[Etching Tank]
        P3[Activation Tank]
        P4[Electroless Plating Bath]
        P5[Electroplate Rectifier Station]
    end

    subgraph Control[Process Control System]
        C1[Temperature Controller]
        C2[pH Monitor]
        C3[Current Density Regulator]
        C4[Filtration Unit]
    end

    subgraph Quality[Quality Assurance Module]
        Q1[Peel Adhesion Tester]
        Q2[XRF Thickness Gauge]
        Q3[Salt Spray Corrosion Tester]
        Q4[Hull Cell]
    end

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5
    C1 -.Monitors.-> P2
    C2 -.Monitors.-> P4
```
