  The pre-conductive parts are loaded into a plating tank. **Electrical contact is made at one end** (e.g., the rim or a designated contact tab). The current then **flows through the bulk conductive skin of the part**, exiting at internal surfaces, through-holes, and recesses. This is the **defining feature** of STEEP versus conventional rack plating.

- **Step 7 — Rinsing, Drying & Post-Treatment:**
  Parts are rinsed in **D.I. water**, dried via **hot-air knife or centrifugal dryer**, and may receive a **chromate conversion coating** (e.g., trivalent chromium) for corrosion resistance.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is etching so aggressive on ABS?**
> Because pure acrylonitrile-styrene would not etch at all. The **butadiene rubber phase** is preferentially attacked because unsaturated C=C double bonds are vulnerable to oxidative cleavage by CrO₃.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the seed layer so thin (sub-micron)?**
> The electroless layer is expensive (Pd catalyst + formaldehyde reducer). It only needs to provide **electrical continuity** — actual thickness is built up cheaply via electroplating.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why pass current through the part itself?**
> For high-volume production (e.g., automotive grilles, PCB through-holes), connecting each part with a flying lead is **labor-intensive and rate-limiting**. STEEP allows **thousands of parts to be plated in parallel** on a single continuous strip or reel-to-reel line.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning of Variables | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | $m$ = mass deposited, $I$ = current, $t$ = time, $M$ = molar mass, $n$ = valency electrons, $F$ = Faraday constant (**96485 C/mol**) | grams | Faraday's First Law — core of all electroplating calculations |
| 2 | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$ | $\eta$ = current efficiency | % | Determines real deposition rate |
| 3 | $J = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | $J$ = current density, $A$ = area | A/dm² | Controls deposit quality |
| 4 | $\text{Throw Power} = \dfrac{\text{Thickness}_{\text{recess}}}{\text{Thickness}_{\text{protrusion}}} \times 100$ | ratio of deposit in low-density vs high-density regions | % | Critical for through-holes |
| 5 | $T = \dfrac{\eta \cdot I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A}$ | $T$ = thickness, $\rho$ = density of metal | μm | Practical plating thickness |
| 6 | $v = \dfrac{T}{t} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot J \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | $v$ = deposition rate | μm/min | Line speed design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants to memorize for KTU exams:**
> - $F = 96485$ C/mol
> - $\rho_{\text{Cu}} = 8.96$ g/cm³
> - $\rho_{\text{Ni}} = 8.90$ g/cm³
> - $\rho_{\text{Cr}} = 7.19$ g/cm³

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Decorative chrome-plated plastic grilles, logos, and door handles — STEEP ensures uniform coverage on deep recesses.
- **Sanitary Ware:** Chrome-plated ABS taps and showerheads where the plating must coat the entire inner bore.
- **PCB Manufacturing:** Through-hole plating in double-sided and multilayer PCBs is, in essence, a STEEP process — the entire board carries current to deposit copper in barrel walls.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Metallized housings for EMI/RFI shielding.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Faraday's Law of Electrolysis (Adapted for STEEP)

**Starting Point (First Law):** The mass of substance deposited at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed.

$$m \propto Q \quad \text{and} \quad Q = I \cdot t$$

Therefore:

$$m = k \cdot I \cdot t$$

Where $k$ is the **electrochemical equivalent**. To find $k$ in terms of fundamental constants:

**Step 1:** One mole of electrons carries a charge equal to Faraday's constant.

$$\text{Charge of 1 mole of } e^{-} = F = 96485 \text{ C}$$

**Step 2:** For a metal requiring $n$ electrons per atom:

$$\text{Charge required to deposit 1 mole} = n \cdot F$$

**Step 3:** One mole of metal has mass equal to its atomic/molar mass $M$. Therefore, the mass deposited per unit charge is:

$$k = \dfrac{M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting back into the proportionality:

$$\boxed{m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}}$$

This is **Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis**, the foundation of all plating thickness calculations.

## 3.2 Derivative: Practical Plating Thickness Equation

For industrial line design, we need the **deposit thickness $T$** (typically in micrometers) rather than the mass.

**Step 1:** Starting from the mass equation:

$$m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 2:** Volume of deposit:

$$V = \dfrac{m}{\rho}$$

**Step 3:** If the deposit is spread uniformly over area $A$:

$$V = A \cdot T \quad \Rightarrow \quad T = \dfrac{V}{A} = \dfrac{m}{\rho \cdot A}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting for $m$:

$$T = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A}$$

**Step 5:** Introducing current efficiency $\eta$ (since not 100 % of current goes to metal deposition):

$$T = \dfrac{\eta \cdot I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A}$$

**Step 6:** Recalling current density $J = I / A$:

$$\boxed{T = \dfrac{\eta \cdot J \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}}$$

**Step 7:** Solving for plating time (a common KTU exam question):

$$t = \dfrac{T \cdot n \cdot F \cdot \rho}{\eta \cdot J \cdot M}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Pattern)

> **Problem:** A straight-through electroplating line is depositing copper on ABS decorative parts. The current density is $J = 4$ A/dm², the current efficiency is $\eta = 95\%$, and the line is run for $t = 30$ minutes. Calculate the thickness of the copper deposit.
>
> **Given:**
> - $J = 4$ A/dm² = $0.04$ A/cm² (convert for consistency)
> - $\eta = 0.95$
> - $t = 30$ min = $1800$ s
> - $M_{\text{Cu}} = 63.55$ g/mol
> - $n = 2$ (Cu²⁺ → Cu⁰)
> - $F = 96485$ C/mol
> - $\rho_{\text{Cu}} = 8.96$ g/cm³

**Solution — Step 1:** Substitute into the thickness equation:

$$T = \dfrac{\eta \cdot J \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

$$T = \dfrac{0.95 \times 0.04 \times 1800 \times 63.55}{2 \times 96485 \times 8.96}$$

**Solution — Step 2:** Compute numerator:

$$0.95 \times 0.04 = 0.038$$

$$0.038 \times 1800 = 68.4$$

$$68.4 \times 63.55 = 4346.82$$

**Solution — Step 3:** Compute denominator:

$$2 \times 96485 = 192970$$

$$192970 \times 8.96 = 1729011.2$$

**Solution — Step 4:** Divide:

$$T = \dfrac{4346.82}{1729011.2} = 2.514 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm}$$

**Solution — Step 5:** Convert to micrometers ($1$ cm $= 10^4$ μm):

$$\boxed{T \approx 25.1 \text{ μm}}$$

This is a **typical decorative Cu thickness** before a thin Ni + Cr topcoat is applied in the STEEP process.

## 3.4 Throw Power Analysis (Macroefficient Method)

The **Throwing Power (TP)** of a plating bath determines whether a STEEP line can plate deep recesses. The Haring-Blum cell method gives:

$$TP = \dfrac{K - M}{K + M - 2} \times 100 \text{ \%}$$

Where:
- $K$ = ratio of far-to-near distances (typically $K = 5$ for a standard Haring-Blum cell)
- $M$ = ratio of metal deposited far : near

For **STEEP** applications, TP should be **> 60 %**. Acid copper sulfate baths achieve **> 90 %** TP — making them the workhorse of through-hole plating.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — STEEP vs Conventional Rack Plating

| Feature | Straight-Through Plating (STEEP) | Conventional Rack Plating |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrical contact** | Through the part body / through-holes | External rack and flyer leads |
| **Labor intensity** | Low (bulk loading) | High (each part individually wired) |
| **Through-hole coverage** | Excellent | Poor (requires special anodes) |
| **Capital cost** | Higher (continuous line) | Lower |
| **Ideal production volume** | High (mass production) | Low to medium |
| **Typical substrate** | ABS, PP, PC, PCBs | Any conductive metal or pre-plated plastic |
| **Process yield** | 95 – 99 % | 85 – 95 % |
| **Throw power required** | **High** | Moderate |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — End-to-End STEEP Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw ABS Plastic Part] --> B[Alkaline Pre-Clean]
    B --> C[Chromic-Sulfuric Etching]
    C --> D[Neutralization Cr6 to Cr3]
    D --> E[Palladium Tin Catalysis]
    E --> F[Acceleration Step]
    F --> G[Electroless Copper Seed 0.5 to 1.0 micron]
    G --> H[STEEP Copper Plate 20 to 30 micron]
    H --> I[STEEP Nickel Plate 8 to 15 micron]
    I --> J[STEEP Chromium Plate 0.25 to 0.5 micron]
    J --> K[Rinse and Dry]
    K --> L[Quality Inspection]
    L --> M[Finished Decorative Part]

    classDef process fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000
    classDef critical fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000

    class A,B,D,F,K,L process
    class C,E,G,H,I,J critical
    class M final
```

## 4.2 Current Path Architecture (Block-Level Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DC[DC Rectifier Power Supply] -->|Positive Lead| ANODE[Anode Basket with Copper Chunks]
    ANODE -->|Ionic Path via CuSO4 Bath| BATH[Plating Bath Solution]
    BATH -->|Current Lines| PLASTIC[ABS Part with Conductive Seed Layer]
    PLASTIC -->|Passes Through-Holes| INTERIOR[Internal Surfaces and Recesses]
    INTERIOR -->|Exits at Far End| CONTACT[Return Contact Tab]
    CONTACT -->|Negative Lead| DC

    classDef electrical fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef chemical fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef part fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000

    class DC,ANODE,CONTACT electrical
    class BATH chemical
    class PLASTIC,INTERIOR part
```

## 4.3 Through-Hole Plating — Cross-Sectional Block Diagram (PCB Analogy)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BOARD["DOUBLE SIDED PCB OR ABS PART"]
        T1["Top Conductive Layer 25 micron Cu"] 
        HOLE["Through-Hole Wall 25 micron Cu Plated by STEEP"]
        B1["Bottom Conductive Layer 25 micron Cu"]
        T1 --- HOLE
        HOLE --- B1
    end

    subgraph ANODE_ZONE["ANODE SIDE"]
        AZ["Sacrificial Cu Anode Releases Cu 2+ Ions"]
    end

    subgraph CATHODE_ZONE["CATHODE SIDE"]
        CZ["Return Path Through Component Lead or Tab"]
    end

    AZ -->|Ionic Migration| HOLE
    HOLE -->|Electron Flow| CZ

    classDef pcb fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    classDef zone fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000

    class T1,HOLE,B1 pcb
    class AZ,CZ zone
```

## 4.4 Comparison Architecture — Why STEEP Wins for Plastics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[Plastic Substrate Problem] --> R1{Is Substrate Conductive?}
    R1 -->|No| R2[Must Deposit Electroless Seed]
    R2 --> R3[Seed Enables STEEP]
    R3 --> R4[Current Passes Through Part]
    R4 --> R5[Uniform Plating on All Surfaces]
    R1 -->|Yes Already| R6[Direct Conventional Plating]
    R5 --> S[Decorative Metallized Plastic Part]
    R6 --> S

    classDef yes fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef no fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000

    class R1 yes
    class R2,R3,R4 no
    class S final
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Straight Through Electroplating. Why is it preferred for plating of plastics?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Straight Through Electroplating is a metallization technique in which the electrical current used for plating is passed **directly through the body of the work-piece** by means of pre-established conductive paths (electroless seed layer and through-holes), rather than using external jumper leads from a rack.
*(1 mark for definition)*

It is preferred for plastics because:
1. Plastics are **non-conductive by nature**, so an electroless seed layer is mandatory — this seed naturally allows current to flow through the part.
2. It enables **uniform deposition on internal surfaces, recesses, and through-holes** which is essential for ABS decorative parts.
3. It is highly suitable for **mass production** (e.g., automotive grilles, PCB through-holes), reducing manual rack-wiring labor.
*(2 marks for the three reasons)*

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the role of the electroless copper layer in the STEEP process?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The electroless copper layer (typically **0.5 – 1.0 μm thick**) is the **conductive foundation** of the entire STEEP process. It is deposited autocatalytically (without external current) using a reducing agent like formaldehyde. Its role is threefold:
1. **Renders the plastic conductive** so it can subsequently act as a cathode in electrolytic plating.
2. **Provides electrical continuity** across the entire surface, including through-holes and recesses.
3. **Acts as an adhesion promoter** by bonding to the micro-cavities created during chromic-sulfuric etching.
*(1 mark per role)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 2 Choice A]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat flowchart, explain the various stages involved in the Straight Through Electroplating of plastics. Mention the chemical reactions involved in the electroless copper deposition step.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A STEEP line is depositing nickel on ABS parts at a current density of **5 A/dm²** for a duration of **45 minutes**. If the current efficiency is **92 %**, calculate:
- (i) The thickness of the nickel deposit.
- (ii) The time required to achieve a thickness of **20 μm** at the same current density and efficiency.

**Given:** $M_{\text{Ni}} = 58.69$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $\rho_{\text{Ni}} = 8.90$ g/cm³, $F = 96485$ C/mol.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Stage 1 — Pre-Cleaning:** Alkaline degreasing at 50 – 60 °C for 5 – 10 min to remove mould-release agents. *(0.5 mark)*

**Stage 2 — Etching:** Chromic acid (CrO₃) + H₂SO₄ at 60 – 70 °C for 5 – 15 min. Butadiene rubber phase is oxidized, creating micro-cavities of 0.1 – 5 μm. *(0.5 mark)*

$$\text{Butadiene} + \text{CrO}_3 \longrightarrow \text{Carboxylic Acid Groups} + \text{Cr}^{3+}$$

**Stage 3 — Neutralization:** NaHSO₃ reduces Cr⁶⁺ to Cr³⁺. *(0.5 mark)*

**Stage 4 — Catalysis:** Pd/Sn colloid (e.g., Shipley 444). Palladium nuclei form catalytic sites. *(0.5 mark)*

**Stage 5 — Acceleration:** HBF₄ or NaOH removes the protective Sn shell, exposing active Pd. *(0.5 mark)*

**Stage 6 — Electroless Copper Deposition (the chemical step):** *(2 marks)*

$$\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2\,\text{HCHO} + 4\,\text{OH}^{-} \longrightarrow \text{Cu}^{0} + \text{H}_{2}\uparrow + 2\,\text{HCOO}^{-} + 2\,\text{H}_{2}\text{O}$$

**Stage 7 — STEEP (Cathodic Electroplating):** Current flows through the conductive seed; Cu → 20 – 30 μm, Ni → 8 – 15 μm, Cr → 0.25 – 0.5 μm. *(0.5 mark)*

**Stage 8 — Rinsing, Drying, Inspection.** *(0.5 mark)*

**Flowchart (text representation, 1 mark):**

ABS Part → Pre-clean → Etch → Neutralize → Catalyze → Accelerate → Electroless Cu → STEEP Cu → STEEP Ni → STEEP Cr → Inspect → Finished Part

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $J = 5$ A/dm² = $0.05$ A/cm², $t = 45$ min = $2700$ s, $\eta = 0.92$, $M = 58.69$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $\rho = 8.90$ g/cm³, $F = 96485$ C/mol.

**Part (i) — Thickness Calculation:** *(3 marks)*

$$T = \dfrac{\eta \cdot J \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

$$T = \dfrac{0.92 \times 0.05 \times 2700 \times 58.69}{2 \times 96485 \times 8.90}$$

**Numerator:** $0.92 \times 0.05 = 0.046$; $0.046 \times 2700 = 124.2$; $124.2 \times 58.69 = 7289.298$

**Denominator:** $2 \times 96485 = 192970$; $192970 \times 8.90 = 1717433$

$$T = \dfrac{7289.298}{1717433} = 4.245 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm} = 42.45 \text{ μm}$$

$$\boxed{T_{\text{Ni}} \approx 42.5 \text{ μm}}$$

**Part (ii) — Time for 20 μm thickness:** *(3 marks)*

$$t = \dfrac{T \cdot n \cdot F \cdot \rho}{\eta \cdot J \cdot M}$$

$$T = 20 \text{ μm} = 20 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm}$$

$$t = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 \times 96485 \times 8.90}{0.92 \times 0.05 \times 58.69}$$

**Numerator:** $2 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 = 4 \times 10^{-3}$; $4 \times 10^{-3} \times 96485 = 385.94$; $385.94 \times 8.90 = 3434.866$

**Denominator:** $0.92 \times 0.05 = 0.046$; $0.046 \times 58.69 = 2.6997$

$$t = \dfrac{3434.866}{2.6997} = 1272.3 \text{ s} \approx 21.2 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 21.2 \text{ minutes}}$$

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 2 Choice B]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the surface preparation of ABS plastic prior to Straight Through Electroplating. Why is ABS the most preferred substrate for this process?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **Throwing Power** in electroplating. A Haring-Blum cell with $K = 5$ is used to evaluate a copper sulfate bath. The metal ratio far : near is found to be **1 : 2.2**. Calculate the throwing power of the bath and comment on its suitability for STEEP.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Surface Preparation of ABS — Stage-wise:** *(5 marks)*

1. **Degreasing:** Alkaline cleaner (NaOH + Na₂CO₃) at 50 – 60 °C for 5 – 10 min. Removes oils, fingerprints, mould-release agents.

2. **Etching:** Immersion in a mixture of **CrO₃ (400 g/L) + H₂SO₄ (350 g/L)** at 65 – 70 °C for 5 – 15 min. The **butadiene rubber phase** is preferentially attacked, creating micro-cavities. The styrene-acrylonitrile matrix remains intact, providing mechanical strength.

3. **Neutralization:** Sodium bisulfite (NaHSO₃, 50 g/L) at room temperature for 2 – 5 min reduces Cr⁶⁺ to Cr³⁺. Critical for environmental compliance.

4. **Predip:** HCl dip (100 mL/L) to prevent water contamination of the catalyst bath.

5. **Catalysis:** Pd/Sn colloid at 25 – 40 °C for 2 – 5 min. Pd²⁺ adsorbs on cavity walls.

6. **Acceleration:** HBF₄ (50 mL/L) or NaOH (50 g/L) at 40 – 50 °C for 2 – 5 min. Removes protective Sn⁴⁺ shell, exposing catalytic Pd⁰ nuclei.

**Why ABS is Most Preferred:** *(2 marks)*

- **Etchable butadiene phase** — unique among commodity plastics.
- **Excellent dimensional stability** (low water absorption).
- **Good impact strength** for decorative parts.
- **Commercial availability** at low cost.
- **High-quality surface finish** post-moulding.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Throwing Power (TP):** The ability of an electroplating bath to deposit metal **uniformly on irregularly shaped surfaces**, particularly into recesses and through-holes. *(2 marks)*

A high TP is critical for STEEP because current naturally prefers low-resistance (protruding) surfaces, and the bath must counter this tendency to plate deep cavities. TP is typically measured using the **Haring-Blum cell** — a rectangular cell with cathodes at two distances ($K$ times apart) from a single anode. *(1 mark)*

**Mathematical Form:** *(1 mark)*

$$TP = \dfrac{K - M}{K + M - 2} \times 100 \text{ \%}$$

**Calculation:** *(2 marks)*

Given: $K = 5$, $M$ (far : near ratio) = $\dfrac{1}{2.2} = 0.4545$

$$TP = \dfrac{5 - 0.4545}{5 + 0.4545 - 2} \times 100$$

$$TP = \dfrac{4.5455}{3.4545} \times 100 = 131.6 \text{ \%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Throwing power can exceed 100 % when the **far cathode receives more deposit than predicted by primary current distribution alone**, indicating a bath with strong polarization control (often due to additive chemistry).

**Comment on Suitability:** *(1 mark)*

A TP of **131.6 %** is **excellent** — far above the **60 %** minimum required for STEEP applications. This bath is **highly suitable** for through-hole plating of PCBs and recess plating on ABS decorative parts.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Forgetting to convert A/dm² to A/cm² (divide by 100) leads to thickness off by 100×. Always state your units in every step.
> 2. **Forgetting $\eta$ (Current Efficiency):** If $\eta$ is not given, assume 100 %. If given, students often forget to multiply it — losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Wrong Valency:** Chromium is plated from CrO₃ as **Cr⁶⁺ → Cr⁰**, so $n = 6$, not 3. Copper is $n = 2$. Nickel is $n = 2$.
> 4. **Confusing Electroless with Electrolytic:** Electroless uses **no external current** — driven by chemical reducer (formaldehyde). Electrolytic = STEEP proper, uses rectifier.
> 5. **Forgetting the Etching Step:** Examiners specifically test whether you understand *why* ABS is etchable (butadiene phase). Vague answers lose marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical points to remember for KTU PEPOT864 — Module 2: Straight Through Electroplating**

- ✅ **Definition:** Current passes **through the part** (not via external leads) — enabled by electroless seed layer.
- ✅ **Why ABS:** Butadiene phase enables chromic-sulfuric etching; other plastics are either over-attacked (PP) or unetchable (pure PS, PMMA).
- ✅ **Process Sequence (8 stages):** Pre-clean → Etch → Neutralize → Catalyze (Pd/Sn) → Accelerate → Electroless Cu → STEEP Cu/Ni/Cr → Inspect.
- ✅ **Electroless Copper Reaction:** Cu²⁺ + 2 HCHO + 4 OH⁻ → Cu⁰ + H₂↑ + 2 HCOO⁻ + 2 H₂O.
- ✅ **Faraday's Law (memorize):** $m = I \cdot t \cdot M / (n \cdot F)$.
- ✅ **Thickness Equation:** $T = \eta \cdot J \cdot t \cdot M / (n \cdot F \cdot \rho)$.
- ✅ **Constants:** $F = 96485$ C/mol, $M_{\text{Cu}} = 63.55$, $M_{\text{Ni}} = 58.69$, $\rho_{\text{Cu}} = 8.96$, $\rho_{\text{Ni}} = 8.90$ (all in g/mol or g/cm³).
- ✅ **Throwing Power Formula:** $TP = (K - M)/(K + M - 2) \times 100$ %.
- ✅ **STEEP Advantages:** High throughput, uniform deep-recess plating, no rack wiring, ideal for PCBs and decorative ABS.
- ✅ **STEEP Limitations:** High capital cost, requires electroless line, limited to etchable plastics.
- ✅ **Typical Deposit Thicknesses:** Electroless Cu ≈ 0.5 – 1 μm; STEEP Cu ≈ 20 – 30 μm; STEEP Ni ≈ 8 – 15 μm; STEEP Cr ≈ 0.25 – 0.5 μm.
- ✅ **Current Density Range:** 2 – 6 A/dm² for decorative plating.
- ✅ **Key Industrial Applications:** Automotive grilles, PCB through-holes, sanitary ware, EMI-shielded housings.
- ✅ **Throw Power Rule of Thumb:** STEEP demands **TP > 60 %**; acid Cu sulfate baths typically deliver **> 90 %**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trivalent Chromium Electroplating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT864 - PLASTICS & DECORATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Trivalent Chromium Electroplating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trivalent Chromium Electroplating (Cr³⁺ Plating)** is an aqueous electrochemical deposition process in which a thin, adherent metallic chromium layer is deposited onto a conductive (or suitably metallized plastic) substrate from an electrolytic bath whose **active chromium species is the trivalent chromium ion, Cr³⁺**, coordinated with organic complexing agents. It is the modern, environmentally compliant alternative to the conventional hexavalent chromium (Cr⁶⁺/chromic acid) decorative plating process.

The half-cell reduction reaction governing the cathodic deposition is:

$$\text{Cr}^{3+} + 3e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{Cr}_{(s)}$$

with a **standard reduction potential $E^{\circ} = -0.744 \text{ V}$ vs. SHE** for the $\text{Cr}^{3+}/\text{Cr}$ couple. Because the deposition potential is highly negative, complexing ligands (formate, oxalate, glycine, acetate) are used to shift the effective reduction potential to a more cathodically favourable region, enabling uniform deposition without violent hydrogen evolution.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Two Painters" Metaphor

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of decorative chrome plating like two painters painting the same wall:**
> - The **Hexavalent Chromium (Cr⁶⁺) painter** is fast, experienced, and gives a deep, slightly bluish, mirror-like finish — but he breathes out highly toxic fumes (CrO₃ mist), needs expensive breathing apparatus (extraction systems), and only 8–12 % of the paint actually sticks to the wall (low cathode efficiency).
> - The **Trivalent Chromium (Cr³⁺) painter** is the safer, modern apprentice. His paint is non-carcinogenic, **up to 60–80 % of it lands on the wall** (high efficiency), and he works well in cool rooms. However, he cannot lay down paint thicker than ~0.5–1.0 µm without losing quality, and his colour is slightly less "blue" than the master painter's.

This analogy captures the central trade-off of trivalent chrome: **environmental safety and efficiency in exchange for a smaller process window and a slightly different visual character.**

## 1.3 Why Trivalent? — The Environmental & Industrial Imperative

- **Cr⁶⁺ is a Group 1 human carcinogen** (IARC classification) and is regulated under **REACH (EU)**, **RoHS**, **OSHA PEL = 5 µg/m³**, and India's **E-Waste Management Rules**.
- The **EU End-of-Life Vehicles (ELV) Directive** and **RoHS 3 (2015/863)** restrict Cr⁶⁺ in automotive and electronic components.
- Hence the plating industry has migrated progressively to **Cr³⁺ decorative plating** for plastics (mainly **ABS**, ABS/PC, polyamide) used in automotive trims, sanitary ware, and consumer electronics.

## 1.4 Substrate Relevance in Plastics Decoration

In the PEPOT864 syllabus context, trivalent Cr plating is normally applied **on top of an electroless nickel (or electroless copper + nickel) layer that has already been deposited on an etched plastic substrate**. The total decorative stack on plastic is therefore:

> **Plastic (ABS) → Etch → Neutralise → Activate → Electroless Ni (± Cu) → Electrolytic Cu (optional) → Bright Ni → Trivalent Cr (top "flash" decorative layer)**

The chromium layer is typically only **0.10 – 0.30 µm thick** and acts as a **non-tarnishing, scratch-resistant, decorative overcoat** that does **not oxidise** (chromium is "self-passivating" by formation of a 2–3 nm Cr₂O₃ skin).

## 1.5 Standard Reference Metrics (KTU Expected Values)

| Parameter | Typical Value | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Active ion | **Cr³⁺** | Distinct from Cr⁶⁺ |
| Cathode efficiency | **60 – 80 %** | High, vs ~10–15 % for Cr⁶⁺ |
| Operating pH | **2.5 – 4.0** | Mildly acidic, near-neutral region |
| Bath temperature | **25 – 45 °C** | Room-temperature plating |
| Cathode CD | **5 – 20 A/dm²** | Lower than Cr⁶⁺ |
| Deposit thickness | **0.10 – 0.30 µm** (decorative) | "Flash" coating |
| Hardness | **400 – 700 HV** | Lower than hard Cr⁶⁺ |
| Anode | **Graphite / insoluble Pb-Sn** | Soluble Cr anodes not used |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Electrochemistry of the Cr³⁺ Bath

The trivalent chromium ion in aqueous solution exists as a **hexaaqua complex** $[\text{Cr}(\text{H}_2\text{O})_6]^{3+}$, which is **kinetically inert** (substitution half-life ≈ hours to days at room temperature). Direct discharge of this aqua-complex requires a very negative potential and produces unacceptable hydrogen evolution.

To overcome this, **complexing (chelating) agents** are introduced. These ligands:

1. **Stabilise Cr³⁺** in solution and prevent precipitation as Cr(OH)₃.
2. **Shift the reduction potential** to less negative values (Nernst effect).
3. **Suppress hydrogen evolution** by partial blocking of active sites.
4. **Improve throwing power** by moderating the deposition kinetics.

The general cathodic reaction in a complexed bath is:

$$\left[\text{Cr}(\text{L})_x\right]^{3-xn} + 3e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{Cr}_{(s)} + x\text{L}^{n-}$$

where $\text{L}^{n-}$ represents the deprotonated form of a complexing ligand L.

## 2.2 Competing Cathodic Reactions

Three reactions occur simultaneously at the cathode:

$$\text{Cr}^{3+} + 3e^{-} \rightarrow \text{Cr} \quad E^{\circ} = -0.744 \text{ V}$$
$$2\text{H}^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{H}_2 \quad E^{\circ} = 0.000 \text{ V}$$
$$\text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^{+} + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad E^{\circ} = +1.229 \text{ V}$$

Because hydrogen evolution is thermodynamically favoured, the **cathode current efficiency (ηc)** is governed by how well hydrogen is suppressed, typically:

$$\eta_c = \frac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100 \% = \frac{m_{\text{actual}}}{\dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M_{\text{Cr}}}{3 \cdot F}} \times 100\%$$

where:
- $m_{\text{actual}}$ = mass of Cr actually deposited (g)
- $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s)
- $M_{\text{Cr}} = 52.00 \text{ g/mol}$, $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$ (Faraday constant)
- The factor **3** in the denominator = number of electrons transferred per Cr atom.

## 2.3 Anodic Reactions — Why Anodes Are Insoluble

In trivalent baths, **soluble chromium anodes cannot be used** because:

- Cr metal anodes would dissolve as Cr³⁺ and re-plate (no net transfer).
- The anode reaction in the chloride/sulphate bath is dominated by **oxygen evolution**:

$$2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^{+} + 4e^{-}$$

- A serious side-reaction is **anodic oxidation of Cr³⁺ to Cr⁶⁺** (chromate), which contaminates the bath and is strictly avoided. This is minimised by:
  - Using **insoluble anodes** (graphite, Pb, Pb-Sn, or platinised titanium).
  - **Divided cells** with anion-exchange membranes separating anode and cathode compartments (in premium installations).
  - Adding **bromate / periodate reducing agents** to scavenge any Cr⁶⁺ formed.

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Process Logic (Why Each Step Matters)

| Step | Action | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Substrate pre-cleaning (alkaline soak, electroclean) | Removes oils; ensures wetting |
| 2 | Acid pickling / activation | Removes oxides; activates surface |
| 3 | Electroless Ni deposit on plastic | Creates conductive base |
| 4 | Bright Ni electroplate (optional Cu underlayer) | Provides corrosion barrier + reflectivity |
| 5 | **Rinse → Cr³⁺ tank** | Avoids drag-in of Cr⁶⁺ contaminants |
| 6 | Cr³⁺ plating at **30–40 °C, pH 3.0–3.5** | Optimal window for bright deposits |
| 7 | **Multi-rectifier pulsed / filtered DC** | Improves uniformity, reduces pitting |
| 8 | Hot DI rinse → dry | Stops drag-out corrosion |
| 9 | **Recovery rinse + ion-exchange / electrodialysis** | Recovers Cr³⁺, treats effluent |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Quick-Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all** equations the KTU examiner may expect for a 3-mark or 14-mark question on this topic. Memorise the constants and the electrode stoichiometry.

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Use | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's First Law — mass deposited | g |
| 2 | $\eta_c = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100\%$ | Cathode current efficiency | % |
| 3 | $E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log[\text{Cr}^{3+}]$ | Nernst equation for Cr³⁺/Cr (at 25 °C) | V |
| 4 | $T_t = \dfrac{\delta \cdot \rho \cdot n \cdot F}{M \cdot i_c \cdot \eta_c}$ | Plating time for thickness $\delta$ | s |
| 5 | $v = \dfrac{i_c \cdot \eta_c \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | Linear plating rate (thickness per second) | µm/s |
| 6 | $T_p = \dfrac{\text{thickness at far cathode}}{\text{thickness at near cathode}}$ | Throwing power (Wagner / Haring-Blum) | ratio (0–1) |
| 7 | $\rho_{\text{Cr}} = 7.19 \text{ g/cm}^3$ | Density of electrodeposited Cr | g/cm³ |
| 8 | $M_{\text{Cr}} = 52.00 \text{ g/mol}$ | Molar mass of Cr | g/mol |
| 9 | $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$ | Faraday constant | C/mol |
| 10 | $E^{\circ}_{\text{Cr}^{3+}/\text{Cr}} = -0.744 \text{ V}$ | Standard reduction potential | V (vs SHE) |
| 11 | $R_a = \dfrac{\text{local CD}}{\text{average CD}}$ | Current distribution ratio | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\text{pH} = -\log[\text{H}^{+}]$ | Bath acidity relationship | unitless |

> **NOTE (KTU examiner pitfall):** Always quote the Faraday constant as **96 485 C mol⁻¹** and use **3 electrons** for Cr (not 6, as some students confuse with Cr⁶⁺ reduction).

## 2.6 Worked Numerical — Plating Time for a Decorative Flash

Given: target thickness $\delta = 0.25\ \mu\text{m}$ on an ABS substrate (area $A = 2 \text{ dm}^2$), cathode current density $i_c = 10 \text{ A/dm}^2$, $\eta_c = 70\%$, density of Cr $\rho = 7.19 \text{ g/cm}^3$.

From the plating-rate formula:

$$v = \dfrac{i_c \cdot \eta_c \cdot M_{\text{Cr}}}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho} = \dfrac{10 \times 0.70 \times 52.00}{3 \times 96{,}485 \times 7.19}\ \text{cm/s}$$

$$v = \dfrac{364}{2.082 \times 10^{6}} = 1.748 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{cm/s} = 1.748\ \mu\text{m/s}$$

Plating time:

$$t = \dfrac{\delta}{v} = \dfrac{0.25\ \mu\text{m}}{1.748\ \mu\text{m/s}} \approx 0.143\ \text{s}$$

In practice, **commercial flash plating uses 1–3 minutes** at much lower CD (~5 A/dm²) to allow nucleation and avoid burning. The student should remember the formula, then **justify the discrepancy with nucleation and current-distribution arguments** in the exam.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Nernst Equation for the Cr³⁺/Cr Couple

Starting from the general half-reaction:

$$\text{Cr}^{3+} + 3e^{-} \rightarrow \text{Cr}_{(s)}$$

The Nernst equation at any temperature is:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln\dfrac{a_{\text{Cr}}}{a_{\text{Cr}^{3+}}}$$

Because Cr is a solid, $a_{\text{Cr}} = 1$:

$$E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln a_{\text{Cr}^{3+}}$$

At the standard reference temperature $T = 298.15 \text{ K}$ and using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{(8.314)(298.15)}{3 \times 96{,}485} \times 2.303 \times \log[\text{Cr}^{3+}]$$

Evaluating the constant:

$$\dfrac{8.314 \times 298.15 \times 2.303}{3 \times 96{,}485} = \dfrac{5706.8}{289{,}455} \approx 0.01972$$

Dividing by 2.303 in the log-conversion (a common KTU trap) gives the **0.0591 V** form when n = 1. With n = 3:

$$\dfrac{0.0591}{3} = 0.0197 \text{ V/decade}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,E = -0.744 + 0.0197 \log[\text{Cr}^{3+}] \text{ (V vs SHE, 25 °C)}\,}$$

**Logical justification (KTU expects this):** The "0.0591/3" factor reflects the **3-electron** transfer. A ten-fold increase in $[\text{Cr}^{3+}]$ shifts the deposition potential by only **+19.7 mV** — a small effect, which is precisely why the complexing ligand, not bulk concentration, is the main tool used to control deposition potential.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Faraday's Law in Trivalent Chromium Plating

We start with the first and second laws of electrolysis combined into the modern form:

$$m = \dfrac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

This is the **theoretical mass** assuming 100 % current efficiency. For trivalent Cr, $n = 3$:

$$m_{\text{theoretical}} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \times 52.00}{3 \times 96{,}485} = 1.796 \times 10^{-4} \cdot I \cdot t\ \text{(g)}$$

Define cathode efficiency:

$$\eta_c = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,m_{\text{actual}} = \eta_c \cdot \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M_{\text{Cr}}}{3 \cdot F}\,}$$

Convert mass to thickness using the deposit density $\rho$ and the plated area $A$:

$$\delta = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{\rho \cdot A}$$

Substituting $m_{\text{actual}}$ and using $i_c = I/A$:

$$\delta = \dfrac{\eta_c \cdot i_c \cdot M_{\text{Cr}} \cdot t}{3 \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

Solving for time:

$$\boxed{\,t = \dfrac{3 \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot \delta}{\eta_c \cdot i_c \cdot M_{\text{Cr}}}\,}$$

This is the **master plating-time equation** that KTU examiners expect students to derive from Faraday's law.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Haring-Blum Throwing Power

The **Haring-Blum cell** is a rectangular cell with one anode at one end and two cathodes at distances $L_{\text{near}}$ and $L_{\text{far}}$ (typically ratio 1 : 5). Measured current distribution is related to throwing power $T_p$:

$$T_p = \dfrac{K - M}{K + M - 2} \times 100\%$$

where:

- $K = L_{\text{far}} / L_{\text{near}}$ = primary current ratio (geometric)
- $M = \text{weight gain at far cathode} / \text{weight gain at near cathode}$ = metal distribution ratio

**Logical step-by-step:**

1. $K = 5$ (standard 5 : 1 cell) — the geometric current-density ratio.
2. Without polarisation, by Ohm's law in the electrolyte, current density is inversely proportional to distance, so $K = 5$ would also be the metal ratio in the absence of polarisation.
3. Cathode polarisation $\dfrac{d\eta}{di}$ introduces non-linearity, causing the **actual metal ratio M to be much closer to 1** than $K$.
4. If $M = 1$ (perfect throwing), $T_p = 100\%$.
5. If $M = K$ (no polarisation benefit), $T_p = 0\%$.
6. The trivalent bath, with its higher polarisation resistance, gives $M$ closer to 1, hence **better throwing power** than Cr⁶⁺ baths.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Why Trivalent is Different from Hexavalent

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 module-learning outcome emphasises **comparative analysis** of decorative plating systems. The following matrix is the highest-yield KTU content for a 7 or 14-mark question.

| Attribute | Hexavalent (Cr⁶⁺) | Trivalent (Cr³⁺) | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Active species | $\text{CrO}_3$ / $\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-}$ | $\text{Cr}^{3+}$ aqua-complex | Toxicity shift |
| Cathode efficiency | 8 – 15 % | 60 – 80 % | Energy savings, less H₂ |
| Operating temp | 35 – 55 °C | 25 – 45 °C | Lower energy |
| Current density | 10 – 40 A/dm² | 5 – 20 A/dm² | Slower deposition |
| Anode | Soluble Pb-Sn (with PbO₂ film) | **Insoluble** (graphite, platinised Ti) | No metal replenishment |
| Throwing power | Moderate | **Very good** | Complex shapes OK |
| Deposit thickness | Unlimited | Limited to ~1 µm decorative | Cannot replace hard Cr |
| Colour | Slight bluish-white | Slight greyish-white | Aesthetic difference |
| Hydrogen embrittlement | High (H codeposition) | Lower | Better for high-strength steel |
| Toxicity | **Carcinogen (Cr⁶⁺)** | Low (Cr³⁺) | Regulatory driver |
| Effluent treatment | Reduction to Cr³⁺ → precipitate | Neutralisation, ion-exchange | Cr⁶⁺ plant more complex |
| RoHS / REACH | Restricted (Annex XIV) | Not restricted | Industry migration |

## 3.5 Critical Derivation — Why $\eta_c$ is Higher in Trivalent Baths

In Cr⁶⁺ baths, the deposition proceeds through intermediate Cr³⁺ and Cr²⁺ steps, and a thick, viscous **cathode film** (Cr³⁺ polymeric chromic species) forms at the surface, blocking the active sites and forcing most current into H₂ evolution — hence the low efficiency.

In Cr³⁺ baths:

1. The ligand complex is **kinetically slower to discharge**, but the **Tafel slope for H₂ is much steeper** than for Cr deposition.
2. The result is a **more uniform current distribution** across microscopic peaks/valleys, suppressing local H₂ gassing.
3. **Additives** (sulphonated surfactants, dicarboxylic acids) act as **levelers and brighteners**, suppressing H₂ at high spots and allowing Cr to plate in valleys.
4. Net effect: $\eta_c$ rises from ~12 % to ~70 %.

This is the central electrochemical reason the industry is migrating.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Trivalent Cr Plating on Plastic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Substrate ABS Plastic] --> B[Alkaline Degrease]
    B --> C[Etch with Chromic Sulphuric Acid]
    C --> D[Neutralise with Sodium Sulphite]
    D --> E[Activate Pd Sn Colloid]
    E --> F[Accelerator HCl HF]
    F --> G[Electroless Ni Deposit]
    G --> H[Electrolytic Cu Strike Optional]
    H --> I[Bright Ni Electroplate]
    I --> J[DI Rinse Pre Cr]
    J --> K[Trivalent Cr Plating Tank]
    K --> L[Hot DI Rinse 50 C]
    L --> M[DI Rinse]
    M --> N[Hot Air Dry]
    N --> O[Quality Check Thickness Colour]
    O --> P[Decorative Chrome Finished Part]
    K --> Q[Drag out Recovery Rinse]
    Q --> R[Ion Exchange]
    R --> S[Closed Loop Reuse]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The vertical sequence is the part flow. The branch from the Cr³⁺ tank to "Drag-out → Ion-Exchange → Closed-loop reuse" illustrates the modern **closed-loop effluent philosophy** that makes Cr³⁺ plating environmentally sustainable.

## 4.2 Electrochemical Cell Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CellA[Plating Cell Cr3 Bath]
        direction LR
        Anode1[Insoluble Anode Pb Sn or Platinised Ti] ---|O2 evolution| ElectrolyteA[Cr3 Sulphate Bath with Formate Oxalate Buffer Boric Acid pH 3 0 3 5]
        ElectrolyteA ---|Cr deposition| Cathode1[Plastic Part with Ni Underlayer]
    end
    PSU[DC Rectifier 6 12 V] ---|positive lead| Anode1
    PSU ---|negative lead| Cathode1
    Filter[Filter Pump 5 micron] --> CellA
    Heater[Heater 25 45 C] --> CellA
    pHProbe[pH Controller with Auto Dosing] --> CellA
    Agitator[Air Agitation or Cathode Rod] --> CellA
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The dashed arrows represent **auxiliary services** (filtration, heating, agitation, pH control) that the KTU examiner expects to be mentioned in a 14-mark question on process engineering.

## 4.3 Decision Tree — When to Choose Trivalent vs Hexavalent

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Plating Line Specification] --> Q1{Is the part plastic or steel with decorative purpose only?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Thickness requirement less than 1 micrometre?}
    Q1 -- No --> QH[Use Hexavalent Hard Cr for engineering surfaces]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Regulatory market EU USA Japan?}
    Q2 -- No --> QH
    Q3 -- Yes --> QT[Select Trivalent Cr Process]
    Q3 -- No --> QE[Cost benefit analysis]
    QE -- Lower OPEX preferred --> QT
    QE -- Lower CAPEX preferred --> QH
    QT --> FinalT[Trivalent Cr bath with bright Ni underlayer]
    QH --> FinalH[Conventional chromic acid bath with fume extraction]
```

## 4.4 Block Architecture — Multi-Layer Decorative Stack on Plastic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Top Surface Air Interface] --> S2[Cr2O3 Passive Film 2 3 nm]
    S2 --> S3[Trivalent Cr Layer 0 10 0 30 micrometre]
    S3 --> S4[Bright Ni Layer 10 25 micrometre]
    S4 --> S5[Semi Bright Ni Optional 10 15 micrometre]
    S5 --> S6[Electroless Ni 0 5 1 micrometre]
    S6 --> S7[Etched ABS Substrate 100 micrometre to 3 mm]
    S7 --> S8[Bulk Plastic Resin ABS]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each block is a functional layer. The **Cr₂O₃ passive film** (block 2) is the actual protective barrier; the underlying Cr metal is the visual reflector. The total decorative stack on plastic rarely exceeds **30 µm**, dominated by the Ni layers, not the Cr.

## 4.5 Hypothetical Failure-Mode Tree (Examiner Favourite)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F[Defective Chrome Deposit] --> F1{Appearance issue?}
    F1 -- Yellow brown stain --> F2[Cr6 contamination or passivation breakdown]
    F1 -- Milky hazy --> F3[High Fe Cu Zn impurity in bath]
    F1 -- Pitting --> F4[Surfactant depletion or H2 bubble sticking]
    F1 -- Dark burnt deposit --> F5[Current density too high or temperature low]
    F1 -- Non uniform thickness --> F6[Anode cathode geometry poor throwing power]
    F1 -- Peeling --> F7[Ni underlayer passivated or rinse broken]
    F --> Q[Check bath chemistry pH temp additive conc]
    Q --> QC[Atomic Absorption Spectrophotometry Cr3 Fe Cu]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEPOT864]**
**Define trivalent chromium electroplating. Why is it preferred over hexavalent chromium in decorative plating of plastics?**

**Model Answer:**

Trivalent chromium electroplating is an electrolytic process in which a decorative chromium coating is deposited onto a substrate from a bath containing **trivalent Cr³⁺ ions** (typically as chromium sulphate) complexed with organic ligands, instead of the toxic hexavalent Cr⁶⁺ (chromic acid) used in conventional chrome plating.

It is preferred over hexavalent chromium because:

1. **Environmental safety** – Cr⁶⁺ is a confirmed human carcinogen (IARC Group 1); Cr³⁺ is far less toxic and not classified as carcinogenic.
2. **Higher cathode current efficiency** – 60–80 % vs 8–15 % for Cr⁶⁺, reducing energy and H₂ evolution.
3. **Lower operating temperature** – 25–45 °C vs 35–55 °C, saving energy.
4. **Better throwing power** – superior metal distribution on complex plastic moulded shapes.
5. **Lower hydrogen embrittlement** – safer for high-strength steel sub-components.
6. **Regulatory compliance** – meets RoHS 3, REACH, ELV, and Indian E-Waste rules.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEPOT864]**
**List the major components of a trivalent chromium plating bath and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Component | Example | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Chromium source** | Chromium sulphate $\text{Cr}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3$ | Supplies Cr³⁺ ions for deposition |
| **Complexing agent** | Sodium formate / oxalate / glycine | Stabilises Cr³⁺; shifts reduction potential |
| **Conducting salt** | Sodium sulphate / potassium sulphate | Improves bath conductivity |
| **Buffer** | Boric acid / aluminium sulphate | Maintains pH at 3.0–3.5 |
| **Wetting agent** | Sodium lauryl sulphate | Reduces surface tension, prevents pitting |
| **Brightener / leveler** | Sulphonated aromatics, dicarboxylic acids | Produces bright, level deposit |
| **Reducing agent (Cr⁶⁺ scavenger)** | Sodium bromate / periodate | Reduces any Cr⁶⁺ formed at anode |

The pH is held in the narrow range **3.0–3.5** because at pH > 4 the Cr³⁺ precipitates as Cr(OH)₃, and at pH < 2.5 the complex dissociates, giving poor deposit quality.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always specify the number of electrons (n = 3)** when using Faraday's law for Cr — the most common error is using n = 6 (from Cr⁶⁺ reduction to Cr⁰, an industrial process, not the electrochemical step).
> 2. **Do not confuse trivalent Cr with hard Cr⁶⁺** — the examiner deducts marks if you claim trivalent can produce "thick engineering hard chrome". It is a **decorative flash only**.
> 3. **Mention the insoluble anode explicitly** — failing to do so costs at least 1 mark on the 7-mark comparative question.
> 4. **Always include a labelled diagram** in 14-mark answers — KTU valuation key gives up to 2 marks extra for a clean, labelled block diagram.
> 5. **State the regulatory push** (RoHS / REACH) at least once — the question on environmental compliance is a KTU favourite and the syllabus maps directly to it.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — 2024 Scheme Pattern**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEPOT864]**
> **(a)** Explain the electrochemistry of trivalent chromium electroplating, including the cathode and anode reactions, with a neat block diagram of the plating cell. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare the trivalent and hexavalent chromium plating processes on the basis of: active species, cathode efficiency, throwing power, toxicity, thickness achievable, and environmental compliance. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
Trivalent chromium electroplating is an aqueous electrodeposition process in which Cr³⁺ ions, stabilised by complexing agents, are reduced to metallic Cr at the cathode. The standard reduction potential of the $\text{Cr}^{3+}/\text{Cr}$ couple is **−0.744 V vs SHE**, which is highly negative. Complexing ligands (formate, oxalate, glycine) shift the effective reduction potential to less negative values, enabling deposition without violent H₂ evolution.

**2. Cathode reactions (2 marks):**
At the cathode, the principal and competing reactions are:

$$\text{Cr}^{3+} + 3e^{-} \rightarrow \text{Cr}_{(s)} \quad E^{\circ} = -0.744\ \text{V (desired)}$$
$$2\text{H}^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{H}_{2(g)} \quad E^{\circ} = 0.000\ \text{V (competing)}$$

Because the hydrogen reaction is thermodynamically more favourable, additives (sulphonated brighteners) and complexing ligands are used to suppress H₂ evolution and raise cathode efficiency to **60–80 %**.

**3. Anode reactions (1 mark):**
Soluble Cr anodes are **not used** in trivalent baths. Insoluble anodes (Pb-Sn, platinised Ti, graphite) are used, and the dominant anodic reaction is **oxygen evolution:**

$$2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^{+} + 4e^{-}$$

A harmful side reaction — **anodic oxidation of Cr³⁺ to Cr⁶⁺** — is minimised by additives and divided-cell designs.

**4. Block diagram (2 marks):**
Draw the cell with:

- **Cathode:** Plastic part with Ni underlayer.
- **Anode:** Insoluble Pb-Sn or platinised Ti.
- **Electrolyte:** Cr₂(SO₄)₃ + formate + boric acid buffer (pH 3.0–3.5).
- **External DC source** (4–8 V rectifier).
- **Services:** filter, heater, pH controller, air agitation, fume hood (mild).

**5. Operating conditions (1 mark):**
Temperature **30–40 °C**, pH **3.0–3.5**, current density **5–15 A/dm²**, plating time 1–3 minutes for 0.25 µm flash.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

| Criterion | Hexavalent (Cr⁶⁺) | Trivalent (Cr³⁺) | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|
| Active species | $\text{CrO}_3$ / $\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-}$ | $[\text{Cr}(\text{L})_x]^{3-xn}$ | Toxicity difference |
| Cathode efficiency | 8–15 % | 60–80 % | Trivalent is 5× more efficient |
| Throwing power | Moderate | Very good | Better for complex plastic shapes |
| Toxicity | Carcinogen (IARC Group 1) | Low toxicity | Key regulatory driver |
| Achievable thickness | Hard Cr up to 500 µm | Decorative flash ≤ 1 µm | Trivalent is **not** for engineering |
| Bath control | Forgiving | Sensitive to Fe, Cu, Cr⁶⁺ | More chemistry expertise needed |
| Environmental | Restricted (RoHS, REACH) | Compliant | Trivalent is future-proof |
| Anode | Soluble Pb-Sn (PbO₂ film) | **Insoluble** graphite/Pb-Sn/Ti | Different anode strategy |
| Hydrogen embrittlement | High | Low | Safer for high-strength steel |
| Operating cost | High effluent treatment | Higher chemical cost, lower effluent | Net cost comparable |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Trivalent Cr is the **preferred process for decorative flash plating on plastics** because of its environmental compliance, higher efficiency, and superior throwing power. Hexavalent remains in use only for **thick engineering hard chrome** where the metallurgical properties of thick Cr cannot yet be matched by Cr³⁺ technology.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEPOT864]**
> **(a)** Describe, with relevant equations, the role of **complexing agents** in stabilising Cr³⁺ ions in a trivalent chromium bath. Why are simple inorganic salts such as CrCl₃ alone unsuitable? **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A trivalent chromium plating bath operates at a cathode current density of **12 A/dm²** with a cathode efficiency of **70 %**. Calculate the **time required** to deposit a chromium layer of thickness **0.30 µm** on a plastic substrate of area **3.0 dm²**, assuming deposit density is **7.19 g/cm³**. Also estimate the **theoretical mass of chromium** deposited. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**1. The stability problem of Cr³⁺ (2 marks):**
The free hexaaqua ion $[\text{Cr}(\text{H}_2\text{O})_6]^{3+}$ is **kinetically inert** — its ligand-substitution rate is slow (half-life hours to days at 25 °C). In a simple aqueous solution at the operating pH (3–3.5), Cr³⁺ would either:

- Hydrolyse and precipitate as $\text{Cr}(\text{OH})_3$ above pH ≈ 4, or
- Discharge at extremely negative potentials causing violent H₂ gassing at the cathode.

**2. Role of complexing agents (3 marks):**
Complexing agents (formate $\text{HCOO}^{-}$, oxalate $\text{C}_2\text{O}_4^{2-}$, acetate, glycine, succinate, malonate) perform four functions:

- **Stabilisation:** Form mixed-ligand complexes like $[\text{Cr}(\text{HCOO})_x(\text{H}_2\text{O})_{6-x}]^{3-x}$ that remain soluble at pH 3–3.5.
- **Potential shift:** The Nernst equation $E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{0.0197}{n}\log[\text{Cr}^{3+}]$ shows the reduction potential depends on the effective activity of the complex; complexation lowers the activity and shifts the deposition to a more practical (less negative) potential.
- **Discharge kinetics:** The complex dissociates at the cathode in a controlled manner, releasing Cr³⁺ locally — this smooths the deposition.
- **Buffering:** Many complexing ligands (formate, acetate) are also conjugate bases of weak acids, providing pH buffering.

**3. Why CrCl₃ alone fails (1 mark):**
Without complexing ligands, the Cr³⁺ aqua ion hydrolyses to $\text{Cr}(\text{OH})^{2+}$, $\text{Cr}(\text{OH})_2^{+}$, and ultimately precipitates as gelatinous $\text{Cr}(\text{OH})_3$, fouling the bath. Also, the deposition potential is so negative that the cathode efficiency collapses to near zero due to H₂ evolution.

**4. Equation summary (1 mark):**

$$\left[\text{Cr}(\text{L})_x\right]^{3-xn} \rightleftharpoons \text{Cr}^{3+} + x\text{L}^{n-}$$
$$\text{Cr}^{3+} + 3e^{-} \rightarrow \text{Cr}_{(s)}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:** $i_c = 12 \text{ A/dm}^2$, $\eta_c = 70\% = 0.70$, $\delta = 0.30\ \mu\text{m} = 0.30 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm}$, $A = 3.0 \text{ dm}^2$, $\rho = 7.19 \text{ g/cm}^3$, $M_{\text{Cr}} = 52.00 \text{ g/mol}$, $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$, $n = 3$.

**Step 1 — Volume of deposit (1 mark):**

$$V = A \cdot \delta = 3.0 \times 10^{2} \text{ cm}^2 \times 0.30 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm} = 9.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm}^3$$

(Note: 1 dm² = 100 cm², so $A = 300$ cm².)

**Step 2 — Actual mass of chromium (1 mark):**

$$m_{\text{actual}} = \rho \cdot V = 7.19 \times 9.0 \times 10^{-3} = 6.471 \times 10^{-2} \text{ g} \approx 0.0647\ \text{g}$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical mass from Faraday's law (1 mark):**

$$m_{\text{theoretical}} = \dfrac{i_c \cdot A \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

Solve for $t$ using $\eta_c = m_{\text{actual}}/m_{\text{theoretical}}$:

$$t = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}} \cdot n \cdot F}{\eta_c \cdot i_c \cdot A \cdot M}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute (2 marks):**

$$t = \dfrac{(6.471 \times 10^{-2}) \times 3 \times 96{,}485}{0.70 \times 12 \times 3.0 \times 10^{2} \times 52.00}$$

Numerator: $6.471 \times 10^{-2} \times 3 \times 96{,}485 = 6.471 \times 10^{-2} \times 289{,}455 = 1.8730 \times 10^{4}$

Denominator: $0.70 \times 12 \times 300 \times 52.00 = 0.70 \times 187{,}200 = 1.3104 \times 10^{5}$

$$t = \dfrac{1.8730 \times 10^{4}}{1.3104 \times 10^{5}} = 0.1429 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
The mathematically calculated time is **~0.143 s**, but in industrial practice the actual plating time is **1–3 minutes** because:

- A **nucleation induction period** of ~30–60 s is required to initiate uniform deposition on the bright Ni surface.
- **Current distribution** is non-uniform; corners and edges receive higher local CD, requiring a lower average CD to avoid burning.
- **Agitation and mass transport** of Cr³⁺ to the cathode surface limit the usable rate.

**Step 6 — Theoretical mass (1 mark):**

$$m_{\text{theoretical}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{\eta_c} = \dfrac{0.0647}{0.70} = 0.0924 \text{ g}$$

**Final Answer Box:**

> **Plating time (theoretical) ≈ 0.143 s; Theoretical mass ≈ 0.0924 g; Actual mass deposited ≈ 0.0647 g.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Rapid-Revision Summary — Trivalent Chromium Electroplating**

- **Active ion:** Cr³⁺ (NOT Cr⁶⁺). Reduction: $\text{Cr}^{3+} + 3e^{-} \rightarrow \text{Cr}$; $E^{\circ} = -0.744$ V vs SHE; **n = 3 electrons.**
- **Cathode efficiency:** **60–80 %** (5–6× higher than Cr⁶⁺).
- **Operating window:** Temperature **25–45 °C**, pH **2.5–4.0** (typical 3.0–3.5), current density **5–20 A/dm²**.
- **Bath components:** Chromium sulphate (source) + formate/oxalate/glycine (complexing) + boric acid or Al sulphate (buffer) + Na₂SO₄ (conducting) + sulphonated surfactants (wetting/brightening) + reducing agent (Cr⁶⁺ scavenger).
- **Anode:** **Insoluble** — graphite, Pb-Sn, or platinised Ti. Soluble Cr anodes are **never** used.
- **Thickness:** Decorative flash only, **0.10–0.30 µm** typical, **max ~1 µm.** Cannot replace hard Cr⁶⁺ for engineering.
- **Faraday's law:** $m = \dfrac{I t M}{n F}$; always $n = 3$ for Cr; $M_{\text{Cr}} = 52.00$ g/mol; $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol; $\rho_{\text{Cr}} = 7.19$ g/cm³.
- **Plating-time formula:** $t = \dfrac{n F \rho \delta}{\eta_c \, i_c \, M}$.
- **Throwing power:** Higher than Cr⁶⁺ because of cathode polarisation and ligand moderation.
- **Deposit appearance:** Slightly **greyish-white** (not the bluish tint of Cr⁶⁺). Acceptable for all decorative uses.
- **Hardness:** ~400–700 HV — softer than hard Cr⁶⁺ (≈ 1000 HV).
- **Plastic substrate route:** Etched ABS → Electroless Ni → (optional Cu) → Bright Ni → **Trivalent Cr flash**.
- **Hydrogen embrittlement:** Significantly lower than Cr⁶⁺.
- **Effluent treatment:** Neutralisation + ion-exchange; Cr⁶⁺ contamination monitored and reduced with bromate/periodate.
- **Regulatory drivers:** **RoHS 3 (2015/863)**, **REACH Annex XIV/XVII**, **ELV Directive**, Indian **E-Waste (Management) Rules 2016, 2022 amendment**.
- **Common KTU exam questions:**
  1. Compare trivalent vs hexavalent (7 marks).
  2. Explain bath composition and role of each component (7 marks).
  3. Derive plating-time from Faraday's law (numerical, 7 marks).
  4. Discuss throwing power and current efficiency (3 marks).
  5. Why are insoluble anodes used? (3 marks).
- **Examiner traps to avoid:** (i) using n = 6, (ii) claiming trivalent can replace hard Cr, (iii) forgetting the pH window, (iv) omitting the complexing-agent role, (v) missing the regulatory/RoHS angle.

> **One-line memory anchor:** *Cr³⁺ = cleaner + safer + efficient + decorative, but cannot go thick.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plating Chemical Removal Methods
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔬 Plating Chemical Removal Methods — Core Foundation

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT864 - PLASTICS & DECORATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Plating Chemical Removal Methods

---

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Plating Chemical Removal Methods** (also termed **Chemical Stripping** or **De-plating**) refer to the controlled chemical dissolution of metallic electrodeposits from the surface of plated plastics, typically ABS (Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene), PC/ABS, or PP substrates. These methods are employed industrially to recover base plastic substrates from reject/defective plated components, salvage expensive precious metals (Au, Ag, Pd), rectify process errors, or decommission end-of-life decorative parts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved Wording):**
> *"Chemical removal of plating is a wet-chemical surface engineering technique in which a selective etchant (acidic, alkaline, or mixed-chelant formulation) is used to dissolve the metallic coating from a non-conductive polymer substrate without chemically attacking or mechanically distorting the underlying plastic body."*

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

### 🍰 The "Cake-Frosting" Analogy

Imagine a chocolate cake covered with a thin, decorative layer of colored fondant icing.

- The **cake** = the molded plastic substrate (ABS).
- The **fondant icing** = the electroplated metallic layer (Cu + Ni + Cr).
- **Mechanical stripping** would be like scraping the icing off with a knife — fast but you might damage the cake.
- **Chemical removal** is like carefully dissolving the fondant with a warm solvent — the icing vanishes, but the cake underneath is unharmed.

The plating is a multi-layer sandwich: usually **Cu (20–25 µm) → Ni (10–15 µm) → Cr (0.25–0.5 µm)**. Chemical removal must strip these layers **selectively, sequentially, or collectively**, depending on the chosen chemistry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this topic important for the KTU board exam?**
> 1. Approximately **3–7 marks** are asked every year from this Module 2 area on "Rejection, Rework & Salvage in Plastics Decoration."
> 2. It tests the student's ability to connect **chemistry, electrochemistry, and polymer science** — a classic KTU interdisciplinary question.
> 3. It carries strong **environmental (GREEN) implications** relevant to NEP 2020 ESG outcomes.

---

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Board-Relevant)

| Parameter | Typical Industry Value | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Strip Rate** | **5 – 25 µm/min** | Affects process time |
| **Operating Temperature** | **20 – 65 °C** | Energy cost & safety |
| **Bath Loading (g/L of metal)** | **80 – 150 g/L** | Determines dump frequency |
| **Selectivity Ratio (Metal : Substrate)** | **>1000 : 1** | Critical quality index |
| **Recovery Yield (Au/Ag/Pd)** | **95 – 99 %** | Economic driver |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Safety Constants to Memorize:**
> - **HCN (Hydrogen Cyanide) Lethal Concentration** = **100–300 ppm** in air.
> - **HNO₃ (Nitric Acid) Fuming Threshold** = **>68 wt%** (red fuming).
> - **Cr(VI) Carcinogen Classification** = **Group 1 IARC Carcinogen**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis — Stripping Mechanisms & KTU Cheat Sheet

---

## 2.1 The Three-Stage Stripping Logic

Any chemical plating-removal operation follows a **three-stage chemical logic**, regardless of which stripper is used:

### **Stage 1 — Wetting & Diffusion (Physical Step)**
The stripper solution must first penetrate the **micro-porosity** of the topmost metal layer (typically micro-cracked or micro-porous Chromium). Surfactants and **wetting agents (e.g., 0.5–2 g/L sodium lauryl sulfate)** are added to lower surface tension.

### **Stage 2 — Redox Dissolution (Chemical Step)**
The metal atoms undergo **oxidation** (lose electrons → become cations → dissolve in the bath). For each metal, a specific redox reaction occurs. This is the **core chemistry stage** and is what differentiates one stripper from another.

### **Stage 3 — Complexation / Chelation (Stabilization Step)**
The dissolved metal ions must be **sequestered** by ligands (NH₃, CN⁻, EDTA, citrate) to:
- Prevent re-deposition onto the plastic surface.
- Prevent sludge precipitation.
- Increase bath loading capacity.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Chemical Removal Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Examiner Classification Mnemonic — "SAFE-CAN"**
> 
> **S** — Sulphuric / Nitric acid strippers (for Cu, Ni)
> **A** — Alkaline (amine-based) strippers
> **F** — Ferric chloride / Ferric sulphate strippers
> **E** — Electrolytic (reversed-current) stripping
> **C** — Cyanide strippers (for Au, Ag, Pd)
> **A** — Ammoniacal strippers
> **N** — Nitric-based bright strippers

### **2.2.1 Acid-Based Strippers**

#### **(a) Sulphuric Acid Strippers (for Copper)**
- **Composition:** H₂SO₄ (10–25 %) + oxidizer (H₂O₂ or HNO₃).
- **Mechanism:** Acid dissolves the **Cu-oxide layer** formed on exposure.
- **Reaction:**

$$\text{Cu} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CuSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

#### **(b) Nitric Acid Strippers (for Ni, Cu)**
- **Composition:** HNO₃ (30–50 %) + NaCl (additive to break Cr passivation).
- **Reaction:**

$$\begin{aligned}
3\text{Cu} + 8\text{HNO}_3 &\rightarrow 3\text{Cu(NO}_3)_2 + 2\text{NO}\uparrow + 4\text{H}_2\text{O} \\
\text{Ni} + 4\text{HNO}_3 &\rightarrow \text{Ni(NO}_3)_2 + 2\text{NO}_2\uparrow + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}$$

#### **(c) Sulphamic Acid Strippers (Mild — for Thin Cr)**
- Used for **decorative Cr thicknesses (<1 µm)** — safer and slower than HCl.

---

### **2.2.2 Alkaline Strippers**

#### **Ammoniacal Etching (for Cu)**
- **Composition:** NH₄OH + (NH₄)₂CO₃ + Cu-amine complex.
- **Reaction:**

$$\text{Cu} + 4\text{NH}_3 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow [\text{Cu(NH}_3)_4]\text{(OH)}_2$$

- **Advantage:** No attack on underlying Ni — **highly selective**.
- **Use Case:** Preferred for **selective Cu-stripping** in triple-layer plating.

#### **Amine-Based Strippers**
- Ethylenediamine (EDA), triethanolamine (TEA) — used for **Sn, Sn-Pb, Sn-Ag** stripping on plastics.

---

### **2.2.3 Cyanide Strippers (Precious Metals)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** *Cyanide strippers are EXTREMELY toxic. They will appear in board exams only for "Au / Ag / Pd recovery from rejects" — never as a recommended production method.*

- **Composition:** NaCN (3–8 %) + oxidizer (H₂O₂ or air sparging).
- **For Gold:**

$$4\text{Au} + 8\text{NaCN} + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 4\text{Na[Au(CN)}_2] + 4\text{NaOH}$$

- **For Silver:**

$$4\text{Ag} + 8\text{NaCN} + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 4\text{Na[Ag(CN)}_2] + 4\text{NaOH}$$

---

### **2.2.4 Electrolytic (Reverse-Current) Stripping**

- The plated plastic is made the **ANODE** (+) in a stripping cell.
- The metal dissolves by **anodic dissolution**.
- **Advantage:** Faster, more uniform, no chemical consumption.
- **Typical Conditions:** 2–10 V, 5–30 A/dm², in H₂SO₄ or NaNO₃ electrolyte.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reverse-Current Stripping** is the **GREENEST** method because it generates **zero spent chemistry** — only dissolved metal ions, which are 100 % recoverable by electrowinning.

---

### **2.2.5 Specialty / Proprietary Strippers**

| Trade Name (Generic Type) | Target Metal | pH | Temperature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Enstrip** (nitric–fluoboric) | Ni, Cu | <1 | 25–40 °C |
| **Chemstrip** (amine–thiourea) | Au | 8–10 | 50–60 °C |
| **Metalx** (sulphamic) | Cr, Ni | 1–2 | 30–50 °C |
| **Electrostrip** (NaNO₃) | Cu, Ni | 6–7 | RT |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> **All quantities, units, and boundary conditions required for KTU ESE problem-solving.**

| # | Parameter / Equation | Formula / Value | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Strip Rate (SR)** | $SR = \dfrac{m}{A \cdot t}$ | µm/min | $m$ = mass dissolved, $A$ = area, $t$ = time |
| 2 | **Faraday's Stripping Law** | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | grams | For electrolytic stripping |
| 3 | **Current Efficiency (η)** | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | % | Quality index |
| 4 | **Selectivity Index (S)** | $S = \dfrac{R_{metal}}{R_{substrate}}$ | dimensionless | $> 1000$ ideal |
| 5 | **Bath Loading (BL)** | $BL = \dfrac{C_{metal}}{C_{max}}$ | fraction | Dump at 0.8 |
| 6 | **Faraday's Constant** | $F = 96485$ | C/mol | Always **bold** in answer |
| 7 | **Activation Energy (Eₐ) of Stripping** | $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ | J/mol | Arrhenius, usually **40–60 kJ/mol** |
| 8 | **Cr Strip Rate (HCl bath)** | $r_{Cr} = 1.5 - 3.0$ | µm/min | At 25 °C, 1 M HCl |
| 9 | **Ni Strip Rate (HNO₃)** | $r_{Ni} = 8 - 15$ | µm/min | At 40 °C, 30 % HNO₃ |
| 10 | **Cu Strip Rate (NH₃ bath)** | $r_{Cu} = 5 - 10$ | µm/min | At 30 °C, 6 M NH₃ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory verbatim quote for any KTU board answer:**
> *"The selection of a chemical removal method is governed by four KTU parameters: (i) the type of metal coating, (ii) the nature of the plastic substrate, (iii) the acceptable environmental load, and (iv) the economic value of recoverable metal."*

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Application of Plating Chemical Removal |
|---|---|
| **Automotive (interior trim)** | Reject salvage — recover ABS from mis-plated knobs, badges |
| **Sanitary ware (bathroom fittings)** | Defective chrome-plated ABS shower heads — rework |
| **Electronics (EMI shields)** | Precious-metal recovery from plated PC/ABS housings |
| **Jewellery & Fashion** | Gold-stripping from display samples for re-use |
| **E-Waste Recycling (NEP 2020 ESG)** | Urban-mining of Au/Ag/Pd from end-of-life decorative plastics |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Solutions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Standard:** Every algebraic transition is explicitly written. No shortcuts. No skipped steps.

---

## 3.1 Problem 1 — Stripping Rate Calculation (Typical KTU 14-mark part (a))

### **Problem Statement (KTU 2019 ESE pattern):**
*"A chromium-plated ABS component has a plating area of 250 cm² and a Cr thickness of 0.50 µm. The component is stripped in a 1.5 M HCl bath at 30 °C. The stripping time observed is 4 minutes. Calculate the strip rate and verify if it meets the industry-acceptable range (1–3 µm/min) for Cr removal from plastics."*

---

### **Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step:**

#### **Step 1: Identify Given Data**
- Plated area, $A = 250 \text{ cm}^2 = 250 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 0.025 \text{ m}^2$
- Thickness, $t_{Cr} = 0.50 \text{ µm} = 0.50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$
- Stripping time, $t = 4 \text{ min}$
- Density of Cr, $\rho_{Cr} = 7.19 \times 10^{3} \text{ kg/m}^3$

#### **Step 2: Compute Volume of Cr Removed**
$$V_{Cr} = A \times t_{Cr} = 0.025 \times (0.50 \times 10^{-6})$$
$$V_{Cr} = 1.25 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3$$

#### **Step 3: Compute Mass of Cr Removed**
$$m_{Cr} = V_{Cr} \times \rho_{Cr}$$
$$m_{Cr} = (1.25 \times 10^{-8}) \times (7.19 \times 10^{3})$$
$$m_{Cr} = 8.9875 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg} = 89.875 \text{ mg}$$

#### **Step 4: Compute Strip Rate**
$$SR = \dfrac{t_{Cr}}{t} = \dfrac{0.50 \text{ µm}}{4 \text{ min}}$$
$$SR = 0.125 \text{ µm/min}$$

#### **Step 5: Verify Against Industry Standard**
- Industry acceptable range: **1 – 3 µm/min**.
- Computed: **0.125 µm/min** → **Below acceptable**.
- **Conclusion:** *The HCl bath at 30 °C is too dilute/cool. To meet the standard, increase HCl molarity to 3 M or raise temperature to 50 °C, OR switch to a nitric–hydrochloric mixed acid system.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this answer scores full marks:** The student explicitly compares the calculated value with the industry band, justifies the deviation, and proposes a corrective engineering action — exactly the **Apply / Analyse** level expected by KTU 2024 Rubrics.

---

## 3.2 Problem 2 — Electrolytic Stripping with Faraday's Law (KTU 2024-style)

### **Problem Statement:**
*"A nickel-plated plastic component is to be stripped using reverse-current electrolysis. The plated area is 400 cm², the Ni thickness is 15 µm, and the current applied is 12 A at a current efficiency of 92 %. Calculate (a) the theoretical time required to strip the entire nickel layer, and (b) the actual time considering efficiency loss."*

---

### **Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step:**

#### **Step 1: Given Data**
- Area, $A = 400 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.04 \text{ m}^2$
- Thickness, $t_{Ni} = 15 \text{ µm} = 15 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$
- Current, $I = 12 \text{ A}$
- Current efficiency, $\eta = 92\% = 0.92$
- Nickel atomic mass, $M_{Ni} = 58.69 \text{ g/mol}$
- Nickel density, $\rho_{Ni} = 8.90 \times 10^3 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Nickel valency in solution, $n = 2$

#### **Step 2: Compute Volume of Ni to be Removed**
$$V_{Ni} = A \times t_{Ni} = 0.04 \times (15 \times 10^{-6})$$
$$V_{Ni} = 6.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3$$

#### **Step 3: Compute Mass of Ni**
$$m_{Ni} = V_{Ni} \times \rho_{Ni}$$
$$m_{Ni} = (6.0 \times 10^{-7}) \times (8.90 \times 10^3)$$
$$m_{Ni} = 5.34 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg} = 5.34 \text{ g}$$

#### **Step 4: Apply Faraday's Law of Electrolysis**
$$m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

Solving for time:
$$t = \dfrac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot M}$$

#### **Step 5: Theoretical Time (100 % Efficiency)**
$$t_{theo} = \dfrac{(5.34) \times (2) \times (96485)}{(12) \times (58.69)}$$
$$t_{theo} = \dfrac{1.0305 \times 10^6}{704.28}$$
$$t_{theo} = 1463 \text{ s} \approx 24.4 \text{ min}$$

#### **Step 6: Actual Time (92 % Efficiency)**
$$t_{actual} = \dfrac{t_{theo}}{\eta} = \dfrac{24.4}{0.92}$$
$$t_{actual} \approx 26.5 \text{ min}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Answer-Writing Tip:** Always quote Faraday's Constant $\mathbf{F = 96485 \text{ C/mol}}$ **in bold**, and mention that $\mathbf{n=2}$ for Ni²⁺ ions in solution. These are the two highest-frequency "mark-grabbing" facts in Module 2 electrochemistry questions.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Selecting Stripping Method

| If the Coating is… | And the Substrate is… | And the Goal is… | **Recommended Method** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cu (thick, 20 µm) | ABS | Salvage plastic | **Ammoniacal (NH₃ + air)** |
| Ni (15 µm) | ABS | Recover Ni²⁺ | **HNO₃ (30 %) + NaCl** |
| Cr (thin, 0.5 µm) | ABS | Strip only Cr | **HCl (1–3 M), warm** |
| Au (0.05 µm) | PC/ABS | Precious metal recovery | **NaCN + H₂O₂ (gold stripper)** |
| Triple-layer Cu+Ni+Cr | ABS | Total strip | **Reverse-current electrolysis** |
| Tin (Sn) | PP | Salvage | **Amine (EDA)-based alkaline** |

---

## 3.4 Worked Energy & Cost Estimation

### **Problem 3 — Quick-Reference KTU Numerical:**
*"A batch of 500 defective chrome-plated ABS parts, each of 100 cm² plated area with 0.4 µm Cr, is to be stripped in a 2 M HCl bath. Assuming a strip rate of 2.5 µm/min and a bath utilization of 75 %, estimate (i) the total HCl volume required if bath loading is 100 g/L Cr, and (ii) the total process time."*

---

#### **Solution — Exhaustive:**

**Step 1: Volume of Cr per Part**
$$V_{Cr/part} = (100 \text{ cm}^2) \times (0.4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm}) = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 2: Mass of Cr per Part**
$$m_{Cr/part} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \times 7.19 = 0.02876 \text{ g/part}$$

**Step 3: Total Mass for 500 Parts**
$$m_{Cr,total} = 0.02876 \times 500 = 14.38 \text{ g}$$

**Step 4: HCl Volume Required (at 100 g/L Loading, 75 % Utilization)**
$$V_{HCl} = \dfrac{m_{Cr,total}}{100 \times 0.75}$$
$$V_{HCl} = \dfrac{14.38}{75} = 0.192 \text{ L} \approx 192 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 5: Process Time per Part**
$$t_{part} = \dfrac{0.4 \text{ µm}}{2.5 \text{ µm/min}} = 0.16 \text{ min/part}$$

**Step 6: Total Process Time (parallel processing assumed)**
$$t_{total} \approx 0.16 \text{ min} \approx 9.6 \text{ s/part (approx.)}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Stripping Reaction Sequence (Cu on ABS)

```mermaid

    participant Bath as Stripper Bath

    Surfactant->>Cu: Penetrates micro-pores
    Cu->>Bath: Oxidation reaction Cu0 to Cu2+
    Bath->>Bath: Complexation with NH3 or CN-
    Note over Cu,Substrate: Selective dissolution occurs

```
