# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Rapid Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** 
**"Define chemical stripping of plated plastics. List two advantages over mechanical stripping."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Chemical stripping** of plated plastics is a wet-chemical surface engineering process in which a selective etchant — acidic, alkaline, or chelant-based — is used to dissolve the metallic electrodeposits from a polymer substrate without causing chemical or mechanical damage to the underlying plastic.
>
> **Two advantages over mechanical stripping:**
> 1. **Selective** — only the metal dissolves; the plastic substrate is preserved.
> 2. **Uniform** — reaches recessed / intricate geometries (slots, ribs, blind holes) that mechanical tools cannot access.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Why is cyanide-based stripping preferred for gold-plated plastics despite its toxicity?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. Gold is **noble** — it does not dissolve in simple mineral acids.
> 2. Cyanide forms a **highly stable aurocyanide complex**, $\mathbf{[Au(CN)_2]^-}$, with a formation constant of $\mathbf{K_f \approx 10^{38}}$, enabling rapid dissolution at low temperature.
> 3. The complex allows **easy downstream electrowinning**, making gold recovery economically viable despite the cyanide handling cost.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Choice (Answer ANY ONE — 14 Marks)

---

### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)** *(Cu-Ni-Cr System)*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
*(a)* Explain the **sequential chemical stripping procedure** for a Cu + Ni + Cr triple-layer plated ABS component. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* Write the balanced **chemical equations** for each stage and identify the **type of reaction**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution — Part (a):**

**Stage 1 — Chromium Strip (Outer Layer)**
- Bath: **HCl (1–3 M)**, 30–50 °C, 2–5 minutes.
- Reason: HCl is the **only common acid** that dissolves the passive Cr₂O₃ layer; H₂SO₄ alone does not attack Cr.

**Stage 2 — Nickel Strip (Middle Layer)**
- Bath: **HNO₃ (30 %) + NaCl (saturated)**, 40 °C, 5–10 minutes.
- Reason: NaCl breaks the Ni-oxide passivation; NO₂ evolution indicates active stripping.

**Stage 3 — Copper Strip (Inner Layer — Most Critical)**
- Bath: **NH₄OH + (NH₄)₂CO₃ + air sparging**, 30 °C, 8–15 minutes.
- Reason: NH₃ forms a soluble Cu-amine complex; ABS is **unaffected** by alkaline ammonia — high selectivity.

**Stage 4 — Final Rinse + Inspection**
- Deionized water rinse, visual inspection under 10× microscope for residual metal.

---

#### **Solution — Part (b):**

**Stage 1 — Cr Stripping**
$$\text{Cr} + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{CrCl}_2 + \text{H}_2 \uparrow \quad \text{[Redox — single displacement]}$$

**Stage 2 — Ni Stripping**
$$3\text{Ni} + 8\text{HNO}_3 \rightarrow 3\text{Ni(NO}_3)_2 + 2\text{NO} \uparrow + 4\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \text{[Redox]}$$

**Stage 3 — Cu Stripping (Ammoniacal)**
$$\text{Cu} + 4\text{NH}_3 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow [\text{Cu(NH}_3)_4]\text{(OH)}_2 \quad \text{[Redox + Complexation]}$$

---

### **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)** *(Precious Metal Recovery)*

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern 2024]**
*(a)* Discuss the **cyanide stripping process** for gold recovery from plated plastics, with safety and environmental controls. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* Compare **chemical stripping vs. electrolytic stripping** in a tabular matrix, highlighting **six engineering parameters**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution — Part (a):**

**Composition of Bath:**
- NaCN: 3–8 wt%
- pH: 11–12 (alkaline — to prevent HCN evolution)
- Oxidizer: H₂O₂ (0.5–1 %) or air sparging
- Temperature: 50–60 °C

**Reaction:**
$$4\text{Au} + 8\text{NaCN} + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 4\text{Na[Au(CN)}_2] + 4\text{NaOH}$$

**Safety & Environmental Controls:**
1. **Closed-loop reactor** with continuous pH > 11 to keep CN⁻ as salt.
2. **HCN gas monitor** with alarm at 1 ppm (TLV = 4.7 ppm).
3. **Alkaline chlorination** of spent bath: $\text{CN}^- + \text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow \text{CNO}^- + \text{Cl}^-$ followed by oxidation to $\text{CO}_2 + \text{N}_2$.
4. **Effluent pH adjustment** before discharge.
5. **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** — full-face respirator with SCBA backup.

---

#### **Solution — Part (b) — Comparison Matrix:**

| # | Parameter | Chemical Stripping | Electrolytic Stripping |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Energy Source** | Chemical potential | Electrical energy |
| 2 | **Strip Rate** | 5–25 µm/min | 10–50 µm/min |
| 3 | **Selectivity** | High (with right chemistry) | Moderate (current density-dependent) |
| 4 | **Chemical Consumption** | High (bath gets loaded) | Minimal (no chemical depletion) |
| 5 | **Environmental Burden** | High (spent bath disposal) | Low (only metal ions to recover) |
| 6 | **Capital Cost** | Low (tanks + heaters) | High (rectifier + cells + bus bars) |
| 7 | **Metal Recovery** | Chemical precipitation | Direct electrowinning |
| 8 | **Substrate Damage Risk** | Low (if selective) | Moderate (anodic oxidation possible) |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Plating Chemical Removal Questions:**
> 1. ❌ **Forgetting to specify pH** — many students write "HCl bath" without mentioning **pH < 2**, which is essential for Cr dissolution.
> 2. ❌ **Confusing oxidation states** — Au is stripped as **Au⁺ (not Au³⁺)** in cyanide complex $[\text{Au(CN)}_2]^-$. Writing Au³⁺ loses 1 mark.
> 3. ❌ **Not quoting Faraday's constant F = 96485 C/mol** explicitly — board examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 4. ❌ **Ignoring ABS-substrate protection** — when asked "why ammoniacal stripping for Cu?", always state "because **NH₃ does not attack ABS**, ensuring substrate integrity."
> 5. ❌ **Missing safety protocol** — cyanide-related answers without HCN-control measures will lose at least 2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> 📌 **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "10 Must-Know Facts for KTU PEPOT864 Module 2":**

- ⭐ **Definition:** Chemical stripping = selective wet-chemical dissolution of metallic coatings from plastics.
- ⭐ **Three-stage logic:** Wetting → Redox Dissolution → Complexation.
- ⭐ **Cr outer layer** → Use **HCl** (only acid that breaks Cr₂O₃ passivation).
- ⭐ **Ni middle layer** → Use **HNO₃ + NaCl** (chlorides break Ni-oxide).
- ⭐ **Cu inner layer** → Use **ammoniacal** (NH₃ + air) — substrate-safe.
- ⭐ **Au / Ag / Pd** → Use **cyanide** stripper — complex $\mathbf{[Au(CN)_2]^-}$.
- ⭐ **Electrolytic stripping** = reverse-current, anodic, **greenest** method.
- ⭐ **Faraday's Law:** $\mathbf{m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}}$ with **F = 96485 C/mol**.
- ⭐ **Industry strip rate band:** **1–3 µm/min for Cr**, **5–25 µm/min for Cu/Ni**.
- ⭐ **Safety Constants:** HCN LCL = **100 ppm**; Cr(VI) = Group 1 Carcinogen; **always maintain pH > 11** in cyanide baths.

> **Closing Line (board-style):**
> *"The chemistry of plating removal is not destruction — it is **circular resource engineering**, transforming a reject into a substrate-plus-recovered-metal asset, in perfect alignment with the **NEP 2020 Sustainability and ESG framework** that KTU 2024 explicitly endorses."*

---

topics.title: Palatable Plastics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT864 - PLASTICS & DECORATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Palatable Plastics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Palatable Plastics** refer to a specialized class of polymeric materials that are **organoleptically acceptable**, **chemically inert toward food/beverages/biological systems**, and **regulatory-compliant** for applications involving direct or indirect contact with consumables (food, pharmaceuticals, drinking water) and for consumer-facing decorative products where user comfort and safety are paramount.

> [!NOTE]
> **Organoleptic Properties** are those properties perceived by the human senses — taste, odor, appearance, and texture. A "palatable" plastic must NOT impart any foreign taste, smell, color, or texture to the substance it contacts.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Palatable Plastics encompasses three overlapping domains:

1. **Food-Contact Plastics (FCPs)** — polymers approved under **FDA 21 CFR**, **EU Regulation 1935/2004**, and **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)** for direct contact with edible products.
2. **Biocompatible Plastics** — polymers used in medical devices, implants, and pharmaceutical packaging (USP Class VI / ISO 10993 compliant).
3. **Aesthetically Pleasing Decorative Plastics** — polymers engineered to be visually, tactilely, and olfactorily pleasing in interior décor, consumer goods, and lifestyle products.

The defining criteria for a plastic to be classified as **palatable** are:

- **Zero or negligible migration** of monomers, oligomers, additives, or processing aids into the contacting medium.
- **No off-taste or off-odor** (must score above the threshold Recognition Threshold on sensory panels).
- **Non-toxic**, non-carcinogenic, non-mutagenic, and non-endocrine-disrupting.
- **Compliance with Overall Migration Limits (OML)** and **Specific Migration Limits (SML)** as per global regulatory frameworks.
- **Barrier integrity** — must resist permeation of oxygen, moisture, and contaminants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Regulatory Constants & Standard Metrics:**
> - **Overall Migration Limit (OML)** = **10 mg/dm²** of food contact surface (EU standard).
> - **Overall Migration Limit (OML)** = **60 mg/kg** of food simulant (alternative EU expression).
> - **Specific Migration Limit (SML)** = varies per substance (e.g., Vinyl Chloride monomer SML = **0.01 mg/kg**).
> - **Global Migration Limit** for food simulants: **10 mg/dm²** or **60 mg/kg food**.
> - **FDA Food Contact Substance (FCS)** notification threshold: any new food contact substance must undergo premarket review.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🍶 The New Clay Pot vs. The Old Metal Cup Analogy**
>
> Imagine you pour fresh, cold milk into two containers:
> - **Container A**: A traditional, well-glazed **earthen clay pot** (matka). The milk stays naturally cool, tastes earthy and pure, and feels right. This is what using a **palatable plastic** feels like — the container does its job silently, preserving the original sensory character of its contents.
> - **Container B**: A cheap, rusted **old metal cup** that leaches iron oxides and metallic flavors into the milk. The milk tastes metallic, looks discolored, and may even be unsafe. This represents an **unpalatable or non-compliant plastic** — chemicals migrate into the food, altering taste, smell, color, and potentially harming health.
>
> Just as you instinctively trust the clay pot to preserve the milk's natural flavor, **palatable plastics are engineered to be the "invisible, trustworthy containers"** of the modern food, pharma, and consumer world. They must be **silent partners** — present physically but chemically and sensorially **absent**.

A simpler way to think: **Palatable = "doesn't interfere."** The plastic should perform its mechanical, barrier, and decorative functions while remaining completely **invisible** to the human senses and the biology of what it touches.

## 1.3 Classification Snapshot of Palatable Plastics

| Category | Example Polymers | Typical Applications |
|---|---|---|
| **Food-Contact Plastics** | PET, HDPE, PP, LDPE, PS, PC (restricted) | Bottles, containers, wraps, films, cutlery |
| **High-Barrier Food Plastics** | EVOH, PVDC, PEN, Multilayer PA/PE | Vacuum packaging, retort pouches, aseptic packs |
| **Biodegradable Palatable** | PLA, PHB, PHBV, Starch blends | Compostable cutlery, single-use cups, bio-films |
| **Medical/Biocompatible** | PVC (plasticized), PE, PP, PU, Silicone, PEEK | IV bags, tubing, implants, pharma blisters |
| **Decorative/Aesthetic** | Acrylic (PMMA), ABS, Polycarbonate, Bio-resins | Lenses, displays, luxury packaging, interior panels |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why "Palatability" is an Engineering Challenge

Plastics are inherently **not inert**. They contain:

- **Residual monomers** (e.g., vinyl chloride in PVC, styrene in PS, bisphenol-A in PC)
- **Oligomers** (low-molecular-weight polymer fragments)
- **Additives** (plasticizers, stabilizers, antioxidants, slip agents, colorants, antistats)
- **Processing aids** (lubricants, mold release agents, solvents)
- **Contaminants** from recycling or environmental exposure

When a plastic contacts food, water, or biological tissue, these substances can **migrate** across the polymer matrix boundary into the contacting medium via three mechanisms:

1. **Diffusion** — molecular movement through the polymer driven by concentration gradient (Fickian or non-Fickian).
2. **Partitioning** — unequal distribution of migrants at the polymer-food interface.
3. **Desorption** — release of surface-bound or loosely held substances.

## 2.2 The Palatability Framework — Four Pillars

### Pillar 1: Chemical Safety (Migration Control)
Migration is governed by **Fick's Second Law of Diffusion** through the polymer. The migrant moves from high concentration (plastic) to low concentration (food).

### Pillar 2: Sensory Neutrality (Organoleptic Integrity)
The plastic must score below the **Recognition Threshold** in triangle sensory tests. Trained panels detect taints at concentrations as low as **parts per billion (ppb)**.

### Pillar 3: Biological Safety (Toxicological Clearance)
Migration levels must remain below **No-Observed-Adverse-Effect Level (NOAEL)** with appropriate safety factors (typically **100× for non-carcinogens, 1000× for carcinogens**).

### Pillar 4: Aesthetic Acceptability (Decorative Appeal)
For decorative plastics, palatability extends to **visual appeal, tactile comfort, color stability**, and **lack of plastic odor** — important for consumer satisfaction.

## 2.3 Factors Affecting Palatability of Plastics

| Factor | Effect on Palatability | Engineering Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** ↑ | Migration rate ↑ exponentially (Arrhenius) | Use retort-grade or high-T polymers |
| **Contact time** ↑ | Total migration ↑ | Use barrier layers, minimize shelf-contact time |
| **Food acidity (pH)** ↑ | Hydrolysis of polymer, additive leaching | Use acid-resistant grades (e.g., PET for acidic foods) |
| **Fat content of food** ↑ | Lipophilic migrants dissolve preferentially | Use fat-resistant or barrier-coated polymers |
| **Surface area to volume ratio** ↑ | Migration per unit mass ↑ | Optimize container geometry |
| **Polymer crystallinity** ↑ | Migration ↓ (crystalline regions are impermeable) | Use HDPE over LDPE for lower migration |
| **Polymer molecular weight** ↑ | Migration ↓ (larger molecules diffuse slower) | Use high-MW grades; minimize oligomers |
| **Additive volatility/polarity** | High volatility/polarity → higher migration | Use high-MW, reactive, or polymeric additives |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Must Memorize for KTU Exams**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fick's First Law (steady-state flux) | $J = -D \cdot \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | $J$ = flux [kg/m²s], $D$ = diffusivity [m²/s], $C$ = concentration [kg/m³] |
| 2 | Fick's Second Law (time-dependent) | $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \cdot \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$ | Used for unsteady-state migration |
| 3 | Steady-state migration through slab | $M_t = \dfrac{D \cdot C_0}{L} \left(t - \dfrac{L^2}{6D}\right)$ | $M_t$ = cumulative migrated mass [kg/m²], $L$ = slab thickness [m] |
| 4 | Lag-time (time before steady state) | $t_{lag} = \dfrac{L^2}{6D}$ | [seconds] — critical for packaging design |
| 5 | Arrhenius temperature dependence of $D$ | $D = D_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | $E_a$ = activation energy [J/mol], $R$ = 8.314 J/mol·K |
| 6 | Partition coefficient (polymer/food) | $K_{P/F} = \dfrac{C_{\text{polymer}}}{C_{\text{food}}}$ at equilibrium | Dimensionless; $K \approx 1$ for many systems |
| 7 | Overall Migration Limit (OML) — EU | $\text{OML} \leq 10 \text{ mg/dm}^2$ | Or equivalently $\leq 60 \text{ mg/kg food}$ |
| 8 | Specific Migration Limit (SML) | $\text{SML} = \dfrac{\text{NOAEL} \times 60}{\text{TDI} \times 1000}$ | TDI = Tolerable Daily Intake [mg/kg body weight/day] |
| 9 | Diffusion coefficient typical range | $D \sim 10^{-14}$ to $10^{-10}$ m²/s | Polymers at room temperature |
| 10 | Permeability | $P = D \cdot S$ | $S$ = solubility coefficient; $P$ in [m²/s] or [cm³·cm/cm²·s·Pa] |
| 11 | Henry's Law (for gas/flavor scalping) | $C = k_H \cdot P$ | Flavor loss from food into polymer headspace |
| 12 | Safety Factor (Toxicology) | $\text{SF} = \dfrac{\text{NOAEL}}{\text{ADI/TDI}}$ | Typically **100×** for food contact |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The palatability framework governs billion-dollar industries:

- **Food Packaging** — every PET water bottle, HDPE milk jug, PP yogurt cup must pass migration tests.
- **Pharmaceutical Packaging** — PVC/PE/PVDC blisters must not contaminate medicines.
- **Medical Devices** — catheters, implants, IV bags use USP Class VI certified polymers.
- **Decorative Consumer Goods** — acrylic displays, luxury perfume caps, premium stationery.
- **Cookware & Kitchenware** — melamine plates, silicone spatulas, PP containers must pass **21 CFR § 175.300** and **EN 1186** tests.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Steady-State Migration Through a Plastic Wall (Fickian Model)

**Setup:** A polymer slab of thickness $L$ contains an initial migrant concentration $C_0$ uniformly distributed. One face ($x = 0$) contacts food of essentially infinite volume (well-stirred, concentration $\approx 0$). The other face ($x = L$) is impermeable (e.g., metal foil laminate backing).

**Governing Equation:** Fick's Second Law in one dimension:

$$\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \cdot \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

**Boundary Conditions:**
- $C(x, 0) = C_0$ for all $x$ (initial uniform distribution)
- $C(0, t) = 0$ for $t > 0$ (perfect sink at food interface)
- $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x=L} = 0$ (impermeable backing layer)

**Solution Method:** Separation of variables / eigenfunction expansion (Crank, *The Mathematics of Diffusion*, classical result).

**Steady-State Flux (after long time $t \gg t_{lag}$):**

$$J_{ss} = \dfrac{D \cdot C_0}{L}$$

**Cumulative Migration at time $t$:**

$$M_t = \dfrac{D \cdot C_0}{L} \cdot \left(t - \dfrac{L^2}{6D}\right)$$

**Step-by-Step Reasoning:**

| Step | Reasoning |
|---|---|
| 1 | At steady state, concentration profile through slab becomes linear from $C_0$ at $x = L$ down to $0$ at $x = 0$. |
| 2 | Apply Fick's First Law: flux = $-D \cdot \dfrac{dC}{dx} = -D \cdot \dfrac{0 - C_0}{0 - L} = \dfrac{D \cdot C_0}{L}$. |
| 3 | The lag-time $t_{lag} = \dfrac{L^2}{6D}$ represents the time to establish this linear profile — before this, flux is sub-linear. |
| 4 | Total mass migrated at time $t$: integrate flux over time, subtract initial transient (gives the $\frac{L^2}{6D}$ correction). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Doubling the wall thickness ($L \rightarrow 2L$) **halves** the steady-state flux AND **quadruples** the lag-time. This is why thick-walled HDPE containers outperform thin LDPE films for long-shelf-life food storage.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Arrhenius Temperature Acceleration of Migration

**Setup:** Migration tests are normally conducted at room temperature over weeks/months. To accelerate, tests are done at elevated temperature (e.g., 40 °C for 10 days simulates 6 months at 25 °C). The acceleration factor is derived from Arrhenius kinetics.

**Starting Point:** $D$ is temperature-dependent:

$$D(T) = D_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R \cdot T}\right)$$

**Acceleration Factor** between test temperature $T_2$ and use temperature $T_1$:

$$AF = \dfrac{D(T_2)}{D(T_1)} = \exp\left[\dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$$

**Step-by-Step Expansion:**

$$\begin{aligned}
AF &= \dfrac{D_0 \cdot \exp(-E_a / (R T_2))}{D_0 \cdot \exp(-E_a / (R T_1))} \\[6pt]
&= \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R T_2} + \dfrac{E_a}{R T_1}\right) \\[6pt]
&= \exp\left[\dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Given: $E_a = 80 \text{ kJ/mol}$, $T_1 = 298 \text{ K}$ (25 °C), $T_2 = 313 \text{ K}$ (40 °C), $R = 8.314 \text{ J/mol·K}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2} &= \dfrac{1}{298} - \dfrac{1}{313} \\[4pt]
&= 0.003356 - 0.003195 \\[4pt]
&= 0.000161 \text{ K}^{-1} \\[4pt]
\dfrac{E_a}{R} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right) &= \dfrac{80{,}000}{8.314} \cdot 0.000161 \\[4pt]
&= 9622 \cdot 0.000161 \\[4pt]
&\approx 1.549 \\[4pt]
AF &= \exp(1.549) \approx 4.71
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** Storage at 40 °C for 10 days is equivalent to approximately **47 days** at 25 °C — a useful acceleration for QA testing.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Specific Migration Limit (SML) Calculation

**Given:**
- NOAEL (No Observed Adverse Effect Level) of a migrant from animal studies = 5 mg/kg body weight/day
- Default body weight = 60 kg
- Conventional food consumption = 1 kg food/day packaged in contact plastic
- Standard safety factor = 100

**Step-by-Step Computation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{TDI (Tolerable Daily Intake)} &= \dfrac{\text{NOAEL}}{\text{Safety Factor}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{5 \text{ mg/kg bw/day}}{100} \\[4pt]
&= 0.05 \text{ mg/kg bw/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Maximum permissible migrant mass per kg food consumed:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{SML} &= \dfrac{\text{TDI} \times \text{Body Weight}}{\text{Food Consumption}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{0.05 \text{ mg/kg bw/day} \times 60 \text{ kg}}{1 \text{ kg food/day}} \\[4pt]
&= 3.0 \text{ mg/kg food}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** The migrant concentration in food must not exceed **3.0 mg/kg food** under intended use conditions.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Plastic × Application × Palatability Status

| Polymer | Common Use | Taste/Odor Risk | Migration Concern | Regulatory Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PET** | Water/soda bottles | Very low | Acetaldehyde (AA) at ppb | FDA & EU approved |
| **HDPE** | Milk jugs, caps | Very low | Minimal; antioxidant transfer | FDA & EU approved |
| **PP** | Yogurt cups, microwave containers | Low | Few additives; good heat resistance | FDA & EU approved |
| **LDPE** | Bread bags, cling film | Low | Slip agent (erucamide) migration | FDA & EU approved |
| **PS** | Yogurt cups, cutlery | **Moderate** (styrene monomer) | Styrene odor threshold ~50 ppb | FDA approved with restrictions |
| **PC** | Baby bottles (legacy), 5-gal water | **High** (BPA) | Bisphenol-A endocrine disruptor | **Restricted/Banned** in baby products in EU & India |
| **PVC** | Meat wrap, blister packs | Moderate-high | Plasticizer (phthalate) migration | Restricted in food contact (EU) |
| **PLA** | Compostable cups | Low | Lactic acid oligomer migration | EU approved; FDA conditional |
| **PMMA** | Decorative displays, lenses | Very low | MMA monomer residual | Approved for indirect contact |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Migration Pathway Flow Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **multi-stage migration pathway** from a plastic article into food, along with the analytical steps used to verify palatability.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plastic Article PET HDPE PP] --> B[Migration Sources]
    B --> B1[Residual Monomers]
    B --> B2[Oligomers Low MW]
    B --> B3[Additives Plasticizers Stabilizers]
    B --> B4[Processing Aids Contaminants]
    B1 --> C[Diffusion through Polymer Matrix]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> D[Partitioning at Polymer Food Interface]
    D --> E[Food Simulant or Real Food]
    E --> F{Migration Exceeds Limits?}
    F -->|No| G[PALATABLE APPROVED]
    F -->|Yes| H[NON-COMPLIANT REJECTED]
    G --> I[Sensory Panel Evaluation]
    I --> J{Off Taste or Odor Detected?}
    J -->|No| K[FINAL APPROVAL FOR MARKET]
    J -->|Yes| H
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Palatability Testing

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    end

    end

    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Decision Output]
        D1{Within OML 10 mg per dm2?}

        D3{Passes Sensory Threshold?}

    end
    S1 --> COND
    S2 --> COND
    COND --> TEST
    T1 --> D1
    T2 --> D2
    T3 --> D3
    T4 --> D1

    D2 --> RESULT
    D3 --> RESULT
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style Question — Set 1]**
>
> **Q1.** Define the term "palatable plastics" and list any **four key requirements** that a polymer must satisfy to be classified as palatable.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Palatable plastics are polymeric materials that are chemically inert, organoleptically neutral, toxicologically safe, and regulatory-compliant for applications involving direct contact with food, pharmaceuticals, or biological systems, as well as for decorative uses where user comfort is essential.

**Four Key Requirements (2 marks — ½ mark each):**

1. **Zero or negligible migration** of residual monomers, oligomers, and additives into the contacting medium (within OML ≤ 10 mg/dm²).
2. **No off-taste or off-odor** transfer — must score below the Recognition Threshold in sensory panels.
3. **Non-toxicity** — must comply with NOAEL-based safety factors and pose no carcinogenic, mutagenic, or endocrine-disrupting risk.
4. **Regulatory compliance** — approval under FDA 21 CFR, EU 1935/2004, FSSAI, or relevant pharmacopeia for intended use.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style Question — Set 2]**
>
> **Q2.** Distinguish between **Overall Migration Limit (OML)** and **Specific Migration Limit (SML)** with suitable examples.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | OML (Overall Migration Limit) | SML (Specific Migration Limit) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | The maximum total mass of all non-volatile substances that can migrate from the plastic into the food simulant under defined test conditions. | The maximum permissible amount of a *specific, identified* substance that can migrate into food. |
| **Value** | **10 mg/dm²** of contact surface (or **60 mg/kg** food) — EU standard. | Varies substance-by-substance; e.g., Vinyl Chloride SML = **0.01 mg/kg**; BPA SML = **0.05 mg/kg** (now banned). |
| **Scope** | Aggregate (all migrants summed). | Individual (per-substance). |
| **Test Method** | Gravimetric (evaporate simulant, weigh residue). | Analytical — typically GC-MS, HPLC, or LC-MS. |
| **Example** | Total residue from a PP yogurt cup into 3% acetic acid. | Migration of phthalate plasticizer from PVC into fatty food. |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Pattern: 14-Mark Question]**

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **mechanisms of migration** of additives and residual monomers from plastics into food. Discuss Fick's laws of diffusion and derive the expression for steady-state migration through a plastic wall. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A **0.5 mm thick HDPE film** contains an antioxidant (Irganox 1076) at a uniform concentration of **800 mg/kg** in the polymer. The diffusion coefficient of the antioxidant in HDPE at 25 °C is $D = 2 \times 10^{-13}$ m²/s. Calculate the **steady-state flux** and the **lag time** before steady migration begins. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

Migration occurs in **three sequential steps**:

**Step 1 — Diffusion within the polymer (1.5 marks):**
Migrant molecules (residual monomers, additives, oligomers) move through the amorphous regions of the polymer matrix driven by the concentration gradient. This is described by **Fick's Second Law**:

$$\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \cdot \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

For a thin plastic film/wall with one side contacting food (a perfect sink, $C = 0$) and the other side impermeable, the solution gives steady-state flux:

$$J_{ss} = \dfrac{D \cdot C_0}{L}$$

**Step 2 — Partitioning at the polymer–food interface (1.5 marks):**
At equilibrium, the migrant distributes between the polymer and food phases according to the **partition coefficient** $K_{P/F} = C_{\text{polymer}} / C_{\text{food}}$. For lipophilic additives contacting fatty foods, $K$ can be very high (preferential migration). For hydrophilic migrants in aqueous foods, $K$ is lower.

**Step 3 — Desorption into bulk food (1 mark):**
The migrant disperses into the food matrix by convection/diffusion. If food is stirred or has high surface renewal, it acts as an infinite sink ($C \approx 0$), maintaining the driving force.

**Fick's Laws Application (1.5 marks):**
- **Fick's First Law** (steady-state): $J = -D \dfrac{dC}{dx}$
- **Fick's Second Law** (unsteady-state): $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$

**Derivation (1.5 marks):**

At steady state in a slab of thickness $L$ with uniform initial concentration $C_0$, boundary conditions $C(0) = 0$ and $C(L) = C_0$:

$$\dfrac{dC}{dx} = \dfrac{C_0}{L}$$

$$J_{ss} = D \cdot \dfrac{C_0}{L}$$

The cumulative migrated mass at time $t$:

$$M_t = \dfrac{D C_0}{L} \cdot \left(t - \dfrac{L^2}{6D}\right) = J_{ss} \cdot (t - t_{lag})$$

where $t_{lag} = \dfrac{L^2}{6D}$.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- Thickness $L = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- Initial concentration $C_0 = 800 \text{ mg/kg} = 800 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/kg}$
- Diffusion coefficient $D = 2 \times 10^{-13} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- Polymer density of HDPE $\rho_{HDPE} \approx 950 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (assume)

**Step 1 — Convert $C_0$ to volumetric units (1.5 marks):**

$$C_0 \text{ [kg/m}^3] = 800 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/kg} \times 950 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 0.76 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Calculate steady-state flux (2 marks):**

$$J_{ss} = \dfrac{D \cdot C_0}{L} = \dfrac{(2 \times 10^{-13}) \cdot (0.76)}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
J_{ss} &= \dfrac{1.52 \times 10^{-13}}{5 \times 10^{-4}} \\[4pt]
&= 3.04 \times 10^{-10} \text{ kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)} \\[4pt]
&= 3.04 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)} \\[4pt]
&= 0.304 \text{ μg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate lag time (1.5 marks):**

$$t_{lag} = \dfrac{L^2}{6D} = \dfrac{(0.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}{6 \times (2 \times 10^{-13})}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{lag} &= \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{-7}}{1.2 \times 10^{-12}} \\[4pt]
&= 2.083 \times 10^{5} \text{ seconds} \\[4pt]
&\approx 58.0 \text{ hours} \approx 2.42 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
- **Steady-state flux:** $J_{ss} \approx 3.04 \times 10^{-10}$ kg/(m²·s) — extremely low, indicating good antioxidant retention by HDPE.
- **Lag time:** ~2.4 days — antioxidant migration begins measurably only after ~2.4 days of food contact at 25 °C, well within typical HDPE food-contact safety limits.

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **(a)** Discuss the **regulatory framework** governing food-contact plastics in the European Union (EU Regulation 1935/2004) and the United States (FDA 21 CFR). Compare the two systems and highlight any **four key differences**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Calculate the **Acceleration Factor (AF)** for migration testing when the test temperature is raised from **25 °C (298 K)** to **40 °C (313 K)**, given an activation energy of diffusion $E_a = 90$ kJ/mol. Comment on the practical significance of this factor for shelf-life prediction. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**EU Regulation 1935/2004 (2.5 marks):**
- **Scope:** All materials and articles intended to come into contact with food.
- **Key Principle:** Article 3 — "Materials and articles shall be manufactured in compliance with good manufacturing practice so that, under normal or foreseeable conditions of use, they do not transfer their constituents to food in quantities which could endanger human health, change food composition unacceptably, or deteriorate organoleptic properties."
- **OML:** 10 mg/dm² or 60 mg/kg food.
- **SML:** Listed in positive lists (Annex I, II).
- **Declaration of Conformity (DoC):** Mandatory.
- **Specific Measures:** Plastic Regulation (EU) 10/2011, Ceramic Directive 84/500/EEC.

**FDA 21 CFR (2.5 marks):**
- **Scope:** Indirect food additives regulated under 21 CFR Parts 170–199.
- **Key Principle:** Premarket approval via **Food Contact Substance (FCS) Notification** (since 1997) — manufacturer submits safety data, FDA issues clearance letter.
- **OML:** Not a single OML — instead, extractives limits per polymer (e.g., 21 CFR 175.300 for coatings).
- **Positive List:** 21 CFR 174–179 lists permitted substances.
- **Threshold of Regulation (TOR):** Substances with dietary concentration ≤ 0.5 ppb are exempt.

**Four Key Differences (2 marks — ½ mark each):**

| # | Aspect | EU System | FDA System |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | OML | Fixed universal limit (10 mg/dm²) | No single OML; substance-specific |
| 2 | Premarket clearance | Self-declaration with DoC | Mandatory FCS notification for new substances |
| 3 | Positive list | Detailed, regularly updated (e.g., Reg. 10/2011) | More permissive; uses TOR exemptions |
| 4 | Labeling | Mandatory traceability logo (glass & fork symbol) | Less prescriptive; ingredient-based labeling |

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given (0.5 mark):**
- $T_1 = 298$ K, $T_2 = 313$ K, $E_a = 90$ kJ/mol = 90,000 J/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$AF = \exp\left[\dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$$

**Step-by-Step Calculation (4 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2} &= \dfrac{1}{298} - \dfrac{1}{313} \\[4pt]
&= 0.003356 - 0.003195 \\[4pt]
&= 0.0001610 \text{ K}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{E_a}{R} &= \dfrac{90{,}000}{8.314} = 10{,}824.6 \text{ K}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Exponent} &= 10{,}824.6 \times 0.0001610 = 1.743
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
AF &= \exp(1.743) \approx 5.71
\end{aligned}$$

**Practical Significance (1.5 marks):**
- Storage at **40 °C for 10 days** simulates approximately **57 days (~ 2 months)** of real-time storage at 25 °C.
- This allows manufacturers to **accelerate shelf-life testing** — instead of waiting months for a 6-month or 12-month shelf-life confirmation, the equivalent migration profile can be obtained in 2–3 weeks.
- **Caution:** $AF$ assumes Arrhenius behavior holds over the temperature range. For polymers near their $T_g$ (glass transition), deviations occur.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Palatable Plastics Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency in Migration Problems** — Students frequently mix mg/dm² with mg/kg food in the same calculation. Remember: **10 mg/dm² = 60 mg/kg food** (using the standard assumption of 6 dm² contact per 1 kg food).
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Convert Thickness to Meters** — In Fick's law calculations, $L$ MUST be in meters and $D$ in m²/s. Mixing cm and mm is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Confusing OML with SML** — OML is *aggregate*; SML is *per-substance*. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
>
> 4. **Skipping the Lag Time** — When asked for migration calculations, students often compute only $J_{ss}$ and forget the lag time $t_{lag} = L^2 / (6D)$. This is a **2-mark trap**.
>
> 5. **Wrong Arrhenius Sign** — In the AF equation, the term $\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$ is **positive** when $T_2 > T_1$. Students sometimes invert it, getting $AF < 1$ (physically absurd).
>
> 6. **Ignoring Partition Coefficient $K_{P/F}$** — Migration is not just diffusion; partitioning at the polymer-food interface can amplify or attenuate migration. Mention $K$ in essays for **full marks**.
>
> 7. **Writing "BPA is safe in PC"** — PC baby bottles are **banned/restricted** in EU, India, and several US states. Stating PC is "palatable" without this caveat will lose marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Palatable Plastics**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ Palatable plastic = organoleptically neutral + chemically inert + toxicologically safe + regulatory compliant.
- ☐ Migration is driven by **concentration gradient + diffusion + partitioning**.
- ☐ Three migration sources: **monomers, oligomers, additives**.
- ☐ Four palatability pillars: chemical safety, sensory neutrality, biological safety, aesthetic appeal.

### Key Numerical Limits
- ☐ **OML = 10 mg/dm²** (or 60 mg/kg food) — EU.
- ☐ **SML** is substance-specific (e.g., VC = 0.01 mg/kg).
- ☐ **Safety Factor = 100** (food), 1000 (carcinogens).
- ☐ **Recognition Threshold** for taints: low ppb range.

### Must-Know Formulas
- ☐ Fick's First Law: $J = -D \cdot \dfrac{dC}{dx}$
- ☐ Steady-state flux: $J_{ss} = \dfrac{D \cdot C_0}{L}$
- ☐ Lag time: $t_{lag} = \dfrac{L^2}{6D}$
- ☐ Arrhenius: $D = D_0 \cdot \exp(-E_a / RT)$
- ☐ Acceleration Factor: $AF = \exp\left[\dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$

### Plastic-Specific Knowledge
- ☐ **PET** — best for water/soda; AA migration concern.
- ☐ **HDPE** — low migration; milk jugs.
- ☐ **PP** — microwave-safe; hot-fill applications.
- ☐ **PS** — styrene odor risk at high temps.
- ☐ **PC** — BPA issue; **avoided** for baby products.
- ☐ **PVC** — phthalate plasticizer migration; restricted.
- ☐ **PLA** — bioplastic, EU-approved, good palatability.

### Regulatory Snapshot
- ☐ **EU 1935/2004** — umbrella law; **10/2011** — plastic-specific.
- ☐ **FDA 21 CFR 170–199** — FCS Notification system.
- ☐ **FSSAI** (India) — IS 14625 standards for plastic packaging.
- ☐ **USP Class VI / ISO 10993** — biocompatibility for medical.

### Quick-Revision Mnemonic
> **"PSAP"** — Palatable plastics follow the **P**urity + **S**ensory + **A**esthetic + **P**rotection framework.

---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the discipline of **Plastics & Decoration**, **Design Factors** constitute the systematic set of *engineering, material, geometric, functional, aesthetic, and economic parameters* that must be concurrently evaluated during the conceptualization and engineering of a decorated plastic article. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme learning framework for Module 2, these factors govern:

- The *selection of the decoration process* (printing, coating, hot-stamping, in-mold decoration, metallization, laser marking, etc.)
- The *substrate-process compatibility matrix*
- The *adhesion permanence* of the decorative layer
- The *visual fidelity and functional durability* of the finished article

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:**
> *Design factors are the multidisciplinary decision variables — substrate, geometry, surface state, functional demand, aesthetics, production volume, and cost — whose joint optimization yields a manufacturable, durable, and commercially viable decorated plastic component.*

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **painting a wall mural in a house**. Before lifting the brush you instinctively ask:
- *What is the wall made of?* (Plaster, plywood, glass — i.e., **substrate**)
- *Is the wall flat, curved, or has corners?* (i.e., **geometry/shape**)
- *Is it indoors or outdoors?* (i.e., **end-use environment**)
- *How long should the painting last?* (i.e., **durability**)
- *How many walls need identical art?* (i.e., **production volume**)
- *What is the budget?* (i.e., **cost**)

The exact same *mental checklist* is what a Plastics & Decoration engineer formalizes under the heading **"Design Factors."** Every one of these instinctive questions maps to a controllable engineering variable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Design Factors Matter:**
> A decoration process is **never selected in isolation**. The choice is dictated by the intersection of the polymer substrate, the article's geometry, the functional demand, and economic constraints. Ignoring any one factor causes failures such as poor ink adhesion, ink bleeding, ghosting, plate-out, or premature abrasion.

### 1.3 Canonical Categories of Design Factors (KTU Mapping)

The KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus consolidates design factors into **seven canonical pillars**:

| # | Pillar | Engineering Concern |
|---|--------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Material (Substrate) Factors** | Polymer type, surface energy, additives |
| 2 | **Geometric (Shape & Size) Factors** | Flatness, curvature, recesses |
| 3 | **Surface State Factors** | Roughness, contamination, pre-treatment |
| 4 | **Functional / Performance Factors** | Abrasion, chemical, UV resistance |
| 5 | **Aesthetic / Optical Factors** | Color, gloss, resolution |
| 6 | **Production / Economic Factors** | Volume, cycle time, tooling cost |
| 7 | **Tolerance & Registration Factors** | Shrinkage, dimensional accuracy |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pillar 1 — Material (Substrate) Factors

The *intrinsic chemistry* of the polymer dictates whether ink/coating will wet, adhere, and survive service.

**Key determinants:**

- **Polymer class** — Thermoplastics (PE, PP, PET, PVC, PS, ABS, PC, PA) vs. Thermosets (UF, MF, EP, UP).
- **Surface energy (γ)** — Must exceed the surface tension of the decorating fluid. *Polyolefins* (PE ≈ 31 mN/m; PP ≈ 30 mN/m) are *low-energy* and notoriously difficult to wet without pre-treatment.
- **Crystallinity** — Higher crystallinity → lower surface free energy → reduced wettability.
- **Mobility of additives** — Slip agents (erucamide, oleamide), antistats, plasticizers, and internal lubricants *bloom* to the surface and *degrade* adhesion.
- **Moisture content** — Critical for PA (nylon), which absorbs 0.5–9 % water.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Fact:** *PE and PP require surface pre-treatment (corona, flame, plasma) to raise surface energy above 38–42 mN/m before any decorating step.*

### 2.2 Pillar 2 — Geometric (Shape & Size) Factors

Geometry is the *silent veto* in decoration planning.

- **Flat panels** — Tolerate screen, pad, offset, digital printing.
- **Cylindrical / conical** — Suited to pad-transfer, wraparound screen, roller coating.
- **Compound curves / 3D free-form** — Need *pad printing*, *in-mold decoration (IMD)*, or *heat-transfer*.
- **Recesses, blind holes, undercuts** — Inaccessible to most direct-contact processes.
- **Sharp edges** — Cause ink-film thinning and edge-pull (telegraphing).

### 2.3 Pillar 3 — Surface State Factors

Defined by the *micro-topography* and *chemistry* of the topmost 1–10 molecular layers.

- **Roughness (Ra)** — Measured in µm; influences wetting via the Wenzel and Cassie-Baxter models.
- **Cleanliness** — Mold-release agents, fingerprints, dust, oils.
- **Pre-treatment level** — *Dyne level* (mN/m) measured using dyne-test inks (ASTM D2578).

### 2.4 Pillar 4 — Functional / Performance Factors

The *service contract* the decoration must honour:

- **Adhesion** (cross-hatch, ASTM D3359; pull-off, ASTM D4541).
- **Abrasion resistance** (Taber Abraser, ASTM D4060).
- **Chemical resistance** (MEK double-rub, ASTM D5402).
- **UV/weatherability** (QUV, Xenon arc — ASTM G154, G155).
- **Thermal cycling** — Coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) mismatch between substrate and ink.
- **Food-contact / FDA / EU 10/2011 compliance** — restricts pigment, plasticizer, and monomer choices.

### 2.5 Pillar 5 — Aesthetic / Optical Factors

- **Gloss** at 60° (ASTM D523) — categorized as Matte (≤10 GU), Satin (10–70 GU), High-gloss (≥70 GU).
- **Color matching** — ΔE (CIELab) tolerances (typically ΔE ≤ 1.0 for brand-critical; ΔE ≤ 3.0 for general).
- **Resolution** — Lines per mm (lp/mm) or dots per inch (dpi).
- **Opacity / transparency** — Influenced by pigment loading and film thickness.

### 2.6 Pillar 6 — Production / Economic Factors

- **Production volume**:
  - Low (< 10 000) → Digital printing, pad printing
  - Medium (10 000 – 500 000) → Screen, pad, hot-stamp
  - High (> 500 000) → In-mold, roller, IMD, heat-transfer
- **Tooling cost amortisation** — Gravure cylinders, screen frames, cliché plates.
- **Setup time** — Affects unit cost for short runs.

### 2.7 Pillar 7 — Tolerance & Registration Factors

- **Mould shrinkage compensation** — built into the artwork.
- **Multi-colour registration tolerance** — typically ±0.1 mm for high-precision work.
- **Post-moulding warpage** of crystalline polymers.

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Cheat-Sheet — All board-relevant equations in one place.** Critical: notice the use of `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve Markdown table integrity.

| Equation | Symbol Key | Units | Engineering Application |
|----------|-----------|-------|-------------------------|
| $\gamma_{SV} \;=\; \gamma_{SL} \;+\; \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ | $\gamma_{SV}$: solid–vapour interfacial tension; $\gamma_{SL}$: solid–liquid; $\gamma_{LV}$: liquid–vapour; $\theta$: contact angle | mN/m | Young's equation — *fundamental wetting criterion* |
| $W_A \;=\; \gamma_{SV} \;+\; \gamma_{LV} \;-\; \gamma_{SL}$ | $W_A$: work of adhesion | mJ/m² | Dupré equation — *energy needed to separate liquid–solid interface* |
| $W_A \;=\; \gamma_{LV}\,(1 + \cos\theta)$ | — | mJ/m² | Young–Dupré combined form — *board-favourite* |
| $S \;=\; \gamma_{SV} \;-\; (\gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV})$ | $S$: spreading coefficient | mN/m | If $S > 0$, liquid spreads spontaneously |
| $r \;\approx\; \dfrac{\cos\theta_{obs}}{\cos\theta_{true}}$ | $r$: roughness ratio (Wenzel) | dimensionless | Wenzel correction — *rough surfaces enhance wetting if $\theta < 90°$* |
| $\Delta E \;=\; \sqrt{(\Delta L^{*})^{2} + (\Delta a^{*})^{2} + (\Delta b^{*})^{2}}$ | $L^{*}, a^{*}, b^{*}$: CIELab coordinates | dimensionless | Color-difference metric — *aesthetic tolerance check* |
| $T_g \;\uparrow\;\Rightarrow\;\text{adhesion}\;(\text{of solvent inks})\;\uparrow$ | $T_g$: glass-transition temperature | °C | Substrate temperature rule — *drying/curing logic* |
| $\sigma_{CTE} \;=\; \lvert\alpha_{s} - \alpha_{i}\rvert \cdot \Delta T \cdot E_{i}$ | $\alpha$: CTE; $E$: elastic modulus; $\Delta T$: thermal swing | MPa | Thermal-mismatch stress — *predicts coating cracking* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These eight equations are the *evaluation engine* of any decoration specification sheet. A KTU examiner expects the candidate to *cite Young's equation by name*, *compute the work of adhesion numerically*, and *interpret ΔE values in color-tolerance decisions*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Young–Dupré Equation (Work of Adhesion)

This is the *single most-asked derivation* on KTU plastics-decor papers. Every step must be written out explicitly; the examiner awards a partial mark for each logical transition.

#### Step 1: Statement of Young's Equation (force balance at a sessile drop)

When a liquid droplet sits on a solid substrate in a vapour atmosphere, three interfacial tensions act on the contact line.

$$
\gamma_{SV} \;=\; \gamma_{SL} \;+\; \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta
$$

where $\theta$ is the equilibrium contact angle measured *inside the liquid*.

#### Step 2: Free-Energy Approach to the Dupré Equation

The **work of adhesion** $W_A$ is defined as the reversible free energy required to separate a unit area of the solid–liquid interface, creating one unit each of solid–vapour and liquid–vapour interfaces:

$$
W_A \;=\; \gamma_{SV} \;+\; \gamma_{LV} \;-\; \gamma_{SL}
$$

#### Step 3: Substitute Young's Expression for $\gamma_{SV}$

Rearrange Young's equation to isolate $\gamma_{SV}$:

$$
\gamma_{SV} \;=\; \gamma_{SL} \;+\; \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta
$$

Substitute this into the Dupré expression:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_A \;&=\; \bigl(\gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta\bigr) \;+\; \gamma_{LV} \;-\; \gamma_{SL} \\
W_A \;&=\; \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta \;+\; \gamma_{LV} \\
W_A \;&=\; \gamma_{LV}\,\bigl(1 + \cos\theta\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4: Physical Interpretation (board-style answer)

- If $\theta \to 0°$ (complete wetting): $W_A \to 2\gamma_{LV}$ (maximum adhesion).
- If $\theta = 90°$: $W_A = \gamma_{LV}$ (intermediate).
- If $\theta \to 180°$ (no wetting): $W_A \to 0$ (zero adhesion).

#### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-typical)

A solvent-based ink has $\gamma_{LV} = 38$ mN/m. After corona treatment, the contact angle on a PP substrate is measured as $\theta = 28°$.

Compute the work of adhesion.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_A \;&=\; 38 \times \bigl(1 + \cos 28°\bigr)\,\text{mN/m} \\
\cos 28° \;&\approx\; 0.8829 \\
W_A \;&=\; 38 \times (1 + 0.8829) \\
W_A \;&=\; 38 \times 1.8829 \\
W_A \;&\approx\; 71.55\,\text{mN/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A work of adhesion of ≈ 71.55 mN/m is *commercially adequate* for general-purpose printing on PP.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Spreading Coefficient ($S$)

The spreading coefficient predicts whether a liquid *will* spread spontaneously on a solid or remain as a discrete droplet.

$$
S \;=\; \gamma_{SV} \;-\; (\gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV})
$$

Using Young's equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S \;&=\; \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta \;-\; (\gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}) \\
S \;&=\; \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta \;-\; \gamma_{LV} \\
S \;&=\; \gamma_{LV}\,(\cos\theta - 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

- $S > 0$: liquid spreads (impossible here since $\cos\theta \leq 1$ ⇒ $S \leq 0$ on a perfectly smooth surface).
- $S < 0$: partial wetting — droplet equilibrium angle $\theta > 0°$.
- $S = 0$: complete wetting — $\theta = 0°$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Wenzel Roughness Correction

A rough surface modifies the apparent contact angle. If $r$ = ratio of true surface area to projected area ( $r \geq 1$ ), Wenzel showed:

$$
\cos\theta_{\text{obs}} \;=\; r \cdot \cos\theta_{\text{true}}
$$

#### Numerical Illustration

A polymer with $\theta_{\text{true}} = 60°$ is micro-roughened so that $r = 1.4$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos\theta_{\text{obs}} \;&=\; 1.4 \times \cos 60° \\
\cos\theta_{\text{obs}} \;&=\; 1.4 \times 0.5 \;=\; 0.7 \\
\theta_{\text{obs}} \;&=\; \arccos(0.7) \;\approx\; 45.57°
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering reading:** Because $\theta_{\text{true}} < 90°$, roughness *improves* wetting — the apparent angle is *smaller*. This is exploited when moulding fine textures on decorative panels to enhance ink hold-out and visual depth.

---

### 3.4 Derivation IV — Color-Difference ($\Delta E$) in CIELab Space

Given two colour samples with coordinates $(L^{*}_{1}, a^{*}_{1}, b^{*}_{1})$ and $(L^{*}_{2}, a^{*}_{2}, b^{*}_{2})$:

$$
\Delta E \;=\; \sqrt{(L^{*}_{1} - L^{*}_{2})^{2} \;+\; (a^{*}_{1} - a^{*}_{2})^{2} \;+\; (b^{*}_{1} - b^{*}_{2})^{2}}
$$

#### Worked Example

A brand specifies target colour $L^{*}=48, a^{*}=22, b^{*}=-31$. A printed batch is measured as $L^{*}=49, a^{*}=21, b^{*}=-30$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta L^{*} \;&=\; 49 - 48 \;=\; 1 \\
\Delta a^{*} \;&=\; 21 - 22 \;=\; -1 \\
\Delta b^{*} \;&=\; -30 - (-31) \;=\; 1 \\
\Delta E \;&=\; \sqrt{1^{2} + (-1)^{2} + 1^{2}} \;=\; \sqrt{3} \;\approx\; 1.732
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verdict:** $\Delta E \approx 1.73$ is *within* a brand-critical tolerance of $\Delta E \leq 2.0$ — *batch approved*.

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Process vs. Design Factor

| Design Factor | Pad Print | Screen | Digital | Hot-Stamp | In-Mold | Roller Coat |
|---------------|:---------:|:------:|:-------:|:---------:|:-------:|:-----------:|
| Flat panel | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ |
| Compound curve | ✓✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✗ |
| Recesses | ✓✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ |
| High resolution | ✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✓ |
| High volume (> 10⁵) | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ |
| Low setup cost | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| Multi-colour | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ |

> Legend: ✓✓ = Excellent, ✓ = Adequate, ✗ = Unsuitable.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Map of Design Factors

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the *decision tree* an engineer traverses when specifying a decorated plastic part.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Decorated Plastic Part] --> B[Material Substrate Factor]
    B --> B1[Polymer Type]
    B --> B2[Surface Energy]
    B --> B3[Additives Bloom]

    A --> C[Geometry Shape Factor]
    C --> C1[Flat Panel]
    C --> C2[Cylindrical]
    C --> C3[3D Compound Curve]
    C --> C4[Recesses Undercuts]

    A --> D[Surface State Factor]
    D --> D1[Roughness Ra]
    D --> D2[Cleanliness]
    D --> D3[Dyne Level]

    A --> E[Functional Factor]
    E --> E1[Abrasion Resistance]
    E --> E2[Chemical Resistance]
    E --> E3[UV Weathering]
    E --> E4[FDA Food Contact]

    A --> F[Aesthetic Factor]
    F --> F1[Gloss Level]
    F --> F2[Color Delta E]
    F --> F3[Resolution dpi]

    A --> G[Production Economic Factor]
    G --> G1[Volume]
    G --> G2[Tooling Cost]
    G --> G3[Cycle Time]

    A --> H[Tolerance Registration Factor]
    H --> H1[Shrinkage Comp]
    H --> H2[Multi color Register]
    H --> H3[Warpage]

    B1 --> Z[Decision: Choose Decoration Process]
    C1 --> Z
    D1 --> Z
    E1 --> Z
    F1 --> Z
    G1 --> Z
    H1 --> Z

    Z --> P1[Pad Printing]
    Z --> P2[Screen Printing]
    Z --> P3[Digital UV]
    Z --> P4[Hot Stamping]
    Z --> P5[In Mold Decoration]
    Z --> P6[Roller Coating]
    Z --> P7[Metallization]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Surface Pre-Treatment Subsystem

Because physical drawings of corona/flame/plasma heads are unsuitable in plain-text Mermaid, the following **block-architecture diagram** depicts the *internal flow* of a typical pre-treatment station that engineering students must understand conceptually.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Incoming Plastic Part] --> nodeB[Cleaning Stage]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Degreaser Wash]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Deionized Rinse]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Drying Tunnel]

    nodeE --> nodeF[Pre Treatment Selection]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Corona Discharge Unit]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Flame Treatment Burner]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Plasma Chamber]

    nodeG --> nodeJ[Dyne Test Station]
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ

    nodeJ --> nodeK{Quality Check pass}
    nodeK -- YES --> nodeL[Decoration Process]
    nodeK -- NO --> nodeM[Re treat and Recycle]
    nodeM --> nodeF

    nodeL --> nodeN[Curing Drying]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Final Inspection]
    nodeO --> nodeP[Finished Decorated Article]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Every decision-point arrow corresponds to a *design-factor variable*. For instance, if *nodeJ* reports a dyne level below 38 mN/m for PE, the article is *rejected and re-circulated* to the pre-treatment loop. This visualises the closed-loop quality philosophy that the KTU 2024 OBE framework expects students to articulate.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
Define *Design Factors* in the context of plastics decoration. List any **four** major categories.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Design factors are the multidisciplinary engineering parameters that govern the selection of a decoration process for a plastic component and ensure its durability, aesthetics, and manufacturability. Four major categories are: (i) **Material/substrate factors** — polymer type, surface energy; (ii) **Geometric factors** — shape, curvature, recesses; (iii) **Surface-state factors** — roughness, dyne level, contamination; (iv) **Functional factors** — abrasion, chemical, and UV resistance. *(Examiner's note: any four correct categories with one-line justification earn full marks.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
Why do polyethylene and polypropylene require *surface pre-treatment* before printing? Mention the minimum dyne level recommended.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
PE and PP are *polyolefins* with inherently low surface energy (≈ 30–31 mN/m) and a non-polar, crystalline surface. Most solvent- and water-based inks have surface tensions of 35–45 mN/m — *higher* than the substrate — leading to poor wetting and high contact angles (> 90°). Adhesion fails. Therefore the surface energy is raised to a **minimum of 38–42 mN/m** by corona, flame, or plasma treatment before printing. *(Cite Young's equation and one numerical dyne value to gain the third mark.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — `(a) 7 + (b) 7`

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified per 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** State **Young's equation** and the **Young–Dupré equation** for work of adhesion. A solvent ink has surface tension $\gamma_{LV} = 42$ mN/m and forms a contact angle $\theta = 35°$ on a corona-treated polypropylene substrate. Calculate the work of adhesion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a flow diagram the **seven categories of design factors** that influence plastics decoration. How does each factor limit process selection? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Step-by-step:

$$
\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta \quad\text{(Young)}
$$

$$
W_A = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta) \quad\text{(Young–Dupré)}
$$

Substitution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_A &= 42 \times \bigl(1 + \cos 35°\bigr) \\
\cos 35° &\approx 0.8192 \\
W_A &= 42 \times (1 + 0.8192) \\
W_A &= 42 \times 1.8192 \\
W_A &\approx 76.41 \,\text{mN/m} \;(\text{or mJ/m}^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** $W_A \approx 76.4$ mJ/m² is *high*, confirming that corona treatment has raised adhesion to commercially viable levels for printing on PP.

**(b)** The seven categories are:
1. **Material substrate factors** — polymer type, surface energy, additives.
2. **Geometric factors** — flatness, curvature, recesses, edges.
3. **Surface-state factors** — roughness, dyne level, cleanliness.
4. **Functional factors** — abrasion, chemical, UV resistance.
5. **Aesthetic factors** — gloss, $\Delta E$ colour tolerance, resolution.
6. **Production economic factors** — volume, tooling, cycle time.
7. **Tolerance registration factors** — shrinkage, register, warpage.

*Process-limitation linkage (board expects cross-relations):*

- Substrate low energy ⇒ *excludes* direct digital aqueous inks.
- Compound curvature ⇒ *favours* pad printing or IMD; *excludes* flat-screen.
- High volume (> 5 × 10⁵) ⇒ *excludes* digital inkjet.
- $\Delta E < 1$ specification ⇒ *favours* gravure or in-mold; *risky* in pad printing.
- Recesses present ⇒ *excludes* roller coating.
- Tight registration ± 0.1 mm ⇒ *favours* pre-printed in-mold film, *risks* free-form IMD.

*(7 marks — distribute as: 3 for listing categories with brief descriptions + 4 for cross-mapping to processes.)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** A moulded ABS panel is measured to have a true contact angle of 62° with a UV-curable ink. The micro-roughness ratio $r = 1.35$. Using the **Wenzel equation**, determine the *apparent* contact angle. Discuss whether this roughness *helps* or *hurts* wetting. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the significance of **gloss, ΔE colour tolerance, and resolution (dpi)** as aesthetic design factors. Provide recommended numerical ranges for high-quality consumer products. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Wenzel equation:

$$
\cos\theta_{\text{obs}} = r \cdot \cos\theta_{\text{true}}
$$

Substitution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos\theta_{\text{obs}} &= 1.35 \times \cos 62° \\
\cos 62° &\approx 0.4695 \\
\cos\theta_{\text{obs}} &= 1.35 \times 0.4695 \;\approx\; 0.6338 \\
\theta_{\text{obs}} &= \arccos(0.6338) \;\approx\; 50.65°
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation (2 marks):** Since $\theta_{\text{true}} < 90°$, Wenzel's correction *reduces* the apparent contact angle (62° → 50.65°). The roughness **enhances wetting** — *helps* the decoration. Designers sometimes *intentionally texture* mould cavities for this reason. However, if $\theta_{\text{true}}$ were > 90°, roughness would *hurt* wetting (super-hydrophobic regime — Cassie–Baxter model).

**(b)** Aesthetic factor specifications:

- **Gloss (60°):** High-gloss decorative trim ≥ 80 GU; satin 30–60 GU; matte ≤ 10 GU.
- **ΔE Tolerance:** Brand-critical (logos, automotive) ≤ 1.0; consumer-electronics housings ≤ 2.0; general merchandise ≤ 3.0.
- **Resolution (dpi):** Graphic overlay artwork ≥ 300 dpi for photographic; ≥ 600 dpi for fine text; industrial decorative graphics ≥ 150 dpi.

*(Allocate marks as: 2 for gloss explanation + 2 for ΔE numerical ranges + 2 for dpi ranges + 1 for overall inter-relation.)*

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU candidates typically lose marks in 'Design Factors' questions:**
> 1. **Confusing surface energy with surface tension** — they are dimensionally identical but conceptually distinct; surface energy belongs to the *solid*, surface tension to the *liquid*. Examiners deduct ½–1 mark for this slip.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers. Work of adhesion is reported in **mJ/m²**, not Newtons. Failing to state units is a guaranteed ½-mark penalty.
> 3. **Omitting the interpretation line** after a numerical calculation. The examiner expects *"the value is acceptable / unacceptable for …"* — silence here loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Mislabelling ΔE tolerance** — many candidates write ΔE ≤ 5 for *brand-critical*. The KTU standard answer key lists ΔE ≤ 1 for brand-critical.
> 5. **Ignoring additive bloom** — when discussing adhesion failure on PE/PP, do not forget to mention *erucamide slip-agent migration*; it is a frequent 1-mark differentiator.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Design factors = 7 pillars:** Material · Geometry · Surface-state · Functional · Aesthetic · Production · Tolerance.
- **PE & PP are low-energy polymers** → always pre-treat to **≥ 38–42 mN/m** (dyne test).
- **Young's equation:** $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$.
- **Young–Dupré work of adhesion:** $W_A = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$ — must state **units mJ/m²**.
- **Wenzel correction:** $\cos\theta_{\text{obs}} = r \cos\theta_{\text{true}}$ — roughness *helps* wetting only when $\theta_{\text{true}} < 90°$.
- **Spreading coefficient:** $S = \gamma_{LV}(\cos\theta - 1) \leq 0$ on ideal smooth solids.
- **ΔE colour tolerance:** Brand-critical ≤ 1.0; Consumer ≤ 2.0; General ≤ 3.0.
- **Gloss at 60°:** Matte ≤ 10 GU; Satin 10–70 GU; High-gloss ≥ 70 GU.
- **Resolution:** ≥ 300 dpi (photo); ≥ 600 dpi (fine text).
- **Process-to-factor map:** Flat → screen/digital; Compound curve → pad/IMD; Recesses → pad/IMD only; High-volume → in-mold/roller.
- **Adhesion test standards:** ASTM D3359 (cross-hatch); ASTM D4541 (pull-off).
- **Wear test standards:** ASTM D4060 (Taber); ASTM D5402 (MEK double-rub).
- **Weathering standards:** ASTM G154 (QUV); ASTM G155 (Xenon).
- **Always remember additive bloom** (erucamide in PE/PP) when explaining adhesion failures.
- **Coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) mismatch** is the root cause of coating cracking during thermal cycling.
- **In-mold decoration (IMD)** is preferred for high-volume, multi-colour, durable parts (e.g., automotive dashboards).
- **Pad printing** is the universal choice for *3D compound curves and recesses*.
- **Digital UV printing** dominates short-run, on-demand, personalised decoration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Thermal Spray Coating (TSC)** is a generic, blanket terminology in surface engineering that describes a family of coating processes in which a metallic, ceramic, polymeric, or composite material in the form of powder, wire, or rod is heated to a **molten or semi-molten state** (or accelerated as a solid in cold spray) and propelled onto a prepared substrate surface, where the impacting particles flatten, rapidly solidify, and build up a **splat-layered, lamellar coating** through mechanical interlocking and (where applicable) metallurgical bonding.

When the substrate is a **polymer (plastic)**, the process is termed **Thermal Spray Coating on Plastics (TSCP)**. It is a non-line-of-sight, additive coating technique governed by ISO 14923 and ASTM C633 standards, and is increasingly used for **functional (EMI shielding, wear, thermal management) and decorative (metallic finish, textures) applications** on engineering plastics such as ABS, PC, PA6, PEEK, PPS, and glass-fibre reinforced composites.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight (Decorative Plastics Module Focus):**
> TSCP is a *hybrid finishing process* — it merges **materials engineering (metallurgy + polymer science)** with **aesthetic design (surface decoration)**. The KTU examiner expects you to treat it as a finishing route alternative to vacuum metallisation, electroplating on plated plastics, and PVD.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are decorating a **thermocol (expanded polystyrene) ball** with a thin metallic shell. You cannot dip it in molten metal (it will melt) and you cannot electroplate it directly (it is non-conductive). So instead, you take a **flame-thrower that sprays finely powdered, half-melted tin particles at very high speed** at the ball. The tin particles are so fast that they *stick* to the soft thermocol **before the heat has time to melt it** — like a hail of hot micro-droplets freezing onto a cold surface on contact.

That is, in essence, **thermal spray on plastics**: we throw hot (or fast-moving) material at a thermally sensitive substrate, relying on **brief contact time + rapid solidification** rather than bulk heating, so the plastic underneath survives intact.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (Why it works on plastics):**
> Heat transfer during thermal spray is dominated by **particle kinetic energy and short contact dwell time (microseconds)**, not by sustained bulk conduction. The plastic surface receives enough energy to *bond* the splat, but not enough to *degrade* the substrate. This is why **Cold Spray** (solid-state, no melting) is the most plastic-friendly variant.

## 1.3 Core Terminology You Must Know for KTU Exams

| Term | Definition | Why it Matters |
|------|------------|----------------|
| **Splat** | The flattened, disc-shaped particle formed when a molten/semi-molten droplet impacts the substrate. It is the **basic building unit** of any TSC. | Coating microstructure is a stack of splats. |
| **Lamellar Structure** | The layered, "onion-skin" microstructure of a thermal spray coating formed by overlapping splats. | Affects porosity, adhesion, anisotropy. |
| **Bond Coat** | A first metallic layer (e.g., Ni-Al, Ni-Cr) sprayed before the top coat to improve adhesion and reduce CTE mismatch. | Mandatory for plastic substrates. |
| **CTE Mismatch** | The difference in Coefficient of Thermal Expansion between plastic (high, ~ 50–100 × 10⁻⁶/°C) and ceramic/metal (low, ~ 5–12 × 10⁻⁶/°C). | Drives residual stress and delamination. |
| **Adhesion Strength** | Measured in **MPa** via ASTM C633 pull-off test. Typical target on plastics: **15–35 MPa**. | Key quality metric in KTU problems. |
| **Deposition Efficiency (DE)** | Ratio of mass deposited on substrate to mass fed, expressed as a **%**. | Process economics indicator. |
| **Standoff Distance** | Distance from spray gun nozzle to substrate, typically **100–300 mm** for plastic substrates. | Critical for preventing overheating. |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown: The Six-Stage Mechanism

Every thermal spray process, regardless of heat source, follows a **six-stage cycle** that the KTU examiner loves to test. Memorise this framework:

1. **Material Feed & Injection** — Powder (typically 10–100 µm) or wire is fed into the heat zone via carrier gas (Ar, N₂, He).
2. **Heat Source Activation** — Energy source (flame, arc, plasma, detonation, kinetic gas) raises particle temperature to **melting point (Tm)** or above.
3. **Particle Acceleration & Melting** — Propellant gas accelerates the particle to **200–1200 m/s**. Particle reaches **Tm** and gains kinetic energy (½mv²).
4. **In-Flight Transport** — Particle travels through atmosphere; possible **oxidation** (a defect in metallic coatings on plastics).
5. **Impact & Flattening** — On impact (dwell time ≈ 1–10 µs), the droplet flattens into a splat of thickness ≈ 0.5–5 µm and width ≈ 5–10× its diameter. This is called the **"splat formation"** stage.
6. **Rapid Solidification & Layer Build-up** — Cooling rate ≈ **10⁶–10⁸ K/s**. The next layer deposits on top, building a **lamellar, anisotropic coating** with interlamellar porosity (typically 1–10 %).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Plastic Substrates Need Special Care:**
> Stages 3, 5, and 6 involve heat. Plastics have low Tg (glass transition, typically 80–150 °C) and low Tm (semi-crystalline plastics 200–350 °C). If the **interfacial temperature** at the splat-substrate interface exceeds Tg, the plastic softens, warps, or outgases, killing adhesion.

## 2.2 Classification of Thermal Spray Methods (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Process | Heat Source / Mechanism | Particle Temp. (°C) | Particle Velocity (m/s) | Suitability for Plastics | Key Use |
|---------|--------------------------|---------------------|--------------------------|---------------------------|---------|
| **Flame Spray (FS)** | Oxy-fuel combustion (acetylene, propane, hydrogen) | 2500–3000 | 30–100 | ⚠️ Low (use only with care, large bond coat) | Thick metal coatings, low-melt alloys |
| **Wire Arc Spray (WAS)** | Electric arc melts two consumable wires | 4000–6000 | 100–300 | ⚠️ Low-Medium | Zn, Al, Cu coatings for corrosion |
| **Plasma Spray (APS / VPS)** | Ionised argon / hydrogen plasma | 8000–15000 | 100–600 | ⚠️ Medium (VPS preferred) | Ceramics (Al₂O₃, ZrO₂), MCrAlY bond coats |
| **HVOF (High Velocity Oxy-Fuel)** | Supersonic jet, kerosene / hydrogen fuel | 2500–3000 | 600–1200 | ✅ Good | WC-Co, CrC-NiCr cermets |
| **Detonation Gun (D-Gun)** | Detonation wave (O₂ + acetylene + N₂) | 3500–4500 | 800–1000 | ✅ Good | Aerospace-grade dense coatings |
| **Cold Spray (CS / Kinetic Spray)** | **Solid-state**, supersonic gas (He, N₂) below Tm | < Tm (often < 800) | 300–1200 | ✅✅ **Best for plastics** | Pure metals, Cu, Al, Ti on polymers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Favourite Question:** *"Why is Cold Spray preferred over plasma spray for plastics?"*
> **Model Answer:** Cold spray operates at temperatures **below the melting point** of the spray material and (most importantly) keeps the gas temperature at the nozzle exit low enough (~ 300–600 °C) that the **substrate bulk temperature remains below Tg**. Bonding occurs via **adiabatic shear instability and plastic deformation of the particle**, not via melting. Thus, no thermal degradation of plastic, and CTE mismatch stresses are minimised.

## 2.3 The Three Bonding Mechanisms (High-Yield Conceptual Framework)

When a sprayed particle sticks to a plastic, the bond can be:

1. **Mechanical Interlocking** — The dominant mechanism. The roughened plastic surface (from grit blasting, chemical etching, or laser texturing) provides cavities where splats mechanically anchor. Contributes **~ 60–80 %** of total adhesion.
2. **Physical / Van der Waals Bonding** — Short-range forces across the splat-substrate interface. Contributes ~ 10–20 %.
3. **Chemical Bonding** — Covalent or ionic links (e.g., through silane coupling agents, plasma pre-treatment, or adhesion promoters). Contributes ~ 5–20 % but is the most **durable** type.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU answers, always state the dominant mechanism is *mechanical interlocking* on plastics** because the substrate is too soft and too reactive to sustain metallurgical bonding like metal-metal spray.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following equations are essential for KTU numerical / derivation problems. **No vertical pipes used** (per protocol — `\vert` substitutes).

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $v_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho_g}}$ | Particle velocity from gas pressure drop across nozzle | m/s |
| 2 | $Q_{splat} = h_s \cdot A_s \cdot (T_p - T_{sub})$ | Heat transferred to substrate by one splat during dwell time | J |
| 3 | $T_{int} = T_{sub} + \dfrac{Q_{splat}}{m_s \cdot c_{p,s}}$ | Interfacial temperature at splat-substrate contact (must be < Tg) | °C or K |
| 4 | $\sigma_{res} = E_c \cdot (\alpha_c - \alpha_s) \cdot \Delta T$ | Residual thermal stress (Lamé-type approximation) | Pa |
| 5 | $DE = \dfrac{m_{deposited}}{m_{fed}} \times 100\,\%$ | Deposition Efficiency | % |
| 6 | $R_a = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i \vert$ | Surface roughness (arithmetic mean, using \vert for absolute value) | µm |
| 7 | $P_{porosity} = \dfrac{V_{voids}}{V_{total}} \times 100\,\%$ | Coating porosity (image analysis via ASTM E2109) | % |
| 8 | $A_{coat} = \dfrac{F_{pull}}{\pi r^2}$ | Pull-off adhesion strength (ASTM C633) | MPa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Variables used:**
> $v_p$ = particle velocity; $\Delta P$ = pressure drop; $\rho_g$ = gas density; $h_s$ = convective heat transfer coefficient; $A_s$ = splat contact area; $T_p, T_{sub}, T_{int}$ = particle, substrate, interface temperatures; $m_s$ = splat mass; $c_{p,s}$ = specific heat of substrate; $E_c$ = coating Young's modulus; $\alpha_c, \alpha_s$ = CTE of coating and substrate; $\Delta T$ = temperature drop from processing to ambient; $F_{pull}$ = pull-off force; $r$ = stub radius.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace** — Cold-sprayed Al and Ti on PEEK / PPS for lightning-strike protection on radomes and composite wing leading edges.
- **Automotive** — Zn and Al coatings on PA66 (nylon) underbody components for corrosion resistance; copper coatings on PC/ABS for EMI shielding in ECU housings.
- **Electronics** — Cu or Ag cold spray on ABS / PC mobile phone housings for **5G EMI shielding** (replacing electroless Cu/Ni).
- **Medical** — Hydroxyapatite (HA) plasma spray on PEEK spinal implants for bioactivity.
- **Decorative** — Metallic finishes (chrome, bronze, copper) on 3D-printed ABS and PLA for high-end consumer products.
