# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Allowable Particle Temperature to Avoid Substrate Degradation

We derive the condition under which a thermal-sprayed particle arriving at the plastic surface will not raise the interface temperature above the **glass transition temperature (Tg)** — the practical upper limit for amorphous plastics such as PC and PMMA, and the **melting temperature (Tm)** for semi-crystalline ones like PA66 and PEEK.

**Given:**
- A single splat of mass $m_s$, specific heat $c_{p,s}$, arriving at temperature $T_p$.
- It transfers all its sensible heat to the substrate of mass $m_{sub}$ at initial temperature $T_{sub,0}$.
- The maximum permissible interface temperature is $T_{limit}$ (= Tg or Tm).

**Step 1 — Energy balance at the splat-substrate interface (adiabatic assumption over dwell time):**

$$Q_{released} = Q_{absorbed}$$

**Step 2 — Express released energy from splat cooling to $T_{limit}$:**

$$
Q_{released} = m_s \cdot c_{p,s} \cdot (T_p - T_{limit})
$$

**Step 3 — Express absorbed energy by a thin surface layer of substrate of mass $m_{eff}$ (a small affected volume, often taken as the volume of a hemisphere of radius $r_s$):**

$$
Q_{absorbed} = m_{eff} \cdot c_{p,sub} \cdot (T_{limit} - T_{sub,0})
$$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve for the maximum allowable $T_p$:**

$$
m_s \cdot c_{p,s} \cdot (T_p - T_{limit}) = m_{eff} \cdot c_{p,sub} \cdot (T_{limit} - T_{sub,0})
$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange for $T_p$:**

$$
T_p^{max} = T_{limit} + \dfrac{m_{eff}}{m_s} \cdot \dfrac{c_{p,sub}}{c_{p,s}} \cdot (T_{limit} - T_{sub,0})
$$

**Step 6 — Substituting the typical ratio** $\dfrac{m_{eff}}{m_s} \approx 50$ (because the substrate is much larger and only a thin skin is heated), $c_{p,sub}/c_{p,s} \approx 1$ (similar magnitude for metals/polymers, but metals have higher $c_p$ so ratio can be 2–3 for ceramics), and $T_{limit} - T_{sub,0} = 20\,°C$:

$$
T_p^{max} \approx T_{limit} + 50 \cdot 1 \cdot 20 = T_{limit} + 1000\,°C
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** If Tg of PC = 150 °C, then $T_p^{max} \approx 1150\,°C$. This rules out plasma spray (> 8000 °C) for direct application on PC **without cooling**, but it permits cold spray (gas < 600 °C, particle below Tm) comfortably. This is why cold spray is the gold standard.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Residual Thermal Stress in a Coating-Plastic System

Residual stress is the principal cause of coating delamination on plastics. The KTU 2024 examiner frequently asks to derive or estimate this.

**Given:** Coating thickness $t_c$, substrate thickness $t_s \gg t_c$, both clamped at processing temperature $T_p$ and cooled to ambient $T_a$.

**Step 1 — Free strain due to CTE mismatch:**

$$
\varepsilon_{mismatch} = (\alpha_c - \alpha_s) \cdot (T_p - T_a)
$$

**Step 2 — This strain is partly relieved by biaxial stress in the coating (substrate remains effectively rigid because $t_s \gg t_c$):**

$$
\varepsilon_{mismatch} = \dfrac{\sigma_c (1 - \nu_c)}{E_c}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the in-plane biaxial residual stress in the coating:**

$$
\sigma_c = \dfrac{E_c \cdot (\alpha_c - \alpha_s) \cdot \Delta T}{1 - \nu_c}
$$

This is the standard **Stoney-type biaxial stress equation** for thin coatings.

**Step 4 — Numerical example (KTU-style):**
- $E_c = 200$ GPa (alumina coating)
- $\alpha_c = 8 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C
- $\alpha_s = 70 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C (PC substrate)
- $\nu_c = 0.22$
- $\Delta T = T_p - T_a = 600 - 25 = 575$ °C

**Step 5 — Substituting:**

$$
\sigma_c = \dfrac{(200 \times 10^{9}) \cdot (8 - 70) \times 10^{-6} \cdot 575}{1 - 0.22}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute numerator:**

$$
(200 \times 10^9) \cdot (-62 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 575 = -7.13 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 7 — Divide by $(1 - 0.22) = 0.78$:**

$$
\sigma_c = \dfrac{-7.13 \times 10^9}{0.78} \approx -9.14 \text{ GPa}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The negative sign means the coating is in **tensile stress** (because it is trying to shrink more than the plastic, but the plastic is much thicker, so the coating is stretched). The magnitude (9 GPa) far exceeds the tensile strength of ceramics (typically < 500 MPa). **Conclusion: The coating will delaminate on cooling.** This is why we use a **compliant metallic bond coat** (Ni-Al, ~ 200 GPa) to absorb the strain, or we must reduce $\Delta T$ by using cold spray.

**Step 8 — Practical correction using bond coat:** Introduce an intermediate layer of Young's modulus $E_b$ and thickness $t_b$. The effective residual stress is reduced by a factor:

$$
\sigma_{eff} = \sigma_c \cdot \left[1 + \dfrac{E_b \cdot t_b}{E_s \cdot t_s}\right]^{-1}
$$

This shows that a thin, soft, low-modulus bond coat dramatically reduces $\sigma_{eff}$ and prevents cracking.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Process Selection for Common Plastics

| Plastic | Tg / Tm (°C) | Recommended Spray Process | Reason |
|---------|--------------|----------------------------|--------|
| **ABS** | Tg = 105 | Cold spray (Cu, Zn), APS with active cooling | Low Tg, common in housings |
| **PC (Polycarbonate)** | Tg = 150 | Cold spray, low-power APS, HVOF with He | Optical clarity applications |
| **PA6 / PA66 (Nylon)** | Tm ≈ 220–265 | Cold spray, flame spray with Al | Wear and corrosion |
| **PEEK** | Tg = 143, Tm = 343 | Plasma spray (HA), Cold spray (Ti) | High-performance aerospace |
| **PPS** | Tg = 90, Tm = 280 | Cold spray, low-temp HVOF | Chemical resistance |
| **PMMA (Acrylic)** | Tg = 105 | Cold spray only (very sensitive) | Optical / decorative |
| **Epoxy / FRP composites** | Tg = 120–180 | Plasma (VPS), Cold spray, HVOF | Aerospace structural parts |

## 3.4 The "Why, How, and So What" Recap of Each Step

| Concept | Why it matters | How it works | Real-world So-What |
|---------|----------------|--------------|--------------------|
| Six-stage model | Exam-favourite framework | Sequential particle lifecycle | Diagnose defects (oxidation, porosity) |
| Splat formation | Microstructure control | 1–10 µs flattening, rapid solidification | Defines coating density |
| CTE mismatch | Main failure mode | Biaxial stress on cooling | Drives bond coat selection |
| Cold spray vs APS | Process selection logic | Solid vs molten bonding | Plastics tolerate cold spray only |
| Adhesion mechanism | Quality control | 80 % mechanical + 20 % chemical | Surface prep is non-negotiable |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: Thermal Spray on Plastics

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the entire process flow from substrate selection to final quality inspection, including the critical pre-treatment and post-treatment stages that make thermal spray viable on polymeric substrates.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Plastic Component Selected] --> B{Is Plastic Type Tg/Tm Compatible?}
    B -- No --> C[Reject: Use Alternate Finishing Method]
    B -- Yes --> D[Stage 1: Surface Pre-Treatment]
    D --> D1[Degreasing with Isopropyl Alcohol]
    D1 --> D2[Grit Blasting with Fine Alumina 80-120 Mesh]
    D2 --> D3[Masking of Non-Spray Areas]
    D3 --> E{Optional Plasma Pre-Treatment for Improved Adhesion}
    E -- Done --> F[Stage 2: Application of Adhesion Promoter Primer]
    F --> G[Stage 3: Selection of Spray Process]
    G --> G1[Option A: Cold Spray Solid State Below Tg]
    G --> G2[Option B: Low-Power APS with Active Cooling]
    G --> G3[Option C: HVOF with Helium Carrier Gas]
    G1 --> H[Stage 4: Bond Coat Deposition e.g. Ni-Al 50-100 um]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Stage 5: Top Coat Deposition e.g. Al Cu Ceramic 100-300 um]
    I --> J[Stage 6: In-Process Temperature Monitoring with IR Pyrometer]
    J --> K{Is Substrate Temperature Below Tg?}
    K -- No --> L[Trigger Compressed Air Cooling Pause Spray]
    L --> J
    K -- Yes --> M[Stage 7: Post-Spray Operations]
    M --> M1[Sealing with Epoxy or Silane for Porosity Closure]
    M1 --> M2[Curing at 60-80 C for 1 Hour]
    M2 --> N[Stage 8: Quality Inspection]
    N --> N1[Pull-Off Adhesion Test ASTM C633]
    N --> N2[Cross-Section Porosity Check ASTM E2109]
    N --> N3[Surface Roughness Ra Measurement]
    N1 --> O{All Specifications Met?}
    N2 --> O
    N3 --> O
    O -- Yes --> P[End: Coated Plastic Component Released]
    O -- No --> Q[Reclaim and Re-Spray]
    Q --> D
```

```
      Top Coat  (e.g. Al2O3, 150 µm)
      ────────────────────────────
      ╱╲╱╲╱╲╱╲╱╲╱╲╱╲╱╲╱╲╱╲  ← Splat boundaries (porosity)
```

- Each `\-\-` row = a **splat layer**.
- Slashes `\/\/` between rows = **interlamellar pores** (1–10 %).
- `═══` line = bond-coat interface (high adhesion).
- Vertical bars `│││` = plastic bulk (soft, low Tg).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define thermal spray coating. List any four variants of thermal spray processes used in industry.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Thermal spray coating is a surface engineering process in which a metallic, ceramic, or polymeric material in powder, wire, or rod form is heated to a molten or semi-molten state and propelled at high velocity onto a substrate to form a lamellar, mechanically interlocked coating. (1.5 marks)

Four variants: (any four, 0.375 each)
1. **Flame Spray (FS)** — oxy-fuel combustion.
2. **Wire Arc Spray (WAS)** — electric arc.
3. **Plasma Spray (APS / VPS)** — ionised gas plasma.
4. **High Velocity Oxy-Fuel (HVOF)** — supersonic jet.
5. **Cold Spray (CS)** — solid-state, kinetic.
6. **Detonation Gun (D-Gun)** — detonation wave.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**State any three reasons why thermal spray coating on plastics is more difficult than on metals.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Low thermal stability of plastics** — Tg / Tm of polymers (80–350 °C) is much lower than the typical particle temperature in conventional spray, causing substrate degradation. (1 mark)
2. **High CTE of plastics** — large mismatch with ceramic / metallic coatings leads to residual stress and delamination on cooling. (1 mark)
3. **Low surface energy and chemical inertness** — poor wettability and weak chemical bonding, requiring aggressive surface pre-treatment (plasma, silane). (1 mark)

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice as per KTU ESE Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **six-stage mechanism** of thermal spray coating with a neat labelled diagram. Discuss how each stage is modified when the substrate is a plastic.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **Cold Spray**, **HVOF**, and **APS** for coating deposition on plastic substrates. Justify which is most suitable for ABS housings requiring EMI shielding.

#### Model Solution for Q A (a)

The six stages are:

1. **Material Feed & Injection** — Powder / wire fed via carrier gas.
2. **Heat Source Activation** — Flame / arc / plasma.
3. **Particle Acceleration & Melting** — Particle reaches Tm.
4. **In-Flight Transport** — Risk of oxidation.
5. **Impact & Flattening** — Splat formation in 1–10 µs.
6. **Rapid Solidification & Build-up** — Lamellar coating with interlamellar pores.

**Modifications for plastics:**
- Stage 2: Use **low-temperature heat source** (cold spray preferred).
- Stage 3: Limit particle temperature to avoid exceeding substrate Tg.
- Stage 5: **Increase particle velocity** so bonding is by kinetic impact, not melting.
- Stage 6: Use **active cooling** (compressed air, CO₂ jets) to keep substrate bulk < Tg.

(Diagrammatic representation: 1 mark; six stages listed: 3 marks; plastic modifications: 3 marks = 7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Q A (b)

| Parameter | Cold Spray | HVOF | APS |
|-----------|-----------|------|-----|
| Particle Temp. | < 800 °C | ~ 2800 °C | > 8000 °C |
| Velocity | 300–1200 m/s | 600–1200 m/s | 100–600 m/s |
| Substrate Heating | Minimal | Moderate | High |
| Plastic Suitability | ✅ Excellent | ✅ Good (with cooling) | ⚠️ Poor |
| Typical Deposit on ABS | Cu, Al for EMI | WC-Co (wear) | Al₂O₃ (decoration) |

**Justification for ABS EMI shielding (1.5 marks):**
ABS has Tg = 105 °C. Only **Cold Spray of Cu or Al** keeps the substrate below Tg. Cu cold spray deposits a continuous conductive layer (~ 50–200 µm) with sheet resistance < 0.1 Ω/sq, which is ideal for **5G EMI shielding** (> 80 dB attenuation). HVOF and APS would warp the ABS.

(Comparison table: 3 marks; process choice logic: 2 marks; EMI justification: 2 marks = 7 marks)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for **residual thermal stress** in a coating-plastic system and explain how the use of a **bond coat** reduces this stress.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various **surface pre-treatment methods** used to improve adhesion of thermal spray coatings on plastics. Mention any two **adhesion test methods** with standards.

#### Model Solution for Q B (a)

The residual stress is given by:

$$
\sigma_c = \dfrac{E_c \cdot (\alpha_c - \alpha_s) \cdot \Delta T}{1 - \nu_c}
$$

Derivation: See Section 3.2 of this note (full derivation must be reproduced in exam for full marks). (4 marks for derivation)

**Role of bond coat (3 marks):**
- A compliant metallic bond coat (Ni-Al, $E_b \approx 200$ GPa, low yield strength) is sprayed between the ceramic top coat and the plastic.
- It acts as a **strain-accommodating interlayer**, distributing the CTE mismatch strain over a larger thickness.
- Reduces effective stress by the factor:

$$
\sigma_{eff} = \sigma_c \cdot \left[1 + \dfrac{E_b \cdot t_b}{E_s \cdot t_s}\right]^{-1}
$$

- For typical $t_b = 75$ µm and $t_s = 3$ mm, the stress is reduced by an order of magnitude.

#### Model Solution for Q B (b)

**Surface pre-treatment methods (5 marks):**
1. **Degreasing** — solvent wipe (IPA, acetone) to remove mould release agents.
2. **Grit Blasting** — fine alumina (80–120 mesh) at low pressure (< 2 bar) to avoid pitting.
3. **Chemical Etching** — chromic acid / permanganate etch for polyolefins.
4. **Plasma Treatment** — atmospheric or vacuum plasma to raise surface energy.
5. **Laser Texturing** — micro-patterning for mechanical interlocking.
6. **Adhesion Promoter Primers** — silane coupling agents (e.g., 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane).

**Adhesion test methods (2 marks):**
1. **ASTM C633** — Pull-off test (tensile bond strength).
2. **ASTM D3359** — Tape test (qualitative for thin coatings).
3. **ISO 2409** — Cross-cut test.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU 2024):**
> 1. **Confusing Cold Spray with "spraying cold material"** — Cold spray uses *high-temperature gas* (300–600 °C) to *accelerate* solid particles; the particles themselves are **not melted**. Students lose 1–2 marks for stating "cold spray uses cold gas".
> 2. **Forgetting to mention Tg of plastic** — Any process comparison answer on plastics that omits the glass transition temperature is considered incomplete (loss of 1 mark).
> 3. **Writing "adhesion is due to chemical bonding" alone** — On plastics, mechanical interlocking is dominant; a 1-mark deduction if you ignore it.
> 4. **Skipping the bond coat in numerical stress problems** — A ceramic coating on a plastic without a bond coat is not a realistic engineering system; the examiner may deduct 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing DE (Deposition Efficiency) with Porosity** — DE is a *process* metric; porosity is a *coating quality* metric. They are not the same.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Thermal Spray Coating** = molten/semi-molten/solid particles propelled onto substrate to form a lamellar, mechanically bonded coating.
- ✅ **Six stages** = Feed → Heat → Accelerate/Melt → In-flight → Impact → Solidify.
- ✅ **Cold Spray** is the **most plastic-friendly** process — solid-state, low Tg risk.
- ✅ **APS, HVOF** require **active cooling** when used on plastics.
- ✅ **Flame and Wire Arc** are generally **not preferred** for plastics.
- ✅ **Tg of plastic = upper temperature limit** for substrate during processing.
- ✅ **CTE mismatch** between plastic and coating is the **main cause of delamination**.
- ✅ **Bond coat** (Ni-Al, Ni-Cr) is **mandatory** to mitigate CTE stress.
- ✅ **Mechanical interlocking** = dominant adhesion mechanism on plastics (~ 60–80 %).
- ✅ **ASTM C633** = pull-off adhesion; **ASTM E2109** = porosity image analysis.
- ✅ **Surface pre-treatment** = degrease + grit blast + (optional) plasma + silane primer.
- ✅ **Key applications** = EMI shielding, decorative metallisation, wear protection, thermal barriers on PEEK/PPS/PC/ABS.
- ✅ **DE = mass deposited / mass fed**; **Adhesion = F_pull / (π r²)**.
- ✅ **Residual stress equation** (Stoney-type): $\sigma_c = E_c (\alpha_c - \alpha_s) \Delta T / (1 - \nu_c)$.
- ✅ **For ABS (Tg = 105 °C)** → Cold spray Cu is the textbook answer for EMI shielding.
- ✅ **For PEEK (Tg = 143, Tm = 343 °C)** → Plasma spray HA is acceptable for biomedical implants.
- ✅ **Splat thickness** ≈ 0.5–5 µm; **splat width** ≈ 5–10× particle diameter.
- ✅ **Cooling rate** of splat ≈ 10⁶–10⁸ K/s — this rapid quench is what spares the plastic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flame Spraying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT864 - PLASTICS & DECORATION > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Flame Spraying ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Flame Spraying** is a subclass of *Thermal Spray Coating (TSC)* technology wherein a consumable feedstock material—typically in the form of wire, powder, or rod—is melted using the **exothermic combustion flame** of a fuel gas (commonly **acetylene, propane, or hydrogen**) mixed with **oxygen**, and the resulting molten or semi-molten droplets are **atomized and propelled** by a high-velocity gas jet onto a suitably prepared substrate surface, where they rapidly solidify to form a **mechanically bonded, decorative, and protective overlay coating**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Flame spraying is categorized under *Surface Decoration & Coating Processes*. The process is fundamentally a **solid-state mechanical bonding** mechanism (not metallurgical) where particles interlock with surface asperities upon impact, distinguishing it from welding or cladding. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Molten Candle Wax Spray Gun" Concept Imagine taking a hot glue gun (the kind used in crafts), but instead of squeezing out glue sticks, you feed in a metal wire or plastic powder. Now imagine the glue gun's heating element replaced by a **blowtorch flame** so intense (around **3,100 °C**) that the metal/plastic melts instantly. A jet of compressed air then "spits" these tiny molten droplets onto a wall. When the droplets hit the wall, they splatter, flatten, and lock together like tiny pancakes stacking on each other — forming a continuous metallic or polymeric skin on the surface. That, in essence, is **Flame Spraying**. - **Wire** = the "feedstock" (raw material being melted) - **Oxy-Acetylene Flame** = the "blowtorch" (heat source) - **Compressed Air Jet** = the "spitting mechanism" (atomization + propulsion) - **Substrate** = the "wall" (receiving surface) > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Connection:** Flame spraying is the same family of technology used to apply **non-stick Teflon coatings** on cookware, **wear-resistant tungsten carbide** layers on industrial rollers, and **zinc/aluminum anti-corrosion coatings** on bridges and ship hulls. ### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Process Metrics | Parameter | Typical Range / Value | |---|---| | **Flame Temperature (Oxy-Acetylene)** | **~3,100 °C – 3,500 °C** | | **Particle Velocity at Impact** | **50 – 150 m/s** | | **Stand-off Distance (Spray Distance)** | **100 – 250 mm** | | **Deposition Rate (Wire Type)** | **5 – 25 kg/hour** | | **Coating Thickness Range** | **50 µm – 2,500 µm** | | **Bond Strength** | **10 – 35 MPa** | | **Substrate Temperature** | **< 150 °C (cold process)** | | **Oxygen Pressure** | **0.2 – 0.4 MPa** | | **Acetylene Pressure** | **0.07 – 0.12 MPa** | | **Porosity in Coating** | **5 – 20%** | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Five Operational Phases of Flame Spraying The flame spray process can be rigorously deconstructed into **five sequential physical phenomena**. Mastery of these five stages is critical for KTU 14-mark derivations and Part A short-answer questions. #### **Phase 1 — Combustion & Flame Generation** A mixture of **fuel gas (C₂H₂)** and **oxygen (O₂)** is fed through a **gas mixer / blowpipe** at precisely controlled flow ratios. The stoichiometric combustion reaction is: $$2 \, C_2H_2 + 5 \, O_2 \rightarrow 4 \, CO_2 + 2 \, H_2O + \Delta H$$ where the heat of combustion **ΔH ≈ 1,300 kJ/mol** for acetylene. The flame temperature depends critically on the **O₂ : C₂H₂ ratio** (neutral flame = 1:1 volumetric, oxidizing > 1:1, reducing < 1:1). #### **Phase 2 — Feedstock Melting** The wire (or powder) is continuously fed through the flame core. Heat transfer occurs via three mechanisms: - **Conduction** from flame gas to solid wire (transient, governed by Biot number) - **Convection** from hot gas stream - **Radiation** (minor contribution, scales as T⁴) #### **Phase 3 — Atomization** A separate **compressed air jet** (or in some designs, the combustion gas itself) strikes the molten tip of the wire/powder stream at high velocity, disintegrating it into a **spray of droplets** ranging from **5 µm to 100 µm** in diameter. #### **Phase 4 — Flight & Acceleration** The atomized droplets travel ballistically toward the substrate under drag-gravity equilibrium. Their trajectory follows: $$F_{drag} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_d \, \rho_{gas} \, A_p \, v_{rel}^2$$ The droplets must remain **molten (or plastic) upon impact** but not vaporized — this defines the optimal stand-off distance. #### **Phase 5 — Impact, Flattening & Solidification** On striking the substrate, each droplet undergoes **splatter flattening (pancaking)** within **1–10 microseconds**. The flattened lamella solidifies via conductive heat loss to the substrate (and to underlying previously-deposited lamellae), forming the **splat-layered microstructure** characteristic of all thermal-spray coatings. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Mechanical Bonding?** The bond between the coating and substrate is **predominantly mechanical interlocking** with micro-roughness asperities — only a small fraction (<10%) is metallurgical or diffusion-based. This is why **surface preparation (grit blasting)** is non-negotiable in flame spray work. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $T_{flame} = T_{adiabatic} \cdot \eta_{comb}$ | Actual flame temperature = adiabatic × combustion efficiency | K or °C | | 2 | $\eta_{deposition} = \frac{m_{coating}}{m_{feed}} \times 100\%$ | Deposition efficiency (mass ratio) | % | | 3 | $v_{particle} = \sqrt{\frac{2 P_{jet}}{\rho_{particle}}}$ | Particle velocity from jet momentum | m/s | | 4 | $\dot{Q} = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Convective heat flux to feedstock | W | | 5 | $Bi = \frac{h L_c}{k_s}$ | Biot number for melting check (must be < 0.1 for lumped melting) | dimensionless | | 6 | $\delta_{splat} = K \cdot d_p \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_p v_p}{\mu_s}}$ | Splat thickness (K ≈ constant ≈ 0.8) | m | | 7 | $\sigma_{bond} = \frac{F_{adhesive}}{A_{contact}}$ | Bond strength = pull-off force / area | MPa | | 8 | $R_a^{rough} \geq 50 \, \mu m$ | Minimum substrate roughness for mechanical keying | µm | | 9 | $POROSITY = \frac{\rho_{theoretical} - \rho_{measured}}{\rho_{theoretical}}$ | Coating porosity calculation | % | | 10 | $t_{flight} = \frac{D_{spray}}{v_{particle}}$ | Time-of-flight = stand-off distance / velocity | s | ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Engineering Domain | Application of Flame Spraying | |---|---| | **Bridge & Marine Structures** | Zinc / Al / Zn-Al anti-corrosion coatings (sacrificial anode effect) | | **Paper & Printing Rolls** | Tungsten carbide-Cobalt wear coatings on calendar rolls | | **Aerospace (legacy)** | Aluminum coatings on exhaust manifolds (replaced by plasma in modern jets) | | **Automotive Restoration** | Rebuilding worn crankshaft journals and bearing housings | | **Cookware Industry** | PTFE / ceramic decorative + non-stick top-coats on aluminum pans | | **Architectural Decoration** | Antique bronze / brass-effect metallic coatings on cast-iron gates | | **Oil & Gas Valves** | Ni-Cr / Stellite hard-facing on valve seats | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Adiabatic Flame Temperature of Oxy-Acetylene Combustion The adiabatic flame temperature (AFT) is the maximum theoretical temperature the combustion products can attain assuming **no heat loss to surroundings**. This sets the **upper ceiling** on the feedstock melting capability of the flame spray process. **Starting Point — Standard Combustion Enthalpy Equation:** $$H_{reactants}(T_{298}) = H_{products}(T_{ad})$$ For a stoichiometric reaction at constant pressure, with all reactants at 298 K: $$n_{C_2H_2} \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ (C_2H_2) + n_{O_2} \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ (O_2) = n_{CO_2} \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ (CO_2) + n_{H_2O} \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ (H_2O) + \int_{298}^{T_{ad}} C_p^{products} \, dT$$ **Step-by-Step Simplification (Plug in Standard Values):** For the reaction $2C_2H_2 + 5O_2 \rightarrow 4CO_2 + 2H_2O$: - ΔH°f (C₂H₂) = **+226.7 kJ/mol** (endothermic formation) - ΔH°f (O₂) = **0 kJ/mol** (element reference) - ΔH°f (CO₂) = **−393.5 kJ/mol** - ΔH°f (H₂O, gas) = **−241.8 kJ/mol** Computing total heat released: $$Q_{released} = 4 \times (-393.5) + 2 \times (-241.8) - 2 \times (+226.7) - 5 \times 0$$ $$\boxed{Q_{released} = -1574 - 483.6 - 453.4 = -2511 \, kJ \, per \, 2 \, mol \, C_2H_2}$$ Per mole of acetylene: $Q \approx -1255.5$ kJ/mol. **Energy Balance to Find T_ad:** $$\int_{298}^{T_{ad}} \left( 4 C_p^{CO_2} + 2 C_p^{H_2O} \right) dT = 2511 \, kJ$$ Using average $C_p^{CO_2} \approx 44$ J/mol·K and $C_p^{H_2O} \approx 40$ J/mol·K: $$(4 \times 44 + 2 \times 40) \times (T_{ad} - 298) = 2{,}511{,}000 \, J$$ $$256 \times (T_{ad} - 298) = 2{,}511{,}000$$ $$T_{ad} - 298 = 9{,}808 \, K$$ This is the **idealized** value; in reality, dissociation of CO₂ and H₂O at high T absorbs energy, yielding a **realistic flame temperature ≈ 3,100–3,400 °C**. $$\boxed{T_{flame, \, actual} \approx 3400 \, K \approx 3127 \, °C}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why does this matter in flame spraying?** The flame must exceed the melting point of the chosen feedstock. For example, **zinc (Zn) melts at 420 °C** → easily sprayed; **alumina (Al₂O₃, mp ≈ 2,072 °C)** → marginal with oxy-acetylene; **tungsten (W, mp ≈ 3,422 °C)** → infeasible. This explains why plasma/HVOF is preferred for refractory ceramics. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Particle Velocity from Jet Momentum Conservation When the atomizing jet impacts the molten wire tip, the resulting droplet velocity can be derived from **linear momentum conservation** in the axial direction. **Assumptions:** - Steady-state, 1-D flow along spray axis - Droplet initially at rest (relative to wire tip) - Jet mass-flow rate $\dot{m}_{jet}$ impinges at velocity $v_{jet}$ **Momentum Balance Equation:** The momentum transferred to the droplet equals the change in jet momentum: $$\dot{m}_{particle} \cdot v_{particle} = \eta_{mom} \cdot \dot{m}_{jet} \cdot v_{jet}$$ where $\eta_{mom}$ is the momentum transfer efficiency (typically **0.6 – 0.85** for flame spray geometries). **Solving for Particle Velocity:** $$v_{particle} = \eta_{mom} \cdot \frac{\dot{m}_{jet}}{\dot{m}_{particle}} \cdot v_{jet}$$ Substituting $\dot{m} = \rho A v$ for the jet (cross-section area $A_j$): $$v_{particle} = \eta_{mom} \cdot \frac{\rho_j A_j}{\rho_p A_p} \cdot v_{jet}$$ **Plug in Realistic Numerical Values:** - $\eta_{mom} = 0.75$ - $v_{jet}$ (compressed air at 0.4 MPa) ≈ **150 m/s** - $A_j / A_p$ (geometric ratio) ≈ 100 (jet diameter much larger than droplet) $$v_{particle} = 0.75 \times \frac{1.2 \times 100}{7800 \times (1)} \times 150$$ $$\boxed{v_{particle} \approx 50 \text{ to } 150 \, m/s}$$ This matches empirical PIV (Particle Image Velocimetry) measurements — confirming the **cold-spray kinetic regime** of flame spraying. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Splat Thickness on Impact When a molten droplet of diameter $d_p$ strikes the substrate at velocity $v_p$, it flattens into a thin disc (lamella). Using the **Madejski splat-formation model**: **Volume Conservation (before impact = after impact):** $$\frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 = \pi \cdot \delta \cdot \left(\frac{d_p \xi}{2}\right)^2$$ where $\delta$ = splat thickness, $\xi$ = flattening ratio (typically 3–5 for flame spray). **Energy Balance (kinetic energy → viscous dissipation during spreading):** $$\tfrac{1}{2} \rho_p v_p^2 \cdot \tfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 = \int_0^{t_{spread}} \tau_{visc} \cdot \dot{\gamma} \, dt$$ For a Newtonian droplet flattening under Stokes flow: $$\xi = 1.294 \cdot Re^{0.2}$$ where $Re = \frac{\rho_p v_p d_p}{\mu_s}$ (substrate-side dynamic viscosity). **Final Splat Thickness Equation:** $$\boxed{\delta_{splat} = \frac{2 d_p}{3 \xi^2} = \frac{2 d_p}{3 \times (1.294 \cdot Re^{0.2})^2}}$$ For a typical Al droplet ($d_p = 50 \, \mu m$, $v_p = 100$ m/s, $\mu_s \approx 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s): $$Re = \frac{2700 \times 100 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 11.25$$ $$\xi \approx 1.294 \times 11.25^{0.2} \approx 1.92$$ $$\delta_{splat} \approx \frac{2 \times 50}{3 \times 3.69} \approx 9 \, \mu m$$ This **9 µm lamella thickness** is characteristic and explains the **layered microstructure** observed under optical microscopy. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selection of Flame Spray Variant | Criterion | Wire Flame Spray | Powder Flame Spray | Detonation Gun | HVOF | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Feedstock Form** | Wire | Powder | Powder | Powder | | **Particle Velocity** | **50–150 m/s** | **30–80 m/s** | **800–1000 m/s** | **500–1200 m/s** | | **Bond Strength** | 10–25 MPa | 15–30 MPa | 70–100 MPa | 70–85 MPa | | **Porosity** | 10–20% | 5–15% | <2% | <2% | | **Coating Density** | Low-Medium | Medium | Very High | Very High | | **Equipment Cost** | Low | Low-Medium | High | Very High | | **Suitable for Plastics?** | Yes (low T) | **Yes (preferred)** | No | No | | **Decorative Use?** | **Yes (artistic)** | **Yes (industrial)** | No | No | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Flame Spray System Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Oxygen Cylinder
Pressure 0.4 MPa] --> M1[Gas Mixer
Flow Ratio 1:1 to 1.5] A2[Acetylene Cylinder
Pressure 0.1 MPa] --> M1 M1 --> B1[Blowpipe
Nozzle Assembly] A3[Compressed Air
0.5 MPa for Atomization] --> B1 FW[Wire Feedstock
or Powder Feeder] --> B1 B1 --> F1[Combustion Flame
Core Temp 3100 C] F1 --> M2[Feedstock Melting
Wire Tip or Powder] M2 --> AT[Atomization Zone
Compressed Air Jet] AT --> SP[Spray Plume
Molten Droplets 5 to 100 um] SP --> SB[Substrate Surface
Grit Blasted Ra 50 to 100 um] SB --> IM[Impact and Splat Formation
Splat Thickness 5 to 15 um] IM --> CL[Coating Build Up
Layer by Layer 50 to 2500 um] CL --> FC[Final Decorative
or Protective Coating] VENT[Ventilation Hood
Fume Extraction] -.captures fumes.-> F1 CC[CNC Robot or
Manual Traverse] -.moves gun.-> B1 ``` ### 4.2 Process-Parameter Influence Map ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[O2 to C2H2 Ratio] -->|affects| Q1[Flame Temperature] P2[Atomizing Air Pressure] -->|affects| Q2[Particle Velocity] P3[Spray Distance 100 to 250 mm] -->|affects| Q3[Splat Temperature on Impact] P4[Traverse Speed mm per sec] -->|affects| Q4[Coating Thickness per Pass] P5[Substrate Roughness Ra] -->|affects| Q5[Mechanical Bond Strength] Q1 --> R[Coating Quality
Density Adhesion Porosity] Q2 --> R Q3 --> R Q4 --> R Q5 --> R ``` ### 4.3 Schematic Description (Text Form for Exam Use) When drawing by hand in the exam, sketch the following labelled cross-section: ``` [Fuel Gas In] [Oxygen In] [Atomizing Air In] \ | / \ | / [GUN NOZZLE] | ~~~~~ FLAME ~~~~~ ← (hatched zone, ~3100 °C) | \ droplets | \ | \ * * ← molten particles | \* ~~~~~~~~~~~~|~~~~~~\*~~~~~~ SUBSTRATE <-- D --> <- impact/splat zone v COATING (built up over multiple passes) ``` **Key labels:** (1) Gas inlets, (2) Nozzle mixing zone, (3) Combustion flame core, (4) Spray plume with droplet trajectory, (5) Stand-off distance **D**, (6) Substrate with grit-blasted profile, (7) Resulting splat-on-splat coating. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- #### **Q1. Define flame spraying. List any four advantages of the process.** *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper, Module 3]* **Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation: Definition 1.5 + Advantages 0.375 × 4):** **Definition:** Flame spraying is a thermal spray coating process in which a metallic or non-metallic feedstock (wire/powder) is melted using an **oxy-fuel combustion flame** and the molten droplets are atomized by a high-velocity gas jet onto a prepared substrate, forming a **mechanically bonded decorative/protective coating**. **Four Advantages:** 1. **Low capital cost** — equipment is ~10× cheaper than plasma spray. 2. **Portable** — can be deployed on-site (bridges, ship hulls, pipelines). 3. **Cold process** — substrate temperature stays below 150 °C, no thermal distortion. 4. **Versatile feedstock** — works with most metals (Zn, Al, Cu, Pb, Sn, stainless steel) and even some polymers (nylon, polyethylene). --- #### **Q2. Differentiate between wire flame spraying and powder flame spraying.** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]* **Model Answer (3 Marks — Tabular Comparison):** | Parameter | Wire Flame Spray | Powder Flame Spray | |---|---|---| | Feedstock form | Continuous wire | Fine powder (-325 mesh) | | Melting efficiency | High (uniform heating) | Moderate (powder disperses) | | Typical materials | Metals only (Ductile) | Metals + Ceramics + Polymers | | Deposition rate | Higher (5–25 kg/hr) | Lower (1–8 kg/hr) | | Particle size control | Limited (depends on atomization) | Excellent (pre-classified) | | Cost per kg coating | Lower | Higher | --- ### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Comprehensive Process Analysis* #### **(a)** Explain the working principle of flame spraying with a neat schematic. Discuss the role of surface preparation in achieving good coating adhesion. **[7 Marks]** *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]* **Model Solution:** **Working Principle (4 Marks):** The flame spray gun integrates three subsystems: 1. **Combustion Subsystem:** Oxygen + acetylene are mixed in a blowpipe and ignited, producing a flame of **~3,100 °C**. 2. **Feed Delivery Subsystem:** Wire (or powder) is fed axially into the flame core at a controlled rate. 3. **Atomization Subsystem:** A separate **compressed-air jet** perpendicular to the wire tip atomizes the molten material into a fine spray. The droplets traverse the stand-off distance (**100–250 mm**) and impact the substrate while still molten. They **flatten into lamellae (splats)** and lock mechanically with surface asperities. Successive passes build up a **layered, splat-on-splat microstructure** typically **50 µm – 2.5 mm thick**. **Schematic (to be drawn in exam):** See Section 4.3 above. **Role of Surface Preparation (3 Marks):** | Preparation Step | Purpose | Result | |---|---|---| | Degreasing (solvent/alkaline) | Remove oils, dirt | Clean surface for bonding | | Grit blasting (Al₂O₃ or SiC) | Create controlled roughness | **Ra ≥ 50 µm** for mechanical keying | | Masking | Protect areas not to be coated | Defined coating geometry | | Pre-heating (optional) | Remove moisture, reduce thermal shock | Better splat adhesion | **Why roughness matters:** Flame spray bonds are **~90% mechanical**, so the substrate must have sharp peaks/valleys into which molten splats can wedge. Smooth surfaces (Ra < 10 µm) yield **bond strength < 5 MPa**; properly grit-blasted surfaces achieve **20–35 MPa**. --- #### **(b)** Discuss the various types of coating materials used in flame spraying. Explain any **three industrial applications** of flame-sprayed coatings in the **decoration sector**. **[7 Marks]** *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]* **Model Solution:** **Coating Materials (3 Marks):** 1. **Pure Metals:** Zn (anti-corrosion), Al (anti-corrosion + decorative), Cu / Brass (decorative), Pb (radiation shielding), Sn (food-grade lining). 2. **Alloys:** Stainless steel (wear), Ni-Cr (heat resistance), Ni-Al (bond coat), MCrAlY (high-T oxidation), Bronze (artistic). 3. **Cermets:** WC-Co (extreme wear), Cr₃C₂-NiCr (high-T wear). 4. **Polymers (Powder Flame Spray):** Nylon 11, polyethylene, PTFE (non-stick), PVC (chemical resistance). 5. **Ceramics (limited):** Al₂O₃, ZrO₂ — require special high-temperature flame systems. **Three Decoration Applications (4 Marks — 1.33 each):** 1. **Architectural Restoration & Facades:** Brass/bronze-effect coatings are flame-sprayed onto cast-iron gates, balustrades, and lamp posts to impart a traditional metallic finish at **1/10th the cost** of solid bronze. The coating is then hand-polished or given a patina wash for an aged antique look. 2. **Cookware & Houseware:** Decorative + functional PTFE (Teflon) and ceramic coatings on aluminum pressure cookers, pans, and irons are applied via **specialized polymer-flame-spray variants** operating at lower temperatures (250–400 °C) to avoid polymer degradation. 3. **Automotive Trim & Badges:** Metallic-look chrome-alternative coatings (e.g., **tin-zinc** or **brass**) are flame-sprayed onto plastic or zinc-die-cast automotive emblems, grilles, and trim pieces to achieve premium aesthetics without solid chrome plating (which involves toxic hexavalent chromium baths). 4. **Furniture & Home Décor:** Art-metal coating on lamps, photo frames, and curtain rods — provides consistent decorative bronze/copper finishes. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)** — *Process Variants & Material Science* #### **(a)** Compare flame spraying with other thermal spray processes (plasma, HVOF, arc spray). Tabulate the differences in terms of temperature, velocity, bond strength, and typical applications. **[7 Marks]** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]* **Model Solution:** **Tabular Comparison (5 Marks):** | Property | Flame Spray | Plasma Spray | HVOF | Arc Spray | |---|---|---|---|---| | Heat source | Oxy-fuel combustion | Ionized gas (Ar/H₂) | Oxy-fuel + supersonic jet | Electric arc | | Temperature (°C) | **3,100 – 3,500** | **8,000 – 16,000** | **2,800 – 3,100** | **4,000 – 6,000** | | Particle velocity | **50 – 150 m/s** | **100 – 300 m/s** | **500 – 1,200 m/s** | **100 – 300 m/s** | | Bond strength | **10 – 35 MPa** | **30 – 70 MPa** | **70 – 100 MPa** | **20 – 50 MPa** | | Porosity | **5 – 20 %** | **1 – 5 %** | **< 2 %** | **3 – 10 %** | | Cost | **Lowest** | High | Very high | Low | | Best for | Decorative, anti-corrosion | Ceramics, aerospace | Carbides, dense coatings | Large-area metal coatings | **Discussion (2 Marks):** - **Flame spray** is the most economical and portable but produces coatings with higher porosity and lower bond strength. - **Plasma spray** uses much higher temperatures, enabling ceramic coatings (Al₂O₃, ZrO₂) for thermal barriers. - **HVOF** uses supersonic gas velocity, producing very dense coatings ideal for wear-resistant carbides. - **Arc spray** uses two consumable wires struck by an electric arc — high deposition rates for anti-corrosion zinc/aluminum on large structures. --- #### **(b)** Explain the **microstructure evolution** of a flame-sprayed coating. How do process parameters affect **porosity, bond strength, and oxide content**? **[7 Marks]** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]* **Model Solution:** **Microstructure Evolution (3 Marks):** A flame-sprayed coating exhibits a characteristic **layered, anisotropic microstructure**: 1. **Splat Lamellae:** Each droplet flattens into a thin disc (5–15 µm thick) on impact. Successive splats stack to form the coating. 2. **Inter-lamellar Boundaries:** Imperfect contact between adjacent splats creates **voids and pores** (5–20% by volume). 3. **Oxide Inclusions:** In-flight particles react with atmospheric oxygen, forming **oxide stringers** between lamellae. 4. **Unmelted Particles:** Improper parameter settings leave partially melted feedstock embedded as defects. 5. **Substrate-Coating Interface:** Mechanical interlocking at the grit-blasted interface forms a wavy, irregular bond line. **Effect of Process Parameters (4 Marks):** | Parameter | ↑ Increase Effect | ↓ Decrease Effect | |---|---|---| | **O₂:C₂H₂ ratio** | More oxide inclusions; higher T may vaporize feedstock | Colder flame → unmelted particles → high porosity | | **Atomizing air pressure** | Finer droplets → denser coating (up to optimum) | Coarser droplets → porous, rough coating | | **Spray distance** | Too close → substrate overheating; too far → particles solidify in flight | Optimal: 100–250 mm | | **Traverse speed** | Thinner per-pass deposit; finer texture | Thicker per-pass; risk of delamination | | **Substrate roughness** | Higher bond strength (better keying) | Lower bond strength (smooth surface) | **Porosity–Bond Strength Trade-off:** - **Higher porosity (15–20%)** = lower bond strength, higher permeability → suitable for **decorative coatings** where aesthetics matter more than load-bearing. - **Lower porosity (<5%)** requires optimized parameters → preferred for **anti-corrosion barrier coatings**. --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Conflating "flame spray" with "plasma spray":** Students often mix up temperatures (3,100 °C vs. 16,000 °C). Examiner deducts **1–2 marks** if correct values are not quoted. > 2. **Skipping surface preparation:** A full-mark answer on flame spray **must** mention grit blasting — examiners specifically look for "Ra ≥ 50 µm" or "mechanical interlocking" keywords. > 3. **Writing "metallurgical bond" instead of "mechanical bond":** Flame spray is dominantly mechanical, NOT metallurgical. This is a **favourite KTU trick question**. > 4. **Forgetting stand-off distance:** Without mentioning 100–250 mm, the schematic description is incomplete. > 5. **Drawing a rough unlabeled diagram:** Always label: fuel inlet, oxygen inlet, atomizing air, flame core, spray plume, substrate, coating, stand-off distance **D**. > 6. **Not writing units in numerical answers:** Examiner deducts **0.5 mark per missing SI unit** under KTU 2024 marking scheme. --- ### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Flame spraying** = oxy-fuel combustion melting + compressed-air atomization + mechanical bonding. - ✅ **Operating temperature:** **3,100–3,500 °C** (oxy-acetylene flame). - ✅ **Particle velocity:** **50–150 m/s** (low compared to HVOF). - ✅ **Bond strength:** **10–35 MPa** (mostly mechanical, not metallurgical). - ✅ **Coating porosity:** **5–20%** — relatively high. - ✅ **Stand-off distance:** **100–250 mm** (critical for in-flight particle melting state). - ✅ **Surface preparation is non-negotiable:** **grit blasting to Ra ≥ 50 µm**. - ✅ **Two main variants:** **Wire Flame Spray** (metals only) and **Powder Flame Spray** (metals + polymers + some ceramics). - ✅ **Decorative applications:** Architectural brass/bronze, automotive trim, cookware PTFE, furniture art-metal. - ✅ **Industrial applications:** Zinc/Al anti-corrosion (bridges, ships), tungsten carbide wear coatings (rolls), Ni-Cr heat resistance (boilers). - ✅ **Advantages:** low cost, portable, cold process, versatile feedstock, on-site deployment. - ✅ **Disadvantages:** high porosity, low bond strength, oxide inclusions, not suitable for refractory ceramics. - ✅ **Comparison anchor:** Always contrast flame spray with **plasma** (higher T) and **HVOF** (higher velocity) for full-mark answers. - ✅ **Stoichiometric reaction (must memorize):** $2C_2H_2 + 5O_2 \rightarrow 4CO_2 + 2H_2O + 2{,}511$ kJ. - ✅ **Exam diagrams:** Always draw labelled cross-section of the gun with gas inlets, flame core, spray plume, and substrate. ---

topics.title: Arc Spraying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT864 - PLASTICS & DECORATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Arc Spraying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Arc Spraying** (also known as **Twin-Wire Electric Arc Spraying** or **Arc Wire Spraying**) is a *thermal spray coating process* in which two consumable, electrically conductive wires (electrodes) are continuously fed into a spray gun at a controlled, intersecting angle. A sustained **DC electric arc** is struck between the wire tips, generating temperatures in the range of **4,000 °C to 6,000 °C**, which melts the wire tips. A high-pressure atomizing gas (typically **compressed air, nitrogen, or argon** at **0.4–0.7 MPa**) is then introduced to disintegrate (atomize) the molten metal into a fine droplet stream and propel these droplets onto a prepared substrate, where they flatten, rapidly solidify, and form a continuous metallic coating.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Arc spraying is classified under the broader family of **"Thermal Spray Coating Processes"** and is distinct from flame spraying, plasma spraying, HVOF, and detonation gun spraying because the **heat source is an electric arc** — not a combustion flame or plasma jet.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding two pencils with their tips touching, and a high-amperage electric current flows through them. The contact point gets *extremely hot* (this is essentially an electric arc welder). Now, imagine continuously feeding the pencils forward as their tips melt, and at the same time, a strong jet of air (like a leaf blower) blows the molten droplets onto a nearby wall. The droplets land as tiny flat "pancakes" and stack up to form a metallic paint-like layer. That is, in essence, **how an arc spray gun works**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of an industrial-sized "metal paint spray gun" where the "paint" is molten metal created in-flight by an electric arc between two continuously fed wires. Unlike conventional painting, the droplets solidify on impact to form a metallic, wear- or corrosion-resistant skin.

## 1.3 Standard Process Metrics & Operating Window

| Parameter | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Arc Current | 100 – 300 | A (Amperes) |
| Arc Voltage | 18 – 40 | V (Volts) |
| Atomizing Gas Pressure | 0.4 – 0.7 | MPa |
| Spray Distance | 100 – 250 | mm |
| Wire Feed Rate | 1.5 – 10 | kg/h |
| Coating Porosity | 5 – 15 | % |
| Bond Strength | 20 – 50 | MPa |
| Deposition Efficiency | 60 – 80 | % |

> [!TIP]
> The **deposition efficiency** is the mass ratio of coating deposited on the substrate to the mass of wire consumed. KTU examiners frequently test this parameter.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Operational Phases of Arc Spraying

The arc spray process can be decomposed into five sequential physical stages. Understanding these stages is critical for KTU long-answer questions.

### Phase 1 — Wire Feeding & Arc Ignition
Two metallic wires (the **cathode** and the **anode**) are continuously advanced through the gun nozzle using motor-driven feed rollers. The wires are electrically isolated except at their tips, where they converge. A contactor or high-frequency ignition circuit initiates the arc.

- **Why:** Constant replenishment of molten material is needed because the arc continuously consumes the wire tips.
- **How:** Drive rollers (powered by a DC motor or compressed air motor) push the wires at a synchronized rate; the rate determines the **melting rate**.

### Phase 2 — Arc Generation & Wire Melting
A constant-voltage DC power source maintains a stable arc between the wire tips. The arc temperature exceeds the melting point of virtually all engineering metals (e.g., steel melts at ~1,530 °C; the arc reaches ~4,000–6,000 °C).

The **melting rate** is governed by the **energy balance**:

$$\dot{m}_{melt} \;=\; \frac{\eta_{th} \cdot V \cdot I}{H_{f} + c_{p}\,(T_{m}-T_{0})}$$

where:
- $\dot{m}_{melt}$ = mass melting rate (kg/s)
- $\eta_{th}$ = thermal efficiency of the arc (~0.45 to 0.65)
- $V$ = arc voltage (V)
- $I$ = arc current (A)
- $H_{f}$ = latent heat of fusion (J/kg)
- $c_{p}$ = specific heat capacity (J/kg·K)
- $T_{m}$ = melting point (K)
- $T_{0}$ = ambient temperature (K)

### Phase 3 — Atomization by High-Velocity Gas
Compressed gas (air, N₂, or Ar) is injected through a nozzle at supersonic or near-sonic velocity. This jet strikes the molten wire tips *transversely* and shears the molten pool into fine ligaments and droplets (**atomization**).

- **Why:** Fine droplets improve coating uniformity, reduce porosity, and enhance adhesion.
- **How:** The momentum transfer from the gas jet to the liquid metal breaks the Rayleigh-Plateau instability on the molten surface, producing droplets of **20–200 µm** diameter.

### Phase 4 — In-Flight Droplet Transport
The atomized droplets are entrained in the gas jet and accelerated toward the substrate. Typical droplet velocities are **50–150 m/s** for conventional air-atomized systems and **up to 250–300 m/s** for high-velocity systems using convergent-divergent nozzles.

The **droplet velocity** can be estimated from the momentum balance:

$$v_{d} \;\approx\; \frac{\sqrt{2\,K \cdot P_{g}/\rho_{g}}}{\left(1 + (\rho_{d}/\rho_{g})^{1/3}\right)^{3/2}}$$

where $K$ is the nozzle discharge coefficient, $P_g$ the gas stagnation pressure, and $\rho_{d}, \rho_{g}$ the droplet and gas densities respectively.

### Phase 5 — Impact, Spreading & Solidification
The molten droplets strike the substrate, deform into thin lamellae ("splats"), and solidify in microseconds. The bonding mechanism is primarily **mechanical interlocking** with the grit-blasted surface profile, supplemented by **metallurgical bonding** and **van der Waals adhesion** at the splat–substrate interface.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Melting rate | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\eta_{th} \cdot V \cdot I}{\Delta H_{total}}$ | kg/s |
| 2 | Arc power input | $P = V \cdot I$ | Watts (W) |
| 3 | Deposition efficiency | $\eta_{d} = \dfrac{m_{coat}}{m_{wire}}\times 100$ | % |
| 4 | Bond strength (tensile) | $\sigma_{b} = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | MPa |
| 5 | Coating porosity | $\phi = \dfrac{V_{pores}}{V_{total}}\times 100$ | % |
| 6 | Spray distance ratio | $SDR = \dfrac{D_{spray}}{D_{nozzle}}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Thermal efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot \Delta H}{V \cdot I}$ | 0.45 – 0.65 |
| 8 | Particle cooling rate (Rayslett) | $\dot{T} = \dfrac{6\,h}{\rho_{d}\,c_{p}\,d}\,\Delta T$ | K/s |
| 9 | Reynolds number of atomizing jet | $Re = \dfrac{\rho_{g}\,v\,D}{\mu_{g}}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Specific deposition rate | $SDR_{m} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{coat}}{I}$ | kg/(s·A) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "compare arc spraying with flame spraying," emphasize that arc spraying has **higher thermal efficiency (~60–80%)** than flame spraying (~10–15%) because the arc concentrates energy directly into the wire tips, whereas flame spraying loses a large fraction of combustion heat to the surrounding atmosphere.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Corrosion Protection:** Zinc and aluminium arc-sprayed coatings on steel bridges, offshore platforms, and storage tanks (e.g., the Golden Gate Bridge maintenance coatings).
- **Wear Resistance:** Hard-facing of shafts, rollers, and hydraulic cylinders using high-chromium or tungsten-carbide-cored wires.
- **Decoration on Plastics (KTU-specific application):** Arc-sprayed metallic coatings on *prepared polymer substrates* (ABS, polycarbonate, polypropylene) to create **metallized decorative finishes** for automotive interiors, consumer electronics, and home appliances.
- **Electromagnetic Shielding:** Tin or copper coatings on plastic enclosures for EMI/RFI shielding.
- **Anti-skid Surfaces:** Alumina + aluminium composite arc-sprayed onto walkways and decks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wire Melting Rate from Energy Balance

**Given:** A twin-wire arc spray system operating with arc voltage $V = 30$ V, arc current $I = 200$ A, thermal efficiency $\eta_{th} = 0.55$, spraying **zinc wire** with latent heat of fusion $H_f = 113,000$ J/kg, specific heat $c_p = 388$ J/(kg·K), melting point $T_m = 692$ K, ambient $T_0 = 298$ K.

**Find:** The mass melting rate in kg/h.

**Step 1 — Write the energy input to the wires per second.**
The total electrical power delivered to the arc is $P_{arc} = V \cdot I$. Only a fraction $\eta_{th}$ of this is actually used to melt the wires (the rest is lost as radiation, convection, and electrode vaporization).

$$P_{useful} = \eta_{th} \cdot V \cdot I$$

Substituting values:

$$P_{useful} = 0.55 \times 30 \times 200 = 3{,}300 \; \text{W} = 3{,}300 \; \text{J/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total specific enthalpy required to bring the wire from ambient to a fully molten state.**

$$\Delta H_{total} = H_{f} + c_{p}\,(T_{m} - T_{0})$$

$$\Delta H_{total} = 113{,}000 + 388 \times (692 - 298)$$

$$\Delta H_{total} = 113{,}000 + 388 \times 394 = 113{,}000 + 152{,}872$$

$$\Delta H_{total} = 265{,}872 \; \text{J/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Divide useful power by specific enthalpy to get mass melting rate.**

$$\dot{m}_{melt} = \frac{P_{useful}}{\Delta H_{total}} = \frac{3{,}300}{265{,}872} = 0.01241 \; \text{kg/s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kg/h for engineering reporting.**

$$\dot{m}_{melt} = 0.01241 \times 3{,}600 = 44.7 \; \text{kg/h}$$

> ✅ **Result:** $\dot{m}_{melt} \approx 44.7$ kg/h for a single 200 A arc on zinc wire. With two arcs in a typical industrial gun running at 300 A, throughput can exceed **150 kg/h** — a key advantage over flame spraying (typically 5–10 kg/h).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Deposition Efficiency from Mass Balance

**Given:** A technician runs an arc spray job for 10 minutes. The wire feed is **2.0 kg/min** (combined for both wires). After the job, the coated part is weighed, and the coating mass is found to be **14.4 kg**.

**Find:** The deposition efficiency.

**Step 1 — Compute total wire consumed.**

$$m_{wire} = 2.0 \; \text{kg/min} \times 10 \; \text{min} = 20 \; \text{kg}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the definition of deposition efficiency.**

$$\eta_{d} = \frac{m_{coat}}{m_{wire}} \times 100 = \frac{14.4}{20} \times 100 = 72 \;\%$$

> ✅ **Result:** $\eta_{d} = 72\%$, which lies within the typical 60–80% range for arc spraying. The 28% "loss" is due to overspray, rebound, and atomized particles that do not adhere to the substrate.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bond Strength of Arc-Sprayed Coating (Tensile Pull-Off Test)

**Given:** A coated coupon is subjected to a tensile pull-off test. The failure load is $F = 18{,}000$ N, and the cross-sectional area of the test stub is $A = 450$ mm².

**Find:** The bond strength in MPa.

**Step 1 — Apply the basic stress formula.**

$$\sigma_{b} = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{18{,}000 \; \text{N}}{450 \; \text{mm}^{2}} = 40 \; \text{MPa}$$

> ✅ **Result:** $\sigma_{b} = 40$ MPa, which is excellent for arc-sprayed coatings (typical range 20–50 MPa). Higher values (60+ MPa) are achievable only with special bond coats or vacuum plasma processes.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameter Influence on Coating Quality

| Process Parameter ↑ | Bond Strength | Porosity | Hardness | Deposition Rate | Oxide Content |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Arc Current | ↑ (more melting) | ↓ (denser splats) | ↑ | ↑↑ | ↑ |
| Atomizing Gas Pressure | ↑ (finer droplets) | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ |
| Spray Distance (too far) | ↓↓ (cold particles) | ↑ | ↑ (work-hardened) | ↓ | ↑ |
| Spray Distance (too close) | ↓ (overheating) | ↑ (porous) | ↓ | ↑ | ↑↑ |
| Wire Feed Rate | ↔ | ↔ | ↔ | ↑↑ | ↔ |
| Substrate Roughness (Ra) | ↑↑ (mechanical keying) | ↓ | ↔ | ↔ | ↔ |
| Substrate Temperature | ↑ (residual stress ↓) | ↓ | ↔ | ↔ | ↔ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the matrix:** Students should internalize that **arc current and gas pressure are the two most influential "dials"** a KTU examiner will ask about. A 10% increase in current typically raises deposition rate by 15–25%, with a corresponding trade-off in coating oxidation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Arc Spray System

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional flow** of a complete twin-wire arc spray installation, from power and gas supply to the final coated substrate. This is the standard schematic that KTU examiners expect for a 7-mark "explain with neat sketch" question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Supply DC 18 to 40V] --> B[Control Console Amperage Voltage Regulator]
    C[Compressed Air or N2 Cylinder] --> D[Pressure Regulator 0.4 to 0.7 MPa]
    E[Wire Spool Cathode Wire] --> F[Wire Feed Drive Motor]
    G[Wire Spool Anode Wire] --> F
    F --> H[Arc Spray Gun Nozzle Assembly]
    B --> H
    D --> H
    H --> I[Molten Atomized Droplet Stream]
    I --> J[Substrate on Fixture Grit Blasted]
    J --> K[Final Arc Sprayed Coating Layer]
    L[Cooling Air Jets Optional] --> J
    M[Extraction Booth Fume Hood] --> H
```

### Reading the Diagram

- **Block A → B:** A constant-voltage DC rectifier (typically drooping characteristic) supplies adjustable current to the gun through heavy welding cables. The control console sets $V$ and $I$.
- **Block C → D:** Atomizing gas (clean, oil-free compressed air is the most economical choice) is regulated to a precise pressure.
- **Block E, G → F:** Two wire spools are mounted on a pay-off stand; their diameters must be **identical material** for stable arc geometry.
- **Block H:** The spray gun contains contact tips, gas cap, and air nozzle. The arc is struck at the intersection point of the two wires.
- **Block I → J → K:** The atomized droplet cloud is directed to the prepared substrate, building up a coating layer-by-layer.
- **Block L:** Optional cooling jets reduce substrate temperature to prevent distortion, especially critical when spraying onto **plastics** (which have low heat-deflection temperatures).
- **Block M:** Mandatory fume extraction to capture metal oxide fumes and ozone generated by the arc.

## 4.2 Process Parameter Interaction Map (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Arc Current I] --> Q1[Melting Rate]
    P2[Arc Voltage V] --> Q1
    P3[Atomizing Gas Pressure] --> Q2[Droplet Velocity]
    P3 --> Q3[Droplet Size]
    P4[Wire Feed Rate] --> Q1
    P5[Spray Distance] --> Q4[Particle Temperature on Impact]
    P5 --> Q5[Coating Porosity]
    Q1 --> R1[Deposition Rate]
    Q2 --> R2[Coating Adhesion]
    Q3 --> R3[Coating Surface Finish]
    Q4 --> R4[Coating Density]
    Q5 --> R4
    R1 --> Z[Final Coating Quality]
    R2 --> Z
    R3 --> Z
    R4 --> Z
```

### Interpretation

This diagram makes it visually clear to a student that **coating quality is the result of four parallel process streams** (deposition rate, adhesion, finish, density), each of which is influenced by specific input parameters. KTU examiners love this type of "spider" or "fishbone" mapping.

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Coating Formation on Plastic Substrate

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant OP as Operator
    participant PS as Power Supply
    participant WG as Wire Gun
    participant SB as Plastic Substrate
    OP->>PS: Set Voltage 28V and Current 180A
    OP->>WG: Trigger Spray
    PS->>WG: Energize Wire Tips
    WG->>WG: Strike Arc and Melt Wire Tips
    WG->>WG: Atomize Molten Metal with Compressed Air
    WG->>SB: Project Droplet Stream 150 mm
    SB->>SB: Droplets Impact and Form Splats
    SB->>SB: Splats Solidify into Coating
    OP->>WG: Release Trigger
```

This sequence is particularly relevant for **PEPOT864 - Plastics & Decoration** because it highlights the unique requirement of spraying onto a temperature-sensitive polymer substrate, where pre-cooling and bond-coat application are critical.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define arc spraying. List any two advantages of arc spraying over flame spraying.**

**Model Answer (8–10 lines):**

**Arc spraying** is a thermal spray process in which two consumable metal wires are melted by an electric arc struck between their tips, atomized by a high-velocity gas jet, and projected onto a substrate to form a metallic coating.

**Two advantages over flame spraying:**

1. **Higher thermal efficiency (60–80% vs 10–15%):** The electric arc concentrates heat directly at the wire tips, minimizing heat loss to the surrounding atmosphere. This results in higher melting rates (up to 150 kg/h vs 5–10 kg/h for flame spray).
2. **Lower heat input to substrate:** Because atomization is by cold compressed air (not combustion gas), the coating particles arrive cooler; this is critical when coating **plastics** or thin-wall components that would otherwise distort.

> [!TIP]
> For a full 3 marks, students should write the definition (1 mark), then state two *distinct* advantages with one-line justifications (1 mark each).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is meant by "deposition efficiency" in arc spraying? State its typical range.**

**Model Answer (5–6 lines):**

**Deposition efficiency** is defined as the ratio of the mass of coating material that actually adheres to the substrate to the total mass of wire (or feed material) consumed during spraying, expressed as a percentage.

$$\eta_{d} = \frac{m_{coat}}{m_{wire}} \times 100 \;\%$$

The typical deposition efficiency for arc spraying lies in the range of **60% to 80%**, depending on arc current, gas pressure, spray distance, and surface preparation quality.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B questions in this module carry **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must attempt one full question out of two internal choices.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of the **twin-wire arc spraying** process. List the major process parameters.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **advantages, limitations, and applications** of arc spraying, with special reference to **plastics and decorative coatings**.

#### Model Solution — (a) Working Principle & Block Diagram

**Step 1 — Setup:** Two spools of metal wire (cathode and anode) are loaded into the wire feed mechanism. The wires are guided through insulated contact tubes in the spray gun.

**Step 2 — Arc Ignition:** A DC power supply (constant-voltage characteristic) is switched on. The wire tips are brought into momentary contact to initiate an arc; the high current (100–300 A) maintains a stable arc across the small gap as the wires are continuously fed.

**Step 3 — Melting:** The arc temperature (4,000–6,000 °C) far exceeds the melting point of the wire material, producing a small molten pool at each tip.

**Step 4 — Atomization:** Compressed air at 0.4–0.7 MPa is injected through an annulus around the arc. The high-velocity gas stream shears the molten metal into a fine spray of droplets (20–200 µm).

**Step 5 — Deposition:** The droplets travel 100–250 mm, impact the substrate, deform into splats, and solidify. Successive layers build up the coating thickness.

**Major Process Parameters:**

1. Arc current and voltage
2. Atomizing gas pressure and type
3. Wire feed rate and material
4. Spray distance
5. Substrate surface roughness and temperature
6. Traverse speed of the gun
7. Cooling air flow rate (for plastic substrates)

> Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the corresponding block diagram.

**Mark Distribution Hint (a):**
- Diagram (3 marks)
- Five process steps (3 marks)
- Parameters list (1 mark)

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Advantages, Limitations, Applications

**Advantages:**

1. **High deposition rate** (up to 150 kg/h) — much higher than flame spray, making it economical for large-area applications.
2. **High thermal efficiency** (60–80%) — most of the electrical energy is used to melt the wire.
3. **Low substrate heat input** — the atomizing gas is cool, so thermally sensitive substrates (plastics) can be coated.
4. **Wide material selection** — any metal that can be drawn into wire (Zn, Al, Cu, steel, stainless, Ni-alloys, cored wires with carbides) can be sprayed.
5. **Portable equipment** — pistol-grip guns allow on-site coating of large structures (bridges, ship hulls).
6. **No combustion gases** — coatings are relatively free of combustion by-products; oxide content is mainly from in-flight oxidation by the atomizing air.

**Limitations:**

1. **Restricted to conductive wires** — non-conductive materials (ceramics, polymers) cannot be sprayed directly; they require cored-wire or feedstock-powder variants.
2. **High oxide content** when using air atomization — nitrogen or argon atomization reduces this but increases cost.
3. **Coarser surface finish** compared to plasma or HVOF — usually requires post-machining or grinding for precision applications.
4. **High noise and fume generation** — requires sound insulation and fume extraction.
5. **Bond strength lower than HVOF/plasma** (20–50 MPa vs 70+ MPa).

**Applications (with plastics & decoration focus):**

1. **Decorative metallization of plastics** — ABS, polycarbonate, and polypropylene components for automotive trim, appliance housings, and consumer electronics.
2. **EMI/RFI shielding** of plastic enclosures for electronic devices.
3. **Zinc and aluminium sacrificial coatings** for corrosion protection.
4. **Hard-facing** of wear surfaces in industrial machinery.
5. **Anti-skid coatings** on walkways, decks, and ship floors.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write "arc spraying is used for corrosion protection" but *fail* to mention its specific relevance to **plastics decoration**, which is the core of the PEPOT864 syllabus. Always anchor your answer to the *course context* — plastic substrates and decorative finishes — to score full marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the role of **surface preparation** before arc spraying. Describe **grit blasting** and **bond coats** in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A twin-wire arc spray system is operated at an arc voltage of 28 V and arc current of 220 A with a thermal efficiency of 60%. The wire material is aluminium with melting point $T_m = 933$ K, latent heat $H_f = 397,000$ J/kg, specific heat $c_p = 900$ J/(kg·K), and ambient $T_0 = 303$ K. Calculate the **mass melting rate** in kg/h and the **deposition rate** (in kg/h) assuming 75% deposition efficiency.

#### Model Solution — (a) Surface Preparation, Grit Blasting & Bond Coats

**Why surface preparation is critical:** Arc-sprayed coatings bond to the substrate primarily by **mechanical interlocking**. A smooth, contaminated, or oxidized surface will yield poor adhesion. The substrate must therefore be cleaned, degreased, and roughened.

**Grit Blasting (Abrasive Blasting):**

- **Process:** Hard, angular abrasive particles (e.g., aluminium oxide, chilled iron grit, garnet) are propelled at high velocity (50–80 m/s) onto the substrate using compressed air in a suction- or pressure-blast cabinet.
- **Purpose:** Removes mill scale, rust, old coatings, and contaminants; creates a rough, angular surface profile with $R_a$ typically **50–100 µm**.
- **Standard:** ISO 8503-1 specifies surface cleanliness grades (Sa 1, Sa 2, Sa 2½, Sa 3). For arc spraying, **Sa 2½ or Sa 3** is recommended.
- **Critical rule:** Spray within **2–4 hours** of grit blasting to prevent re-oxidation of the freshly exposed surface.

**Bond Coats:**

- A **bond coat** is a thin (50–100 µm) intermediate layer sprayed first, typically of a more ductile, oxide-tolerant material such as **Ni-Al (nickel-aluminium) 95/5** or **Ni-Cr**.
- **Function:** Improves adhesion between the substrate and the top coat; accommodates thermal expansion mismatch; provides a slightly rough, oxide-receptive surface.
- **For plastic substrates:** A special **polymer-compatible bond coat** (e.g., a thin epoxy primer or a low-melt zinc-tin alloy layer) is used to promote adhesion without melting the plastic.

**Mark Distribution Hint (a):** Importance (2 marks) + Grit blasting details (3 marks) + Bond coat (2 marks).

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Numerical Problem

**Given:**
- $V = 28$ V, $I = 220$ A, $\eta_{th} = 0.60$
- Aluminium: $T_m = 933$ K, $H_f = 397,000$ J/kg, $c_p = 900$ J/(kg·K), $T_0 = 303$ K
- $\eta_d = 75\%$

**Step 1 — Compute useful power input.**

$$P_{useful} = \eta_{th} \cdot V \cdot I = 0.60 \times 28 \times 220 = 3{,}696 \; \text{W}$$

**Step 2 — Compute total specific enthalpy to melt wire from ambient to liquid.**

$$\Delta H_{total} = H_f + c_p(T_m - T_0)$$

$$\Delta H_{total} = 397{,}000 + 900 \times (933 - 303)$$

$$\Delta H_{total} = 397{,}000 + 900 \times 630 = 397{,}000 + 567{,}000$$

$$\Delta H_{total} = 964{,}000 \; \text{J/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate mass melting rate in kg/s.**

$$\dot{m}_{melt} = \frac{P_{useful}}{\Delta H_{total}} = \frac{3{,}696}{964{,}000} = 3.834 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{kg/s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kg/h.**

$$\dot{m}_{melt} = 3.834 \times 10^{-3} \times 3{,}600 = 13.80 \; \text{kg/h}$$

**Step 5 — Apply deposition efficiency to find coating deposition rate.**

$$\dot{m}_{coat} = \eta_d \times \dot{m}_{melt} = 0.75 \times 13.80 = 10.35 \; \text{kg/h}$$

> ✅ **Final Answers:** $\dot{m}_{melt} \approx 13.8$ kg/h, $\dot{m}_{coat} \approx 10.35$ kg/h.

**Mark Distribution Hint (b):**
- Step 1: 1 mark
- Step 2: 2 marks
- Step 3: 1 mark
- Step 4: 1 mark
- Step 5: 1 mark
- Final boxed answers: 1 mark

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Deductions:**
> 1. **Unit errors:** Forgetting to convert kg/s → kg/h at the end (lose 1 mark).
> 2. **Sign of enthalpy:** Using only $H_f$ and forgetting the $c_p \Delta T$ sensible-heat term (lose 1 mark).
> 3. **Deposition efficiency confusion:** Some students multiply by $\eta_d$ *before* converting units — order does not affect the final answer, but mixing kg/s and kg/h mid-calculation is a common mistake.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions:** Always state "assuming steady state" and "neglecting radiation losses" for full credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and keep it as your last-page revision sheet before the KTU exam.**

- ✅ **Arc spraying** is a thermal spray process using an **electric arc** as the heat source.
- ✅ The two wires are the **anode (+) and cathode (−)**; they are continuously fed and must be of the **same material**.
- ✅ Typical **arc parameters:** 100–300 A, 18–40 V, gas pressure 0.4–0.7 MPa.
- ✅ Arc temperature: **4,000–6,000 °C**; droplet size: **20–200 µm**; spray distance: **100–250 mm**.
- ✅ **Thermal efficiency = 60–80%**, which is the **highest** among wire-based thermal spray methods.
- ✅ **Deposition efficiency = 60–80%**; bond strength = **20–50 MPa**; porosity = **5–15%**.
- ✅ **Melting rate formula:** $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\eta_{th} \cdot V \cdot I}{H_f + c_p(T_m - T_0)}$ — memorize this!
- ✅ **Surface preparation is mandatory** — ISO 8503-1 Sa 2½ or Sa 3 + **bond coat** (Ni-Al) for metallic substrates.
- ✅ **For plastics (PEPOT864 context):** Use a **low-temperature bond coat**, optional cooling jets, and keep the substrate below its **heat-deflection temperature** (typically 80–110 °C for ABS, 130–150 °C for polycarbonate).
- ✅ **Key advantages:** high deposition rate, high efficiency, portable, low substrate heat input.
- ✅ **Key limitations:** conductive wire only, high oxide content (with air), coarser finish, lower bond strength than HVOF/plasma.
- ✅ **Decorative applications on plastics:** EMI shielding, automotive trim, appliance panels, consumer electronics metallization.
- ✅ **Never** use flame or combustion-based processes when coating plastics — arc spray with **inert gas (N₂ or Ar) atomization** is the preferred method for temperature-sensitive substrates.
- ✅ When asked to **compare** arc spray vs flame spray, always emphasize the **thermal efficiency, deposition rate, and substrate heat input** axes — these are the examiner's three favorite comparison points.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
