topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT864 - PLASTICS & DECORATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Plasma

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚛️ MODULE 3: PLASMA IN PLASTICS & DECORATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PLASMA (Fourth State of Matter):** Plasma is a quasi-neutral, partially ionized gas consisting of a complex mixture of free electrons, ions (positive and negative), neutral atoms, molecules, radicals, photons, and electromagnetic fields, exhibiting collective behaviour governed by long-range Coulombic forces.

In the context of **plastics and decoration engineering**, **plasma treatment** refers to a non-thermal, non-contact, dry surface modification process in which a low-temperature ionized gas is allowed to impinge upon a polymer surface, thereby altering its topmost molecular layers (typically 1–10 nm depth) without affecting the bulk polymer properties. This is achieved by the controlled interaction of energetic plasma species with the polymer chain, leading to the formation of new functional groups, surface crosslinking, chain scission, and micro-etching.

The principal **KTU syllabus deliverable** for this topic is to develop a working understanding of how cold plasma acts as a *pre-decorative activation step* for otherwise inert, low-surface-energy polymers such as **polyolefins (PP, PE), PET, PTFE, and polystyrene** — materials that would otherwise reject inks, paints, adhesives, and metallic coatings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is plasma used for plastics?**
> Most commodity plastics have a **low surface energy** (typically **29–35 mN/m for PE, 28–32 mN/m for PP**) compared to water (~72 mN/m) and most coating/adhesive systems (~38–45 mN/m). Without surface activation, decoration processes (printing, painting, vacuum metallization, adhesive bonding) fail due to poor **wettability** and weak **interfacial adhesion**. Plasma treatment elevates the surface energy to **≥ 50 mN/m**, restoring the printing/bonding efficiency.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are trying to stick a **waterproof label onto a smooth plastic lunchbox**. The label simply peels off because the plastic surface is chemically inert and slippery — water and glue cannot "grip" it.

Now imagine the same lunchbox is passed briefly through a **silent, cool, glowing purple-violet flame** (a corona/plasma discharge). You cannot feel any heat, but at the molecular level, the plasma has:

1. **Bombarded** the surface with energetic ions (knocking off weak boundary layers),
2. **Inserted** new polar chemical groups (-OH, -C=O, -COOH, -NH₂) — these act like molecular "velcro hooks",
3. **Roughened** the surface at the nanoscale (creating more area for glue to grab).

Now the same label sticks **firmly**, and ink or paint spreads **uniformly** without beading. The bulk plastic is unchanged; only the top few nanometres have been activated.

> **Real-world parallels of plasma:**
> - **Aurora Borealis** — solar plasma interacting with Earth's upper atmosphere.
> - **Neon signs** — low-pressure glow discharge plasma.
> - **Lightning** — transient thermal plasma.
> - **Plasma TV screens** (legacy) — pixelated glow discharge cells.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Boltzmann constant | **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K** | $k_B$ |
| Elementary charge | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** | $e$ |
| Ionization energy of Ar | **15.76 eV** | $E_i$ |
| Electron temperature (cold plasma) | **1–10 eV** (≈ 10⁴–10⁵ K) | $T_e$ |
| Gas temperature (cold plasma) | **300–500 K** (near room temp) | $T_g$ |
| Degree of ionization | **10⁻⁶ to 10⁻¹** | $\alpha$ |
| Treatment depth | **1–10 nm** | $d$ |
| Plasma frequency (electron) | $f_p = 9 \sqrt{n_e}$ Hz | $f_p$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Plasma (Operational Logic)

Plasmas are broadly classified along two engineering-relevant axes:

**(A) By thermodynamic equilibrium (Temperature):**

- **Thermal / Hot / Equilibrium Plasma** — $T_e \approx T_i \approx T_g \approx 10^4$ K. Examples: arc discharge, plasma torch, fusion plasma. **Not suitable for plastics** (would melt/degrade the polymer).
- **Non-Thermal / Cold / Non-Equilibrium Plasma** — $T_e$ (10,000–100,000 K equivalent) $\gg T_g$ (≈ 300–500 K). The electrons are "hot" enough to ionize/react, but the gas remains cool. **This is what is industrially used for plastics.** Examples: corona discharge, dielectric barrier discharge (DBD), low-pressure RF plasma, atmospheric pressure plasma jet (APPJ).

**(B) By pressure regime:**

- **Low-pressure (vacuum) plasma** — 0.01–10 mbar. Uniform, controlled, expensive (batch process).
- **Atmospheric-pressure plasma** — 760 Torr. Cheaper, in-line compatible, more localized.

## 2.2 Plasma–Polymer Interaction Mechanism

When energetic plasma species strike a polymer surface, **four simultaneous mechanisms** occur:

1. **Surface Activation (Functionalization):**
   Plasma-produced radicals (•OH, •OOH, •NH, •O) react with surface polymer radicals (formed by ion bombardment) to graft **polar functional groups** (C=O, C–OH, COOH, NH₂). This **increases the polar component of surface energy**.

2. **Surface Cleaning (Ablation of Contaminants):**
   UV photons and energetic ions volatilize organic contaminants, weak boundary layers, oils, and release trapped oligomers. This is a *physical-chemical cleaning*.

3. **Surface Etching (Nano-roughening):**
   Sputtering by ions + chemical etching by reactive species (O-atoms) roughens the surface at the nanometre scale, **increasing effective surface area** and providing mechanical interlocking sites (anchor profile).

4. **Cross-linking (CASING — Crosslinking by Activated Species of INert Gases):**
   Inert gas (He, Ar) plasmas create surface radicals that **recombine to form a cross-linked skin layer**, which improves barrier properties and adhesion by reducing weak boundary layers.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "CASING" mechanism** (proposed by Schonhorn & Hansen, 1966) explains why even **non-reactive noble gas plasmas** improve adhesion in polyolefins — the cross-linked skin is more receptive to adhesives than the original weak boundary layer.

## 2.3 Methods of Plasma Generation (Industrial)

| Method | Pressure | Frequency | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Corona Discharge** | Atmospheric | 10–30 kHz | In-line film treatment, sheet treatment |
| **Dielectric Barrier Discharge (DBD)** | Atmospheric | 1–100 kHz | 3D objects, large-area webs |
| **Atmospheric Pressure Plasma Jet (APPJ)** | Atmospheric | 13.56 MHz / 2.45 GHz | Localized treatment, robotics |
| **Low-pressure RF Plasma** | 0.01–10 mbar | 13.56 MHz | Batch, high uniformity, R&D |
| **Low-pressure Microwave Plasma** | 0.01–10 mbar | 2.45 GHz | High density, faster treatment |
| **Pulsed DC Glow Discharge** | Low / atmospheric | DC pulsed | Polymerization, grafting |

## 2.4 Process Parameters Affecting Treatment Quality

- **Power input (W)** — higher power → faster treatment, but risk of thermal damage (melting, yellowing, shrinkage).
- **Treatment time (s)** — typical industrial: **0.1–10 s** (corona) or **30–300 s** (low-pressure).
- **Distance electrode-to-substrate (mm)** — usually **1–5 mm**.
- **Process gas composition** — air, O₂, N₂, Ar, He, CF₄, NH₃ (each gives different chemistry).
- **Web speed (m/min)** — for in-line processes.
- **Substrate temperature** — should stay < $T_g$ of the polymer.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULAS** — These are the equations most frequently tested in KTU University Examinations for surface energy / wettability problems.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta$ | **Young's Equation** — equilibrium contact angle |
| 2 | $W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)$ | **Young-Dupré** — work of adhesion |
| 3 | $\gamma_s = \gamma_s^d + \gamma_s^p$ | **Fowkes-Owens decomposition** of surface energy |
| 4 | $(1+\cos\theta)\gamma_{lv} = 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^d \gamma_{lv}^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^p \gamma_{lv}^p}\right)$ | **Owens-Wendt-Rabel-Kaelble (OWRK)** — surface energy from 2 probe liquids |
| 5 | $S = \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl} - \gamma_{lv}$ | **Spreading coefficient** — $S \geq 0$ ⇒ spontaneous spreading |
| 6 | $\cos\theta = 1 - b(\gamma_c - \gamma_{lv})$ | **Zisman's empirical** — critical surface tension $\gamma_c$ |
| 7 | $f_p = 9\sqrt{n_e}$ Hz | **Plasma frequency** — electron density $n_e$ in m⁻³ |
| 8 | $E_i = \frac{13.6 \, Z_{\text{eff}}^2}{n^2}$ eV | **Ionization energy** (Hydrogen-like approximation) |
| 9 | $E_k = \frac{3}{2} k_B T_e$ | **Average electron kinetic energy** |
| 10 | $\alpha = \frac{n_e}{n_e + n_n}$ | **Degree of ionization** |

> **Engineering utility:** The **OWRK method (Equation 4)** is the *de facto standard* in industry to verify plasma treatment efficacy. A single contact angle measurement is *not enough*; two probe liquids (typically **diiodomethane** — purely dispersive — and **distilled water** — dispersive + polar) are used to solve for $\gamma_s^d$ and $\gamma_s^p$ simultaneously. The rise in $\gamma_s^p$ (polar component) is the direct evidence of plasma functionalization.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation convention used throughout:** $s$ = solid, $l$ = liquid, $v$ = vapour, $d$ = dispersive component, $p$ = polar component. All angles in **radians** inside equations, **degrees** for engineering results.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Young's Equation (Force Balance at Three-Phase Contact Line)

**Physical setup:** A liquid droplet rests on a solid surface in a vapour atmosphere. Three interfacial tensions act along the contact line:
- $\gamma_{sv}$ — solid–vapour (tries to spread the solid)
- $\gamma_{sl}$ — solid–liquid (resists wetting)
- $\gamma_{lv}$ — liquid–vapour (acts tangentially on the droplet surface)

**Step 1 — Horizontal force balance** (resolution along the solid surface plane, with the contact angle $\theta$ measured *inside* the liquid):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Horizontal pull by solid-vapour tension} &= \gamma_{sv} \\
\text{Horizontal pull by liquid-vapour tension} &= \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta \\
\text{Horizontal pull by solid-liquid tension} &= \gamma_{sl} \quad (\text{acting opposite to } \gamma_{sv})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Equilibrium condition** (vector sum = 0):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl} - \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearranging** to the canonical form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\therefore \quad \gamma_{sv} &= \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **Young's Equation (1805)**, the cornerstone of surface energetics.

> **Wettability interpretation:** Smaller $\theta$ ⇒ better wetting. $\theta = 0°$ ⇒ complete spreading; $\theta > 90°$ ⇒ non-wetting (e.g., water on PTFE = ~112°).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Work of Adhesion (Young-Dupré Equation)

**Definition:** The reversible work required to separate a unit area of liquid–solid interface into a unit area of solid–vapour + liquid–vapour interfaces.

**Step 1 — Energy balance for separation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_a &= \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Eliminate $\gamma_{sl}$ using Young's Equation** (rearranged: $\gamma_{sl} = \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_a &= \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - (\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta) \\
W_a &= \cancel{\gamma_{sv}} + \gamma_{lv} - \cancel{\gamma_{sv}} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta \\
W_a &= \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering implication:** Plasma treatment **decreases $\theta$**, which **increases $\cos\theta$**, and thus **increases $W_a$** — meaning the adhesive/coating binds more strongly to the polymer.

> **Sanity check:** For $\theta = 0°$ (complete wetting), $W_a = 2\gamma_{lv}$ (twice the cohesion energy of the liquid — pure spreading).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Owens-Wendt-Rabel-Kaelble (OWRK) Method

This is the most important formula for **quantifying plasma treatment efficiency** in KTU lab/practical viva questions.

**Starting point — Fowkes hypothesis (1964):** The solid–liquid interfacial tension can be decomposed into contributions from **dispersive (London) forces** and **polar forces (Keesom, Debye, H-bonding)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_{sl} &= \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - 2\sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{d}\gamma_{lv}^{d}} - 2\sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{p}\gamma_{lv}^{p}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute $\gamma_{sl}$ into Young's Equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_{sv} &= \left[\gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - 2\sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{d}\gamma_{lv}^{d}} - 2\sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{p}\gamma_{lv}^{p}}\right] + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Cancel $\gamma_{sv}$ on both sides and rearrange:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta) &= 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{d}\gamma_{lv}^{d}} + \sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{p}\gamma_{lv}^{p}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Experimental procedure (solving for two unknowns $\gamma_{sv}^{d}$ and $\gamma_{sv}^{p}$):**

Measure contact angles of **two probe liquids** with known $\gamma_{lv}^d$ and $\gamma_{lv}^p$ (Table of standard liquids provided in the next section). The system becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\gamma_{lv,1}(1+\cos\theta_1)}{2\sqrt{\gamma_{lv,1}^{d}}} &= \sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{d}} + \sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{p}}\cdot\sqrt{\frac{\gamma_{lv,1}^{p}}{\gamma_{lv,1}^{d}}} \\
\frac{\gamma_{lv,2}(1+\cos\theta_2)}{2\sqrt{\gamma_{lv,2}^{d}}} &= \sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{d}} + \sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{p}}\cdot\sqrt{\frac{\gamma_{lv,2}^{p}}{\gamma_{lv,2}^{d}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a system of two linear equations in $\sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{d}}$ and $\sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{p}}$, solvable by **substitution** or **Cramer's rule**.

**Step 4 — Total surface energy of solid:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_{sv}^{\text{total}} &= \gamma_{sv}^{d} + \gamma_{sv}^{p}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Standard probe liquids (must memorize for KTU lab/practical exam):**
>
> | Liquid | $\gamma_{lv}$ (mN/m) | $\gamma_{lv}^{d}$ | $\gamma_{lv}^{p}$ |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Distilled water** | 72.8 | 21.8 | 51.0 |
> | **Diiodomethane** | 50.8 | 50.8 | ≈ 0 |
> | **Glycerol** | 64.0 | 34.0 | 30.0 |
> | **Formamide** | 58.0 | 39.0 | 19.0 |
> | **Ethylene glycol** | 48.0 | 29.0 | 19.0 |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Plasma Frequency (Quasi-Neutrality Limit)

The **electron plasma frequency** is the natural oscillation frequency of free electrons displaced from the ionic background.

**Step 1 — Equation of motion for a displaced electron slab of density $n_e$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_e \frac{d^2 x}{dt^2} = -e E = -e \cdot \frac{n_e e x}{\varepsilon_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange into SHM form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d^2 x}{dt^2} + \frac{n_e e^2}{m_e \varepsilon_0} x &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the angular frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_p &= \sqrt{\frac{n_e e^2}{m_e \varepsilon_0}} \\
f_p &= \frac{\omega_p}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{n_e e^2}{m_e \varepsilon_0}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation (with $n_e$ in m⁻³):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_p \,[\text{Hz}] &\approx 9\sqrt{n_e \,[\text{m}^{-3}]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering meaning:** RF plasma sources (13.56 MHz, 2.45 GHz) operate **above** $f_p$, ensuring the bulk plasma responds collectively; below $f_p$, electromagnetic waves are **cut off** (evanescent).

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-Style Problem)

> **Problem:** A polyethylene (PE) film is plasma-treated and its contact angles with water and diiodomethane are measured as $\theta_{\text{water}} = 55°$ and $\theta_{\text{DIM}} = 28°$. Calculate the dispersive and polar components of the surface energy, the total surface energy, and the work of adhesion with water. (Compare with untreated PE having $\theta_{\text{water}} = 95°$.)

**Step 1 — Apply OWRK for water (liquid 1):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{72.8(1 + \cos 55°)}{2\sqrt{21.8}} &= \sqrt{\gamma_s^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_s^p}\cdot\sqrt{\frac{51.0}{21.8}} \\
\frac{72.8 \times 1.5736}{2 \times 4.669} &= \sqrt{\gamma_s^d} + 1.5295 \sqrt{\gamma_s^p} \\
\frac{114.58}{9.338} &= \sqrt{\gamma_s^d} + 1.5295\sqrt{\gamma_s^p} \\
12.27 &= \sqrt{\gamma_s^d} + 1.5295\sqrt{\gamma_s^p} \quad \text{...(i)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply OWRK for diiodomethane (liquid 2):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{50.8(1 + \cos 28°)}{2\sqrt{50.8}} &= \sqrt{\gamma_s^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_s^p}\cdot\sqrt{\frac{0}{50.8}} \\
\frac{50.8 \times 1.8829}{2 \times 7.127} &= \sqrt{\gamma_s^d} + 0 \\
\frac{95.65}{14.255} &= \sqrt{\gamma_s^d} \\
6.71 &= \sqrt{\gamma_s^d} \quad \text{...(ii)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\gamma_s^d$ and $\gamma_s^p$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_s^d &= (6.71)^2 = 45.0 \text{ mN/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting (ii) into (i):

$$
\begin{aligned}
12.27 - 6.71 &= 1.5295\sqrt{\gamma_s^p} \\
5.56 &= 1.5295\sqrt{\gamma_s^p} \\
\sqrt{\gamma_s^p} &= 3.635 \\
\gamma_s^p &= (3.635)^2 = 13.2 \text{ mN/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total surface energy of plasma-treated PE:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_s^{\text{total}} &= \gamma_s^d + \gamma_s^p = 45.0 + 13.2 = 58.2 \text{ mN/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Work of adhesion with water (Young-Dupré):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_a &= 72.8 \times (1 + \cos 55°) \\
W_a &= 72.8 \times 1.5736 \\
W_a &= 114.6 \text{ mN/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Comparison with untreated PE** ($\theta_{\text{water}} = 95°$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{a,\text{untreated}} &= 72.8 \times (1 + \cos 95°) = 72.8 \times 0.9128 = 66.4 \text{ mN/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** Plasma treatment increased the work of adhesion with water from **66.4 mN/m to 114.6 mN/m** — a **~72 % improvement**, validating plasma activation. (✓ Matches typical industrial data: $W_a$ for treated PE ≈ 100–120 mN/m.)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Industrial Plasma Treatment Line

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Unwinder] --> B[Tension Control Unit]
    B --> C[Plasma Treatment Station]
    C --> C1[Electrode Assembly]
    C --> C2[Gas Delivery Manifold]
    C --> C3[High Frequency Power Supply]
    C2 --> C1
    C3 --> C1
    C1 --> D[Cooling Roller]
    D --> E[Inline Quality Monitoring]
    E --> E1[Contact Angle Sensor]
    E --> E2[Surface Energy Meter]
    E --> F[Re-winder]
    F --> G[Slitting and Packaging]
```

> **Reading the flow:** Polymer film is unwound → tension-controlled → passed through the **plasma treatment zone** (electrodes + gas + RF power) → cooled → measured for surface energy in real-time → re-wound → finished. The inline quality sensors provide **closed-loop control** of the power input to compensate for any drift in treatment level (an essential feature in modern KTU-relevant Industry 4.0 lines).

## 4.2 Process Logic: Surface Modification Cascade

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Polymer Bulk: Inert, Low Surface Energy] --> Q1[Plasma Exposure Initiated]
    Q1 --> R1[Electron Impact Ionization]
    R1 --> R2[Radical Formation on Surface]
    R2 --> S1[Functional Group Insertion: OH, COOH, C=O, NH2]
    R2 --> S2[Crosslinking: CASING Layer Formation]
    R2 --> S3[Nano Etching: Roughness Increase]
    R2 --> S4[Contaminant Removal: Cleaning]
    S1 --> T1[Surface Energy Increase: Polar Component Up]
    S2 --> T2[Mechanical Integrity of Skin Layer Up]
    S3 --> T3[Mechanical Interlocking Sites Formed]
    S4 --> T4[Weak Boundary Layer Eliminated]
    T1 --> U1[Improved Wettability: Lower Contact Angle]
    T2 --> U1
    T3 --> U1
    T4 --> U1
    U1 --> V1[Strong Adhesion to Inks, Paints, Adhesives, Metals]
    V1 --> W1[Successful Decoration: Print, Coat, Metallize, Bond]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Architecture: Corona vs. DBD vs. Low-Pressure RF

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph CoronaSystem[Corona Discharge Setup]
        CA[Metallic Electrode] --> CB[Air Gap 1 to 3 mm]
        CB --> CC[Polymer Film]
        CC --> CD[Grounded Roll]
    end
    subgraph DBDSystem[Dielectric Barrier Discharge]
        DA[High Voltage Electrode] --> DB[Quartz Dielectric Barrier]
        DB --> DC[Discharge Gap]
        DC --> DD[Polymer Substrate]
        DD --> DE[Second Dielectric or Grounded Electrode]
    end
    subgraph LPSystem[Low Pressure RF Plasma]
        LA[RF Electrode 13.56 MHz] --> LB[Vacuum Chamber 0.01 to 10 mbar]
        LB --> LC[Polymer Substrate on Holder]
    end
```

| Architecture | Strength | Limitation | Typical KTU Industry Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Corona** | Fast, in-line, cheap | Non-uniform, limited gases | Polyolefin film, foil |
| **DBD** | Large area, 3D capable | Filamentary discharge | Furniture, automotive |
| **Low-Pressure RF** | Uniform, reproducible, clean | Batch, expensive | Aerospace, optics, R&D |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

### **Q1. Define plasma. Differentiate between thermal and non-thermal plasma with at least three points.** *(CO1, L1-L2)*

**Model Answer:**

Plasma is a quasi-neutral, ionized gas containing free electrons, ions, neutral particles, and radicals, often regarded as the **fourth state of matter**. It exhibits collective behaviour due to long-range electromagnetic interactions.

| Parameter | Thermal Plasma | Non-Thermal (Cold) Plasma |
|---|---|---|
| Thermodynamic state | Equilibrium ($T_e \approx T_i \approx T_g$) | Non-equilibrium ($T_e \gg T_g$) |
| Electron temperature | ~ $10^4$ K | 1–10 eV (10⁴–10⁵ K) |
| Gas temperature | ~ $10^4$ K | 300–500 K (near ambient) |
| Suitability for plastics | **Not suitable** (degrades polymer) | **Highly suitable** (preserves bulk) |
| Example | Arc torch, fusion plasma | Corona, DBD, low-pressure RF |

---

### **Q2. Explain the effect of plasma treatment on the surface energy of a polymer.** *(CO2, L2)*

**Model Answer:**

Plasma treatment increases the **surface energy** of a polymer (e.g., PE from ~31 mN/m to ≥ 55 mN/m). This is achieved by:

1. **Polar functional group insertion** — grafting of –OH, –COOH, C=O, –NH₂ groups, which raises the **polar component** ($\gamma_s^p$).
2. **Increase in surface roughness** (nano-etching) — raises the apparent surface energy via the **Wenzel equation** $\cos\theta_{\text{apparent}} = r \cos\theta_{\text{true}}$, where $r > 1$ is the roughness ratio.
3. **Removal of low-molecular-weight contaminants** — eliminates the weak boundary layer.

The net effect is a **decrease in water contact angle** and an **increase in work of adhesion**, enabling better wetting by inks, paints, and adhesives. **Limitation:** the effect is **not permanent** (hydrophobic recovery), and re-treatment may be required within hours to days (KTU FAQ).

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – December 2023 / Model Paper Pattern]**
> *Internal choice: Answer ANY ONE full question from each pair (A or B).*

---

### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) Explain the different methods of plasma generation used for polymer surface treatment with neat diagrams. Discuss their relative merits and demerits.** *(7 Marks) — CO2, L2-L3*

**Model Solution:**

The four industrially relevant plasma generation methods for polymers are:

**1. Corona Discharge Treatment (CDT):**

In a corona system, a **high-voltage (10–30 kV), high-frequency (10–30 kHz)** sinusoidal signal is applied between a **knife-edge metallic electrode** and a **grounded metal roller** (or counter-electrode). The polymer film/web passes through the narrow air gap (~1–3 mm). The strong non-uniform electric field at the sharp edge ionizes the air, producing a bluish-white corona discharge of cold plasma (mostly ions, electrons, and ozone). 

*Mechanism:* The plasma species interact with the polymer surface for a very short residence time (0.1–5 s in industrial lines), functionalizing and oxidizing the surface.

*Merits:* Continuous in-line, high throughput (up to 500 m/min), low cost, simple to integrate with extrusion/coating lines.

*Demerits:* Treatment uniformity is poor, requires single-side treatment, ozone generation is a health hazard, and treatment quality is operator-dependent (electrode aging).

**2. Dielectric Barrier Discharge (DBD):**

DBD uses **two electrodes separated by at least one dielectric barrier** (usually quartz, ceramic, or alumina). The polymer web is passed between the barrier and the counter-electrode. Operation is typically at 1–100 kHz with 5–30 kV amplitude. The dielectric limits current, preventing arc transition, and produces a **filamentary or diffuse micro-discharge** pattern.

*Merits:* Can treat both conductive and non-conductive surfaces, more uniform than corona, works on 3D objects.

*Demerits:* Filamentary DBD can cause local micro-pitting; complex electrode design.

**3. Low-Pressure Radio-Frequency (RF) Plasma:**

A **vacuum chamber** is evacuated to 0.01–10 mbar, and a process gas (O₂, Ar, N₂, air) is introduced at controlled flow. RF power at **13.56 MHz** (an ISM-protected frequency) is coupled to the gas via external or internal electrodes, generating a **uniform glow discharge plasma**. The polymer is placed on a grounded electrode (or in the afterglow region).

*Merits:* Excellent uniformity, reproducible, low power, works on complex 3D parts, allows use of exotic gases (CF₄, SF₆, NH₃).

*Demerits:* Batch process (slow), requires expensive vacuum systems, not in-line compatible.

**4. Atmospheric Pressure Plasma Jet (APPJ):**

A jet of carrier gas (He or Ar) is ionized by RF or microwave energy, and the plasma plume is directed via a nozzle onto the polymer surface. The substrate may be at atmospheric pressure and ambient temperature.

*Merits:* Localized treatment, robotic integration possible, no vacuum needed, works on 3D and uneven surfaces.

*Demerits:* Small treatment area, gas consumption is high.

**Summary Table:**

| Method | Pressure | Uniformity | Cost | Throughput |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Corona | Atmospheric | Low | Low | High |
| DBD | Atmospheric | Medium | Medium | High |
| RF Vacuum | Low | Very High | High | Low (batch) |
| APPJ | Atmospheric | Localized | Medium | Medium |

---

#### **(b) Discuss the role of plasma treatment as a pre-decoration step in plastic finishing. Mention any three industrial applications with examples.** *(7 Marks) — CO3, L3*

**Model Solution:**

Plastic decoration processes such as **printing, painting, vacuum metallization, adhesive bonding, and in-mould decoration** all require the polymer surface to have **high surface energy and good wettability** for the ink/paint/adhesive to spread uniformly and adhere strongly. Most commodity polymers (PE, PP, PTFE) are intrinsically **low-surface-energy, chemically inert, and non-polar**, leading to poor decoration. Plasma treatment is used as a **dry, environmentally friendly, in-line capable pre-treatment** to overcome this barrier.

**Three industrial applications:**

1. **Pre-treatment of polyolefin films for printing and lamination:**
   BOPP and LDPE films used in flexible packaging are corona- or DBD-treated just before the printing station. The treatment raises the dyne level (surface energy) from ~30 mN/m to ≥ 38 mN/m, ensuring **pinhole-free ink transfer** in flexographic and gravure printing. Example: snack packaging, milk pouches.

2. **Pre-treatment of automotive plastic components before painting:**
   PP bumpers and TPO fascias are plasma (DBD or APPJ) treated before the primer/painting step in OEM lines. The treatment **eliminates the need for solvent-based chlorinated primers**, reduces VOC emissions, and improves the cross-hatch adhesion rating from 2B to 5B (ASTM D3359). Example: Maruti, Tata Motors paint lines.

3. **Pre-treatment of polymer substrates before vacuum metallization:**
   PET and BOPP films used in the capacitor industry and decorative foils are low-pressure plasma-treated to enhance the **adhesion of the Al or Zn layer**. The plasma removes the top contamination layer, activates the surface, and provides a clean, polar interface for the evaporated metal. This improves **metal–polymer peel strength** and **shelf life** of the metallized film.

> **Conclusion:** Plasma treatment is now a **mandatory, environmentally compliant pre-decoration step** in nearly every industrial plastic finishing line, replacing wet-chemical primers and flame treatment.

---

### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **(a) Discuss in detail the four surface modification mechanisms produced by plasma on a polymer. Use a diagram to illustrate the effect of treatment time on contact angle (decay curve).** *(7 Marks) — CO2, L3-L4*

**Model Solution:**

When cold plasma interacts with a polymer surface, four overlapping modification processes occur:

**1. Surface Functionalization (Activation):**
Free radicals created on the polymer chain (by ion/electron impact) react with plasma-generated active species such as •OH, •O, •N, •NH. This grafts **oxygen- and nitrogen-containing polar groups** (–OH, –COOH, C=O, –NH₂) onto the surface. The polar component of surface energy $\gamma_s^p$ rises sharply, while the dispersive component $\gamma_s^d$ is largely unchanged.

**2. Surface Cleaning (Ablation of Contaminants):**
Oxygen plasma reacts with adsorbed hydrocarbons, oils, release agents, and migrating oligomers to form CO₂ and H₂O, which are pumped away. This eliminates the **weak boundary layer** (WBL) — a major cause of adhesive failure.

**3. Surface Etching (Nano-roughening):**
Physical sputtering (by energetic ions) and chemical etching (by O atoms) progressively erode the polymer, creating nano-scale roughness (Ra ~ 5–100 nm). This increases the **effective surface area** and provides mechanical interlocking, raising the apparent surface energy via the **Wenzel equation**:

$$
\cos\theta_{\text{apparent}} = r \cos\theta_{\text{true}}, \quad r = \frac{\text{Actual area}}{\text{Projected area}}
$$

**4. Cross-linking (CASING):**
Even inert gases (He, Ar) produce a thin cross-linked skin (~5–50 nm) on the surface due to radical–radical recombination after chain scission. This layer is **mechanically tougher** and **less permeable**, improving barrier and adhesion properties.

**Contact Angle Decay Curve (the classic plasma-treat signature):**

| Phase | Time | Contact Angle | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Induction** | 0 – t₁ | Slow change | Radical buildup |
| **Rapid decay** | t₁ – t₂ | Steep fall | Functionalization dominant |
| **Saturation** | > t₂ | Plateau | Equilibrium with degradation |
| **Over-treatment** | >> t₂ | May rise | Excessive etching, chain scission into bulk |

> **KTU critical insight:** The optimum treatment time is the *knee point* — beyond this, the surface is over-etched, the contact angle may *increase* again, and surface degradation (yellowing, embrittlement) sets in.

---

#### **(b) Explain the Owens-Wendt-Rabel-Kaelble (OWRK) method to determine the surface energy of a plasma-treated polymer using two probe liquids. A PP film after plasma treatment shows water contact angle 50° and diiodomethane contact angle 32°. Calculate $\gamma_s^d$, $\gamma_s^p$, and total surface energy. Comment on the effectiveness of the treatment.** *(7 Marks) — CO3, L4*

**Model Solution:**

**Theory recap:** The OWRK method decomposes total surface energy $\gamma_s$ into a **dispersive** component $\gamma_s^d$ and a **polar** component $\gamma_s^p$. Using two probe liquids of known $\gamma_{lv}^d$ and $\gamma_{lv}^p$, the contact angle data are solved to give both components.

**Step 1 — Apply the OWRK equation for water** ($\gamma_{lv} = 72.8$, $\gamma_{lv}^d = 21.8$, $\gamma_{lv}^p = 51.0$ mN/m, $\theta_1 = 50°$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\gamma_{lv,1}(1+\cos\theta_1)}{2\sqrt{\gamma_{lv,1}^{d}}} &= \sqrt{\gamma_s^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_s^p}\sqrt{\frac{\gamma_{lv,1}^{p}}{\gamma_{lv,1}^{d}}} \\
\frac{72.8(1+\cos 50°)}{2\sqrt{21.8}} &= \sqrt{\gamma_s^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_s^p}\sqrt{\frac{51.0}{21.8}} \\
\frac{72.8 \times 1.6428}{2 \times 4.669} &= \sqrt{\gamma_s^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_s^p}\times 1.5295 \\
\frac{119.6}{9.338} &= \sqrt{\gamma_s^d} + 1.5295\sqrt{\gamma_s^p} \\
12.81 &= \sqrt{\gamma_s^d} + 1.5295\sqrt{\gamma_s^p} \quad \text{...(i)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the OWRK equation for diiodomethane** ($\gamma_{lv} = 50.8$, $\gamma_{lv}^d = 50.8$, $\gamma_{lv}^p \approx 0$ mN/m, $\theta_2 = 32°$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{50.8(1+\cos 32°)}{2\sqrt{50.8}} &= \sqrt{\gamma_s^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_s^p}\sqrt{\frac{0}{50.8}} \\
\frac{50.8 \times 1.8480}{2 \times 7.127} &= \sqrt{\gamma_s^d} + 0 \\
\frac{93.88}{14.255} &= \sqrt{\gamma_s^d} \\
6.585 &= \sqrt{\gamma_s^d} \quad \text{...(ii)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\gamma_s^d$ and $\gamma_s^p$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_s^d &= (6.585)^2 = 43.4 \text{ mN/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting (ii) into (i):

$$
\begin{aligned}
12.81 - 6.585 &= 1.5295\sqrt{\gamma_s^p} \\
6.225 &= 1.5295\sqrt{\gamma_s^p} \\
\sqrt{\gamma_s^p} &= 4.069 \\
\gamma_s^p &= (4.069)^2 = 16.6 \text{ mN/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total surface energy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_s^{\text{total}} &= 43.4 + 16.6 = 60.0 \text{ mN/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Effectiveness comment:**
Untreated PP typically has $\gamma_s^{\text{total}} \approx 30$ mN/m with $\gamma_s^p \approx 1$ mN/m. After plasma treatment, the **total surface energy has roughly doubled (30 → 60 mN/m)**, with a *dramatic* rise in the polar component (1 → 16.6 mN/m). This confirms the grafting of polar oxygen-containing functional groups, and indicates that the PP film is now **highly suitable for printing, painting, and adhesive bonding** (target threshold for water-based inks is $\gamma_s^{\text{total}} \geq 38$ mN/m — easily met).

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students LOSE MARKS in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing thermal and non-thermal plasma** — Thermal plasma is *not* used for plastics; if a student writes "plasma is very hot" without specifying "non-equilibrium", full credit may be denied. Always state "cold/non-thermal plasma with $T_e \gg T_g$" for plastics.
>
> 2. **Mixing up $\gamma_{sv}$, $\gamma_{sl}$, $\gamma_{lv}$ in Young's equation** — Examiners *always* test sign convention. The correct form is $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$. Writing it backwards is a guaranteed **-1 mark** penalty.
>
> 3. **Forgetting units in OWRK calculation** — Answer must be in **mN/m** (or dyne/cm, where 1 mN/m = 1 dyne/cm). Numerical values alone without units fetch partial credit only.
>
> 4. **Ignoring hydrophobic recovery in answers** — Plasma treatment is *not permanent*. Examiners often ask "why does the dyne level drop after a few days?" Failing to mention the inward reorientation of polar groups and diffusion of oligomers loses **2 marks**.
>
> 5. **Not mentioning the depth of treatment (1–10 nm)** — A common viva question. If you claim "the surface is modified", specify that only the *top few nanometres* are affected; the bulk properties (Tₘ, Tg, MFI) remain unchanged.
>
> 6. **Over-claiming universality** — Plasma treatment does *not* work equally well on all polymers. Fluoropolymers (PTFE) need extended treatment or special gas chemistry. Know the limitations.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density, exam-ready summary — read this the night before the exam.**

### ⚛️ Plasma Fundamentals
- [ ] Plasma is the **4th state of matter**; quasi-neutral, ionized gas.
- [ ] For plastics, use **non-thermal (cold) plasma** — $T_e$ high, $T_g$ near ambient.
- [ ] Typical industrial treatment depth: **1–10 nm** (only the top molecular layers).
- [ ] Degree of ionization $\alpha = 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-1}$.

### 🔧 Generation Methods (must know all four)
- [ ] **Corona** — atmospheric, in-line, low cost, non-uniform.
- [ ] **DBD** — dielectric barrier, more uniform, 3D capable.
- [ ] **Low-pressure RF (13.56 MHz)** — most uniform, batch, expensive.
- [ ] **APPJ** — plasma jet, localized, robotic.

### 🧪 Surface Modification Mechanisms (the "Big Four")
- [ ] **Functionalization** (polar group insertion) — *increases $\gamma_s^p$*
- [ ] **Cleaning** (contaminant ablation) — *removes weak boundary layer*
- [ ] **Etching** (nano-roughening) — *increases area, Wenzel effect*
- [ ] **Crosslinking (CASING)** — *forms tough skin, even in inert gas plasma*

### 📐 Core Equations (must memorize & derive on demand)
- [ ] $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$ — **Young's**
- [ ] $W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1+\cos\theta)$ — **Young-Dupré**
- [ ] $\gamma_s = \gamma_s^d + \gamma_s^p$ — **Fowkes decomposition**
- [ ] OWRK: $(1+\cos\theta)\gamma_{lv} = 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^d\gamma_{lv}^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^p\gamma_{lv}^p}\right)$
- [ ] $f_p \approx 9\sqrt{n_e}$ Hz — **Plasma frequency**

### 🧷 Practical / Lab Knowledge
- [ ] Standard probe liquids: **water** (polar) and **diiodomethane** (dispersive).
- [ ] Surface energy threshold for water-based inks: **≥ 38 mN/m**.
- [ ] Untreated PP: $\gamma_s \approx 30$ mN/m; plasma-treated PP: $\gamma_s \approx 55$–65 mN/m.
- [ ] **Hydrophobic recovery** — treatment decays in hours–days; store in clean, dry, low-light conditions.
- [ ] **ASTM D3359** (cross-hatch adhesion) — target 5B post-plasma for paint systems.

### 🏭 Industrial Applications (must be able to give 3 with examples)
- [ ] **Printing & lamination** of BOPP/LDPE films (flexible packaging).
- [ ] **Automotive painting** of PP bumpers / TPO fascias.
- [ ] **Vacuum metallization** of PET films (capacitors, decorative foils).
- [ ] **Adhesive bonding** of PTFE, PE (medical, plumbing).
- [ ] **Bio-functionalization** of polymer scaffolds (tissue engineering).

### ❌ Common Misconceptions to AVOID
- [ ] Plasma does **not** melt the polymer (it's cold, non-thermal).
- [ ] Treatment is **not permanent** — hydrophobic recovery occurs.
- [ ] Plasma is **not** a coating — it modifies the existing surface.
- [ ] Not all polymers respond equally — fluoropolymers need special conditions.
- [ ] Plasma is **not** a substitute for cleaning — actually, it *is* a cleaning step (removes WBL).

> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"Cold plasma = electron-hot, gas-cold, surface-friendly. Functionalize, Clean, Etch, Crosslink. Raise the dyne level, lower the contact angle, save the polymer."* 🎯
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cosmetics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT864 - PLASTICS & DECORATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cosmetics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Cosmetics (Plastics & Decoration Context)

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cosmetics (KTU 2024 Definition):** Cosmetics are defined under the **Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940 (India)** and **EU Regulation 1223/2009** as any substance or mixture intended to be placed in contact with the external parts of the human body (epidermis, hair, nails, lips, external genital organs) or with the teeth and the mucous membranes of the oral cavity, with a view exclusively or mainly to cleaning them, perfuming them, changing their appearance, protecting them, keeping them in good condition, or correcting body odours.

From the **Plastics & Decoration engineering** perspective (KTU PEPOT864 Module 3), cosmetics represent one of the **largest end-use application sectors** for decorative plastics. Cosmetic products are the **finished formulated substances** (creams, lotions, lipsticks, nail enamels, powders, perfumes, deodorants) that are dispensed, contained, and aesthetically presented through **engineered plastic packaging systems** combined with **surface decoration techniques**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of cosmetics like a "dressed-up cake."**
> The cake itself is the **formulation** (the actual cream, lotion, or lipstick). But what makes you *want* to buy it? The **icing, the shiny box, the printed ribbon, the colour, the shape of the mould, and the gold lettering on the lid.** In the same way, the *real product* is the cosmetic, but the *buying decision* is made on the **plastic container** and its **decoration**. Therefore, plastics engineers design the jar/bottle, and decoration engineers apply the print, foil, embossing, and colour to make the cosmetic look luxurious and marketable.

## 1.3 Classification of Cosmetic Products

Cosmetics are broadly classified into the following categories, each requiring different plastic substrates and decoration strategies:

| S.No | Category | Example Products | Typical Plastic Used |
|------|----------|------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Skin Care** | Creams, lotions, moisturizers | PP, PE, PET jars and tubes |
| 2 | **Hair Care** | Shampoos, conditioners, hair oils | HDPE bottles, PET |
| 3 | **Colour Cosmetics** | Lipsticks, foundations, eye-shadow | ABS, SAN, acrylic compacts |
| 4 | **Nail Cosmetics** | Nail polish, nail enamels | Glass with PP cap, PETG |
| 5 | **Fragrances** | Perfumes, deodorants, body sprays | SAN, PETG, glass with PP collar |
| 6 | **Personal Hygiene** | Soaps, shower gels, toothpaste | HDPE, PP tubes |
| 7 | **Men's Grooming** | Shaving creams, beard oils | PET, PP, aluminium + plastic |

## 1.4 Historical Context

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Evolution of Cosmetic Packaging:**
> - **Ancient era (3000 BC – 1500 AD):** Ceramics, alabaster, gold/silver boxes for unguents and kohl.
> - **1900–1950:** Cellulose acetate, Bakelite (phenol-formaldehyde), early polyethylene.
> - **1950–1980:** Advent of **injection-moulded PP and PS**; tube packaging introduced.
> - **1980–2000:** **PET, PETG, multilayer coextrusion**, in-mould labelling (IML), hot stamping.
> - **2000–Present:** Eco-friendly bioplastics (PLA, PHA), 3D decoration, digital printing, refillable systems aligned with **sustainability and circular-economy principles**.

## 1.5 Why Cosmetics is a Critical Topic in Plastics & Decoration

The cosmetic industry consumes approximately **30–35% of all decorative plastic packaging** produced globally. The KTU 2024 syllabus positions cosmetics as a **high-value, high-volume application** where students must integrate:

- **Material selection** (polymer chemistry)
- **Processing technology** (injection blow moulding, extrusion blow moulding, injection moulding, tube forming)
- **Decoration techniques** (silk-screen printing, hot stamping, lacquering, metallization, IML, IMD)
- **Aesthetic engineering** (colour science, gloss, surface texture, tactile feel)
- **Regulatory compliance** (FDA, EU, BIS, ISO 22715)

## 1.6 Core Performance Requirements of Cosmetic Plastic Containers

A plastic cosmetic pack must satisfy the **"5-P Engineering Framework"**:

1. **Protection** – barrier against oxygen, moisture, UV light, microbial contamination.
2. **Preservation** – chemical inertness; no leaching; preservation of fragrance and actives.
3. **Presentation** – attractive shape, gloss, transparency/opacity, brand identity.
4. **Practicality** – ergonomic, easy to hold, dispense, close, and carry.
5. **Planet-friendly** – recyclable, refillable, lightweight, low carbon footprint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Any cosmetic plastic pack must be evaluated on these five P-parameters. Examination questions frequently test the *interrelationship* between the decoration technique and the performance of the plastic substrate.

## 1.7 Market Significance (Numbers to Remember)

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Global cosmetic packaging market (2024) | **USD 35–40 billion** |
| Plastic share within cosmetic packaging | **~65%** |
| Largest end-use polymer | **PP, PE, PET (combined > 75%)** |
| India cosmetic market (2024) | **USD 25+ billion**, growing at **CAGR 8–10%** |
| Decoration premium over plain pack | **15–40% higher shelf value** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Cosmetic Plastics Engineering

```
PILLAR 1: SUBSTRATE (Polymer Selection)
   ↓
PILLAR 2: SHAPING (Processing Technology)
   ↓
PILLAR 3: SURFACE (Decoration Engineering)
   ↓
FINAL COSMETIC PRODUCT (Container + Decoration + Formulation)
```

## 2.2 Pillar 1 – Polymer Selection for Cosmetics

### 2.2.1 Selection Criteria

The choice of polymer is governed by a **Weighted-Decision Matrix** considering:

- Chemical resistance to alcohol, oils, esters (perfume solvents)
- Oxygen and moisture barrier (Water Vapour Transmission Rate – WVTR)
- Transparency/clarity (for glass-like effect)
- Mechanical strength (drop, top-load, torque resistance)
- Processability (melt flow, mould shrinkage)
- Cost and recyclability
- Compatibility with **food-grade** or **cosmetic-grade** regulatory norms

### 2.2.2 Common Polymers – Property Matrix

| Polymer | Abbreviation | Clarity | Barrier to O₂ | Barrier to H₂O | Chemical Resistance | Cost Index | Typical Application |
|---------|--------------|---------|---------------|----------------|---------------------|------------|---------------------|
| Polyethylene Terephthalate | **PET** | Excellent | Good | Good | Good | 1.0 (baseline) | Bottles, jars |
| High-Density Polyethylene | **HDPE** | Translucent | Poor | Excellent | Excellent | 0.7 | Shampoo bottles |
| Polypropylene | **PP** | Translucent | Poor | Excellent | Excellent | 0.8 | Caps, tubes, jars |
| Polyvinyl Chloride | **PVC** | Transparent | Excellent | Good | Moderate | 0.9 | Compacts, tubes |
| Polystyrene | **PS / HIPS** | Transparent | Poor | Poor | Moderate | 0.8 | Dry cases, jars |
| Acrylic (PMMA) | **PMMA** | Crystal-clear | Good | Moderate | Poor | 1.4 | Luxury jars, caps |
| Acrylonitrile-Butadiene-Styrene | **ABS** | Opaque | Poor | Moderate | Good | 1.2 | Compact cases |
| Styrene-Acrylonitrile | **SAN** | Glossy-clear | Good | Moderate | Good | 1.3 | Perfume bottles |
| Polyethylene Naphthalate | **PEN** | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | 2.5 | Premium perfume |
| Glycol-Modified PET | **PETG** | Excellent | Good | Good | Good | 1.3 | Premium bottles |
| Polylactic Acid (Bio) | **PLA** | Excellent | Poor | Poor | Moderate | 1.6 | Eco-friendly jars |

## 2.3 Pillar 2 – Processing Technologies

### 2.3.1 Injection Blow Moulding (IBM)

Used for small bottles (lipstick, nail-polish removers, deodorant). Produces **bottles with no weld line, accurate neck finish, and excellent clarity**.

Process Flow:
1. **Injection** of molten polymer into a preform cavity (parison).
2. **Blowing** the parison inside a blow mould using compressed air.
3. **Ejection** of the finished bottle.

### 2.3.2 Extrusion Blow Moulding (EBM)

Used for **HDPE shampoo bottles, lotion containers**. A continuous parison is extruded, clamped in a mould, and inflated.

### 2.3.3 Stretch Blow Moulding (SBM)

Two-stage: PET preforms are re-heated and bi-axially stretched. Provides **high clarity, high strength, low weight**. Used for **PET perfume and lotion bottles**.

### 2.3.4 Injection Moulding (IM)

Used for **caps, closures, compacts, lipstick cases, mascara wands**.

### 2.3.5 Tube Extrusion

Used for **laminated and plastic tubes** (toothpaste, face-wash, hand cream). Modern tubes are **ABL (Aluminium Barrier Laminate)** or **PBL (Plastic Barrier Laminate)** with **EVOH** barrier.

### 2.3.6 Thermoforming

Used for **vacuum-formed compacts, sample trays, blister packs** of single-dose cosmetics.

## 2.4 Pillar 3 – Decoration Techniques (Core Module 3 Content)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decoration is what differentiates a "commodity pack" from a "premium cosmetic."** The same PET bottle can sell for ₹50 or ₹500 depending on the decoration.

### 2.4.1 Surface Preparation

Before any decoration, the plastic surface must be **pre-treated** to ensure ink/adhesive adhesion:

- **Flame treatment** (oxidises PE/PP surface; surface energy rises from ~31 dyne/cm to ~42 dyne/cm)
- **Corona treatment** (electrical discharge; raises surface energy)
- **Plasma treatment** (advanced; raises surface energy to >50 dyne/cm)
- **Priming** (chemical coupling agents, e.g., chlorinated polyolefins for PP)

### 2.4.2 Decoration Techniques – Comprehensive List

| S.No | Technique | Process Description | Typical Application |
|------|-----------|---------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Silk-Screen Printing** | Ink forced through mesh onto substrate; one colour per screen | Glass-effect bottles, opaque jars |
| 2 | **Pad Printing (Tampography)** | Silicone pad picks image from etched plate and transfers to 3-D surface | Mascara caps, lipstick bullets, curved jars |
| 3 | **Hot Stamping (Foil Stamping)** | Heated die presses metallised foil onto plastic | Gold/silver lettering on caps, luxury feel |
| 4 | **In-Mould Labelling (IML)** | Pre-printed label placed inside mould; becomes part of wall during moulding | Premium tubs, ice-cream containers |
| 5 | **In-Mould Decoration (IMD)** | Decorative film inserted into mould; back-injected with polymer | Mobile covers, dashboards (also used in cosmetics) |
| 6 | **Metallisation (Vacuum)** | Aluminium deposited under vacuum; gives mirror finish | Caps, decorative inserts |
| 7 | **Electroplating on Plastic** | ABS made conductive, then electroplated with Cr/Ni/Au | Cap clips, perfume collars |
| 8 | **Spray Lacquering** | Coloured or clear lacquer sprayed; baked | Frosted, glossy, soft-touch finishes |
| 9 | **Soft-Touch Coating (Velvet/Suede)** | Rubber-like coating gives velvety feel | Premium caps, deodorant buttons |
| 10 | **Digital Printing** | Inkjet direct-to-container; no label needed | Limited editions, mass customisation |
| 11 | **UV Screen Printing** | UV-cured inks; high scratch & chemical resistance | High-end bottles |
| 12 | **Embossing / Debossing** | Mechanical pressure creates raised/recessed pattern | Logo on caps, brand identity |
| 13 | **Heat Transfer Labelling (HTL)** | Pre-printed film transferred by heat + pressure | Cylindrical bottles |
| 14 | **Sleeve Labelling (Shrink Sleeve)** | Pre-printed plastic sleeve shrinks over container by heat tunnel | Tamper-evident, full-body decoration |
| 15 | **Decalcomania (Decals)** | Ceramic/paper decals; fired or cured | Perfume bottles |
| 16 | **Frosting (Acid Etch / Sandblast)** | Surface roughened to give frosted-glass look | Premium perfume bottles |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Equations

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are examinable. Master these for Part-A and Part-B questions.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Units | Application |
|---|---------------------|----------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | **Water Vapour Transmission Rate** | $\text{WVTR} = \dfrac{\Delta m}{A \cdot t}$ | $\mathrm{g/m^2 \cdot day}$ | Barrier performance of plastic |
| 2 | **Oxygen Transmission Rate** | $\text{OTR} = \dfrac{q \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta p \cdot t}$ | $\mathrm{cm^3 \cdot mm / m^2 \cdot day \cdot atm}$ | Shelf-life of perfumes, oils |
| 3 | **Surface Energy (Young's Equation)** | $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$ | $\mathrm{dyne/cm}$ or $\mathrm{mN/m}$ | Adhesion of inks/coatings |
| 4 | **Mould Shrinkage** | $S = \dfrac{L_{mould} - L_{part}}{L_{mould}} \times 100\%$ | % | Dimensional accuracy of jar |
| 5 | **Blow-Up Ratio (Blow Moulding)** | $\text{BUR} = \dfrac{D_{bottle}}{D_{parison}}$ | dimensionless | Bottle quality control |
| 6 | **Stretch Ratio (SBM)** | \text{SR} = \dfrac{L_{stretched}}{L_{original}} \times \dfrac{D_{bottle}}{D_{preform}} | dimensionless | PET orientation |
| 7 | **Drop-Impact Energy** | $E_d = m g h$ | $\mathrm{J}$ or $\mathrm{N \cdot m}$ | Bottle drop test |
| 8 | **Top-Load Strength** | $\sigma_{TL} = \dfrac{F_{cr}}{\pi D t}$ | $\mathrm{N/mm^2}$ | Bottle stack strength |
| 9 | **Gloss (Reflectance)** | $G = \dfrac{I_{reflected}}{I_{incident}} \times 100$ | % (at 60° or 20°) | Surface quality |
| 10 | **Adhesion Test (Cross-Cut)** | Pass/Fail based on ASTM D3359 | — | Coating adhesion |
| 11 | **Recycled Content Ratio** | $R = \dfrac{m_{recycled}}{m_{total}} \times 100\%$ | % | Sustainability scoring |
| 12 | **Mass of Container (Wall)** | $m = \rho \cdot \pi D L t$ | $\mathrm{g}$ | Material cost estimation |
| 13 | **Coextrusion Layer Ratio** | $L_r = \dfrac{t_{barrier}}{t_{total}}$ | dimensionless | Barrier layer design |
| 14 | **Curing Time (UV Ink)** | $t_c = \dfrac{E_{req}}{I_{lamp}}$ | $\mathrm{s}$ | UV printing line speed |
| 15 | **Haze (Optical Clarity)** | $\text{Haze (\%)} = \dfrac{I_{diffuse}}{I_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Transparency of PET/PMMA |

### 2.5.1 Critical Boundary Conditions

- **PET stretch ratio** must be **2.0–2.5 ×** axially and **3.0–4.0 ×** circumferentially to obtain **strain-induced crystallinity** (bottle strength).
- **Surface energy** for good ink adhesion must be **> 38 dyne/cm** after treatment.
- **Gloss value** for premium cosmetics is typically **> 90 GU at 60°**.
- **WVTR** of cosmetic-grade PP must be **< 0.5 g/m²·day** to preserve actives.
- **OTR** of a perfume-grade PET bottle must be **< 1.0 cm³·mm/m²·day·atm**.

### 2.5.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Decoration Used | Polymer | Reason |
|-------------|----------------|---------|--------|
| Nivea Creme Tin | Litho printing + varnish | Tinplate lid / HDPE jar | Brand recognition |
| Lakme Compact | Spray-paint + hot-stamp logo | ABS + mirror | Premium feel |
| L'Oreal Shampoo | Sleeve label + silk screen | HDPE + PE sleeve | Tamper-evidence + 360° branding |
| Maybelline Mascara | Pad printing | PP cap | Curved surface decoration |
| Chanel Perfume | Electroplated cap + frosted bottle | Glass + ABS + Cr plating | Ultra-luxury positioning |
| Mamaearth Face Wash | IML | PP | Eco + durable |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 – Bottle Wall Mass Calculation

A **200 mL PET cosmetic bottle** has a cylindrical body of diameter **50 mm**, length **120 mm**, and average wall thickness **0.35 mm**. Calculate the mass of the bottle (excluding neck and base).

### Step-by-Step:

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry**

The bottle wall is approximated as a thin cylindrical shell. The relevant variables are:

- Diameter of the cylinder: $D = 50 \ \mathrm{mm}$
- Cylinder length: $L = 120 \ \mathrm{mm}$
- Wall thickness: $t = 0.35 \ \mathrm{mm}$
- Density of PET: $\rho_{PET} = 1.38 \ \mathrm{g/cm^3}$

**Step 2 — Convert all dimensions to consistent units (mm → cm)**

$$D = 50 \ \mathrm{mm} = 5.0 \ \mathrm{cm}$$

$$L = 120 \ \mathrm{mm} = 12.0 \ \mathrm{cm}$$

$$t = 0.35 \ \mathrm{mm} = 0.035 \ \mathrm{cm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the surface area of the cylindrical wall**

The lateral (side) surface area of a cylinder is:

$$A = \pi \cdot D \cdot L$$

Substituting values:

$$A = \pi \times 5.0 \ \mathrm{cm} \times 12.0 \ \mathrm{cm}$$

$$A = 60\pi \ \mathrm{cm^2} \approx 188.5 \ \mathrm{cm^2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the volume of the wall material**

Wall volume is the area multiplied by the wall thickness:

$$V = A \cdot t = \pi \cdot D \cdot L \cdot t$$

$$V = 188.5 \ \mathrm{cm^2} \times 0.035 \ \mathrm{cm}$$

$$V \approx 6.60 \ \mathrm{cm^3}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the mass of the wall**

$$m = \rho_{PET} \cdot V$$

$$m = 1.38 \ \mathrm{g/cm^3} \times 6.60 \ \mathrm{cm^3}$$

$$\boxed{m \approx 9.11 \ \mathrm{g}}$$

**Step 6 — Add base and shoulder mass (typical 25–35% extra)**

For a real bottle, base + neck + shoulder add roughly **30%** to the cylindrical mass:

$$m_{total} = 1.30 \times 9.11 \ \mathrm{g} \approx 11.84 \ \mathrm{g}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** A lightweight 200 mL PET cosmetic bottle weighing ~12 g uses only **0.012 kg of polymer** to deliver a **luxury product worth ₹200–500**. The **value-add of decoration** (printing, hot-stamping, metallisation) often **doubles the perceived value** without changing the substrate mass.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 – Surface Energy Condition for Adhesion (Young–Dupré Equation)

The **Young–Dupré equation** predicts the **work of adhesion** between a liquid ink and a solid plastic surface. A cosmetic jar must satisfy a minimum work of adhesion for the print to last.

**Step 1 — Start with Young's Equation**

$$\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$$

Where:
- $\gamma_{SV}$ = surface energy of the solid (plastic) in equilibrium with vapour
- $\gamma_{SL}$ = interfacial energy between solid and liquid (ink)
- $\gamma_{LV}$ = surface tension of the liquid (ink)
- $\theta$ = contact angle

**Step 2 — Express the work of adhesion**

According to the Young–Dupré equation, the work of adhesion $W_a$ is:

$$W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values**

Suppose an ink with surface tension $\gamma_{LV} = 35 \ \mathrm{mN/m}$ forms a contact angle $\theta = 25^{\circ}$ on a corona-treated PP surface.

$$\cos 25^{\circ} = 0.9063$$

$$W_a = 35 \times (1 + 0.9063) \ \mathrm{mJ/m^2}$$

$$W_a = 35 \times 1.9063$$

$$\boxed{W_a \approx 66.72 \ \mathrm{mJ/m^2}}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the threshold for good adhesion**

A **work of adhesion greater than 50 mJ/m²** is generally considered acceptable for cosmetic packaging. Since $66.72 \ \mathrm{mJ/m^2} > 50 \ \mathrm{mJ/m^2}$, the **ink will adhere adequately** to the corona-treated PP.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** Surface treatment (corona, flame, plasma) is **always required for PE and PP** because their as-moulded surface energy is only **29–31 mN/m** — far too low for inks to wet properly. Treatment raises it above 40 mN/m.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 – Drop-Impact Energy Requirement for a Bottle

A filled shampoo bottle has **mass 250 g**. It must survive a drop from **1.2 m** onto a hard surface. Compute the impact energy the bottle must absorb.

**Step 1 — Identify the variables**

$$m = 250 \ \mathrm{g} = 0.25 \ \mathrm{kg}$$

$$g = 9.81 \ \mathrm{m/s^2}$$

$$h = 1.2 \ \mathrm{m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the potential energy equation**

$$E_d = m g h$$

**Step 3 — Substitute**

$$E_d = 0.25 \times 9.81 \times 1.2$$

$$\boxed{E_d = 2.943 \ \mathrm{J}}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation**

The bottle and its contents (and the decoration coating) must absorb at least **2.94 J** of energy without cracking. Common mitigations:
- Use of **impact-modified HDPE** (with rubber modifier).
- Designing a **panel-free cylindrical body** to avoid stress concentrations.
- Choosing a **PETG** body for higher impact strength than PET.
- Ensuring decoration (paint/foil) is **flexible** to avoid cracking on impact.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 – Blow-Up Ratio for an IBM Bottle

A preform has outer diameter **22 mm** and the final bottle has body diameter **55 mm**. Find the **Blow-Up Ratio (BUR)**.

**Step 1 — Recall the definition**

$$\text{BUR} = \frac{D_{bottle}}{D_{parison}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$\text{BUR} = \frac{55}{22}$$

$$\boxed{\text{BUR} = 2.5}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering interpretation**

A BUR between **2.0 and 3.0** is considered optimal for PET bottles. It provides:
- Good molecular orientation (strength)
- Acceptable wall thinning
- Uniform material distribution

If BUR > 3.5, the wall becomes too thin and may haze. If BUR < 1.8, the bottle may be cloudy and have poor clarity.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 – Surface Energy Threshold Calculation (Crispin–Klasson Model)

The **Crispin–Klasson model** is widely used in the printing industry to predict the **critical surface tension** of a plastic surface for a given ink.

$$\gamma_c = \gamma_{ink} \cdot \left[1 - \left(\frac{\cos\theta_{ink} - 1}{\cos\theta_{ink} + 1}\right)^2 \right]$$

For a UV-curable ink with $\gamma_{ink} = 30 \ \mathrm{mN/m}$ and contact angle $\theta_{ink} = 18^{\circ}$ on treated PP:

$$\cos 18^{\circ} = 0.9511$$

**Step 1 — Compute the term inside the brackets**

$$\frac{0.9511 - 1}{0.9511 + 1} = \frac{-0.0489}{1.9511} \approx -0.02507$$

**Step 2 — Square the term**

$$(-0.02507)^2 \approx 0.000629$$

**Step 3 — Subtract from 1**

$$1 - 0.000629 = 0.999371$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by $\gamma_{ink}$**

$$\gamma_c = 30 \times 0.999371$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_c \approx 29.98 \ \mathrm{mN/m}}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation**

The critical surface tension required is essentially equal to the ink's surface tension (~30 mN/m). This means the PP surface must be **treated to at least 30 mN/m** for the ink to spread. Standard corona or flame treatment of PP achieves 38–44 mN/m, comfortably exceeding this requirement.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix – Selecting a Decoration Technique

The following matrix helps an engineer **choose a decoration technique** for a given cosmetic pack:

| Criterion | Silk Screen | Pad Print | Hot Stamp | IML | Sleeve Label | Metallisation | Soft-Touch |
|-----------|:----------:|:---------:|:---------:|:---:|:------------:|:-------------:|:----------:|
| Suitable for flat surface | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Suitable for curved surface | ✗ | ✓✓ | ✗ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Multi-colour capable | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✗ | ✗ |
| Metallic finish | ✗ | ✗ | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✗ |
| Recyclable (mono-material) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ |
| Cost per unit (low–high) | Low | Low | Med | High | Med | High | Med |
| Durability (scratch/chemical) | Med | Med | High | High | High | High | Med |
| Volume suitability | Med-High | Low-Med | Med | High | High | Low-Med | Med |

(✓✓ = best, ✓ = acceptable, ✗ = not suitable)

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU 2024 – Part A (3-Mark Short-Answer Questions)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style] 

**Define cosmetics. List any four categories of cosmetics and give one example of a plastic material used in their packaging.**

**Model Answer:**

> Cosmetics are substances or mixtures intended to be applied to the external parts of the human body (skin, hair, nails, lips) for the purposes of cleansing, beautifying, perfuming, or protecting them, as defined under the **Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940** and **EU Regulation 1223/2009**.

**Four categories and plastic materials:**

1. **Skin Care** – e.g., Creams, moisturisers, lotions → packaged in **PP jars** or **PET bottles**.
2. **Hair Care** – e.g., Shampoos, conditioners → packaged in **HDPE bottles**.
3. **Colour Cosmetics** – e.g., Lipsticks, foundations → packaged in **ABS compacts** and **SAN lipstick cases**.
4. **Fragrances** – e.g., Perfumes, deodorants → packaged in **SAN/PETG bottles** with **ABS metallised caps**.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

**Differentiate between In-Mould Labelling (IML) and In-Mould Decoration (IMD). State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | In-Mould Labelling (IML) | In-Mould Decoration (IMD) |
|-----------|--------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Process** | A pre-printed **paper or plastic label** is placed inside the mould; the polymer is injected behind it, fusing the label into the wall. | A pre-printed **decorative film** (often with multiple layers) is inserted in the mould; the polymer is injection-moulded behind it. |
| **Layer thickness** | Label thickness: **100–300 µm** | Film thickness: **200–500 µm** |
| **Durability** | High – label becomes part of container | Very high – 3-D textured effects possible |
| **Recyclability** | Limited (label is often PP, but contamination possible) | Limited (multi-layer) |
| **Cost** | Medium | Higher |
| **Advantage** | **High print quality, scratch-resistant, no secondary printing line required** | **3-D textures, metallic effects, anti-scratch surfaces achievable in one operation** |

---

## 5.2 KTU 2024 – Part B (14-Mark Questions) – ESE Module Choice

> [!NOTE]
> KTU end-semester exam Part-B carries 14 marks per question, with **internal choice**. Each question typically has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Below, **Question A and Question B** are two **mutually exclusive 14-mark questions**; the student answers either one.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Module 3 Internal Choice]

**Q.A. (a)** Explain in detail the various **polymer substrates** used in cosmetic packaging, with a comparison of their barrier, mechanical, optical, and chemical-resistance properties. (7 marks)

**Q.A. (b)** Describe the **process, advantages, and applications of In-Mould Labelling (IML)** in cosmetic plastic packaging. Compare it with **sleeve labelling** in terms of recyclability, durability, and cost. (7 marks)

#### Model Answer – Q.A (a)

The selection of a polymer for cosmetic packaging is governed by the **5-P Engineering Framework** (Protection, Preservation, Presentation, Practicality, Planet-friendly). Major polymers used are:

1. **PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate):**
   - Clarity: Excellent (crystal clear, glass-like)
   - Barrier (O₂): Good; (H₂O): Good
   - Chemical resistance: Good against alcohols and oils
   - Used in: Lotion bottles, perfume bottles (via Stretch Blow Moulding)
   - Recyclability: Code **#1**, widely recycled

2. **HDPE (High-Density Polyethylene):**
   - Clarity: Translucent (opaque)
   - Barrier (H₂O): Excellent
   - Chemical resistance: Excellent
   - Impact strength: Excellent
   - Used in: Shampoo bottles, conditioner bottles (via Extrusion Blow Moulding)
   - Recyclability: Code **#2**

3. **PP (Polypropylene):**
   - Heat resistance: Excellent (autoclavable, hot-fill possible)
   - Barrier (H₂O): Excellent
   - Used in: Caps, closures, tubs, tubes
   - Recyclability: Code **#5**

4. **PVC (Polyvinyl Chloride):**
   - Clarity: Transparent
   - Barrier (O₂): Excellent
   - Used in: Cosmetic compacts, blister packs
   - Note: Environmental concerns → shifting to PET and PP

5. **ABS, SAN, PMMA, PETG, PEN:**
   - Used in **premium** cosmetic packaging (lipstick cases, perfume bottles)
   - ABS → metallisable
   - PMMA → luxury feel, scratch resistance
   - PETG → easy moulding, high clarity

**Comparative table** (as in Section 2.2.2) should be drawn in the answer to fetch full marks.

#### Model Answer – Q.A (b)

**In-Mould Labelling (IML) – Detailed Process:**

1. A **pre-printed label** (PP, PE, or PET-based) is produced on a **gravure or offset printing press** with brand colours, graphics, and even metallic foils.
2. The label is **automatically placed inside the injection mould** cavity by a robotic arm.
3. Molten polymer (e.g., PP) is **injected behind the label**, fusing it with the container wall.
4. The result is a **single integrated part** with the label fully embedded — no secondary printing step.

**Advantages of IML:**
- High print resolution (up to **1200 dpi**)
- Scratch, water, and chemical resistance (the label is protected by the polymer layer)
- Wide design range: metallic effects, wood, leather, fabric textures
- Reduced labour and line cost (printing is offline)
- Suitable for food and cosmetic contact (food-grade inks)

**Disadvantages:**
- High mould and tooling cost
- Limited to **injection-moulded** parts
- Difficult to recycle in mono-material streams (unless label = same polymer as container)

**Sleeve Labelling – Process:**
A pre-printed **polyolefin or PET film sleeve** is slipped over the container and **heat-shrunk** in a heat tunnel.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | IML | Sleeve Label |
|-----------|-----|--------------|
| Durability | Excellent (label inside wall) | Good (label on surface) |
| Print Quality | Very high | High |
| Recyclability | Mono-material possible (PP-on-PP) | Difficult (sleeve is different polymer) |
| Cost (low-volume) | Higher tooling | Lower tooling |
| Cost (high-volume) | Competitive | Competitive |
| Application | Premium tubs, ice-cream containers | Shampoo bottles, tamper-evident packs |

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Module 3 Internal Choice – Alternative]

**Q.B. (a)** Explain the principles of **surface preparation** (corona, flame, plasma) of plastic cosmetic containers prior to decoration. How do these treatments improve ink adhesion? (7 marks)

**Q.B. (b)** Describe **vacuum metallisation** and **electroplating on plastic** as decoration techniques for cosmetics. Compare their end-use applications and durability. (7 marks)

#### Model Answer – Q.B (a)

Plastic surfaces — particularly polyolefins (PE, PP) — have **low surface energy** (29–31 mN/m), which is **below the surface tension of most inks** (30–40 mN/m). As a result, inks **do not wet** the surface, producing poor adhesion, beading, and print failure.

**Surface preparation techniques:**

1. **Corona Treatment:**
   - The plastic surface is exposed to a **high-voltage, high-frequency electrical discharge** in air.
   - Oxygen is ionised and reacts with the polymer surface, creating **polar groups (C=O, –OH, –COOH)**.
   - Surface energy rises from ~31 mN/m to **40–45 mN/m**.
   - Used widely for **PE and PP films and bottles** in line with printing.

2. **Flame Treatment:**
   - The plastic passes over an **oxidising flame** (typically 1200–2000 °C).
   - The flame **oxidises** the polymer surface, similar mechanism to corona.
   - Surface energy rises to **40–46 mN/m**.
   - Used for **3-D objects** (bottles, tubes, caps).

3. **Plasma Treatment:**
   - The plastic is exposed to **low-pressure or atmospheric plasma** (oxygen, argon, nitrogen).
   - Most **effective and controllable** treatment; surface energy can exceed **50 mN/m**.
   - Also used to apply **nano-coatings** for functional surfaces (anti-fog, anti-bacterial).

4. **Priming (Chemical Coupling):**
   - A thin layer of **chlorinated polyolefin (CPO) or polyurethane** is applied.
   - Provides a **wetting layer** for ink and adhesive.
   - Common for **PP bumpers** and cosmetic jars.

**Why adhesion improves:**

The **Young–Dupré equation**:

$$W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$$

As $\theta$ decreases (better wetting), $W_a$ (work of adhesion) **increases**, leading to stronger ink–substrate bonding.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write "corona treatment increases roughness" — this is **partially correct** but not the main mechanism. The correct answer should emphasise **surface energy increase via polar group formation**. Marks are lost if the *chemistry* of the surface is not explained.

#### Model Answer – Q.B (b)

**1. Vacuum Metallisation:**
- The plastic part (e.g., ABS cap, PETG insert) is placed inside a **vacuum chamber**.
- A small amount of **aluminium wire** is heated to ~1400 °C, **evaporating** it.
- The aluminium vapour **condenses** on the cooler plastic surface, forming a **uniform metallic film of 50–100 nm**.
- A **clear top-coat lacquer** is then applied to protect the metal layer.
- The result is a **mirror-like silver finish** that can be tinted gold, rose-gold, or coloured.

**Applications:** Lipstick cases, mascara caps, deodorant buttons, perfume bottle collars, decorative inserts.

**2. Electroplating on Plastic:**
- The plastic part is made of **ABS** (preferred because butadiene rubber domains can be chemically etched to form micro-cavities).
- Surface is **etched** (chromic-sulphuric acid), then **sensitised and activated** with palladium.
- **Electroless plating** deposits a thin layer of copper or nickel.
- The part is then placed in an **electrolytic bath** where **Cr, Ni, Ag, or Au** is electrodeposited to a thickness of 5–25 µm.
- Result: a **durable, multi-layer metallic finish** (Cu + Ni + Cr or Au) with high corrosion resistance.

**Applications:** Premium perfume caps, lipstick cases, fashion accessory packaging.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Vacuum Metallisation | Electroplating on Plastic |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------------------|
| Metal layer | Aluminium (thin, 50–100 nm) | Cu/Ni/Cr/Au (thick, 5–25 µm) |
| Durability | Moderate (needs top-coat) | Very high (true metallic thickness) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Visual effect | Mirror finish | True metallic, can be polished |
| Adhesion | Moderate | Excellent (interlocked with ABS) |
| Recyclability | Easier (thin metal layer) | Harder (metal-plastic composite) |
| Common use | Mid-range cosmetics | Luxury cosmetics (Chanel, Dior) |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in the KTU Board Exam:**
> 1. **Confusing IML with IMD** — they are *not* synonyms. IML uses a printed label; IMD uses a pre-formed decorative film.
> 2. **Writing "corona treatment makes the surface rough"** — the primary mechanism is **chemical oxidation**, not just physical roughening.
> 3. **Forgetting units** in numerical problems (especially g vs. kg, dyne/cm vs. mN/m).
> 4. **Skipping the substrate–decoration compatibility** — for example, suggesting vacuum metallisation on PE (not possible; PE cannot hold a uniform metal layer).
> 5. **Not mentioning recyclability** — KTU 2024 scheme emphasises sustainable design; an answer without a "recycling" mention loses at least 1 mark.
> 6. **Wrong polymer codes** — remember: PET = #1, HDPE = #2, PVC = #3, LDPE = #4, PP = #5, PS = #6.
> 7. **Mixing up WVTR and OTR** — WVTR is for water vapour (g/m²·day); OTR is for oxygen (cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cosmetics in Plastics & Decoration (KTU 2024, Module 3)**

- **Definition:** Cosmetics = external-body applications for cleaning, beautifying, perfuming, protecting (per Drugs & Cosmetics Act, 1940; EU 1223/2009).
- **Major categories:** Skin care, hair care, colour cosmetics, nail care, fragrances, personal hygiene.
- **Core polymers:** PET, HDPE, PP, PVC, PS, ABS, SAN, PMMA, PETG, PEN, PLA (bio).
- **Polymer codes:** PET #1, HDPE #2, PVC #3, LDPE #4, PP #5, PS #6.
- **Key processing technologies:** Injection moulding, blow moulding (IBM, EBM, SBM), tube extrusion, thermoforming.
- **BUR (Blow-Up Ratio):** $D_{bottle}/D_{parison}$ — optimum 2.0–3.0.
- **Surface treatment is mandatory** for PE/PP before printing; target surface energy **> 38 mN/m**.
- **Decoration techniques:** Silk screen, pad print, hot stamping, IML, IMD, metallisation (vacuum + electroplating), lacquering, soft-touch, digital print, sleeve label, embossing, frosting.
- **IML ≠ IMD:** IML uses a paper/plastic label; IMD uses a film with textures.
- **Vacuum metallisation:** thin Al layer (50–100 nm); needs top-coat.
- **Electroplating on plastic:** uses ABS; deposits Cu + Ni + Cr/Au (5–25 µm); high durability.
- **Critical formulas:** WVTR, OTR, Young's equation, work of adhesion (Dupré), mould shrinkage, drop impact energy, BUR, gloss, haze.
- **Barrier hierarchy for cosmetics:** PEN > EVOH > PET > PVC > PP > HDPE > LDPE.
- **Drop impact energy:** $E_d = mgh$ — used in bottle drop tests.
- **5-P framework:** Protection, Preservation, Presentation, Practicality, Planet-friendly.
- **Sustainability keywords:** mono-material, refillable, recyclable, PCR (post-consumer recycled), bio-based PLA, lightweighting.
- **Quality tests:** Drop test, top-load test, gloss, haze, adhesion (cross-cut), WVTR, OTR, torque resistance.
- **Premium cosmetic cues:** Mirror finish (metallisation), soft-touch cap, gold hot-stamp, heavyweight base, glass-clear bottle (PETG/PMMA).
- **Market fact to remember:** Cosmetics consume ~30–35% of all decorative plastic packaging globally.
- **Regulatory standards:** ISO 22715 (cosmetic packaging), FDA 21 CFR, EU 1223/2009, BIS IS 4011.

---

**End of Module 3 – Cosmetics Notes | PEPOT864 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
