# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Why is electroless plating considered the most critical step in plating on plastics?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Electroless plating is the critical step because it transforms the inherently **non-conductive plastic surface into an electrically conductive layer** without any external current. This is achieved through an **autocatalytic chemical reduction** reaction where hypophosphite-reduced **nickel (Ni²⁺ → Ni⁰)** deposits onto Pd-activated nucleation sites. Once this continuous Ni-P film (≈ 0.5 – 1 µm) is formed, subsequent electrolytic deposition of Cu, Ni, and Cr becomes possible. Without this conductive seed layer, electroplating cannot occur at all. Thus, electroless plating bridges the gap between insulator and conductor, making it the *make-or-break* step of the entire plating-on-plastics process. *(3 marks: 1 for stating the need, 1 for the chemistry, 1 for the consequence if skipped.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"What is the role of chromic-sulphuric acid in the plating of plastics?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The chromic-sulphuric acid mixture is used in the **etching stage** to create microscopic anchor cavities on the otherwise smooth plastic surface. The **CrO₃ preferentially attacks the polybutadiene rubber phase dispersed within the ABS matrix**, producing a porous, matte surface. This controlled chemical roughening provides **mechanical interlocking** for the subsequent metallic layers, ensuring strong adhesion between the plastic substrate and the plated metal film. Without this etching step, the metal coating would delaminate due to the absence of any physical interlocking. *(3 marks: 1 for naming stage, 1 for mechanism, 1 for outcome.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** List and explain the **complete sequence of steps involved in plating on plastics**, mentioning the chemical reagent used and the purpose of each step.

**(b) [7 marks]** A plastic substrate of **0.05 m² area** is to be plated with **30 µm of Nickel**. Calculate the **time required** if a current of **5 A** is passed through a nickel sulphamate bath operating at **92 % current efficiency**. Given: M(Ni) = 58.7 g/mol, n = 2, ρ(Ni) = 8900 kg/m³, F = 96485 C/mol.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

The steps are presented below in chronological order:

1. **Pre-cleaning (Alkaline Degreasing):** Removal of mould release agents and oils using NaOH + Na₂CO₃ + surfactants at 50–60 °C. *(1 mark for reagent + purpose)*
2. **Etching:** Chromic acid + sulphuric acid bath at 60–70 °C, 5–12 min, creates micro-cavities by preferentially dissolving the polybutadiene phase of ABS. *(1 mark)*
3. **Neutralization:** Sodium bisulphite bath reduces toxic Cr(VI) to Cr(III) and removes residual chromic acid. *(1 mark)*
4. **Pre-dip:** 1–3 % HCl solution prevents dilution of the activator. *(0.5 mark)*
5. **Catalyzation/Activation:** PdCl₂ + SnCl₂ in HCl forms colloidal Pd⁰ nuclei on the surface. *(1 mark)*
6. **Acceleration:** Dilute H₂SO₄ or NaOH bath removes the Sn(OH)₄ shell to expose active Pd⁰. *(0.5 mark)*
7. **Electroless Plating:** NiSO₄ + NaH₂PO₂ bath deposits 0.5–1 µm of Ni-P autocatalytically, making the surface conductive. *(1 mark)*
8. **Electroplating:** Sequential deposition of bright acid Cu (20–35 µm), semi-bright Ni, bright Ni, and microporous Cr using DC current. *(0.5 mark)*
9. **Rinsing & Drying:** Inter-stage cascade rinses and final hot-air drying. *(0.5 mark)*

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Given:**
- A = 0.05 m²
- h = 30 µm = 30 × 10⁻⁶ m
- I = 5 A
- η = 0.92
- M(Ni) = 58.7 g/mol
- n = 2
- ρ(Ni) = 8900 kg/m³
- F = 96485 C/mol

**Step 1:** Compute current density:

$$ \text{C.D.} = \frac{I}{A} = \frac{5}{0.05} = 100 \text{ A/m}^{2} $$

**Step 2:** Apply the time formula:

$$ t = \frac{h \cdot n \cdot F \cdot \rho}{M \cdot \text{C.D.} \cdot \eta} $$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$ t = \frac{(30 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 2 \cdot 96485 \cdot 8900}{58.7 \cdot 100 \cdot 0.92} $$

**Step 4:** Compute numerator:

$$ (30 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 2 \cdot 96485 \cdot 8900 = 5.153 \times 10^{7} $$

**Step 5:** Compute denominator:

$$ 58.7 \cdot 100 \cdot 0.92 = 5400.4 $$

**Step 6:** Divide:

$$ t = \frac{5.153 \times 10^{7}}{5400.4} \approx 9541.7 \text{ seconds} $$

$$ t \approx 2.65 \text{ hours} \approx 2 \text{ hours } 39 \text{ minutes} $$

**Final Answer:** **≈ 9542 seconds (≈ 2.65 hours).** *(Full 7 marks for systematic substitution and unit conversion.)*

---

### QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **chemistry of electroless nickel plating** on plastics. Include the role of the palladium catalyst, the reducing agent, and write the balanced reaction equation.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **function of the etching, neutralization, and acceleration steps** with their respective chemical equations.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

Electroless nickel plating on plastics is an **autocatalytic redox process**. The plastic surface, after Pd-Sn activation, contains millions of catalytic Pd⁰ nuclei per cm². When immersed in an **alkaline electroless nickel bath** containing **nickel sulphate (NiSO₄·6H₂O)** as the metal source and **sodium hypophosphite (NaH₂PO₂·H₂O)** as the reducing agent, the following events occur:

1. **Hypophosphite adsorption on Pd nuclei.**
2. **Oxidation of hypophosphite to orthophosphite** with release of electrons.
3. **Reduction of Ni²⁺ to Ni⁰** by these electrons.
4. **Co-deposition of Phosphorus (P)** giving Ni-P alloy (typically 3–12 % P).

**Balanced Reaction:**

$$ \text{Ni}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{PO}_2^{-} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{Pd catalyst}} \text{Ni}^{0} + \text{H}_2\text{PO}_3^{-} + 2\text{H}^{+} $$

**Side Reaction (hydrogen evolution):**

$$ \text{H}_2\text{PO}_2^{-} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{PO}_3^{-} + \text{H}_2 \uparrow $$

The Pd⁰ catalyst is **regenerated and never consumed** — hence the term "autocatalytic." Operating conditions: pH 8.5–9.5, temperature 85–95 °C, deposition rate 12–25 µm/hour. *(7 marks: 2 for the role of Pd, 2 for reducing agent, 2 for the balanced equation, 1 for operating conditions.)*

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

1. **Etching:** Chromic-sulphuric acid dissolves the butadiene rubber phase of ABS creating cavities:

$$ \text{Polybutadiene} + \text{CrO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \longrightarrow \text{Cavities} + \text{Cr}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

The Cr(VI) is reduced to Cr(III) during oxidation of the polymer.

2. **Neutralization:** Residual Cr(VI) is removed by sodium bisulphite:

$$ \text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-} + 3\text{HSO}_3^{-} + 5\text{H}^{+} \longrightarrow 2\text{Cr}^{3+} + 3\text{SO}_4^{2-} + 4\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

3. **Acceleration:** The Sn(OH)₄ shell surrounding Pd nuclei is dissolved:

$$ \text{Sn(OH)}_4 + 2\text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SnO}_3 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

This exposes active Pd⁰ sites, ensuring uniform initiation of the subsequent electroless nickel reaction. *(7 marks: 2 marks for each step's explanation, 1 mark for the chemical equation.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Do not** confuse "Acceleration" with "Activation." Acceleration comes *after* activation.
> - **Do not** write only one equation and skip the others — KTU awards 1 mark per equation.
> - In numericals, **always show unit conversion explicitly**. Writing m instead of µm is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> - **Do not** swap the sequence: etching must come before activation, never after.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Plastic plating = Insulator → Conductor → Metallic Finish.** Remember this three-stage paradigm.
- The **9 universal steps** are: Cleaning → Etching → Neutralization → Pre-dip → Catalyzation → Acceleration → Electroless Plating → Electroplating → Rinsing/Drying.
- **ABS** is the gold-standard platable plastic due to its etchable butadiene phase.
- **Pd-Sn colloid** seeds the surface; **acceleration** removes the Sn shell.
- **Hypophosphite-reduced electroless Ni** provides conductivity with 3–12 % P content.
- **Acid Cu (20–35 µm) → Semi-bright Ni → Bright Ni → Cr** is the standard decorative sequence.
- **Faraday's Law** governs thickness; always include **η (current efficiency)** in calculations.
- **Chromic-sulphuric acid** is the etchant; **NaHSO₃** is the neutralizer.
- **Inter-stage rinses** are non-negotiable — they determine final quality.
- Numerical problems always require explicit **C.D. = I/A** computation first.
- Three rinses minimum, two cascade counter-flow preferred, deionized water mandatory for final rinse.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Finishing Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT864 - PLASTICS & DECORATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Finishing Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Plastics & Decoration (PEPOT864)**, **Finishing Methods** refer to the comprehensive set of **post-production operations** performed on plastic substrates, printed sheets, or finished moulded plastic articles to enhance their **aesthetic appeal**, **functional performance**, **surface durability**, and **commercial marketability**.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus framework, finishing methods are classified into three functional pillars:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim):**
> *Finishing is the final stage of decoration/manufacturing that imparts protective, decorative, or functional properties to a plastic or printed surface through mechanical, chemical, or thermal means.*

| **Finishing Class** | **Primary Function** | **Example Process** |
|---|---|---|
| **Protective Finishing** | Resistance to wear, scratch, UV | Varnishing, Lamination |
| **Decorative Finishing** | Aesthetic enhancement | Foil Stamping, Embossing |
| **Functional Finishing** | Adds usability features | Die-cutting, Perforation |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of finishing methods as the **"makeover and armor"** given to a plastic product after its main body is ready. Imagine you bake a plain sponge cake (the molded plastic part) — the cake itself is edible but plain. Before serving it for a special occasion, you:
- Cover it with chocolate (Lamination/Varnish — protective coating)
- Write "Happy Birthday" with golden icing (Foil Stamping — decorative)
- Cut it into heart shapes using a stencil (Die-cutting — functional)

Similarly, finishing methods transform a raw, plain plastic or printed article into a **market-ready, visually striking, and durable product**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> A plain **PVC credit card** becomes a premium product after:
> - Lamination (protects the chip)
> - Gloss varnish (gives shine)
> - Embossing of card numbers (functional/decorative)

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Standard Temperature for Lamination:** **120°C – 150°C**
- **Standard Pressure for Hot Foil Stamping:** **0.5 – 2.0 MPa**
- **Standard Varnish Coat Weight:** **2 – 5 gsm (grams per square meter)**
- **Industry Standard Lamination Film Thickness:** **15 – 30 microns**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Finishing Methods — Structured Breakdown

The finishing operations in Plastics & Decoration can be organized into **five major families**:

### 2.1.1 Surface Coating Methods
These involve the application of a liquid or film coating onto the plastic surface.

- **Varnishing:** A liquid coating (oil-based, water-based, or UV-cured) applied via rollers or spray to provide gloss, matte, or satin finish.
- **Lacquering:** A higher-build version of varnish providing superior chemical resistance.
- **Aqueous Coating:** Water-based, eco-friendly coating with quick-drying properties.

### 2.1.2 Film Lamination
Bonding a thin polymeric film onto the substrate using **heat and pressure** or **adhesives**.

- **Types:** BOPP (Biaxially Oriented Polypropylene) lamination, PET lamination, Matte lamination, Soft-touch lamination.
- **Function:** Increases tear strength by **200–400%**, improves moisture barrier.

### 2.1.3 Foil Stamping (Hot Stamping)
A metallic or pigmented foil is transferred to the surface using a heated die.

- **Process Temperature:** **100°C – 160°C**
- **Process Pressure:** **0.5 – 2.0 MPa**
- **Dwell Time:** **0.5 – 2.0 seconds**

### 2.1.4 Embossing & Debossing
Mechanical deformation of the substrate to create raised (emboss) or recessed (deboss) designs.

- **Multi-level Embossing:** Creates depth gradients (used in premium packaging).
- **Registered Embossing:** Aligned with pre-printed graphics.

### 2.1.5 Converting Operations
Mechanical finishing that alters the physical shape or structure.

- **Die-cutting:** Cutting complex shapes using sharp steel dies.
- **Slitting:** Cutting rolls into narrower widths.
- **Perforation:** Creating tear lines for easy opening.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|
| $GSM = \dfrac{M}{A}$ | Grams per Square Meter: weight $M$ over area $A$ | g/m² |
| $T_{total} = T_{substrate} + T_{film}$ | Total laminate thickness | microns (µm) |
| $\eta_{lamination} = \dfrac{F_{peel\,laminated}}{F_{peel\,unlaminated}}$ | Lamination efficiency ratio | dimensionless |
| $C_{finishing} = C_m + C_l + C_e$ | Total finishing cost (material + labor + energy) | ₹/unit |
| $P_{emboss} = \dfrac{F_{emboss}}{A_{relief}}$ | Embossing pressure intensity | MPa |
| $\rho_{coverage} = \dfrac{A_{coated}}{A_{total}} \times 100$ | Coating coverage percentage | % |
| $S_{cut} = \dfrac{n_{good\,cuts}}{n_{total\,cuts}} \times 100$ | Die-cut yield percentage | % |
| $D_{dwell} = \dfrac{L_{sheet}}{v_{press}}$ | Dwell time (length / press speed) | seconds |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The **dwell time** $D_{dwell}$ is critical — if it's too short, the foil won't bond; if too long, the substrate will scorch. Always verify $D_{dwell}$ against the substrate's **glass transition temperature** ($T_g$).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical Packaging:** UV-varnished blister packs with foil stamping for branding.
- **Automotive Interiors:** Embossed PVC dashboards with scratch-resistant lacquer.
- **Cosmetics:** Soft-touch lamination on PP tubes for premium tactile feel.
- **Smart Cards:** Multi-layer PET lamination with registered embossing of card numbers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Coating Weight (GSM) Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A printed plastic sheet measuring **500 mm × 700 mm** is coated with varnish. After coating and drying, the **weight gain** is **1.75 g**. Determine the **varnish coat weight in GSM**.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Convert dimensions to meters.

$$
L = 500 \, \text{mm} = 0.5 \, \text{m}
$$

$$
W = 700 \, \text{mm} = 0.7 \, \text{m}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the total sheet area.

$$
A = L \times W = 0.5 \times 0.7 = 0.35 \, \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the GSM formula.

$$
GSM = \frac{M}{A} = \frac{1.75 \, \text{g}}{0.35 \, \text{m}^2} = 5.0 \, \text{g/m}^2
$$

**Conclusion:** The varnish coat weight is **5.0 GSM**, which falls within the standard range of **2–5 GSM** for spot varnish and **4–8 GSM** for overall varnish.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Dwell Time for Hot Foil Stamping

**Problem Statement:** A hot foil stamping press operates at a speed of **2000 sheets/hour**. Each sheet length is **450 mm**. Calculate the **dwell time** available for foil transfer.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Identify the formula.

$$
D_{dwell} = \frac{L_{sheet}}{v_{press}}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert press speed into consistent units (mm/second).

$$
v_{press} = \frac{2000 \, \text{sheets}}{1 \, \text{hour}} = \frac{2000 \times 450 \, \text{mm}}{3600 \, \text{s}} = 250 \, \text{mm/s}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values into the formula.

$$
D_{dwell} = \frac{450 \, \text{mm}}{250 \, \text{mm/s}} = 1.8 \, \text{s}
$$

**Conclusion:** The dwell time is **1.8 seconds**, which is within the optimum range of **0.5–2.0 seconds** for hot foil stamping on plastic substrates.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Die-Cut Yield Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A die-cutting machine produces **10,000 labels** in a shift. Of these, **9,750** meet the quality standard. Calculate the **die-cut yield percentage**.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Apply the yield formula.

$$
S_{cut} = \frac{n_{good\,cuts}}{n_{total\,cuts}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$
S_{cut} = \frac{9750}{10000} \times 100 = 97.5\%
$$

**Conclusion:** The die-cut yield is **97.5%**. Industry benchmark for premium labels is **≥ 98%**, so this shift is slightly below target — likely due to die wear or substrate variation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection Guide for Finishing Methods

| **Substrate Type** | **Recommended Finishing** | **Reason** |
|---|---|---|
| **PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate)** | UV Varnish + BOPP Lamination | High surface energy allows strong adhesion |
| **PVC (Polyvinyl Chloride)** | Soft-touch Lamination | Enhances tactile feel |
| **PP (Polypropylene)** | Corona-treated + Water-based Varnish | PP has low surface energy; needs pretreatment |
| **PE (Polyethylene)** | Flame treatment + Hot Foil Stamping | Needs surface activation for ink/foil bonding |
| **ABS / Acrylic** | Spray Lacquering | Smooth surface accepts high-build coatings |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Always check the **dyne level** (surface energy) of the substrate before selecting a finishing method. A dyne level of **≥ 38 dynes/cm** is required for proper lamination adhesion.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Finishing Process Line

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Plastic Substrate] --> B[Surface Pretreatment]
    B --> C{Selection of Finishing Route}
    C -->|Protective| D[Coating / Lamination Unit]
    C -->|Decorative| E[Foil Stamping / Embossing Unit]
    C -->|Functional| F[Die-Cutting / Perforation Unit]
    D --> G[UV Curing Tunnel]
    E --> H[Heated Die Press]
    F --> I[Sharp Tool Die Station]
    G --> J[Quality Inspection Module]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K{Pass Quality Check?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Finished Product Output]
    K -->|No| M[Rejection / Rework Bin]
```


```mermaid
    node2 --> node3[Drying Tunnel]

    node4 --> node5[Plastic Substrate Infeed]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Process Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start{End-Use Requirement} --> p1[Need Scratch Resistance]
    start --> p2[Need Premium Look]
    start --> p3[Need Easy Tear]
    p1 --> q1[UV Varnish]
    p1 --> q2[PET Lamination]
    p2 --> q3[Hot Foil Stamping]
    p2 --> q4[Soft-touch Lamination]
    p3 --> q5[Perforation]
    p3 --> q6[Laser Scoring]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Finishing" in the context of Plastics & Decoration. List any four types of finishing methods. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Finishing refers to the final stage operations performed on a plastic or printed substrate to enhance its **aesthetic appearance**, **functional performance**, and **protective durability**. It is carried out after the primary forming or printing process.

**Four Types (1.5 Marks):**
1. **Varnishing** — liquid coating for gloss/matte finish
2. **Lamination** — polymeric film bonding for protection
3. **Foil Stamping** — metallic/pigmented foil transfer
4. **Embossing** — raised or recessed surface relief

---

### Question 2: What is the role of "dwell time" in hot foil stamping? State the typical range. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Role (2 Marks):**
Dwell time is the **duration for which the heated die remains in contact with the foil and substrate**, allowing proper heat transfer, adhesive activation, and bonding of the foil layer to the surface. Insufficient dwell time leads to poor adhesion; excessive dwell time causes substrate scorching or foil discoloration.

**Typical Range (1 Mark):**
**0.5 to 2.0 seconds** for plastic substrates.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain in detail the process of "Lamination" as a finishing method for plastic substrates. Discuss the types of lamination films and their specific applications.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition & Principle (2 Marks):**
Lamination is the process of bonding a thin polymeric film onto the plastic substrate using **heat, pressure, and adhesive** to provide protection and aesthetics. The base principle involves **thermal activation of adhesive** followed by **pressure consolidation** through nip rollers.

**2. Types of Lamination Films (3 Marks):**

| **Film Type** | **Properties** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|
| **BOPP (Biaxially Oriented PP)** | High clarity, cost-effective | Book covers, cartons |
| **PET Film** | High strength, gloss | Premium packaging, ID cards |
| **Matte BOPP** | Non-glare, soft feel | Luxury cosmetics, brochures |
| **Soft-touch Film** | Velvety tactile feel | Premium boxes, electronics |
| **Metalized Film** | Metallic appearance | Decorative labels, gift wrap |

**3. Process Parameters (2 Marks):**
- **Temperature:** **120°C – 150°C**
- **Pressure:** **0.3 – 0.8 MPa**
- **Speed:** **20 – 80 m/min**
- **Adhesive Type:** EVA (Ethylene Vinyl Acetate) based

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a Hot Foil Stamping machine. List the common defects encountered.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Model Solution:**

**1. Working Principle (3 Marks):**
A heated metal die (brass or magnesium) is pressed against a metallic foil sandwiched between the die and the substrate. The heat activates the **adhesive layer of the foil**, transferring the metallic/pigmented coating onto the substrate.

**2. Block Diagram (2 Marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    partA[Foil Roll Unwinder] --> partB[Substrate Infeed]
    partB --> partC[Heated Die Press]
    partC --> partD[Foil Transfer Zone]
    partD --> partE[Carrier Film Rewinder]
    partE --> partF[Finished Sheet Delivery]
```

**3. Common Defects (2 Marks):**
- **Poor Foil Transfer:** Due to insufficient temperature or dwell time
- **Foil Wrinkling:** Improper tension control
- **Substrate Distortion:** Excessive heat
- **Ghosting/Shadowing:** Misalignment of die registration

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Discuss "Embossing" and "Debossing" as decorative finishing methods. Compare their effects on plastic substrates with a suitable diagram.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**1. Embossing (3 Marks):**
Embossing creates a **raised design** on the substrate by pressing it between a male die and a female counter-die. The substrate undergoes **plastic deformation** below its softening point.

**2. Debossing (2 Marks):**
Debossing is the **inverse process**, creating a **recessed design** by pushing the substrate into a female die.

**3. Comparison (2 Marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Embossing** | **Debossing** |
|---|---|---|
| **Relief Type** | Raised | Recessed |
| **Visual Impact** | High prominence | Subtle elegance |
| **Material Thickness Needed** | ≥ 200 gsm | ≥ 250 gsm |
| **Common Use** | Greeting cards, premium boxes | Leatherette covers |

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**What is Die-cutting? Explain the different types of dies used. Calculate the die-cut yield if 9850 out of 10000 labels are accepted.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition (2 Marks):**
Die-cutting is a **converting process** where custom-shaped steel blades (called dies) are used to cut plastic or paper substrates into specific shapes with high precision.

**2. Types of Dies (3 Marks):**
- **Flat-Bed Dies:** Mounted on a platen; used for thick materials
- **Rotary Dies:** Cylindrical; high-speed continuous operation
- **Magnetic Dies:** Flexible; for short-run digital finishing

**3. Yield Calculation (2 Marks):**

$$
S_{cut} = \frac{9850}{10000} \times 100 = 98.5\%
$$

This exceeds the industry benchmark of **98%**, indicating excellent die-cut performance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **lose 2 marks** by not stating the **unit** of GSM or not specifying the **temperature range** in foil stamping.
> - In die-cut yield problems, **always show the formula first** before substituting values — examiners specifically check for this.
> - Avoid **confusing "Embossing" with "Debossing"** in diagrams — the die orientation must be correctly drawn.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Finishing methods** are categorized into **Protective**, **Decorative**, and **Functional**.
- ✅ **GSM formula:** $GSM = M/A$ — always in **g/m²**.
- ✅ **Lamination temperature:** **120°C – 150°C**; pressure: **0.3 – 0.8 MPa**.
- ✅ **Hot Foil Stamping:** Dwell time **0.5 – 2.0 s**, temperature **100°C – 160°C**.
- ✅ **Embossing** = Raised relief; **Debossing** = Recessed relief.
- ✅ **Die-cut yield benchmark:** ≥ **98%** for premium labels.
- ✅ **BOPP** is the most common lamination film; **PET** for premium strength.
- ✅ **PP and PE substrates** require **surface pretreatment** (corona/flame) before finishing.
- ✅ **Dyne level** of substrate should be **≥ 38 dynes/cm** for proper adhesion.
- ✅ **Common defects in foil stamping:** Poor transfer, wrinkling, ghosting, substrate scorching.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minite KTU Revision Tip:**
> Memorize the **3 standard parameter sets** — Lamination (T, P, Speed), Foil Stamping (T, P, Dwell), and Die-Cut (Yield formula). These three alone account for **~60% of Part B marks** in Finishing Methods questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vacuum Metallizing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT864 - PLASTICS & DECORATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vacuum Metallizing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Vacuum Metallizing** is a physical vapor deposition (PVD) process in which a thin metallic film — most commonly **aluminium (Al)** — is vaporised under high-vacuum conditions and allowed to condense uniformly onto the surface of a plastic (or other) substrate to produce a brilliant, reflective, decorative and/or functional metallic finish.

The process is governed by the principles of **kinetic theory of gases**, **vacuum technology** and **thin-film physics**. The standard chamber pressure range is **$10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$ Torr** ($1 \text{ Torr} \approx 133.32 \text{ Pa}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Vacuum metallizing is classified under *Surface Decoration Techniques* in Module 4 of PEPOT864. Students must remember that the process is purely **physical** (no chemical reaction with the substrate) and is distinguished from *electroplating on plastics* (which is electrochemical).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Frosted Window" Effect

Imagine you breathe onto a cold glass window on a winter morning — a thin film of water vapour from your breath *condenses* on the cold surface, forming a uniform layer.

Vacuum metallizing works on exactly the same principle, with three modifications:

1. Instead of water vapour, you vaporise a metal (aluminium).
2. Instead of a cold window, the cold surface is your **plastic moulding**.
3. Instead of atmospheric pressure, the process happens in a near-perfect vacuum so that the metal atoms travel in straight lines (ballistic) without colliding with air molecules — ensuring a **mirror-bright finish**.

In one line: *“Boil a metal in empty space, and let the vapour freeze onto the plastic.”*

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> The following constants and metrics must be memorised for KTU numerical problems.

- **Boltzmann Constant ($k_B$):** $1.380 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$
- **Avogadro’s Number ($N_A$):** $6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$
- **Universal Gas Constant ($R$):** $8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- **1 Torr:** $133.32 \text{ Pa}$
- **Standard chamber vacuum for metallizing:** $\mathbf{10^{-4} \text{ to } 10^{-5} \text{ Torr}}$
- **Boiling point of Aluminium:** $660 \text{ °C (melt)}$, $\sim 2450 \text{ °C (vapour)}$
- **Typical Al film thickness for decoration:** $0.05 \text{ to } 0.1 \text{ \mu m}$ ($50$–$100 \text{ nm}$)
- **Reflectance of metallised surface (Al):** $\geq 90\%$ in visible range
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

The process can be deconstructed into **seven sequential physical/chemical events**:

1. **Substrate Selection & Moulding** — Plastics such as ABS, PS, PP, PC, PMMA, PVC, and phenolic resins are used. ABS is the most common because its surface energy allows uniform film adhesion.
2. **Pre-treatment (Base Coat Application)** — A solvent-based or UV-curable **base coat / lacquer** is applied to:
    - Seal mould release residues,
    - Fill micro-porosities,
    - Provide a smooth, glossy under-layer for the metal.
3. **Loading & Pumping Down** — Substrates are mounted on a rotating jig (planetary or barrel type) inside a bell-jar or in-line vacuum chamber. A **rotary pump + diffusion pump (or cryo-pump)** combination reduces pressure to **$10^{-4}$–$10^{-5}$ Torr**.
4. **Metal Vaporisation** — Pure aluminium wire (or pellets) is fed onto a **tungsten filament (resistance heating)**, an **induction-heated crucible**, or struck by an **electron beam (e-beam gun)**. The energy input melts and vaporises the metal.
5. **Vapour Transport (Line-of-Sight)** — In high vacuum, mean free path $\gg$ chamber dimensions, so Al atoms travel in straight lines (ballistic flow) from source to substrate.
6. **Condensation & Nucleation** — Al atoms strike the cooler substrate, lose kinetic energy, nucleate, and grow a continuous thin film (Volmer–Weber or Frank–van der Merwe growth).
7. **Top Coat (Protective Lacquer)** — A transparent **top coat** is applied to:
    - Protect the soft metal layer from abrasion & oxidation,
    - Provide tinting (gold, copper, blue tones via dyes),
    - Enhance chemical resistance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why high vacuum is mandatory:** At atmospheric pressure ($760 \text{ Torr}$), the mean free path of an Al atom is only $\sim 10^{-7} \text{ m}$ (sub-micron). The metal vapour would collide with air molecules, oxidise, scatter, and produce a dull grey film instead of a mirror.

### 2.2 Sub-Techniques of Vacuum Metallizing

| Sub-Technique | Energy Source | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Resistance (Filament) Evaporation** | Resistive heating of W filament | Decorative Al on plastics |
| **Electron Beam (E-beam) Evaporation** | Focused e-beam on crucible | High-purity films, optical coatings |
| **Induction Evaporation** | RF induction coil | Large-area continuous metallizing |
| **Sputter Metallizing** | Plasma (Ar⁺) bombardment | Hard, adherent films (tooling, EMI shielding) |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise every row below. KTU Part-A (3-mark) questions frequently test these formulae.

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Mean Free Path (MFP) of gas molecule | $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi\, d^{2}\, P}$ | m | Decide minimum vacuum level |
| 2 | Kinetic-theory pressure | $P = \dfrac{1}{3} \rho \overline{c^{2}}$ | Pa | Residual gas analysis |
| 3 | Number density of gas | $n = \dfrac{P}{k_B T}$ | $\text{molecules/m}^{3}$ | Vacuum calculation |
| 4 | Deposition rate (mass) | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{4 \pi \rho r^{2} t}{1}$ (geometry factor) | kg/s | Evaporator design |
| 5 | Film thickness (point source, cosine law) | $t = \dfrac{m \cos\theta}{4 \pi \rho r^{2}}$ | m | Uniformity prediction |
| 6 | Evaporation rate (Langmuir) | $N_{evap} = \dfrac{P_s}{\sqrt{2 \pi M k_B T}}$ | $\text{atoms/(m}^{2}\text{s)}$ | Boil-off calculation |
| 7 | Saturated vapour pressure (Clausius–Clapeyron) | $\ln P_s = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R T} + C$ | Pa | Operating temperature selection |
| 8 | Reflectance of Al film (R) | $R = \vert (n - 1)^{2} + k^{2} \vert / \vert (n+1)^{2} + k^{2} \vert$ | dimensionless | Optical quality check |
| 9 | Pumping speed relation | $Q = S \cdot P$ | $\text{Torr·L/s}$ | Pump selection |
| 10 | Chamber pump-down time | $t = \dfrac{V}{S} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_0}{P}\right)$ | s | Production cycle planning |

> [!NOTE]
> In Formula 8, notice the use of `\vert` (not the bare `\|` symbol) to express absolute value. This avoids breaking markdown table syntax in KTU answer sheets.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Metallised plastics for grilles, headlamp reflectors, and interior trim.
- **Packaging:** Decorative cosmetic caps, bottle closures, gift wraps (metallised BOPP, PET films).
- **Electronics:** EMI/RFI shielding enclosures for electronic devices.
- **Optics:** Front-surface mirrors, dichroic filters, beam splitters.
- **Solar/Energy:** Reflective backing for solar thermal panels and vacuum-insulated panels.
- **Toys & Decor:** Christmas tinsel, artificial jewellery, badges.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mean Free Path of Residual Gas Molecules

**Objective:** Show why a vacuum of $10^{-4} \text{ Torr}$ is essential so that the mean free path of gas molecules exceeds the source-to-substrate distance.

**Step 1 — Number density of gas at pressure $P$ and temperature $T$:**

By the ideal gas law applied per molecule:

$$
P \cdot V = N k_B T \quad \Longrightarrow \quad n = \frac{N}{V} = \frac{P}{k_B T}
$$

*Logic:* The number of molecules per unit volume $n$ is directly proportional to pressure and inversely proportional to absolute temperature.

**Step 2 — Collision cross-section between two molecules of diameter $d$:**

$$
\sigma = \pi (2r)^{2} = \pi d^{2}
$$

*Logic:* Two molecules collide when their centres come within a distance $d = 2r$. The effective cross-section is the area of a circle with diameter $2d$.

**Step 3 — Rate of collisions per molecule (mean free path):**

A molecule sweeping through area $\sigma$ per unit path length collides with $n \sigma$ molecules. The average distance between collisions is:

$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}\, n\, \sigma} = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\, \pi d^{2}\, P}
$$

*Logic:* The $\sqrt{2}$ factor accounts for the average relative velocity of two molecules (vs. velocity of one), since both are moving.

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation for air at $T = 300 \text{ K}$, $d \approx 3.7 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}$:**

$$
\lambda (\text{m}) \approx \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{P (\text{Torr})}
$$

*Logic:* Substituting $k_B T / (\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2}) \approx 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Torr·m}$ gives the handy rule-of-thumb used by vacuum engineers.

At $P = 10^{-4} \text{ Torr}$:

$$
\lambda \approx \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-4}} = 50 \text{ m}
$$

At $P = 760 \text{ Torr}$ (atmospheric):

$$
\lambda \approx \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{760} \approx 6.6 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 6.6 \text{ \mu m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** In a chamber of $1 \text{ m}$ size, $10^{-4} \text{ Torr}$ vacuum makes the mean free path ($50 \text{ m}$) **50 times larger than the chamber** — guaranteeing ballistic, line-of-sight transport of Al atoms. This is the central scientific justification of vacuum metallizing.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Chamber Pump-Down Time

**Objective:** Calculate how long the rotary + diffusion pump combination takes to evacuate a chamber.

**Step 1 — Definition of pumping speed $S$:** Volume of gas removed per unit time at a given pressure ($\text{L/s}$ or $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$).

**Step 2 — Throughput (gas load):**

$$
Q = S \cdot P
$$

*Logic:* If pump removes $S$ litres per second at pressure $P$, the volumetric flow rate of gas (in pressure-volume units) is $S \cdot P$.

**Step 3 — Pressure decay in chamber of volume $V$:**

The mass balance gives:

$$
-V \, \frac{dP}{dt} = S \cdot P
$$

*Logic:* The rate of pressure decrease times volume equals the pump throughput.

**Step 4 — Separation of variables and integration:**

$$
\int_{P_0}^{P} \frac{dP}{P} = -\int_{0}^{t} \frac{S}{V} \, dt
$$

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = -\frac{S t}{V}
$$

**Step 5 — Final formula:**

$$
\boxed{\,t = \frac{V}{S} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_0}{P}\right)\,}
$$

*Logic:* The pump-down time depends logarithmically on the pressure ratio — it takes as much time to go from $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ Torr as from $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ Torr.

**Step 6 — Worked example:** $V = 500 \text{ L}$, $S = 500 \text{ L/s}$ (combined), $P_0 = 760 \text{ Torr}$, $P = 10^{-4} \text{ Torr}$:

$$
t = \frac{500}{500} \ln\!\left(\frac{760}{10^{-4}}\right) = 1 \times \ln(7.6 \times 10^{6})
$$

$$
t = \ln(7.6) + \ln(10^{6}) \approx 2.03 + 13.82 = 15.85 \text{ s}
$$

*Logic:* A high-speed pump reaches working vacuum in ~16 s — explaining why modern in-line metallizers can coat thousands of parts per hour.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Film Thickness from Cosine Emission Law

**Objective:** Relate film thickness $t$ at a point on the substrate to the mass $m$ evaporated from a point source and the geometry.

**Step 1 — Mass conservation:** Total mass evaporated must equal mass deposited (assuming 100% condensation):

$$
m = \rho \int t \, dA
$$

**Step 2 — Lambert’s cosine law for a point source:** The mass emitted per unit solid angle is proportional to $\cos\theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle from the source normal.

**Step 3 — Integrating over a sphere of radius $r$:**

$$
m = 4 \pi \rho r^{2} \cdot t_{\text{normal}}
$$

**Step 4 — Film thickness at angle $\theta$ from the source normal:**

$$
\boxed{\,t = \frac{m \cos\theta}{4 \pi \rho r^{2}}\,}
$$

*Logic:* Films are thicker at points directly facing the source ($\theta = 0$, $\cos\theta = 1$) and thinner at oblique angles. This is why substrate rotation (planetary jig) is essential for uniform metallizing.

**Step 5 — Application:** If $m = 0.5 \text{ g}$ Al, $\rho = 2700 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$, $r = 0.4 \text{ m}$, $\theta = 0$:

$$
t = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \pi (2700)(0.4)^{2}} \approx \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{5.43 \times 10^{3}} \approx 9.2 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m} = 92 \text{ nm}
$$

*Logic:* A mere 92 nm of aluminium — about 1/1000th the thickness of a human hair — produces a fully reflective mirror finish.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance

| Parameter | Atmospheric Plating | Vacuum Metallizing |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pressure | $760 \text{ Torr}$ | $10^{-4} \text{ to } 10^{-5} \text{ Torr}$ |
| Mean free path | $0.07 \text{ \mu m}$ | $\sim 50 \text{ m}$ |
| Film thickness control | Poor (chemical) | Excellent ($\pm 5\%$) |
| Surface finish | Matte/satin | Mirror-bright |
| Energy consumption | High (chemical baths) | Low (evaporation only) |
| Environmental impact | Heavy metal effluent | Minimal effluent |
| Cost per piece | Low (mass production) | Moderate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Architecture (Mermaid)

The block-level functional architecture below maps the complete vacuum metallizing workflow, including pre-treatment, vacuum cycle, evaporation, and post-treatment modules. Each node uses an alphanumeric identifier (alpha rule) with raw uppercase labels only — no bold, italics, or HTML formatting inside the labels.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[PLASTIC SUBSTRATE IN] --> A2[BASE COAT APPLICATION]
    A2 --> A3[DRYING OVEN 60 TO 80 DEG C]
    A3 --> A4[LOAD ON ROTARY JIG]
    A4 --> B1[VACUUM CHAMBER SEAL]
    B1 --> B2[ROTARY PUMP ROUGH VACUUM]
    B2 --> B3[DIFFUSION PUMP HIGH VACUUM]
    B3 --> B4{PRESSURE LESS THAN 10 TO POWER MINUS 4 TORR}
    B4 -- NO --> B2
    B4 -- YES --> C1[ALUMINIUM WIRE FEED]
    C1 --> C2[RESISTIVE EVAPORATION 1400 TO 1500 DEG C]
    C2 --> C3[VAPOUR TRANSPORT BALLISTIC]
    C3 --> C4[CONDENSATION ON SUBSTRATE]
    C4 --> C5[PLANETARY JIG ROTATION]
    C5 --> C6[FILM THICKNESS 50 TO 100 NM]
    C6 --> D1[VENT CHAMBER]
    D1 --> D2[UNLOAD METALLISED PARTS]
    D2 --> D3[TOP COAT APPLICATION]
    D3 --> D4[CURING OR AIR DRY]
    D4 --> D5[QUALITY INSPECTION REFLECTANCE ADHESION]
    D5 --> E1[FINISHED DECORATED PRODUCT OUT]
```

### 4.2 Vacuum System Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[ATMOSPHERE 760 TORR] --> V1[ROUGHING VALVE]
    V1 --> RP[ROTARY VANE PUMP]
    RP --> R1[ROUGH VACUUM 10 TO POWER MINUS 2 TORR]
    R1 --> V2[BAFFLE VALVE]
    V2 --> DP[OIL DIFFUSION PUMP]
    DP --> H1[HIGH VACUUM 10 TO POWER MINUS 5 TORR]
    H1 --> CH[PROCESS CHAMBER BELL JAR]
    CH --> EV[EVAPORATION SOURCE TUNGSTEN FILAMENT]
    EV --> AL[ALUMINIUM VAPOUR PLUME]
    AL --> SB[SUBSTRATE HOLDER PLANETARY]
    SB --> TC[TOP COAT STATION]
```

### 4.3 Functional Component Schematic

| Stage | Hardware Component | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pre-treatment | Spray booth, dip tank | Apply base coat lacquer |
| Loading | Planetary jig, barrel | Hold parts, rotate during deposition |
| Vacuum | Rotary + Diffusion pump | Achieve $10^{-4}$ Torr |
| Evaporation | Tungsten filament / crucible | Vaporise Al wire |
| Monitoring | Quartz crystal microbalance | Measure deposition rate |
| Vent | Air admittance valve | Return to atm. pressure |
| Post-treatment | Lacquer booth, oven | Apply protective top coat |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1.** Define **vacuum metallizing**. Why is the process carried out under high vacuum conditions? *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Vacuum metallizing is a Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD) process in which a thin layer of metal (commonly aluminium) is vaporised in a high-vacuum chamber and deposited onto a plastic substrate to form a uniform, reflective metallic film.

The process is carried out under high vacuum ($10^{-4}$–$10^{-5}$ Torr) because:
1. It increases the **mean free path** of metal atoms to several metres, ensuring **line-of-sight (ballistic)** travel from source to substrate.
2. It prevents **oxidation** of the hot metal vapour.
3. It eliminates air-molecule scattering that would otherwise produce a dull, hazy film instead of a mirror finish.

---

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**

**Q2.** List **any four** advantages of vacuum metallizing over conventional electroplating on plastics. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| # | Advantage of Vacuum Metallizing |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Dry process** — no toxic effluent / wastewater (eco-friendly). |
| 2 | **Superior optical finish** — true mirror brightness achievable. |
| 3 | **No electrolytic conductivity required** — works on any plastic (PP, PE, ABS). |
| 4 | **Very thin film** ($\sim 100 \text{ nm}$) — economical metal usage. |
| 5 | **Uniform coating on complex 3-D shapes** via planetary jig rotation. |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students often confuse *sputtering* with *evaporation* — they are **different** PVD sub-processes. Always state the energy source.
> - When asked "why high vacuum?", merely writing "to remove air" is **incomplete**. You **must** mention *mean free path > chamber dimension* and *oxidation prevention*.
> - For numericals, forgetting to convert Torr → Pa costs 1–2 marks. Use $1 \text{ Torr} = 133.32 \text{ Pa}$.
> - Do not write "vacuum coating" generically — specify **vacuum metallizing** and the metal used (usually **Al**).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4 — Dec 2024]*

**(a)** Describe in detail the **process sequence of vacuum metallizing of plastics** with a neat labelled block diagram. Explain the role of (i) base coat and (ii) top coat. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A vacuum chamber of volume $V = 600 \text{ L}$ is evacuated by a pump of effective speed $S = 400 \text{ L/s}$ from atmospheric pressure $P_0 = 760 \text{ Torr}$ to working pressure $P = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Torr}$. Calculate (i) the **pump-down time**, and (ii) the **mean free path** of residual air molecules at $300 \text{ K}$ (take molecular diameter $d = 3.7 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}$). *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Process sequence of vacuum metallizing:**

1. **Substrate preparation:** The plastic part (commonly ABS, PS, PC) is cleaned to remove mould-release agents, dust and oils.
2. **Base coat application:** A solvent-based or UV-curable **base coat lacquer** is sprayed/dipped and dried at $60$–$80 \text{ °C}$. Its roles are:
    - Seals surface porosity,
    - Provides a uniform glossy under-layer,
    - Improves adhesion of the metal film,
    - Reduces moulding defects visible through the metal.
3. **Loading:** Parts are mounted on a **planetary rotary jig** inside the vacuum chamber.
4. **Pumping down:** A rotary pump brings pressure to $\sim 10^{-2} \text{ Torr}$; a diffusion pump brings it down to $10^{-4}$–$10^{-5} \text{ Torr}$.
5. **Evaporation:** Aluminium wire is fed onto a heated **tungsten filament** at $\sim 1500 \text{ °C}$; the metal melts, vaporises and forms a vapour cloud.
6. **Deposition:** The vapour travels ballistically and **condenses** on the rotating plastic parts, forming a thin metallic film ($50$–$100 \text{ nm}$).
7. **Vent and unload:** Chamber is vented with dry air/nitrogen; parts are removed.
8. **Top coat:** A transparent protective lacquer is applied. Its roles are:
    - Protects the soft metal from abrasion, oxidation and fingerprints,
    - Provides tinting (gold, copper, blue via soluble dyes),
    - Enhances chemical and weathering resistance.

*(Block diagram as shown in Section 4.1 above.)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Part (i) — Pump-down time:**

Using $t = \dfrac{V}{S} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_0}{P}\right)$:

$$
t = \frac{600}{400} \ln\!\left(\frac{760}{5 \times 10^{-4}}\right)
$$

$$
= 1.5 \times \ln(1.52 \times 10^{6})
$$

$$
= 1.5 \times \left[\ln(1.52) + \ln(10^{6})\right]
$$

$$
= 1.5 \times [0.4187 + 13.8155] = 1.5 \times 14.2342 = 21.35 \text{ s}
$$

**Answer:** $t \approx 21.35 \text{ seconds}$.

**Part (ii) — Mean free path:**

Using $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2} P}$.

Convert pressure: $P = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Torr} \times 133.32 = 0.0667 \text{ Pa}$.

$$
\lambda = \frac{(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi (3.7 \times 10^{-10})^{2} \times 0.0667}
$$

Numerator:

$$
= 4.14 \times 10^{-21} \text{ J}
$$

Denominator:

$$
\sqrt{2}\,\pi \approx 4.443
$$

$$
(3.7 \times 10^{-10})^{2} = 1.369 \times 10^{-19} \text{ m}^{2}
$$

$$
4.443 \times 1.369 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.0667 = 4.058 \times 10^{-20}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\lambda = \frac{4.14 \times 10^{-21}}{4.058 \times 10^{-20}} \approx 0.102 \text{ m} = 10.2 \text{ cm}
$$

**Answer:** $\lambda \approx 10.2 \text{ cm}$, which is comparable to the chamber dimension — confirming the process is in the molecular-flow regime.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Internal Choice — KTU 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** Compare **vacuum metallizing** with **electroplating on plastics**. Mention the engineering applications of metallised plastics. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the expression for **mean free path** of gas molecules. Using this expression, explain why vacuum metallizing fails to give a bright finish if the chamber pressure rises above $10^{-3} \text{ Torr}$. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Vacuum Metallizing | Electroplating on Plastics |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Process type | Physical (PVD) | Electrochemical |
| Pre-treatment | Base coat lacquer | Etching + electroless plating |
| Conductivity needed? | No | Yes (after electroless seed layer) |
| Metals used | Al, Ag, Au, Cu | Cu, Ni, Cr (rarely Al) |
| Film thickness | $50$–$500 \text{ nm}$ | $5$–$25 \text{ \mu m}$ |
| Finish | Mirror-bright | Satin to semi-bright |
| Effluent | Negligible | Heavy metal waste water |
| Cost | Moderate | Lower for thick coatings |
| Suitability for PP/PE | Excellent | Poor (needs special etching) |

**Engineering Applications of Metallised Plastics:**

1. **Automotive:** Headlamp reflectors, radiator grilles, badges, interior trim.
2. **Decorative packaging:** Cosmetics caps, perfume bottles, gift boxes, Christmas decorations.
3. **Electronics:** EMI/RFI shielding enclosures for laptops, mobile phones, medical instruments.
4. **Optics:** Front-surface mirrors, dichroic filters, beam splitters.
5. **Solar energy:** Reflective surfaces for solar thermal collectors.
6. **Toys and badges:** Foil-decorated badges, medals, imitation jewellery.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Derivation of mean free path** (as derived in Section 3.1):

$$
\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2}\, P}
$$

*(Full derivation reproduced — see Section 3.1 for step-by-step derivation.)*

**Why vacuum metallizing fails above $10^{-3} \text{ Torr}$:**

Substituting numerical values for air at $300 \text{ K}$:

$$
\lambda (\text{m}) \approx \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{P (\text{Torr})}
$$

At $P = 10^{-3} \text{ Torr}$:

$$
\lambda \approx \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-3}} = 5 \text{ m}
$$

This is marginally sufficient for chambers up to $\sim 1 \text{ m}$ in size. However, if pressure rises to $10^{-2} \text{ Torr}$ or higher:

- The mean free path drops to $0.5 \text{ m}$ or less — comparable to or smaller than the chamber.
- Al atoms collide repeatedly with residual gas molecules.
- These collisions **oxidise** the hot aluminium (forming $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$) and **scatter** the vapour, producing a hazy, dull, brownish film instead of a mirror finish.
- Residual gas is also trapped in the growing film, reducing reflectance and adhesion.

Therefore, working pressure **must remain below $\sim 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Torr}$** for decorative-quality mirror finishes on plastics.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU PEPOT864 – Module 4: Vacuum Metallizing**

- Vacuum metallizing is a **Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD)** technique — **not chemical**.
- Most widely used metal: **Aluminium (Al)** — cheap, low melting point of vapour phase, high reflectance.
- Working vacuum: **$10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$ Torr** (mandatory for mirror finish).
- Rule-of-thumb: $\lambda (\text{m}) \approx 5 \times 10^{-3} / P(\text{Torr})$ for air at $300 \text{ K}$.
- Film thickness for decorative use: **$50$–$100 \text{ nm}$**.
- Process sequence: *Substrate → Base coat → Load → Pump down → Evaporate → Condense → Vent → Top coat*.
- **Base coat** seals surface and provides gloss; **top coat** protects metal and provides tinting.
- **Planetary jig rotation** ensures uniform thickness on 3-D parts.
- Evaporation methods: **Resistive (filament), E-beam, Induction** (memorise all three).
- Sputtering is **different** from evaporation — uses plasma, not thermal vaporisation.
- Applications: Automotive trim, cosmetics packaging, EMI shields, optical mirrors, toys.
- **Mean free path derivation** is a guaranteed KTU question — must derive $k_B T / (\sqrt{2}\pi d^{2} P)$ cleanly.
- **Pump-down time formula:** $t = (V/S) \ln(P_0/P)$ — a logarithmic pressure ratio.
- Always specify **why high vacuum**: prevent oxidation + increase mean free path for ballistic transport.
- Conversion to remember: $1 \text{ Torr} = 133.32 \text{ Pa}$.
- Boiling/vapour temperature of Al: $\sim 2450 \text{ °C}$; filament operates at $\sim 1500 \text{ °C}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metallizing Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT864 - PLASTICS & DECORATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Metallizing Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Metallizing** in the context of plastics decoration refers to the industrial process of depositing a thin, continuous, adherent metallic layer — typically **Aluminium (Al)**, but also **Chrome (Cr)**, **Nickel (Ni)**, **Copper (Cu)**, **Silver (Ag)**, or **Gold (Au)** — onto the surface of a plastic substrate (ABS, PC, PP, PE, PMMA, PET, PVC, polyamide, etc.) for aesthetic, functional, barrier, or electromagnetic purposes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEPOT864 – Module 4):**
> *Metallizing equipment* refers to the integrated set of **vacuum, electrical, mechanical, chemical, and thermal hardware** required to convert a raw plastic part into a metallized finished product. The equipment chain spans **pre-treatment (cleaning, etching, priming) → metallization → post-treatment (lacquering, top-coating, curing) → inspection**.

The principal metallizing routes catalogued under KTU Module 4 are:

| Route | Governing Principle |
|---|---|
| **Vacuum Metallization (PVD)** | Thermal evaporation of metal under high vacuum |
| **Sputter Metallization** | Plasma-driven momentum transfer of target atoms |
| **Electroplating** | Electrolytic reduction of metal ions onto conductive layer |
| **Electroless Plating** | Autocatalytic chemical reduction (no external current) |
| **Hot-Stamping Foil Metallization** | Thermo-mechanical transfer of metallic foil |
| **Metallic Spray / Arc Spray** | Molten metal atomization and projection |
| **Pad-Printing & Screen Metallic Inks** | Mechanical deposition of metallic pigmented inks |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine giving a plastic spoon the **shiny, mirror-like finish of a polished steel ladle** — without actually making it from steel. That is metallizing. The spoon stays plastic (light, cheap, mouldable), but on the outside it *looks* and *behaves* like metal.

**Everyday examples you already know:**

- The **chrome trim around your car headlights** → likely vacuum-metallized or electroplated ABS.
- The **shiny gold lettering on a liquor bottle** → hot-stamped foil.
- The **metallic interior lining of a snack-food pouch** → vacuum-metallized PET film.
- The **rainbow-tinted decorative cosmetic cap** → sputtered multi-layer coating.

**Why metallize plastic at all?**
- **Decoration** (luxury aesthetic at plastic cost)
- **EMI/RFI shielding** for electronic enclosures (Faraday-cage behaviour)
- **Gas/light barrier** in food packaging
- **Reflectivity** in automotive lighting & solar concentrators
- **Wear, scratch, and chemical resistance**

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** The metallizing equipment essentially solves *three engineering problems simultaneously*:
> 1. Generate metal atoms (atomization, evaporation, sputtering, ion supply).
> 2. Transport those atoms to the plastic part without contaminating or destroying them.
> 3. Make them stick permanently to an otherwise non-metallic, chemically inert surface.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants

The following benchmark constants and metrics are universally referenced in metallizing equipment design:

- **Vacuum threshold for metallization:** ≤ **1 × 10⁻⁴ mbar (≈ 0.075 mTorr)** — below the molecular-flow regime.
- **Typical evaporation temperature of Aluminium:** **1212 °C to 1517 °C** (depending on source geometry).
- **Mean free path of Al atoms at 10⁻⁴ mbar:** ≈ **λ ≈ 50 m**, ensuring line-of-sight deposition.
- **Typical PVD deposition rate:** **10 – 100 nm/s**; **Sputtering:** **1 – 10 nm/s**; **Electroplating:** **1 – 50 µm/min**.
- **Standard coating thickness window:** **30 nm – 1 µm** (decorative) up to **5 – 25 µm** (functional EMI shielding).
- **Adhesion strength benchmark (cross-hatch + tape test):** **ASTM D3359 Class 5B**.
- **Surface resistivity target for EMI shielding:** **≤ 1 Ω/sq** (per ASTM D4935).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Metallizing Equipment

The equipment used in plastics metallization is organized along the **process chain axis**:

### A. Pre-Treatment Equipment
1. **Ultrasonic degreasers** — cavitation cleaning with isopropanol/deionized water.
2. **Corona / Plasma treatment units** — raise surface energy from ~30 dyne/cm to ≥ 48 dyne/cm (for ink/adhesion).
3. **Etching tanks** — chromic-sulphuric acid for ABS (creates micro-pores for mechanical keying).
4. **Base-coat / primer spray booths** — apply thin lacquer to seal substrate and level surface.
5. **Flash-off and convection drying ovens** — typically **60–80 °C** for 10–20 min.

### B. Core Metallizing Equipment
The four pillars:

#### 2.1.1 Vacuum Metallizer (Thermal Evaporation PVD)
**Components in series:**
- **Load-lock chamber** (entry, isolates main chamber).
- **Main vacuum chamber** (bell jar / box coater).
- **Vacuum pumping stack:** rotary vane (10⁻³ mbar) → diffusion/turbomolecular pump (10⁻⁶ mbar).
- **Crucible + tungsten filament / boat** (resistive or e-beam).
- **Watch / tungsten wire-feed source** for Al pellets.
- **Substrate holder (planetary calotte / rotating drum).**
- **Quartz crystal microbalance (QCM)** — rate & thickness monitor.
- **Masking fixtures** (selective decoration).
- **Optical emission monitor / photometer.**

**Operating sequence:** Pump down → preheat sources → open shutter → evaporate at controlled rate → close shutter → vent → unload.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **vacuum condition** is what makes PVD work — at ≤ 10⁻⁴ mbar, evaporated metal atoms travel in **straight lines** (no scattering) and reach the cold plastic substrate where they condense, nucleate, and grow as a thin film.

#### 2.1.2 Sputter Metallizer
**Components:**
- **Sputter-down/up chamber** with multiple **magnetron cathodes**.
- **Target (e.g., Al 99.99 % purity).**
- **Process gas manifold** — Argon (Ar) at 1–10 mTorr, optional O₂/N₂ for oxides/nitrides.
- **DC, pulsed-DC, RF, or HIPIMS power supplies** (1–15 kW typical).
- **Biased substrate holder** (rotary / planetary).
- **Load-lock, cryopump, turbopump.**
- **End-block / D.C. block cooling** on cathodes.

**Operating principle:** Ar⁺ ions, accelerated by cathode potential, **momentum-knock** atoms off the target; the sputtered atoms deposit on plastic substrates facing the target.

#### 2.1.3 Electroplating Line
**Components (in process order):**
- **Pre-treatment dip tanks** (cleaner, etchant, neutralizer, activator, electroless seed).
- **Electroless nickel/copper strike bath** (renders plastic conductive — most critical step).
- **Electroplating cells** (Ni, Cr, Cu, Ag, Au solutions) with anodes, cathodes, bus bars, rectifiers.
- **Agitation system** (air sparging or eductor).
- **Filtration and temperature control** (50–70 °C).
- **Rinse tanks (cascade, counter-current DI water).**
- **Dryer (centrifuge / oven).**
- **Rectifier** — DC source **0–12 V / 0–5000 A**, with pulse/ramp capability.

#### 2.1.4 Hot-Stamping Foil Metallizer
**Components:**
- **Heated silicone/rubber die** (50–250 °C).
- **Foil feed and take-up reels.**
- **Pressure (pneumatic) cylinder.**
- **Dwell-time control**.
- **Indexing table / rotary press.**

### C. Post-Treatment Equipment
- **Top-coat spray / dip lacquer booths.**
- **UV-curing tunnels** (mercury or LED lamps, 365–395 nm).
- **IR/convection drying ovens.**
- **Inspection jigs, gloss meters, salt-spray chambers, adhesion testers.**

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All equations below are examinable for KTU Module 4 numerical or descriptive questions. Symbols follow IUPAC convention.

### 2.2.1 Vacuum & Kinetic Theory Equations

$$
\lambda \;=\; \frac{k_{\mathrm{B}}\,T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi\,d^{2}\,P}
$$

where $\lambda$ = mean free path [m], $k_{\mathrm{B}}$ = **Boltzmann constant = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K**, $T$ = absolute temperature [K], $d$ = molecular diameter [m], $P$ = pressure [Pa].

**Rule of thumb:** $\lambda \,[\mathrm{m}] \approx \dfrac{6.6 \times 10^{-3}}{P\,[\mathrm{Pa}]}$

### 2.2.2 Hertz–Knudsen Evaporation Rate

$$
R_{\mathrm{e}} \;=\; \frac{\alpha\,P_{\mathrm{sat}}(T)}{\sqrt{2\,\pi\,M\,R\,T}}
$$

where $R_{\mathrm{e}}$ = evaporation flux [kg/(m²·s)], $\alpha$ = accommodation coefficient (≈ 1 for clean Al), $P_{\mathrm{sat}}$ = saturation vapour pressure [Pa], $M$ = molar mass [kg/mol], $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**.

### 2.2.3 Film Thickness from Mass Conservation

$$
t \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}\,\tau}{\rho_{\mathrm{f}}\,A_{\mathrm{sub}}}
$$

where $t$ = deposited thickness [m], $\dot{m}$ = mass evaporation rate [kg/s], $\tau$ = deposition time [s], $\rho_{\mathrm{f}}$ = film density [kg/m³], $A_{\mathrm{sub}}$ = substrate area [m²].

### 2.2.4 Sputtering Yield (Magnetron)

$$
Y \;=\; \frac{\mathrm{atoms\,ejected}}{\mathrm{incident\,ion}} \;\approx\; 0.3 \text{ – } 1.5 \;\;\mathrm{for\,Al^{+}\,on\,Al}
$$

Deposition rate from sputtering:

$$
R_{\mathrm{s}} \;=\; \frac{Y\,j_{\mathrm{i}}\,M}{\rho_{\mathrm{f}}\,N_{\mathrm{A}}\,e}
$$

where $j_{\mathrm{i}}$ = ion current density [A/m²], $N_{\mathrm{A}}$ = **Avogadro's number = 6.022 × 10²³ /mol**, $e$ = **electron charge = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**.

### 2.2.5 Faraday's Law of Electrolysis (Electroplating)

$$
m \;=\; \frac{M\,I\,\tau}{z\,F}
$$

where $m$ = deposited mass [kg], $I$ = current [A], $\tau$ = time [s], $z$ = charge number, $F$ = **Faraday constant = 96 485 C/mol**.

Equivalent thickness:

$$
t_{\mathrm{dep}} \;=\; \frac{M\,I\,\tau}{z\,F\,\rho_{\mathrm{f}}\,A}
$$

Current efficiency:

$$
\eta_{\mathrm{c}} \;=\; \frac{m_{\mathrm{actual}}}{m_{\mathrm{theoretical}}} \times 100\,\%
$$

### 2.2.6 Sheet Resistance & EMI Shielding

$$
R_{\mathrm{s}} \;=\; \frac{\rho_{\mathrm{f}}}{t}
$$

$$
\mathrm{SE}_{\mathrm{dB}} \;=\; 50 \;+\; 10\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{R_{\mathrm{s}}}\right) \;+\; 1.7\,t\,\sqrt{f\,\mu_{\mathrm{r}}\,\sigma_{\mathrm{r}}}
$$

where $\rho_{\mathrm{f}}$ = film resistivity [Ω·m], $t$ = thickness [m], $f$ = frequency [Hz], $\mu_{\mathrm{r}}$, $\sigma_{\mathrm{r}}$ are relative permeability and conductivity vs. copper.

### 2.2.7 Pumping Speed / Throughput (Vacuum)

$$
Q \;=\; S_{\mathrm{p}}\,\cdot\,P \quad\quad \mathrm{and} \quad\quad \frac{1}{S_{\mathrm{eff}}} \;=\; \frac{1}{S_{\mathrm{p}}} \;+\; \frac{1}{C}
$$

where $Q$ = throughput [Pa·m³/s], $S_{\mathrm{p}}$ = pump speed [m³/s], $C$ = conductance [m³/s], $S_{\mathrm{eff}}$ = effective speed at chamber.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Comparison Matrix — Equipment vs. Application

| Equipment Class | Capital Cost | Throughput | Coating Purity | Adhesion on Plastic | Typical Coating | Best Plastic Substrate |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Vacuum Metallizer | Medium | High | High | Good (with primer) | 30–100 nm Al | PET, PP, PVC, PC |
| Sputter Metallizer | High | Medium | Very High | Excellent | 20–500 nm (Al, Cr, Ti) | PC, PMMA, ABS |
| Electroplating Line | Very High | High | High | Excellent (after electroless) | 5–25 µm Ni/Cr/Cu | ABS (best), PP, PC |
| Electroless Bath | Low | High | Medium-High | Excellent (chemical bond) | 0.5–2 µm Ni/P | ABS, PC, PA |
| Hot-Stamping Foil | Very Low | Very High | Medium | Mechanical (no chemical bond) | < 1 µm (Al / pigment) | PS, PP, PVC, ABS |
| Spray Metallization | Low | Medium | Low | Fair | 10–100 µm (Zn, Al) | Large PP/HDPE parts |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

> [!NOTE]
> The following analytical derivations are written **step-by-step** for KTU Part B / numerical questions. Each algebraic transition is explicitly justified.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Mean Free Path & Vacuum Requirement

**Problem:** A vacuum metallizing chamber operates at $P = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ mbar with $T = 300$ K. Calculate the mean free path of residual gas molecules. Comment on whether this is sufficient for line-of-sight deposition. Take $d = 3.0 \times 10^{-10}$ m.

### Step 1 — Convert pressure to SI (Pascals)

$$
P \;=\; 5 \times 10^{-4}\,\mathrm{mbar} \times \frac{100\,\mathrm{Pa}}{1\,\mathrm{mbar}} \;=\; 5 \times 10^{-2}\,\mathrm{Pa}
$$

**Reasoning:** SI base unit for pressure is Pascal; 1 mbar = 100 Pa. Converting ensures dimensional consistency in the kinetic theory equation.

### Step 2 — Substitute into the mean free path equation

$$
\lambda \;=\; \frac{(1.381 \times 10^{-23})\,\times\,300}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi\,\times\,(3.0 \times 10^{-10})^{2}\,\times\,5 \times 10^{-2}}
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate numerator

$$
\mathrm{Num} \;=\; 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 \;=\; 4.143 \times 10^{-21}\,\mathrm{J}
$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate denominator

$$
\sqrt{2}\,\pi \;=\; 4.443
$$

$$
d^{2} \;=\; (3.0 \times 10^{-10})^{2} \;=\; 9.0 \times 10^{-20}\,\mathrm{m^{2}}
$$

$$
4.443 \times 9.0 \times 10^{-20} \times 5 \times 10^{-2} \;=\; 2.0 \times 10^{-20}
$$

### Step 5 — Final division

$$
\lambda \;=\; \frac{4.143 \times 10^{-21}}{2.0 \times 10^{-20}} \;\approx\; 0.207\,\mathrm{m} \;=\; 20.7\,\mathrm{cm}
$$

### Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation

Since the chamber dimensions are typically **0.3–1 m**, $\lambda \approx 0.21$ m means gas-phase scattering is minimal but **NOT** negligible. For premium optical-quality coatings, $P$ must drop to $10^{-5}$ mbar so $\lambda \gg$ chamber dimension.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Aluminium Film Thickness from Vacuum Evaporation

**Problem:** Aluminium is evaporated at $\dot{m} = 0.25$ g/s onto 200 substrates, each of area $50\,\mathrm{cm}^{2}$, for $\tau = 12$ s. Density of Al film $\rho_{\mathrm{f}} = 2700$ kg/m³. Compute the average film thickness.

### Step 1 — Compute total area

$$
A_{\mathrm{sub}} \;=\; 200 \times 50\,\mathrm{cm}^{2} \;=\; 10\,000\,\mathrm{cm}^{2} \;=\; 1.0\,\mathrm{m}^{2}
$$

### Step 2 — Convert mass evaporation rate to SI

$$
\dot{m} \;=\; 0.25\,\mathrm{g/s} \;=\; 2.5 \times 10^{-4}\,\mathrm{kg/s}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the thickness equation

$$
t \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}\,\tau}{\rho_{\mathrm{f}}\,A_{\mathrm{sub}}} \;=\; \frac{(2.5 \times 10^{-4})\,\times\,12}{2700\,\times\,1.0}
$$

### Step 4 — Numerator evaluation

$$
(2.5 \times 10^{-4}) \times 12 \;=\; 3.0 \times 10^{-3}\,\mathrm{kg}
$$

### Step 5 — Denominator evaluation

$$
2700 \times 1.0 \;=\; 2700\,\mathrm{kg}
$$

### Step 6 — Division

$$
t \;=\; \frac{3.0 \times 10^{-3}}{2700} \;\approx\; 1.11 \times 10^{-6}\,\mathrm{m} \;=\; 1.11\,\mu\mathrm{m}
$$

**Engineering check:** This thickness lies in the decorative-to-functional range (good for both optics and EMI shielding).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Faraday's Law in Electroplating

**Problem:** A current of $I = 250$ A is passed through a Nickel-plating bath for $\tau = 1800$ s. Charge number $z = 2$, $M_{\mathrm{Ni}} = 58.69$ g/mol, $\rho_{\mathrm{f}} = 8900$ kg/m³, $A = 0.5$ m². Calculate (a) mass deposited, (b) average thickness, (c) deposition efficiency if actual mass is 145 g.

### (a) Mass via Faraday's Law

$$
m_{\mathrm{theoretical}} \;=\; \frac{M\,I\,\tau}{z\,F} \;=\; \frac{58.69 \times 10^{-3}\,\mathrm{kg/mol} \times 250 \times 1800}{2 \times 96\,485}
$$

Numerator:

$$
0.05869 \times 250 \times 1800 \;=\; 26\,410.5
$$

Denominator:

$$
2 \times 96\,485 \;=\; 192\,970
$$

$$
m_{\mathrm{theoretical}} \;=\; \frac{26\,410.5}{192\,970} \;\approx\; 0.1369\,\mathrm{kg} \;=\; 136.9\,\mathrm{g}
$$

### (b) Thickness

$$
t_{\mathrm{dep}} \;=\; \frac{M\,I\,\tau}{z\,F\,\rho_{\mathrm{f}}\,A} \;=\; \frac{26\,410.5}{192\,970 \times 8900 \times 0.5}
$$

Denominator:

$$
192\,970 \times 8900 \times 0.5 \;\approx\; 8.587 \times 10^{8}
$$

$$
t_{\mathrm{dep}} \;\approx\; \frac{26\,410.5}{8.587 \times 10^{8}} \;\approx\; 3.08 \times 10^{-5}\,\mathrm{m} \;=\; 30.8\,\mu\mathrm{m}
$$

### (c) Current Efficiency

$$
\eta_{\mathrm{c}} \;=\; \frac{145}{136.9} \times 100 \;\approx\; 105.9\,\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> $\eta_{\mathrm{c}} > 100\,\%$ indicates measurement inconsistency (likely the actual mass includes entrained water, or $z$ was effectively 1). In KTU valuation, **state the assumption** clearly.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Sputtering Rate and Throughput

**Problem:** A magnetron sputters Al at ion current density $j_{\mathrm{i}} = 50$ A/m², $Y = 1.0$, on a substrate $A = 0.5$ m² for $\tau = 60$ s. Calculate the deposited mass.

### Step 1 — Convert constants

$$
M_{\mathrm{Al}} \;=\; 26.98 \times 10^{-3}\,\mathrm{kg/mol}, \quad \rho_{\mathrm{Al}} \;=\; 2700\,\mathrm{kg/m^{3}}, \quad N_{\mathrm{A}} = 6.022 \times 10^{23}, \quad e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\,\mathrm{C}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute deposition rate

$$
R_{\mathrm{s}} \;=\; \frac{Y\,j_{\mathrm{i}}\,M}{\rho_{\mathrm{f}}\,N_{\mathrm{A}}\,e} \;=\; \frac{1.0 \times 50 \times 0.02698}{2700 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}
$$

Numerator:

$$
50 \times 0.02698 \;=\; 1.349
$$

Denominator:

$$
2700 \times 9.648 \times 10^{4} \;\approx\; 2.605 \times 10^{8}
$$

$$
R_{\mathrm{s}} \;\approx\; \frac{1.349}{2.605 \times 10^{8}} \;\approx\; 5.18 \times 10^{-9}\,\mathrm{m/s} \;=\; 5.18\,\mathrm{nm/s}
$$

### Step 3 — Thickness in 60 s

$$
t_{\mathrm{film}} \;=\; R_{\mathrm{s}} \times \tau \;=\; 5.18 \times 10^{-9} \times 60 \;\approx\; 3.11 \times 10^{-7}\,\mathrm{m} \;=\; 311\,\mathrm{nm}
$$

**Engineering note:** Consistent with typical sputtered decorative Al.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
