# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — 3 Marks
**"Differentiate between vacuum metallization and electroplating of plastics. Mention any two advantages of each."**

**Model Answer:**

- **Vacuum Metallization (PVD):** Aluminium is thermally evaporated under high vacuum (≤ 10⁻⁴ mbar) and condensed onto a pre-coated plastic surface. The film thickness is very thin (30–100 nm). **Advantages:** (i) Very high purity, decorative mirror finish; (ii) Dry, solvent-free, environmentally clean.
- **Electroplating:** A plastic part is first rendered conductive by an electroless strike, then plated electrolytically (Ni/Cr/Cu) in an aqueous cell. **Advantages:** (i) Thick, durable, abrasion-resistant film (10–25 µm); (ii) Excellent EMI shielding and corrosion resistance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to mention the **electroless pre-conditioning step** before electroplating. Marks are docked if it is not stated.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — 3 Marks
**"What is the role of a quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) in vacuum metallizing equipment?"**

**Model Answer:**
The QCM is an *in-situ* thin-film thickness monitor. Its resonant frequency decreases linearly with mass loading as metal deposits on its surface. By measuring the frequency shift $\Delta f$, the controller calculates deposition rate and accumulated film thickness in real time, allowing automated shutter control to achieve target thickness — critical for KTU benchmark 30–100 nm decorative coatings.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Choice Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**Q.A(a)** Describe the construction and working of a **batch-type vacuum metallizing plant** for plastic components, with a neat block diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

The batch vacuum metallizing plant consists of the following subsystems:

1. **Vacuum Chamber (1 mark):** A stainless-steel bell-jar or box-coater housing the substrate holder, evaporation sources, masks, and monitoring heads. Sealed with Viton O-rings.
2. **Pumping System (1 mark):** Two-stage — a **rotary vane pump** (rough vacuum, 10⁻² mbar) backed by a **diffusion pump or turbomolecular pump** (high vacuum, 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁶ mbar).
3. **Evaporation Source (2 marks):** Tungsten helical filament or dimpled boat, resistively heated. Aluminium wire is fed automatically onto the boat, where it melts at 660 °C and evaporates at ~1250 °C. The vapour travels in straight lines to the substrates.
4. **Substrate Holder (1 mark):** A planetary calotte rotating at 5–20 rpm ensures uniform coating on complex 3-D plastic parts.
5. **Monitoring & Control (1 mark):** QCM rate monitor, optical pyrometer, ionization + Pirani gauges, automatic shutter.
6. **Working sequence (1 mark):** Load → pump-down (45 min) → preheat → evaporate → close shutter → vent → unload. Cycle time 60–90 min.

**Q.A(b)** A vacuum metallizer deposits an aluminium layer of **80 nm** thickness on **600 ABS cosmetic caps**, each of projected area **30 cm²**. Aluminium film density = **2700 kg/m³**, density correction factor = 1 (100 % packing). Calculate the **mass of aluminium** that must be evaporated assuming 60 % source-to-substrate transfer efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total substrate area**

$$
A_{\mathrm{total}} \;=\; 600 \times 30\,\mathrm{cm}^{2} \;=\; 18\,000\,\mathrm{cm}^{2} \;=\; 1.8\,\mathrm{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Required film mass**

$$
m_{\mathrm{final}} \;=\; t \times \rho_{\mathrm{f}} \times A_{\mathrm{total}} \;=\; (80 \times 10^{-9}) \times 2700 \times 1.8
$$

$$
m_{\mathrm{final}} \;=\; 2.16 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.8 \times 10^{0} \;\Rightarrow\; m_{\mathrm{final}} \;=\; 3.888 \times 10^{-4}\,\mathrm{kg} \;=\; 0.389\,\mathrm{g}
$$

**Step 3 — Account for transfer efficiency (60 %)**

$$
m_{\mathrm{required}} \;=\; \frac{m_{\mathrm{final}}}{\eta_{\mathrm{trans}}} \;=\; \frac{0.389}{0.60} \;\approx\; 0.648\,\mathrm{g}
$$

**Step 4 — Wire-feed or pellet quantity**

For aluminium wire $\rho_{\mathrm{b}} = 2700$ kg/m³, the equivalent volume = $2.4 \times 10^{-7}$ m³ — a tiny load, achievable in under 5 s of evaporation. **The bottleneck is thermal management, not material.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students forget the **transfer efficiency factor** (typically 40–70 % in industrial systems). Always multiply deposited mass by $1/\eta$ to find the actual amount evaporated from the source.

---

### Choice Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**Q.B(a)** With a labelled block diagram, explain the **construction and working of a sputter metallizing system** for plastics. Compare it with vacuum metallization. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

A **sputter metallizer** deposits metal by **plasma-driven momentum transfer** rather than thermal evaporation. Its subsystems are:

1. **Process Chamber (1 mark):** Stainless-steel vacuum vessel with multiple **magnetron cathodes** holding planar targets (Al, Cr, Ti, or alloys).
2. **Magnetron Cathode (2 marks):** A permanent magnet array beneath the target confines electrons, creating a high-density Ar⁺ plasma close to the target. This boosts sputter yield 10–100× over diode sputtering.
3. **Power Supplies (1 mark):** DC for conductive targets, pulsed-DC for oxides, RF for insulators. HIPIMS for highest density films.
4. **Gas Manifold (1 mark):** Argon at 1–10 mTorr; reactive gases (O₂, N₂) for compound films.
5. **Substrate Holder (1 mark):** Biased, rotating planetary for uniform coating on 3-D parts.
6. **Comparison with Vacuum Metallization (1 mark):**

| Parameter | Sputtering | Vacuum Evaporation |
|---|---|---|
| Energy of depositing atom | 5–100 eV | 0.1–1 eV |
| Film adhesion | Excellent | Moderate (needs primer) |
| Coating uniformity on 3-D | Better (rotating, biased) | Poorer (line-of-sight) |
| Target material flexibility | Metals, alloys, oxides, ceramics | Mostly metals (Al, Au, Ag) |
| Deposition rate | Slower (1–10 nm/s) | Faster (10–100 nm/s) |

**Q.B(b)** A decorative **chrome-effect coating** is applied to ABS by sputter deposition. The sputtering yield for Ar⁺ on Cr is **Y = 0.9**, ion current density $j_{\mathrm{i}} = 80$ A/m², substrate area = **0.4 m²**, deposition time = **45 s**. Compute **(i)** deposition rate and **(ii)** film thickness achieved. Molar mass Cr = **52.00 g/mol**, density Cr film = **7140 kg/m³**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Assemble constants**

$$
M_{\mathrm{Cr}} = 0.052\,\mathrm{kg/mol}, \quad \rho_{\mathrm{f}} = 7140\,\mathrm{kg/m^{3}}, \quad N_{\mathrm{A}} = 6.022 \times 10^{23}, \quad e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\,\mathrm{C}
$$

**Step 2 — Sputtering rate**

$$
R_{\mathrm{s}} \;=\; \frac{Y\,j_{\mathrm{i}}\,M}{\rho_{\mathrm{f}}\,N_{\mathrm{A}}\,e} \;=\; \frac{0.9 \times 80 \times 0.052}{7140 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}
$$

**Numerator:** $0.9 \times 80 \times 0.052 = 3.744$

**Denominator:**

$$
7140 \times (6.022 \times 1.602) \times 10^{4} \;\approx\; 7140 \times 9.647 \times 10^{4} \;\approx\; 6.888 \times 10^{8}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute**

$$
R_{\mathrm{s}} \;\approx\; \frac{3.744}{6.888 \times 10^{8}} \;\approx\; 5.43 \times 10^{-9}\,\mathrm{m/s} \;=\; 5.43\,\mathrm{nm/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Thickness for 45 s**

$$
t_{\mathrm{film}} \;=\; R_{\mathrm{s}} \times \tau \;=\; 5.43 \times 10^{-9} \times 45 \;\approx\; 2.44 \times 10^{-7}\,\mathrm{m} \;=\; 244\,\mathrm{nm}
$$

This thickness (~ 0.24 µm) is within the typical decorative chrome-effect range (50–300 nm), giving the signature mirror finish.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Mixing up **mass [kg]** vs **molar mass [kg/mol]**. Always carry the unit through to the answer. Also remember to convert ion current density units (A/m² = C/(s·m²)) correctly when dividing by Faraday constants.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common marks-loss zones in Module 4 — Metallizing Equipment:**
> 1. **Forgetting pre-treatment steps** (plasma/corona + base coat) before listing vacuum metallizing components — examiner docks **1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing PVD with CVD** — PVD = physical (no chemical reaction), CVD = chemical precursor decomposition. Cost **1 mark** if confused.
> 3. **Wrong vacuum threshold** — always state pressure in both **mbar and Torr**; the KTU-suggested benchmark is **≤ 10⁻⁴ mbar (≈ 10⁻⁴ Torr)**.
> 4. **Skipping pump stack details** — students must list at least the rough pump **and** the high-vacuum pump with their typical working pressures.
> 5. **Numerical errors in Faraday's Law** — incorrect placement of $z$ or using $F = 96\,500$ C/mol (should be **96 485** for KTU grading rigour, but **96 500** acceptable).
> 6. **Ignoring transfer efficiency** in vacuum-metallizing mass calculations — common 1-mark deduction.
> 7. **No block diagram** — for a 7-mark descriptive question, a diagram is mandatory. Marks are allotted in the question paper scheme.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Metallizing equipment refers to the integrated hardware that deposits an adherent metallic film on plastic substrates for decorative, functional, or barrier purposes.
- **Primary routes:** Vacuum (PVD) evaporation, sputtering, electroplating, electroless plating, hot-stamping, spray metallization.
- **Vacuum metallizer** works at ≤ **10⁻⁴ mbar**; uses a **rotary + diffusion/turbo pump stack**, **Al wire fed onto tungsten filament**, **planetary calotte**, **QCM monitor**.
- **Sputter metallizer** uses **Ar plasma + magnetron cathode**; superior adhesion, flexible targets (Al, Cr, Ti, oxides), lower deposition rate.
- **Electroplating line** needs **electroless pre-conditioning** (Pd/Sn activation → electroless Ni or Cu) before electrolytic Ni/Cr/Cu.
- **Faraday's Law** governs electroplating thickness; current efficiency typically **90–98 %** for sulfamate nickel.
- **Mean-free-path rule:** $\lambda \approx 0.66/P[\mathrm{mPa}]$ for $T=300$ K. For line-of-sight PVD, $\lambda \gg$ chamber dimension.
- **Decorative thickness range:** 30–100 nm; **Functional/EMI range:** 5–25 µm.
- **Adhesion testing standards:** ASTM D3359 (cross-hatch), ASTM B533 (plastics), ISO 2409.
- **Typical applications:** Automotive trim, cosmetic caps, EMI enclosures, decorative packaging, lighting reflectors, sanitaryware.
- **Emerging technologies:** Atomic layer deposition (ALD), HIPIMS, roll-to-roll PVD for films, in-mould metallization with conductive additives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute KTU Mnemonic — "VASE-EH"** for metallizing equipment classes:
> **V**acuum Metallization · **A**tomic Layer / **A**lloy Sputtering · **S**pray (arc/powder) · **E**lectroplating · **E**lectroless · **H**ot-stamping foil.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT864 - PLASTICS & DECORATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Coating Application

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coating Application** is defined as the controlled deposition of a liquid, powder, or vapor-phase decorative/functional film onto a plastic substrate in order to enhance aesthetic appeal, surface protection, barrier properties, or to impart specific functional characteristics such as conductivity, scratch resistance, or UV stability.

In the context of the KTU **PEPOT864 – Plastics & Decoration** syllabus (2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 aligned), coating application is treated as one of the **three primary decoration routes** for plastics, alongside printing and in-mold decoration. The process essentially involves **film formation** — converting a fluid/powder coating into a continuous, adherent solid film on a thermoplastic or thermoset substrate.

The standard SI unit for **coating film thickness** is the **micrometer ($\mu m$)**, with typical industrial coatings on plastics ranging from **5–250 $\mu m$** (decorative) up to **500 $\mu m$** (heavy-duty protective coatings).

### 1.1.1 Classification of Coating Application Methods

The broad families of coating application techniques are:

| Family | Physical State of Coating | Substrate Contact Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Spray coating** | Liquid atomization | Non-contact |
| **Dip coating** | Liquid immersion | Full immersion |
| **Roll / Knife coating** | Liquid metered by roller | Contact |
| **Flow / Curtain coating** | Liquid gravity/pressure flow | Non-contact (gap) |
| **Spin coating** | Liquid centrifugal spread | Contact (substrate spins) |
| **Powder coating** | Solid powder (electrostatic/fluidized) | Contact/spray |
| **Vacuum metallization** | Vapor-phase metal | Vacuum chamber |
| **Electrodeposition** | Liquid ionic dispersion | Immersion + electric field |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Painting a Wall vs. Wrapping a Gift":**
> Think of coating application like **painting a wall** in your house. The wall (plastic part) needs to be clean (pre-treated), you choose a brush or roller (application method), you control how much paint goes on (viscosity/thickness), and you let it dry properly (curing). Different rollers (rollers, sprays, dips) are just different *delivery tools* — the chemistry of the paint (coating) stays the same, but the **physics of delivery** changes how the final finish looks.

In industry, the "wall" is often ABS, PC, PMMA, PP, or PE — and the "paint" is a specially formulated polyurethane, acrylic, epoxy, or metallic vapor that must adhere to a typically **low-surface-energy** polymer without peeling.

## 1.3 Why Coating Application is Critical in Plastics Decoration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Engineering Justifications:**
> 1. **Aesthetic Value Enhancement** — Color, gloss, metallic effects, soft-touch feel.
> 2. **Functional Surface Engineering** — Scratch resistance (Mar resistance), anti-fog, anti-microbial, EMI shielding.
> 3. **Barrier Performance** — Moisture, oxygen, and chemical permeation control (relevant for plastic fuel tanks, food packaging, medical devices).

## 1.4 Pre-Coating Substrate Preparation (Mandatory)

Before *any* coating application, the plastic substrate **must** be prepared to overcome its inherently low surface energy. The standard sequence is:

**Clean → Activate → Prime → Coat → Cure**

- **Cleaning:** Removal of mold-release agents, dust, oils (using isopropyl alcohol or aqueous detergents).
- **Activation:** Increasing surface energy above the coating's surface tension. Common techniques:
  - **Corona discharge treatment** (typical dosage: 38–44 dyne/cm)
  - **Plasma treatment** (oxygen/argon plasma)
  - **Flame treatment** (for polyolefins like PP, PE)
  - **Chemical etching** (chromic-sulfuric acid for polyolefins)

> [!NOTE]
> **Wetting Rule of Thumb:** For adequate wetting, the **substrate surface energy must exceed the coating surface tension by at least 8–10 dyne/cm**. This is the single most-examined concept in coating application questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Film Formation

Coating application, irrespective of the technique used, follows a universal **four-stage physical model**:

1. **Transport Stage:** The coating material is delivered from reservoir → application point.
2. **Deposition Stage:** Material contacts the substrate (atomization, immersion, or contact).
3. **Spreading/Wetting Stage:** Liquid flows to form a uniform film under surface tension & gravity.
4. **Solidification Stage:** Film forms via solvent evaporation, chemical crosslinking, or cooling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Each application method essentially differs in *how* Stage 2 is executed — but Stages 1, 3, and 4 are governed by the same underlying physics (rheology, surface chemistry, thermodynamics).

## 2.2 Detailed Method Analysis

### 2.2.1 Spray Coating

**Operational Principle:** Liquid coating is atomized into fine droplets via pressurized air or hydraulic pressure and propelled onto the substrate.

**Sub-variants:**
- **Conventional Air Spray:** Uses compressed air (3–5 bar); transfer efficiency ≈ **30–40%** (high overspray losses).
- **Airless Spray:** Uses hydraulic pressure (100–250 bar); no air mixing; transfer efficiency ≈ **50–60%**.
- **HVLP (High Volume Low Pressure):** Atomization at <0.7 bar; transfer efficiency ≈ **65–75%** (mandated by EPA for finishing operations).
- **Electrostatic Spray:** Droplets charged at 30–100 kV; attracted to grounded substrate; transfer efficiency up to **90–95%**.

### 2.2.2 Dip Coating

**Operational Principle:** Substrate is immersed in coating tank, withdrawn at a controlled velocity, allowing film thickness to be governed by **gravity drainage** and **viscosity**.

**Landau–Levich–Derjaguin (LLD) Theory** governs the entrained film thickness $h_0$ for Newtonian liquids:

$$h_0 = 0.944 \cdot \frac{(\eta \cdot U)^{2/3}}{\gamma_{LV}^{1/6} \cdot (\rho \cdot g)^{1/2}}$$

Where:
- $\eta$ = liquid viscosity (Pa·s)
- $U$ = withdrawal speed (m/s)
- $\gamma_{LV}$ = liquid–vapor surface tension (N/m)
- $\rho$ = liquid density (kg/m³)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/s²**)

### 2.2.3 Roll Coating

**Operational Principle:** Coating is metered by the gap between two counter-rotating rollers (direct roll, reverse roll, or kiss roll configurations). Provides the **most precise thickness control** for flat plastic sheets and films.

### 2.2.4 Spin Coating

**Operational Principle:** Liquid is dispensed onto a rapidly rotating substrate (500–6000 rpm); centrifugal force spreads the fluid uniformly. Used for **thin, uniform optical coatings** (anti-reflective coatings on polycarbonate lenses, photoresist in microelectronics).

Theoretical film thickness (for Newtonian fluid):

$$h = \frac{h_0}{\left(1 + \frac{4 \rho \omega^2}{3 \eta} \cdot h_0^2 \cdot t\right)^{1/2}}$$

### 2.2.5 Powder Coating on Plastics

**Operational Principle:** Electrostatic powder is sprayed onto a (pre-heated or post-heated) plastic substrate, where it melts and flows to form a continuous film. Requires plastics with sufficient thermal resistance (e.g., **Nylon, PBT, PPS, PC/ABS blends**). Pure PP and PE typically cannot be powder-coated due to thermal degradation below the powder melt-flow temperature.

### 2.2.6 Vacuum Metallization (Vapor-Phase Coating)

**Operational Principle:** Aluminum (or other metal) is **evaporated under high vacuum** ($10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$ torr) and condensed onto a cold plastic substrate to form a mirror-like reflective film. Used for decorative chrome-effect plastics (cosmetic caps, automotive trim, Christmas ornaments).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Vacuum metallization requires a **basecoat** (to smooth substrate roughness) and a **topcoat** (to protect the thin metal layer from abrasion). Always mention both in exam answers.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are the **most frequently tested** numerical/derivation concepts for this topic in KTU ESE papers.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Key Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wet-to-Dry Film Thickness | $t_{dry} = t_{wet} \times \frac{\% \text{ solids}}{100}$ | $t_{dry}, t_{wet}$ in $\mu m$ |
| 2 | Theoretical Coverage / Spread Rate | $SR = \frac{\% \text{ solids} \times 10}{d_{dry} \cdot \rho_{wet}}$ | $SR$ in m²/L; $d_{dry}$ in $\mu m$; $\rho_{wet}$ in g/cm³ |
| 3 | Dip Coating Film Thickness (LLD) | $h_0 = 0.944 \frac{(\eta U)^{2/3}}{\gamma_{LV}^{1/6}(\rho g)^{1/2}}$ | $h_0$ in m; $\eta$ in Pa·s; $U$ in m/s |
| 4 | Capillary Number (Wetting Criterion) | $Ca = \frac{\eta U}{\gamma_{LV}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Weber Number (Atomization) | $We = \frac{\rho v^2 d}{\gamma_{LV}}$ | Dimensionless; $v$ = droplet velocity, $d$ = droplet diameter |
| 6 | Reynolds Number (Roll Coating) | $Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\eta}$ | $D$ = roll diameter |
| 7 | Spin Coating Film Thickness | $h(t) = \frac{h_0}{\sqrt{1 + \frac{4\rho \omega^2 h_0^2}{3\eta}t}}$ | $h_0$ = initial thickness; $\omega$ in rad/s |
| 8 | Surface Energy Wetting Condition | $\gamma_{SV} > \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}$ | Young's equation condition (spreading) |
| 9 | Adhesion Work (Young–Dupré) | $W_a = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL}$ | $W_a$ in J/m² |
| 10 | Drying Time (Solvent Evaporation) | $t_{dry} \propto \frac{h^2 \cdot \eta}{D \cdot \gamma_{LV} \cdot P_{sat}}$ | $D$ = diffusion coeff; $P_{sat}$ = vapor pressure |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitution Note:** In the formula table above, when presenting absolute values inside math expressions in any future use (e.g., $\vert x \vert$), always use the LaTeX commands `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

| Industry Sector | Coating Application Used | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Automotive (interior & exterior) | Electrostatic spray + clearcoat | Gloss, scratch resistance, color matching |
| Mobile phones & electronics | Vacuum metallization + UV topcoat | Metallic finish on PC/ABS |
| Medical devices | Dip coating (Parylene, silicone) | Biocompatibility, drug release |
| Optical lenses | Spin coating (AR coating) | Anti-reflection |
| Flexible packaging | Gravure / roll coating | Barrier layers (PVDC, EVOH) |
| Aerospace composites | Powder coating | Thermal & chemical resistance |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wet-to-Dry Film Thickness Conversion

This is the **most commonly asked numerical** in KTU exams for this topic. Given the wet film thickness and the volume fraction of non-volatile solids, derive the resulting dry film thickness.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Given:** A coating has wet film thickness $t_{wet}$ and contains $V_s$ % solids (by volume). After solvent evaporation, the solids remain to form the dry film.

**Step 1 — Set up the conservation of solids volume per unit area:**

Consider a unit area $A$ of substrate. The volume of wet coating applied:

$$V_{wet} = t_{wet} \cdot A$$

**Step 2 — Identify the volume of solids present in the wet film:**

The solids fraction by volume is $\dfrac{V_s}{100}$, so:

$$V_{solids} = \frac{V_s}{100} \cdot V_{wet} = \frac{V_s}{100} \cdot t_{wet} \cdot A$$

**Step 3 — Equate to dry film volume (since solids alone form the dry film):**

$$V_{dry} = t_{dry} \cdot A$$

**Step 4 — Set $V_{solids} = V_{dry}$ and solve for $t_{dry}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{dry} \cdot A &= \frac{V_s}{100} \cdot t_{wet} \cdot A \\
t_{dry} &= \frac{V_s}{100} \cdot t_{wet}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{t_{dry} = \frac{V_s \cdot t_{wet}}{100}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A coating with 40% solids applied at 100 µm wet film produces only **40 µm dry film** — a critical number for quality control and cost estimation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Theoretical Coverage (Spread Rate)

**Given:** Coating density $\rho_{wet}$ (g/cm³), solids content $V_s$ (%), and desired dry film thickness $d_{dry}$ ($\mu m$). Find the area covered per litre of wet coating.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Express one litre of coating in mass terms:**

$$m_{wet} = 1000 \cdot \rho_{wet} \;\; \text{(grams, since 1 L = 1000 cm³)}$$

**Step 2 — Determine the mass of solids in one litre:**

The solids content is given by volume fraction $V_s/100$. So the volume of solids per litre is:

$$V_{solids\_perL} = \frac{V_s}{100} \times 1000 = 10 \cdot V_s \;\; \text{cm}^3$$

The mass of solids per litre is:

$$m_{solids} = V_{solids\_perL} \cdot \rho_{dry}$$

For simplicity, assume the wet and dry densities are approximately equal (typical assumption for moderate solids):

$$m_{solids} \approx 10 \cdot V_s \cdot \rho_{wet} \;\; \text{grams}$$

**Step 3 — Determine the volume of dry film produced:**

The dry film of thickness $d_{dry}$ (in $\mu m$) covering an area $A$ (m²) has volume:

$$V_{dry\_film} = A \cdot d_{dry} \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{4} = A \cdot d_{dry} \times 10^{-2} \;\; \text{L}$$

Wait — let me convert carefully. $d_{dry}$ in $\mu m = d_{dry} \times 10^{-6}$ m. Area in m². So volume in m³:

$$V_{dry\_film} = A \cdot d_{dry} \times 10^{-6} \;\; \text{m}^3 = A \cdot d_{dry} \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{L}$$

**Step 4 — Equate solids mass to dry film mass and solve for coverage:**

Equating solids mass to dry film mass (since solids = dry film):

$$\begin{aligned}
10 \cdot V_s \cdot \rho_{wet} \cdot 10^{-3} \;\; \text{kg} &= A \cdot d_{dry} \times 10^{-6} \cdot \rho_{dry} \;\; \text{kg} \\
\frac{A}{V_{wet}} &= \frac{10 \cdot V_s}{d_{dry} \cdot \rho_{wet}} \;\; \text{m}^2/\text{L}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{SR = \frac{10 \cdot V_s}{d_{dry} \cdot \rho_{wet}} \;\; \text{m}^2/\text{L}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> **[KTU Numerical — Module 4 Pattern]:**
> A polyurethane coating has 35% solids by volume and wet density of 1.05 g/cm³. It is applied at a wet film thickness of 120 µm on an ABS housing. Determine:
> (i) Dry film thickness
> (ii) Theoretical coverage in m²/L
> (iii) How many litres are required to coat 250 parts, each having a coating area of 0.08 m², if two coats are applied?

### Solution:

**Part (i):** Dry film thickness

$$t_{dry} = \frac{35 \times 120}{100} = 42 \;\; \mu m$$

**Part (ii):** Theoretical coverage

$$SR = \frac{10 \times 35}{42 \times 1.05} = \frac{350}{44.1} \approx 7.94 \;\; \text{m}^2/\text{L}$$

**Part (iii):** Volume required

Total area to coat = $250 \times 0.08 \times 2 = 40 \;\; \text{m}^2$ (factor of 2 for two coats).

Volume of wet coating needed:

$$V_{wet} = \frac{40}{7.94} \approx 5.04 \;\; \text{L}$$

Including a **10% loss factor** (typical overspray/wastage for spray application):

$$V_{actual} = 5.04 \times 1.10 \approx 5.54 \;\; \text{L}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Capillary Number and Wetting Criterion

The **Capillary Number ($Ca$)** determines whether a coating will wet a substrate uniformly or dewet/bead up.

**Step 1 — Define the dimensionless groups:**

$$Ca = \frac{\text{Viscous forces}}{\text{Surface tension forces}} = \frac{\eta \cdot U}{\gamma_{LV}}$$

**Step 2 — Define Bond Number for gravitational drainage:**

$$Bo = \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot L^2}{\gamma_{LV}}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the operating regimes:**

| Regime | Condition | Physical Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Capillary-dominated | $Ca \ll 1$, $Bo \ll 1$ | Smooth, uniform film |
| Viscous-gravity | $Ca \gg 1$, $Bo \gg 1$ | Thick, non-uniform (runs/sags) |
| Dewetting | $Ca < Ca_{critical}$ | Coating beads up; poor adhesion |

**Step 4 — The critical capillary number for entrainment in dip coating:**

$$Ca_{crit} \approx \frac{(\rho g)^{1/2} \cdot \eta^{1/6}}{\gamma_{LV}^{1/3}}$$

For typical polyurethane coatings on corona-treated ABS:
- $\eta \approx 0.5$ Pa·s, $U = 0.05$ m/s, $\gamma_{LV} \approx 0.035$ N/m
- $Ca = (0.5 \times 0.05)/0.035 \approx 0.71$
- Acceptable wetting (well above $Ca_{crit}$).

---

## 3.5 Defect–Cause–Remedy Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Table — Defects in Coating Application.** Memorize this for full marks in Part B.

| Defect | Visual Appearance | Probable Cause | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Orange peel** | Rough, dimpled surface (like skin of an orange) | Inadequate atomization, high viscosity, rapid solvent flash | Reduce viscosity; use slower solvent; increase atomization air |
| **Runs/Sags** | Drooping, curtain-like flow on vertical surfaces | Excessive wet film; low viscosity; over-application | Reduce flow rate; apply thinner passes; increase viscosity |
| **Pinholes** | Tiny holes through coating to substrate | Dust, trapped air/solvent, contamination | Clean substrate; adjust flash-off time; thinner coats |
| **Cratering** | Small bowl-shaped depressions | Surface tension mismatch (silicone contamination) | Eliminate contamination; add wetting agent; re-clean |
| **Crawling** | Coating retreats from substrate in patches | Poor wetting; low substrate energy | Increase corona/plasma treatment; reduce coating surface tension |
| **Bubbling** | Raised, blister-like surface | Solvent trapped under film; high humidity | Increase flash time; reduce oven ramp rate |
| **Hazing** | Loss of gloss, cloudy appearance | Incompatible solvents; moisture in coating | Use proper solvents; control humidity <70% RH |
| **Delamination** | Coating peels off | Inadequate surface prep; wrong primer | Re-prep substrate; apply compatible primer |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Process Flow Diagram — Coating Application Decision Tree

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **selection logic** for choosing the correct coating method based on part geometry, production volume, and coating type — a frequent KTU exam concept.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Plastic Part to be Coated] --> B{Part Geometry}
    B -- Simple Flat Sheet --> C[Roll Coating]
    B -- Complex 3D Shape --> D{Production Volume}
    B -- Optical Lens / Thin Film --> E[Spin Coating]
    D -- Low Volume --> F[Dip Coating]
    D -- Medium Volume --> G[Spray Coating]
    D -- High Volume --> H[Electrostatic Spray]
    A --> I{Special Effect Needed?}
    I -- Metallic Chrome Finish --> J[Vacuum Metallization]
    I -- Functional Barrier Film --> K[Extrusion Coating]
    I -- Decorative Pattern --> L[Printing Methods]
    C --> M[Quality Check Thickness and Adhesion]
    E --> M
    F --> M
    G --> M
    H --> M
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Final Curing Stage]
    N --> O[End: Finished Decorated Plastic Part]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Spray Coating System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Air Compressor 3 to 5 bar] --> B1[Air Filter and Regulator]
    A2[Coating Reservoir] --> B2[Pressure Pot or Pump]
    B1 --> C1[Spray Gun Atomizer]
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Atomized Droplets]
    D1 --> E1[Plastic Substrate on Conveyor]
    E1 --> F1[Flash Off Zone]
    F1 --> G1[Oven Curing 60 to 80 deg C]
    G1 --> H1[Cooling Zone]
    H1 --> I1[QC Inspection Station]
    I1 --> J1[Output Finished Part]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Vacuum Metallization Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V1[Load Plastic Part on Rotating Fixture] --> V2[Close Vacuum Chamber]
    V2 --> V3[Evacuate to 10 to the power minus 5 torr]
    V3 --> V4[Heat Tungsten Filament with Aluminum Wire]
    V4 --> V5[Aluminum Vapor Deposits on Substrate]
    V5 --> V6[Film Thickness Monitor Quartz Crystal]
    V6 -- Target Thickness Reached --> V7[Shut Off Filament Power]
    V7 --> V8[Vent Chamber with Inert Gas]
    V8 --> V9[Unload Metallized Part]
    V9 --> V10[Apply Protective Topcoat]
    V10 --> V11[End: Decorative Metallized Plastic]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Dip Coating Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Step A: Surface Preparation Cleaning and Corona] --> D2[Step B: Lowering into Coating Tank]
    D2 --> D3[Step C: Immersion Dwell Time]
    D3 --> D4[Step D: Controlled Withdrawal Speed U]
    D4 --> D5[Step E: Drainage and Film Formation Landau Levich]
    D5 --> D6[Step F: Flash Off Solvent Evaporation]
    D6 --> D7[Step G: Thermal Curing Oven]
    D7 --> D8[Step H: Inspection Film Thickness and Adhesion]
    D8 --> D9[End: Coated Plastic Component]
```

## 4.5 Comparative Selection Chart — Method vs. Plastic Substrate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Plastic Substrate Type] --> S2{Is Substrate Thermally Stable Above 180 deg C?}
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Powder Coating Possible]
    S2 -- No --> S4{Is Optical Clarity Required?}
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[Spin Coating or Vacuum Metallization]
    S4 -- No --> S6{Is Metallic Finish Desired?}
    S6 -- Yes --> S7[Vacuum Metallization]
    S6 -- No --> S8{Is Part Hollow or Tubular?}
    S8 -- Yes --> S9[Dip Coating Recommended]
    S8 -- No --> S10[Roll or Spray Coating]
    S3 --> Z[Choose Best Method]
    S5 --> Z
    S7 --> Z
    S9 --> Z
    S10 --> Z
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU:** Examiner's often ask: *"Recommend a coating method for the following part..."* — Use the decision tree above as a structured answer. You'll score bonus marks for **justification**, not just naming the method.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**Q:** Define *coating application* in the context of plastics decoration. List any **four** major methods used for applying coatings to plastic substrates.

**Model Answer:**
Coating application refers to the controlled deposition of a liquid, powder, or vapor-phase coating material onto a plastic substrate to obtain a continuous, adherent film for decorative or functional purposes.

The four major methods are:
1. **Spray coating** (conventional, airless, HVLP, electrostatic)
2. **Dip coating**
3. **Roll coating**
4. **Vacuum metallization**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**Q:** Why is **surface preparation** essential before coating plastic substrates? Mention two common pre-treatment methods.

**Model Answer:**
Most plastics (especially polyolefins like PP, PE) have **low surface energy**, which leads to poor wetting and adhesion of coatings. Surface preparation increases surface energy above the coating's surface tension, ensuring proper wetting and adhesion.

Two common methods:
1. **Corona discharge treatment** — exposes surface to high-voltage discharge, increasing surface energy to 38–44 dyne/cm.
2. **Chemical etching** — uses chromic-sulfuric acid to oxidize surface and create polar functional groups.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern:** Module 4 typically offers **internal choice**. Two independent 14-mark questions are provided below — answer **any ONE**.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **spray coating technique** for plastics. Discuss the **four sub-variants** (conventional, airless, HVLP, electrostatic) with their working principles, typical operating parameters, and **transfer efficiency** values.

**(b)** A coating has **50% solids by volume** and a wet density of **1.10 g/cm³**. It is applied to give a **wet film thickness of 150 µm**.
   (i) Calculate the dry film thickness.
   (ii) Calculate the theoretical coverage in m²/L.
   (iii) How much wet coating (in litres) is needed to coat **200 parts** each of **0.15 m² area**, assuming **two coats** and a **15% wastage factor**?

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) — Spray Coating Technique (7 Marks):**

**Working Principle:**
In spray coating, the liquid coating is **atomized** into fine droplets via compressed air or hydraulic pressure and projected onto the substrate. The droplets coalesce on impact to form a continuous wet film, which subsequently dries/cures.

**The Four Sub-Variants:**

| Variant | Atomization Mechanism | Operating Pressure | Transfer Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Conventional Air Spray | Compressed air shears liquid stream | 3–5 bar | **30–40%** |
| Airless Spray | Hydraulic pressure forces liquid through small orifice | 100–250 bar | **50–60%** |
| HVLP (High Volume Low Pressure) | High volume of low-pressure air | < 0.7 bar | **65–75%** |
| Electrostatic Spray | Charged droplets (30–100 kV) attracted to grounded part | Atomizing air + electric field | **90–95%** |

**Detailed Discussion:**

1. **Conventional Air Spray:** Most common; produces fine finish but high overspray loss. Used for low-volume, high-quality finishing (automotive refinish).

2. **Airless Spray:** No air mixing; suitable for high-viscosity coatings and large surface areas (truck bodies, structural plastics). Faster but coarser finish.

3. **HVLP Spray:** Mandated by **EPA (USA) regulations** for finishing operations due to low overspray and reduced VOC emissions. Provides high-quality finish with improved transfer efficiency.

4. **Electrostatic Spray:** Droplets are given a **negative charge** (30–100 kV). The grounded plastic substrate (sometimes pre-treated with a conductive primer) attracts the droplets, including those that would otherwise be lost as overspray. This dramatically increases transfer efficiency (up to 95%) and is the preferred method for **complex 3D plastic parts** in the automotive and consumer electronics industries.

**Operational Parameters Common to All:**
- **Spray distance:** 15–25 cm
- **Gun movement speed:** 0.5–1.0 m/s
- **Wet film build per pass:** 25–75 µm
- **Flash-off time between coats:** 5–10 minutes

**(b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $V_s = 50\%$, $\rho_{wet} = 1.10$ g/cm³, $t_{wet} = 150$ µm, $n = 200$ parts, $A_{part} = 0.15$ m², 2 coats, 15% wastage.

**(i) Dry film thickness:**

$$t_{dry} = \frac{V_s \times t_{wet}}{100} = \frac{50 \times 150}{100} = 75 \;\; \mu m$$

**(ii) Theoretical coverage:**

$$SR = \frac{10 \times V_s}{d_{dry} \times \rho_{wet}} = \frac{10 \times 50}{75 \times 1.10} = \frac{500}{82.5} \approx 6.06 \;\; \text{m}^2/\text{L}$$

**(iii) Volume required:**

Total coating area:

$$A_{total} = 200 \times 0.15 \times 2 = 60 \;\; \text{m}^2$$

Volume of coating:

$$V_{wet} = \frac{A_{total}}{SR} = \frac{60}{6.06} \approx 9.90 \;\; \text{L}$$

Including 15% wastage:

$$V_{actual} = 9.90 \times 1.15 \approx 11.39 \;\; \text{L}$$

**Final Answer:** Approximately **11.4 litres** of wet coating is required.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **vacuum metallization process** for plastics with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of **basecoat and topcoat**, and list any **five applications**.

**(b)** With reference to the **dip coating technique**, derive the **Landau–Levich–Derjaguin equation** for the entrained film thickness. State clearly the assumptions made.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) — Vacuum Metallization (7 Marks):**

**Process Description:**

Vacuum metallization is a **Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD)** technique in which a metal (typically aluminum) is evaporated under high vacuum and condensed onto a cold plastic substrate to form a thin, mirror-like metallic film.

**Block Diagram (refer Section 4.3 of these notes):** The process consists of: Load → Vacuum → Evaporate → Deposit → Vent → Unload → Topcoat.

**Key Process Parameters:**
- **Vacuum level:** $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$ torr (≈ $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$ Pa)
- **Metal:** Aluminum (99.99% purity) — cheap, low melting point (660°C)
- **Heating source:** Tungsten filament or electron beam
- **Film thickness:** 30–100 nm (typical decorative layer)
- **Deposition rate:** ~50–100 nm/second

**Role of Basecoat:**
The basecoat is a **smooth, pigmented lacquer** applied to the plastic substrate **before** metallization. It:
- Smooths out surface roughness (plastic surfaces are not mirror-smooth).
- Provides the **color** of the final finish (gold basecoat + aluminum → gold metallic appearance).
- Improves adhesion between plastic and metal layer.
- Releases volatiles that could otherwise cause **outgassing** in the vacuum chamber (which would cloud the metal film).

**Role of Topcoat:**
The topcoat is a **transparent protective lacquer** applied **over** the metal layer. It:
- Protects the soft aluminum layer from **abrasion, oxidation, and fingerprints**.
- Provides **gloss control** (matte, semi-gloss, high gloss).
- Adds chemical resistance and weatherability.

**Five Applications:**
1. Cosmetic caps and closures (chrome-look on PP).
2. Automotive interior trim (metallic accents on PC/ABS).
3. Christmas ornaments and decorative items.
4. Lighting reflectors and lamp housings.
5. EMI/RFI shielding enclosures for electronic devices.

**(b) — Derivation of Landau–Levich–Derjaguin Equation (7 Marks):**

**Starting Setup:**
Consider a flat plastic substrate being vertically withdrawn from a Newtonian liquid coating bath at constant velocity $U$. A thin liquid film is entrained on the substrate. The film thickness far from the meniscus (in the static bath region) is denoted $h_0$.

**Assumptions:**
1. The liquid is **Newtonian** (constant viscosity $\eta$).
2. Flow is **steady, laminar, and incompressible**.
3. The film is thin compared to the substrate dimensions.
4. **Inertia is neglected** — viscous and surface tension/gravity forces dominate.
5. The liquid–vapor interface is flat (parallel to substrate) far from the meniscus.
6. **Quasi-steady state**: at any instant, the meniscus profile is in equilibrium.

**Derivation Steps:**

**Step 1 — Identify the governing forces in the meniscus region:**

In the thin-film region near the contact line, three forces balance:
- **Viscous force** (drag from moving substrate): $F_v \sim \eta U / h$
- **Capillary (surface tension) force**: $F_c \sim \gamma_{LV} / h^2$
- **Gravitational force**: $F_g \sim \rho g$

**Step 2 — Match viscous and capillary forces (dominant balance near meniscus):**

Setting $F_v \sim F_c$:

$$\frac{\eta U}{h} \sim \frac{\gamma_{LV}}{h^2}$$

$$\Rightarrow h^2 \sim \frac{\eta U h^2}{\gamma_{LV} \cdot h} \;\;\text{(rearranging)}$$

This gives the characteristic length scale:

$$\ell_c = \left(\frac{\eta U}{\gamma_{LV}}\right)^{1/2} = \sqrt{\frac{\eta U}{\gamma_{LV}}}$$

But the **capillary length** (gravity vs. surface tension balance) is:

$$\ell_g = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma_{LV}}{\rho g}}$$

**Step 3 — Combine the two length scales via dimensional analysis:**

The film thickness $h_0$ must scale with some combination of $\ell_c$ and $\ell_g$:

$$h_0 \sim \ell_c^{2/3} \cdot \ell_g^{1/3}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back to obtain the LLD equation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
h_0 &\sim \left(\frac{\eta U}{\gamma_{LV}}\right)^{1/3} \cdot \left(\frac{\gamma_{LV}}{\rho g}\right)^{1/6} \\
h_0 &\sim \frac{(\eta U)^{1/3}}{\gamma_{LV}^{1/3}} \cdot \frac{\gamma_{LV}^{1/6}}{(\rho g)^{1/6}} \\
h_0 &\sim \frac{(\eta U)^{2/3}}{\gamma_{LV}^{1/6} (\rho g)^{1/2}}
\end{aligned}$$

The proportionality constant has been computed as **0.944** by rigorous asymptotic matching:

$$\boxed{h_0 = 0.944 \cdot \frac{(\eta U)^{2/3}}{\gamma_{LV}^{1/6} (\rho g)^{1/2}}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** Higher withdrawal speed $U$ → thicker film; higher viscosity → thicker film; higher surface tension → thinner film (better drainage); higher density → thinner film (gravity drains more).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up "solids by weight" vs "solids by volume"** in the wet-to-dry thickness formula. The formula uses **% solids by VOLUME** unless explicitly stated otherwise. Always read carefully.
> 2. **Forgetting the 10%–15% wastage/loss factor** in numericals. Real coating calculations must include overspray losses. Examiners specifically check this.
> 3. **Not mentioning surface preparation** in answers about coating application. Even if the question focuses on spray coating, you **must** mention corona/plasma pre-treatment. This is a 1–2 mark penalty if missed.
> 4. **In dip coating derivations, missing the assumption list** (Newtonian, steady, inertia-free). Examiners allocate **2 marks** for assumptions alone.
> 5. **In vacuum metallization, missing either basecoat or topcoat.** Both are **mandatory** in the full process — many students mention only one.
> 6. **Confusing "transfer efficiency" of different spray methods.** Memorize: Conventional < Airless < HVLP < Electrostatic.
> 7. **In roll coating, not mentioning the metering gap** — the gap between rollers is what controls film thickness, not the roller speed alone.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Coating Application (Module 4)**

### A. Fundamental Concepts
- [ ] Coating application = controlled deposition of fluid/powder/vapor film onto plastic substrate.
- [ ] Four-stage universal model: **Transport → Deposition → Spreading/Wetting → Solidification**.
- [ ] **Wetting Rule:** Substrate surface energy > Coating surface tension by **8–10 dyne/cm**.
- [ ] Plastic substrates require **pre-treatment** (corona, plasma, flame, etching).

### B. Application Methods — Key Facts
- [ ] **Spray coating** sub-variants: Conventional, Airless, HVLP, Electrostatic (increasing transfer efficiency).
- [ ] **Electrostatic spray** has highest transfer efficiency (~95%).
- [ ] **HVLP** is mandated by EPA for finishing operations.
- [ ] **Dip coating** film thickness given by **Landau–Levich–Derjaguin equation**.
- [ ] **Roll coating** = best thickness control for flat sheets/films.
- [ ] **Spin coating** = thin uniform films on optical components.
- [ ] **Powder coating** on plastics requires thermal stability > 180°C.
- [ ] **Vacuum metallization** = PVD of aluminum at $10^{-5}$ torr; needs basecoat + topcoat.

### C. Key Formulas to Memorize
- [ ] $t_{dry} = \dfrac{V_s \cdot t_{wet}}{100}$
- [ ] $SR = \dfrac{10 \cdot V_s}{d_{dry} \cdot \rho_{wet}}$ (m²/L)
- [ ] $h_0 = 0.944 \cdot \dfrac{(\eta U)^{2/3}}{\gamma_{LV}^{1/6} (\rho g)^{1/2}}$
- [ ] $Ca = \dfrac{\eta U}{\gamma_{LV}}$ (wetting criterion)
- [ ] Young's equation: $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$
- [ ] Adhesion work: $W_a = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL}$

### D. Defects & Remedies
- [ ] Orange peel → reduce viscosity/improve atomization.
- [ ] Runs/sags → reduce wet film thickness.
- [ ] Crawling → improve wetting (increase corona treatment).
- [ ] Pinholes → clean substrate / improve flash-off.
- [ ] Bubbling → control oven ramp rate.
- [ ] Delamination → proper surface preparation + primer.

### E. Industry Applications
- [ ] Automotive (clearcoat + basecoat electrostatic spray).
- [ ] Mobile phones (vacuum metallization + UV topcoat on PC/ABS).
- [ ] Optical lenses (spin coating for AR layers).
- [ ] Medical devices (Parylene dip coating).
- [ ] Flexible packaging (roll coating of barrier films).

### F. Examiner's High-Yield Triggers
- [ ] Always include **process flow diagrams** in long answers (2–3 extra marks).
- [ ] Always state **assumptions** in derivations.
- [ ] Always mention **surface preparation** in any coating question.
- [ ] Always include **numerical values with units** (µm, m²/L, dyne/cm, bar).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT864 - PLASTICS & DECORATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Drying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Drying** in plastics processing is defined as the controlled removal of volatile components — primarily moisture (water) and residual solvents — from a polymeric material prior to melt processing (injection moulding, extrusion, blow moulding) or surface decoration (printing, painting, metallizing, coating). The operation is a coupled **heat-and-mass transfer** process in which sensible heat is supplied to the feedstock to vaporize the absorbed liquid, while the generated vapour is carried away by a sweeping gas stream.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, drying is positioned as a **mandatory pre-processing unit operation** for hygroscopic polymers, because entrapped moisture hydrolyses the polymer chains during melting — degrading molecular weight, mechanical strength, optical clarity, and surface finish.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — PEPOT864 (Module 4):**
> The syllabus mandates study of: *principles of drying, moisture in polymers, drying equipment (desiccant, hot-air, vacuum, infrared), drying parameters (temperature, time, dew point, airflow), and the influence of inadequate drying on finished/decorated plastics.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have just washed a cotton T-shirt and need to wear it immediately.

- If you simply hang it in **humid monsoon air** (like Kerala during June–August), the water stays — because the surrounding air is already saturated and cannot accept more moisture. This is exactly what happens when plastics are dried in ordinary atmospheric air: the moisture *refuses to leave* the polymer.
- If you place the same T-shirt in a **closed room with a dehumidifier** that pulls water vapour out of the air continuously, the shirt dries fast. This is analogous to a **desiccant (dehumidified) dryer**, where the drying air has a very low **dew point** (typically **−40 °C to −60 °C**) and a high capacity to absorb moisture.
- If you put the shirt in a **microwave oven**, internal heat is generated uniformly inside the fabric — water evaporates from *within*. This corresponds to **microwave / vacuum drying** of plastics.

So: **Drying = driving water OUT of the polymer using heat + a moisture-hungry (dry) atmosphere.**

### 1.3 Why Drying is Critical — Physical Constants & Standards

- The standard unit of moisture content in plastics is **% by weight** (mass of water per 100 g of wet polymer).
- Typical permissible moisture levels at the processing machine hopper:

| Polymer | Maximum Permissible Moisture (%) |
| :--- | :--- |
| PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate) | **0.02** |
| PC (Polycarbonate) | **0.02** |
| PMMA (Polymethyl Methacrylate) | **0.10** |
| PA 6 / Nylon 6 | **0.10** |
| PA 66 / Nylon 6,6 | **0.10** |
| POM (Polyoxymethylene / Acetal) | **0.20** |
| ABS (Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene) | **0.10** |
| PBT (Polybutylene Terephthalate) | **0.04** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Atmosphere for Drying Calculations:**
> - Reference temperature: **T₀ = 0 °C = 273.15 K**
> - Reference pressure: **P₀ = 101.325 kPa**
> - Universal gas constant for water vapour: **R_w = 0.4615 kJ/(kg·K)**
> - Latent heat of vaporization of water at 100 °C: **λ ≈ 2257 kJ/kg**

### 1.4 Classification of Polymers by Hygroscopicity

- **Hygroscopic (must be dried):** PA, PC, PET, PMMA, POM, PBT, PU, ABS (borderline)
- **Non-hygroscopic (generally not dried, but surface moisture may be removed):** PE, PP, PS, PTFE, PVC (rigid)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Hygroscopic vs Non-Hygroscopic:**
> A **hygroscopic polymer** chemically or physically absorbs water into its molecular structure (hydrogen bonding with polar groups such as —CONH—, —COO—, —OH). A **non-hygroscopic polymer** only retains *surface* moisture, which evaporates easily.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Drying Mechanism — Two Sequential Stages

Drying of plastics does NOT happen in one uniform step. It occurs in **two distinct physical stages**, governed by different rate-controlling mechanisms:

#### Stage A — Surface Moisture Removal (Constant Rate Period)
- Water exists as a free film or loosely-held layer on the outer surface of the granule/pellet.
- Drying rate is governed by **external heat and mass transfer** — i.e., by the temperature, velocity, and humidity of the surrounding air.
- Surface temperature of the pellet ≈ **wet-bulb temperature** of the air (≈ constant).
- The rate is essentially constant as long as surface moisture remains.

#### Stage B — Internal Moisture Diffusion (Falling Rate Period)
- Once surface moisture is gone, the remaining water must migrate from the **interior** of the pellet to the surface.
- Drying rate is now controlled by **internal diffusion** (Fickian diffusion through the polymer matrix).
- The pellet surface temperature begins to rise toward the dry-bulb air temperature.
- This stage is **slow, temperature-sensitive, and dominates the total drying time** for engineering plastics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter for KTU?**
> Most student errors arise from assuming drying is purely a surface phenomenon. In practice, **60–80 % of the total drying time** is spent in the falling-rate period. This is why merely *blowing hot air* for 30 minutes is grossly insufficient for Nylon or PC.

### 2.2 Driving Forces in Drying

The mass-transfer driving force is the **concentration gradient** of water between the polymer interior and the surrounding air:

$$\Delta C = C_{interior} - C_{surface}^*$$

The heat-transfer driving force is the **temperature gradient** between the hot drying air and the cooler wet pellet:

$$\Delta T = T_{air} - T_{pellet}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention:** All absolute-value bars are rendered using `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity. Subscripts `w`, `db`, `wb`, `s` denote *wet-bulb*, *dry-bulb*, *saturated*, etc.

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Moisture content (wet basis) | $X_w$ | $X_w = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{wet}} \times 100$ | % |
| 2 | Moisture content (dry basis) | $X_d$ | $X_d = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{dry\,solid}}$ | kg water / kg dry solid |
| 3 | Conversion between bases | — | $X_w = \dfrac{X_d}{1 + X_d}$ | — |
| 4 | Free moisture content | $X^*$ | $X^* = X_d - X_d^{eq}$ | kg/kg |
| 5 | Drying rate | $R$ | $R = -\dfrac{dW}{d\theta}$ | kg water / (m²·s) |
| 6 | Heat-transfer rate | $Q$ | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | W |
| 7 | Mass-transfer rate (convective) | $N_A$ | $N_A = k_c \cdot A \cdot (p_w - p_w^*)$ | kg/s |
| 8 | Latent heat of vaporization | $\lambda$ | $\lambda \approx 2501 - 2.42\,T$ | kJ/kg |
| 9 | Humidity ratio (absolute) | $\omega$ | $\omega = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{p_v}{P - p_v}$ | kg water / kg dry air |
| 10 | Dew-point temperature | $T_{dp}$ | Defined as the temperature at which air becomes saturated on cooling | °C |
| 11 | Fick's First Law (internal diffusion) | $J$ | $J = -D \cdot \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | kg/(m²·s) |
| 12 | Drying time (Fickian, slab) | $\theta$ | $\dfrac{X - X^*}{X_0 - X^*} = \dfrac{8}{\pi^2}\sum \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 D\theta}{4L^2}\right)$ | s |
| 13 | Equilibrium moisture (Henderson eqn.) | $X_{eq}$ | $1 - X_{eq}/X_m = (T/C_1)^{C_2}$ | — |
| 14 | Air sensible heat | $Q_s$ | $Q_s = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$ | kW |
| 15 | Specific volume of moist air | $v$ | $v = \dfrac{R_a T}{P - p_v}\left(1 + 1.6078\,\omega\right)$ | m³/kg dry air |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Drying directly determines part quality.** Streaks, silver streaks, voids, brittleness, and poor surface gloss in injection-moulded parts almost always trace back to insufficient drying.
- **Energy economics:** Drying consumes 5–15 % of the total electrical energy in a plastics processing plant. Selecting the right dryer (desiccant vs hot-air) is a major CAPEX/OPEX decision.
- **Decoration compatibility:** Drying defects (residual moisture, surface bloom, contamination) cause print adhesion failures, paint peeling, and metallization delamination — making drying *the first* step of any decoration process chain.
- **Process digitalization:** Modern dryers integrate dew-point sensors and adaptive PID control, tying drying to **Industry 4.0** / IIoT dashboards on the shop floor.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Conversion Between Wet-Basis and Dry-Basis Moisture

**Statement:** Convert moisture content from **wet basis (% by wet mass)** to **dry basis (kg water per kg dry solid)**, and vice versa.

**Given:** A polymer sample weighs $m_{wet} = 1.050$ kg, and after complete oven drying weighs $m_{dry} = 1.000$ kg.

**Step 1 — Mass of water present.**

$$m_{water} = m_{wet} - m_{dry} = 1.050 - 1.000 = 0.050 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Compute wet-basis moisture (the standard plastics industry value).**

$$X_w = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{wet}} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.050}{1.050} \times 100 = 4.762 \text{ \%}$$

**Step 3 — Compute dry-basis moisture (used in engineering drying calculations).**

$$X_d = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{dry}} = \dfrac{0.050}{1.000} = 0.0500 \text{ kg water / kg dry solid}$$

**Step 4 — Verify the standard conversion identity.**

$$X_w = \dfrac{X_d}{1 + X_d} = \dfrac{0.0500}{1 + 0.0500} = 0.04762 = 4.762 \text{ \%} \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Required to Dry a Polymer Batch

**Statement:** Calculate the total thermal energy required to dry 500 kg of Nylon 6 from an initial moisture content of 1.0 % (wet basis) to a final moisture content of 0.08 % (wet basis). The drying air is supplied at 80 °C and the polymer enters at 25 °C. Use $c_{p,polymer} \approx 1.7$ kJ/(kg·K).

**Step 1 — Convert wet-basis to dry-basis for both states.**

$$X_{d,i} = \dfrac{0.010}{1 - 0.010} = 0.01010 \text{ kg/kg}$$

$$X_{d,f} = \dfrac{0.0008}{1 - 0.0008} = 0.000801 \text{ kg/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Compute mass of water to be removed per kg of dry polymer.**

$$\Delta X = X_{d,i} - X_{d,f} = 0.01010 - 0.000801 = 0.009299 \text{ kg water / kg dry solid}$$

**Step 3 — Total dry solid and water removed (basis: 500 kg wet feed).**

$$m_{dry} = \dfrac{500}{1 + X_{d,i}} = \dfrac{500}{1.01010} = 495.0 \text{ kg dry polymer}$$

$$m_{water,removed} = 495.0 \times 0.009299 = 4.603 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Sensible heat to raise polymer from 25 °C to 80 °C.**

$$Q_{sens} = m_{dry} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 495.0 \times 1.7 \times (80 - 25)$$

$$Q_{sens} = 495.0 \times 1.7 \times 55 = 46\,282.5 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 5 — Latent heat to vaporize the removed water at ~80 °C.**

$$\lambda_{80} = 2501 - 2.42 \times 80 = 2501 - 193.6 = 2307.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$Q_{lat} = m_{water,removed} \cdot \lambda = 4.603 \times 2307.4 = 10\,622 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 6 — Total thermal energy (neglecting dryer wall losses).**

$$Q_{total} = Q_{sens} + Q_{lat} = 46\,282.5 + 10\,622 = 56\,904.5 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 7 — Express in kWh (1 kWh = 3600 kJ).**

$$E = \dfrac{56\,904.5}{3600} = 15.81 \text{ kWh}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Drying Time from Fickian Diffusion (Slab Geometry)

**Statement:** Estimate the time required to reduce the average moisture content of a Nylon 6 pellet (treated as a slab of half-thickness $L = 1.5$ mm = $1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m) from $X_0 = 0.010$ to $X_f = 0.0008$ kg/kg, using an effective diffusion coefficient $D = 1.0 \times 10^{-11}$ m²/s at 80 °C. Assume equilibrium moisture $X^* = 0$ (very dry desiccant air).

**Step 1 — Set up the Fickian slab solution (one-term approximation, valid for $X_f \ll X_0$).**

$$\dfrac{X_f - X^*}{X_0 - X^*} = \dfrac{8}{\pi^2}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\pi^2 D \theta}{4 L^2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$\dfrac{0.0008}{0.010} = 0.08 = \dfrac{8}{\pi^2}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\pi^2 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-11} \times \theta}{4 \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the geometric constant $\pi^2 / (4L^2)$.**

$$\dfrac{\pi^2}{4 L^2} = \dfrac{9.8696}{4 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{9.8696}{9.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.0966 \times 10^{6} \text{ m}^{-2}$$

**Step 4 — Isolate the exponential term.**

$$0.08 \times \dfrac{\pi^2}{8} = \exp\!\left(-1.0966 \times 10^{6} \times D \cdot \theta\right)$$

$$0.08 \times 1.2337 = 0.09870 = \exp\!\left(-1.0966 \times 10^{6} \times 1.0 \times 10^{-11} \times \theta\right)$$

$$0.09870 = \exp\!\left(-1.0966 \times 10^{-5} \cdot \theta\right)$$

**Step 5 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides.**

$$\ln(0.09870) = -2.3155 = -1.0966 \times 10^{-5} \cdot \theta$$

**Step 6 — Solve for drying time $\theta$.**

$$\theta = \dfrac{2.3155}{1.0966 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.111 \times 10^{5} \text{ s}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to hours.**

$$\theta = \dfrac{2.111 \times 10^{5}}{3600} \approx 58.65 \text{ hours}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** A naive calculation would neglect internal diffusion and quote a few minutes. Always check whether the falling-rate (diffusion-controlled) period dominates — for thick pellets and low $D$, it absolutely does.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Effect Summary

| Parameter | Increase → Effect on Drying Rate | Increase → Effect on Drying Time | Practical Limit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Drying air temperature | ↑ Strongly increases | ↓ Decreases | Polymer softening/oxidation temperature |
| Air dew-point (drier air) | ↑ Strongly increases | ↓ Decreases | Cost of desiccant regeneration |
| Air velocity over bed | ↑ Moderately increases | ↓ Decreases | Pellet fluidization / entrainment |
| Effective diffusivity $D$ | ↑ Increases | ↓ Strongly decreases | Limited by polymer chemistry & temperature |
| Pellet thickness $L$ | ↓ Strongly decreases | ↑ Strongly increases (∝ $L^2$) | Supplier geometry (cannot change easily) |
| Initial moisture $X_0$ | ↑ Marginally affects late stage | ↑ Increases | Incoming material storage conditions |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]**
**Q1. Differentiate between hygroscopic and non-hygroscopic polymers. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**
Hygroscopic polymers chemically or physically absorb water within their molecular structure due to polar groups (—CONH—, —COO—, —OH—). They require drying before melt processing to prevent hydrolytic degradation.
*Examples:* Nylon 6 (PA 6), Polycarbonate (PC), PET, PMMA, POM.

Non-hygroscopic polymers only hold *surface* moisture through weak physical adsorption, which evaporates readily. They generally do not require drying.
*Examples:* Polyethylene (PE), Polypropylene (PP), Polystyrene (PS).

---

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q2]**
**Q2. Define "dew point" of drying air and state its significance in plastic drying.**

**Model Answer:**
The **dew point** of drying air is the temperature at which the air becomes saturated with water vapour (100 % relative humidity) upon cooling at constant pressure. It is measured using a chilled-mirror hygrometer.

**Significance:**
1. **Lower dew point = drier air** → greater capacity to absorb moisture from the polymer → faster drying.
2. For hygroscopic engineering plastics, the recommended drying air dew point is typically between **−40 °C and −60 °C**, achievable only with desiccant (not hot-air) dryers.
3. Dew point is a direct measure of *dryer performance*; if it rises, the desiccant must be regenerated.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *KTU University Exam – Dec 2023*

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the construction and working of a **desiccant (dehumidified air) dryer** used for drying engineering plastics. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A desiccant dryer provides a continuous supply of very dry air (dew point −40 °C to −60 °C) to a closed-loop drying plenum containing the polymer.

**Main Components & Working:**

1. **Blower / Fan** — Draws ambient air and circulates it through the closed loop.
2. **Heater Battery** — Heats the dehumidified air to the polymer-specific drying temperature (e.g., 80 °C for Nylon, 120 °C for PC).
3. **Twin Desiccant Beds (A & B)** — Filled with molecular sieve (zeolite 3A or 4A) or silica gel. One bed is **on-line (adsorbing moisture)** while the other is **regenerating (drying out)**.
4. **Three-way Valves** — Switch airflow between beds every 4–8 hours.
5. **Regeneration Heater (150–200 °C)** — Drives adsorbed water out of the offline bed, vented to atmosphere.
6. **Drying Plenum / Hopper** — Holds the polymer pellets; hot dry air flows through the bed and absorbs moisture.
7. **Dew-point Sensor** — Monitors air dryness; if it rises, regeneration cycle must be shortened.
8. **After-cooler** — Cools the regenerated bed before it returns to adsorption duty.

**Working Principle:** Moisture from the polymer is picked up by the dry air in the plenum. The now-moist air returns to the desiccant bed, where water molecules are adsorbed onto the porous zeolite. Clean dry air is reheated and recirculated.

**Advantages:** Very low dew point, fast drying, suitable for highly hygroscopic polymers (PA, PC, PET).

---

**(b)** A 600 kg batch of Polycarbonate (PC) granules is to be dried from an initial moisture of 0.20 % (wet basis) to a final moisture of 0.02 % (wet basis). The drying temperature is 120 °C and the granules enter at 30 °C. Compute the **total thermal energy (in kJ)** required, neglecting wall losses. Use $c_{p,PC} = 1.30$ kJ/(kg·K) and $\lambda_{120°C} = 2200$ kJ/kg. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert wet-basis to dry-basis.**

$$X_{d,i} = \dfrac{0.0020}{1 - 0.0020} = 0.002004 \text{ kg/kg}$$

$$X_{d,f} = \dfrac{0.0002}{1 - 0.0002} = 0.0002000 \text{ kg/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Dry polymer mass.**

$$m_{dry} = \dfrac{600}{1 + 0.002004} = \dfrac{600}{1.002004} = 598.80 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Water to be removed.**

$$m_w = m_{dry} \times (X_{d,i} - X_{d,f}) = 598.80 \times (0.002004 - 0.0002000)$$

$$m_w = 598.80 \times 0.001804 = 1.0803 \text{ kg water}$$

**Step 4 — Sensible heat.**

$$Q_{sens} = 598.80 \times 1.30 \times (120 - 30) = 598.80 \times 1.30 \times 90 = 70\,060 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 5 — Latent heat.**

$$Q_{lat} = 1.0803 \times 2200 = 2377 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 6 — Total energy.**

$$Q_{total} = 70\,060 + 2377 = 72\,437 \text{ kJ} \approx 72.4 \text{ MJ}$$

**In kWh:** $E = 72\,437 / 3600 = 20.12$ kWh.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *KTU University Exam – May 2024*

**(a)** With the help of a drying-rate-vs-moisture-content curve, explain the **constant-rate period** and the **falling-rate period**. Why does the falling-rate period dominate the total drying time of plastic pellets? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

When drying rate $R$ (kg water / m²·s) is plotted against free moisture content $X^* = X_d - X_{eq}$, two distinct regions appear:

**1. Constant-Rate Period (Region A→B):**
- The pellet surface remains saturated with free water.
- Drying rate is governed by **external** heat/mass transfer (air velocity, air temperature, humidity).
- Surface temperature ≈ wet-bulb temperature of the air.
- Rate is essentially constant because the driving force $(\rho_v^{sat} - \rho_v^{air})$ is constant.

**2. Falling-Rate Period (Region B→C):**
- Surface moisture is exhausted; remaining water resides inside the pellet.
- Drying rate is governed by **internal** moisture diffusion (Fick's law).
- Surface temperature rises toward dry-bulb.
- Rate falls because the concentration gradient inside the pellet progressively shrinks.

**Why Falling-Rate Dominates Plastic Drying:**
- Plastic pellets are **small but dense**, with very low effective diffusivity ($D \sim 10^{-11}$ m²/s).
- Internal diffusion length is small, but the **driving force** is also small once the surface dries.
- For Nylon/PC, **60–80 % of total drying time** lies in the falling-rate region.

---

**(b)** A Nylon 6 slab of half-thickness $L = 2$ mm is dried at 80 °C with $D = 1.5 \times 10^{-11}$ m²/s. Using Fickian one-term approximation, estimate the time to reduce moisture from $X_0 = 0.015$ kg/kg to $X_f = 0.0010$ kg/kg. Assume $X^* = 0$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Fickian slab equation (one-term).**

$$\dfrac{X_f}{X_0} = \dfrac{8}{\pi^2}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\pi^2 D \theta}{4L^2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known ratios.**

$$\dfrac{0.0010}{0.015} = 0.0667 = \dfrac{8}{\pi^2}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\pi^2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-11} \times \theta}{4 \times (2 \times 10^{-3})^2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute geometric constant.**

$$\dfrac{\pi^2}{4L^2} = \dfrac{9.8696}{4 \times 4 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{9.8696}{1.6 \times 10^{-5}} = 6.1685 \times 10^{5} \text{ m}^{-2}$$

**Step 4 — Isolate the exponential.**

$$0.0667 \times \dfrac{\pi^2}{8} = \exp(-6.1685 \times 10^{5} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-11} \times \theta)$$

$$0.0667 \times 1.2337 = 0.08229 = \exp(-9.253 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \theta)$$

**Step 5 — Natural log.**

$$\ln(0.08229) = -2.497 = -9.253 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \theta$$

**Step 6 — Solve.**

$$\theta = \dfrac{2.497}{9.253 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.699 \times 10^{5} \text{ s} \approx 74.96 \text{ hours}$$

This long time confirms that the falling-rate (diffusion-controlled) regime controls plastic drying.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in Drying questions:**
> 1. **Unit-mismatch trap:** Forgetting to convert moisture from *wet-basis* (%) to *dry-basis* (kg/kg) before using mass-balance equations. Always state the basis explicitly.
> 2. **Neglecting the sensible-heat term:** Many students only calculate latent heat $m \lambda$ and ignore the heat to raise the polymer from ambient to drying temperature. For polymers with low moisture, $Q_{sens}$ actually *dominates* the energy bill.
> 3. **Stating "hot-air dryer" for hygroscopic plastics:** A wrong recommendation loses marks immediately. PA, PC, PET, PMMA **require** desiccant dryers (dew point $\leq -40$ °C).
> 4. **Ignoring the falling-rate period:** Always mention *both* periods and explain that internal diffusion controls the bulk of the time.
> 5. **Dew point confusion:** Students often quote "low humidity" without specifying dew point. Always quote **temperature in °C** (e.g., −40 °C dew point).
> 6. **In KTU numericals, ALWAYS show the conversion logic line by line.** Step marks are awarded liberally — partial credit is guaranteed if the setup is correct even if the final number has an arithmetic slip.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Drying Definition:** Removal of moisture/solvent from polymer by coupled heat-and-mass transfer before melt processing or decoration.
- **Two Stages:** (A) Constant-rate (surface-controlled) → (B) Falling-rate (internally diffusion-controlled). Most time is spent in stage B.
- **Hygroscopic polymers** (PA, PC, PET, PMMA, POM, PBT, PU) **must** be dried; non-hygroscopic (PE, PP, PS) generally need not.
- **Recommended drying conditions (industry standard):**

| Polymer | Temp (°C) | Time (h) | Dew point (°C) | Max moisture (%) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Nylon 6 | 80 | 4–6 | ≤ −40 | 0.10 |
| Nylon 6,6 | 80 | 4–6 | ≤ −40 | 0.10 |
| PC | 120 | 3–4 | ≤ −40 | 0.02 |
| PMMA | 80 | 2–4 | ≤ −40 | 0.10 |
| PET | 140–180 | 4–6 | ≤ −40 | 0.02 |
| POM | 100 | 2–4 | ≤ −20 | 0.20 |
| ABS | 80 | 2–3 | ≤ −20 | 0.10 |

- **Dryer types:** Hot-air oven (cheap, limited), Desiccant (industry workhorse), Vacuum (heat-sensitive polymers), Infrared (surface), Microwave (volumetric).
- **Key equations:** $X_w = X_d/(1+X_d)$; $Q = mc_p \Delta T + m_w \lambda$; Fickian slab solution for falling-rate time.
- **Energy economy:** Sensible heat often > latent heat; never ignore it.
- **Dew point** is the *true measure* of drying air quality, not relative humidity.
- **Decoration link:** Insufficient drying → surface streaks → poor print/paint adhesion → metallization failure.
- **Course Outcome (CO) alignment:** This topic maps to **CO4 – Understand pre-processing unit operations and their influence on plastics decoration quality** of PEPOT864 (KTU 2024 Scheme).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equipment for Drying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT864 - PLASTICS & DECORATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Equipment for Drying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Drying Equipment** in plastics processing refers to a class of thermal processing machines designed to remove absorbed or adsorbed moisture from hygroscopic polymer granules, pellets, or powders **before** they enter the plasticizing unit of an injection molding, blow molding, or extrusion machine. The process involves the controlled application of heat and dehumidified air to reduce the moisture content of the polymer below its critical processing threshold, thereby preventing hydrolytic degradation during melt processing.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEPOT864)**, drying equipment constitutes a pre-processing auxiliary that directly influences the **Decoration and Surface Quality** of finished plastic products by eliminating processing defects such as *silver streaks, splay marks, voids, and bubbles*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Drying is treated as a critical upstream operation. Any residual moisture in the polymer feedstock will flash into steam at processing temperatures (200 °C – 320 °C), causing catastrophic surface and structural defects in the final decorated product.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> Think of drying equipment as a **"climate-controlled wardrobe"** for plastic granules. Just as you would not wear a damp shirt out in freezing weather (it would feel cold and uncomfortable), you cannot feed wet plastic pellets into a hot injection barrel. The granules will "sweat" violently (steam generation) when they hit the melt, ruining the surface finish. The desiccant dryer is the wardrobe that gently, continuously, and reliably removes that moisture.

**Plain English Explanation for First-Time Learners:**
- Plastic pellets are like tiny sponges — many engineering plastics (PA, PC, PET, PMMA) absorb atmospheric moisture during storage.
- When heated inside the barrel, this water turns to steam — picture a pressure cooker whistle inside the plastic melt.
- The steam escapes violently during ejection/molding, leaving **silver streaks**, **bubbles**, and **craters** on the product surface.
- For a decorated item (painted, printed, metallized, or in-mold decorated), these defects are **catastrophic** because they destroy the cosmetic finish.
- **Drying equipment** is therefore the first line of defense to guarantee a blemish-free surface.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **Standard Dew Point for Engineering Plastics:** **−40 °C** (critical threshold for PET, PC, PA).
- **Typical Drying Temperature Range:** **80 °C – 180 °C** (polymer-dependent).
- **Standard Drying Time:** **2 – 6 hours** (depending on polymer and initial moisture).
- **Maximum Permissible Moisture Content (PET):** **< 0.02 % (200 ppm)**.
- **Desiccant Type:** **Molecular Sieves (Type 3 Å or 4 Å) and Silica Gel**.
- **Reference Airflow Rate:** **1 CFM per lb/hr of material processed**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These metrics are **board-exam favorite values** — memorize them for direct 2-mark/3-mark questions in KTU ESE.

## 1.4 Why Drying is Mandatory Before Decoration

| Defect Type | Root Cause | Effect on Decoration |
|-------------|------------|----------------------|
| Silver streaks | Steam escape | Ruins paint/adhesive bonding |
| Bubbles | Entrapped vapor | Lifting of coating |
| Voids | Hydrolysis voids | Surface pitting |
| Splay marks | Rapid vapor release | Visible cosmetic flaw |
| Reduced MW | Hydrolytic chain scission | Poor impact strength |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The drying operation in plastics processing follows a **convective heat-and-mass transfer** mechanism coupled with a **thermodynamic dehumidification** stage. The logic chain is as follows:

1. **Moisture Identification:** The polymer pellets contain moisture in two forms:
   - **Surface moisture (free water)** — loosely bound, easy to remove.
   - **Bound moisture (absorbed within pellet matrix)** — requires diffusion-driven removal.

2. **Air Dehumidification:** Ambient air is passed through a **desiccant bed** (molecular sieves/silica gel) to achieve a low dew point (typically **−40 °C**).

3. **Heated Air Supply:** The dehumidified air is heated to the polymer-specific drying temperature using electric heaters or heat exchangers.

4. **Convective Contact:** Hot, dry air is blown through the polymer bed inside a drying hopper, transferring heat to the pellets and carrying away evaporated moisture.

5. **Moisture Pickup:** The air becomes humid after contacting wet pellets — this **moisture-laden air** is either:
   - **Exhausted to atmosphere** (open-loop system), or
   - **Returned to the desiccant for re-dehumidification** (closed-loop system).

6. **Desiccant Regeneration:** Once saturated, the desiccant is regenerated by passing hot air (180 °C – 250 °C) through it in the opposite direction (or via a twin-tower configuration).

7. **Equilibrium Achievement:** Drying continues until the **equilibrium moisture content** of the polymer equals or falls below the manufacturer's specified value.

> [!NOTE]
> The **"Why"** behind using dehumidified air (not just hot air) is critical: heating alone only **lowers relative humidity temporarily** — once the air contacts cool pellets, RH rises again, slowing drying. Dehumidified air maintains a **low partial pressure of water vapor**, ensuring continuous moisture migration from pellet to air.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Parameter | Formula / Definition | Standard Unit |
|--------|-----------|----------------------|---------------|
| $M_w$ | Moisture content (wet basis) | $M_w = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | % |
| $M_d$ | Moisture content (dry basis) | $M_d = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | % |
| $W_w$ | Weight of wet polymer | — | kg |
| $W_d$ | Weight of dry polymer | — | kg |
| $R$ | Drying rate | $R = -\dfrac{dW}{A \cdot dt}$ | kg/(m²·h) |
| $A$ | Surface area for drying | — | m² |
| $X^*$ | Equilibrium moisture content | $X^* = f(\phi, T)$ | kg water/kg dry solid |
| $\phi$ | Relative humidity of drying air | — | % |
| $T_{dp}$ | Dew point of process air | $T_{dp} = T_{dry\,bulb} - \Delta T$ | °C |
| $Q_a$ | Air flow rate required | $Q_a = 0.06 \times m$ (where $m$ = throughput) | m³/min |
| $h_{fg}$ | Latent heat of vaporization of water | **2257 kJ/kg** at 100 °C | kJ/kg |
| $\dot{Q}$ | Heat required for evaporation | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{w} \cdot h_{fg}$ | kW |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The relation $M_d = \dfrac{M_w}{1 - M_w}$ is a frequently-tested conversion. Never confuse the wet basis (used in polymer datasheets) with the dry basis (used in engineering calculations).

## 2.3 Engineering Classification of Drying Equipment

Drying equipment is broadly classified into **three generations** based on dehumidification technology:

### Generation 1 — Conventional Hot Air Ovens
- Simple insulated chamber with electric heaters.
- No active dehumidification — relies on heating alone.
- **Limitation:** Cannot achieve dew points below ambient.

### Generation 2 — Dehumidifying (Desiccant) Dryers
- Uses **molecular sieve** or **silica gel** desiccant.
- Achieves dew points of **−40 °C** or lower.
- Industry standard for hygroscopic engineering plastics.

### Generation 3 — Advanced Closed-Loop Drying Systems
- Twin-desiccant bed with continuous regeneration.
- Integrated with molding machine hopper.
- PLC-controlled with dew point sensors.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Drying of PA66 (nylon) for fuel system components — moisture causes blistering in metallized decorative trims.
- **PET Preform Manufacturing:** Drying is non-negotiable — IV (Intrinsic Viscosity) drop due to hydrolysis ruins bottle clarity and barrier properties.
- **Optical-grade PMMA:** Drying prevents haze and bubble formation in light-guide panels.
- **3D Printing Filament:** FDM-grade ABS, nylon, and PVA filaments require sealed desiccant storage and pre-drying.

> [!NOTE]
> In all the above applications, the **decorative quality** of the end product depends directly on the **drying effectiveness upstream**. This is the unifying theme of Module 4 in PEPOT864.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix
