## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Conversion Between Wet-Basis and Dry-Basis Moisture Content

This is a **favourite KTU derivation question** (asked frequently in Part B for 7 marks).

**Given:**
- Weight of wet polymer: $W_w$
- Weight of dry polymer: $W_d$
- Weight of moisture: $W_m = W_w - W_d$

**Step 1: Define moisture content on wet basis.**
By definition, $M_w$ is the ratio of moisture mass to total (wet) mass:

$$M_w = \dfrac{W_m}{W_w} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w}$$

**Step 2: Define moisture content on dry basis.**
By definition, $M_d$ is the ratio of moisture mass to dry solid mass:

$$M_d = \dfrac{W_m}{W_d} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d}$$

**Step 3: Express $W_w$ in terms of $W_d$ and $M_w$.**
From Step 1, we isolate $W_w$:

$$M_w \cdot W_w = W_w - W_d$$

$$W_w (1 - M_w) = W_d$$

$$W_w = \dfrac{W_d}{1 - M_w}$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the expression for $M_d$.**

$$M_d = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} = \dfrac{\frac{W_d}{1-M_w} - W_d}{W_d}$$

**Step 5: Simplify numerator.**

$$M_d = \dfrac{W_d \left(\frac{1}{1-M_w} - 1\right)}{W_d}$$

$$M_d = \dfrac{1 - (1 - M_w)}{1 - M_w}$$

$$M_d = \dfrac{M_w}{1 - M_w}$$

**Final boxed result (KTU examiner's expected answer):**

$$\boxed{M_d = \dfrac{M_w}{1 - M_w}}$$

**Step 6: Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style).**
A polymer sample has a wet-basis moisture of **0.5 %**. Find the dry-basis moisture.

$$M_d = \dfrac{0.005}{1 - 0.005} = \dfrac{0.005}{0.995} = 0.005025 \approx 0.5025\,\%$$

**Conclusion:** For low moisture contents (< 5 %), wet and dry basis values are nearly equal — but the distinction becomes critical at high moisture levels (e.g., freshly-extruded PET at 0.8 % wet basis).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Drying Rate Equation (Thin-Layer Model)

The **drying rate** quantifies the mass of water removed per unit surface area per unit time.

**Step 1: Differential form of moisture loss.**

$$dW = -R \cdot A \cdot dt$$

The negative sign indicates that the polymer weight decreases as drying proceeds.

**Step 2: Express $R$ in terms of dry-basis moisture $X$.**

$$R = -\dfrac{W_d}{A} \cdot \dfrac{dX}{dt}$$

**Step 3: Constant Rate Period (CRP).**
In the CRP, free surface water evaporates at a rate controlled by external air conditions:

$$R_{CRP} = \dfrac{h_m \cdot (p_{s,T_s} - p_a)}{1}$$

where:
- $h_m$ = mass transfer coefficient (m/s)
- $p_{s,T_s}$ = saturation vapor pressure at surface temperature
- $p_a$ = partial pressure of water vapor in drying air

**Step 4: Falling Rate Period (FRP).**
Once surface water is exhausted, drying becomes **diffusion-controlled** through the pellet interior:

$$\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D_{eff} \cdot \nabla^2 C$$

where:
- $C$ = moisture concentration inside pellet (kg/m³)
- $D_{eff}$ = effective diffusion coefficient of water in polymer (m²/s)

**Step 5: Arrhenius Temperature Dependence of $D_{eff}$.**

$$D_{eff} = D_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R_g \cdot T}\right)$$

where:
- $D_0$ = pre-exponential factor (m²/s)
- $E_a$ = activation energy of diffusion (kJ/mol)
- $R_g$ = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** (universal gas constant)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This Arrhenius relation explains why **every 10 °C increase in drying temperature approximately doubles the diffusion rate** — a fact that KTU examiners love to test as a short numerical.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Heat Duty for a Drying Hopper

**Given:** Throughput $\dot{m}_p$ = 50 kg/h of nylon, initial moisture = 0.8 % wet basis, final moisture = 0.1 % wet basis, drying temperature = 80 °C.

**Step 1: Mass of water to be removed per hour.**

$$\dot{m}_w = \dot{m}_p \times (M_{w,i} - M_{w,f})$$

$$\dot{m}_w = 50 \times (0.008 - 0.001) = 50 \times 0.007 = 0.35\,\text{kg/h}$$

**Step 2: Convert to kg/s.**

$$\dot{m}_w = \dfrac{0.35}{3600} = 9.72 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{kg/s}$$

**Step 3: Latent heat of vaporization at 80 °C.**
Using $h_{fg} = 2309$ kJ/kg (steam tables):

$$\dot{Q}_{latent} = 9.72 \times 10^{-5} \times 2309 = 0.2245\,\text{kW}$$

**Step 4: Sensible heat to raise pellet temperature from 25 °C to 80 °C.**

$$\dot{Q}_{sensible} = \dot{m}_p \cdot c_{p,p} \cdot \Delta T$$

$$\dot{Q}_{sensible} = \dfrac{50}{3600} \times 1.67 \times (80 - 25)$$

$$\dot{Q}_{sensible} = 0.01389 \times 1.67 \times 55 = 1.276\,\text{kW}$$

**Step 5: Total heat duty (assuming 30 % heat loss to surroundings).**

$$\dot{Q}_{total} = 1.30 \times (\dot{Q}_{latent} + \dot{Q}_{sensible})$$

$$\dot{Q}_{total} = 1.30 \times (0.2245 + 1.276) = 1.95\,\text{kW}$$

**Final answer:**

$$\boxed{\dot{Q}_{total} \approx 1.95\,\text{kW} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Heater rating} \approx 2\,\text{kW}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Insight:** Real dryers are oversized by a factor of 1.5 – 2.0 to account for startup transients, ambient variations, and insulation losses. The answer **2 kW** aligns with industry-standard 50 kg/h nylon dryers.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Selection of Drying Equipment

| Equipment Type | Dew Point Achievable | Typical Polymers | Capital Cost | Energy Efficiency | Suitability for Decoration |
|----------------|----------------------|------------------|--------------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| Hot Air Oven | Ambient | Non-hygroscopic (PE, PP) | Very Low | Low | Poor |
| Desiccant Dryer (Single Bed) | −20 °C to −40 °C | ABS, PC, PA | Medium | Medium | Good |
| Desiccant Dryer (Twin Bed) | −40 °C to −60 °C | PET, PA, PC, PMMA | High | High | Excellent |
| Vacuum Dryer | Effectively 0 % RH | High-temp polymers | High | Medium | Excellent |
| Fluidized Bed Dryer | −30 °C | Powder coatings, PVC | Medium | High | Good |
| Microwave Dryer | Depends on air | Specialty polymers | Very High | High | Excellent |
| Hot Air Circulation Oven | Ambient | Pre-drying of granules | Low | Low | Moderate |
| Compressed Air Dryer | −40 °C | Pneumatic conveying | Medium | Medium | Good |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Generic Desiccant Drying System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ambient Air Inlet] --> B1[Pre-Filter]
    B1 --> B2[After Cooler]
    B2 --> C[Process Blower]
    C --> D1{Desiccant Tower 1}
    D1 -- Active Drying --> E1[Heater Bank]
    E1 --> F[Drying Hopper]
    F --> G[Injection / Extrusion Machine]
    D1 -. Saturated .-> D2[Desiccant Tower 2]
    D2 -- Regeneration Mode --> H[Regeneration Blower]
    H --> I[Regeneration Heater]
    I --> D2
    F --> J[Return Air to Desiccant]
    J --> D1
    D2 --> K[Exhaust to Atmosphere]
    L[PLC Controller with Dew Point Sensor] -. Monitors .-> D1
    L -. Monitors .-> D2
    L -. Controls .-> E1
    L -. Controls .-> F
    style A fill:#b3d9ff
    style F fill:#ffd9b3
    style G fill:#ffb3b3
    style L fill:#d9b3ff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide for Students:** The active drying tower (solid arrow path) supplies dehumidified hot air to the hopper. Simultaneously, the second tower (dashed line) undergoes regeneration — heated air strips moisture from the saturated desiccant and exhausts it. Twin-tower designs ensure **continuous, uninterrupted drying**, which is essential for production lines serving decorated parts.

---

## 4.2 Process Flow — Closed-Loop Drying with Return Air

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Wet Pellets Loaded into Hopper] --> A2[Pellets Sit in Hot Dehumidified Air]
    A2 --> A3[Moisture Diffuses from Pellet Core to Surface]
    A3 --> A4[Surface Moisture Evaporates into Air Stream]
    A4 --> A5[Moisture-Laden Air Leaves Hopper]
    A5 --> A6{Loop Type}
    A6 -- Open Loop --> A7[Exhaust to Atmosphere]
    A6 -- Closed Loop --> A8[Return to Desiccant for Re-Dehumidification]
    A8 --> A9[Desiccant Adsorbs Water Vapor]
    A9 --> A10[Reconditioned Air Sent Back to Process]
    A10 --> A2
    style A1 fill:#ffe6e6
    style A7 fill:#e6e6e6
    style A9 fill:#b3d9ff
```

---

## 4.3 Architecture Diagram — Classification of Drying Equipment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Drying Equipment for Plastics] --> B[Conventional]
    A --> C[Dehumidifying]
    A --> D[Advanced]
    B --> B1[Hot Air Oven]
    B --> B2[Hot Air Circulation Oven]
    B --> B3[Infrared Dryer]
    C --> C1[Single-Bed Desiccant Dryer]
    C --> C2[Twin-Bed Desiccant Dryer]
    C --> C3[Compressed Air Dryer]
    D --> D1[Vacuum Dryer]
    D --> D2[Fluidized Bed Dryer]
    D --> D3[Microwave Dryer]
    D --> D4[PLC Integrated Drying System]
    style A fill:#fff4cc
    style B fill:#ffe0b3
    style C fill:#b3d9ff
    style D fill:#d9b3ff
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic — Twin-Tower Desiccant Cycle (KTU Board Favorite)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TowerA[Tower A: ACTIVE DRYING MODE]
        T1[Desiccant Bed Molecular Sieve] --> T2[Dries Process Air to minus 40 deg C]
    end
    subgraph TowerB[Tower B: REGENERATION MODE]
        T3[Hot Air at 200 deg C] --> T4[Strips Moisture from Desiccant]
        T4 --> T5[Moist Air Exhausted]
    end
    TowerA --> SW[3 Way Diverter Valve]
    SW --> TowerB
    TowerB -. After 4 to 6 Hours .-> SW
    SW -. Role Reversal .-> TowerA
    style TowerA fill:#cce6ff
    style TowerB fill:#ffd9b3
    style SW fill:#e6ccff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concept to Memorize:** The **3-way diverter valve** automatically switches the roles of the two towers every 4–6 hours. This is the **heart** of continuous closed-loop drying and is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in KTU ESE.

---

## 4.5 Block Architecture — Vacuum Dryer for Heat-Sensitive Polymers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Polymer Charged into Sealed Vacuum Chamber] --> P2[Vacuum Pump Evacuates Chamber to 10 mbar]
    P2 --> P3[Heating Jackets Supply Conductive Heat to Chamber Wall]
    P3 --> P4[Pellets Heat Up Under Reduced Pressure]
    P4 --> P5[Boiling Point of Water Drops Below 30 deg C]
    P5 --> P6[Moisture Vaporises Rapidly at Low Temperature]
    P6 --> P7[Condenser Collects Vapor Outside Chamber]
    P7 --> P8[Vacuum Pump Discharges Non-Condensables]
    style P1 fill:#ffe6e6
    style P7 fill:#b3d9ff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define "Dew Point" in the context of plastics drying. Why is a dew point of −40 °C considered the industry standard for hygroscopic engineering plastics?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Dew point is the temperature at which the water vapor in the air becomes saturated and begins to condense. In plastics drying, a **low dew point** indicates **very dry air** (low absolute humidity).

A dew point of **−40 °C** is the industry standard because:
1. It corresponds to an extremely low absolute humidity of approximately **0.12 g water per kg of dry air**.
2. At this dryness level, the **mass transfer driving force** (partial pressure gradient) between the polymer pellet interior and the surrounding air is maximized, ensuring rapid and complete moisture removal.
3. Polymers like **PET, PA, PC, and PMMA** require moisture levels below **200 ppm** to prevent hydrolytic degradation — a −40 °C dew point reliably achieves this.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for justification.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any four hygroscopic plastics that require mandatory drying before processing, along with their typical drying temperature and time.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Polymer | Drying Temperature (°C) | Drying Time (h) | Max Moisture (ppm) |
|---------|-------------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| **PET** | 160 – 180 | 4 – 6 | 50 |
| **PA 6 / PA 66** | 80 – 100 | 4 – 8 | 1000 |
| **PC (Polycarbonate)** | 120 | 3 – 4 | 200 |
| **PMMA** | 80 – 90 | 2 – 4 | 300 |

*(0.5 marks per correct entry; 0.5 marks for correct format. Full marks for tabular presentation.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram, the working principle of a **twin-desiccant bed hopper dryer**. Discuss the role of the **molecular sieve desiccant**, **regeneration cycle**, and **process air dew point control** in achieving effective drying of hygroscopic polymers. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A PET processing unit handles **100 kg/h** of PET pellets with an initial moisture content of **0.6 % (wet basis)**. The required final moisture is **0.02 % (wet basis)**. The drying air enters at **180 °C** with a dew point of **−40 °C**, and leaves at **60 °C** with 80 % relative humidity. Calculate:
- (i) The mass of water removed per hour.
- (ii) The total heat duty of the dryer (latent + sensible), assuming a heat loss factor of 1.25. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Working Principle of Twin-Desiccant Bed Hopper Dryer:**

1. **Process Air Path:** Ambient air is drawn through a pre-filter → after-cooler → process blower → **active desiccant tower (Tower A)** → heater bank → drying hopper → return to desiccant.
2. **Molecular Sieve Action:** The desiccant (typically **3 Å or 4 Å molecular sieves**) selectively adsorbs water molecules from the process air via physical adsorption in its uniform pore structure. This reduces the air's dew point to **−40 °C or lower**.
3. **Drying in Hopper:** The hot (−40 °C dew point), dry air at 160 – 180 °C contacts PET pellets in the hopper, picks up moisture through convection and diffusion, and exits the hopper.
4. **Return Air Handling:** In a **closed-loop system**, this moisture-laden return air re-enters the desiccant tower for re-dehumidification, ensuring energy efficiency.
5. **Regeneration Cycle:** Simultaneously, **Tower B** undergoes regeneration: hot air at **200 – 250 °C** is blown through the saturated desiccant in the reverse direction, stripping adsorbed water and exhausting it.
6. **Auto-Switching:** A PLC with dew point sensor automatically **swaps the roles** of Tower A and Tower B every 4 – 6 hours, ensuring **continuous, uninterrupted drying**.
7. **Dew Point Control:** The dew point sensor provides feedback to the PLC, which modulates heater output and cycle timing to maintain the set dew point.

**Key Advantages (Board-Expected Points):**
- Continuous operation (no downtime for desiccant regeneration).
- Energy-efficient closed-loop design.
- Precise dew point control for consistent product quality.
- Ideal for high-value decorated parts where surface perfection is non-negotiable.

*(Distribute 7 marks as: 2 for diagram, 3 for working explanation, 2 for advantages.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**(i) Mass of water removed per hour:**

**Step 1: Convert wet basis to absolute moisture removed (in % units).**

$$M_{removed} = M_{w,i} - M_{w,f} = 0.6\% - 0.02\% = 0.58\%$$

**Step 2: Apply to throughput.**

$$\dot{m}_w = \dot{m}_p \times M_{removed}$$

$$\dot{m}_w = 100 \times 0.0058 = 0.58\,\text{kg/h}$$

**Step 3: Convert to kg/s.**

$$\dot{m}_w = \dfrac{0.58}{3600} = 1.611 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{kg/s}$$

**(2 marks for correct calculation.)**

---

**(ii) Total heat duty:**

**Step 1: Latent heat for water evaporation.**
At an average temperature of 120 °C, $h_{fg} \approx 2203$ kJ/kg:

$$\dot{Q}_{latent} = \dot{m}_w \cdot h_{fg} = 1.611 \times 10^{-4} \times 2203 = 0.355\,\text{kW}$$

**Step 2: Sensible heat to raise PET pellets from 25 °C to 160 °C.**
Specific heat of PET: $c_p = 1.05$ kJ/(kg·K)

$$\dot{Q}_{sensible} = \dfrac{100}{3600} \times 1.05 \times (160 - 25)$$

$$\dot{Q}_{sensible} = 0.02778 \times 1.05 \times 135 = 3.938\,\text{kW}$$

**Step 3: Sensible heat to raise drying air.**
Assuming air mass flow rate for 100 kg/h PET ≈ 50 m³/h → 60 kg/h, with $c_{p,air} = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), $\Delta T = 180 - 25 = 155$ K:

$$\dot{Q}_{air} = \dfrac{60}{3600} \times 1.005 \times 155 = 2.596\,\text{kW}$$

**Step 4: Total heat duty before losses.**

$$\dot{Q}_{gross} = 0.355 + 3.938 + 2.596 = 6.889\,\text{kW}$$

**Step 5: Apply heat loss factor of 1.25.**

$$\dot{Q}_{total} = 1.25 \times 6.889 = 8.61\,\text{kW}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_w = 0.58\,\text{kg/h} \quad ; \quad \dot{Q}_{total} \approx 8.61\,\text{kW}}$$

**Heater selection:** A standard **10 kW heater bank** is appropriate.

*(Distribute 7 marks as: 2 for part (i), 4 for part (ii) breakdown, 1 for final boxed answer and engineering interpretation.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Compare the working, advantages, and limitations of **Hot Air Ovens**, **Desiccant Dryers**, and **Vacuum Dryers** for plastics. Which of these is most suitable for drying PET before injection stretch blow molding, and why? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a diagram the construction and working of a **fluidized bed dryer** for plastic powders. Discuss its energy efficiency and limitations when used for hygroscopic polymers. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Comparison Table (Board-Exam Quality Presentation):**

| Parameter | Hot Air Oven | Desiccant Dryer | Vacuum Dryer |
|-----------|--------------|------------------|---------------|
| Dehumidification | None (ambient RH) | Active (molecular sieve) | Inherent (vacuum = no air) |
| Achievable Dew Point | +5 °C to +15 °C | −40 °C to −60 °C | Effectively 0 % RH |
| Drying Temperature | 60 – 120 °C | 80 – 180 °C | 30 – 80 °C |
| Drying Time | 6 – 12 h | 2 – 6 h | 1 – 3 h |
| Capital Cost | Very Low | Medium – High | High |
| Operating Cost | High (long cycles) | Medium | High (vacuum pump energy) |
| Energy Efficiency | Low | High (closed loop) | Medium |
| Risk of Oxidation | Low | Low | None (vacuum) |
| Suitability for PET | Unsuitable | **Best Choice** | Good but expensive |

**Most Suitable for PET: Desiccant Dryer**

**Justification:**
1. PET is **extremely hygroscopic** and degrades rapidly via hydrolysis even at moisture levels of **50 ppm**.
2. A **−40 °C dew point** is the only reliable way to consistently achieve this low moisture.
3. The desiccant dryer's **closed-loop design** prevents re-introduction of atmospheric moisture.
4. The drying temperature of **160 – 180 °C** matches PET's crystallization window, ensuring uniform drying without agglomeration.

*(Distribute 7 marks as: 4 for tabular comparison, 3 for PET-specific justification.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Construction and Working of Fluidized Bed Dryer:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plastic Powder Feeded from Top] --> B[Perforated Distribution Plate]
    B --> C[Hot Air Inlet from Blower]
    C --> D[Air Passes Through Powder Bed]
    D --> E[Powder Particles Fluidize and Mix]
    E --> F[Intensive Heat and Mass Transfer]
    F --> G[Moist Air Exits via Top Filter]
    G --> H[Cyclone Separator Collects Fines]
    H --> I[Exhaust to Atmosphere]
    style A fill:#ffe6e6
    style E fill:#b3d9ff
    style F fill:#d9b3ff
```

**Constructional Features:**
1. **Perforated distribution plate** at the bottom — supports the powder bed and ensures uniform air distribution.
2. **Plenum chamber** below the plate — receives hot air from the blower.
3. **Expanded drying chamber** above — accommodates the fluidized bed.
4. **Top-mounted bag filter** — captures entrained fine particles.
5. **Cyclone separator** — recovers and returns fines to the bed.
6. **Heater** upstream of the blower.

**Working Principle:**
Hot air is forced upward through the powder bed at a velocity sufficient to **suspend the particles** (fluidization velocity). The particles behave like a boiling liquid, providing:
- **Maximum surface area exposure** per unit mass.
- **Isothermal conditions** throughout the bed.
- **Continuous mixing** — eliminates hot/cold spots.

**Energy Efficiency:**
- Heat transfer coefficients are **5–10 times higher** than in static bed dryers.
- Drying times reduced from **hours to minutes**.
- Lower air consumption per kg of water removed.

**Limitations for Hygroscopic Polymers:**
1. Operates with **ambient air** (unless integrated with a desiccant unit) — cannot achieve low dew points required for PET/PA.
2. **High air velocity** causes **attrition and fines generation** — problematic for granular feedstock.
3. Not suitable for pellets — primarily used for **powders** (PVC, coating powders, masterbatches).
4. Electrostatic charging risk for fine powders (explosion hazard in some cases).

*(Distribute 7 marks as: 2 for diagram, 3 for working explanation, 2 for limitations.)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones (Validated by KTU Board Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Confusing wet basis and dry basis:** When a question states moisture in "%", students often forget to specify the basis. Always state "wet basis" or "dry basis" explicitly — failure to do so costs **1–2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting unit conversions:** $W$ in kJ/h must be divided by 3600 to obtain kW. Many students lose 1 mark by writing the answer in inconsistent units.
>
> 3. **Omitting the latent heat of vaporization at the actual drying temperature:** The value of $h_{fg}$ varies from **2257 kJ/kg at 100 °C** to **2203 kJ/kg at 120 °C**. KTU expects you to state the temperature at which $h_{fg}$ is evaluated.
>
> 4. **Diagrams without labels:** Block diagrams must have **labeled arrows and component names**. An unlabeled diagram fetches only 50 % of the marks allotted.
>
> 5. **Skipping the regeneration explanation in desiccant dryer answers:** The twin-tower regeneration cycle is **not optional** — it is the defining feature. Skipping it loses up to **3 marks**.
>
> 6. **Ignoring the connection to decoration:** PEPOT864 is "Plastics & Decoration". Examiners reward answers that link drying effectiveness to **decorative surface quality** (silver streaks, paint adhesion, etc.). A purely mechanical/thermal answer misses the contextual marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Print and Carry to Exam Hall):**
>
> ☐ **Drying is mandatory** for hygroscopic polymers (PET, PA, PC, PMMA, ABS) before processing.
>
> ☐ **Dew point of −40 °C** is the industry-standard benchmark.
>
> ☐ **Twin-tower desiccant dryer** is the gold standard for continuous industrial drying.
>
> ☐ **Molecular sieves (3 Å / 4 Å)** are the most common desiccants; regeneration occurs at **200 – 250 °C**.
>
> ☐ **Master the wet-basis ↔ dry-basis conversion:** $M_d = M_w / (1 - M_w)$.
>
> ☐ **Drying time** depends on temperature via **Arrhenius diffusion**: $D_{eff} = D_0 \exp(-E_a / R_g T)$.
>
> ☐ **Open-loop dryers** exhaust humid air; **closed-loop dryers** return it to the desiccant for energy efficiency.
>
> ☐ **Vacuum dryers** are used for heat-sensitive polymers — low temperature, no oxidation.
>
> ☐ **Fluidized bed dryers** suit powders, not granules; high heat transfer but no active dehumidification.
>
> ☐ **For decorated parts** (painted, metallized, in-mold decorated), residual moisture causes **silver streaks, bubbles, paint delamination** — drying is the first quality checkpoint.
>
> ☐ **Standard airflow rule:** ~1 CFM per lb/hr of throughput.
>
> ☐ **PET critical moisture:** < 50 ppm; **PA 6 critical moisture:** < 1000 ppm.
>
> ☐ **Linkage to Module 4:** Drying is a **pre-decoration** operation that ensures surface integrity, which is the foundation for **painting, printing, vacuum metallization, and in-mold decoration** (the broader scope of PEPOT864).
>
> ☐ **Energy efficiency hierarchy:** Hot Air Oven < Desiccant Dryer < Twin-Bed Desiccant Dryer ≈ Vacuum Dryer.
>
> ☐ **Always state assumptions** in numerical problems (ambient temperature, heat loss factor, $c_p$ values) — KTU examiners reward methodological clarity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Control of Metallizing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT864 - PLASTICS & DECORATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Process Control of Metallizing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Metallizing** is a surface-decoration process in which a thin metallic film (commonly aluminum, with thickness ranging from **30 nm to 500 nm**) is physically deposited onto a polymeric substrate under high-vacuum conditions, primarily for aesthetic, barrier, or functional (EMI/RFI shielding) purposes.

**Process Control of Metallizing** is defined as the systematic regulation, monitoring, and feedback adjustment of the critical process variables (vacuum pressure, evaporation temperature, substrate temperature, deposition rate, line/web speed, and source-to-substrate distance) using closed-loop instrumentation (PID controllers, quartz crystal microbalances, optical monitors, and vacuum gauges) to ensure *reproducible film thickness*, *uniform optical density*, *adequate adhesion*, and *consistent production yield* across batches.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight – Module 4**
> Process control is not merely "monitoring." It is a **closed-loop feedback system** where deviations in the measured output (e.g., film thickness) automatically drive corrections in the input actuators (e.g., crucible heater power or web speed).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Painting with Atoms" Intuition

Imagine you are spray-painting a wall using an aerosol can in a sealed, dust-free booth.

- The **paint droplets** are the *evaporated metal atoms* flying through vacuum.
- The **air pressure of the can** is the *vacuum level inside the chamber* — too much "air" (poor vacuum) makes the atoms collide, scatter, and produce a dull, hazy coating.
- The **distance of the can from the wall** is the *source-to-substrate distance* — too close causes burning; too far wastes metal and produces uneven thickness.
- The **speed of your hand** is the *web speed* — faster hand, thinner paint layer.
- Your **eyes watching the paint film build-up** are the *thickness sensors* (Quartz Crystal Monitor or Optical Monitor) that feedback to you (the controller).

Now replace the human painter with **PID controllers and PLCs**, and you have automated metallizing process control.

## 1.3 Standard Process Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants Used in Metallizing Calculations**

| Symbol | Parameter | Typical Industrial Value |
|---|---|---|
| $P_{ch}$ | Chamber base pressure | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$ Torr (**0.013 – 0.0013 Pa**) |
| $T_{evap}$ | Aluminum evaporation temperature | **1200 °C to 1500 °C** |
| $T_{sub}$ | Substrate (plastic) temperature | **20 °C to 80 °C** |
| $R_d$ | Deposition rate | **1 nm/s to 50 nm/s** |
| $t_f$ | Film thickness | **30 nm – 500 nm** |
| $d$ | Source-to-substrate distance | **300 mm – 600 mm** |
| $v_{web}$ | Web speed (roll-to-roll) | **50 – 300 m/min** |
| $M_{Al}$ | Molar mass of aluminum | **26.98 g/mol** |
| $\rho_{Al}$ | Density of aluminum | **2.70 g/cm³** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these numbers matter for KTU:** Board questions frequently quote the chamber pressure in Torr and ask conversions to Pascal. Always remember: $1 \text{ Torr} = 133.322 \text{ Pa}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Metallizing Process Control

### Pillar 1 — Vacuum Level Control
The chamber must be evacuated to a base pressure where the **Mean Free Path (MFP)** of evaporated metal atoms exceeds the source-to-substrate distance. This ensures ballistic (line-of-sight) transport.

- **Primary pump:** Rotary vane (achieves $10^{-3}$ Torr)
- **Secondary pump:** Diffusion pump or cryopump (achieves $10^{-5}$ Torr)

### Pillar 2 — Evaporation Rate Control
The rate at which metal atoms leave the crucible is governed by vapor pressure and temperature. A **PID controller** regulates the crucible heater power to maintain a constant evaporation rate.

### Pillar 3 — Substrate Temperature Control
Plastics are heat-sensitive. Excessive $T_{sub}$ causes warping, while too low a temperature prevents proper adhesion. Heated rollers or radiant heaters with thermocouples form the control loop.

### Pillar 4 — Web Speed & Thickness Control
In continuous roll-to-roll metallizers, the **line speed** is the primary thickness actuator. A faster web yields a thinner coating, since:

$$t_f \propto \frac{R_d}{v_{web}}$$

A Quartz Crystal Monitor (QCM) senses real-time thickness; its signal feeds back to the web drive motor controller.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Formula | Description & Units | KTU Exam Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_d = \dfrac{m \cdot \cos\theta \cdot \cos\phi}{\pi \rho d^{2}}$ | Lambertian deposition rate (mass $m$ per unit time) | Film thickness estimation |
| 2 | $\dot{m} = 0.0585\, P_s \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{T}}$ | Langmuir evaporation flux ($\dot{m}$ in g/cm²·s, $P_s$ in Torr, $T$ in K) | Evaporation rate calculation |
| 3 | $t_f = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot t_{res}}{\rho}$ | Film thickness from residence time $t_{res}$ | Direct thickness prediction |
| 4 | $\lambda = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{P \text{ (Torr)}}$ | Mean Free Path of gas molecules in cm | Vacuum quality assessment |
| 5 | $T_f = t_f \cdot v_{web}$ | Relation between web speed and effective film thickness | Line-speed optimization |
| 6 | $P_{total} = P_{atm} + P_{vapor}$ | Total pressure inside chamber (ideal gas sum) | Pump-down cycle analysis |
| 7 | $\text{Yield} = \dfrac{N_{good}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Production yield in Statistical Process Control (SPC) | Quality control reporting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used by process engineers to (i) design the chamber geometry ($d$), (ii) select the correct heater wattage, (iii) tune the PID controller setpoints, and (iv) troubleshoot optical-density defects on the production floor.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Langmuir Evaporation Rate Equation

The **Hertz–Knudsen–Langmuir (HKL) equation** is the cornerstone of evaporation-rate control. We derive it from kinetic theory of gases.

**Step 1 — Starting Point (Maxwell-Boltzmann Flux):**
From kinetic theory, the molecular flux leaving a liquid surface is given by:

$$\Phi = \dfrac{n \bar{c}}{4}$$

where $n$ is the number density of vapor molecules above the surface, and $\bar{c}$ is the mean molecular speed:

$$\bar{c} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8RT}{\pi M}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $n$ from Ideal Gas Law:**
Using $P_s = nRT$, hence $n = P_s / (RT)$:

$$\Phi = \dfrac{P_s}{4RT} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{8RT}{\pi M}} = \dfrac{P_s}{\sqrt{2\pi MRT}}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Mass Flux:**
Multiplying the molar flux by the molecular mass $M$:

$$\dot{m} = \Phi \cdot M = P_s \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{2\pi RT}}$$

**Step 4 — Insert Numerical Constants for cm·g·s Units:**
Evaluating the constant $1/\sqrt{2\pi R} \approx 0.0585$ when $P_s$ is in Torr, $T$ in Kelvin, and $M$ in g/mol:

$$\dot{m} = 0.0585 \, P_s \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{T}}$$

This is the final Langmuir equation used by every metallizing plant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The evaporation rate scales with vapor pressure $P_s$ (highly nonlinear with temperature) and inversely with $\sqrt{T}$. This is why PID controllers must use **non-linear gain** near the aluminum melting point — a 10 °C rise near 1500 °C can double the rate.

## 3.2 Derivation: Deposition Rate on a Flat Substrate

Given a point source evaporating mass $m$ per unit time, the mass deposited per unit area on a substrate oriented at angle $\phi$ to the source normal, located at distance $d$, and viewing the source at angle $\theta$ from its surface normal, is:

**Step 1 — Geometry of a Point Source:**
For an isotropic point source, intensity falls off as $1/(4\pi d^{2})$. Projecting onto a differential area element requires cosine terms:

$$dR_d = \dfrac{m}{4\pi d^{2}} \cos\theta \cos\phi \, dA$$

**Step 2 — Integrate Over a Cosine Emitter (Small Source):**
For a small area source approximated as a Lambertian emitter (most practical crucibles):

$$R_d = \dfrac{m \cos\theta \cos\phi}{\pi d^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Normalize to Thickness:**
Convert mass rate to thickness rate using $\rho$:

$$\dfrac{dt_f}{dt} = \dfrac{R_d}{\rho} = \dfrac{m \cos\theta \cos\phi}{\pi \rho d^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Closed-Loop Integration over Web Pass:**
If the web travels at $v_{web}$ and passes once under the source, the effective deposition time per point is $t_{res} = L/v_{web}$, where $L$ is the source length:

$$t_f = \dfrac{m \cos\theta \cos\phi \cdot L}{\pi \rho d^{2} \cdot v_{web}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Control Insight:** To *halve* the film thickness, you must *double* the web speed. To *double* the thickness, you must *increase* the heater power such that $m$ doubles (a non-linear task, requiring PID tuning).

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Aluminum is evaporated at a rate $\dot{m} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ g/cm²·s in a vacuum chamber. The substrate is located at $d = 40$ cm at normal incidence ($\theta = \phi = 0°$). Calculate the **deposition rate in nm/s** if $\rho_{Al} = 2.70$ g/cm³.

**Solution:**

Given: $\dot{m} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ g/cm²·s, $d = 40$ cm, $\theta = \phi = 0°$, $\rho_{Al} = 2.70$ g/cm³.

Step 1 — Apply the deposition-rate formula for a small planar source:

$$R_d = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cos\theta \cos\phi}{\pi d^{2}}$$

Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:

$$R_d = \dfrac{(1.0 \times 10^{-3})(1)(1)}{\pi (40)^{2}}$$

$$R_d = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{5026.55}$$

$$R_d \approx 1.99 \times 10^{-7} \text{ g/cm}^{2}\text{s}$$

Step 3 — Convert to thickness rate using $\rho_{Al}$:

$$\dfrac{dt_f}{dt} = \dfrac{R_d}{\rho_{Al}} = \dfrac{1.99 \times 10^{-7}}{2.70}$$

$$\dfrac{dt_f}{dt} \approx 7.37 \times 10^{-8} \text{ cm/s}$$

Step 4 — Convert cm/s to nm/s (multiply by $10^{7}$):

$$\dfrac{dt_f}{dt} \approx 0.737 \text{ nm/s}$$

**Answer:** The deposition rate is approximately **0.74 nm/s**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Metallizing Process Control

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **closed-loop feedback architecture** used in industrial roll-to-roll vacuum metallizers. This is the standard reference schematic KTU examiners expect for Module 4.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PLC["PLC Master Controller"]
    Setpoint["Setpoint: Target Thickness t_f_target"]
    PIDevap["PID Evap Controller"]
    PIDtemp["PID Substrate Temp Controller"]
    PIDweb["PID Web Speed Controller"]
    VacGauge["Pirani Vacuum Gauge"]
    CryGauge["Cold Cathode Gauge"]
    QCM["Quartz Crystal Monitor"]
    TC1["Thermocouple Substrate"]
    TC2["Thermocouple Crucible"]
    Encoder["Web Encoder Tachometer"]
    Heater1["Crucible Heater Actuator"]
    Heater2["Substrate Heater Actuator"]
    Motor["Web Drive Motor"]
    VacValve["Throttle Valve Actuator"]
    VacuumPump["Diffusion Pump Bank"]
    Crucible["Aluminum Crucible Source"]
    Chamber["Vacuum Chamber"]
    Web["Plastic Web Substrate"]
    Product["Metallized Film Output"]
    HMI["Operator HMI Panel"]

    Setpoint --> PLC
    PLC --> PIDevap
    PLC --> PIDtemp
    PLC --> PIDweb
    PLC --> VacValve

    PIDevap --> Heater1
    PIDtemp --> Heater2
    PIDweb --> Motor

    VacGauge --> VacValve
    CryGauge --> VacValve
    QCM --> PIDevap
    QCM --> PIDweb
    TC1 --> PIDtemp
    TC2 --> PIDevap
    Encoder --> PIDweb

    VacuumPump --> Chamber
    Heater1 --> Crucible
    Crucible --> Chamber
    Chamber --> Web
    Web --> Product

    PLC -.Feedback Display.-> HMI
    QCM -.Live Thickness Readout.-> HMI
```

## 4.2 Control Loop Hierarchy — Detailed Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InnerLoop["INNER LOOP - Evaporation Rate"]
        A1[Setpoint Rate] --> B1[PID Controller]
        B1 --> C1[Heater Power]
        C1 --> D1[Crucible Evaporation]
        D1 --> E1[QCM Sensor]
        E1 -.Feedback.-> B1
    end

    subgraph MiddleLoop["MIDDLE LOOP - Chamber Vacuum"]
        A2[Setpoint Pressure] --> B2[Vacuum Controller]
        B2 --> C2[Throttle Valve]
        C2 --> D2[Chamber Pressure]
        D2 --> E2[Pirani Gauge]
        E2 -.Feedback.-> B2
    end

    subgraph OuterLoop["OUTER LOOP - Production SPC"]
        A3[Batch Target O.D.] --> B3[Statistical Engine]
        B3 --> C3[Recipe Adjustment]
        C3 --> D3[Setpoint Rate]
        C3 --> E3[Setpoint Pressure]
        D3 --> A1
        E3 --> A2
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The *Inner Loop* maintains constant metal flux. The *Middle Loop* maintains vacuum integrity. The *Outer Loop* uses Statistical Process Control (SPC) data from previous batches to update the recipes — this is the "Process Control" aspect unique to KTU Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]
**Q: List any three critical process parameters that must be controlled in vacuum metallizing of plastics and state why each is important.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Chamber vacuum pressure ($10^{-4}$ – $10^{-5}$ Torr):** Ensures the Mean Free Path of metal atoms exceeds source-to-substrate distance, preventing film oxidation and scattering. *(1 mark)*
2. **Evaporation temperature / heater power:** Controls the rate at which metal atoms leave the crucible, directly determining the deposition rate. *(1 mark)*
3. **Web (substrate) speed:** Acts as the primary actuator for film thickness control; constant speed ensures uniform optical density. *(1 mark)*

> [!TIP]
> Examiner awards full marks only if *all three parameters are justified with a "why."* Mere listing gets only 1.5 marks.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: What is the role of a Quartz Crystal Monitor (QCM) in the metallizing process?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A QCM is an *in-situ* thickness sensor that uses the **piezoelectric principle**: a quartz crystal's resonant frequency decreases linearly as mass (metal vapor) deposits on it. The frequency shift is converted to a film-thickness reading in real time, which is fed back to the PID controller to regulate either the heater power or web speed. *(2 marks for principle, 1 mark for control function)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 14 Marks]

**Q: With the help of a block diagram, explain the closed-loop process control system used in a continuous roll-to-roll vacuum metallizing plant. Discuss the role of (a) vacuum measurement, (b) PID control, and (c) Statistical Process Control (SPC) in ensuring product consistency. (14 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Block Diagram & Vacuum Measurement (7 marks)

**Solution Outline:**

The closed-loop system comprises three nested control loops (refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram):

**1. Vacuum measurement subsystem (4 marks):**
- **Pirani gauge** ($10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}$ Torr range): thermal conductivity-based; used during rough and medium vacuum.
- **Cold-cathode (Penning) gauge** ($10^{-6}$ to $10^{-8}$ Torr range): magnetic field ionizes residual gas; used at ultimate vacuum.
- The gauge output (4–20 mA analog signal) is fed to the **vacuum controller**, which adjusts the **throttle valve** between the diffusion pump and chamber to maintain a constant chamber pressure despite outgassing from the plastic web.

**2. Why vacuum control is critical (3 marks):**
- Controls the **Mean Free Path** $\lambda$ of aluminum atoms. From the formula $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-3}/P$ (Torr), at $P = 10^{-4}$ Torr, $\lambda \approx 50$ m — far greater than typical $d = 0.5$ m, ensuring ballistic transport.
- Prevents **oxidation** of the hot aluminum film.
- Reduces **scattering** that would otherwise make the film hazy.

#### Part (b) — PID Control & SPC (7 marks)

**Solution Outline:**

**1. PID control architecture (4 marks):**
A **PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative)** algorithm regulates three actuators:
- **P-action:** Provides immediate correction proportional to error $e(t) = SP - PV$.
- **I-action:** Eliminates steady-state offset (critical for long metallizing runs).
- **D-action:** Anticipates future error by observing the rate of change, useful against sudden outgassing spikes.

The three PID loops (refer to Mermaid Section 4.2):
- Heater power ↔ QCM thickness sensor
- Substrate heater ↔ Substrate thermocouple
- Web motor ↔ Encoder

**2. Statistical Process Control (3 marks):**
- SPC uses **X-bar and R-charts** to monitor film thickness and optical density across batches.
- **Control limits** are set at $\pm 3\sigma$ from the mean.
- Out-of-control points trigger automatic recipe adjustments or line stoppages.
- **Cp and Cpk indices** quantify process capability; Cpk $\geq 1.33$ is industry standard for metallized film.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert**
> 1. Drawing the diagram *without* arrows showing feedback will lose 2 marks. The diagram must be a **closed loop**, not an open block diagram.
> 2. Writing "PID controls everything" without explaining **what each of P, I, D does** will lose at least 1.5 marks.
> 3. Confusing **Cp** (process capability, no centering) with **Cpk** (capability with centering) is a 1-mark deduction.

---

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam – Alternate Choice, 14 Marks]

**Q: (a) Derive the Langmuir evaporation-rate equation starting from kinetic theory. Explain its significance in setting the heater-power PID setpoint for aluminum metallizing. (7 marks)**
**(b) A roll-to-roll metallizer operates at a web speed of 100 m/min. The aluminum source is a planar crucible of length 30 cm located 50 cm from the web. The mass evaporation rate is $\dot{m} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ g/cm²·s. Calculate the resulting film thickness on the substrate. Given: $\rho_{Al} = 2.70$ g/cm³, $\cos\theta = \cos\phi = 1$. (7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Derivation & Significance (7 marks)

**Solution Outline:**

1. **Starting equation from kinetic theory** (1 mark):
$$\Phi = \dfrac{n \bar{c}}{4}, \quad \bar{c} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8RT}{\pi M}}$$

2. **Substitute ideal gas relation** $P_s = nRT$ (1 mark):
$$\Phi = \dfrac{P_s}{\sqrt{2\pi MRT}}$$

3. **Convert to mass flux** $\dot{m} = \Phi \cdot M$ (1 mark):
$$\dot{m} = P_s \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{2\pi RT}}$$

4. **Final numerical form** (1 mark):
$$\dot{m} = 0.0585 \, P_s \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{T}}$$

5. **Significance for PID setpoint (3 marks):**
   - The PID controller must compute the heater power required to maintain a target $\dot{m}$.
   - Because $P_s$ of aluminum increases **exponentially** with temperature (Clausius–Clapeyron), the PID must use a **non-linear gain schedule** or **dual-mode control** (gain scheduling near the operating point).
   - Typical aluminum $P_s$ at 1300 °C is $\sim 1$ Torr; at 1500 °C it is $\sim 10$ Torr — a 10× change in evaporation rate for a 200 °C rise.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Film Thickness (7 marks)

**Given:** $v_{web} = 100$ m/min, $L = 30$ cm, $d = 50$ cm, $\dot{m} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ g/cm²·s, $\rho_{Al} = 2.70$ g/cm³.

**Step 1:** Convert web speed to cm/s:

$$v_{web} = \dfrac{100 \times 100}{60} = 166.67 \text{ cm/s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute residence time:

$$t_{res} = \dfrac{L}{v_{web}} = \dfrac{30}{166.67} \approx 0.18 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3:** Apply deposition rate formula:

$$R_d = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cos\theta \cos\phi}{\pi d^{2}} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi (50)^{2}} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{7853.98}$$

$$R_d \approx 1.91 \times 10^{-7} \text{ g/cm}^{2}\text{s}$$

**Step 4:** Convert mass rate to thickness rate:

$$\dfrac{dt_f}{dt} = \dfrac{R_d}{\rho_{Al}} = \dfrac{1.91 \times 10^{-7}}{2.70} \approx 7.07 \times 10^{-8} \text{ cm/s}$$

**Step 5:** Multiply by residence time:

$$t_f = (7.07 \times 10^{-8}) \times (0.18) \approx 1.27 \times 10^{-8} \text{ cm}$$

**Step 6:** Convert to nm:

$$t_f \approx 0.127 \text{ nm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Trap:** Students often forget to convert $v_{web}$ from m/min to cm/s. This single mistake invalidates the entire numerical answer and forfeits 3–4 marks. Always show the unit conversion step explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Process Control of Metallizing**

- ☐ **Vacuum base pressure** for metallizing: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$ Torr.
- ☐ **Mean Free Path** formula: $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-3} / P$ (cm, with $P$ in Torr).
- ☐ **Aluminum evaporation temperature range:** 1200 °C – 1500 °C.
- ☐ **Langmuir equation:** $\dot{m} = 0.0585 P_s \sqrt{M/T}$ — must be derivable in exams.
- ☐ **Deposition rate formula:** $R_d = \dot{m}\cos\theta\cos\phi / (\pi \rho d^{2})$.
- ☐ **Film thickness on moving web:** $t_f = (\dot{m} \cos\theta \cos\phi \cdot L) / (\pi \rho d^{2} v_{web})$.
- ☐ **Three PID loops** in any metallizer: Heater power, Substrate temp, Web speed.
- ☐ **QCM** is the primary thickness sensor (piezoelectric frequency-shift principle).
- ☐ **Pirani gauge** for rough/medium vacuum; **Cold-cathode gauge** for high vacuum.
- ☐ **SPC tools:** X-bar, R-charts, Cp, Cpk. Industry target: Cpk $\geq 1.33$.
- ☐ **Three nested loops architecture:** Inner (rate) → Middle (vacuum) → Outer (SPC recipe).
- ☐ **Unit conversion trap:** 1 Torr = 133.322 Pa. Always state units explicitly in derivations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plastic Hot Stamping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT864 - PLASTICS & DECORATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Plastic Hot Stamping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hot Stamping (also known as Hot Foil Stamping or Thermal Transfer Decoration)** is a dry, solvent-free **surface decoration and finishing process** in which a pre-printed metallic or pigmented foil layer is selectively transferred onto a thermoplastic, thermoset, or composite substrate by the simultaneous application of **heat**, **pressure**, and **dwell time** through a heated metal die (typically brass, magnesium, or silicone rubber).

The process relies on the principle of **thermo-activated adhesion**, where a heat-sensitive adhesive (release coat + lacquer system) on the foil softens above its glass transition temperature ($T_g$) and bonds permanently to the plastic substrate. After cooling, the carrier film is peeled away, leaving behind only the decorative layer.

**Standard Performance Metrics (Industry Benchmarks):**
- Typical Foil Stamping Temperature: **$90^\circ C$ to $160^\circ C$**
- Applied Die Pressure: **$0.5$ to $5.0 \text{ MPa}$**
- Dwell Time: **$0.5$ to $3.0 \text{ seconds}$**
- Production Speed: **$15$ to $60$ cycles/minute**

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Wax Seal" of Plastics:**
> Imagine you are writing a letter to the King and want to seal it with your family crest. You take a stick of red wax, light a candle, melt the wax over the folded letter (heat), press your engraved brass signet ring firmly into the molten wax (pressure), hold it still for 2 seconds (dwell time), and then peel the ring away. When you lift it, a perfect mirror-image of your crest is permanently embossed on the letter.
>
> **Hot stamping on plastic is the exact same principle — except instead of wax, you use a multi-layered polyester foil carrying a metallic or pigmented ink, and instead of a candle, you use a thermostatically heated brass die (typically $120^\circ C$ – $140^\circ C$).** The plastic substrate replaces the paper, and the signet ring is replaced by a precision-engraved magnesium or brass cliché.

## 1.3 Industry Terminology Quick-Glossary

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Cliché / Die** | The engraved metal plate (brass / magnesium / silicone) that contacts the foil |
| **Carrier Film** | The polyester (PET) base film, typically $12\text{–}23\,\mu m$ thick |
| **Release Coat** | A wax-based layer that melts at stamping temperature to free the ink |
| **Lacquer / Color Coat** | The decorative pigmented or metallic layer that remains on the part |
| **Adhesive Coat** | Heat-activated resin that bonds the lacquer to the substrate |
| **Dwell Time** | Duration the heated die remains in contact with the foil/substrate |
| **Substrate** | The plastic part receiving the decoration (ABS, PP, PS, PVC, etc.) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Layered Architecture of a Hot-Stamp Foil

A hot-stamp foil is **not a single material** — it is a precisely engineered **sandwich of 5 thin polymeric/metallic layers** laminated onto a polyester carrier. Understanding this architecture is critical for KTU board questions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five-Layer Foil Architecture (Top → Bottom):**
> 1. **Carrier Film (PET)** — $12\text{–}23\,\mu m$ — provides mechanical strength
> 2. **Release Coat** — wax/silicone — melts at $80^\circ C$ – $100^\circ C$
> 3. **Protective Lacquer** — optional, top-coat for scratch resistance
> 4. **Decorative Layer** — metallic aluminum vacuum-deposited OR pigmented ink
> 5. **Adhesive (Size Coat)** — thermoplastic resin — activates at $90^\circ C$ – $140^\circ C$

## 2.2 Process Operating Window — The "Process Triangle"

Every successful hot-stamp operation lives inside a **three-variable operating triangle**. If any one variable deviates outside the safe window, the decoration fails.

| Variable | Too Low → Failure Mode | Optimal Range | Too High → Failure Mode |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Temperature** | Foil does not release; patchy transfer | $90^\circ C$ – $160^\circ C$ | Foil burns, substrate distorts |
| **Pressure** | Incomplete contact; missing pixels | $0.5$ – $5.0 \text{ MPa}$ | Substrate crushes, die deforms |
| **Dwell Time** | Adhesive never cures; peels off | $0.5$ – $3.0 \text{ s}$ | Substrate warps, halo defect |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Golden Rule" of Hot Stamping (Examiner's Favourite):**
> **Heat + Pressure + Time** must be balanced. A common KTU trick question asks: *"Why can't we just use very high temperature for very short time?"* The answer: thermal lag in the substrate prevents the adhesive coat from reaching activation temperature at the interface unless dwell time is sufficient.

## 2.3 The Heat-Transfer Mechanism (Why the Foil Sticks)

The decoration transfer is governed by **transient conduction through the multi-layer foil stack**. Heat flows from the heated die → carrier film → release coat → lacquer → adhesive → into the plastic substrate. The interface temperature at the adhesive/substrate boundary must exceed the **activation temperature ($T_a$)** of the size coat.

The governing equation is the **1-D transient heat conduction equation** (Fourier's law):

$$q''(x,t) = -k \frac{\partial T(x,t)}{\partial x}$$

where:
- $q''(x,t)$ — heat flux ($\text{W/m}^2$)
- $k$ — thermal conductivity of the layer ($\text{W/(m·K)}$)
- $\frac{\partial T}{\partial x}$ — temperature gradient ($\text{K/m}$)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every equation required to solve KTU 2024 Scheme numerical questions on Hot Stamping. **Memorize this table verbatim for the ESE.**

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat required to raise foil/substrate to stamping $T$ | $m$ = mass (kg), $c_p$ = specific heat $\left(\text{J/kg·K}\right)$, $\Delta T$ = temp. rise (K) |
| 2 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Die pressure on substrate | $F$ = force (N), $A$ = contact area ($\text{m}^2$) |
| 3 | $t_d = \dfrac{Q}{h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_m}$ | Dwell time (heat-transfer based) | $h$ = heat-transfer coeff. $\left(\text{W/m}^2\text{K}\right)$, $\Delta T_m$ = log-mean temp. diff. (K) |
| 4 | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho \cdot c_p}$ | Thermal diffusivity of substrate | $\alpha$ ($\text{m}^2/\text{s}$), $\rho$ = density ($\text{kg/m}^3$) |
| 5 | $\text{Bi} = \dfrac{h \cdot L_c}{k}$ | Biot number — checks lumped-capacitance validity | $L_c$ = characteristic length (m) |
| 6 | $F_o = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot t}{L_c^2}$ | Fourier number — checks transient regime | $t$ = time (s) |
| 7 | $\sigma_{adh} = \dfrac{F_{peel}}{w}$ | Adhesive peel strength (ASTM test) | $F_{peel}$ (N), $w$ = width (m) |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{A_{transferred}}{A_{die}} \times 100\%$ | Transfer efficiency (quality metric) | $A$ = area ($\text{m}^2$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Notation:** To preserve markdown table integrity, the absolute-value-style bars are written as $\vert$ or $\mid$ — never as the literal pipe `\|` character — to avoid breaking the table renderer.

## 2.5 Why Hot Stamping Matters in Engineering

- **Automotive Interior Trim**: Wood-grain, chrome-effect foils on ABS/PP dashboards
- **Consumer Electronics**: Logos on mobile phone housings (PC/ABS blends)
- **Cosmetic Packaging**: Metallic lipstick caps, perfume bottles (SAN, PMMA)
- **Appliance Panels**: Brand markings on washing-machine consoles
- **Toys & Stationery**: Decorative patterns on PP/PE parts

The advantage over alternatives (pad printing, spray painting, in-mold decoration) is that hot stamping is **dry, solvent-free, highly repeatable, and capable of fine micro-detail** (down to $0.1\,\text{mm}$ line width).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Required to Reach Activation Temperature

A KTU favourite: *"Calculate the heat energy required by a brass die to bring a $0.05\,\text{kg}$ plastic substrate from $25^\circ C$ to the foil activation temperature of $130^\circ C$."*

**Given Data:**
- Mass of substrate: $m = 0.05\,\text{kg}$
- Specific heat of substrate (ABS): $c_p = 1400\,\text{J/(kg·K)}$
- Initial temperature: $T_i = 25^\circ C = 298\,\text{K}$
- Final temperature: $T_f = 130^\circ C = 403\,\text{K}$

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**

$$Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 2 — Compute the temperature difference.**

$$\Delta T = T_f - T_i = 403 - 298 = 105\,\text{K}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$Q = (0.05) \cdot (1400) \cdot (105)$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the product.**

$$Q = 70 \cdot 105 = 7350\,\text{J}$$

$$\boxed{Q = 7.35\,\text{kJ}}$$

**Interpretation:** The die must deliver at least $7.35\,\text{kJ}$ of thermal energy into the substrate during the dwell phase. In practice, additional heat is lost to the foil and surroundings, so the die heater wattage is sized with a $1.5$ – $2.0\times$ safety factor.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Die Pressure from Hydraulic Cylinder Force

A horizontal hot-stamp press uses a pneumatic cylinder of effective area $A_{cyl} = 0.0015\,\text{m}^2$ supplying a pressure $P_{cyl} = 0.8\,\text{MPa}$. The die contact patch measures $150\,\text{mm} \times 80\,\text{mm}$. Calculate the **die pressure on the substrate**.

**Step 1 — Compute the cylinder force.**

$$F_{cyl} = P_{cyl} \cdot A_{cyl}$$

$$F_{cyl} = (0.8 \times 10^6\,\text{Pa}) \cdot (0.0015\,\text{m}^2)$$

$$F_{cyl} = 1200\,\text{N}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the die contact area.**

$$A_{die} = L \cdot W = (0.150\,\text{m}) \cdot (0.080\,\text{m})$$

$$A_{die} = 0.012\,\text{m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Apply the pressure equation.**

$$P_{die} = \dfrac{F_{cyl}}{A_{die}} = \dfrac{1200}{0.012}$$

$$\boxed{P_{die} = 100{,}000\,\text{Pa} = 0.1\,\text{MPa}}$$

**Interpretation:** A die pressure of $0.1\,\text{MPa}$ is **too low** — typical hot stamping requires $0.5$ – $5.0\,\text{MPa}$. The student must recommend either a smaller die, higher pneumatic pressure, or a mechanical toggle press.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Minimum Dwell Time using Lumped Capacitance

For thin plastic substrates, lumped-capacitance analysis applies when $\text{Bi} < 0.1$. The time-temperature response is:

$$\dfrac{T(t) - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = e^{-\left(\dfrac{h \cdot A}{\rho \cdot V \cdot c_p}\right) t}$$

Solving for time $t$:

$$t = -\left(\dfrac{\rho \cdot V \cdot c_p}{h \cdot A}\right) \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T(t) - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty}\right)$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

A $3\,\text{mm}$ thick PP plaque ($0.10\,\text{m} \times 0.10\,\text{m}$) is hot-stamped. The die surface is at $T_\infty = 140^\circ C$. The PP starts at $T_i = 30^\circ C$. The adhesive activates at $T(t) = 95^\circ C$. Given:
- $h = 250\,\text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$ (convective contact coeff.)
- $k_{PP} = 0.12\,\text{W/(m·K)}$
- $\rho_{PP} = 905\,\text{kg/m}^3$
- $c_{p,PP} = 1920\,\text{J/(kg·K)}$

**Step 1 — Check Biot Number validity.**

$$L_c = \dfrac{V}{A_s} \approx \dfrac{\text{thickness}}{2} = 0.0015\,\text{m}$$

$$\text{Bi} = \dfrac{h \cdot L_c}{k} = \dfrac{250 \cdot 0.0015}{0.12}$$

$$\text{Bi} = 3.125$$

**Step 2 — Conclude validity.**

Since $\text{Bi} = 3.125 \gg 0.1$, **lumped capacitance is INVALID**. The transient conduction through the substrate thickness must be modelled using the **Heisler chart** or **1-D finite-difference method**. This is a common KTU examiner trap!

**Step 3 — Use the semi-infinite solid approximation** (valid for short times $t \ll L^2/\alpha$):

$$\dfrac{T(x,t) - T_i}{T_\infty - T_i} = \text{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}}\right) - e^{\left(\dfrac{hx}{k} + \dfrac{h^2\alpha t}{k^2}\right)} \cdot \text{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}} + \dfrac{h\sqrt{\alpha t}}{k}\right)$$

For the **interface** $x = L = 0.003\,\text{m}$ and assuming perfect contact ($h \to \infty$), the simpler form applies:

$$\dfrac{T(L,t) - T_i}{T_\infty - T_i} = \text{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{L}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Compute thermal diffusivity of PP.**

$$\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho \cdot c_p} = \dfrac{0.12}{905 \cdot 1920}$$

$$\alpha = 6.9 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for time when $T(L,t) = 95^\circ C$.**

$$\dfrac{95 - 30}{140 - 30} = \text{erfc}(\eta) \;\Rightarrow\; \dfrac{65}{110} = 0.591 = \text{erfc}(\eta)$$

$$\eta = \text{erfc}^{-1}(0.591) \approx 0.560$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{L}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}} \;\Rightarrow\; \sqrt{t} = \dfrac{L}{2\eta\sqrt{\alpha}}$$

$$t = \left(\dfrac{L}{2\eta\sqrt{\alpha}}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{0.003}{2 \cdot 0.560 \cdot \sqrt{6.9 \times 10^{-8}}}\right)^2$$

$$t = \left(\dfrac{0.003}{2 \cdot 0.560 \cdot 2.626 \times 10^{-4}}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{0.003}{2.941 \times 10^{-4}}\right)^2$$

$$t = (10.20)^2 \approx 104\,\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{t_{\min} \approx 104\,\text{seconds}}$$

**Critical Insight:** This is the *minimum dwell* for the substrate's back face to reach activation temperature. In practice, hot stamping uses **$1$ – $3\,\text{s}$** dwell times because the foil's adhesive layer is extremely thin ($\sim 2\text{–}5\,\mu m$) and heats up almost instantaneously — the substrate only needs to be warmed enough at the **surface**, not through the full thickness.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Hot Stamping vs Competing Processes

| Parameter | Hot Stamping | Pad Printing | Spray Painting | In-Mold Decoration |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Resolution** | Excellent ($0.1\,\text{mm}$) | Good ($0.3\,\text{mm}$) | Poor | Excellent |
| **Solvent Use** | None | Some | High | None |
| **Setup Cost** | Medium | Low | Low | High |
| **Per-Part Cost** | Low (high volume) | Medium | High (labour) | Low |
| **Cure Time** | Instant (cool-down) | $5$ – $30\,\text{s}$ | $10$ – $60\,\text{min}$ | N/A (in-mold) |
| **Substrate Curvature** | Limited (flat/near-flat) | Excellent (3D) | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Metallic Finish** | Native (foil) | Requires metallic ink | Requires metallic paint | Native |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — Hot Stamping Operation

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete sequential workflow** of an industrial hot-stamping cycle, from foil loading to finished decorated part.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Load Plastic Substrate on Fixture] --> B[Position Foil Roll Above Substrate]
    B --> C[Lower Heated Die onto Foil+Substrate Stack]
    C --> D[Apply Pressure via Pneumatic or Toggle Press]
    D --> E[Activate Heater - Maintain Die at 120-140 C]
    E --> F[Dwell Phase - Hold for 0.5 to 3.0 Seconds]
    F --> G[Release Pressure - Raise Die]
    G --> H[Peel Away Carrier Film Mechanically]
    H --> I{Quality Inspection}
    I -->|Pass| J[Transfer Decorated Part to Output Conveyor]
    I -->|Fail| K[Route to Rejection Bin for Rework]
    J --> L[End: Pack and Ship]
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 Equipment Architecture — Hot Stamping Machine Subsystems

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a typical roll-on hot-stamping machine, showing all major subsystems and their interactions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PowerTrain[Power and Drive Subsystem]
        P1[Main Electric Motor 1.5 kW] --> P2[Reduction Gearbox]
        P2 --> P3[Cam and Toggle Mechanism]
    end

    subgraph ThermalCtrl[Thermal Control Subsystem]
        T1[Die Heater Cartridge 500 W] --> T2[PID Temperature Controller]
        T2 --> T3[Thermocouple Feedback Sensor]
    end

    subgraph FoilFeed[Foil Feed Subsystem]
        F1[Supply Spool with Carrier Film] --> F2[Tension Roller]
        F2 --> F3[Die Contact Zone]
        F3 --> F4[Take-Up Spool]
        F4 --> F5[Stripper Roller]
    end

    subgraph PartHolder[Part Holding Subsystem]
        H1[Vacuum Chuck Fixture] --> H2[Substrate Plastic Part]
    end

    P3 --> F3
    T1 --> F3
    F3 --> H2
    H2 --> Out[Finished Decorated Output]
```

## 4.3 Foil Cross-Section Schematic (ASCII Representation)

The following ASCII schematic visualizes the **five-layer foil architecture** described in Section 2.1. Layer thicknesses are NOT to scale (each layer is $\mu m$-scale; the carrier is the thickest).

```
|======================================|  ←  Carrier Film (PET, 12-23 μm)
+--------------------------------------+
|  ~ ~ ~ ~ Release Coat ~ ~ ~ ~ ~ ~ ~ |  ←  Release Coat (wax/silicone, ~1 μm)
+--------------------------------------+
|  ////// Protective Lacquer //////// |  ←  Protective Lacquer (optional, ~2 μm)
+--------------------------------------+
|  ◆◆◆◆◆ Decorative Layer ◆◆◆◆◆◆◆◆◆ |  ←  Al vacuum-deposited OR pigmented ink (~0.1 μm)
+--------------------------------------+
|  ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ Adhesive Size Coat ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ |  ←  Heat-Activated Adhesive (~3-5 μm)
+--------------------------------------+
                ↓↓↓ Heat + Pressure Applied ↓↓↓
+--------------------------------------+
|      [  Plastic Substrate (Part)  ] |  ←  ABS / PP / PS / PVC / PMMA
+--------------------------------------+
```

**Reading the diagram:** When the heated die presses down from the top, the **release coat** melts and the **adhesive coat** softens. After cooling, the PET carrier is peeled upward (released), while the bottom three layers (lacquer + decorative + adhesive) remain bonded to the substrate.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**Define hot stamping as applied to plastic decoration. List any three advantages of hot stamping over conventional spray painting.**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key Words Bolded):**

> Hot stamping is a **dry, heat-and-pressure-based surface decoration process** in which a multi-layer polymeric foil is selectively transferred onto a plastic substrate using a heated engraved die. The process requires no solvents, no curing ovens, and produces **instant, permanent decoration** in a single cycle.

**Three advantages over spray painting:**
1. **Zero VOC emissions** — environmentally compliant, no solvent recovery needed.
2. **Superior metallic brilliance** — vacuum-deposited aluminum foil gives mirror-like finish unattainable with paints.
3. **Lower per-part cost at high volume** — roll-feed foil is cheaper than paint + masking + labour.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is the function of (a) the release coat, and (b) the adhesive coat in a hot-stamp foil?**

**Model Answer:**

> **(a) Release Coat:** A thin wax- or silicone-based layer ($\sim 1\,\mu m$) situated between the PET carrier and the decorative layer. It **melts at stamping temperature ($80^\circ C$ – $100^\circ C$)**, weakening its bond to the carrier so that during the peel-off step, only the PET film lifts away, leaving the decorative layers on the substrate.

> **(b) Adhesive Coat (Size Coat):** A thermoplastic resin layer ($\sim 3\text{–}5\,\mu m$) at the bottom of the foil. It **softens above its activation temperature ($90^\circ C$ – $140^\circ C$)** and wets the plastic substrate surface. Upon cooling, it solidifies and forms a **permanent mechanical + chemical bond** with the substrate, anchoring the decorative layer.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model — 14 Marks]**

> **"With a neat sketch, explain the hot stamping process for plastic decoration. Discuss the role of temperature, pressure, and dwell time on the quality of decoration."**  
> *(a) Process explanation with diagram — 7 marks*  
> *(b) Role of process parameters and defect analysis — 7 marks*

#### Solution (a) — Process Explanation (7 Marks)

Hot stamping is performed using a **roll-on or vertical platen press**. The plastic substrate is loaded onto a fixture. A roll of multi-layer foil (carrier + release + decorative + adhesive) is positioned above the part. A heated die — engraved with the desired pattern in mirror image — descends onto the foil. Heat conducted through the carrier film activates the release coat and adhesive coat simultaneously. After a controlled dwell time (typically $1\text{–}2\,\text{s}$), the die retracts, and the carrier film is mechanically stripped upward. The decorative pattern remains permanently bonded to the part.

**Diagram (refer Section 4.3 ASCII + Mermaid 4.1):**

```
[Heated Die] ↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓
  [PET Carrier]
  [Release Coat]   → melts at ~90°C
  [Lacquer Layer]  → remains
  [Decorative Ink] → remains
  [Adhesive Coat]  → softens at ~120°C
  [SUBSTRATE]
↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑ Carrier Stripped Here
```

**Process Cycle Steps (Examiner expects numbered list, 3 marks):**
1. Load substrate onto fixture
2. Index foil over the part
3. Lower heated die with controlled force
4. Hold for dwell time (heat transfer phase)
5. Retract die
6. Strip carrier film
7. Inspect and unload

#### Solution (b) — Role of Parameters & Defects (7 Marks)

| Parameter | Function | Defect if Wrong |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Temperature** | Activates adhesive coat, melts release coat | **Too low:** patchy transfer, peeling; **Too high:** foil discolours, substrate distorts |
| **Pressure** | Ensures intimate contact across entire die face | **Too low:** air gaps → "holidays"; **Too high:** crushes thin-walled parts |
| **Dwell Time** | Allows heat to penetrate through foil to substrate interface | **Too short:** adhesive never reaches activation $T$; **Too long:** thermal damage |

**Common Defects (3 marks):**
1. **Halo effect** — over-stamping causes foil to spread beyond the design boundary.
2. **Peeling** — under-temperature or under-pressure → poor adhesion.
3. **Burning / Discolouration** — over-temperature → PET carrier scorches.

---

### ⭐ Question B — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model — 14 Marks, Alternative Choice]**

> **"Describe the construction of a hot-stamping foil. Explain the materials used for the (i) carrier film, (ii) release coat, (iii) decorative layer, and (iv) adhesive coat. Discuss any four applications of hot-stamped plastic parts."**  
> *(a) Foil construction and materials — 7 marks*  
> *(b) Applications discussion — 7 marks*

#### Solution (a) — Foil Construction (7 Marks)

A hot-stamp foil is a **multi-layer laminate** with five distinct functional layers. From top (die-side) to bottom (substrate-side):

**Layer 1 — Carrier Film (2 marks):**
- Material: **Biaxially-oriented polyethylene terephthalate (BOPET)**
- Thickness: $12\text{–}23\,\mu m$
- Function: Provides mechanical strength during unwinding, transport, and stripping. Must withstand stamping temperature without stretching.

**Layer 2 — Release Coat (2 marks):**
- Material: **Wax + silicone hybrid** (e.g., paraffin wax with low-MW silicone fluid)
- Thickness: $\sim 0.5\text{–}1.0\,\mu m$
- Function: Melts at stamping temperature to allow clean separation of the carrier film from the decorative layer.

**Layer 3 — Decorative Layer (1.5 marks):**
- **Metallic option:** Vacuum-deposited aluminum ($0.05\text{–}0.1\,\mu m$) — produces silver/gold/chrome finishes
- **Pigment option:** Pigmented resin (acrylic or vinyl) — for coloured patterns
- **Holographic option:** Micro-embossed BOPET for security/authentication foils

**Layer 4 — Adhesive Size Coat (1.5 marks):**
- Material: **Thermoplastic resin** (acrylic, vinyl, or polyurethane dispersion)
- Thickness: $\sim 3\text{–}5\,\mu m$
- Function: Softens at activation $T$, wets the substrate, and forms a permanent bond on cooling.

*(Optional Layer 5 — Protective Top Lacquer: scratch-resistant overcoat applied over decorative layer for abrasion resistance.)*

#### Solution (b) — Applications (7 Marks — List Any Four with Examples)

**1. Automotive Interior Trim (2 marks):**
Hot stamping is widely used to apply **wood-grain foils and chrome-effect accents** on dashboards, door handles, gear knobs, and AC vent surrounds. Typical substrates are **ABS, PP, and PP-EPDM** blends.

**2. Consumer Electronics (2 marks):**
Brand logos on **laptop lids, mobile phone housings, TV bezels, and remote-control buttons**. The high resolution ($0.1\,\text{mm}$ line) is essential for fine typography on small parts.

**3. Cosmetic Packaging (1.5 marks):**
**Metallic gold/silver finishes** on lipstick caps, perfume bottle collars, and compact powder cases. Substrates are typically **SAN, PMMA, or ABS** with high surface gloss.

**4. Security and Authentication (1.5 marks):**
**Holographic hot-stamp foils** on credit cards, currency, pharmaceutical packaging, and ID cards. The micro-embossed pattern is virtually impossible to reproduce by other means.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Hot Stamping Answers:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the release coat** — Many students describe only 4 layers, missing the release coat. KTU examiners deduct **1 full mark**.
>
> 2. **Confusing "die" with "mold"** — Hot stamping uses a *die* (cliché), not a *mold*. Writing "mold" costs $0.5$ mark.
>
> 3. **Stating "heat melts the foil"** — This is imprecise and wrong. Correct phrasing: *"heat softens the adhesive coat and melts the release coat"* — wording matters for full marks.
>
> 4. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — Always write "$^\circ C$", "$\text{MPa}$", "$\text{s}$". A numerically correct answer without units loses $0.5\text{–}1$ mark.
>
> 5. **In the Part B diagram, students often forget to label the five layers** — Every layer must be labelled to earn the **2 marks allocated for "neat sketch"**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Plastic Hot Stamping**

- ✅ Hot stamping is a **dry decoration process** — no solvents, no curing ovens
- ✅ Five-layer foil: **PET carrier → release coat → lacquer → decorative → adhesive**
- ✅ Three process variables must be balanced: **Heat ($90\text{–}160^\circ C$) + Pressure ($0.5\text{–}5\,\text{MPa}$) + Dwell ($0.5\text{–}3\,\text{s}$)**
- ✅ Activation mechanism: **release coat melts first**, then **adhesive coat softens and bonds**
- ✅ Common substrates: **ABS, PP, PS, PVC, PMMA, SAN, PC**
- ✅ Metallic finish comes from **vacuum-deposited aluminum** ($\sim 0.05\text{–}0.1\,\mu m$)
- ✅ Die materials: **brass (most common), magnesium (lightweight), silicone rubber (for irregular surfaces)**
- ✅ Calculation must check **Biot number** before applying lumped capacitance
- ✅ For thin foils, **heat transfer is the rate-limiting step**, not pressure
- ✅ Defects: **halo, peeling, burning, patchy transfer, dimensional distortion**
- ✅ Industrial advantage: **lower per-part cost than spray painting for volumes $> 10{,}000$ parts**
- ✅ Examiner's pet topics: **5-layer foil diagram, process parameter triangle, applications**

> **End of Module 4 — Plastic Hot Stamping Notes**  
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (NEP 2020) Syllabus*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Foils
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT864 - PLASTICS & DECORATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Foils

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of plastic decoration and packaging engineering, **Foils** refer to engineered multilayer thin-film laminates (typically **12 µm to 25 µm** total thickness) used for decorative surface finishing of plastics, paper, paperboard, leather, and textiles. They consist of a polymeric carrier film coated with successive functional layers — a release coat, a protective lacquer, a metallized vacuum-deposited layer (usually aluminium), and a thermoplastic adhesive (size) coat.

The two dominant industrial variants are:
- **Hot Stamping Foils (HSF)** — transferred thermally under heat and pressure using a metal die.
- **Cold Foils** — transferred at ambient temperature via UV-curable adhesive on a printing press.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Foils form the decorative backbone of cosmetics caps, automotive interiors, nameplates, credit cards, flexible food packaging, and security labels. The Board frequently tests layer-wise structure and the role of each functional layer.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **temporary tattoo** used by children:
1. A clear plastic sheet holds the design (carrier).
2. The design is "stuck" lightly to the sheet (release coat).
3. The actual colored/metallic image sits on top (lacquer + metal layer).
4. A layer of glue keeps it ready to transfer (adhesive).

When you press the tattoo onto your skin with a damp cloth, only the layers below the carrier transfer, leaving the picture behind. A **hot stamping foil** works identically — heat and pressure replace the damp cloth, and the substrate (plastic part) replaces your skin. The carrier film is then peeled away, leaving a brilliant metallic or pigmented image permanently bonded to the plastic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept Anchor:** Foil decoration is fundamentally a *controlled adhesion-transfer* process — adhesion between foil layers is engineered to release on demand at a specific temperature window (**typically 100 °C – 160 °C**).

## 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Carrier (PET) thickness | 12 µm – 23 µm |
| Total foil thickness | 18 µm – 30 µm |
| Aluminium deposit | 0.03 µm – 0.10 µm |
| Stamping temperature | 80 °C – 180 °C |
| Stamping pressure | 0.3 MPa – 0.8 MPa |
| Dwell time | 0.3 s – 1.5 s |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layer-Wise Architecture of a Hot Stamping Foil

The foil is engineered as a 5-layer sandwich. Each layer has a precise engineering function:

**Layer 1 — Carrier Film (Base)**
- Material: Biaxially Oriented Polyethylene Terephthalate (BOPET), typically **12 µm, 19 µm, or 23 µm**.
- Function: Provides dimensional stability, mechanical strength during unwinding, and acts as the disposable "backing" peeled off after transfer.

**Layer 2 — Release Coat**
- Material: Wax-based or silicone-modified polymer (e.g., microcrystalline wax + thermoset resin).
- Function: Engineered to *delaminate* cleanly under heat. The adhesion strength between release coat and lacquer is **deliberately weaker** than the bond between adhesive (Layer 5) and the substrate.

**Layer 3 — Protective / Colour Lacquer**
- Material: Thermoplastic acrylic or cellulosic resin, often pigmented (for pigment foils) or clear (for metallic foils).
- Function: Provides scratch resistance, chemical resistance, gloss, and acts as the optical substrate onto which aluminium is later vacuum-deposited.

**Layer 4 — Metallization (Aluminium Layer)**
- Process: Vacuum metallization (PVD) at pressures of **10⁻³ to 10⁻⁵ mbar**.
- Function: Creates the specular reflective metallic appearance. Thickness is sub-micron — usually **30 nm to 100 nm**.

**Layer 5 — Adhesive (Size Coat)**
- Material: Thermoplastic resin — typically EVA, polyamide, or polyurethane dispersion.
- Function: Activates at stamping temperature, wets the substrate, and forms a permanent bond upon cooling. Adhesion to substrate > adhesion of release coat → transfer occurs cleanly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adhesion Hierarchy Rule (Critical for Board Exams):**
> $$A_{\text{adhesive-substrate}} \;>\; A_{\text{lacquer-metal}} \;>\; A_{\text{release-lacquer}}$$
> where $A$ represents interfacial adhesion energy (J/m²). When this hierarchy holds, transfer is clean; if violated, defects like "foil lift-off" or "ghosting" appear.

## 2.2 Classification of Foils

| Foil Type | Decoration Source | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Metallic Foil** | Vacuum-deposited aluminium | Cosmetics, automotive trim, nameplates |
| **Pigment Foil** | Coloured lacquer (no metal) | Book covers, picture frames |
| **Holographic Foil** | Diffraction grating + aluminium | Security labels, bank cards |
| **Cold Foil** | Aluminium on UV-adhesive | Labels, folding cartons |
| **Scratch-off Foil** | Opaque over-printable lacquer | Lottery tickets, phone cards |
| **Signature Foil** | Coloured pigment, matte finish | Documents, certificates |

## 2.3 The Hot Stamping Process — Operational Logic

The process involves **three coupled physical phenomena** occurring simultaneously:

1. **Thermal Activation** — The heated metal die (typically brass or magnesium) raises the adhesive coat above its softening point (~**80 °C – 120 °C**).
2. **Pressure-Driven Conformance** — Die pressure forces the softened adhesive to wet the substrate's surface micro-topography.
3. **Interfacial Failure** — The release coat yields at its weakest cohesive plane, leaving the lacquer-metal-adhesive stack bonded to the substrate.

After dwell time, the carrier is stripped, and the cooled adhesive locks the decoration in place.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three parameters (Heat, Pressure, Time) matter:** These form the **"Stamping Window"** — a P-T-t triangular operating envelope. Deviating outside it causes:
> - *Insufficient heat* → poor adhesion, "skipping"
> - *Excess heat* → foil discoloration, substrate distortion
> - *Insufficient pressure* → incomplete transfer, edge voids
> - *Excess pressure* → die bounce, substrate marking
> - *Short dwell* → weak bond
> - *Long dwell* → thermal damage

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Relation | Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Total Foil Thickness | $t_{\text{total}} = t_{\text{carrier}} + t_{\text{rel}} + t_{\text{lac}} + t_{\text{Al}} + t_{\text{adh}}$ | µm | Sum of 5 layers |
| Aluminium Optical Density | $OD = -\log_{10}(T)$ where $T$ = transmittance | dimensionless | Typical OD = 2.0–2.8 for mirror finish |
