# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Drawing Convention:** Always use **straight lines, arrowheads, and labelled boxes**. Use **capital letters** for component labels (e.g., `A`, `B`, `C`) and explain them in a **legend** below the diagram.

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of a Vertical Press

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator / Loader] --> B[Charge Hopper / Preheater]
    B --> C[Bottom Fixed Platen - Heated]
    C --> D[Mold Lower Cavity - Receives Charge]
    D --> E[Hydraulic Pump Unit]
    E --> F[Hydraulic Cylinder - Generates Force]
    F --> G[Ram and Moving Top Platen]
    G --> H[Mold Upper Cavity - Closes on Charge]
    H --> I[Curing Zone - Heat and Pressure]
    I --> J[Breathing Operation - Gas Release]
    J --> K[Mold Opening Stroke]
    K --> L[Ejector Pins - Knockout]
    L --> M[Finished Cured Part]
    M --> N[Quality Check and Trimming]
    N --> O[Cycle Repeats]
    O --> A
```

## 4.2 Component-Level Architecture (For Labelled Diagram Questions)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Frame[Press Frame - Strain Rods and Bed]
        A1[Top Crown]
        A2[Bottom Bed]
        A3[Tie Bars - 4 Strain Rods]
    end
    A1 --- A3
    A3 --- A2

    subgraph Clamp[Clamping System]
        B1[Hydraulic Cylinder]
        B2[Piston and Ram]
        B3[Moving Platen]
    end
    B1 --> B2 --> B3

    subgraph MoldArea[Mold Assembly]
        C1[Upper Mold Half]
        C2[Lower Mold Half]
        C3[Cavity - Thermoset Charge]
        C4[Ejector Pins]
    end
    B3 --> C1
    A2 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C2 --> C4

    subgraph Heat[Heating and Control]
        D1[Steam or Electric Heaters]
        D2[Platen Temperature Controller]
        D3[Pressure Gauge]
        D4[Timer and PLC]
    end
    D1 --> C2
    D2 --> D1
    D3 --> B1
    D4 --> B1
```

## 4.3 Process Cycle Flow (KTU Flowchart Favourite)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Start Cycle] --> S2[Close Mold - Fast Approach]
    S2 --> S3[Squeeze - Low Speed High Pressure]
    S3 --> S4[Hold and Cure - 30 to 90 s per mm]
    S4 --> S5{Breathing Required?}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Open Slightly - Release Gases]
    S5 -- No --> S7[Full Mold Opening]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8[Eject Part - Knockout Pins]
    S8 --> S9[Trimming and Inspection]
    S9 --> S1
```

## 4.4 Up-Stroke vs Down-Stroke Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Down[Down-Stroke Vertical Press]
        D1[Fixed Top Platen] --> D2[Moving Platen Travels Down]
        D2 --> D3[Bottom Fixed Platen with Ejectors]
    end
    subgraph Up[Up-Stroke Vertical Press]
        U1[Top Fixed Platen] --> U2[Bottom Platen Travels Up]
        U2 --> U3[Hydraulic Cylinder Below Bed]
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** If the question says *"labelled diagram of a vertical press"* — draw the **down-stroke type with tie bars** and label at least **8 components**. The mark split is typically: 2 marks diagram + 3 marks labels + 2 marks working.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a vertical press. Mention any two advantages of vertical presses over horizontal presses in compression molding.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **vertical press** is a compression-molding machine in which the **clamping force, the ram movement, and the platen travel are aligned along a single vertical axis**.

**Two advantages over horizontal presses:**

1. **Easier manual loading** — the operator can place the charge at **waist height** in down-stroke presses, reducing fatigue.
2. **Better gravity-assisted mold closing** — the weight of the moving platen aids initial mold closure, requiring **less hydraulic energy**.
3. (Bonus) Compact footprint, suitable for shop-floor installation.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any six main components of a vertical compression-molding press.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark each):**

1. Frame / bed
2. Fixed and moving platens
3. Hydraulic cylinder and ram
4. Heating system
5. Ejector / knockout pins
6. Control panel (pressure, temperature, timer)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`
**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the **construction and working of a hydraulic vertical compression-molding press**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A melamine-formaldehyde disc of diameter **150 mm** and thickness **5 mm** is to be compression-molded. The resin requires a molding pressure of **20 MPa**, and the press is **60% efficient** mechanically. Calculate: **(i)** the required **clamping tonnage** and **(ii)** the **hydraulic ram diameter** if the line pressure is **120 bar**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Construction and Working (7 marks):**

**Construction** (3.5 marks):

- **Frame:** Four-tie-bar construction absorbs reaction loads.
- **Bottom fixed platen:** Mounted on the bed; carries the lower mold half; contains steam/electric heaters.
- **Top moving platen:** Carries the upper mold half; connected to the hydraulic ram.
- **Hydraulic system:** Pump → control valves → pressure gauge → cylinder.
- **Heaters:** Cartridge or cast-in heaters maintain 150 – 200 °C.
- **Ejectors:** Knockout pins beneath the lower platen.
- **Control panel:** PLC for pressure, temperature, time, stroke.

**Working** (3.5 marks):

1. Pre-heat the platens to the curing temperature.
2. Place the **charge** in the lower mold cavity.
3. The hydraulic ram drives the top platen **downward** in **fast-approach** mode.
4. Switch to **low-speed, high-pressure squeeze**; the material flows and fills the cavity.
5. Hold under pressure for the **cure time** — cross-linking occurs.
6. **Breathing** (if needed) to release volatiles.
7. Open the press; **eject** the part with knockout pins.
8. Trim flash and inspect.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**

$$d = 150~\text{mm}, \quad t = 5~\text{mm}, \quad P = 20~\text{MPa}, \quad \eta = 0.60, \quad p_{\text{hyd}} = 120~\text{bar}$$

**Step 1 — Projected area:**

$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (150)^2 = 17671.5~\text{mm}^2 = 176.7~\text{cm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical clamping force:**

$$F_{\text{theor}} = P \times A = 20 \times 17671.5 = 353430~\text{N} = 353.43~\text{kN}$$

**Step 3 — Accounting for mechanical efficiency (60%):**

$$F_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{theor}}}{\eta} = \dfrac{353.43}{0.60} = 589.05~\text{kN}$$

**Step 4 — Tonnage:**

$$F_{\text{T}} = \dfrac{589.05 \times 10^3}{9.81 \times 10^3}$$

$$\boxed{F_{\text{T}} \approx 60.05~\text{tonnes}}$$

So a **standard 75 T or 100 T press** would be selected.

**Step 5 — Hydraulic ram diameter:**

$$D^2 = \dfrac{4 F_{\text{actual}}}{\pi p_{\text{hyd}}}$$

Convert: $p_{\text{hyd}} = 120 \times 10^5 = 1.2 \times 10^7~\text{N/m}^2$ and $F_{\text{actual}} = 5.8905 \times 10^5~\text{N}$.

$$D^2 = \dfrac{4 \times 5.8905 \times 10^5}{\pi \times 1.2 \times 10^7}$$

$$D^2 = \dfrac{2.3562 \times 10^6}{3.7699 \times 10^7} = 0.06251~\text{m}^2 = 62510~\text{mm}^2$$

$$D = \sqrt{62510}$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 250~\text{mm}}$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Alternative Choice]`
**(a)** Compare **up-stroke and down-stroke vertical presses**. State **two examples** of parts best suited for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A phenolic molding powder with **bulk factor 2.8** and **cured density 1.4 g/cm³** is to be compression-molded into a rectangular plate of **200 mm × 150 mm × 10 mm**. Calculate: **(i)** the **mass of charge** required, and **(ii)** the **cure time** if the cure constant is **50 s/mm**. The press platen temperature is **170 °C**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison (7 marks):**

| Feature | Up-Stroke Press | Down-Stroke Press |
|---|---|---|
| Moving platen | **Bottom** moves up | **Top** moves down |
| Hydraulic cylinder | Located **below the bed** | Located in the **crown** above |
| Loading height | Fixed top — loading at **floor level** | Fixed bottom — loading at **waist height** |
| Operator comfort | Lower (bending required) | Higher (ergonomic) |
| Part ejection | Part drops by **gravity** from upper half | Ejected by **knockout pins** from lower half |
| Maintenance | Cylinder access harder | Cylinder easily accessible |
| Suitability | **Large flat parts** (decorative laminates) | **Small-to-medium intricate parts** (switchgear, dinnerware) |
| Floor space | Taller, narrower | Wider, more compact |

**Examples (2 marks):**

- **Up-stroke:** Decorative laminate sheets, large rubber mats.
- **Down-stroke:** Phenolic switchgear parts, melamine plates, electrical housings.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Part volume:**

$$V = 200 \times 150 \times 10 = 300000~\text{mm}^3 = 300~\text{cm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Mass of cured part:**

$$m_{\text{cured}} = \rho \times V = 1.4 \times 300 = 420~\text{g}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of charge (using bulk factor):**

The **mass** of the charge equals the **mass of the cured part** (conservation of mass). The **volume** of the charge is enlarged by BF:

$$V_{\text{charge}} = V \times \text{BF} = 300 \times 2.8 = 840~\text{cm}^3$$

$$\boxed{m_{\text{charge}} = 420~\text{g (loose powder volume = 840 cm}^3\text{)}}$$

**Step 4 — Cure time:**

$$t_{\text{cycle}} = k \times h = 50 \times 10 = 500~\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{t_{\text{cycle}} = 500~\text{s} \approx 8.33~\text{minutes}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Platen temperature 170 °C** is within the standard phenolic range (150 – 200 °C) — no correction needed. Mention this explicitly in the answer to score the **1-mark "comment" credit**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on Vertical Presses questions:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Forgetting to convert $\text{cm}^2 \rightarrow \text{m}^2$ before using $F = P \times A$. → **Lose 2 marks**.
> 2. **Not selecting a standard press:** Writing "38.2 T" without saying "**select 50 T standard press**" → Examiner deducts **1 mark** for "industrial non-feasibility".
> 3. **Ignoring mechanical efficiency ($\eta$):** KTU often hides $\eta$ in the problem statement. Skipping it gives an **under-rated press** → **Lose 2 marks**.
> 4. **Confusing projected area with surface area:** Use the **shadow area** of the part on the parting line, not total surface area. → **Lose 1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Forgetting the breathing step** in the working description → Lose **½ mark** in 14-mark answers.
> 6. **Calling the moving platen "the press head"** — KTU expects the term **"moving platen / ram platen"**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this the night before the exam):**

- ✅ Vertical press = clamping force, ram, and platen travel all on **one vertical axis**.
- ✅ **Main types:** Hydraulic (most common), Mechanical (toggle), Hydro-mechanical.
- ✅ **Two sub-classes:** Up-stroke (bottom platen moves up) and Down-stroke (top platen moves down).
- ✅ **Key components:** Frame, tie bars, fixed platen, moving platen, hydraulic ram, heaters, ejector pins, control panel.
- ✅ **Cycle:** Load → Close (fast) → Squeeze (slow) → Cure → (Breathe) → Open → Eject → Trim.
- ✅ **Master formulas:**
  - $F = P \times A$
  - $F_{\text{tonne}} = F_{\text{N}} / 9810$
  - $D = \sqrt{4F / (\pi p_{\text{hyd}})}$
  - $V_{\text{charge}} = V_{\text{part}} \times \text{BF}$
  - $t_{\text{cycle}} = k \times h$
- ✅ **Standard molding pressures:** Phenolic 10 – 25 MPa, UF 15 – 30 MPa, MF 15 – 25 MPa.
- ✅ **Always include mechanical efficiency $\eta$** in tonnage calculations.
- ✅ **Always select the next standard press size** (50, 75, 100, 150, 200, 300, 500 T).
- ✅ **Heater temperature range:** 150 – 200 °C for most thermosets; melamine up to 180 °C.
- ✅ **Breathing** is essential for UF, MF, and polyester — not for phenolic.
- ✅ **Bulk factor (BF)** = loose powder volume / finished part volume. Typical BF = 2 – 4.
- ✅ **Applications in decoration industry:** Decorative laminates, melamine dinnerware, PU artificial leather, vinyl flooring tiles — all produced on **vertical compression presses**.
- ✅ Examiner's favourite question formats:
  - 3-mark: *Define + list components / advantages.*
  - 7-mark: *Labelled diagram + working.*
  - 7-mark: *Numerical on tonnage, ram diameter, or charge weight.*
- ✅ **Always write units in every line of the numerical** — partial-mark safeguard.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Contact Machines
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT864 - PLASTICS & DECORATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Linear Contact Machines

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Linear Contact Machines** are a class of plastics decorating and converting equipment in which the decorating medium (printing plate, ink reservoir, coating die, or applicator roller) makes direct, line-of-contact engagement with the plastic substrate as it travels in a continuous, straight-line or tangential path through the machine. The contact line is generated either by the nip between two counter-rotating cylinders, by a roller pressing against a moving web, or by a stationary applicator (blade, die, or screen) contacting a substrate moving linearly beneath it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Linear contact machines are characterized by the formation of a *line contact* (as opposed to point contact in digital/inkjet systems or non-contact in spray systems) between the metering/applicator element and the plastic web. This line contact governs the uniformity of ink film laydown, the achievable print resolution, and the machine's operating speed envelope.

The five principal categories of linear contact machines used in plastics decoration are:

| S. No. | Machine Category | Contact Geometry |
|:------:|:-----------------|:-----------------|
| 1 | **Rotogravure Printing Press** | Engraved cylinder $\leftrightarrow$ impression roller nip |
| 2 | **Flexographic Printing Press** | Relief plate cylinder $\leftrightarrow$ anilox $\leftrightarrow$ substrate |
| 3 | **Offset (Lithographic) Press** | Plate cylinder $\leftrightarrow$ blanket cylinder $\leftrightarrow$ substrate |
| 4 | **Screen Printing Press** | Squeegee presses ink through mesh onto substrate |
| 5 | **Roller/Blade Coating Machines** | Applicator roller or blade $\leftrightarrow$ substrate web |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Rolling Paint Brush"**
>
> Imagine you are decorating a long plastic sheet by hand. The most natural, uniform way to apply ink is to roll a paint-laden rubber roller along the sheet in a straight line. The line where the roller touches the sheet — that thin stripe of contact — is the *linear contact*. A **Linear Contact Machine** automates this hand-rolling action: cylinders replace the roller, the plastic web replaces your hand-stretched sheet, and the ink is metered by engraved cells or relief patterns instead of brush hairs.
>
> The longer and more uniform the contact line, the more even the ink layer. The pressure at this contact line (called the *nip pressure*) determines how thick the ink film gets and whether fine detail is preserved.

## 1.3 Governing Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following engineering metrics are universally used to characterize linear contact machines for plastics:

- **Nip Pressure (P):** Typically **50 – 500 N/cm** of web width, depending on application.
- **Web Tension (T):** Maintained at **1 – 5 N/cm** for films, **5 – 20 N/cm** for thicker sheets.
- **Machine Speed (v):** Ranges from **5 m/min** (screen) to **600 m/min** (rotogravue).
- **Ink Film Thickness (t):** Usually **1 – 8 µm** for printing, **10 – 100 µm** for coating.
- **Standard Process Temperature:** **20 – 60 °C** for printing, **80 – 250 °C** for hot-melt coating.
- **Anilox Line Screen:** **80 – 800 lines per inch (lpi)** for flexo.
- **Engraving Screen:** **40 – 200 lines per inch (lpi)** for gravure cells.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Linear" and not "Point"?** A point-contact system (like inkjet) deposits ink droplets one pixel at a time. A linear-contact system deposits ink along an entire line simultaneously, which is **100× to 10 000× faster** but requires the substrate and applicator to be in perfect mechanical synchronization — hence the elaborate tension-control, registration, and roller-concentricity engineering that defines this class of machines.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of the Linear Contact Principle

The operational theory of every linear contact machine reduces to **three fundamental physical processes** acting simultaneously at the contact line:

1. **Ink (or coating) metering** — the volumetric delivery of fluid to the contact line.
2. **Ink splitting (film splitting)** — the division of the ink film between the applicator and the substrate as they separate.
3. **Ink transfer and setting** — the absorption, oxidation, evaporation, or curing of the ink on the plastic surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — "The Three-Zone Model"**
> At the linear contact region, engineers visualize three zones:
> - **Zone A (Inlet):** Ink is squeezed into the nip.
> - **Zone B (Contact):** Maximum pressure; ink flows laterally and fills substrate texture.
> - **Zone C (Exit):** Ink film splits between applicator and substrate.
>
> The split ratio in Zone C determines the final ink film thickness on the substrate.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Logic

**Step 1 — Web Transport and Tensioning**
The plastic web (film, sheet, or foil) is unwound from a feed roll, threaded through idler rollers, and held under controlled tension by a **dancer roll** or **load cell** system. Stable tension is a prerequisite for accurate registration.

**Step 2 — Ink Metering**
The ink is metered onto a transfer element (anilox roller, engraved cell, or ink tray) at a precisely controlled volume. For gravure, the cell volume fixes the ink quantity; for flexo, the anilox cell volume does the same; for offset, the dampening solution controls the non-image area.

**Step 3 — Linear Contact Establishment**
The metered ink-bearing element (cylinder, roller, or screen) is brought into line contact with the substrate. The width of this contact line is governed by the **Hertzian contact mechanics** of the two cylinders.

**Step 4 — Ink Splitting and Transfer**
As the cylinders separate, the ink film splits. The classic **Bierwagen–Mannheimer split model** (1970) gives the fraction transferred to the substrate.

**Step 5 — Drying / Curing**
The wet ink film is dried by hot air, IR radiation, or UV lamps before the web reaches the next printing unit or the rewind station.

**Step 6 — Web Winding**
The decorated web is wound onto a take-up roll under constant torque or taper tension to prevent telescoping or stretching.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value / Unit | Governing Use |
|:-:|:--------|:---------------|:---------------------|:--------------|
| 1 | $v = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$ | $v$ = web speed, $D$ = cylinder diameter, $N$ = rotational speed | $D$ in m, $N$ in rev/s | Synchronizing all cylinder speeds |
| 2 | $h = \dfrac{V_c}{A_c}$ | $h$ = ink film thickness, $V_c$ = cell volume, $A_c$ = cell footprint area | $h$ in µm | Gravure cell ink delivery |
| 3 | $\phi = \dfrac{V_s}{V_t} = \dfrac{1}{1 + k \cdot (\eta_s / \eta_a)^{0.5}}$ | $\phi$ = fraction transferred, $\eta_s, \eta_a$ = viscosities, $k$ = constant | $\phi$ = 0.3 – 0.7 | Ink-splitting model |
| 4 | $P_{nip} = \dfrac{F}{L \cdot w}$ | $P_{nip}$ = nip pressure, $F$ = loading force, $L$ = roller length, $w$ = web width | $P$ in N/mm² | Setting roller loading |
| 5 | $R_z = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert z_i - z_{mean} \vert$ | $R_z$ = surface roughness, $z_i$ = peak heights | µm | Surface energy correlation |
| 6 | $F_{adh} = 2 \pi R \gamma_{LV} (1 + \cos\theta)$ | $F_{adh}$ = adhesion force, $\gamma_{LV}$ = surface tension, $\theta$ = contact angle | mN | Substrate wetting criterion |
| 7 | $Q = v \cdot w \cdot t$ | $Q$ = volumetric ink flow, $t$ = wet film thickness | cm³/s | Anilox/engraving capacity check |
| 8 | $\sigma = \dfrac{T}{A}$ | $\sigma$ = web tensile stress, $T$ = tension, $A$ = cross-section | N/mm² | Web strain calculation |
| 9 | $\epsilon = \dfrac{\sigma}{E}$ | $\epsilon$ = strain, $E$ = Young's modulus of plastic | dimensionless | Dimensional stability |
| 10 | $\Delta t_{dry} = \dfrac{h^2 \cdot \rho}{D_v \cdot C_s}$ | $\Delta t_{dry}$ = drying time, $\rho$ = density, $D_v$ = vapor diffusivity, $C_s$ = saturation concentration | seconds | Dryer length design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Discipline:** All length units in formulas are to be kept in **metres (m)**, all force units in **newtons (N)**, and all time units in **seconds (s)** before final calculation. KTU examiners deduct marks for unit-mismatch errors in numerical problems.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Linear contact machines are the **workhorses of the global plastics converting industry** because they satisfy the three industrial demands simultaneously:

- **High Productivity** — web speeds of 300 m/min are routine, producing >1 million m² of decorated film per shift.
- **High Repeatability** — once set, an engraved cylinder reproduces the same image 10⁶ times without variation.
- **Versatility** — the same machine architecture accepts different inks (water-based, solvent, UV, electron-beam) and substrates (PE, PP, PET, PVC, BOPP, metallized films).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Real-World Hook:** When you see a flexible snack-food packet, a shampoo sachet, a credit card, or the dashboard of a car — the printing on the plastic was almost certainly laid down by a linear contact machine. Exam questions on "industrial applications" earn easy marks if you can name at least one such end product per machine type.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Engraved Cell Ink Volume and Wet Film Thickness (Gravure)

This is a **favourite 7-mark KTU derivation** because it links cylinder geometry to the final printed film thickness.

### Given

A gravure cylinder has cells engraved in a regular pattern with the following parameters:

- Cell depth: $d$ (typically 5 – 50 µm)
- Cell opening width: $b$ (typically 50 – 200 µm)
- Cell wall length: $l$ (typically 100 – 400 µm)
- Screen ruling: $S$ (lines per cm)
- Cell shape: pyramidal or quadrangular pyramid

### Step 1 — Express the Cell Footprint Area

For a pyramidal cell of opening $b \times l$, the plan (footprint) area is:

$$
A_c = b \cdot l
$$

**Logic:** The footprint is the area of the cell opening projected onto the substrate. This is the area that "prints" the dot of ink.

### Step 2 — Express the Cell Volume

For a pyramidal cell, the volume is one-third of the base area times the depth:

$$
V_c = \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot A_c \cdot d = \dfrac{b \cdot l \cdot d}{3}
$$

**Logic:** A pyramid's volume equals 1/3 of base × height. The constant "1/3" arises from the linear taper of the pyramid walls.

### Step 3 — Relate to Screen Ruling

If the screen ruling is $S$ lines per cm (with cells at $S$ lines/cm in both machine and cross-machine directions), then one cell occupies an area of:

$$
A_{unit} = \left( \dfrac{1}{S} \right)^2 = \dfrac{1}{S^2}
$$

**Logic:** Each cell center is spaced $1/S$ cm from the next, so the unit cell area on the cylinder surface is $(1/S)^2$.

### Step 4 — Compute the Effective Ink Film Thickness on the Substrate

When the cell wipes onto the substrate, it spreads over the footprint. The wet film thickness $h$ of the printed dot is the cell volume divided by its footprint area:

$$
h = \dfrac{V_c}{A_c} = \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot d
$$

**Logic:** This is independent of $b$ and $l$ — only the cell depth matters. A 30 µm deep cell gives a 10 µm wet film.

### Step 5 — Introduce the Cell Coverage (Wall-to-Wall) Factor

In practice, only a fraction $\alpha$ of the cylinder surface is engraved. The **average** ink film thickness across the printed area is:

$$
\bar{h} = \alpha \cdot h = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot d}{3}
$$

Typical $\alpha = 0.4$ to $0.7$ for halftone gravure.

### Step 6 — Compute the Ink Volume per Unit Web Area

For a web of width $w$ and length $L$, the total ink volume deposited is:

$$
Q_{ink} = w \cdot L \cdot \bar{h} = \dfrac{w \cdot L \cdot \alpha \cdot d}{3}
$$

### Step 7 — Dimensional Check

$[\bar{h}] = \mu m$ ✓ ; $[Q_{ink}] = m^3$ ✓. The formula is dimensionally consistent.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Frequently-Asked Sub-Question:** "Why does gravure use an electrostatic assist (ESA)?" Answer: Because the wet film thickness $h = d/3$ is small, the ink volume is too low to fully cover the plastic at high speed. ESA (a 30 kV DC field at the nip) pulls the ink out of the cell, raising the transfer fraction $\phi$ by 15 – 30 %.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Nip Pressure Between Two Cylinders (Hertzian Contact)

### Given

Two steel cylinders of radii $R_1$ and $R_2$, lengths $L$, loaded with force $F$.

### Step 1 — Define Equivalent Radius

The two cylinders are replaced by a single equivalent cylinder against a flat:

$$
\dfrac{1}{R_{eq}} = \dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2}
$$

**Logic:** Curvatures add for two cylinders pressed together externally (positive curvature convention).

### Step 2 — Hertzian Half-Width of Contact

For two elastic cylinders, the half-width $b$ of the line contact is:

$$
b = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot F \cdot R_{eq}}{\pi \cdot L \cdot E^*}}
$$

where $E^*$ is the combined elastic modulus:

$$
\dfrac{1}{E^*} = \dfrac{1 - \nu_1^2}{E_1} + \dfrac{1 - \nu_2^2}{E_2}
$$

**Logic:** The contact patch is a thin rectangle of width $2b$ along the cylinder axis. As $F$ rises, $b$ grows as $\sqrt{F}$.

### Step 3 — Maximum Contact Pressure

The peak pressure occurs on the centerline of the contact:

$$
P_{max} = \dfrac{2 F}{\pi \cdot b \cdot L}
$$

Substituting the expression for $b$:

$$
P_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{F \cdot E^*}{\pi \cdot L \cdot R_{eq}}}
$$

**Logic:** Pressure scales as $\sqrt{F/R_{eq}}$ — doubling the load increases pressure by only $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$.

### Step 4 — Numerical Example (KTU-style)

- $R_1 = 0.15$ m (gravure cylinder), $R_2 = 0.10$ m (impression roller), $L = 1.0$ m, $F = 3000$ N, $E_1 = E_2 = 2.1 \times 10^{11}$ N/m² (steel), $\nu_1 = \nu_2 = 0.30$.

**Equivalent radius:**

$$
R_{eq} = \dfrac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \dfrac{0.15 \times 0.10}{0.15 + 0.10} = \dfrac{0.015}{0.25} = 0.060 \text{ m}
$$

**Combined modulus:**

$$
E^* = \dfrac{E}{2(1 - \nu^2)} = \dfrac{2.1 \times 10^{11}}{2(1 - 0.09)} = \dfrac{2.1 \times 10^{11}}{1.82} = 1.154 \times 10^{11} \text{ N/m}^2
$$

**Half-width:**

$$
b = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 3000 \times 0.060}{\pi \times 1.0 \times 1.154 \times 10^{11}}} = \sqrt{1.99 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 1.41 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.41 \text{ mm}
$$

**Maximum pressure:**

$$
P_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3000 \times 1.154 \times 10^{11}}{\pi \times 1.0 \times 0.060}} = \sqrt{1.836 \times 10^{15}} \approx 4.29 \times 10^{7} \text{ N/m}^2 \approx 42.9 \text{ MPa}
$$

This is the typical nip pressure for a 3000 N load on a 1 m wide web.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ink Splitting Fraction (Bierwagen Model)

### Given

The wet ink film between the applicator and substrate has a viscosity $\eta$ and a velocity $v$. On separation, it splits into two films — one on the applicator (thickness $h_a$) and one on the substrate (thickness $h_s$).

### Step 1 — Conservation of Volume

$$
h = h_a + h_s
$$

where $h$ is the original wet film thickness at the nip.

### Step 2 — Define the Split Fraction

$$
\phi = \dfrac{h_s}{h} = \dfrac{h_s}{h_a + h_s}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Mannheimer–Bierwagen Balance

The fraction depends on the relative viscosities of the ink against the two surfaces:

$$
\phi = \dfrac{1}{1 + (\eta_a / \eta_s)^n}
$$

where $\eta_a$ and $\eta_s$ are the effective viscosities of the ink as it wets the applicator and substrate respectively, and $n \approx 0.5$ experimentally.

**Logic:** If the ink wets the substrate more strongly (lower $\eta_s$ relative to $\eta_a$), the split favours the substrate ($\phi \to 1$).

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example

- $h = 12$ µm, $\eta_a = 0.15$ Pa·s, $\eta_s = 0.05$ Pa·s, $n = 0.5$.

$$
\phi = \dfrac{1}{1 + (0.15/0.05)^{0.5}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 1.732} = \dfrac{1}{2.732} \approx 0.366
$$

$$
h_s = 0.366 \times 12 = 4.39 \text{ µm on substrate}
$$

$$
h_a = 12 - 4.39 = 7.61 \text{ µm retained on applicator}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters:** Only 36.6 % of the ink reaches the plastic — the rest stays on the roller and must be wiped/scraped. The split fraction is the single most important variable controlling ink consumption cost.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Machine Comparison

| Parameter | Gravure | Flexo | Offset | Screen | Roller Coat |
|:----------|:--------|:------|:-------|:-------|:------------|
| Typical speed (m/min) | 100 – 600 | 50 – 400 | 30 – 200 | 5 – 30 | 20 – 300 |
| Ink film (µm) | 1 – 8 | 1.5 – 6 | 1 – 3 | 10 – 100 | 20 – 200 |
| Print resolution (lpi) | 150 – 200 | 120 – 200 | 150 – 250 | 60 – 100 | N/A |
| Substrate compatibility | All films | All films + paper | Paper + films | All + rigid | All films + sheets |
| Cylinder/plate cost | Very high | Low | Medium | Low | N/A |
| Set-up time | Long | Short | Medium | Medium | Short |
| Ink system | Solvent / water | Solvent / water / UV | Oxidising + dampening | Plastisol / UV / solvent | All |
| Best suited run length | > 500 000 m | 1000 – 500 000 m | < 50 000 m | < 5000 m | All |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Linear Contact Printing Line

> [!NOTE]
> The schematic below is rendered as a **functional block flow** because the physical layout of a multi-station printing line is too complex for a single Mermaid graph. The blocks represent the canonical stations in the order they appear along the web path.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Unwind Stand] --> A2[Web Cleaner Corona Treater]
    A2 --> A3[Edge Guide and Tension Control]
    A3 --> B1[Station 1 Ink Pan and Anilox]
    B1 --> B2[Station 1 Plate Cylinder]
    B2 --> B3[Station 1 Impression Cylinder]
    B3 --> C1[Inter Station Dryer 1]
    C1 --> B4[Station 2 Ink Pan and Anilox]
    B4 --> B5[Station 2 Plate Cylinder]
    B5 --> B6[Station 2 Impression Cylinder]
    B6 --> C2[Inter Station Dryer 2]
    C2 --> D1[Final Tunnel Dryer]
    D1 --> D2[Cooling Drum]
    D2 --> E1[Web Inspection Vision System]
    E1 --> E2[Slitter and Rewind]
    E2 --> F1[Finished Decorated Roll]
    G1[Servo Drive and Registration Controller] -. controls .-> B1
    G1 -. controls .-> B4
    G2[Central Ink Viscosity Controller] -. meters .-> B1
    G2 -. meters .-> B4
    G3[Operator HMI Panel] -. commands .-> G1
    G3 -. commands .-> G2
```

**Block Definitions:**

- **Unwind Stand:** Holds the blank plastic film roll and feeds the web.
- **Corona Treater:** Increases surface energy of the plastic (typically to 38 – 42 dyne/cm) for ink adhesion.
- **Tension Control:** Maintains web tension within $\pm 2$ % of setpoint.
- **Ink Pan / Anilox:** Meters the exact volume of ink to the plate cylinder.
- **Plate Cylinder:** Carries the raised image (flexo) or the planographic image (offset).
- **Impression Cylinder:** Provides the counter-pressure to form the linear contact nip.
- **Inter-Station Dryer:** Partially dries the ink before the next colour is applied (prevents re-wetting).
- **Final Tunnel Dryer:** Completes drying/curing of the full ink film.
- **Slitter / Rewind:** Cuts the wide web into narrower rolls and winds the finished product.

## 4.2 Detailed Mermaid — Inside a Single Flexo Printing Station

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Ink Pan Reservoir] --> S2[Anilox Roller Engraved Cells]
    S2 --> S3[Doctor Blade Scraper]
    S3 --> S4[Metered Ink Film on Anilox]
    S4 --> S5[Plate Cylinder with Relief Image]
    S5 --> S6[Impression Cylinder]
    S6 --> S7[Plastic Web Substrate]
    S7 --> S8[Ink Transferred at Linear Contact]
    S8 --> S9[Exit Nip and Splitting Zone]
    S9 --> S10[Web to Next Station or Dryer]
    M1[Motor Drive M1] -. rotates .-> S2
    M2[Motor Drive M2] -. rotates .-> S5
    M3[Pneumatic Loading M3] -. loads .-> S6
    C1[Registration Encoder] -. feedback .-> M1
    C1 -. feedback .-> M2
```

## 4.3 Gravure Printing Station — Functional Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1[Ink Fountain Trough] --> G2[Gravure Cylinder with Engraved Cells]
    G2 --> G3[Doctor Blade Assembly]
    G3 --> G4[Clean Cylinder Surface Cells Filled with Ink]
    G4 --> G5[Impression Roller Counter Rotating]
    G5 --> G6[Plastic Web Passing Through Nip]
    G6 --> G7[Ink Transfer at Line Contact]
    G7 --> G8[Optional Electrostatic Assist Bar]
    G8 --> G9[Dried and Decorated Web]
    E1[Cylinder Engraving Pattern] -. defines .-> G2
    T1[Doctor Blade Angle and Pressure Controller] -. regulates .-> G3
```

## 4.4 Roller Coating Station — Functional Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R1[Coating Pan with Resin or Lacquer] --> R2[Picking Up Roller]
    R2 --> R3[Metering Roller Reverse Rotation]
    R3 --> R4[Doctor Roller or Blade]
    R4 --> R5[Applicator Roller Final Film]
    R5 --> R6[Backing Roller or Rubber Counter Roller]
    R6 --> R7[Plastic Web Sandwiched in Nip]
    R7 --> R8[Linear Contact Coating Application]
    R8 --> R9[Coated Web to Oven for Curing]
    SP1[Speed Ratio Between R2 R3 R4 R5] -. controls .-> R3
    SP2[Roller Gap Setting] -. controls .-> R4
    SP3[Nip Pressure] -. controls .-> R6
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagrams in the KTU Exam:** When a question says "sketch a linear contact machine," draw the two cylinders in contact (with arrows for rotation), label the nip, indicate the ink feed (anilox, fountain, or pan) and the web direction. A clean schematic with all four labels — *nip, web, ink feed, impression* — fetches full marks even without photographic realism.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

### Question A1
**"Distinguish between contact and non-contact printing methods used in plastic decoration. Give one example of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Contact (Linear) Printing | Non-Contact Printing |
|:--------|:--------------------------|:---------------------|
| Ink transfer mechanism | Physical contact at a line or zone | Droplet ejection (no contact) |
| Examples | Rotogravure, Flexo, Offset | Inkjet, Spray coating |
| Speed | 100 – 600 m/min (high) | 10 – 100 m/min (moderate) |
| Resolution | Medium–High | Very High (no plate) |
| Substrate flexibility | Wide range | Best for irregular surfaces |
| Set-up cost | High (cylinders) | Low (no plate) |

*Conclusion:* Linear contact methods dominate long-run, high-speed production; non-contact methods dominate short-run, variable-data printing.

---

### Question A2
**"What is the role of an anilox roller in a flexographic printing machine? State two of its key parameters."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The anilox roller is the **metering cylinder** in a flexo press. It receives ink from the ink pan, and its engraved cells hold a precise volume of ink. As the doctor blade scrapes the surface clean, only the ink inside the cells remains; this is then deposited on the raised image of the plate cylinder. The anilox therefore **controls the ink film thickness** delivered to the substrate.

Two key parameters:

1. **Line screen (lpi):** Number of cells per inch — typically 80 – 800 lpi. Higher lpi = finer ink film.
2. **Cell volume (BCM = billion cubic microns per square inch):** Volumetric capacity — typically 1.0 – 12.0 BCM.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style — Module 4 Internal Choice Pattern]**

### Question B-A (14 Marks)
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **rotogravure printing machine** for plastic films. Label all major components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gravure cylinder has cells with opening $0.20 \text{ mm} \times 0.30 \text{ mm}$ and depth $24 \text{ \mu m}$. The screen ruling is 80 lines/cm. Calculate: (i) the volume of one cell, (ii) the wet film thickness deposited on the substrate, and (iii) the total ink volume required to print a 1000 m long, 0.5 m wide web, assuming 60 % cell coverage. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

A rotogravure press consists of the following stations in series:

1. **Unwind unit** that feeds the plastic film under controlled tension. (1 mark)
2. **In-feed rollers and registration control** that align the web. (0.5 mark)
3. **Printing unit:** the **gravure cylinder** (chrome-plated copper sleeve engraved with millions of tiny cells) rotates in an **ink fountain**; a flexible **doctor blade** scrapes the cylinder surface leaving ink only in the cells; the cylinder contacts the **impression roller** forming a *linear contact nip* with the web; the ink transfers to the web. (2.5 marks)
4. **Dryer** (hot air or IR) that evaporates the solvent from the wet ink film. (0.5 mark)
5. **Cooling drum and out-feed rollers** that lower the web temperature. (0.5 mark)
6. **Rewind unit** that winds the printed web. (0.5 mark)
7. **Registration and tension controllers** that maintain print-to-print alignment. (1 mark — for diagram label completeness)

**Key feature:** A multi-colour press repeats stations 3 and 4 in series (typically 4 – 10 stations).

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**

- $b = 0.20$ mm, $l = 0.30$ mm, $d = 24$ µm = 0.024 mm, $S = 80$ lines/cm, web: 1000 m × 0.5 m, coverage $\alpha = 0.6$.

**(i) Volume of one cell (pyramidal):** (2 marks)

$$
V_c = \dfrac{b \cdot l \cdot d}{3} = \dfrac{0.20 \times 0.30 \times 0.024}{3} = \dfrac{0.00144}{3} = 4.8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mm}^3
$$

**(ii) Wet film thickness on substrate:** (2 marks)

$$
h = \dfrac{V_c}{b \cdot l} = \dfrac{d}{3} = \dfrac{24}{3} = 8 \text{ µm}
$$

**(iii) Total ink volume:** (3 marks)

**Step 1 — Average ink film thickness:**

$$
\bar{h} = \alpha \cdot h = 0.6 \times 8 = 4.8 \text{ µm} = 4.8 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Web area:**

$$
A_{web} = 1000 \times 0.5 = 500 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Ink volume:**

$$
Q_{ink} = A_{web} \times \bar{h} = 500 \times 4.8 \times 10^{-6} = 2.4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 = 2.4 \text{ litres}
$$

**Final Answer:** (i) $V_c = 4.8 \times 10^{-4}$ mm³; (ii) $h = 8$ µm; (iii) $Q_{ink} = 2.4$ litres.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE
**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **flexographic printing press**. Discuss the role of the anilox roller and the doctor blade. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two steel cylinders of radii 0.20 m and 0.15 m, each 0.80 m long, are pressed together with a load of 2400 N. Take $E = 2.1 \times 10^{11}$ N/m² and $\nu = 0.30$. Calculate: (i) the equivalent radius, (ii) the half-width of the line contact, and (iii) the maximum contact pressure. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

A flexographic press is a **rotary relief printing** machine. Its key stations:

1. **Ink fountain / chambered doctor blade:** A closed ink reservoir with a doctor blade controls the ink film on the anilox. (1 mark)
2. **Anilox roller:** A chrome cylinder laser- or mechanically engraved with millions of fine cells (typically 80 – 800 lpi). It rotates partially immersed in the ink and meters a precise volume. (1.5 marks)
3. **Doctor blade:** Strips excess ink from the anilox surface, leaving ink only in the cells. (1 mark)
4. **Plate cylinder:** Carries a flexible photopolymer relief plate (raised image). The raised areas lift the ink off the anilox. (1.5 marks)
5. **Impression cylinder:** Presses the substrate against the plate cylinder to form the *linear contact nip* and transfer the ink. (1 mark)
6. **Dryer, cooling, and rewind:** Standard finishing. (0.5 mark)
7. **Registration and tension control:** Ensures colour-to-colour alignment. (0.5 mark)

**Role of the anilox:** It is the *ink-metering heart* of the press; without it, the ink film would be uncontrolled and printing would be non-uniform.

**Role of the doctor blade:** It is the *ink-volume regulator*; by adjusting its angle (typically 30° – 55°) and pressure (typically 2 – 5 N/cm), the operator controls how much ink stays in the cells.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $R_1 = 0.20$ m, $R_2 = 0.15$ m, $L = 0.80$ m, $F = 2400$ N, $E = 2.1 \times 10^{11}$ N/m², $\nu = 0.30$.

**(i) Equivalent radius:** (2 marks)

$$
R_{eq} = \dfrac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \dfrac{0.20 \times 0.15}{0.20 + 0.15} = \dfrac{0.030}{0.35} \approx 0.0857 \text{ m}
$$

**(ii) Half-width of line contact:** (2.5 marks)

**Combined elastic modulus:**

$$
E^* = \dfrac{E}{2(1 - \nu^2)} = \dfrac{2.1 \times 10^{11}}{2(1 - 0.09)} = \dfrac{2.1 \times 10^{11}}{1.82} \approx 1.154 \times 10^{11} \text{ N/m}^2
$$

**Half-width:**

$$
b = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 F R_{eq}}{\pi L E^*}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 2400 \times 0.0857}{\pi \times 0.80 \times 1.154 \times 10^{11}}}
$$

$$
b = \sqrt{\dfrac{822.86}{2.9 \times 10^{11}}} = \sqrt{2.838 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 5.33 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 53.3 \text{ µm}
$$

**(iii) Maximum contact pressure:** (2.5 marks)

$$
P_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{F E^*}{\pi L R_{eq}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2400 \times 1.154 \times 10^{11}}{\pi \times 0.80 \times 0.0857}}
$$

$$
P_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2.77 \times 10^{14}}{0.2153}} = \sqrt{1.286 \times 10^{15}} \approx 3.59 \times 10^{7} \text{ N/m}^2 \approx 35.9 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Final Answer:** (i) $R_{eq} \approx 0.0857$ m; (ii) $b \approx 53.3$ µm; (iii) $P_{max} \approx 35.9$ MPa.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Unit-mismatch error:** Mixing mm and µm in the same calculation. *Fix:* Convert all lengths to metres **before** plugging into the formula.
> 2. **Forgetting the 1/3 factor** in the gravure cell volume (pyramidal, not cubical). *Fix:* Always draw the cell shape; if it tapers, divide by 3.
> 3. **Treating "impression cylinder" as a passive idler.** It is actually a **load-bearing** element that sets the nip pressure. Examiners expect this to be stated explicitly.
> 4. **Skipping the anilox** in a flexo diagram. Even a single-line drawing must show ink pan → anilox → plate → impression → web, in that order.
> 5. **Using Poisson's ratio incorrectly** in Hertzian derivations. The combined modulus formula $E^* = E / [2(1 - \nu^2)]$ is for *two identical* cylinders of the *same* material. If materials differ, you must keep the two-term form.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist before entering the exam hall:**

- [ ] **Linear contact = a *line* of physical contact** between applicator and substrate — the defining feature of gravure, flexo, offset, and roller-coating machines.
- [ ] **Five machine types to remember:** Gravure, Flexo, Offset, Screen, Roller Coat.
- [ ] **Gravure formula (memorize!):** $h = d/3$ for pyramidal cells; $V_c = bld/3$; total ink $Q = wL \alpha d/3$.
- [ ] **Flexo key components (in order):** Ink pan → Anilox → Doctor blade → Plate cylinder → Impression cylinder → Web.
- [ ] **Anilox parameters:** Line screen (lpi) and cell volume (BCM).
- [ ] **Offset key principle:** Planographic — image and non-image are on the *same* plane; differentiated by wet-ability, not height.
- [ ] **Screen printing:** Squeegee floods ink through a stencil mesh; line contact between screen and substrate.
- [ ] **Hertzian contact:** $R_{eq} = R_1 R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$ and $E^* = E / [2(1 - \nu^2)]$ for identical cylinders.
- [ ] **Nip pressure scales as** $\sqrt{F}$ — doubling the load increases pressure by only $\sqrt{2}$.
- [ ] **Bierwagen split model:** $\phi = 1 / [1 + (\eta_a/\eta_s)^{0.5}]$ — controls how much ink stays on the substrate vs. the applicator.
- [ ] **Typical speed order:** Gravure > Flexo > Offset > Screen.
- [ ] **Typical run-length order:** Gravure > Flexo > Offset > Screen.
- [ ] **Pre-treatment is mandatory:** Corona treatment raises surface energy of PE/PP films to **38 – 42 dyne/cm** before any linear-contact printing.
- [ ] **Wet film vs. dry film:** Always quote both. Dry film = wet film × (1 + solvent content).
- [ ] **Industrial applications to name in viva:** Snack packaging (gravure), shampoo sachets (flexo), milk pouches (flexo), credit cards (offset + screen), car dashboards (in-mould + coating).
- [ ] **Always state the "why"** in answers — examiners reward engineering *reasoning*, not just descriptive text.
